Prophylactic continuous positive airway pressure therapy (CPAP) was initiated in hospital setting in infants with body weight ≥5 kg. CPAP...
presentations.authors_text: B. Fabrizzi1, A. Peruzzi1, L. Luciani1, M. Bordicchia1, N. Caporelli1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1CF Centre- AOU delle Marche, Ancona, Italy

sections.Background: Pulmonary atelectasis may represent an early radiological manifestation of lung disease in infants with cystic fibrosis (CF). In early life, therapeutic options are limited, and supportive respiratory strategies may play a role in preventing progression of lung abnormalities.
sections.Methods: Since 2024 in our CF Centre, infants underwent to low-dose chest CT at 3-6 months of age. Infants with CT evidence of pulmonary atelectasis were admitted to our hospital for bronchoalveolar lavage followed by empirical intravenous antibiotic therapy adjusted by microbiological data. Prophylactic continuous positive airway pressure therapy (CPAP) was initiated in hospital setting in infants with body weight ≥5 kg. CPAP was delivered via nasal mask at 4–6 cmH₂O and continued at home. Clinical and adherence follow-up was conducted at least every 3 months.
sections.Results: Between 2023 and 2024, six infants with CF were diagnosed at our CF Centre and underwent early chest CT; all showed pulmonary atelectatis lesions at 3 months of age. CPAP therapy was initiated in four infants with severe atelectasis. Two infants did not require ventilatory support due to mild severity of atelectasis. Long-term CPAP was well tolerated, with no discontinuations. During follow-up, treated infants showed reduction of existing atelectasis and no development of new pulmonary lesions. None received CFTR modulator therapy during the study period.
sections.Conclusion: In the absence of approved CFTR modulator therapy for CF infants, early CF management still requires a multidisciplinary approach, including supportive respiratory interventions. Our preliminary experience suggests that nocturnal CPAP is feasible, well tolerated, and may be effective in reducing and preventing pulmonary atelectasis in infants with CF.

presentations.title: Evolving physiotherapy challenges through a lung cancer journey in a woman with cystic fibrosis
presentations.summary: 66 year old woman with Cystic Fibrosis (CF) diagnosed 2002 aged 44 via cascade screening. She had a 40 pack year smoking history, stopped March 2023. History of emphysema, osteopenia, depression, anxiety, agoraphobia, breathing pattern disorder (BPD) and chronic pain. Started Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor October 2021; FEV1 improved from 1.43L (67%) to 1.59L (74%), FVC 2.24 L (83%) to 2.53 (97%). October 2024 she presented with haemoptysis, shortness of breath, increased cough and sputum. Chest x-ray showed slight left lower lobe collapse, resolved with intravenous antibiotics, physiotherapy and tranexamic acid, but then recurred 6 weeks later. presentations.summary: Background: Exercise as an Airway Clearance Technique (ExACT) is an exercise-based airway clearance program that includes at least 20 minutes of aerobic exercise at an intensity sufficient to induce deep breathing, combined with breath-control techniques, huffing, and coughing. Objective: To compare two aerobic exercise modalities used as substitutes for traditional airway clearance techniques (ACT), assessing their ability to maintain clinical stability and to determine whether one provides superior clinical benefit. presentations.authors_text: A. Scorretti1, A. Peruzzi1, L. Luciani1, M. Bordicchia1, N. Caporelli1, V. Zamponi1, B. Fabrizzi1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1AOU delle Marche, CF Centre, Ancona, Italy

sections.Background: Exercise as an Airway Clearance Technique (ExACT) is an exercise-based airway clearance program that includes at least 20 minutes of aerobic exercise at an intensity sufficient to induce deep breathing, combined with breath-control techniques, huffing, and coughing.
sections.Objectives: To compare two aerobic exercise modalities used as substitutes for traditional airway clearance techniques (ACT), assessing their ability to maintain clinical stability and to determine whether one provides superior clinical benefit.
sections.Methods: A 23-year-old man with CF, undergoing elexacaftor–tezacaftor–ivacaftor (ETI) treatment for 3 years, stable clinical condition for at least 3 months prior to starting the study, poor expectoration, and with FEV1 ≥ 80% of predicted value, was enrolled. During the study, the second daily traditional ACT session was replaced for 4 months with two alternating ExACT modalities: a running session (endurance training) during the 1st and 3rd months, and a high-intensity interval training circuit (HIIT) during the 2nd and 4th months. Both modalities were performed once daily on at least 5 days per week, for a total of 20 sessions per month, with exercise intensity reaching at least 70% of the theoretical maximum heart rate and a Borg scale rating ≥ 7/10 for 30 minutes. ExACT is structured according to the FITT principle (Frequency, Intensity, Time, and Type) to ensure a standardized yet individualized exercise prescription. FEV1, 6-minute walking distance (6MWD), Borg, overnight oximetric monitoring, Numeric Rating Scale on perceived sleep quality (NRS), Bioelectrical Impedance Analysis (BIA), hand-grip strength test, Visual Analogic Scale (VAS), Cystic Fibrosis Questionnaire-revised (CFQ-R) and training adherence (through the use of a monitoring platform) were administered at T0, T1 (after 4 weeks), T2 (after 8 weeks), T3 (after 12 weeks) and T4 (after 16 weeks).
sections.Results: The practice of ExACT, in both modalities, improved and ensured clinical stability, as evidenced by a 10% improvement in FEV₁ maintained throughout the entire study period, a progressive increase in 6MWD (T0: 720 m; T4: 811 m), improved mean nocturnal oxygen saturation and perceived sleep quality, as well as favorable changes in body composition and muscle mass, with improvements in physical energy and emotional well-being as assessed by the CFQ-R.
sections.Conclusion: The case report shows that both ExACT modalities are effective in maintaining clinical stability and are well appreciated by the patient, who in this case demonstrated high adherence to the prescribed program (70 out of 80 days), monitored via a telemonitoring system.

presentations.authors_text: A. Mena-Córdova1, M. Reyes-Apodaca2, J. Belmont-Sánchez1, J.L. Lezana-Fernández3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Hospital Infantil de México Federico Gómez, Rehabilitation Department, Mexico City, Mexico, 2Hospital Infantil de México Federico Gómez, Research and Diagnosis Unit in Nephrology and Bone Mineral Metabolism, Mexico City, Mexico, 3Hospital Infantil de México Federico Gómez, Cystic Fibrosis Clinic, Mexico City, Mexico

sections.Background: People with Cystic Fibrosis (PwCF) struggle with exercise intolerance and dyspnea, which can gradually limit participation in daily activities. Beyond the physiological burden, repeated episodes of breathlessness may also generate fear or avoidance of physiotherapy, particularly airway clearance techniques. In this context, supervised and individualized physiotherapy may play a role not only in improving physical performance but also in rebuilding confidence and treatment adherence. Case Description: We report the case of a 9-year-old boy with CF who presented with exercise-related dyspnea, increased oxygen requirements during physical activity, and marked discomfort during airway clearance. These difficulties appeared to interfere with school attendance, recreational play, and independent mobility. At the time of intervention, no acute radiological changes were identified, and the patient was clinically stable. Physiotherapy Problems Identified: The main issues identified were an inefficient breathing pattern, early onset of dyspnea during exertion, slow recovery after exercise, and reliance on higher supplemental oxygen flow. In addition, fear associated with coughing and airway clearance seemed to affect technique execution and overall adherence to physiotherapy. Physiotherapy Intervention: A supervised physiotherapy program was implemented over a three-month period. Sessions combined breathing retraining with airway clearance techniques, including diaphragmatic breathing, autogenic drainage, total slow expiration with open glottis in lateral decubitus (ETGOL), cough training, and the use of an Aerobika® device. These were followed by aerobic and strength training. Aerobic exercise consisted of 20 minutes of treadmill walking at approximately 65% of the speed achieved during the six-minute walk test (6MWT). Strength exercises focused on pectoral muscles, anterior and middle deltoids, and the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles, using a yellow TheraBand®, with 10 repetitions per exercise. Airway clearance and strengthening exercises were also prescribed for home practice. For cardiopulmonary safety, all supervised sessions were performed with supplemental oxygen at 4 L/min. Outcome Measures and Discussion: Changes were assessed using the 6MWT, oxygen requirements during exercise, post-exercise recovery to baseline cardiopulmonary parameters, and perceived exertion and dyspnea measured by the Borg scale. While the distance walked did not change substantially, oxygen requirements during exercise decreased by 3 L/min, and recovery after exertion was noticeably faster. The Borg score also declined, suggesting less perceived fatigue and dyspnea. (Table 1) Six-Minute Walk TestBaselinePost-InverventionDistance walked (m)395393Recovery within 5 minutesNoYesOxygen Saturation Recovery SpO2, (baseline-end)No (92% - 86%)Yes (86% - 90%)Heart rate (bpm) recovery (baseline-end)No (130 - 136 bpm)Yes (138 - 132 bpm)Borg Scale Rating (baseline, end)Dyspnea 1, Dyspnea 5 Fatigue 1, Fatigue 3Dyspnea 0, Dyspnea 1 Fatigue 0, Fatigue ​1 Supplemental oxygen required during test6 L/min3 L/minPauses during walkingYesNo These changes appeared to extend beyond exercise performance, translating into better tolerance of school activities, play, and daily mobility. Interestingly, the patient also reported less fear when performing airway clearance, which may reflect improved symptom control and greater confidence developed through supervision. From a broader perspective, this case highlights how physiotherapy-related benefits may not be fully captured by distance-based outcomes alone, particularly when gains in recovery, perceived exertion, emotional comfort, and overall participation in everyday life point toward a meaningful improvement in quality of life.

presentations.title: Physiotherapy as a key driver of adherence in an adolescent with cystic fibrosis: a case presentation
presentations.summary: Adolescence is a well-known risk period for reduced adherence to cystic fibrosis (CF) therapies. Despite CFTR modulator therapy, effective airway clearance and inhaled antibiotics remain essential to prevent pulmonary exacerbations and preserve lung function. Physiotherapists support adolescents and their families in maintaining adherence during this complex period. We present a 14-year-old female with CF (ΔF508 heterozygous), diagnosed at the age of 2 years, with intermittent disease severity (Leeds criteria). presentations.title: Physiotherapy management of prolonged admission required for Mycobacterium abscessus eradication
presentations.summary: We present two cases of prolonged admission in patients with Cystic Fibrosis colonised with Mycobacterium Abscessus. Patient A presented with multiple episodes of haemoptysis, one large volume requiring embolisation. He previously failed NTM eradication, required multiple courses of intravenous (IV) antibiotics, more recently commencing IV bacteriophage therapy. Admission lasted 18 months. Patient B presented unwell with a background history of NTM, on a maintenance oral regime. NTM pneumonias were discovered on CT, requiring eradication therapy. Admission lasted 17 weeks. presentations.summary: Background: Progressive decline in pulmonary function in CF is accompanied by the development of chronic respiratory failure. Long-term non-invasive ventilation (NIV) helps correct hypercapnia, improve oxygenation, and can be used as a component of comprehensive respiratory support. Objective: To present the first one-year follow-up of a CF patient and hypercapnic respiratory failure receiving long-term home NIV. Materials and Methods: A 19-year-old male CF patient was observed. He received low-flow oxygen therapy for 6 months without clinical benefit. Due to the progression of respiratory failure, long-term home NIV was initiated. Assessments were performed at baseline and after 6 and 12 months and included g...
presentations.authors_text: A. Serikova1, I. Mukatova1, S. Kim1, E. Amelina1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Astana Medical University, Department of Internal Medicine №3, Astana, Kazakhstan

sections.Background: Progressive decline in pulmonary function in CF is accompanied by the development of chronic respiratory failure. Long-term non-invasive ventilation (NIV) helps correct hypercapnia, improve oxygenation, and can be used as a component of comprehensive respiratory support.
sections.Objectives: To present the first one-year follow-up of a CF patient and hypercapnic respiratory failure receiving long-term home NIV. Materials and
sections.Methods: A 19-year-old male CF patient was observed. He received low-flow oxygen therapy for 6 months without clinical benefit. Due to the progression of respiratory failure, long-term home NIV was initiated. Assessments were performed at baseline and after 6 and 12 months and included gas exchange analysis, weight changes, and quality of life using the EQ-5D-3L.
sections.Results: Baseline arterial blood parameters: PaCO₂ - 58.6 mmHg, PaO₂ - 59.1 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ - 30.2 mmol/l. NIVL was well tolerated. After 6 months, a decrease in dyspnea (mMRC from 4 to 3), a decrease in hypercapnia (PaCO₂ - 49.7 mmHg), an improvement in oxygenation (PaO₂ - 68.7 mmHg) and a weight gain of 2 kg were noted. After 12 months, gas exchange parameters remained stable: PaCO₂ - 48.3 mmHg, PaO₂ - 68.2 mmHg; the total weight gain was 5 kg. According to the EQ-5D-3L data, an improvement in mobility, daily activity and a decrease in anxiety and depressive manifestations were recorded after one year; Subjective health assessment increased from 40 to 60 points.
sections.Conclusion: Long-term home NIV in a CF patient and hypercapnic respiratory failure resulted in sustained correction of gas exchange disorders, improved nutritional status, and enhanced quality of life over a one-year follow-up period. This case is the first reported experience of long-term management of a CF patient on NIV in Kazakhstan.

presentations.title: Initiation of CFTR modulator therapy in the first 2 years of life: real-world outcomes with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor
presentations.summary: Background: Advances in newborn screening and supportive care have significantly improved early cystic fibrosis (CF) outcomes. Early use of highly effective CFTR modulators is an emerging strategy, but data on elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) in children under 2 years remain scarce. We report early outcomes of ETI in infants included in the MODUL-CF study (NCT04301856). Methods: The initiation of ETI was decided after a multidisciplinary discussion in patients with severe cystic fibrosis. Follow-up included clinical assessment, sweat testing, microbiology, laboratory tests, CFTR modulator plasma levels, and chest CT. Age- and weight-based ETI dosing were extrapolated from ivacaftor monotherapy recommenda...
presentations.authors_text: A. Le1, N.H. Truong2, M. Baravalle3, S.N. Benaboud2, H. Corvol4, L. Couderc5, F. Foissac2, A. Gabsi6, G. Labe7, C. Ohlmann8, T. Perrin8, E. Peulier-Maitre4, M. Ribault9, N. Stremler3, C. Tillard10, M. Mittaine11, I. Sermet-Gaudelus1, MODUL-CF Study Group
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Necker Enfants Malades Hospital, Paris, France, 2Cochin Hospital, Paris, France, 3Timone Hospital, Marseille, France, 4Trousseau Hospital, Paris, France, 5Rouen Hospital, Rouen, France, 6Versailles Hospital, Le Chesnay-Rocquencourt, France, 7Clermont Ferrand Hospital, Clermont Ferrand, France, 8Hospices Civils de Lyon, Lyon, France, 9Fougères Hospital, Fougères, France, 10Hôpital Alexandra Lepève, Dunkerque, France, 11Toulouse Hospital, Toulouse, France

sections.Background: Advances in newborn screening and supportive care have significantly improved early cystic fibrosis (CF) outcomes. Early use of highly effective CFTR modulators is an emerging strategy, but data on elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) in children under 2 years remain scarce. We report early outcomes of ETI in infants included in the MODUL-CF study (NCT04301856).
sections.Methods: The initiation of ETI was decided after a multidisciplinary discussion in patients with severe cystic fibrosis. Follow-up included clinical assessment, sweat testing, microbiology, laboratory tests, CFTR modulator plasma levels, and chest CT. Age- and weight-based ETI dosing were extrapolated from ivacaftor monotherapy recommendations.
sections.Results: Eleven infants carrying at least one F508del allele were treated for severe respiratory disease, including lung atelectasis (n=9) and/or bronchiectasis (n=4) on CT, despite maximal therapy. ETI was initiated at a mean age of 7.2 months (range 3–14). Rapid improvement in respiratory symptoms was observed and resolution of CT abnormalities in the 2 children who underwent chest CT at 3 months ETI at 01/01/2026. Mean sweat chloride decreased from 114 mmol/L (85-114) to 31.7 mmol/L at 1 month (21-47). Fecal elastase improved from 48.8 (0-251) to 314 µg/g (0-591). Treatment was generally well tolerated. Two infants experienced mild, transient sleep disturbances, and one had a transient elevation of liver enzymes not attributed to ETI. Plasma ETI levels were within adult reference ranges.
sections.Conclusion: ETI in children under 2 years with severe respiratory disease is associated with clinical improvement and good tolerance consistent with that observed in older age group. These findings support further investigation of early ETI initiation to modify CF lung disease progression.

presentations.title: Organoid testing to implement theratyping for CFTR modulator therapy
presentations.summary: Objectives: The successful history of CFTR modulator therapy in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) began in 2012 with the corrector ivacaftor. Thirteen years later, two highly effective CFTR modulator therapies are already available in Europe for the treatment of non-class I mutations. The extent to which the CFTR modulator therapy response using patient-derived intestinal organoids (PDIOs) correlates with the clinical response is of great importance, as theratyping could provide a basis for deciding between one CFTR modulator or another. Methods: Human intestinal organoids were established from biopsy specimens by standard methods. Human colonic and duodenal biopsy specimens were obtained from CF-patients and...
presentations.authors_text: A. Müller1, E. Romanowska2,3, S. Reusch1, G. Kliem1, C. Klotz1, C. Schwarz2,3,4
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Robert Koch Institute, Department of Infectious Diseases, Unit for Mycotic and Parasitic Agents and Mycobacteria, Berlin, Germany, 2HMU University Potsdam, Internal Medicine, Potsdam, Germany, 3Klinikum Westbrandenburg, Potsdam, Germany, 4Clinic Ernst von Bergmann, CF Center, Potsdam, Germany

sections.Objectives: The successful history of CFTR modulator therapy in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) began in 2012 with the corrector ivacaftor. Thirteen years later, two highly effective CFTR modulator therapies are already available in Europe for the treatment of non-class I mutations. The extent to which the CFTR modulator therapy response using patient-derived intestinal organoids (PDIOs) correlates with the clinical response is of great importance, as theratyping could provide a basis for deciding between one CFTR modulator or another.
sections.Methods: Human intestinal organoids were established from biopsy specimens by standard methods. Human colonic and duodenal biopsy specimens were obtained from CF-patients and one healthy individual at Charité Virchow Clinic, Berlin and at CF Center Potsdam (with ethics approval: #2022-40-BO). For the forskolin-induced swelling (FIS)-assay multiple rounds were performed, in which 6-8 organoid cultures from different 66 donors were tested in parallel. For comparison, 1 healthy control and 1 homozygous F508del/F508del control culture were included in each round. The image analysis for quantification of the organoid surface area increase over time was performed using the ImageJ-software with Bio-Formats plugin. Human intestinal organoids were treated with CFTR modulator therapy (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor and vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor). In this prospective study, organoid responses were compared with clinical biomarker response.
sections.Results: In total, 32 pwCF were enrolled into the study. The triple CFTR modulator therapy with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor was inferior compared to vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaftor in the PDIOs swelling assay.
sections.Conclusion: The new, once daily triple CFTR modulator therapy with vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor shows a higher response in the forskolin-induced swelling-assay in intestinal organoids compared to elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor.

presentations.title: Real-world experience of switching from elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor to vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaftor in a UK adult cystic fibrosis centre
presentations.summary: Objectives: To evaluate decision-making, clinical outcomes and tolerability of switching from elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) to vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaftor (VTD) in a UK adult cystic fibrosis centre. Methods: Eligible patients were offered to switch to VTD following discussion of benefits and risks. Demographics, decisions, clinical parameters (weight, FEV₁, FVC, liver function) and side effects were recorded. Baseline and three-month data were compared using paired t-tests or Wilcoxon signed-rank tests. Results: Of 159 patients on ETI, 78 chose to switch, 69 declined and 12 were undecided. No significant difference in age (p=0.27) or baseline FEV₁ %predicted (p=0.69) existed between those who...

sections.Objectives: To evaluate decision-making, clinical outcomes and tolerability of switching from elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) to vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaftor (VTD) in a UK adult cystic fibrosis centre.
sections.Methods: Eligible patients were offered to switch to VTD following discussion of benefits and risks. Demographics, decisions, clinical parameters (weight, FEV₁, FVC, liver function) and side effects were recorded. Baseline and three-month data were compared using paired t-tests or Wilcoxon signed-rank tests.
sections.Results: Of 159 patients on ETI, 78 chose to switch, 69 declined and 12 were undecided. No significant difference in age (p=0.27) or baseline FEV₁ %predicted (p=0.69) existed between those who chose to switch and those who declined. Reasons for switching were patient preference (52%), non-adherence to twice daily ETI (31%) and ETI side effects (17%), while patients declined mainly due to stability with good adherence (65%), monitoring burden (14%) and family planning (10%). At three months, no significant changes were observed in weight (p=0.15), FEV₁ (p=0.51), FVC (p=0.79), bilirubin, ALP, ALT, GGT and AST (p>0.05) among those on VTD. Of 38 respondents, 27 reported no side effects. Itching (n=3) and headache (n=2) resolved spontaneously. Six patients developed new or worsening diarrhoea: three resolved spontaneously within three months, with one aided by increased Pancreatic Enzyme Replacement Therapy (PERT) dosage and probiotics; one reverted to ETI due to tripled PERT requirement; two remain ongoing. Two patients had transient creatine kinase elevation (2,114 U/L and 813 U/L) which normalised within two weeks without intervention.
sections.Conclusion: VTD switching showed comparable three-month clinical outcomes with good tolerability. Patient preference and adherence concerns drove switching decisions, whilst stability on ETI therapy was the primary reason for declining. Gastrointestinal side effects, particularly diarrhoea, warrant close monitoring.

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor (ETI) has transformed outcomes for people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF), but adverse events (AEs) may require dose modifications. Limited evidence exists to guide dose reduction strategies in paediatric patients. To evaluate the clinical outcomes and AE resolution following ETI dose reduction in children and adolescents with CF.
sections.Methods: This multicentre, retrospective observational study included pwCF aged 6-18 years from seven Italian CF centres who underwent ETI dose reduction due to drug-related AEs between August 1, 2021 and December 31, 2024. Clinical parameters including lung function, sweat chloride, body mass index z-score (BMI), and laboratory data were assessed at baseline (T0), AE onset (T1), and 3 (T2), 6 (T3) and 12 (T4) months post-dose reduction.
sections.Results: Among 593 paediatric pwCF on ETI therapy, 50 (8.5%) showed AE related to ETI and 31 (5%) required dose reduction (median age 11.7 years, IQR 9.1-15.8). The most common AEs were elevated liver transaminases (19/31, 61%), gastrointestinal symptoms (4/31, 13%), and neuropsychiatric symptoms (4/31, 13%). Median time from ETI initiation to AE onset was 131 days (IQR 41-364). Dose reduction strategies varied significantly among centres. AE resolution occurred in 22/31 subjects (70%), with highest success rates for hepatic (13/19, 68%) and gastrointestinal events (4/4, 100%). Clinical efficacy parameters remained stable following dose reduction, with no significant deterioration in lung function, BMI.
sections.Conclusion: In this exploratory multicentre study, individualized dose reduction strategies resolved AEs in 70% of cases without short-term clinical deterioration, though the heterogeneity of approaches limits definitive recommendations. Prospective trials with standardized protocols are urgently needed.

sections.Background: CFTR-modulator therapy improved the clinical outcome of people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) remarkably. So far, little is known on pancreatic function recovery especially in children. Individual case descriptions indicate a recovery of the pancreatic function in young children with early initiation of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) therapy. We therefore aimed to systematically evaluate the pancreatic function in preschool children with ETI treatment.
sections.Methods: In a multicenter manner four large CF centers in Germany collected stool samples from newly ETI eligible children aged 2-5 years prior and after 3 and 12 months of ETI therapy. Fecal-elastase (< 200µ/g defines pancreatic insuffenciency) was determined prospectively in each center.
sections.Results: 68 children were included. Median age was 3.5 years at ETI start (range 2.0-6.1). 59 were pancreatic insufficient prior ETI therapy. Thereof 3 (4,4%) became pancreatic sufficient after a median of six months (range 3-9) on ETI. On average, the level of fecal elastase increased 50µg/g(p=0.002).
sections.Conclusion: In preschool children fecal elastase should be monitored after ETI start as the pancreas resumes functioning in some children. Further and longer studies are needed to assess this effect, especially if pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy is reduced.

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a multisystem genetic disease leading to defective chloride transport. Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ELX/TEZ/IVA)) has demonstrated significant clinical benefit but adherence may be limited by side effects. Dose reduction is a potential strategy to improve tolerability while maintaining efficacy; however, there is limited data for paediatric populations. This study aims to investigate when children have significant side effects on full dose ELX/TEZ/IVA, whether a reduced dose is still associated with a clinically relevant improvement in sweat chloride whilst lowering the burden of side effects in children.
sections.Methods: A retrospective cohort study using data obtained from the Leeds Children’s Hospital Paediatric Cystic Fibrosis Centre. Data on sweat chloride concentrations were used as a biomarker for drug efficacy. The study analysed 119 children (aged 2–17 years) with at least one F508del mutation treated with ELX/TEZ/IVA.
sections.Results: Of 119 patients, 100 had complete results at either full dose or half dose ELX/TEZ/IVA. 88 were on full dose and 12 on half dose with a mean age of 10.65 years. Mean sweat chloride decrease from diagnosis in those on full dose was 62.33 ± 21.94 mmol/L, and mean sweat chloride decrease on half dose was 55.42 ± 14.83 mmol/L. Both decreases were significant (p<0.001). Mean sweat chloride at a half dose was 36.67 mmol/L. The difference in mean sweat chloride between full dose and half dose (6.91 mmol/L) was not statistically significant (p>0.05). Patients on half dose with only one copy of F508del variant had a larger mean decrease than those with two copies (62 and 53 mmol/L respectively) but this was a small sample size and not statistically significant (p=0.3758).
sections.Conclusion: In this population, a half dose of ELX/TEZ/IVA still showed a significant reduction of sweat chloride from baseline. Dose reduction may be effective in improving tolerability in children with side effects whilst maintaining a clinically relevant improvement in sweat chloride from pre-treatment baseline.

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a multisystem, genetic disease caused by mutations in the CFTR gene. The CFTR modulator combination elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has significantly improved clinical outcomes in people with CF, including pediatric patients. However, real-world data on adverse events and dose management, particularly in younger children, remain limited. The aim of this study is to evaluate the frequency of adverse events in children with CF treated with ETI, to assess dose adjustments in response to these events, to explore the effect of dose reduction on CFTR function and lung function and to document the recurrence of adverse events after dose modification.
sections.Methods: This observational real-world study included 80 children with CF aged 2–18 years (median age 10 years, IQR 6–12.5) treated with ETI at UZ Leuven. Clinical data were collected during routine outpatient visits. Outcomes included adverse events, dose reductions, sweat chloride concentration (SCC), forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV1) and frequency of pulmonary exacerbations.
sections.Results: Adverse events were reported in 28% of patients, with neuropsychiatric symptoms, such as irritability, mood changes, behavioral alterations and sleep disturbances, being the most frequent. Dose reduction was implemented in affected patients and proved effective in alleviating symptoms. Importantly, dose reduction had only a limited impact on CFTR function as assessed by SCC, while clinical benefits were preserved. The recurrence of adverse events after dose modification is still being investigated.
sections.Conclusion: Individualized dose reduction appears to be an effective strategy to manage adverse effects without substantially compromising therapeutic efficacy. These findings support careful monitoring and personalized dosing strategies in younger children receiving ETI.

sections.Objectives: CFTR modulator therapy with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) is a cornerstone of cystic fibrosis (CF) treatment. Whether the manufacturer’s three fixed dosage levels adequately account for changes in body weight and composition in growing children remains unclear. In addition, drug–drug interactions and dose reductions due to adverse effects highlight the need for therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM).
sections.Methods: Routinely collected blood samples from paediatric people with CF (pwCF) treated at the Children’s Hospital Heidelberg between November 2023 and March 2025 were analysed using isotope dilution liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (ID-LC-MS/MS) at LMU University Hospital Munich (Habler et al., 2021, DOI: 10.1515/cclm-2021-0724). Serum concentrations were compared with maximum drug concentrations (C_max) reported in the manufacturer’s summary of product characteristics, stratified by whether the full recommended dose was administered and whether sampling occurred within the median time to reach C_max (t_max).
sections.Results: In total, 63 samples from 46 pwCF (43.5% female) were analysed. Among pwCF that received the full dose, in samples obtained within t_max, serum concentrations exceeding C_max ± standard deviation (SD) were observed in 30%, 20%, and 11.8% of cases for elexacaftor, tezacaftor, and ivacaftor, respectively. Concurrently, 50%, 53.3%, and 58.8% of samples fell within the C_max ± SD range.
sections.Conclusion: Serum concentrations above the recommended target ranges are also observed in paediatric patients, although less frequently than reported in adults (Naehrig et al., 2024, DOI: 10.3390/jpm14101065). These findings support the feasibility of low-threshold dose reductions in the presence of drug interactions or adverse effects, preferably guided by therapeutic drug monitoring.

sections.Results: An increase in BMI was noted in both groups of patients: in group 1 Me (Q1-Q3) BMI kg/m2 at the start was 17.0 (15.5; 19.8), after 3 months 17.8 (16.2; 20.3) p<0.001, in group 2 Me (Q1-Q3) BMI kg/m2 at the start was 16.7 (15.2; 18.6), after 3 months 17.9 (16.6; 19.8) p<0.001. The sweat test values (conductivity, mmol/l) differed both at the start of therapy and after a month in each group: group 1 at the start Me (Q1;Q3) 108.0 (96;115), after 1 month - 43.5.0 (35;49) p=0.028. Group 2 at the start 123.0 (102;133), after 1 month - 75.0 (62;93), p=0.036. The comparing of the sweat test Me (Q1; Q3) (mmol/l) between the groups revealed significant difference at the start of therapy, p=0.011, and after 30 days of therapy, p=0.009. The difference between the average change of the sweat test in both groups was comparable - group 1 - 43 (35.5; 55), group 2 - 42 (32; 50.3) mmol/l, p=0.86. FEV1 and FVC showed a tendency to improve with two drugs. The groups did not differ in the number of adverse events (AE), which were not severe and did not lead to drug discontinuation.
sections.Conclusion: The generic drug TN Trilexa® is effective and safe for use in patients with CF. The spectrum of reported AE with Trilexa® is comparable to that with the original drug Trikafta. The study was carried out as part of the research RCMG (122032300396-1).

sections.Objectives: Clinical response to ETI among eligible people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) is heterogeneous. A lower-than-expected response compared with clinical trial outcomes may reflect primary non-responsiveness or secondary, potentially modifiable factors. The aim of this registry-based project is to identify low responders in real time and develop an algorithm for their subsequent management.
sections.Methods: Annual SwCl and FEV1 data were analyzed for all Czech pwCF who had been treated with ETI for ≥ 6 months. They were identified as primary low responders if their first SwCl measurement (usually within 4-6 months of ETI initiation) showed values ≥60 mmol/L with <20% reduction from baseline, together with <5% absolute ppFEV₁ improvement. Secondary low responders were defined as those who met these SwCl criteria in any subsequent (not the first) year and showed a ≥10% persistent decline in ppFEV₁ from the best on-treatment value over the preceding 6 months. A stepwise evaluation was performed on all low responders, including CFTR genotype reanalysis, assessment of adherence and drug–drug interactions, and screening for complications or comorbidities. Follow-up comprised clinical reassessment and repeat SwCl measurement. Persistent cases were referred for inpatient monitored ETI therapy, multidisciplinary review, and ex vivo CFTR function testing.
sections.Results: Of 535 pwCF receiving ETI, 21 met entry criteria of the SwCl-based algorithm. Data collection is ongoing to further clarify causes of low response. Preliminary validation indicates that confirmed low responders are rare, with five pwCF classified as primary low responders.
sections.Conclusion: This registry-based national strategy supports individualized management of rare low responders to ETI and identifies missing or incomplete data. Regular annual registry-based reviews are essential for timely identification of secondary low responders. SwCl and ppFEV₁ should be interpreted within a long-term and broader clinical context.

sections.Objectives: We assessed the real-world outcomes of treatment with ETIgf in pwCF with responding variants, with and without prior treatment with an mCFTR. Patients and
sections.Methods: Our single center, prospective study included pwCF >6 years, indicated for treatment with ETIgf (Trixacar, Gador Argentina) for 12 months or more. We assessed pulmonary function (percent predicted forced expiratory volume in 1 second [ppFEV1]; Lung Clearance Index [LCI]), BMI z-score, quality of life (Cystic Fibrosis Questionnaire Revised [CFQ-R], respiratory domain), sweat chloride, fecal elastase, and respiratory exacerbations prior to initiation and after 6–12 months of treatment.
sections.Results: A total of 71 pwCF were included in this study. 43 pwCF (60%; 24 females, median age 10.6 years) were treatment-naïve and included in group 1; and 28 pwCF (40%; 7 females, median age 10.9 years) had received previous treatment with an mCFTR and were included in group 2. Treatment with ETIgf demonstrated significant improvements in pulmonary function (ppFEV1: +21 and 19%; LCI (−13 and 11%), and quality of life (CTF-R respiratory domain: +66 and 30%), and decreases in sweat chloride measurements (−53 and 58%) and severe respiratory exacerbations (−95%), for groups 1 and 2, respectively. Median (IQR) BMI z-score at baseline and after 12 months treatment was −0.2 (-0.9 to 0.3) and 0.3 (-0.3 to 1.1) (p<0.001), and -0.1 (-0.8 to 0.6) and -0.1 (-0.8 to 0.8) (p=0.3) for groups 1 and 2, respectively
sections.Conclusion: In pwCF and responding variants in the real-world, treatment with ETIgf demonstrates significant clinical efficacy and is well tolerated.

sections.Objectives: To evaluate the long-term effect of a generic triple modulator therapy formulation (ETIg) on airway infection in paediatric patients with Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF). Patients and
sections.Methods: Prospective cohort study included pwCF aged 6 to 18 years with responsive variants, who initiated ETIg (Trixacar, Gador Argentina) treatment between Nov. 2021 and Nov. 2023. Bacterial isolates identified in quarterly cultures of spontaneous sputum (ss) or cough swab (cs), according to patient capability, were analysed. Bacteriological results obtained over a period of two years of follow-up were compared with those from the year prior to ETIg initiation. Variables were analysed using Fisher’s exact test.
sections.Results: Sixty-nine pwCF were included in this study (39 male). Median (IQR) age was 10.9 (8.6-14.1) years. Previous yr (A): 69 patients, 272 cultures, 209 ss (77%)/ 63 cs (23%) 1st yr (B): 69 patients, 274 cultures, 142 ss (52%)/ 132 cs (48%) (p<0.001) 2nd yr (C): 68 patients, 241 cultures, 119 ss (49%)/ 122 cs (51%) (p<0.001) Positive cultures in A, B and C, respectively, were: P. aeruginosa (Pa): 73/272 (27%), 40/274 (15%) and 33/241 (14%) (p<0.001) MSSA: 166/272 (61%), 132/274 (48%) and 125/241 (52%), (p<0.03) MRSA: 66/272 (24%), 48/274 (17%) and 42/241 (17%) (p=0.036) S. maltophilia: 19/272 (7%), 3/274 (1%) and 3/241 (1%) (p<0.001) Achromobacter sp.: 15/272 (6%), 6/274 (2%), 3/241 (1%) (p<0.03) M. abscessus: 8/272 (3%), 4/274 (1%) (p=NS), 0/241 (p=0.02) Patients with chronic infection in A, B and C, respectively, isolated: Pa: 15/69 (22%), 8/69 (16%) (p=NS) and 5/68 (7%) (p=0.01) MSSA: 36/69 (52%), 31/69 (44%) (p=NS) and 21/68 (30%) (p=0.009) MRSA: 15/69 (22%), 11/69 (16%) (p=NS) and 8/68 (12%) (p=0.04)
sections.Conclusion: The use of ETIg had a significant impact on reducing mucus production. Pa, MSSA, MRSA, S. maltophilia, and Achromobacter sp decreased over the two years of treatment. Chronic infection with Pa, MSSA, and MRSA decreased after the second year of treatment.

sections.Objectives: Limited evidence exists to guide Elexacaftor-Tezacaftor-Ivacaftor (ETI) use in adults with cystic fibrosis (awCF) and preserved lung function (ppFEV1 >90%). To address the resulting variability in prescribing practices, we conducted a European survey among a group of adult CF centres aiming at identifying factors influencing ETI initiation decisions in adults with preserved lung function.
sections.Methods: Between April and June 2024, we invited 25 ECFS prescribers from 25 adult CF centres to participate in a web-based survey to explore factors influencing ETI initiation in adults with CF and ppFEV₁ >90%. The survey questionnaire collected data on centre characteristics, prescribing ETI attitudes, and the impact of specific clinical variables on ETI prescription decision in this subgroup.
sections.Results: Twenty-three CF specialists (92%) responded. Most specialists (69.6%) favoured early treatment initiation in all eligible patients. Key factors influencing ETI initiation decisions were the presence of respiratory symptoms. In adults with preserved lung function, however, microbiological and imaging features emerged as the most influential factors driving treatment decision. While patient willingness and symptoms strongly encouraged ETI use, factors like age over 50, a history of mental health issues, and pregnancy desire resulted in conflicting prescribing attitudes among centers.
sections.Conclusion: Although several disease markers more consistently support the decision to initiate ETI in individuals with preserved lung function, the absence of clear clinical guidance contributes to heterogeneous prescribing practices across centres. This highlights the need for longitudinal studies to clarify the long-term outcomes of ETI in this population.

sections.Objectives: To evaluate exocrine pancreatic function using FE-1 levels in PwCF aged 2-5 years receiving ETI therapy. Materials: Data from the Russian Federation CF Patient Registry, therapy with CFTR modulators section, were used. The study included 54 patients (48.1% boys, 51.9% girls) with CF who had elastase-1 data recorded before and during the year of ETI therapy. The median age was 3.5 (2.7; 4.5), min 0.3, max 6.1 years. All patients had a severe genotype (homozygotes F508del 48.1%, compound heterozygotes F508del/non F508del 46.3%, non F508del/non F508del 5.6%). Children received ETI granules in doses corresponding to their weight. Pancreatic function was assessed by the concentration of FE-1 at the start of therapy and during the year of observation. The best result of elastase-1 achieved during the year of observation after the start of therapy was assessed.
sections.Results: In 54 children at the start of therapy the median FE-1 was 15(15;40)μg/g. During the year of observation, the median was 19.5(15;136), p<0.001. An increase in FE-1 levels during the year of observation was noted in 21 patients (38.8%). 14 of 21 patients (66.6%) showed an increase in FE-1 More than 100mcg/g at the start of therapy, Me (Q1;Q3) 15 (15;15)mcg/g, over the course of a year — 369 (155;500) mcg/g (p<0.001). 10 of the 14 patients normalized FE-1 levels (more than 200mcg/g).
sections.Conclusion: When using ETI in a group of young children, pancreatic function improved in 38.8% of cases, and FE-1 levels normalized in 18.5%. It is necessary to strive to prescribe CFTR modulators as early as possible. Further observations are needed to determine the duration and sustainability of the improvement and normalization of FE-1 levels.

presentations.summary: Objectives: CFTR modulators have improved clinical outcomes in people with Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF), yet many still require complex supportive therapy regimens. Adherence to supportive therapies remains variable, and the role of structured medication reviews in optimising treatment is not well established. This study aimed to assess medication adherence across CF therapies using pharmacy refill and self-reported data, to compare adherence within individuals, and to identify Drug Related Problems (DRPs) and related interventions through structured pharmacist-led medication reviews. Methods: A single-centre cross-sectional study was conducted at the CF centre of Maastricht University Medical Centre+ (February-Apri...
presentations.authors_text: L. Roeke1,2, L.I van de Ven1, S.EM Vonk1, J.JM Coremans1, E. Dompeling3, M.AGE Bannier3, F. Karapinar-Carkit1,2, J. de Ruijter-van Dalem1,4
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Maastricht University Medical Centre+, Department of Clinical Pharmacy and Toxicology, Maastricht, Netherlands, 2Maastricht University Medical Centre+, Department of Clinical Pharmacy, Cardiovascular Research Institute Maastricht (CARIM), Maastricht, Netherlands, 3Maastricht University Medical Centre+, Department of Paediatric Respiratory Medicine, School for Public Health and Primary Care (CAPHRI), Maastricht, Netherlands, 4Maastricht University Medical Centre+, Department of Clinical Pharmacy, Institute of Nutrition and Translational Research in Metabolism (NUTRIM), Maastricht, Netherlands

sections.Objectives: CFTR modulators have improved clinical outcomes in people with Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF), yet many still require complex supportive therapy regimens. Adherence to supportive therapies remains variable, and the role of structured medication reviews in optimising treatment is not well established. This study aimed to assess medication adherence across CF therapies using pharmacy refill and self-reported data, to compare adherence within individuals, and to identify Drug Related Problems (DRPs) and related interventions through structured pharmacist-led medication reviews.
sections.Methods: A single-centre cross-sectional study was conducted at the CF centre of Maastricht University Medical Centre+ (February-April 2025), including adults and children. Adherence to CFTR modulators and supportive therapies was assessed using pharmacy refill data (Medication Possession Ratio, MPR) and self-reported adherence (Medication Adherence Report Scale-5, MARS-5). Structured pharmacist-led medication reviews were performed to identify DRPs and interventions were proposed to physicians. Data were analysed descriptively.
sections.Results: Forty-three pwCF (mean age 23.3 years, SD 14.4, range 6 months - 50 years) were included. Adherence to CFTR modulators was high (MPR 94.4%; MARS-5 91.2%), whereas adherence to supportive therapies was lower and more variable (MPR 45.5-69%; MARS-5 46.7-86.7%). Within-patient adherence was significantly higher for CFTR modulators than for supportive therapies (p<0.001). Medication reviews were completed in 41 participants and identified 51 DRPs, most commonly overtreatment (33.3%) and medication use or adherence problems (31.4%), resulting in 51 interventions.
sections.Conclusion: Adherence to CFTR modulators is high, while adherence to supportive therapies was suboptimal. Structured medication reviews revealed important opportunities to optimise treatment and improve adherence, highlighting a key role for pharmacists in routine CF care.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Institute of Mother and Child, Cystic Fibrosis, Warsaw, Poland, 2Paediatric Hospital, Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Dziekanow Lesny, Poland

sections.Background: Sweat testing, based on sweat chloride concentration (SCC), is a widely used tool for assessing CFTR channel function. SCC directly reflects CFTR activity in sweat glands; therefore, it serves as a sensitive biomarker of treatment response in cystic fibrosis and is useful for monitoring the effects of CFTR modulators over time.
sections.Objectives: To evaluate sweat chloride concentration (mmol/L) longitudinally in patients with cystic fibrosis treated with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) and to assess the dynamics and durability of treatment response. Materials and
sections.Methods: Among 57 patients who initiated ETI therapy in 2022, 32 remained under follow-up for >12 months and were included in the long-term analysis. Changes in SCC (mmol/L) were evaluated between consecutive time points (0–6, 6–12, 12–18, and 18–36 months) and as the overall change from baseline to 36 months. Sex-stratified analyses were performed.
sections.Results: SCC decreased significantly in the early period (Δ0–6 mean −42.18 mmol/L; p < 0.001) and subsequently stabilized, with no significant changes thereafter. At 36 months, SCC remained significantly lower than baseline (Δ0–36 mean −35.88 mmol/L; p < 0.001). Sex-stratified analyses indicated a difference between women and men during the 18–36 month period (p = 0.035), with women showing an increasing trend in sweat chloride concentration.
sections.Conclusion: An early decrease in sweat chloride concentration following ETI therapy was observed, with values remaining relatively stable over time. Exploratory analyses at later follow-up suggested a possible sex-related divergence, with higher sweat chloride concentrations observed in women.

presentations.title: Awareness and perceptions of a novel once-daily CFTR modulator (deutivacaftor/tezacaftor/vanzacaftor) in people with cystic fibrosis
presentations.summary: Introduction: Advances in CFTR modulation continues to change outcomes for people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). Deutivacaftor/tezacaftor/vanzacaftor (DTV) is a next-generation once-daily CFTR modulator demonstrating non-inferiority to existing triple therapy; therefore, understanding pwCF awareness of novel therapies and factors influencing decisions to switch is crucial. Exploring patient motivations, perceived benefits, and concerns is critical for successful integration of DTV into clinical practice. Methods: A structured questionnaire was developed by the CF team in Belfast. Eligible, consenting adult pwCF attending routine clinic appointments were offered the questionnaire over a 3-month period. Anonymised...
presentations.authors_text: J. Hanna1, S. Armstrong1, S. Bansal2, J. Faughey1, V. Mills1, E. Wright1, S. Caskey1, D. Downey1,3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Northern Ireland Regional Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, BHSCT, Belfast, United Kingdom, 2Queen’s University Belfast, School of Medicine, Belfast, United Kingdom, 3Centre for Experimental Medicine, Queen's University Belfast, Belfast, United Kingdom

sections.Methods: A structured questionnaire was developed by the CF team in Belfast. Eligible, consenting adult pwCF attending routine clinic appointments were offered the questionnaire over a 3-month period. Anonymised responses were transcribed into Microsoft Excel and coded for descriptive statistical analysis.
sections.Results: Eighty-eight pwCF participated in the survey. Familiarity with DTV was low (2% “very familiar”), with most learning of DTV from the CF team (60%) or social media (22%). Most frequent reasons for considering switching were “once-daily dosing” (70%), “trial to improve lung function” (61%), and “to improve quality of life” (60%). Concerns that current modulator treatment was ineffective was rare (8%). Fears around switching centred most frequently on “potential side effects” (42%), “may not be as effective” (41%), “risk of destabilising current health” (31%), and “feeling well on current treatment” (31%). Concerns relating to “peer influences” (1%) or for “additional monitoring” (8%) were minimal.
sections.Conclusion: Awareness of DTV among pwCF was limited. Key motivators to switch included once-daily dosing and hope for further clinical benefit. Hesitancy around switching centred on the potential side effects and perceived efficacy, rather than peer influence or monitoring. These findings highlight the need for patient education and shared decision-making when introducing novel CFTR modulators. Further research in warranted to understand perceptions in greater detail.

presentations.summary: Objectives: Cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator modulators (CFTRm) improve nutritional status, lung function and quality of life (QoL) in people with cystic fibrosis (PwCF); however, the magnitude of response varies considerably. Identification of baseline factors associated with treatment response may support individualised clinical assessment and follow-up. Assessments were performed immediately before treatment initiation and after 12 months. Primary outcomes included 12-month changes (Δ) in body mass index (BMI), force...
presentations.authors_text: P. Famulska1, I. Wojsyk-Banaszak2, E. Sapiejka1, A. Steinert-Dymecki1, M. Osińska1, B. Więckowska3, B. Narożna2, A. Musiał4, A. Szczepankiewicz2, K. Tąpolska – Jóźwiak2, A. Wiesner2, M. Jóźwiak1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Polanki Children’s Hospital, Department of Pediatrics and Cystic Fibrosis, Gdańsk, Poland, 2Poznan University of Medical Sciences, Department of Paediatric Pulmonology, Allergy and Clinical Immunology, Poznań, Poland, 3Poznan University of Medical Sciences, Department of Computer Science and Statistics, Poznań, Poland, 4Poznan University of Medical Sciences, Department of Diabetology, Auxology and Pediatric Obesity, Poznań, Poland

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator modulators (CFTRm) improve nutritional status, lung function and quality of life (QoL) in people with cystic fibrosis (PwCF); however, the magnitude of response varies considerably. Identification of baseline factors associated with treatment response may support individualised clinical assessment and follow-up.
sections.Methods: In this prospective observational cohort study, 146 PwCF aged 2–56 years treated with CFTRm were evaluated at two specialised cystic fibrosis (CF) centres in Poland. Assessments were performed immediately before treatment initiation and after 12 months. Primary outcomes included 12-month changes (Δ) in body mass index (BMI), forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV₁), forced vital capacity (FVC), reactance at 10 Hz (X₁₀), sweat chloride concentration (SwCl) and the respiratory domain of the Cystic Fibrosis Questionnaire–Revised (RD-CFQ-R). For each outcome, patients were classified as super-responders (>90th percentile) or non-responders (<10th percentile). Greater SwCl reduction indicated stronger response. Ridge regression models were applied to identify baseline predictors while accounting for multicollinearity.
sections.Results: Response to CFTRm therapy varied markedly across clinical domains. Greater improvements were associated with lower baseline lung function, poorer nutritional status and higher inflammatory markers. Chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa colonisation was linked to more pronounced responses in several outcomes. Treatment with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) was associated with a higher likelihood of super-responder status compared with tezacaftor/ivacaftor (TI) or lumacaftor/ivacaftor (LI).
sections.Conclusion: Baseline clinical, microbiological and inflammatory characteristics influence the magnitude of response to CFTRm therapy. These findings support phenotype-informed prognostication and personalised monitoring strategies in contemporary CF care.

Materials and methods: Data from the Russian CF Patient Registry for the period from 01 Jan 2025 to 01 Aug 2025 were analyzed. sections.Objectives: To evaluate the efficacy and safety of ETI therapy in children with cystic fibrosis (CF) in two age groups after switching drugs within the same INN. Materials and
sections.Methods: Data from the Russian CF Patient Registry for the period from 01 Jan 2025 to 01 Aug 2025 were analyzed. Efficacy and safety parameters were assessed at baseline (last day of original therapy) and after 30 and 90 days of generic therapy. A total of 151 patients were included and divided into two groups: 55 children aged 6–11 years (group 1) and 96 adolescents aged 12–18 years (group 2).
sections.Results: Both groups showed further increases in weight and height after 3 months of therapy (p<0.05); BMI also improved in group 1 (p<0.05). Spirometry parameters achieved with the original drug remained stable during 90 days of generic therapy. Sweat conductivity remained within borderline values after 1 month; it decreased in group 1 without statistical significance (p=0.451) and increased in group 2 (p=0.048). In group 2, median AST decreased from 22.5 (16.8;29.0) to 21.0 (16.0;26.0) U/L (p=0.005). Median total bilirubin decreased to 12.25 (9.4;17.4) μmol/L after 3 months (p=0.017). All parameters remained within reference ranges. The incidence of adverse events (AE) was low and did not require dose adjustment or drug discontinuation.
sections.Conclusion: Switching between ETI drugs within the same INN in children with CF maintained the therapeutic effect achieved with the original drug. The incidence of AE with the generic did not exceed that observed with the original therapy. Further studies of Trilexa® efficacy and safety are ongoing.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Skåne University Hospital, Pediatrics, Lund, Sweden

sections.Objectives: Several cases of prenatal treatment of meconium ileus in infants with cystic fibrosis (CF) through treating their healthy mother have been described. We present a case of prenatal treatment to avoid cross infection from an older sibling with CF.
sections.Methods: An older sibling with CF (homozygous with F508) has chronic, pulmonary infection caused by Burkholderia cepacia complex since the first visit in a Swedish CF centre. This child suffered from recurrent exacerbations with fever and purulent mucus and required intravenous antibiotics every two months prior to modulator treatment with Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) at the age of 5 years. After initiation of ETI, cultures remained positive for Burkholderia but exacerbations were fewer and the need for intravenous treatment decreased to every 6 months. Cultures showed resistance to almost all antibiotics. When the mother became pregnant with another CF child, the discussion of prenatal treatment was initiated with the mother, within the team and with paediatric colleagues working in CF centres. The goal of the treatment was to prevent cross infection from the older sibling.
sections.Results: Treatment of the mother was initiated during gestational week 23 in December 2024. Treatment continued until the baby started treatment at 6 weeks of age. We did liver enzyme testing on the mother before treatment and after 3 months of treatment. The baby was breastfed and thus received some dose of ETI in the breastmilk. First faecal elastase (FE) was 35 μg/g, second FE at 3 months was 207 μg/g. Pancreatic enzymes were started after the first FE but discontinued after ETI was initiated and the stool became normal. Liver enzymes are normal, and the baby thrives.
sections.Conclusion: The possibility of prenatal treatment of CF foetuses may change the newborn clinical status of these children and even their future.

sections.Methods: A retrospective study using the Russian Cystic Fibrosis Patient Registry, Targeted Therapy section, for 2021–2024. A total of 1,025 pwCF (529/51.6%female, 496/48.4%male) over 6 years of age who received ETI were included. Efficacy was assessed in pairs of patients using sweat testing, anthropometric data, and pulmonary function tests (FVC, FEV1; % predicted) at therapy initiation and during 3 years. Safety was evaluated using ALT, AST, total bilirubin, and adverse events (AE).
sections.Results: Median sweat chloride at baseline was 112 (96;121) mmol/L and decreased to 63.0 (49;78) mmol/L after one year (p<0.0001), with sustained results at years 2 and 3. Pulmonary function improved during the first year and remained stable thereafter. Median FVC increased from 82.15 (69.17;94.25)% to 95.5 (82.25;103.52)% after 3 years (p<0.0001). Median FEV1 increased from 75 (59.55;93.77)% to 90.5 (77.25;104)% (p<0.0001). Median BMI increased from 16.0 (14.6;18.2) to 18.58 (16.6;21.0) (p<0.0001). Liver function parameters remained within reference ranges. AE were reported in 9.4% of patients at therapy initiation and were rare after 3 years, mainly related to mental health.
sections.Conclusion: Pw CF who received three-component ETI therapy showed significant positive dynamics in basic health indicators compared to the initial ones they had while receiving only basic therapy. The long-term efficacy and safety of the ETI has been demonstrated for three years.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Lithuanian University of Health Sciences, Medical Academy, Pediatric Department, Kaunas, Lithuania

sections.Objectives: Highly effective CFTR modulator therapy has transformed cystic fibrosis (CF) care worldwide. However, in Lithuania CFTR modulators became available only in October 2024 with the introduction of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI). This study aimed to assess early clinical, inflammatory, and respiratory changes over one year in children initiating ETI for the first time.
sections.Methods: This prospective observational single-center study included children aged 2–17 years with at least one F508del mutation. Clinical assessment, sweat chloride, fecal elastase, inflammatory blood markers, spirometry (FEV₁), and Lung Clearance Index (LCI2.5) were evaluated at baseline and after 1, 3, 6, and 12 months. Paired comparisons were performed using the Wilcoxon signed-rank test.
sections.Results: Fifteen patients (median age 7 years) were analyzed. All reported clinical improvement. Cumulative inpatient days decreased from 134 to 84. BMI increased modestly but significantly (15.21 to 15.86, p=0.023), while fecal elastase remained unchanged. Sweat chloride decreased significantly from month 1 onward (p<0.05), with greater reductions in F508del homozygous patients. Inflammatory changes were delayed: neutrophils and neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio decreased at 6 and 12 months, while total leukocytes decreased only at 12 months; CRP and platelets remained stable. Mean baseline FEV₁ was normal (99.8% predicted) and increased significantly within the first three months, remaining higher than baseline at 12 months. LCI2.5 showed a non-significant decrease (10.68 to 9.12).
sections.Conclusion: ETI induced rapid clinical and FEV₁ improvement in previously modulator-naive children, while inflammatory markers, growth, and pancreatic function showed limited early response. Minimal LCI change suggests persistent small airway disease. These findings highlight that spirometry alone may overestimate early treatment success, supporting multidimensional monitoring.

sections.Background: In 2025 vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaftor (Alyftrek®) was licensed and commissioned by the National Health Service (NHS) for treatment of people with cystic fibrosis over 6 years of age in the United Kingdom. The development of this new modulator has broadened eligibility criteria. From our cohort, we have been able to prescribe Alyftrek® to three patients with non-class I mutations that were previously modulator naïve, including ‘off label’ prescriptions. The aim of this study is to follow our three modulator naïve patients and their response to Alyftrek®.
sections.Methods: Our primary end point was to assess a change in sweat chloride and lung function. Patients had baseline sweat chloride, FEV1 and Lung Clearance Index (LCI) prior to starting modulator therapy. Response to treatment was assessed with repeat lung function and sweat chloride 6-8 weeks after commencing therapy and again at 4-6 months. Our secondary end point was to assess a change in reported clinical symptoms at these intervals.
sections.Results: The three patients were female, between the ages of 9 and 16 with rare CFTR mutations. At present two of our three patients have commenced Alyftrek®. At 6 weeks after commencing modulator therapy, patient 1 (homozygous 3199del6) demonstrated a 20 mmol/L reduction in sweat chloride (119 to 99 mmol/L) as well as an improvement in ppFEV1 of 11.8% (110.6 to 122.4%). Further, patient 1 describes an improvement in appetite with a weight gain of 3 kg as well as resolution of nocturnal cough. Patient 2 (homozygous Y569D) is yet to have a repeat sweat test and has been on modulator therapy for 3 weeks. Her lung function remains unchanged at this stage (FEV1 93%). Patient 3 (G542X/ deletion of exon 2) will start modulator therapy in January 2026.
sections.Conclusion: Patient 1’s improvement in sweat chloride, lung function and reported clinical symptoms is promising. Data collection is in progress and will be complete by the end of May.

sections.Objectives: To evaluate clinical outcomes after 6 months of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) treatment in a low-income population.
sections.Methods: It was a prospective, longitudinal, observational study. Follow-up was assessed at two time points: T0 (initiation of therapy) and T1 (6 months after initiation). Outcomes included nutritional status (weight [kg], height [cm], body mass index [BMI]), pulmonary function (percent predicted forced vital capacity [FVC] and forced expiratory volume in 1 second [FEV1]), sweat chloride levels (mmol/L), and adverse events. Paired t-tests or Wilcoxon signed-rank tests were used to compare baseline and follow-up outcomes, depending on data distribution.
sections.Results: Twenty-eight individuals were included; 60.7% were male, mean age (SD) was 15.6 (5.1) years. After 6 months, the percent predicted FVC increased from 95.9 (18.3)% to 100.4 (9.4)%, but the difference was not statistically significant (p = 0.32). Percent predicted FEV1 increased from 86.4 (20.6)% to 96.6 (13.4)%, without a statistically significant difference (p = 0.05). There was a statistically significant reduction in mean sweat chloride levels (p < 0.005), from 97.2 (14.2) mmol/L to 40.3 (24.8) mmol/L. Anthropometric parameters showed significant improvement during follow-up, with an increase in weight (p < 0.005) from 40.7 (19.2) kg to 44.4 (18.6) kg and in height (p < 0.005) from 145.5 (18.5) cm to 148.4 (17.2) cm. BMI (kg/m²) also increased significantly (p < 0.005), from 18.1 (4.7) to 19.4 (4.6). Twelve individuals reported adverse events, most of them mild. Four children required temporary discontinuation of the medication due to adverse reactions, with no recurrence after ETI reintroduction.
sections.Conclusion: During follow-up, all clinical parameters improved. Approximately 50% of individuals experienced adverse events, none of which were severe. Despite the low-income setting, ETI therapy was highly effective.

sections.Objectives: The CFTR modulator therapies (mCFTR), especially elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI), has improved CFTR function and clinical efficacy in patients with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). The aim of this study was to determinate the effect of mCFTR on nutritional status, pulmonary function and sputum isolates, 18 months after initiation of therapy in pwCF.
sections.Methods: We have included 28 adolescent and adult pwCF, from the CF Center in University Clinic for Respiratory Diseases in Children “Kozle”- Skopje, Republic of North Macedonia. Body mass index (BMI), Forced expiatory volume in 1 s (FEV1) expressed % of predictive value and microbiological data were assessed before the first dose of mCFTR and after 18 months.
sections.Results: Median age was 29.03 years (range 16-45), 16 male (57%) and 12 female (43%). The mean values of BMI at baseline and 18 months after starting mCFTR were 23.03 (±3.80) and 24.15 (±4.73), respectively. FEV1, baseline mean values 67.01% (±25.79) and after 18 months 71.05% (±24.00). We have observed there was no statistically significant change in the BMI and FEV1, p=0.494, p=0.246, respectively. Before starting ETI almost all participants had positive culture, 39.28% (11 patients) Pseudomonas aeruginosa (PA), 28.57% (8 patients) Staphylococcus aureus (SA) and methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) 14.2% (4 patients). After 18 months positive were 17.8% (5 patients) for PA, 7.14% (2 patients) for SA and 3.57% (1 patients) for MRSA.
sections.Conclusion: Our study provides evidence of the beneficial effect of ETI treatment in pwCF 18 months after initiation in microbiological isolates, there was increase in the number of negative cultures, but there is no significant impact on nutritional status and FEV1.

sections.Objectives: Lumacaftor–ivacaftor (LUM-IVA) is a CFTR modulator approved for children aged >1 year who are homozygous for the F508del variant, targeting the underlying molecular defect. Despite evidence from clinical trials, real-world data in preschool-aged children remain limited. This study evaluated the effectiveness, safety, and tolerability of LUM-IVA in this population over 12 months of follow-up.
sections.Methods: We conducted a multicenter, prospective study in Italy including 69 children with CF aged 1–6 years treated with LUM-IVA. Anthropometric, laboratory, microbiological, and functional parameters were collected at baseline, and after 3, 6 and 12 months.
sections.Results: The median age was 3.82 years. LUM-IVA treatment was associated with significant reductions in sweat chloride concentration and pulmonary exacerbation frequency between baseline and 3/6 months. Weight improved significantly between T0 and T3 and between T1 and T2. Lung Clearance Index (LCI) also showed significant improvement between baseline and 6-12 months. Adverse events were mild and manageable; temporary treatment interruptions occurred, with no permanent discontinuations.
sections.Conclusion: In this real-world cohort of preschool children with CF homozygous for F508del, LUM-IVA therapy was associated with improvements in sweat chloride, pulmonary exacerbation frequency and weight over 12 months, with an acceptable safety profile. These observational findings support the feasibility of early CFTR modulator therapy.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Children's Clinical University Hospital, Pediatric Pulmonology and Allergology, Riga, Latvia, 2Rigas Stradiņš University, Pediatric, Riga, Latvia, 3Rigas Stradiņš University, Postgraduate Medical Education, Riga, Latvia

sections.Background: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a rare autosomal recessive disease caused by mutations in the CFTR gene, leading to impaired chloride transport, viscous secretions and progressive multi-organ involvement. The introduction of CFTR modulator therapy has substantially changed CF management; however, real-world data from smaller national cohorts remain limited. Aim: To evaluate the clinical effectiveness and safety of CFTR modulator therapy during the first year after initiation in patients with CF in Latvia.
sections.Methods: A prospective cohort study was conducted between August 2023 and February 2025. Patients with confirmed CF and at least one F508del mutation were included. Clinical, functional and laboratory parameters were assessed at therapy initiation and after 6 and 12 months. Outcomes included anthropometric measurements, lung function (forced expiratory volume in one second, FEV1), sweat chloride concentration, liver function tests, fat-soluble vitamins, trace elements and adverse events classified by severity.
sections.Results: Fifteen patients were included (mean age 22.7 ± 8.8 years). Mean body weight increased by 4.4 kg and body mass index by 1.3 kg/m². Sweat chloride concentration decreased by 37.7%. Mean FEV1 increased by 0.5 L, and FEV1 per cent predicted improved by 7%. No respiratory exacerbations were observed in the male subgroup. Liver function parameters remained stable and exceeded the upper limit of normal only once. Mild to moderate adverse events were reported, including tinnitus and skin rash; one severe psychiatric adverse event was recorded.
sections.Conclusion: CFTR modulator therapy was associated with clinically meaningful improvements in nutritional status, lung function and sweat chloride concentration without significant hepatic toxicity. These findings are consistent with previously published real-world studies. Larger studies are needed to evaluate long-term effectiveness and safety.

sections.Background: The triple combination elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) is one of the most effective CF therapies. Information on the efficacy and safety of its generic forms remains scarce. In Kazakhstan, triple therapy has been used in pediatric practice.
sections.Objectives: To evaluate the efficacy and safety of the generic ETI drug Trilexa® (Tutor S.A.C.I.F.I.A., Buenos Aires, Argentina) in children with CF after 12 months of treatment in real-life clinical practice.
sections.Methods: 21 patients aged 6 to 18 years receiving a generic ETI were observed. Efficacy and safety indicators were assessed every 3 months during one year.
sections.Results: Sweat conductivity before ETI therapy was equivalent to 116.7±24.3 (83-157) mmol/l NaCl, and after 12 months of therapy – 74.67±28.2 (38.0-101) mmol/l NaCl, p<0.001. Body mass index (BMI) at the start of therapy was 15.54±3.09 (10.65-22.2) kg/m2, after one year of treatment it was 17.208±3.29 (13.58-23.95) kg/m2. Improvement of respiratory function (FEV1): baseline values 78.6±31.3%, after 6 months – 84.9±21.7%, after one year 89.7±23.6%. Clinical improvement was noted, including the disappearance or reduction of cough, sputum volume, and increased exercise tolerance. Adverse events were hypertension (1), anxiety (1), increased cough (1), increased sputum production (1), elevated transaminases levels, and rash. These adverse events did not require discontinuation of the therapy.
sections.Conclusion: The efficacy and safety of generic ETI therapy were demonstrated in a pediatric clinical practice.

sections.Objectives: To present the real-life clinical practice of inpatient CF treatment in Kazakhstan and the impact of CFTR modulators on it. Materials and
sections.Methods: A retrospective analysis of the medical records of pwCF hospitalized in 2020–2025. 10 pwCF (5 men), aged 18–36 years, were hospitalized due to severe respiratory exacerbation and/or progression of respiratory failure (RF). 9 were infected with Pseudomonas aeruginosa, 9 had malnutrition, 8 were on long-term oxygen. Hospitalizations before and after initiation of therapy with generic CFTR modulators elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (Trilexa®, Tuteur S.A.C.I.F.I.A., Argentina) were analyzed.
sections.Results: Before the initiation of targeted therapy, severe respiratory exacerbations were the cause of hospitalization in 10 patients, 1 with hemoptysis. All patients had RF, their BMI was 16.72 kg/m². Patients received intravenous antibiotics, inhaled Dornase, bronchodilators, hypertonic saline, physiotherapy; PERT, all required oxygen therapy, 2 had NIV in BiPAP mode. At discharge, 6 patients required home oxygen therapy and continued oral antibiotic therapy. The average in-hospital stay in this group was 14.5 days. After initiation of CFTR modulator therapy in 2023, only five patients in this group required hospitalization. Only 2 patients still have RF, and the BMI in the group increased to 19.2 kg/m². The duration of intravenous antibacterial therapy and hospitalization was reduced to 8.5 hospital days.
sections.Conclusion: The use of CFTR modulators reduces the number and severity of CF exacerbations and decreases the need for inpatient management and its duration.

sections.Background: Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) is one of the most effective treatments for cystic fibrosis (CF). However, drug availability remains limited in many countries, leading to interruptions in therapy. Data on the clinical outcomes of these interventions remain limited. Objective. To evaluate the efficacy of ETI triple therapy in adult patients with CF in Kazakhstan in a real-world clinical practice setting.
sections.Methods: The study included 19 adult patients (7 men), with a mean age of 26 years. Patients received a generic ETI (Trilexa®, Tuteur S.A.C.I.F.I.A., Argentina). The dynamics of clinical parameters were assessed during the full dose and after therapy discontinuation.
sections.Results: Nineteen patients received the ETI at the full dose for 10–22 months. Forced therapy discontinuation due to lack of drug availability occurred in 7 of 19 patients (36.8%) within 2–4 months. After receiving the full dose, all patients experienced a regression of respiratory symptoms and an increase in exercise tolerance. BMI was 19.7 kg/m² (baseline 17.6 kg/m²), the frequency of pulmonary exacerbations decreased from 5.5±2.7 to 2.1±1.3; FEV1 from 43.5% to 61.1%; FVC — from 56.1% to 72.8%. Sweat chlorides decreased from 101.3±8.9 to 64.1±5.7 mmol/L. After complete discontinuation of therapy, all patients developed a pulmonary exacerbation within 1–2 weeks, requiring antibacterial therapy. Clinical characteristics worsened: BMI—17.9 kg/m², FEV1—49.3%, FVC—61.5%, sweat chlorides 81.8 mmol/L.
sections.Conclusion: Triple therapy with a generic ETI demonstrated high efficacy. Forced discontinuation of therapy is associated with clinical deterioration and a high risk of exacerbations. Continuity of treatment is a key factor in targeted therapy.

sections.Background: There have been reports of children with cystic fibrosis (CF) presenting with chalazions or hordeola (C/H) while receiving elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor (ETI). To gain a broader perspective and better assess the implications of ETI, we conducted a national case-control survey through the network of 47 French CF centers.
sections.Objectives: Our primary objective was to report the prevalence of C/H in people with CF (pwCF) who were treated or not with ETI. Our secondary objectives were to evaluate the association between ETI initiation timing, eye dryness, and other known C/H risk factors. Material and
sections.Methods: Physicians and pwCF constructed the questionnaires. Photographs were included to help pwCF recognize eyelid lesions. Dry eye was evaluated using the Ocular Surface Disease Index (OSDI) score. Treatment, progression, and impact on quality of life were also evaluated. QR codes were sent through French CF centers and the patient organization's social media.
sections.Results: A total of 975 questionnaires concerning pwCF treated with ETI and 119 questionnaires concerning pwCF without ETI were received from 46 centers. Seventy-five percent of the responses were from adults. C/H has occurred at least once since the initiation of ETI in 330 (34%) of the ETI group. It occurred in 14 (12%) in the control group. C/H were unilateral (50.4%) or bilateral (14%). The treated group reported a mean of 2.9 (2.3) episodes. The time to onset was longer than 6 months in two-third of cases (median 1.5 years IQR 0.4-2.6). Three pwCF stopped taking ETI, 2 suspended it, and 2 needed an ETI dosage adjustment. Main ETI-related C/H risk factors were adult female sex, eye dryness (high OSDI), rosacea, and blepharitis.
sections.Conclusion: Our large-scale study suggests the role of ETI and other risk factors in the development of C/H in pwCF. Both CF professionals and ophthalmologists must be aware of this potential adverse eyelid event in order to provide appropriate counsel.
