sections.Objectives: To assess long-term safety and efficacy of vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaftor (VTD) in people with CF aged ≥12yrs, with ≥1 F508del or an elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI)-responsive allele without F508del, and who completed the pivotal studies SKYLINE 102 or 103.
sections.Methods: Study VX20-121-104 is an ongoing phase 3, open-label extension study of VTD consisting of Parts A (96 wks) and B (48 wks); Part A is reported here. Primary endpoint is safety/tolerability; efficacy endpoints include absolute change from baseline on ETI in the parent study in ppFEV1 and sweat chloride (SwCl), proportion with SwCl <30 and <60mmol/L, and number of pulmonary exacerbations (PEx).
sections.Results: 822 participants (pts) were enrolled and dosed. 96.8% of pts had an adverse event (AE); most were mild (23.6%) or moderate (60.7%) in severity and were consistent with manifestations of CF. Rates of serious AEs and AEs leading to treatment discontinuation were low (18.21 and 2.46 events/100 patient-yrs). Event rates of rash, transaminase and creatine kinase elevations, and cataracts were consistent with the established VTD safety profile. Neuropsychiatric events/rates were consistent with those observed with background neuropsychiatric events in the CF population and those in the parent studies. Efficacy was similar regardless of treatment arm in the parent study (Table 1).
sections.Conclusion: VTD was generally safe and well tolerated, consistent with the established safety profile. VTD demonstrated sustained improvements in clinical outcomes and further restoration of CFTR function, with more pts achieving SwCl <60 and <30mmol/L compared to ETI. sections.Objectives: The increasing number of pregnancies in women with cystic fibrosis (wwCF) receiving elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor(ETI) therapy highlights the need for data on maternal safety and pregnancy outcomes. The aim of this study was to assess maternal health during ETI therapy and to evaluate neonatal outcomes.
sections.Methods: Clinical data on lung function, body mass index (BMI), gestational weight gain and antibiotic use before, during and after pregnancy were retrospectively collected from medical records of wwCF receiving ETI therapy. Neonatal outcomes were assessed using maternity records from 12 pregnancies, including gestational age, birth weight, Apgar score and mode of delivery.
sections.Results: The study included 18 wwCF with a mean age of 31 years (SD=5) at the start of pregnancy. Three wwCF (17%) required intravenous antibiotics, nine (50%) received oral antibiotics during pregnancy. Median percent predicted FEV₁ before pregnancy (n=15) was 70% (range 32–113), with a median change of -1% (range -9–4) from pre-pregnancy to pregnancy. In 16 women with available weight data, median pre-pregnancy BMI was 22kg/m² (range 18–33). The weight gain goals according to pre-pregnancy BMI were met by 5 women (31%), 9 (56%) gained less and 2 (13%) more than recommended. Neonatal outcome data were available for 12 pregnancies. The caesarean section rate was 58% (n=12). Median Apgar score at 5 minutes was 10 (range 8–10, n=12). Two (18%) of 11 births were born preterm, while nine (82%) were delivered at term with median gestational age of 38.1 weeks (range 36.4–41.1). Median birthweight percentile was 55 (range 12–100, n=11), two infants (17%) were macrosomic and none were small for gestational age.
sections.Conclusion: ETI therapy appears to be compatible with stable maternal health during pregnancy and generally favourable neonatal outcomes. Most infants were delivered at term with appropriate growth. Inter-individual differences highlight the importance of continued monitoring.

presentations.authors_text: K. Bakewell1, C. Jolley1, S.-A. Ho1, W. Carroll1,2, J. Chapman1, F. Gilchrist1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University Hospitals of North Midlands NHS Trust, North West Midlands CF Centre, Stoke on Trent, United Kingdom, 2Keele University, Faculty of Medicine and Health Sciences, Keele, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaftor (VTD) was approved for use in individuals with cystic fibrosis aged ≥6 years by the UK Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) in March 2025 and recommended for NHS use by the National Institute for Clinical excellence (NICE) in July 2025. Whilst VTD has similar clinical benefits to elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI), it has the advantage of being given once rather than twice a day.
sections.Methods: The possibility of switching from ETI to VTD was discussed with all eligible children and young people (CYP) at our centre. The CYP/parent made the final decision but were advised more strongly to switch if they had a suboptimal response to ETI. We report the number who chose to switch and the factors affecting their decision.
sections.Results: Fifty-three CYP with a mean (SD) age of 13.5 (3.7) years were eligible to switch from ETI to VTD (30 homozygous and 23 heterozygous for Phe508del). Their mean (SD) FEV1 percentile (n=52) was 94 (18) and their mean (SD) drop in sweat chloride after starting ETI (n=50) was 52 (24) mmol/L. Thirty-four (64%) have agreed to start VTD, 14 (26%) declined and 5 (9%) are as yet undecided. The commonest reasons for declining to switch were not wanting additional blood tests and being happy with their response to ETI. Of the 50 children with a sweat chloride result whilst on ETI, 5/6 (83%) whose sweat chloride was ≥60mmol/L switched to VTD, compared to 29/44 (66%) with a chloride <60mmol/L.
sections.Conclusion: The convenience of VTDs once daily administration was the major reason the majority of CYP at our centre chose to switch from ETI to VTD. Despite this, a number decided to remain on ETI due to the inconvenience or fear of additional blood tests and being happy with their response to ETI. We plan compare quality of life scores and sweat chloride results before and after switching to VTD.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Haga teaching Hospital, The Hague, Netherlands, 2Haga teaching Hospital, Pharmacy, The Hague, Netherlands, 3University Medical Center Utrecht, Pulmonology, Utrecht, Netherlands, 4Apotheek Haagse Ziekenhuizen, Pharmacy, The Hague, Netherlands, 5Haga teaching Hospital, Pulmonology, The Hague, Netherlands

sections.Background: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) therapy is used by most people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). While generally efficacious, some patients experience adverse events. This is typically managed by empirical dose reduction, but the optimal regimen remains unknown. We evaluated the pharmacokinetic (PK) and pharmacodynamic (PD) impact of ETI dose reduction using model-based simulations.
sections.Methods: Available ETI population PK models were used to simulate ETI PK under five dose reduction strategies. First, standard dose ETI PK was simulated, after which individuals were selected for dose reduction based on elexacaftor trough levels (Cmin). Subsequently, ETI exposure was translated to predicted efficacy using pharmacodynamic models constructed from available exposure-response data.
sections.Results: Demographic data of 885 pwCF were available from the Dutch CF registry. Dose reduction for pwCF showing elexacaftor Cmin >75th percentile (9075 µg/L) yielded limited PK and PD impact compared to the standard dose. Reduction to a 100/50/75 mg ETI morning dose with a 150 mg (regimen A) or 75 mg (regimen B) ivacaftor evening dose resulted in limited shifts in relative effect medians of 94.0% to 92.9% and 89.9% to 88.6% for elexacaftor and tezacaftor, and 96.2% to 95.8% and 96.2% to 95.5% for ivacaftor. This was accompanied by limited EC70-90 attainment reductions of 0%, 0.1%, and 12.5% for elexacaftor, 0.5%, 6.7% and 30.3% for tezacaftor, and 0%, 0.2% and 4.4% and 0.1%, 0.7% and 7.5% for ivacaftor with regimen A and B, respectively.
sections.Conclusion: ETI dose reduction to one morning ETI tablet (100/50/75 mg) with an evening ivacaftor dose of 75-150 mg in adult pwCF with high elexacaftor exposure is likely to maintain sufficient exposure to expect a limited impact on treatment efficacy. This regimen can be considered for managing ETI‑related adverse events in pwCF with high drug exposure, but should be evaluated in a dose reduction trial before widespread clinical implementation.

Real-world data shows that ETI...
presentations.authors_text: L. Friedman1, E. Kerem1,2, G. Hart1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1SpliSense Ltd., Jerusalem, Israel, 2CF Center, Department of Pediatrics, Hadassah Medical Center and Faculty of Medicine, Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Jerusalem, Israel

sections.Background: SPL84 is an inhaled antisense oligonucleotide (ASO) for the treatment of pwCF carrying the 3849 +10kb C->T (3849) mutation. SPL84 was shown to fully restore CFTR function in HNEs and HBEs from pwCF. In addition, SPL84 and ETI had a comparable and additive effect on HBEs from 3849/F508del pwCF. SPL84-002 is a global Phase 2a study in 3849 pwCF to evaluate the safety and preliminary efficacy of SPL84 as mono therapy. Twelve participants received 9 weekly doses of 25 or 50 mg SPL84 (n=6 each). SPL84 lead to a clinically meaningful improvement in ppFEV1 in up to ~70% of participants per cohort, with an average absolute change from baseline in ppFEV1 of 5 for both cohorts. Real-world data shows that ETI provides only moderate and variable benefit for 3849 pwCF, due to limited residual WT CFTR. Meanwhile, SPL84 corrects the underlying splicing defect and may complement ETI by restoring CFTR through a distinct MoA. Evidence of a ceiling effect for ETI further supports the need for alternative approaches. There, an additional cohort in study SPL8-002 is planned to examine the combined effect of SPL84 and CFTR modulators.
sections.Methods: Forty 3849 pwCF will participate in the SPL84-modulator combo cohort (3:1 active:placebo). Participants must be stable on ETI or VTD for at least 3 months and have ppFEV1 40-80. The study will consist of a 28-day screening period, a 28-day run-in period to confirm ppFEV1 is stable on modulators, a 9-week treatment period where participants will received 50 mg SPL84 once-weekly, and a 28-day follow up.
sections.Results: The primary objective of the study is safety. The secondary objective is preliminary efficacy, which will be evaluated by spirometry and CFQ-R RSS.
sections.Conclusion: Clinical and preclinical data demonstrates that SPL84 has the potential to provide substantial additive improvement over available CFTR modulator therapy (ETI/VTI) when given in combination to 3849 pwCF.

sections.Objectives: This study investigated the impact of standard combination chemotherapy versus single-agent therapy in metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma in cystic fibrosis patients. The aim was to compare objective response rate, toxicity, progression-free survival, and overall survival.
sections.Methods: Involved 35 CF patients with newly diagnosed metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma, maintained on CFTR modulators, bronchodilators, dornase alfa, and chest physiotherapy. 18 patients received standard chemotherapy (combination of Folinic acid, Fluorouracil, Irinotecan, Oxaliplatin), and 17 received single-agent chemotherapy (Capecitabine). TNF-α levels were measured before and after six cycles to monitor CF activity.
sections.Results: Among group A, 72% achieved disease control and 28% had progressive disease while, in group B, 43% achieved disease control and 57% had progressive disease with significant differences (P=0.04). Group A observed better PFS (median, 7 months vs. 5 months; P=0.043). Also, group A demonestrated longer OS (median, 11 months vs. 9 months; P=0.033). A significant drop in basal CA 19.9 (Carbohydrate Antigen 19-9) after sex cycles of treatment in group A (mean ±SD; 235 ±124 U/mL drop to 65±32 U/mL) versus group B (P=0.001). No significant increase in TNF-α after finishing sex cycles of chemotherapy in both treatment groups. In group A basal TNF-α was 132 ± 40 pg/mL while, after sex cycles of chemotherapy it was 140 ± 31 pg/mL with P=0.12. No significant difference observed between both groups regarding chemotherapy toxicity; neutropenia (P=0.1), chest infection (P=1.0), GI upset (P=0.3) and hepatotoxicity (P=0.2).
sections.Conclusion: This study suggests that standard combination chemotherapy, possibly mFOLFIRINOX, is a more effective treatment option for this specific patient population, offering higher disease control, improved survival, and reduced CA 19.9, with tolerable toxicity and without significantly affecting CF disease activity.

Results: 24 patients completed the six-month study; four discontinued due to non-pulmonary adverse events (gastrointestinal upset, skin rash). Importantly,...
presentations.authors_text: Y. Hussein1, A. Shabana2, Y. Sadeq3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Dakahlia INsurance Hospital, DK, Egypt, 2Mansoura University Hospital, Immunology and Rheumatology, Mansoura, Egypt, 3Mansoura University Hospital, Clinical Pathology, Mansoura, Egypt

sections.Objectives: Tolerability and toxicity of RA medications on pulmonary CF patients. Monitor the levels of CRP and serum Calprotectin for RA treatment response. Evaluate serum IL-17 levels (as a biomarker for both CF and RA).
sections.Methods: 28 CF patients with recent-onset RA. All CF patients were managed either with CFTR modulators (for those with at least one F508del mutation) or standard treatments such as Dornase alfa, bronchodilators, and chest physiotherapy. Patients received treatment with Hydroxychloroquine, either alone or in combination with Sulfasalazine.
sections.Results: 24 patients completed the six-month study; four discontinued due to non-pulmonary adverse events (gastrointestinal upset, skin rash). Importantly, no patients experienced worsening lung function attributable to the study medications. Manageable recurrent chest infection was observed in 12.5% of patients, hemoptysis in 4.1%, and pneumothorax in 8.3%; no respiratory failure was documented. Significant reductions were observed after sex months of treatment in key inflammatory markers. Mean CRP levels decreased from mean ±SD of 18.2 ± 7.3 mg/L to 8.9 ± 5.6 mg/L (over 50% reduction), correlating with improved DAS28-CRP criteria. Serum Calprotectin levels dropped from a mean ±SD of 3,630 ± 1,250 ng/mL to 1,550 ± 640 ng/mL, correlating with DAS28-CRP and ultrasound synovitis. Serum IL-17 levels significantly decreased mean ±SD; 58.6 ± 12.2 pg/mL to 24.4 ± 8.1 pg/mL, correlating with clinical improvement. A concordance of drop in these biomarkers was observed in 70.8% of evaluated patients.
sections.Conclusion: Hydroxychloroquine and Sulfasalazine are well-tolerated and demonstrate a favorable pulmonary safety profile in CF patients with co-existing RA. The observed reductions in CRP, Calprotectin, and IL-17, coupled with improvements in clinical assessments, suggest these medications are effective in managing RA activity in this complex patient population.

sections.Objectives: To evaluate the efficacy of an intensified vitamin D supplementation strategy to achieve and sustain serum vitamin D (vitD) levels >30 ng/mL in pediatric patients with cystic fibrosis (CF). Secondary objectives included assessment of bone health and evaluation of safety and tolerability in the context of optimized standard CF care beyond CFTR modulator therapies.
sections.Methods: Double-blind, randomized controlled trial including children and adolescents aged 5–18 years with CF followed at a tertiary multidisciplinary CF center. Participants with baseline serum vitD levels between 10 and 30 ng/mL were randomly assigned (1:1) to receive either an intensified supplementation regimen with a fixed initial dose of 4000 IU/day of cholecalciferol or supplementation according to Cystic Fibrosis Foundation (CFF) guidelines. Vitamin D doses were adjusted during follow-up based on serial vitD measurements across seven visits over 12 months. All other aspects of CF management followed standard multidisciplinary practice.
sections.Results: Seventeen participants completed follow-up. The intensified supplementation group achieved and sustained vitD levels >30 ng/mL more consistently than the CFF-guided group (p=0.021). A significant improvement in total body less head (TBLH) z-score from baseline to final visit was observed in the intervention group (p = 0.03). In contrast, no significant change occurred in the control group (p = 0.89). Overall, bone outcomes showed upward trends, with greater gains in the intensified group. No bone density decline or supplementation-related adverse events were observed. sections.Conclusion: Intensified vitamin D supplementation was safe and more effective than CFF-guided dosing for achieving and sustaining adequate vitD levels and was associated with improved bone health. These findings support optimized vitamin D strategies as a feasible component of pediatric CF care beyond CFTR modulator therapies.

sections.Background: Cystic fibrosis (CF) patients with pancreatic insufficiency, pathological sweat tests, and pulmonary symptoms have long been known to develop linoleic acid (LA) deficiency, documented for over 60 years. Abnormal profiles of other fatty acids, such as palmitoleic acid, Mead acid, and often docosahexaenoic acid (DHA), are also common.
sections.Objectives: This randomized, double-blind study investigated the effects of LA supplementation in children with CF. Patients aged 5–15 years, carrying class I–II mutations and classical CF symptoms, were assigned to receive either LA or oleic acid (10 g/day) for one year. All participants also received DHA (400 mg/day). Forty patients completed the study. Standard CF treatment remained unchanged, except for antibiotic therapy during infectious exacerbations. Anthropometry, biochemical analyses, pulmonary function, and exacerbation frequency were monitored. The study was approved by the local ethic committees.
sections.Methods: Clinical and biochemical assessments, along with plasma lipidomic, were performed at baseline and after 12 months. Lipidomic was conducted on total lipid extracts from 40 µL plasma using LC-MS/MS, covering >350 lipid species across six lipid classes. Each species was normalized to the total concentration of its respective class.
sections.Results: Of LA-containing lipid species, 24% increased significantly in the LA+DHA group and 4% in the DHA group. Of DHA-containing species, 54% were elevated in the control group and 22% in the LA group. In the control group significant increases (p < 0.01) were observed, most of them containing DHA. In contrast, the DHA+LA group showed both increases and decreases in various lipids ; PE species and the total amount of PE decreased.
sections.Conclusion: Supplementation of LA or LA and DHA showed unforeseen changes in the lipidomic.

sections.Background: People with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) and primary ciliary dyskinesia (pwPCD) are particularly vulnerable to the harmful effects of air pollution due to the impairment of mucociliary clearance (MCC). Despite growing evidence supporting the use of indoor air purification in common lung conditions, its role in CF and PCD remains unexplored.
sections.Methods: The Clean Air for Rare MUcociliary Clearance dIsorders (CARMUCI) study is a randomized, double-blind, cross-over, sham-controlled trial aiming to evaluate the impact of indoor air purification on respiratory health of pwCF and pwPCD (children and adults) in Cyprus. Sixty-two participants will receive two 3-month intervention periods using identical-looking air purifiers: one 3-month period with active filtration via high efficiency filters and one 3-month period with a sham unit. Primary outcome will be a mean difference of at least 10% change in lung clearance index (LCI), and secondary outcomes will include spirometry, pulmonary exacerbations, inflammatory biomarkers, and health-related quality of life. Exposure to indoor particulate matter (PM) will be continuously monitored using device operation logs and wearable activity trackers.
sections.Results: By August 2025, 24 pwCF and 26 pwPCD (20 females, age: 31 [16.1] years) have been enrolled. CARMUCI continues enrolment and started data collection in September 2025. Results are expected to provide novel evidence on the efficacy of air purification in reducing indoor PM exposure and improving respiratory outcomes in people with chronic MCC disorders.
sections.Conclusion: CARMUCI trial first results will be released in late-2027. The conclusions of this study are expected to inform future clinical guidelines and environmental health strategies for managing rare MCC disorders.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1University Children`s Hospital of Ruhr University Bochum at St. Josef-Hospital, Pediatric Pulmonology, Cystic Fibrosis Center, Bochum, Germany, 2University Hospital Essen - Ruhrlandklinik, Department of Pulmonary Medicine, Adult Cystic Fibrosis Center, Essen, Germany, 3University Hospital Essen, Pediatric Pulmonology, Pediatric Cystic Fibrosis Center, Essen, Germany

sections.Objectives: Pain is a common and often underestimated problem. Self-medication has a risk of adverse drug reaction and interactions. Most studies to date have been conducted without validated questionnaires. There is few information about pain in children and adolescents. The aim of the ongoing CF Pain study is to develop and review a standardized pain questionnaire for people with CF (pwCF) and started in 2022.
sections.Methods: We use a pain questionnaire and interview children and adolescents aged 6 years and older and caregiver at the same time or adults with CF by phone. PwCF were treated in three locations of the Christiane-Herzog-Centre Ruhr. Additionally the first 10 patients of each age group were asked for feedback regarding the questionnaire. We supplement our data with clinical data of CF.
sections.Results: Until now we included 23 adults, 16 adolescents and their caregiver and 40 children and their caregiver. It is noteworthy here that adolescents often reject the study. In children 55% were female with a median FEV1 of 91%. In adolescents 62,5% were female with a median FEV1 of 91,5%. In adults 26% were female with a median FEV1 of 90%. Asked for any pain in the last 3 months 32,5% of the children affirmed, 56% adolescents affirmed and 78% adults affirmed. The most common location of pain in children was initially abdominal pain, followed by headaches, while in adolescents and adults it was headaches, followed by abdominal pain. 50% of all pwCF took pain medication in the last 3 months; ibuprofen was the most common in 80%. 60% of pain medication was freely available for purchase. When asked about everyday limitations caused by pain, most people in all age groups reported limitations in meeting friends and participating in sports.
sections.Conclusion: Pain is important in pwCF at any age with little attention given by treatment team. A validated questionnaire is needed. Therefore, we would like to increase our numbers and also use the questionnaire on healthy subjects.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Fachklinik Satteldüne, Nebel, Germany

sections.Objectives: To investigate the relationship between body composition parameters, VO₂peak, and peak workload (Wpeak) in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF).
sections.Methods: Forty-seven pwCF (28 female/19 male; mean age 17.7 ± 10.7 years, range 10–49 years; mean ppFEV₁ 85.0 ± 17.9, range 40–114%) participated. Cycle ergometry was performed to determine VO₂peak and Wpeak. Body composition was assessed using bioelectrical impedance analysis (BIA), including fat mass (FM), fat mass index (FMI), fat-free mass (FFM), and fat-free mass index (FFMI). Data were collected during a four-week inpatient rehabilitation program.
sections.Results: Exercise capacity was VO₂peak at 78.6 ± 17.1% predicted and Wpeak at 82.7 ± 16.6% predicted. After controlling for age and lung function (FEV₁%predicted), partial correlation analyses revealed significant correlations between FFM derived parameters and exercise capacity parameters. FFM was positively correlated with absolute Wpeak (W; r = 0.420, p = 0.005) and VO₂peak (L·min⁻¹; r = 0.443, p = 0.003), while FFMI was correlated with VO₂peak (L·min⁻¹; r = 0.306, p = 0.046). No significant partial correlations were observed between FM or FMI and VO₂peak or Wpeak (all p > 0.10). VO₂peak (% predicted) and Wpeak (% predicted) were not associated with any body composition parameter after adjustment for age and FEV₁.
sections.Conclusion: Across a broad age range from childhood to adulthood, exercise capacity in pwCF was associated with fat-free mass–derived body composition parameters, whereas fat mass showed no association with VO₂peak or peak workload after adjustment for age and lung function. These findings highlight the relevance of fat-free mass alongside established pulmonary markers when interpreting exercise capacity.

presentations.authors_text: M. Uehling1, J. Emery2, J. Lowers2, D. Kavalieratos2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Emory University, Department of Sociology, Atlanta, United States, 2Emory University, Department of Family and Preventive Medicine, Atlanta, United States

sections.Objectives: Many people with cystic fibrosis have significant unmet physical, emotional, and care planning needs. Prior work has indicated that palliative care may improve a range of outcomes for people with CF. The present RCT compared the addition of specialty palliative care to outpatient CF clinic care vs usual care at five North American sites. Participants received four outpatient telehealth palliative care visits over 12 months, with visits focusing on needs assessment, symptom management, and advanced care planning.
sections.Methods: We conducted semi-structured interviews with 48 participants. Interview questions centered on general experiences in the study, perceptions of the role of palliative care, and their relationships with the palliative care clinician compared to usual CF treatment.
sections.Results: Participants generally reported experiencing significant benefit from outpatient palliative care, specifically in the domains of care coordination, emotional and psychological stress, advanced care planning, and physical symptom burden. Participants varied significantly in which domains they experienced the greatest benefit. Many participants noted that a strong relationship with their clinician had a positive impact on their overall experience with palliative care, regardless of symptom burden. Some participants furthermore noted that the palliative care clinician was able to address long-standing or previously unaddressed symptoms or challenges of their illness, due to the open-ended nature of the visits, the less time-constrained structure of the conversations, and the close relationships with the clinicians. Finally, the vast majority of participants indicated they would recommend palliative care to other CF patients after their experiences in the study.
sections.Conclusion: People with CF found adjunctive specialist palliative care helpful for addressing ongoing symptoms and other challenges of CF not addressed in usual care.

presentations.authors_text: C. Saunders1,2, R. Dobra2,1, M. Abkir1,2, J. Davies2,1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Royal Brompton & Harefield Hospitals, Part of Guys and St Thomas’ Trust, Cystic Fibrosis, London, United Kingdom, 2National Heart and Lung Institute, Imperial College London, Cf&CLI, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Research in infants with CF (iwCF) is imperative to understand pathophysiology in the early months and to ensure safe, effective treatments can be started early. There are several observational and interventional studies active in iwCF, however infant research poses practical and ethical challenges. We aimed to understand the experiences of research active healthcare professionals (HCPs) to support recruitment of iwCF to studies.
sections.Methods: An anonymous survey was distributed via Qualtrics to UK-based HCPs with experience recruiting iwCF to research Apr-May ‘25. Closed & open-ended questions were organised around five themes.
sections.Results: Respondents (n=23) highlighted the importance of navigating recruitment of iwCF appropriately but flagged that training rarely focusses on skills needed to recruit infants/families. Respondents shared facilitators/barriers to recruiting iwCF. Research participation can provide families with hope following a CF diagnosis. Comprehensive knowledge and strong communication between clinical/care teams, allowing adequate time to consider participation, approaching families at a suitable time, accommodating families’ needs & an environment in which research is valued were highlighted as key to successful recruitment of iwCF. Respondents reported that these can be supported by staff training & effective collaboration between research/care teams. Complex studies, language barriers & poor principal investigator engagement were identified as hindering recruitment, representing key, measurable targets for improvement.
sections.Conclusion: Studies in iwCF are critical to optimise care. Ways to support recruitment may include developing training specific to recruiting/consenting iwCF and their families. Increasing awareness of barriers to participation may reveal tangible strategies to overcome such challenges. Characterising the team dynamics associated with successful recruitment of iwCF to research may guide others to optimise interactions.

presentations.authors_text: W. Shin1,2, M. Ursino1, D. Muilwijk3, C.K. van der Ent3, I. Huys2, S. Zohar1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Université Paris Cité, Inserm, Inria, UMRS1346, HeKA, Paris, France, 2KU Leuven, Department of Pharmaceutical and Pharmacological Sciences, Leuven, Belgium, 3University Medical Center Utrecht, Wilhelmina Children's Hospital, Department of Pediatric Pulmonology, Utrecht, Netherlands

sections.Objectives: Since its introduction in the ICH E9 (R1) guideline, estimands have been considered an important element in designing clinical trials. While the use of classical and well-known estimands is straightforward, the building of new ones lacks detailed methodological guidance, and this may be attributable to the limited usage of the estimand framework in more complex clinical trials. In our study, we propose a framework for constructing estimands using expert elicitation and Bayesian statistical models.
sections.Methods: We designed a two-stage elicitation process to obtain expert opinions on organoids and on estimand attributes under prespecified scenarios. Using the data, we applied a series of hierarchical joint Bayesian statistical models to identify appropriate estimand attributes that showed the highest relative rankings.
sections.Results: Ten individuals from the ORGESTRA consortium provided opinions, primarily regarding organoids, for designing four clinical trial scenarios during the first-stage elicitation. Next, 39 participants, recruited mainly from the European cystic fibrosis networks, contributed expert input on estimand attributes. Across the four scenarios, FEV1, LCI (lung clearance index), and pulmonary exacerbations were selected, either individually or in combinations of two, based on their rankings. Discontinuation of treatment due to lack of improvement was identified as the most likely, and death as the least likely, intercurrent event in all the four scenarios. Full estimand attributes were constructed based on the selected endpoints and intercurrent events.
sections.Conclusion: This study demonstrates a framework for establishing clinical trial estimands using Bayesian expert elicitation. With this framework, we intend to support future clinical studies involving novel cystic fibrosis treatments and innovative biological platforms, such as organoids.

presentations.authors_text: M. Ferreira Duarte1, M. Lima1, C. Constant2, L. Pereira2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Hospital de Santa Maria, Unidade Local de Saúde de Santa Maria, Serviço Pediatria Médica, Departamento Pediatria, Lisboa, Portugal, 2Hospital de Santa Maria, Unidade Local de Saúde de Santa Maria, Unidade de Pneumologia Pediátrica, Serviço Pediatria Médica, Departamento Pediatria, Lisboa, Portugal

sections.Background: The introduction of highly effective cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) modulators has shifted the treatment paradigm of cystic fibrosis (CF). Reducing treatment burden is now a priority for patients/caregivers and healthcare professionals.However, guidance for CF therapy stepdown is not standardized. We aimed to evaluate the impact of dornase alfa (DA) discontinuation on pulmonary function, quality of life (QoL), and the frequency of respiratory exacerbations in clinically stable paediatric CF patients receiving CFTR modulators.
sections.Methods: Longitudinal observational study conducted at a CF reference centre. Fifty-four children with CF were included; 29 clinically stable patients discontinued DA. Spirometry was assessed before and after discontinuation, with a minimum follow-up of six months. QoL was evaluated using the CF Questionnaire-Revised (CFQ-R). Respiratory exacerbations requiring antibiotic therapy were analysed before and after DA discontinuation using intra-individual analysis. Descriptive analysis was done.
sections.Results: DA discontinuation was not associated with clinically significant pulmonary function decline. Mean ppFEV₁, ppFVC, and FEV₁/FVC values were similar before and after discontinuation (change in ppFEV1 −0.34 %; in ppFVC −2.14% and FEV₁/FVC +0.006). QoL scores remained stable, with no significant pre- and post-discontinuation differences. In the three years prior to discontinuation, patients experienced a mean of three respiratory exacerbations per year. After discontinuation, five patients experienced a single exacerbation requiring antibiotic therapy, with no hospitalizations recorded.
sections.Conclusion: In clinically stable CF children receiving highly effective CFTR modulators, DA discontinuation appears to be a safe short- to mid-term strategy, with no relevant negative impact on pulmonary function or QoL . This encourages a more personalized approach to disease management and treatment burden reduction.

sections.Background: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) leads to rapid improvements in lung function in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). However, real-world data on the long-term trajectory and durability of lung function response beyond the first year remain limited.
sections.Objectives: To evaluate the longitudinal trajectory of percent predicted forced expiratory volume in one second (ppFEV1) up to 36 months after ETI initiation in routine clinical practice.
sections.Methods: We conducted a retrospective observational study including pwCF who initiated ETI at a single CF center and completed at least 12 months of treatment. Lung function was assessed at baseline and at 12, 18, 24, and 36 months. Changes in ppFEV1 were analyzed using paired comparisons with an available-case approach, reporting the number of patients contributing data at each timepoint. Secondary outcomes included changes in sweat chloride concentration, hospitalizations, oxygen therapy use, and lung transplant listing at 12 months.
sections.Results: Thirty-nine pwCF initiated ETI (mean age 31.4 ± 11.7 years; baseline ppFEV1 51.5 ± 22.7%). At 12 months (n=37), mean ppFEV1 increased by 11.4 percentage points (p<0.001). Among patients with available follow-up, ppFEV1 remained higher than baseline at 18 months (mean change +12.8; n=22; p<0.001), 24 months (+13.2; n=19; p<0.001), and 36 months (+12.5; n=11; p<0.001). The improvement observed at 12 months was maintained over time among patients with continued follow-up. At 12 months, significant reductions were observed in hospitalizations, oxygen therapy use, and active lung transplant listing (all p<0.05). Sweat chloride concentration also decreased significantly.
sections.Conclusion: In this real-world cohort, ETI was associated with sustained improvement in lung function for up to 36 months. These findings support the long-term durability of ETI benefits beyond the first year of treatment in routine clinical practice.

sections.Background: Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor /Ivacaftor (ETI), a Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Conductance Regulator modulator therapy for Cystic Fibrosis (CF), gained European authorisation in August 2020 and was available on the National Health Service in England shortly after. At the Leicester Adult CF Unit our first patients commenced ETI in March 2020 on a managed access scheme. Here we present data from our centre on Forced Expiratory Volume in 1 Second (FEV₁) for patients who commenced ETI in 2020. Inclusion: 72 modulator naïve patients commenced ETI in 2020. All values are presented as change in FEV₁ % predicted and peak is defined as the highest FEV₁ % predicted.
sections.Results: Mean change from baseline to peak FEV₁ % predicted was +20.36% (range +58 to -5). 1 patient had decreased lung function, 3 no change (defined as range +1 to -1 %) and 68 increased. Mean change in FEV₁ % predicted from peak to latest was -7.34 (range +1 to -29), 61 lost lung function and 11 remained stable. Mean change in FEV₁ % predicted from starting ETI to latest was +13.29% (range +51 to -9). 63 patients remained higher than baseline FEV₁, 2 were stable and 7 were lower than baseline. Discussion: In our experience, most patients treated with ETI gained an initial significant increase in lung function however the majority (84.7%) also experienced a drop from their peak. Some patients were back to baseline and 9.7% of patients had a lower FEV₁ than their baseline. The cause for this loss of FEV₁ is likely to be multifactorial and individual. The natural disease course for patients with lower lung function and decreased CF treatment adherence, including with ETI, are possible contributing factors. Limitations: This is data from one centre. Spirometry was obtained in clinic, not in a controlled research environment. The patient numbers are small, but represent our whole modulator naïve cohort who started ETI in 2020. The cause for the loss of FEV₁ needs further investigation.

The aim of this study was to evaluate correlations between FOT parameters and conventional spirometric and plethysmographic indices in adults with CF. sections.Objectives: Lung disease monitoring in cystic fibrosis (CF) relies on spirometry and body plethysmography. In the era of highly effective CFTR modulator therapy (HEMT), lung function is often preserved, increasing the need for sensitive non-invasive methods to detect subtle airway abnormalities. The forced oscillation technique (FOT) enables assessment of respiratory mechanics during tidal breathing. The aim of this study was to evaluate correlations between FOT parameters and conventional spirometric and plethysmographic indices in adults with CF.
sections.Methods: Fifty-five adult CF patients receiving HEMT were included. All participants underwent spirometry, body plethysmography and FOT. FOT parameters included respiratory resistance (Rrs), reactance (Xrs), resonant frequency (Fres) and area of reactance (AX). Statistical analysis was performed using Spearman rank correlation (JASP, version 0.95.2.0); p < 0.05 was considered significant.
sections.Results: The median age was 26.0 years; 56.4% were female, 76.3% were homozygous for F508del, and 62% had normal spirometric lung function. FOT parameters showed significant correlations with airflow obstruction and air trapping. Rinsp and Rexp at 5 Hz, X5insp and AX correlated negatively with FEV₁/FVC (r = −0.57, −0.51, −0.44 and −0.51, respectively; all p < 0.001). Reactance-based parameters (AX and Fres) correlated positively with RV/TLC (r up to 0.57, p < 0.001). Correlations with MMEF were less consistent, while correlations with PEF were weak or absent. Significant correlations with FEV₁ were also observed (r up to −0.59, p < 0.001), although associations were stronger with FEV₁/FVC and RV/TLC.
sections.Conclusion: In adults with CF receiving HEMT, FOT provides a rapid, non-invasive assessment of airway mechanics and may serve as a useful complementary tool for routine lung disease monitoring when conventional spirometric measures are preserved.

sections.Background: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has proven efficacy in clinical trials in individuals with cystic fibrosis (CF), including the paediatric population. However, real-world data in young children is limited, and the consistency of outcomes with older age groups remains to be established.
sections.Objectives: This post-approval, retrospective, single-centre study aimed to assess the effects of ETI on CFTR biomarkers and lung function over 6 months of clinical use in Polish paediatric patients.
sections.Methods: In CF children aged 2–12 years with at least one F508del allele who initiated ETI therapy between May and November 2025, demographic and clinical characteristics were analysed, including changes from baseline in sweat chloride (SwCl) concentration and lung clearance index 2.5 (LCI2.5) through 6 months.
sections.Results: Among 128 patients receiving ETI therapy (mean age 7.3 ± 2.8 years), 61 patients (mean age 8.75 ± 2.4 years; 32 females; F508del homozygous/heterozygous: 28/33 ) with diagnostic-quality multiple-breath nitrogen washout and SwCl measurements were included. Mean LCI₂.₅ decreased significantly from baseline (7.98 ± 1.80) to 3 months (6.94 ± 1.20) and remained significantly lower at 6 months (7.04 ± 1.32; p < 0.001 vs baseline for both time points), with no significant difference between 3 and 6 months (p = 0.054). The mean change in LCI₂.₅ was −1.20 at 3 months and −0.89 at 6 months. SwCl concentration decreased significantly after 6 months of therapy (−33.3 mmol/L). No correlation was found between LCI₂.₅ and SwCl changes, and treatment response did not differ by genotype or sex.
sections.Conclusion: In a real-world setting, ETI therapy in young children led to clinically meaningful reductions in SwCl concentration and LCI2.5 within 3 months, which were sustained at 6 months, indicating rapid improvements in lung function and CFTR biomarker response. These findings are consistent with clinical trial data and observations in the older population.

sections.Objectives: To evaluate the clinical response to elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor(ETI)in pediatric cystic fibrosis(CF)patients and to identify factors associated with limited or no treatment response in a real-world setting.
sections.Methods: This retrospective study analyzed 90pediatric CFpatients treated with ETI for ≥3months. Genotype,sweat chloride(SwCl),pulmonary function(FEV₁),and anthropometric parameters were evaluated. Patients were classified as “responsive/responsive”(R/R) or “responsive/unresponsive”(R/U) based on ETI eligibility.Treatment response was evaluated according to the Canadian Clinical Consensus Guideline for CFTR Modulator Therapies.
sections.Results: The mean age was 13.30±4.48 years.At baseline,mean SwCl was89.22 ± 20.65mmol/L and mean FEV₁ was88.00±24.66%.After 3 months,SwCl significantly decreased to 44.04±20.65mmol/L,while mean FEV₁ increased to100.75 ± 21.63%.Female patients had lower 3-month SwCl levels(p = 0.007)and greater SwCl decline rates(p = 0.045).The absence of P. aeruginosa(Pa)was associated with a sufficient increase in FEV₁(p = 0.046),but not with SwCl reduction(p = 0.716).No significant difference was observed between R/R and R/U genotypes.Insufficient SwCl reduction was observed in 13patients.Among these,one patient carried the N1303K mutation and another carried the 2789+5G>A mutation.5 patients had the F508del homozygous mutation,and the L467F homozygous resistance gene was detected in 3patients,including siblings.A significant FEV₁increase was not observed in 20 patients,8 of whom were F508del homozygous.Among patients unresponsive in both SwCl and FEV₁,4 showed no adequate response in either parameter.
sections.Conclusion: ETI therapy provides significant clinical benefits.Female gender and absence of Pa colonization were associated with better response. ETI response is closely related to genetic background. As this study evaluated early treatment response, patients classified as non-responders may show treatment response in long-term follow-up.

sections.Objectives: To evaluate the respiratory impact of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) treatment in people with Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF) aged 2 to 5 years. To describe baseline demographic and clinical characteristics.
sections.Methods: single-center, prospective observational study including pwCF initiating ETI, with a year follow-up. Demographic, clinical, and microbiological data were collected. Respiratory cultures, pulmonary exacerbations (PEx), and antibiotic courses during the first year of ETI therapy were compared to the previous year. Statistical analysis was performed using SPSS software.
sections.Results: Data were obtained from 18 pwCF, with a median age of 3 years (IQR 2–4), 66.7% male. All were diagnosed via newborn screening; 27.8% were F508del homozygous. Pancreatic insufficiency was present in 88.9%; none had CF-related diabetes or liver disease. Previously, 22% had received a CFTR modulator. Lung function data were available for only 7 patients (median FEV1 0.93; IQR - 0.90–2.38). Oxygen saturation remained normal during the follow-up period. Regarding PEx, a median of 2­ exacerbations per year (IQR 1–1.25) was recorded, with no significant differences before ETI (p>0.05). No significant modifications were observed in hospital admissions or oral antibiotic cycles (1, IQR 0-2), p>0.05. Sweat test (chloride concentration) showed a median of 31.5 (IQR: 15.5–38) with statistically significant results (p<0.05). Respiratory cultures showed microbiological changes, with increased Haemophilus influenzae and reduced Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
sections.Conclusion: In this cohort, ETI did not significantly alter the number of pulmonary exacerbations, antibiotic requirements, or admissions compared to the previous year. A microbiological shift was observed, notably a reduction in P. aeruginosa. Larger studies with longer follow-up are necessary to confirm these results. Table 1. sections.Objectives: To describe long-term clinical outcomes and safety of ETI in a Colombian cohort of patients with CF.
sections.Methods: We conducted a descriptive longitudinal study including patients with CF followed at a CF center (Hospital Infantil Universitario de San José in Bogotá) who completed 24 months of ETI treatment. Anthropometric measures and lung function were assessed quarterly, and liver function tests semiannually. Clinical data were collected during each visit of the cystic fibrosis clinic. Longitudinal changes were analyzed using mixed-effects models.
sections.Results: Thirty-one patients were included; 70% were female, mean age at ETI initiation was 16.6 (±7.5) years, and 51.6% were <18 years. Pancreatic insufficiency was present in 93% of patients, cystic fibrosis–related diabetes in 25%, and chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection in 35%. Median ppFEV₁ improved from 51% (IQR 37–76) at baseline to 70% (IQR 54–93) at 24 months. A significant improvement in FEV₁ was observed from 3 months onward and remained sustained through 24 months (+15.7 percentage points; 95% CI 11.8–19.5; p<0.001). Improvements were greater in patients <18 years (+17.9% at 3 months and +18.6% at 24 months) compared with adults, who also showed significant improvement. Body mass index increased from 16.10 (IQR 14.60–17.75) to 18.85 (IQR 16.45–21.25) at 24 months, with an adjusted mean change of +2.28 (95% CI 1.79–2.77; p<0.001). Handgrip strength improved from 15.6 (IQR 12.03–21.8) to 18.45 (IQR 13.75–29.3), with an adjusted increase of +3.83 (95% CI 2.20–5.46; p<0.001). Liver enzymes remained stable; mild increases in bilirubin were observed without clinical relevance.
sections.Conclusion: In this real-world Colombian cohort, ETI was associated with sustained improvements in pulmonary function, nutritional status, and muscle strength over two years, with an acceptable safety profile, despite challenges in treatment access and continuity.

sections.Background: Advanced cystic fibrosis lung disease (ACFLD) remains a major cause of morbidity and mortality among young people with cystic fibrosis (CF), despite highly effective CFTR modulators.
sections.Objectives: To study the eficacy and safety of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) in ACFLD.
sections.Methods: Retrospective observational study including patients with ACFLD followed at the CF Reference Centre of Hospital de Santa Maria between March 2021 and December 2025 on treatment with ETI for at least 36 months.
sections.Results: Of 78 adult patients followed at the centre, 11 were enrolled in the study. 54.5% were female; 72.7% were aged 18–39 years. Median age at initiation of ETI was 31 years. All patients carried at least one F508del mutation; two patients had prior exposure to Lumacaftor/Ivacaftor. At baseline median FEV₁ was 0.95 L/35% of predicted. Long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT) was required in 36.4% of patients and non-invasive ventilation (NIV) in 9.1%. Prior to ETI initiation, hemoptysis was present in 36.4% of patients and recurrent pneumothorax in 18.2%. After ETI initiation, lung function improved early. Median absolute FEV₁ change from baseline was +0.16 L at 3 months, +0.27 L at 6 months, peaking at +0.85 L at 9 months, +0.32 L at 12 months and maintained at 36 months (+0.32 L). Median sweat chloride decreased from 78 mmol/L pre-ETI to 32.5 mmol/L at 6 months. Nutritional status improved, with median BMI increasing from 21.1 kg/m² pre-ETI to 23.0 kg/m² at 12 months. Clinical improvement allowed discontinuation of inhaled antibiotic therapy in 36.4% of patients. Hemoptysis persisted in only one patient and no new pneumothoraces were recorded. One patient stopped LTOT, one patient underwent lung transplantation and one death occurred.
sections.Conclusion: ETI was associated with early functional, biological and nutritional improvements followed by sustained stabilisation, supporting its use in ACFLD and potentially preventing the need for lung transplantation.

This has previously been documented in a retrospective review in Germany, but not with prospectively collected data. Methods: SNOT-22 scores were recorded prospectively during annual reviews of adult patients with cystic fibrosis. We analysed these retrospectively, comparing the mean scores from 2018-2019 (pre ETI) and 2021-2025 (post ETI), using paired t-test for normally distributed paired data, Wilcoxon signe...

sections.Objectives: We tested the hypothesis that SNOT-22 score would improve after the introduction of elexecaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI). This has previously been documented in a retrospective review in Germany, but not with prospectively collected data.
sections.Methods: SNOT-22 scores were recorded prospectively during annual reviews of adult patients with cystic fibrosis. We analysed these retrospectively, comparing the mean scores from 2018-2019 (pre ETI) and 2021-2025 (post ETI), using paired t-test for normally distributed paired data, Wilcoxon signed rank test for non-parametric paired data, and Mann-Whitney U test for non-paired non-parametric data.
sections.Results: Of 72 patients in our centre with SNOT-22 scores, 31 patients had scores before and after the introduction of Kaftrio (mean age 39.3, SD 11.7, 74% male). Median score was 13.0 before ETI and 5.0 after (Wilcoxon signed-rank test, p=0.019). During the study period, 12 had at least one elevated total IgE (mean SNOT-22 score 16.5) and 19 had never had an elevated total IgE (mean SNOT-22 score 12.7), Mann-Whitney U-test p=0.027. No statistically significant improvement in score was seen when comparing pre-ETI and post-ETI within each of those groups.
sections.Conclusion: SNOT-22 scores improved after the introduction of ETI therapy, though not to the degree expected. Scores were higher in atopic than non-atopic individuals.

sections.Objectives: The aim of this study was to evaluate the effects of the highly effective modulator treatment (HEMT) on lipid profiles in children with cystic fibrosis (CF).
sections.Methods: This retrospective cohort study includes 29 children with CF who have received HEMT for at least 1 year. Clinical, anthropometric, spirometric, and lipid parameters were compared at baseline and after one year of treatment.
sections.Results: Descriptive characteristics of the patients are given in Table 1. After one year, BMI increased significantly from 15.7 to 16.2 kg/m² (p = 0.043), whereas the BMI Z-score remained stable (p = 0.259). Pulmonary function improved significantly: forced expiratory volume in 1 second (FEV1) lt, FEV1%, and FEV1 Z-score all increased (p < 0.05 for all), as did forced vital capacity (FVC) (L) and FVC Z-score (p < 0.001), while FVC % showed no significant difference (Table 2). Among lipid parameters, only high-density lipoprotein (HDL) increased significantly, from 42 to 46 mg/dL (p = 0.044); low-density lipoprotein (LDL), very-low-density lipoprotein (VLDL), total cholesterol, and triglycerides did not change significantly. Changes in lipid parameters did not differ between children with and without BMI increase.
sections.Conclusion: CFTR modulator therapy in children leads to significant improvements in lung function and nutritional status without inducing an atherogenic lipid profile. The selective rise in HDL, together with stable LDL and triglycerides, suggests a metabolically favorable effect of CFTR modulation that is independent of BMI gain. Table 1. presentations.title: Development and validation of a human scalp hair analysis for determining long-term exposure to CFTR modulators
presentations.summary: Objectives: Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Conductance regulator (CFTR) modulators (elexacaftor (ELX), tezacaftor (TEZ), ivacaftor (IVA), lumacaftor (LUM) are the cornerstone of CF’s pharmacologic treatment. While adherence to CFTR modulators is critical for effectiveness, a reliable noninvasive method for determining long-term medication adherence is lacking. Direct measurements such as plasma only reflect a snapshot of last day’s adherence and are invasive to perform. Therefore, we aimed to develop and validate a method for quantification of CFTR modulators in human scalp hair. Methods: LC-MSMS was used for quantification. For method development scalp hair from people with CF (pwCF) using CFTR modulators was...
presentations.authors_text: C.M. Hansen1,2, A.M.A Wessels1, P.M. van den Bemt1, J.F. van Boven1,2, D.J. Touw1,2,3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Groningen, University Medical Center Groningen, Clinical Pharmacy and Pharmacology, Groningen, Netherlands, 2Medication Adherence Expertise Center of the northern Netherlands (MAECON), Groningen, Netherlands, 3University of Groningen, Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, Groningen Research Institute of Pharmacy (GRIP), Groningen, Netherlands

sections.Objectives: Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Conductance regulator (CFTR) modulators (elexacaftor (ELX), tezacaftor (TEZ), ivacaftor (IVA), lumacaftor (LUM) are the cornerstone of CF’s pharmacologic treatment. While adherence to CFTR modulators is critical for effectiveness, a reliable noninvasive method for determining long-term medication adherence is lacking. Direct measurements such as plasma only reflect a snapshot of last day’s adherence and are invasive to perform. Therefore, we aimed to develop and validate a method for quantification of CFTR modulators in human scalp hair.
sections.Methods: LC-MSMS was used for quantification. For method development scalp hair from people with CF (pwCF) using CFTR modulators was used. Blanc hair for quality control (QC) and calibration samples was donated by healthy volunteers. Solvents to remove external contamination, extraction and extraction times were tested. After method optimization, validation was carried out following guidelines from FDA, EMA and Society of Hair testing (SoHT).
sections.Results: Isopropanol showed to be the best washing solvent, with methanol being the most optimal extraction solvent. Optimization of the extraction time resulted in a 90-minute extraction time using ball mill. Selectivity of the developed method was confirmed for IVA, its metabolite IVA-M1, ELX, LUM and TEZ. For linearity, all calibration curves met the acceptance criteria of bias less than 15%. Accuracies of the QC samples for IVA, IVA-M1 and LUM did not comply with FDA/EMA criteria of being between 85% and 115%. ELX and TEZA accuracies were between 86.2% and 98.5% and complied. Precision for ELX and TEZ (LLOQ, LOW, MEDIUM and HIGH) ranged between 1.0-7.0% and 0.0-2.7% (intra cv’s), and 3.4-6.6% and 2.7-7.4% (inter cv’s).
sections.Conclusion: A hair analysis method for CFTR modulators was developed and validated, demonstrating that elexacaftor and tezacaftor can be reliably quantified in human hair.

presentations.title: The “last families” in cystic fibrosis: equity, non-responders, and the translational imperative
presentations.summary: Objectives: Highly effective CFTR modulators have transformed outcomes for approximately 90% of patients in the United States. Individuals with Class I mutations, disproportionately Hispanic and Black, derive no benefit from these therapies and continue to experience the full burden of disease. These “last families,” identified from birth as non-responders, represent a growing equity and translational gap. Methods: Through a CFF Research Development Program, our rural CF center partnered with Cedars-Sinai to implement a patient-derived nasal epithelial “nose-on-a-chip” platform for individualized drug testing. We conducted a longitudinal clinical and social evaluation of a 19-year-old Hispanic male with a Clas...
presentations.authors_text: C. Landon1, P. Lyanage2, J. Lee2, K. Arora2, A. Naren2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Ventura County Medical Center, Pedistrics, Ventura, United States, 2Cedars Sinai, Los Angeles, United States

sections.Objectives: Highly effective CFTR modulators have transformed outcomes for approximately 90% of patients in the United States. Individuals with Class I mutations, disproportionately Hispanic and Black, derive no benefit from these therapies and continue to experience the full burden of disease. These “last families,” identified from birth as non-responders, represent a growing equity and translational gap.
sections.Methods: Through a CFF Research Development Program, our rural CF center partnered with Cedars-Sinai to implement a patient-derived nasal epithelial “nose-on-a-chip” platform for individualized drug testing. We conducted a longitudinal clinical and social evaluation of a 19-year-old Hispanic male with a Class I R75X/R75X genotype. Multidisciplinary interventions included digital adherence monitoring, pharmacy surveillance, home visits, and intensive social work engagement. Social vulnerability was assessed using the Childhood Opportunity Index and Area Deprivation Index.
sections.Results: Engagement with conventional care was delayed due to prior sibling mortality, reliance on traditional healing practices, and immigration-related concerns. Social vulnerability indices demonstrated high structural disadvantage. With culturally concordant support, the family consented to nasal epithelial sampling. In vitro testing of a five-drug combination demonstrated restoration of CFTR protein production not achievable with approved modulator therapies.
sections.Conclusion: This case highlights the emerging population of CF non-responders who remain excluded from therapeutic advances and are disproportionately affected by structural inequities. Equity-centered translational platforms and culturally responsive engagement strategies are essential. Registry Implications: CF registries should stratify patients by therapeutic responsiveness, incorporate social vulnerability indices, and track participation in individualized translational platforms to prevent systematic underrepresentation of unmet needs.

presentations.summary: Aim: To assess the dynamics of infection and survival of adult patients with cystic fibrosis (CF) in groups of patients with chronic respiratory tract infection with Pseudomonas aeruginosa(Pa) and Burkholderia cepacia complex(Bcc); to compare the group of Pa and Bcc by age, age of diagnosis, genotype, anthropometric parameters, spirometry, frequency of major complications of CF, volume of the received therapy. Methods: To analyse survival, we compared groups of adult patients with chronic Рa/Bсс infection (852 patients in total, 188 of them with Bсс) observed from November 1992 to December 2025. Survival was assessed using the Kaplan-Meier curve. A one-time analysis of Рa/Всс groups was conducted in December 2...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Federal State Budgetary Institution “Pulmonology Scientific Research Institute” under Federal Medical and Biological Agency of Russia, Moscow, Russian Federation, Cystic Fibrosis Laboratory, Moscow, Russian Federation, 2Federal State Budgetary Institution “Pulmonology Scientific Research Institute” under Federal Medical and Biological Agency of Russia, Moscow, Russian Federation, Pulmonology Department, Moscow, Russian Federation

sections.Methods: To analyse survival, we compared groups of adult patients with chronic Рa/Bсс infection (852 patients in total, 188 of them with Bсс) observed from November 1992 to December 2025. Survival was assessed using the Kaplan-Meier curve. A one-time analysis of Рa/Всс groups was conducted in December 2025 (a total of 862 patients, 89 of them with Всс).
sections.Results: Тhe median survival for the Pa/Bcc was 28,1 [95%CI: 25,21-30,98] and 12,6 years [95%CI: 10,95-14,32](the differences are significant: LogRank Test: χ2 = 97,7, p<0,001). The proportion of patients who survived the period of 3,5,7,10, and 15 years for the Ра/Bсс groups: 98,5% and 89,1%, 95,2% and 82,3%, 92,4% and 77,2%, 82,1% and 59,0%, and 72,8% and 24,6%, respectively. A one-time analysis show no significant differences between the Pa/Bcc groups. In the Всс group, the age of diagnosis was significantly lower: 1,2 years against 4,3 years in the Pa group (p=0,001). The Bcc group is also characterized by a lower prevalence of gastroesophageal reflux disease (p=0,053) and a significantly higher percentage of patients taking ursodeoxycholic acid(UDCA)(p=0,004).
sections.Conclusion: Bcc infection significantly reduces the life expectancy of patients with CF. When comparing groups of patients with Pa/Bcc infection, no significant differences were found in the main parameters of the severity of the disease, however, the Bcc group is characterized by an earlier diagnosis and a high percentage of patients taking UDCA.

Methods: We conducted a prospective, observational study including 56 people with CF (pwCF), aged 8–41 years. Peripheral blood neutrophil counts were measured at baseline and after 12 months of ETI ther...
presentations.authors_text: V. Cvejoska Cholakovska1,2, E. Krsteska1, A. Andreevska Stepanovska1, L. Spirevska1, A. Jovanovska3,2, S. Fushtikj Naceva1,2

Cyril and Methodius University in Skopje, Skopje, North Macedonia, The Republic of, 3University Children's Clinic, Department of Haematology and Oncology, Skopje, North Macedonia, The Republic of

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is characterized by persistent neutrophil-driven inflammation, which plays a central role in progressive lung disease and systemic inflammatory burden. While the triple cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) modulator therapy elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has significantly improved lung function and clinical outcomes, its effects on circulating inflammatory markers, particularly peripheral blood neutrophil counts, remain insufficiently characterized.
sections.Methods: We conducted a prospective, observational study including 56 people with CF (pwCF), aged 8–41 years. Peripheral blood neutrophil counts were measured at baseline and after 12 months of ETI therapy. As data were non-normally distributed, paired comparisons were performed using the Wilcoxon signed-rank test. Effect size was estimated using rank-biserial correlation. A responder analysis was conducted based on relative neutrophil reduction, and associations between baseline neutrophil counts and treatment-related changes were assessed using Spearman rank correlation.
sections.Results: The median absolute neutrophil count decreased from approximately 5.6 ×10⁹/L at baseline to 3.4 ×10⁹/L after 12 months of therapy, representing a 40–45% reduction. The median absolute change was −2.2 ×10⁹/L, with a large effect size (r ≈ 0.6). The reduction was statistically significant (p < 0.001) and observed in more than 90% of patients; approximately 80% achieved a clinically relevant reduction (≥20%). Comparable reductions were observed across genotypes and in both sexes. Baseline neutrophil counts were moderately correlated with the magnitude of reduction (ρ ≈ 0.5, p < 0.01). Neutrophil counts remained within normal reference ranges.
sections.Conclusion: ETI therapy is associated with significant and sustained reductions in circulating neutrophils, a marker of systemic inflammation, supporting its systemic anti-inflammatory effect.

sections.Methods: The analysis included 188 adult patients with Bcc, who were observed at the CF center from November 1992 to December 2025. The onset of infection was considered the time when Bcc appeared in a sputum culture, followed by chronic infection. Survival was assessed using the Kaplan-Meier curve. A one-time analysis was performed in 89 patients with Bcc infection as of December 2025.
sections.Results: As a result of intraspecific typing by molecular genetic methods, it was shown that the main type of Bcc in the Moscow CF Center is B.cenocepacia- 84%, followed by B.multivorans- 7%, B.gladioli- 3%, B.contaminans- 3% and B.stabilis- 2%. At the same time, B.cenocepacia is represented by hospital genotypes ST709- 85% and ST208-14%(ST-sequence type). The median survival from the moment of infection with genotypes ST709 + ST208/other ST was 11,9 years [95% CI: 10,6-13,2] and 15,8 years [95% CI: 14,8-16,8] (p=0,5). A one-time analysis showed that 72% of patients with Bcc receive CFTR-modulators, of which 86% are on the three-component modulator. Analysis of sputum in patients on CFTR-modulator therapy showed that Bcc is not detected by standard microbiological culture methods in 8% of patients,in 92% there is a decrease in the degree of Bcc colonization in sputum and an increase in microbial species diversity.
sections.Conclusion: The main type of Bcc in Russia is B.cenocepacia, which is represented by hospital genotypes ST709 and ST208, with a predminance of ST709. Infection with hospital genotypes ST709 and ST208 significantly limits the life expectancy of patients infected with them. During CFTR-modulator therapy, a decrease in the sputum contamination with B. cenocepacia and an increase in microbial species diversity were observed in most patients, as well as several cases of absence of Bcc seeding in sputum culture.

presentations.title: The effect of Staphylococcus aureus in the lung epithelial barrier in cystic fibrosis
presentations.summary: Background: The bronchial epithelium acts as first defence against a wide range of exogenous substances and pathogens by forming a barrier that prevents them from penetrating the tissue. The epithelial barrier function is maintained by apical junctional proteins (AJP) that form between neighbouring cells and consist of apical tight junctions (TJ) such as claudins, occludin and JAM-A and adherent junctions (AJ) such as E-cadherin. People with cystic fibrosis (CF) have a weakened lung epithelial barrier; at the same time, CF patients are also more susceptible to infections such as Staphylococcus aureus(Sa), in part due to this same weakened barrier. It has been proposed that CFTR interacts with some AJPs to help...
presentations.authors_text: A. Montes Gomez1, N. Robinson1, R. Carvajal Quisalema1, R. Gray1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Glasgow, School of Infection and Immunity, Glasgow, United Kingdom

sections.Background: The bronchial epithelium acts as first defence against a wide range of exogenous substances and pathogens by forming a barrier that prevents them from penetrating the tissue. The epithelial barrier function is maintained by apical junctional proteins (AJP) that form between neighbouring cells and consist of apical tight junctions (TJ) such as claudins, occludin and JAM-A and adherent junctions (AJ) such as E-cadherin. People with cystic fibrosis (CF) have a weakened lung epithelial barrier; at the same time, CF patients are also more susceptible to infections such as Staphylococcus aureus(Sa), in part due to this same weakened barrier. It has been proposed that CFTR interacts with some AJPs to help form the barrier in the lung epithelium and they miss-localise in the absence of CFTR, contributing to the weakened barrier, however, the effect of the Sa at the barrier level and AJPs in CF has not been investigated. Aim: 1. To determine the effect of Staphylococcus aureus infection in TJ and AJ proteins in CF cells at the protein level and their cellular localisation.
sections.Methods: CF and non-CF cells are infected with Staphylococcus aureus strain USA300 and TJ and AJ proteins are evaluated at the mRNA and protein level. Cells are also infected and the cellular localisation of TJ and AJ proteins are evaluated by immunofluorescence.
sections.Results: No differences in the expression of the TJ proteins or AJ at the mRNA or protein level were observed. However, we found that upon Sa infection, in the absence of CFTR, some cells express more E-cadherin and JAM-A, possibly as a compensatory mechanism to tighten the barrier. On the contrary, occludin is observed at high levels at the membrane of non-CF cells and decreases drastically upon infection. This protein is observed already in low levels in CF cells and seems to almost completely disappear upon infection.
sections.Conclusion: Staphylococcus aureus causes miss-localisation of AJPs with a more severe effect in CF cells.

sections.Methods: This is a cross-sectional, observational study that included 44 children with CF and 44 healthy subjects. Serum of Tenascin-C, interleukin (IL)-8, and neutrophil elastase (NE) were determined by the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) method. Relationships between tenascin-C concentrations and clinical parameters in patients with CF were also evaluated. The study was granted by TUBİTAK 2209-A.
sections.Results: There were no significant differences between patients and controls in age and gender (p=0.857 and p=0.829, respectively) (Table 1). NE concentrations were higher in the patient group than in healthy controls, and tenascin-C was higher in healthy controls (p=0.001 and p=0.016, resp.) (Table 2). However, there were no significant differences between groups according to IL-8 concentrations. There was a negative correlation between Tenascin-C and NE (rho=-0.457, p=0.002). However, tenascin-C levels did not correlate with age, gender, BMI, or laboratory parameters. Serum tenascin-C, NE, and IL-8 did not significantly differ between patients using modulators and those not using modulators.
sections.Conclusion: This is the first study investigating tenascin-C levels in CF. Tenascin-C levels in CF patients were lower than in healthy controls. Serum tenascin-C levels play a limited role in CF inflammation. Further studies are necessary to determine this role. Table 1. sections.Objectives: Prolonged P. aeruginosa infections have been linked to chronic inflammation in Cystic Fibrosis (CF) lung, worsening the damage to lung tissue by neutrophil-derived reactive oxygen species (ROS) and proteases. Current anti-inflammatories are poorly effective and have dramatic side effects in people with CF (pwCF). Therefore, identifying innovative, effective, and safe drugs to restore redox balance and mitigate inflammation remains a critical unmet need.
sections.Methods: We performed a dose-response of an IkB-kinase-b (IKKb) inhibitor, PS-1145, in F508del-CFTR CFBE cells and primary nasal epithelial (HNE) cells from pwCF bearing F508del mutation. Inflammatory and oxidative status were evaluated by detecting specific markers. We investigated pro-inflammatory cytokine gene expression and protein release (via RT-qPCR and ELISA) and assessed the antioxidant activity of the compound by measuring intracellular ROS levels (using a ROS-sensitive fluorescent probe) in both F508del-CFTR CFBE and HNE cells under inflammatory conditions.
sections.Results: PS-1145 significantly reduced IL-6 and IL-8 levels (both mRNA and protein) in a dose-dependent manner in both CFBE and HNE cells. To further investigate the antioxidant activity of these compounds, we developed a high-throughput fluorometric assay to measure ROS levels using a ROS-sensitive fluorescent probe. We performed a dose-response of PS-1145 to investigate its antioxidant activity. Interestingly, PS-1145, exhibited antioxidant activity, in a dose-dependent manner, in F508del-CFTR CFBE cells treated with H₂O₂.
sections.Conclusion: These findings provide strong evidence that PS-1145 can prevent redox imbalance and attenuate inflammation in CF airway epithelial cells, potentially limiting the drivers of chronic lung damage.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Liverpool, Biochemistry, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 2University of Manchester, Manchester, United Kingdom, 3Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust, Manchester, United Kingdom, 4University of Liverpool, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 5Queen's University Belfast, Belfast, United Kingdom, 6All Wales Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Penarth, United Kingdom, 7Newcastle upon Tyne Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Newcastle, United Kingdom, 8Leeds Institute of Medical Research, Leeds, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Pulmonary exacerbations are a major cause of morbidity in cystic fibrosis and are commonly triggered by viral infection and dysregulated airway immune responses. The upper airway plays a key role in shaping responses to viral exposure and downstream lower airway inflammation. The nasal mucosa provides an accessible site in which upper airway immune processes may offer insight into mechanisms underlying pulmonary exacerbations.
sections.Methods: Nasal mucosal lining fluid was collected from people with cystic fibrosis (PwCF; n=12) and age-matched healthy controls (n=8). These samples represent the initial participants from the CF-Tracker study; an ongoing study investigating pulmonary exacerbations, with longitudinal clinical data and viral exposures. Samples were analysed using liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry–based DIA proteomics. Multivariate, differential abundance and functional enrichment analyses were performed.
sections.Results: Proteomic profiling demonstrated clear differences between PwCF and healthy controls. Principal component analysis showed group separation, most evident along the PC2 axis. Differential abundance analysis identified widespread alterations in protein expression in PwCF, with enrichment of pathways related to immune activation, metabolic reprogramming, and cellular stress responses. Proteins involved in intracellular protein transport and localisation were downregulated.
sections.Conclusion: The nasal proteome captures disease-associated immune and metabolic alterations in cystic fibrosis, supporting the upper airway as a biologically informative compartment. These initial data provide a foundation for future analyses integrating nasal proteomics with longitudinal clinical data to define relationships between upper airway immune responses, viral exposures, and pulmonary exacerbation risk. Funding: LifeArc / Cystic Fibrosis Trust Innovation Hub Network.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Pharmetheus AB, Uppsala, Sweden, 2Boehringer Ingelheim International GmbH, Ingelheim am Rhein, Germany, 3Boehringer Ingelheim Pharmaceuticals, Inc., Ridgefield, United States, 4Metrum Research Group, Boston, United States

sections.Objectives: Bronchiectasis, a chronic lung disease, is driven by dipeptidyl peptidase 1 (DPP1)-activated neutrophil serine proteases. Verducatib, a DPP1 inhibitor, reduced pulmonary exacerbation risk in people with NCFBE and had similar safety, PK and pharmacodynamic profiles in adults with CFBE and NCFBE in Phase (Ph) 2 studies (CLAIRAFLY® and AIRLEAF®). To support the verducatib dose in the Ph 3 AIRTIVITY™ study, a popPK model was developed to characterise and simulate exposure, and evaluate steady-state exposure, in adults with CFBE vs. NCFBE.
sections.Methods: PopPK analysis, including covariates, was performed using NONMEM®. Individual parameter estimates of primary and secondary PK parameters for the Ph 2 population were derived from the final model. Exposure simulations evaluated the Ph 3 dose (verducatib 2.5 mg once daily).
sections.Results: Overall, 288 people (63 healthy, 210 with NCFBE, 15 with CFBE) were included in the analysis (median age, 54.5 years; median weight, 65.1 kg; 55% male; 75% White). Visual predictive checks and exposure simulations showed no meaningful differences in CFBE and NCFBE steady-state exposure. The final two-compartment model included dose-dependent bioavailability, categorical clearance (CL higher at Day 1 and lower thereafter) and linear distribution.
sections.Conclusion: The final PK model provided an adequate fit to the Ph 2 data. These results support the use of verducatib 2.5 mg in both those with CFBE and NCFBE in AIRTIVITY™; recruitment began in June 2025.

sections.Objectives: Asthma is present in 8.9% of adult patients with cystic fibrosis (pwCF)1. Biological therapies have been used safely in the management of severe asthma in non-CF patients for a number of years. Use of these drugs in the CF population is limited thus far and there have been some anecdotal reports of CF patients deteriorating after starting them in other centres.
sections.Methods: Patients in our adult CF centre who had been prescribed asthma biologics were retrospectively identified and data collected before and after biologic started. Data collected included demographics, oral corticosteroid (OCS) burden, infective exacerbation rate and lung function. This was done for each biologic if patients had taken multiple biologics.
sections.Results: Demographical data is demonstrated in Table 1. Benralizumab was taken by 3 patients, with all experiencing reduced number of OCS courses or maintenance steroid doses. Tezepelumab was taken by 3 patients, with reduced OCS courses reported in two of these patients. Median (IQR) months on benralizumab was 8 (6.0-31.5) and was 4 (3.5-5.5) on tezepelumab. There was no change in infective exacerbation rate. There was no significant change in median forced expiratory volume in 1 second (FEV1) after at least three months. There were no reported biologic-associated adverse reactions in any patient.
sections.Conclusion: While our dataset is limited, there is no evidence of harm in the use of asthma biologics in these patients and most have reduced steroid burden. Further research is required to fully understand the use of these medicines in pwCF. References: 1. UK Cystic Fibrosis Registry 2024 Annual Data Report (2025), Cystic Fibrosis Trust.
