presentations.summary: Background: The lung clearance index (LCI) derived from multiple-breath washout (MBW) testing is a sensitive marker of early lung disease in cystic fibrosis (CF). presentations.affiliations_text: 1The Hospital for Sick Children, Division of Respiratory Medicine, Department of Pediatrics, Toronto, Canada, 2Hadassah Medical Center and Faculty of Medicine, Pediatric Pulmonology Unit and Cystic Fibrosis Center, Jerusalem, Israel, 3The Hospital for Sick Children, Translational Medicine, Research Institute, Toronto, Canada, 4University of Toronto, Department of Paediatrics, Toronto, Canada

sections.Background: The lung clearance index (LCI) derived from multiple-breath washout (MBW) testing is a sensitive marker of early lung disease in cystic fibrosis (CF). However, widespread clinical implementation remains limited, partly due to long test duration. Current ATS/ERS technical standards recommend reporting LCI as the mean of at least three technically acceptable trials. Previous studies have shown minimal differences in group mean LCI when reducing repetitions from three to two; however, agreement between the first technically acceptable trial and the mean LCI has not been evaluated.
sections.Methods: We performed a retrospective analysis of MBW test occasions from three large studies (TRACK, SIMPLIFY and PROSPECT)1-3, including children and adults with CF and healthy controls. Visits with at least three technically acceptable trials, defined using a predefined quality control framework, were included and the mean LCI was calculated according to ATS/ERS criteria. The first technically acceptable LCI trial was compared with the mean LCI using Bland–Altman analysis, intraclass correlation coefficients (ICC), and Pearson correlation.
sections.Results: A total of 1,394 MBW test occasions from 530 unique participants (age range 5–56 years), including 46 healthy controls, were analyzed. Across studies, agreement between the first acceptable run and mean LCI was excellent (ICC = 0.99; r = 0.99). Mean (SD) LCI was 7.76 (2.54), 7.83 (2.63) for the first acceptable trial. Mean bias (0.06 LCI units), limits of agreement (−0.72 to 0.84). Results were unchanged when the mean LCI was calculated using two instead of three technically acceptable trials.
sections.Conclusion: In three independent cohorts, the first technically acceptable MBW trial demonstrated excellent agreement with the mean LCI. Under strict quality criteria, reliance on the first acceptable run may represent a pragmatic approach to reduce test burden and duration while maintaining measurement reliability.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Stockholm CF Center, Karolinska University Hospital Huddinge, Stockholm, Sweden, 2Department of Paediatrics, CLINTEC, Karolinska Institutet, Stockholm, Sweden, 3Clinical Genetics and Genomics Department, Karolinska University Hospital Solna, Stockholm, Sweden, 4Department of Women´s and Children´s Health, Uppsala University, Uppsala, Sweden, 5Department of Gothenburg CF Centre, Queen Silvia Children’s Hospital, Gothenburg, Sweden, 6Department of Pulmonology and Allergology, Skåne University Hospital, Lund, Sweden, 7Department of Biology and Medical Genetics, 2nd Faculty of Medicine, Charles University and Motol University Hospital, Prague, Czech Republic

sections.Objectives: Description of people with CF (pwCF) not treated with CFTR modulators (CFTRm) is needed to improve health of this population. We aimed to describe pwCF not treated with CFTRm in Sweden.
sections.Methods: Data entered in the National Swedish CF Registry were used. PwCF not receiving CFTRm treatment at the annual visit during 2024 (+/- 6 months) were classified as not treated with CFTRm. Lung-transplanted pwCF were excluded (n=75). Chi-square and Mann-Whitney U test were used. A multiple linear regression (MLR) model was created with FEV1 (% predicted) as dependent variable and CFTRm treatment, bronchodilator use, P. aeruginosa colonization, age, body mass index, immunoglobulin G (IgG), and leukocyte count as independent variables.
sections.Results: Out of 737 pwCF identified in the registry, 152 were not treated with CFTRm. There was no difference in sex between the groups (p=0.627), but the group not treated with CFTRm was younger (14 vs. 24 years, p<0.001) and had lower prevalence of pancreatic insufficiency (65 vs. 89%, p<0.001). Despite that, pwCF not treated with CFTRm had higher leukocyte count (7.2 vs. 6.1 x109/L, p<0.001) and sedimentation rate (8.0 vs. 6.0 mm, p=0.003) and higher Lung Clearance Index (LCI, 7.8 versus 6.9, p<0.001). FEV1 did not differ between the groups (87.2% vs 88.7%, p=0.692). CFTRm treatment was not a significant determinant of FEV1 in the MLR model (B=-0.066 (95 CI -0.150 – 0.017); p=0.12; adj. R2=0.33). Within the group not treated with CFTRm, pwCF carrying two non-responsive class I CFTR variants (n=45) had higher prevalence of P. aeruginosa colonization (51.1% vs. 13.5%, p<0.001), higher treatment burden, and higher serum IgG (11.6 vs. 9.6 g/L, p<0.001) but they did not differ in FEV1 (p=0.201) or in LCI (p=0.467).
sections.Conclusion: PwCF not treated with CFTRm in Sweden differed from pwCF treated with CFTRm in LCI but not in FEV1. Within this group, pwCF carrying two non-responsive class I CFTR variants had higher infection and treatment burden.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Hospital de Niños Ricardo Gutierrez, Respiratory Center, Buenos Aires, Argentina, 2Hospital de Niños Ricardo Gutierrez, Image Center, Buenos Aires, Argentina, 3Universidad de Buenos Aires, Statistics Department, Buenos Aires, Argentina
presentations.presenter: German Ezequiel Rodriguez Novoa (Buenos Aires, AR)

sections.Objectives: To correlate LCI and spirometry with structural damage measured by the Bhalla Score.
sections.Methods: Spirometry, LCI and computed tomography (the latter two within the last two years) were measured in patients with Cystic Fibrosis attending the Respiratory Centre of Hospital Gutiérrez. A descriptive analysis of demographic variables, infection by pathogens, presence of the p.F508del variant, LCI, spirometric values (FEV1 and FEF) and Bhalla Score was performed. All patients with FEV1 above the lower limit of normal were included. LCI, FEV1 (z-score), FEF and Bhalla Score were correlated using bivariate analysis with Spearman’s ρ coefficients. For group variables (LCI–Bhalla), the Mann–Whitney test was used.
sections.Results: A total of 33 pCF patients (23 male and 10 female), aged between 6 and 18 years (mean 11.8, SD 3.68), were included. All had FEV1 values above the lower limit of normal on spirometry. Of these, 60.6% were homozygous for p.F508del and 33.1% were heterozygous for this allele. Regarding infection by pathogens, normal flora was found in 81.8%, Pseudomonas aeruginosa in 9.1%, Burkholderia cepacia in 6.1%, and Mycobacterium abscessus in 3%. The mean (SD) values were: FEV1 96 (12.97), FEV1 z-score 0.27 (1.33), FEF 97.7 (26.5), LCI 9.07 (2.81), Bhalla Score 20.42 (3.98). When comparing spirometric variables FEV1 and FEF with the Bhalla Score, correlations of 0.38 and 0.37 (p < 0.05), respectively, were obtained, while LCI correlated with FEV1 z-score at -0.49 (p < 0.01). Patients with abnormal LCI (>7.8) had a mean (SD) Bhalla Score of 20 (3.89), whereas those with normal LCI had a mean score of 25 (2.96). This difference was statistically significant (p = 0.032).
sections.Conclusion: The correlation between LCI and Bhalla Score was adequate and superior to that of spirometry. In this group of patients with normal spirometry, 75.8% had abnormal LCI. This group showed a higher median Bhalla Score compared with those who had normal values. It is noteworthy that only one patient presented abnormal FEF.

Methods: Cross-sectional observational study included children with cystic fibrosis (CF) who underwent N₂-MBW, spirometry and whole-body plethysmography on the same day in 2025. Lung function parameters were analyzed according...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1ULS Santa Maria, Paediatric Lung Function Laboratory, Department of Paediatrics, Lisbon, Portugal, 2ULS Santa Maria, Paediatric Pulmonology Unit, Department of Paediatrics, Lisbon, Portugal, 3University of Lisbon, Faculty of Medicine, Lisbon, Portugal, 4ULS Santa Maria, Cystic Fibrosis Reference Centre, Lisbon, Portugal

sections.Objectives: Our aim was to determine the agreement between LCI in absolute value and z-score(zsc), evaluate the correlation of LCI with FEV1 and MMEF75–25 and compare FRC measured by N₂-MBW with TGV obtained by plethysmography.
sections.Methods: Cross-sectional observational study included children with cystic fibrosis (CF) who underwent N₂-MBW, spirometry and whole-body plethysmography on the same day in 2025. Lung function parameters were analyzed according to ERS/ATS and GLI guidelines and expressed in absolute values and zsc. Correlations between LCI and spirometric indices and between FRC (MBW) and volumes (plethysmography), were evaluated using Spearman coefficients and multiple linear regression (p<0.05).
sections.Results: Thirty-one children were included, 16(21%) female, mean age 11.5±3.3 years. The absolute LCI was 7.5±1.4 and in zsc 2.08±2.02, FRC absolute was 1.6±0.6L and in zsc −0.02 ±0.84. Spirometry showed globally preserved ventilatory function(FEV₁zsc 0.46±1.06; FEV₁/FVCzsc 0.02±0.87; MMEF75-25zsc 0.19±1.08). Plethysmography revealed TGV absolute 1.9±0.6L and zsc 0.32±0.65. There was strong agreement between LCI absolute values and zsc. In multiple linear regression, neither FEV1 nor MMEF75-25 proved to be independent predictors of LCI (FEV1: B=-0.227; β=-0.120; p=0.612; MMEF75-25: B=-0.700; β=-0.376; p=0.118). The N₂-MBW FRC correlated strongly with TGV in absolute values (ρ=0.93; p<0.05) and zsc(ρ=0.60; p<0.05).
sections.Conclusion: Despite a preserved ventilatory function, LCI revealed impairment in some patients, as previously described. Concerning lung volumes there was high volumetric reliability between the N2-MBW and plethysmography, reinforcing the use of N2-MBW in integrated functional assessment of CF patients.

HCPs (doctors, nurses, and physiotherapists) rated their level of familiarity with each pwCF and categorised them as infrequent, expected, or highly frequent spirometry users. They were also asked to est...
presentations.authors_text: A. Azam1,2, A.M. Jones1,2, R.J. Bright-Thomas1,2, A. Horsley1,2, P.J. Barry1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Manchester Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Manchester, United Kingdom, 2The University of Manchester, Manchester, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Home spirometry has been widely adopted in the delivery of cystic fibrosis (CF) care. While existing literature largely supports its feasibility and positive outcomes, behaviour around home disease monitoring remains poorly understood. This study aimed to evaluate healthcare professionals’ (HCPs') ability to estimate home spirometry usage pwCF and compare these with actual recorded data.
sections.Methods: Home spirometry data, from a single large adult CF centre, for the year 2024, was obtained from NuvoAir. HCPs (doctors, nurses, and physiotherapists) rated their level of familiarity with each pwCF and categorised them as infrequent, expected, or highly frequent spirometry users. They were also asked to estimate spirometry usage as an open-ended numerical response. CF experience was defined by the number of years the HCP had worked at the centre. Estimation accuracy was assessed using mean bias and mean absolute error (MAE).
sections.Results: 10 doctors (35.7%), 6 nurses (21.4%), and 12 physiotherapists (42.9%) responded to the survey, with an overall response rate of 96.6%. There were 790 completed categorical estimates and 794 numerical estimates. The mean (±SD) CF experience was 15.7 (±8.2) years. Across all roles, HCPs systematically underestimated home spirometry usage. Additionally, HCPs were better at identifying pwCF who underuse home spirometry than those who overuse it i.e; the more frequently an individual performed spirometry, the more likely HCPs were to underestimate it. No significant differences in estimation accuracy (mean bias and MAE) were observed based on professional role, HCP's reported familiarity level with pwCF or years of CF experience.
sections.Conclusion: This study found that CF caregivers tend to underestimate home spirometry usage, in contrast to other studies showing they often overestimate treatment adherence. This highlights gaps in understanding behaviour in pwCF and the need for CF teams to adapt to evolving models of remote monitoring.

presentations.summary: Background: Indoor and outdoor air pollution are major health risks. Indoor air quality (IAQ) accounts for > 90% of exposure. In people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) heightened susceptibility could mean that peaks in particulate matter (PM) or poorly ventilated conditions may trigger symptoms. Yet indoor pollutant levels, links to outdoor air, and clinical relevance in CF are poorly characterised. Methods: As part of the CF-Tracker study, real-time IAQ pollution sensors were placed in the homes of a subset of CF participants. Sensors continuously record CO2, PM2.5, temperature, relative humidity, and total VOCs, in the kitchen and bedroom. Monitoring is for 12 months from baseline, which includes assessment of l...
presentations.authors_text: S.E Weinberg1,2, T. Bannan1, C. Song1, E. Caamaño Gutiérrez3, R. Lord1,2, R.E Maher3, A.M Jones1,2, M.M Tunney4, G. McFiggans1, A. Horsley1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Manchester, Manchester, United Kingdom, 2Manchester Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Wythenshawe, Manchester, United Kingdom, 3University of Liverpool, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 4Queen's University Belfast, School of Pharmacy, Belfast, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Indoor and outdoor air pollution are major health risks. Indoor air quality (IAQ) accounts for > 90% of exposure. In people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) heightened susceptibility could mean that peaks in particulate matter (PM) or poorly ventilated conditions may trigger symptoms. Yet indoor pollutant levels, links to outdoor air, and clinical relevance in CF are poorly characterised.
sections.Methods: As part of the CF-Tracker study, real-time IAQ pollution sensors were placed in the homes of a subset of CF participants. Sensors continuously record CO2, PM2.5, temperature, relative humidity, and total VOCs, in the kitchen and bedroom. Monitoring is for 12 months from baseline, which includes assessment of lung inflammation and prospective clinical outcomes. Questionnaires capture building characteristics, ventilation, occupancy, and household behaviours. Outdoor air quality data is from UK AURN network and Manchester Air Quality Supersite.
sections.Results: To date, 14 pwCF have been recruited, with data collected up to seven months. Mean IAQ levels show strong seasonal variation and substantial between-home variability. Indoor PM2.5 was frequently higher than outdoor levels and often exceeded WHO guidelines (annual mean < 5 µg/m³), particularly in winter. Kitchens are the most polluted rooms, with short-duration PM2.5 peaks linked to cooking, driving elevated daily means and extreme peak exposures up to 200 µg m⁻³. CO2 levels are generally acceptable, but prolonged exceedances above the WHO recommended upper limit of 1,000 ppm occurs in bedrooms, indicating inadequate ventilation.
sections.Conclusion: PwCF are frequently exposed to significant indoor air pollution within their homes. Ongoing analyses aim to link these IAQ measurements with clinical records to clarify their role in exacerbations. Improved understanding of these relationships can inform interventions to enhance IAQ and potentially reduce health risks. Funding: LifeArc / Cystic Fibrosis Trust Innovation Hub Network.

presentations.title: Real-world impact of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor on functional exercise capacity in paediatric cystic fibrosis
presentations.summary: Introduction: In people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF), exercise intolerance and reduced functional capacity are relevant outcomes with prognosis implications. Although elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) improves lung function and nutritional status, its impact on functional exercise capacity (feC) remains incompletely characterized. The 6-minute walk test (6MWT) is a standardized tool to assess feC, whose role in the era of highly effective CFTR modulators requires further evaluation. sections.Objectives: This study aimed to assess the impact of ETI on feC using the 6MWT and to evaluate associated clinical and laboratory changes in pwCF.
sections.Methods: Single-center, retrospective, observational before–after study including pediatric pwCF treated with ETI with available 6MWT data before and after treatment. Clinical, functional and laboratory variables were analyzed pre and post-ETI.
sections.Results: Nineteen pwCF were included (64.8% female); 78.9% carried at least one F508del variant. Median age at ETI initiation was 145 months (IQR 8).Baseline mean 6MW distance (6MWD) was 621.7±64.5m. At reassessment after ETI initiation (median follow-up 11 months), 6MWD increased significantly by 27.1m (p=0.004), with 42.1% achieving a clinically relevant improvement (≥30 m). No significant associations were found between changes in 6MWD and changes in FEV₁, body mass index percentile (pBMI) or follow-up duration. No independent predictors of functional response were identified. Compared to baseline, after ETI, pBMI increased significantly, while pulmonary exacerbations, antibiotic use, and sweat chloride levels decreased.
sections.Conclusion: ETI was associated with meaningful improvements in feC alongside significant clinical and biological changes. The absence of significant associations between functional gains and traditional resting markers supports the complementary value of the 6MWT in assessment of therapeutic response.

sections.Objectives: Predicting lung function (LF) trajectories is vital for cystic fibrosis (CF) patients without access to CFTR modulators. This study aimed to develop and validate machine learning (ML)-based time-series models for predicting short-term FEV1 and forced vital capacity (FVC) trajectories using the CF Registry of Turkey (CFRT).
sections.Methods: In this retrospective cohort study, patient-level longitudinal data from individuals in the CFRT registry followed between 2019 and 2024 were analysed. Patients who received CFTR modulator therapy at any point during follow-up were excluded to focus on modulator-naive trajectories. Four LF outcomes were modelled: FEV1 Z-score (FEV1_Z), FEV1 percent predicted (FEV1_PP), FVC Z-score (FVC_Z), and FVC percent predicted (FVC_PP). CatBoost regression models were trained using nested cross-validation and hyperparameter optimisation. Model interpretability and feature contributions were assessed using SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations).
sections.Results: After excluding 452 patients on modulator therapy, a total of 1,384 patients contributing 4,714 clinical visits were analysed. The highest predictive performance was achieved by models trained on 3 years of historical data with a 1-year forecasting horizon, using 764 visits from 423 patients. Optimised models achieved R2 values of 0.63 (95% CI: 0.59–0.67) for FEV1_Z and 0.62 (95% CI: 0.57–0.66) for FVC_Z. Feature importance analysis showed that prior LF measurements were the strongest predictors; among non-LF variables, anthropometric parameters and sweat chloride levels contributed most to the model.
sections.Conclusion: Machine learning-based time-series models using national registry data demonstrated moderate-to-high prognostic performance for short-term lung function forecasting in CF patients not receiving modulator therapy. These findings support the feasibility of data-driven approaches for individualised risk stratification in routine clinical follow-up.

sections.Background: Body plethysmography complements spirometry in cystic fibrosis by assessing lung volumes and airway resistance, enabling detection of lung hyperinflation and air trapping, as well as supporting longitudinal treatment monitoring.
sections.Objectives: The study aimed to evaluate long-term changes in body plethysmography after initiation of ETI therapy and to determine whether these changes were accompanied by changes in sweat chloride.
sections.Methods: Children with cystic fibrosis aged 8 -18 years were included in the study. Body plethysmography was performed at baseline (pre-ETI) and repeated at 12 - 24 months following ETI initiation. Sweat chloride concentration (mmol/L) was measured at baseline and at 6, 12, and 24 months. Paired statistical tests and Spearman’s rank correlation were used for data analysis.
sections.Results: In the study group (n = 60), airway resistance decreased significantly after ETI therapy (Rtot −19.0 pp, p = 0.004; Reff −15.0 pp, p = 0.003). Total lung capacity increased (median +6.0 pp; p < 0.001), while indices of air trapping (RV, RV/TLC) remained unchanged. Sweat chloride levels decreased markedly at 6 months and remained reduced at 24 months (p < 0.001), with no correlation between changes in plethysmography parameters and sweat chloride.
sections.Conclusion: ETI therapy in pediatric CF patients is associated with reduced airway resistance and sustained sweat chloride improvement, while air-trapping indices remain unchanged.

sections.Objectives: Amongst the public and non-specialists, Cystic Fibrosis (CF) remains most known for its respiratory manifestations. The classical model of impaired mucociliary clearance resulting in chronic infection, bronchiectasis, and ultimately respiratory failure is shifting with the advent of CFTR modulators. We assessed changes in the prevalence of bronchiectasis on imaging, airway colonisation, and lung function amongst people with CF (pwCF) joining our adult service over time.
sections.Methods: We retrospectively reviewed 10 years of multi-modality data from pwCF transferring to the All-Wales adult CF service from 2015-2025.
sections.Results: Among 165 pwCF (91 male, 74 female), most (150/165, 91%) transitioned from paediatric services. Of the study population, 149/165 (90%) are currently using highly effective modulator therapy (HEMT). Table 1: Presence of bronchiectasis, current lung function, and Pseudomonas Aeruginosa status by year of joining service 2015-2019 2020-2025 New patients (n) 70 95 Bronchiectasis on CT (%) 53 (75.7%) 38 (40%) P<0.001 FEV1 (% pred) (median±IQR) 82.5 ± 35 91.5 ± 20 P=0.001 Chronic Pseudomonas 11 (15.7%) 5 (5.3%) Intermittent Pseudomonas 20 (28.6%) 20 (21.1%) Pseudomonas free 39 (55.7%) 70 (73.7%) Detection of Bronchiectasis on CT imaging has fallen- from the majority early in the review period to <2/5 over the past 4 years, parallelled by higher FEV1 results. Whilst the widespread introduction of HEMT in Wales (from 2020) coincides with our study period, this trend predates this. Pseudomonas colonisation has also fallen; no chronic colonisation has been identified for the past 2 years amongst new centre users.
sections.Conclusion: As care has advanced, bronchiectasis has become a rarer imaging finding amongst pwCF. Most joining our service now have normal CT imaging and lung function. Whether they receive the same benefit from traditional approaches to airways clearance and mucoactive therapies is unclear and the role of the CF multi-disciplinary team needs to evolve in parallel to the changing needs of pwCF. Tools such as lung clearance index and MRI imaging in future may have to play a greater clinical role to assess respiratory involvement in pwCF.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Nicolae Testemițanu State University of Medicine and Pharmacy, Department of Pediatrics, Chișinău, Moldova, Republic of, 2Children Emergency Hospital, Department of Pediatric Surgery and Orthopedics, Cluj-Napoca, Romania

sections.Objectives: To evaluate pulmonary changes associated with Aspergillus fumigatus in children with cystic fibrosis using CT imaging investigations. Materials and
sections.Methods: The study included 17 children with cystic fibrosis, hospitalized in the Pulmonology Department of the Mother and Child Institute, who were confirmed with Aspergillus fumigatus infection and underwent pulmonary CT imaging using a modern Toshiba Aquilion Prime 80 scanner in the Department of Radiology and Imaging. Quantitative assessment of total anti-Aspergillus fumigatus antibodies (IgM, IgG) was performed using the ELISA (enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay) method. Results were expressed quantitatively in units/mL relative to a cut-off threshold (>15 U/mL suggests the presence of anti-Af IgG/IgM antibodies).
sections.Results: The mean age of the patients was 11.2 ± 4.3 years (range: 5–16 years). All children presented elevated titers of anti-Aspergillus fumigatus antibodies (IgM, IgG >15 U/mL), with serum galactomannan antigen detected as positive in 3 children. Pulmonary CT examination revealed a variety of structural changes, predominantly cylindrical and varicose bronchiectasis with central distribution, endobronchial mucus impaction, nodular opacities and peribronchial infiltrates, as well as frequently identified areas of segmental atelectasis.
sections.Conclusion: Computed tomography represents an essential tool in the evaluation of pulmonary involvement in children with cystic fibrosis, particularly in cases associated with Aspergillus fumigatus. Through early detection and monitoring of lesion progression, CT contributes to treatment adjustment and prevention of severe complications, thereby improving prognosis and quality of life in pediatric patients with cystic fibrosis.

From 129Xe -MRI the ventilation defect percent (VDP) quantifies the proportion of non-ventilated lung. Results: Patient 1 (F, aged 8) had a new persistent cough and reduced FEV1 from 85%...
presentations.authors_text: I. Aldag1, L. Smith2, D. Hughes1, H. Marshall2, J. Wild2, N. West1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Sheffield Children's Hospital, Sheffield, United Kingdom, 2POLARIS, University of Sheffield, Division of Clinical Medicine, Sheffield, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: To demonstrate the clinical impact of 129Xe ventilation MRI on CF care at Sheffield Children’s Hospital, UK, we here describe 3 recent typical clinical scenarios where Xe-MRI uniquely impacted decision making.
sections.Methods: Clinical referral for lung 129Xe-MRI is considered where there is a deterioration in clinical status/lung function to assess for ventilation defects. We reviewed 35 referrals over a 3 year period and describe 3 typical scenarios where the 129Xe-MR images obtained influenced clinical decision making. From 129Xe -MRI the ventilation defect percent (VDP) quantifies the proportion of non-ventilated lung.
sections.Results: Patient 1 (F, aged 8) had a new persistent cough and reduced FEV1 from 85% to 57%. Parents were reluctant to start intravenous antibiotics (IVA). 129Xe-MRI showed ventilation defects and a large VDP of 15%. The MRI enabled parents to visualise the acute deterioration. A repeat MRI, post IVA, showed a large improvement with 1.5% VDP (FEV1. = 90%). The family engagement with treatment has improved due to the MRI. Patient 2 (F, aged 13) had stable FEV1 of 86%, which reduced to 72%. The patient was asymptomatic and we performed 129Xe-MRI to further assess her lung disease. The MRI was normal with a VDP of 0.7% and we refrained from increasing her treatment. FEV1 has remained at 72%. Patient 3 (F, aged 14) had a recurrent wet cough. MRI in 2022 showed a VDP of 2.8%. With exacerbations her FEV1 dropped but recovered to 108%. To better understand her lung disease 129Xe-MRI was repeated in 2025 and found multifocal bilateral ventilation defects with 13% VDP and we therefore decided to initiate regular IVA.
sections.Conclusion: 129Xe-MRI is an effective clinical tool that enables informed decision making on lung disease without exposing patients to ionising radiation. This enables repeat scanning after short intervals to assess the impact of intervention and personalised care.

presentations.title: Longitudinal lung ultrasound assessment in cystic fibrosis patients undergoing CFTR modulator therapy: a 2-year prospective study

Chest computed tomography (CT) remains the gold standard for structural lung assessment; however, its repeated use is limited by cumulative radiation exposure. Lung ultrasound (LUS) has emerged as a reliable, radiation-free tool for the detection of consolidations, atelectasis, and bronchiectasis. The primary objective of this prospective study was to evaluate the evolution of the CF Lung Ultrasound Score (CF-LUS) over a 2-year period in patients with CF treated with CFTR modulators and to assess its correlation with functional parameters . Methods: CF patients aged >6 years eligible for CFTR modulator thera...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Victor Babes University of Medicine and Pharmacy, Pediatrics, Timisoara, Romania

sections.Objectives: Pulmonary involvement in cystic fibrosis (CF) is the main determinant of disease prognosis. Chest computed tomography (CT) remains the gold standard for structural lung assessment; however, its repeated use is limited by cumulative radiation exposure. Lung ultrasound (LUS) has emerged as a reliable, radiation-free tool for the detection of consolidations, atelectasis, and bronchiectasis. The primary objective of this prospective study was to evaluate the evolution of the CF Lung Ultrasound Score (CF-LUS) over a 2-year period in patients with CF treated with CFTR modulators and to assess its correlation with functional parameters .
sections.Methods: CF patients aged >6 years eligible for CFTR modulator therapy were enrolled. Lung ultrasound was performed using a standardized CF-LUS score at treatment initiation, at 1, 3, 6 months, 1 and 2 years. CF-LUS scores were correlated with chest CT scores, FEV₁ and LCI.
sections.Results: 48 patients with CF were included. The mean baseline CF-LUS score was 9.18, with a significant reduction to 5.4 at 3 months and 2.6 at 2 years.CF-LUS showed a very strong correlation with CT rs = 0.91, p < 0.001). Higher CF-LUS scores, particularly reflecting atelectasis and multiple consolidations (>3), were strongly correlated with FEV₁ (rs = 0.75).
sections.Conclusion: CF-LUS is a useful, radiation-free tool for longitudinal monitoring of CF lung disease in patients receiving CFTR modulators, particularly in advanced disease. When combined with lung function parameters, lung ultrasound may complement conventional imaging in the long-term assessment of CF lung involvement.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Nottingham, Perinatal and Child Health, Lifespan and Population Health, School of Medicine, Nottingham, United Kingdom, 2University of Nottingham, Centre of Evidence Based Dermatology, Lifespan and Population Health, School of Medicine, Nottingham, United Kingdom, 3University of Sheffield, POLARIS, Division of Clinical Medicine, School of Medicine and Population Health, Sheffield, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: As an emerging technology in cystic fibrosis (CF), there is a need to support the understanding and communication of lung magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan results. This study sought to develop an online toolkit of resources to meet this need.
sections.Methods: A scoping review of imaging communication literature and the results of an online imaging survey (n=100 participants) were used to shape topic guides for focus groups with healthcare professionals, people with CF (pwCF) and carers. These groups (n=7, 30 participants) provided insight into the process of imaging results communication in CF and suggestions for toolkit design and content. This information was taken into a co-development process of online meetings with a group of pwCF, carers, healthcare professionals and researchers.
sections.Results: Using the Behaviour Change Wheel framework, the group identified behaviours relevant to results communication and developed resources to address these. It was agreed that a website would host the toolkit resources, consisting of written documents, an animation and series of good practice videos. The resources were refined through rounds of ‘think aloud’ interviews with pwCF, carers and healthcare professionals (n=12 participants to date). In these, individuals were presented with the draft resources and asked to verbalise their thoughts on them. Feedback was mapped into a table of changes and reviewed by the co-development group. Changes were agreed and actioned, before proceeding with the next interview cycle. Participants responded positively to the resources, with most feedback focusing on content that could be added rather than proposing amendments.
sections.Conclusion: Using qualitative and co-development methodologies, a toolkit of well-received resources has been created to support the understanding and communication of lung MRI results. This will go live in spring 2026. It is hoped it will help aid the successful implementation of lung MRI more widely in CF care.

We re...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Leeds Teaching Hospitals NHS Trust, Leeds Children’s Cystic Fibrosis Centres, Leeds Children's Hospital, Leeds, United Kingdom

sections.Background: It is the standard of care to perform an annual chest x-ray (CXR) for all children with Cystic Fibrosis (CF) in the UK to assess clinical and physiological progression and complications. However, the value of routine CXR has been questioned given significant increases in life expectancy in in the era of CFTR modulator therapy and increasing concern regarding the risk of radiation-induced cancer from cumulative radiation exposure.
sections.Methods: Reviewing all children at Leeds Children’s Cystic Fibrosis Centre receiving CFTR modulator therapy 188 (F 85, M 103) children had received at least one annual CXR after starting CFTR modulators (ELX/TEZ/IVA, or IVA for those with responsive gating mutations). We retrospectively reviewed all their annual CXRs after starting modulators. A total of 384 radiographs were compared with previous images and any changes in clinical management due to new findings were identified. Result: Of the 384 annual CXRs, only one (0.2%) had unexpected findings on annual CXR that led to a change in management. This patient required hospital admission due to multifocal areas of consolidation consistent with Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis. The patient had appeared clinically stable. There were no other objective signs indicating a modification in treatment. The remaining 383 CXRs did not lead to a change in clinical management.
sections.Conclusion: Our data show that clinical yield from routine annual CXR in children with CF taking CFTR modulator drugs is very low. Further study is needed to determine whether frequency should be reduced to every 2 years or whether other modalities such as ultra low dose High-Resolution Computed Tomography (HRCT) or Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) should be used instead.

presentations.title: Chest x-ray utilisation and impact on clinical decision making in the modulator era: a retrospective cohort study
presentations.summary: Introduction: Highly effective modulator therapy (HEMT) has transformed survival in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF), with life expectancy 71.6 years. Routine annual chest x-ray (CXR) is recommended in UK standards, but cumulative radiation from repeated imaging may increase malignancy risk. This study evaluated CXR utilisation, impact on clinical management, and projected lifetime CXR-related radiation exposure in the context of contemporary survival. Methods: A five-year retrospective cohort study at a large adult CF centre included all CXRs performed between 2020–2025 for pwCF. Adherence to guideline-recommended annual review CXR practice and the impact of annual review CXRs on management were assessed by...
presentations.authors_text: L. Rossiter1, R. Joint1, F. Frost1, D. Nazareth1, M. Walshaw1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Liverpool Heart and Chest Hospital, Respiratory Medicine, Liverpool, United Kingdom

sections.Methods: A five-year retrospective cohort study at a large adult CF centre included all CXRs performed between 2020–2025 for pwCF. Adherence to guideline-recommended annual review CXR practice and the impact of annual review CXRs on management were assessed by case-note review. An effective dose of 0.02 mSv per CXR was used to calculate annual and predicted lifetime exposure for estimated pre- and post-HEMT life expectancy.
sections.Results: 2423 CXRs were performed over 5 years from 351–414 patients annually. Each year, 42–51% had an annual review CXR, while 73–85% had at least one CXR outside annual review. Annual review CXRs led to a documented change in management in only 0.3–1.7% of the total cohort per year and 0.5–4% of those imaged at annual review. Mean radiation dose per patient over the study period was 0.11 mSv, and modelling contemporary survival suggested an additional lifetime CXR-related exposure of 0.672 mSv with HEMT. Patients (n=35) with worsening radiographic appearances over five years had significantly greater spirometric decline (median FEV₁ decrease 5%, P=0.025), suggesting clinically important progression is usually mirrored by physiological change.
sections.Conclusion: At this centre, CXR utilisation has remained stable in the modulator era, but annual review CXRs rarely alter management despite modest cumulative radiation exposure. These findings support exploration of de-escalating routine annual CXR in clinically stable pwCF, reserving imaging for those with spirometric decline or clinical need.

presentations.title: Exhaled breath volatile organic compounds as surrogate biomarkers for airway inflammation in cystic fibrosis
presentations.summary: Objectives: Neutrophilic airway inflammation is a hallmark of cystic fibrosis (CF) lung disease and often persists despite CFTR modulator therapy. We therefore investigated which volatile organic compounds (VOCs) in exhaled breath correlate with IL-8 in sputum, with the aim of determining whether exhaled breath VOCs could eventually serve as a surrogate marker. sections.Objectives: Neutrophilic airway inflammation is a hallmark of cystic fibrosis (CF) lung disease and often persists despite CFTR modulator therapy. Although reduced, pulmonary exacerbations still occur in people with CF (pwCF), and early detection remains essential to prevent irreversible lung damage. New biomarkers are needed to monitor airway inflammation in the modulator era. Sputum interleukin-8 (IL-8) is a key marker of neutrophilic inflammation but is difficult to obtain routinely. We therefore investigated which volatile organic compounds (VOCs) in exhaled breath correlate with IL-8 in sputum, with the aim of determining whether exhaled breath VOCs could eventually serve as a surrogate marker.
sections.Methods: Paired exhaled breath and sputum samples were collected from 32 pwCF across 64 visits. Breath VOCs were analysed by gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS), and sputum IL-8 by ELISA. The 50 VOCs most correlated with IL-8 were identified via exploratory heatmap analysis. Elastic net regression with cross-validation was then applied to select VOCs most strongly associated with sputum IL-8 and derive a composite VOC score. The composite score was calculated as a weighted linear combination of selected VOCs and evaluated using Spearman's correlation.
sections.Results: The elastic net model selected 14 VOCs from 64 paired observations. Nine VOCs showed significant individual correlations with sputum IL-8 (p < 0.05; Spearman’s ρ –0.34 to 0.32, median |ρ| = 0.26). The composite VOC score from these 9 VOCs correlated moderately with sputum IL-8 (ρ = 0.54, p < 0.001).
sections.Conclusion: An exhaled breath composite VOC score correlated significantly with sputum IL-8. Using multiple VOCs as a surrogate biomarker could reduce the need for sputum sampling in routine CF care. However, further studies should focus on validating these VOCs for routine monitoring of lung inflammation in PwCF care. Funding: This study was funded by a grant from Stichting TAAI.

presentations.summary: Background: Analysis of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) in exhaled breath offers a non-invasive method to capture metabolic, inflammatory, and microbial processes within the cystic fibrosis (CF) airway. Emerging breath metabolomics studies have identified VOC signatures associated with exacerbation status and lung function, highlighting their potential as diagnostic biomarkers for pulmonary exacerbations in CF. The aim is to identify exhaled breath VOCs associated with CF pulmonary exacerbations. presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Manchester, Manchester, United Kingdom, 2University of Leeds, Leeds, United Kingdom, 3Cardiff and Vale University, Cardiff, United Kingdom, 4The Newcastle upon Tyne Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Newcastle upon Tyne, United Kingdom, 5Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust, Manchester, United Kingdom, 6University of Liverpool, Liverpool, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Analysis of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) in exhaled breath offers a non-invasive method to capture metabolic, inflammatory, and microbial processes within the cystic fibrosis (CF) airway. Emerging breath metabolomics studies have identified VOC signatures associated with exacerbation status and lung function, highlighting their potential as diagnostic biomarkers for pulmonary exacerbations in CF. The aim is to identify exhaled breath VOCs associated with CF pulmonary exacerbations.
sections.Methods: Background samples of filtered air and tidal end expiratory breath samples were collected using the Owlstone ReCIVA system as part of the CF-Tracker study, a UK-wide longitudinal surveillance study aiming to identify triggers and susceptibility factors for pulmonary exacerbations in adults with CF. Collection of VOCs in people with CF (pwCF) are obtained at five study sites at up to three time points throughout the study period. VOCs trapped within sorbent tubes were shipped to a central laboratory and analysed by Thermal Desorption-Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry in full scan mode.
sections.Results: Initial preliminary analyses are available on matched breath and background samples from eight healthy volunteers (HV) and eight pwCF. VOCs commonly identified in breath and indoor air (n=32) were assessed for differences between matched background and breath samples. Acetone, isoprene, and limonene were significantly higher in breath, whereas toluene was higher in background samples. The VOC ethylbenzene was significantly higher in HV compared to pwCF after adjusting for false discovery rate.
sections.Conclusion: VOC sampling offers a non-invasive method of assessing lung infection and/or inflammation. Preliminary differences between pwCF and HV support further evaluation of breath VOCs as potential biomarkers of disease state and pulmonary exacerbations within the CF-Tracker cohort. Funding: LifeArc / Cystic Fibrosis Trust Innovation Hub Network.

Features were selected by repeated subsampling with XGBoost importance aggregation (24 VOCs). Group differences were tested on normalized...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1I.M. Sechenov First Moscow State Medical University, Moscow, Russian Federation, 2Pulmonology Research Institute under FMBA of Russia, Moscow, Russian Federation

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) and bronchial asthma (BA) may both present with airflow obstruction, but reflect distinct inflammatory and microbial-metabolic endotypes. We evaluated whether real-time exhaled volatile organic compounds (VOCs) using proton transfer reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometry (PTR-TOF-MS) can separate CF from BA.
sections.Methods: In a cross-sectional analysis of adult cohorts, PTR-TOF-MS (Ionicon, Austria) breath profiles were analyzed from BA (n=204 measurements) and CF (n=97). Features were selected by repeated subsampling with XGBoost importance aggregation (24 VOCs). Group differences were tested on normalized data with Holm-corrected Mann-Whitney tests. Classification performance was evaluated on an independent hold-out test set using one-vs-one ROC AUC.
sections.Results: CF showed markedly lower signals at phenol-related m/z 95.054 and 95.082 (median 0.0186-0.0185) versus BA (median 0.15970-0.1599; p<0.001 for both). Indole-related m/z 118.071 was also reduced in CF (median 0.0026; p=0.007). BA exhibited higher pentanoic acid or 3-methylbutanoic acid with m/z 103.076 (median 0.0197; p<0.001). The 24-VOC model discriminated CF from BA with AUC 0.895 (95% CI 0.777-0.974).
sections.Conclusion: PTR-TOF-MS breathomics identified a compact VOC signature that robustly differentiates CF from BA, supporting rapid non-invasive adjunct assessment and motivating prospective validation in diagnostically challenging obstructive disease.

presentations.summary: Background: Pulmonary exacerbations (PEx) in cystic fibrosis (CF) are clinically heterogeneous, varying in symptoms, triggers, and treatment response. These episodes typically feature increased cough, sputum production, and breathlessness, and are usually treated with oral or intravenous antibiotics. The exposome, encompassing cumulative environmental, microbial, and lifestyle exposures, may underpin this variability and enable identification of biologically distinct PEx phenotypes. The CF-Tracker study aims to characterize the exposome in people with CF (pwCF) to guide more targeted treatment strategies. Methods: This is a longitudinal prospective observational study enrolling pwCF across the United Kingdom,...

sections.Background: Pulmonary exacerbations (PEx) in cystic fibrosis (CF) are clinically heterogeneous, varying in symptoms, triggers, and treatment response. These episodes typically feature increased cough, sputum production, and breathlessness, and are usually treated with oral or intravenous antibiotics. The exposome, encompassing cumulative environmental, microbial, and lifestyle exposures, may underpin this variability and enable identification of biologically distinct PEx phenotypes. The CF-Tracker study aims to characterize the exposome in people with CF (pwCF) to guide more targeted treatment strategies.
sections.Methods: This is a longitudinal prospective observational study enrolling pwCF across the United Kingdom, alongside a healthy volunteer control group. Recruitment began in May 2025 and will continue through 2026. Participants undertake home-based monitoring of symptoms, lung function, and environmental and viral exposures. A two-tiered design balances breadth and depth: Group A (n = 200) provides high-frequency scalable data, while group B (n=100) contributes intensive exposomic sampling. Participants are followed for 12 months, with fortnightly at-home sampling for six months, and one to three in-person visits. Up to 50 participants undergo continuous home pollution monitoring for 12 months with supplementary mould sampling. During suspected PEx, participants complete additional sampling or attend an in-person assessment prior to antibiotic initiation.
sections.Results: To date, 55 participants have been consented across seven centres, with 11 additional centres open or opening shortly. The cohort is 55% female, mean age 40 years (range 20-77 years). Mean FEV1 is 58.9% predicted (range 25-111% predicted) and 7% are not on CFTR modulators. Updated data will be presented.
sections.Conclusion: CF-Tracker will generate high-resolution longitudinal exposome data to support improved characterization of PEx phenotypes in CF. Funding: LifeArc/Cystic Fibrosis Trust Innovation Hub Network.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Imam Abdulrahman Al faisal Hospital, Department of Pediatrics, Dhahran, Saudi Arabia, 2University of Calgary, Department of Pediatrics, Calgary, Canada

sections.Methods: A cross-sectional survey was conducted among patients aged 8 to 18 years attending the CF Clinic at Alberta Children’s Hospital between February 18 and June 24, 2025. Of 64 eligible patients, 47 were approached and 36 consented to complete the survey, adapted from the CDC National Youth Tobacco Survey and administered via Qualtrics with QR code access. Data included lifetime and recent e-cigarette use, product types, motivations, and perceptions of harm. Results were compared with the 2023–2024 Canadian Student Alcohol and Drug Survey (CSADS).
sections.Results: Participants were aged 8-17 years (mean 13.8 years, median 14 years). Overall, 19.4% (n = 7) reported ever using of e-cigarettes and 11.1% (n = 4) reported past-30-day use; 75% of recent users used nicotine products. Among ever users, 57.1% were female. Highest prevalence was in grade 12. Compared with CSADS, the CF group showed slightly higher experimentation but lower recent use. Gender differences in vaping were similar to CSADS. Among respondents, 85.7% reported initiating e-cigarette use primarily because a friend used them, representing the most frequently cited reason. Regarding perceptions of harm, 48.6% of participants believed that e-cigarettes cause "a lot of harm", and 37.8% perceived "some harm". Clinical implications: The findings offer valuable insights into vaping behaviors in adolescents with CF, a population for whom inhalational exposures may carry greater risk. Incorporating routine screening and preventive counseling in CF clinics could inform care planning and early intervention strategies, particularly for older adolescents.

presentations.summary: Background: Pulmonary exacerbations remain a prominent feature of cystic fibrosis (CF) but causes and pathophysiology are poorly understood. Evidence indicates dysregulated immune responses occur in people with CF (pwCF). We hypothesised that specific immune signatures may be helpful in predicting which pwCF are likely to have pulmonary exacerbations. Methods: Spectral flow cytometry was used to identify 38 subsets of immune cells in the circulation of pwCF (n=19) at baseline, alongside healthy controls (n=11). Any pulmonary exacerbations during a 4 month[EM1] follow up phase were recorded. Results: CD4+ and CD8+ T cells expressing the immune suppression markers PD1 (p<0.05) and CD38 (p<0.01; respectively) wer...
presentations.authors_text: E. Mann1, L. Pearmain1,2, S. Weinberg1,2, F. Hunter1, R. Maher3, N. Scott1, A. Jones1,2, E. Caamano-Gutierrez3, M. Tunney4, R. Lord1,2, A. Horsley1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Manchester, Manchester, United Kingdom, 2Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust, Manchester, United Kingdom, 3University of Liverpool, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 4Queen's University Belfast, Belfast, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Pulmonary exacerbations remain a prominent feature of cystic fibrosis (CF) but causes and pathophysiology are poorly understood. Evidence indicates dysregulated immune responses occur in people with CF (pwCF). We hypothesised that specific immune signatures may be helpful in predicting which pwCF are likely to have pulmonary exacerbations.
sections.Methods: Spectral flow cytometry was used to identify 38 subsets of immune cells in the circulation of pwCF (n=19) at baseline, alongside healthy controls (n=11). Any pulmonary exacerbations during a 4 month[EM1] follow up phase were recorded.
sections.Results: CD4+ and CD8+ T cells expressing the immune suppression markers PD1 (p<0.05) and CD38 (p<0.01; respectively) were reduced in total pwCF regardless of exacerbations, corresponding to reports of chronic/sustained activation of T cells in CF. Double negative (CD4-CD8-) T cells that can help maintain immune tolerance and prevent overt inflammation were reduced in pwCF (p<0.05), alongside a reduction in memory (antibody-producing) B cells (p<0.05). At baseline, FEV1 was lower in pwCF who went on to have exacerbations within 4 months (p<0.01) and importantly, both double negative T cells (p<0.01) and memory B cells (p<0.05) positively correlated with FEV1, thus indicating low amounts of these cells in the bloodstream may reflect risk for pulmonary exacerbations.
sections.Conclusion: Within 38 subsets of circulating immune cells analysed, we have found strong indication of a role for T and B cells in CF pathology. The reduction in immuno-suppressive T cell subsets likely contributes to the overt/inappropriate inflammation observed in pwCF. The loss of antibody-producing B cells has critical implications for vaccine responses in pwCF against respiratory viruses (a major driver of pulmonary exacerbations). Critically, our results indicate systemic changes to B and T cell function in pwCF that may be useful in predicting pulmonary exacerbations and thus tailoring treatment options.

sections.Objectives: Pulmonary exacerbations (PEx) remain a significant cause of morbidity and lung function decline in cystic fibrosis (CF). Identifying age-specific predictors is crucial for optimizing clinical outcomes. This study utilizes data from the National CF Registry to identify independent risk factors for PEx in pediatric patients across different developmental stages.
sections.Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted on 2,228 patients from the Cystic Fibrosis National Registry, stratified by age: <6 years and ≥6 years. A comprehensive suite of clinical, functional, and microbiological parameters were analyzed. Predictors identified in univariate analysis (p<0.20) were included in a forward stepwise multivariable binary logistic regression model to determine independent risk factors for acute pulmonary exacerbations.
sections.Results: In the ≥6 years cohort, Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis (ABPA) was the strongest predictor (OR: 4.221, p=0.006), followed by CF-Related Diabetes (CFRD) (OR: 2.777, p=0.002), MRSA colonization (OR: 2.605, p<0.001), and Non-tuberculous Mycobacteria (NTM) growth (OR: 2.509, p<0.001). Higher FVC values provided significant protection (OR: 0.964, p<0.001). For children <6 years, Pseudomonas aeruginosa colonization (OR: 5.651, p<0.001) and NTM (OR: 4.378, p<0.001) were the primary drivers of risk. Conversely, elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) therapy (OR: 0.348, p=0.024) and improved weight-for-age z-scores (OR: 0.767, p=0.002) were associated with a substantial reduction in PEx odds. Maintenance therapies correlated with higher risk, likely reflecting baseline disease severity.
sections.Conclusion: This nationwide registry study highlights distinct, age-specific risk profiles for CF. The findings emphasize the importance of early microbial surveillance and nutritional optimization. Early initiation of ETI therapy is critical for enhancing disease control in the younger pediatric population.

Highly effective modulator therapy (HEMT) has improved clinical outcomes in CF, but its effects on anemia and iron status remain unclear. This study aimed to evaluate the impact of HEMT on iron parameters in pediatric CF patients.
sections.Methods: This single-center retrospective study included 28 patients receiving elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) or ivacaftor. hemoglobin (Hb), hematocrit (Hct), mean corpuscular volume (MCV), red blood cell count (RBC), white blood cell count (WBC), platelet count (PLT), absolute neutrophil count (ANC), absolute lymphocyte count (ALC), neutrophil/lymphocyte ratio (NLR) and mean platelet volume (MPV) were recorded at baseline and at 1, 6, and 12 months. sections.Objectives: To evaluate changes in nutritional status and pulmonary function (FEV1) in adults with CF following the initiation of CFTR modulator treatment.
sections.Methods: Observational, descriptive, and longitudinal study. Twenty-two adult patients from an acute hospital who initiated modulators between 2018 and 2024 were included. Anthropometric parameters (BMI, mid-arm muscle circumference -MAMC-, sum of skinfolds) and functional parameters (FEV1) were compared at two time points: 6 months prior to initiation and between 6-12 months post-treatment. Data were analysed with Jamovi software, using Student's t-tests for related samples (p < 0.05).
sections.Results: The mean age was 35.8 years. A significant improvement in pulmonary function was observed, with an increment in FEV1 from 2.09 L to 2.42 L (p = 0.009). Regarding nutritional status, BMI increased significantly from 20.95 kg/m² to 22.29 kg/m² (p = 0.005). The percentage of patients who were underweight (BMI < 20 kg/m²) reduced from 77.3% to 50%. Regarding body composition, a significant increase in MAMC was found (23 cm to 24 cm, p = 0.015), which suggests a recovery of fat-free mass. The increase in skinfolds (adiposity) showed an upward trend, although it did not reach statistical significance (p = 0.085).
sections.Conclusion: Treatment with modulators was associated with significant nutritional and functional recovery. The improvement in MAMC highlights a positive impact on muscle mass. It is imperative to re-evaluate traditional "high-calorie" nutritional guidelines to prevent the risk of overweight in this new therapeutic era.

sections.Background: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a multisystem disorder frequently complicated by cystic fibrosis–related liver disease (CFLD). Using data from the Cystic Fibrosis Registry of Turkey(CFRT), we aimed to determine the prevalence and predictors of CFLD and cirrhotic CFLD.
sections.Methods: This study analyzed 2023 CFRT data.Patients were classified as having CFLD or not,and clinical and genetic characteristics were compared between groups.Patients with CFLD were further divided into cirrhotic and non-cirrhotic subgroups to identify risk factors for cirrhosis.
sections.Results: Of 2,258 patients,17 were excluded due to missing data.Among the remaining 2,241, 425(19%)had CFLD.Patients with CFLD had higher sweat chloride values and were more likely to have meconium ileus and pancreatic insufficiency (p<0.001).Biallelic severe CFTR variants were strongly associated with CFLD and were accompanied by higher mortality and a greater burden of CF-related complications (p<0.005).Multivariable regression analysis identified CFTR variant severity, sweat chloride values, and CF-related diabetes(CFRD) as independent predictors of CFLD.Complete data were available for 343 with CFLD, of whom 27(7.8%) had cirrhosis.Cirrhotic patients were older, predominantly male, and had lower percent-predicted forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV₁%) (p<0.005). Hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, variceal bleeding, and ultrasonographic heterogeneity were significantly more frequent (p<0.001).Cirrhotic patients also had higher albumin–bilirubin (ALBI) scores and lower albumin, platelet, and leukocyte counts. In multivariable analysis, elevated ALBI score, reduced FEV₁%, and lower leukocyte count independently predicted cirrhosis.
sections.Conclusion: Severe genotypes,CFRD,and elevated sweat chloride levels were major risk factors for CFLD,while the presence of at least one mild allele was protective.Among patients with CFLD,reduced lung function,leukopenia,and higher ALBI scores independently predicted cirrhosis. presentations.summary: Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a multisystem disease primarily affecting the respiratory system and is associated with muscle weakness and reduced exercise capacity. Keniş Coşkun2, C. Yılmaz Yeğit1, A.P. Ergenekon1, Y. Gökdemir1, E. Erdem Eralp1, E. Karadağ Saygı2, F. Karakoç1, B. Karadağ1

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a multisystem disease primarily affecting the respiratory system and is associated with muscle weakness and reduced exercise capacity. Previous studies have shown that Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Conductance Regulator (CFTR) modulator therapies, particularly Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor /Ivacaftor(ETI) can improve muscle strength, making muscle function assessment an important component of follow-up since reimbursement of modulator therapy in Türkiye in July 2025.
sections.Methods: This ongoing prospective study includes patients with CF aged 6–20 years who are evaluated before initiation of ETI therapy and follow-up assessments are scheduled at 3, 6, 9, and 12 months. Assessments include maximal inspiratory and expiratory pressures (MIP/MEP), handgrip strength, biceps curl test, biceps and quadriceps muscle strength measured by dynamometer, the 1-minute sit-to-stand test(1-m STS), and the A-STEP test. All tests were performed three times, and the best values were used for analysis.
sections.Results: The mean age of the 10 patients who currently enrolled in the study was 15.7 ± 2.5 years. Mean weight, height, and BMI Z-scores were −0.88 ± 0.64, −0.54 ± 0.80, and −0.80 ± 0.78, respectively. The mean FEV₁ Z-score was −0.99 ± 1.28. Prior to ETI therapy, mean handgrip strength was 28.7 ± 14.3 , mean biceps curl repetitions were 16.2 ± 4.89, median biceps muscle strength was 14(9–30) , mean quadriceps muscle strength was 13 ± 3.65 and the mean 1-m STS repetitions were 25.8 ± 3.68. The mean achieved level in the A-STEP test was 6.3 ± 2. Follow-up evaluations are ongoing.
sections.Conclusion: Preliminary findings indicate that muscle strength and functional exercise capacity in patients with CF can be effectively assessed prior to ETI therapy. Follow-up assessments planned with additional patients are expected to clarify the effects of ETI therapy on muscle strength and functional performance.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Gazi University Medicine Faculty, Pediatric Pulmonology, Yenimahalle, Turkey, 2Gazi University Medicine Faculty, Yenimahalle, Turkey

sections.Objectives: To examine associations between child anxiety and maternal well-being and clinical/laboratory parameters in pwCF.
sections.Methods: Children aged 5–18 years were included. Demographics, age at diagnosis, BMI Z-score, pancreatic insufficiency, airway colonization, spirometry, SKS, and modulator use were recorded. PwCF completed SASC-R and mothers SWEMWBS. Groups were compared by modulator use, colonization, and pancreatic insufficiency.
sections.Results: Thirty-nine pwCF (61.5% male) were included; mean age 12.56±4.55 years, BMI Z-score −0.96±1.46. Modulators were used by 66.7%, pancreatic insufficiency was present in 76.9%, and airway colonization in 46.1%. Mean FEV1 Z-score was −0.82±1.46 and SKS 83.2±17.4. SASC-R and SWEMWBS did not differ by sex, pancreatic insufficiency, colonization, or modulator use (all p>0.05), and pwCF and maternal scores were not correlated (p=0.298). In patients on modulator, SASC-R correlated with SKS (r=0.43, p=0.033) and SWEMWBS with MEF25–75 Z-score (r=0.42, p=0.038). In non-colonized pwCF, SASC-R was not correlated with FEV1 Z-scores (r=0.38, p=0.10) but showed a positive correlation with MEF25–75 Z-scores (r=0.47, p=0.036), while SWEMWBS correlated negatively with age at diagnosis (r=−0.49, p=0.022). Social anxiety was low in 17 (43.6%), moderate in 18 (46.2%), and high in 4 (10.3%). Maternal well-being was low in 1 (2.6%), low–moderate in 2 (5.1%), moderate in 6 (15.4%), and high in 30 (76.9%).
sections.Conclusion: Earlier diagnosis is associated with better maternal psychosocial well-being. The presence of social anxiety in pwCF suggests clinical parameters may influence social anxiety and maternal well-being in pwCF and their families.

Methods: This observational study included adult CF patients followed at a tertiary care center. Participants were classified according to access to CFTR modulator therapy. presentations.authors_text: A. Kara1, O. Karcıoğlu1, B. Gemicioğlu2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Hacettepe University Faculty of Medicine, Pulmonology and Chest Diseases, Ankara, Turkey, 2Marmara University Faculty Of Medicine, Pulmonology and Chest Diseases, Istanbul, Turkey

sections.Objectives: CFTR modulator therapies have substantially improved clinical outcomes in cystic fibrosis (CF); however, unequal access to these treatments remains a major concern. This study aimed to evaluate the psychosocial and economic effects of access to CFTR modulator therapy in adult patients with CF.
sections.Methods: This observational study included adult CF patients followed at a tertiary care center. Participants were classified according to access to CFTR modulator therapy. Psychosocial outcomes were assessed using the PHQ-9 and GAD-7 screening tools. Economic indicators and key clinical variables, including comorbidities, hospitalization history, and airway microbial colonization, were recorded. Statistical analyses were performed with significance set at p < 0.05.
sections.Results: Forty-eight adult patients with CF were included. Demographic, clinical, and socioeconomic characteristics are summarized in Table 1, and PHQ-9 and GAD-7 results are shown in Table 2. No or minimal depressive symptoms were observed in more than half of the cohort, while moderate to severe depression was identified in approximately one-quarter of patients. No or minimal anxiety was present in the majority of patients. Major depressive symptoms were significantly more frequent in male patients (p = 0.03) and in those with methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) growth in sputum cultures (p = 0.04). No statistically significant associations were identified between evaluated factors and anxiety symptoms.
sections.Conclusion: Psychosocial outcomes in adult patients with CF are influenced by both clinical and socioeconomic factors. Male sex and MSSA colonization were associated with a higher burden of depressive symptoms, whereas no clear determinants of anxiety were identified. By emphasizing psychosocial aspects that may be overlooked in routine care, this study highlights the importance of integrating mental health assessment into comprehensive CF management. presentations.title: Clinical findings, quality of life, and self-care management in children with cystic fibrosis and advanced lung disease: a multifaceted evaluation
presentations.summary: Objectives: Advanced lung damage remains the primary cause of morbidity and mortality in cystic fibrosis (CF). While clinical care focuses on pulmonary stability, the impact of disease severity on quality of life (QoL) and self-care management in the paediatric advanced-stage cohort requires comprehensive evaluation, especially in the era of CFTR modulators. Methods: This cross-sectional study evaluated 15 paediatric patients (median age: 15.5 years, range: 11.2–19.0) with advanced lung disease (consecutive ppFEV1<50%) followed at Hacettepe University between 2015 and 2025. Evaluations included clinical data, body composition via bioelectrical impedance (fat-free mass [FFM], FFM index [FFMI]), respiratory musc...
presentations.authors_text: Y. Kiriş Ayhan1, Ö.F. İpek1, E. Yalçın1, K. Kılıç1, N. Emiralioğlu Ordukaya1, D. Doğru Ersöz1, U. Özçelik1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Division of Pulmonology, Department of Paediatrics, Hacettepe University Faculty of Medicine, Ankara, Turkey, Altındağ, Turkey

sections.Objectives: Advanced lung damage remains the primary cause of morbidity and mortality in cystic fibrosis (CF). While clinical care focuses on pulmonary stability, the impact of disease severity on quality of life (QoL) and self-care management in the paediatric advanced-stage cohort requires comprehensive evaluation, especially in the era of CFTR modulators.
sections.Methods: This cross-sectional study evaluated 15 paediatric patients (median age: 15.5 years, range: 11.2–19.0) with advanced lung disease (consecutive ppFEV1<50%) followed at Hacettepe University between 2015 and 2025. Evaluations included clinical data, body composition via bioelectrical impedance (fat-free mass [FFM], FFM index [FFMI]), respiratory muscle strength (maximal inspiratory/expiratory pressure [MIP/MEP]), exercise capacity (six-minute walk test [6MWT]), QoL (CFQ-R), and self-care management (SCMP-G).
sections.Results: Six patients (40%) received CFTR modulator therapy. The modulator group demonstrated significantly higher ppFEV1 compared to the non-modulator group (83% vs 35.4%; p=0.02) and a reduction in pulmonary exacerbations. Significant positive correlations were found between ppFEV1 and FFMI (r=0.651, p=0.009), MIP (r=0.532, p=0.041), and 6MWT distance (r=0.663, p=0.007). Higher CFQ-R digestive scores were strongly associated with FFM and muscle mass (r=0.647, p=0.009). While not statistically significant due to cohort size, the modulator group reported better self-care management (115.8 vs 99.1; p=0.068) and lower COPD Assessment Test (CAT) scores (11.1 vs 13.9). Socioeconomic status was positively associated with both QoL and self-care outcomes.
sections.Conclusion: CFTR modulators enhance clinical and psychosocial well-being even in advanced paediatric CF. Incorporating FFMI, respiratory muscle strength, and psychosocial assessments into routine care provides essential data for the multifaceted management of high-risk patients.

sections.Background: Using data from the Cystic Fibrosis Registry of Turkey(CFRT),we aimed to evaluate the effects of CFTR modulator therapies on respiratory support requirements and lung transplantation status in individuals with advanced lung disease.
sections.Methods: This retrospective,multicenter study analyzed CFRT data.Individuals of all ages with advanced respiratory disease,defined as a percent-predicted forced expiratory volume in 1 second (FEV₁) <40%,were included.Lung transplantation candidacy was determined based on FEV₁ values(<40% considered eligible for wait-listing; ≥40% considered delisted).Patients were grouped according to CFTR modulator use (≥3 months vs. non-users).Patients with missing FEV₁ data or who died during follow-up were excluded.
sections.Results: A total of 46 patients were included;19 received CFTR modulator therapy and 27 did not.Twelve patients in the non-modulator group died during follow-up and were excluded from further analysis.Baseline demographic and clinical characteristics were comparable between groups.Over two years,the modulator group showed a significant improvement in FEV₁(0.86 vs. 1.17; p=0.007),whereas no significant change was observed in the non-modulator group(0.86 vs. 1.07; p=0.272).No significant changes were observed in exacerbation rates,oxygen use,NIPPV use,or transplant status in the non-modulator group,while mortality increased significantly(p=0.001).In contrast,the modulator group demonstrated significant improvements in FEV₁% (p=0.023),discontinuation of oxygen therapy (p=0.025),and delisting from the lung transplant waiting list(p=0.014).
sections.Conclusion: CFTR modulator therapy was associated with improved lung function,reduced need for respiratory support,and higher rates of delisting from lung transplantation in patients with advanced CF lung disease,whereas non-modulator-treated patients showed increased mortality and no significant clinical improvement. sections.Objectives: LTx READY CF 2 is a fully remote, multicenter randomized controlled trial of a lung transplant education website that aimed to enroll populations underrepresented in CF research (goal >33% minoritized race/ethnicity, Medicaid insurance, or high school education or less). In this study we sought to understand demographics and reasons people with CF (PwCF) declined participation in LTx READY CF 2.
sections.Methods: During enrollment, all eligible PwCF (FEV1 <50%) at 11 US CF Centers were approached (universal screening) and reasons for declining participation were noted. Descriptive statistics were used to summarize demographics of both participants and eligible PwCF who declined participation. Reasons for declining were categorized using content analysis.
sections.Results: Of 187 PwCF approached; 132 enrolled and 55 declined. Among the 132 enrollees, 57 (43%) belonged to populations historically underrepresented in research: 9 (7%) identified as Black/African American, 10 (8%) as Hispanic ethnicity, 42 (32%) had Medicaid insurance, and 25 (19%) reported a high school education or less, some with more than one underrepresented identity. Of 55 PwCF who declined participation, 29 (53%) belong to underrepresented populations: 2 (4%) were Black/African American, 2 (4%) were Hispanic, 25 (45%) had Medicaid insurance, and 12 (22%) reported a high school education or less. Among those historically underrepresented versus well represented in research, reasons for declining participation included: time constraints (31% vs 53%, respectively), lack of interest in research in general (28% vs 12%), no interest in lung transplant (3% vs 12%), and lack of interest in the type of study (31% vs 23%).
sections.Conclusion: Targeted strategies in LTx READY CF 2 successfully enrolled PwCF from historically underrepresented populations. Among those who declined to enroll, lack of interest in research in general was more common among people who are historically underrepresented in clinical research.

sections.Objectives: Test whether a CF-specific lung transplant (LTx) education website, Take on Transplant (ToT), improves preparedness for LTx decisions compared to a general transplant website (unos.org).
sections.Methods: Multicenter, randomized controlled clinical trial comparing ToT to unos.org (ClinicalTrials.gov NCT06030206). Eleven US CF centers enrolled people with CF (PwCF) age ≥ 18 years, FEV1 ≤ 50% predicted, through Dec 2025. PwCF were randomized 1:1 to 3 months of ToT or unos.org, then 3 months of access to both websites. Questionnaires were obtained at baseline, 2 weeks, 3 months, and 6 months. The primary endpoint is an intention-to-treat assessment of the difference in mean Preparedness for Shared Decision Making (PrepDM) Scale between arms at 3 months. Participants also rated preparedness for LTx decisions on a Likert scale and completed the decisional conflict scale (DCS) at each visit. The DCS ranges from 0 [no decisional conflict] to 100 [extremely high decisional conflict], scores < 25 suggest readiness to decide.
sections.Results: Participants (n=132) were a mean of 41.4 years old (SD 13.0), 54 (41%) were men, 9 (7%) were Black race, and 10 (8%) were Hispanic. Twenty-five (19%) had a high school education or less and 42 (32%) had US public (Medicaid) insurance. At baseline, 17 (13%) had never discussed LTx with their CF doctor and 58 (44%) reported feeling not prepared, unsure, or only a little prepared to decide about LTx, but 121 (92%) said it was moderately or very important to feel prepared for LTx decisions. The mean baseline DCS was 39.1 (SD 26.3). The results of the primary (PrepDM) and key secondary analyses (preparedness for LTx decisions; DCS; LTx knowledge) will be available for the conference in June 2026.
sections.Conclusion: At baseline, there were moderate levels of decisional conflict and many participants reported feeling underprepared for LTx decisions. Results of the trial will reveal whether ToT is superior to unos.org in preparing PwCF for LTx decisions.

Pediatric Sleep Questionnaire (PSQ) scores, home sleep study data, growth parameters, and spirometry results were retrospectively evaluated. Results: Fifteen patients (60% male), aged 6-19 years, were included. The mean age was 13.6±4.3 years. The median body we...
presentations.authors_text: A.B. Cetinkaya1, T. Sismanlar Eyuboglu1, F.G. Ersan1, A.T. Aslan1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Gazi University Faculty of Medicine, Pediatric Pulmonology, Ankara, Turkey

sections.Objectives: CFTR modulator therapies can suppress chronic inflammation in patients with cystic fibrosis (CF), potentially leading to improvement in upper airway obstruction. This study aimed to compare sleep parameters before and during CFTR modulator therapy in patients with CF.
sections.Methods: Patients with CF, aged 2–19 years, followed in the Pediatric Pulmonology Clinic between 2023-2026 and treated with CFTR modulators for at least 3 months were included. Pediatric Sleep Questionnaire (PSQ) scores, home sleep study data, growth parameters, and spirometry results were retrospectively evaluated.
sections.Results: Fifteen patients (60% male), aged 6-19 years, were included. The mean age was 13.6±4.3 years. The median body weight Z-score was −0.96, height Z-score was −0.77, and body mass index Z-score was −1.23. Ten patients received elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor and five patients received ivacaftor. The median apnea–hypopnea index (AHI) decreased from 2.1 to 0.6 during treatment; however, this reduction did not statistically significant(p = 0.147). Obstructive apnea index (OAI), hypopnea index (HI), oxygen desaturation index (ODI) and mean oxygen saturation remained unchanged. The mean sweat chloride level decreased from 87.3 ± 14.2 mmol/L to 54.3 ± 12.1 mmol/L during modulator therapy(p=0.008). The mean FEV₁ Z-score improved from −1.5 ± 1.6 to −0.55 ± 1.1 during treatment(p = 0.028). The mean PSQ score decreased from 0.14 ± 0.35 to 0.03 ± 0.05 during treatment(p=0.109).
sections.Conclusion: Although the reduction in median AHI from 2.1 to 0.6 after CFTR modulator therapy was not statistically significant, a decrease in AHI to below 1 was considered clinically meaningful. The limited sample size and the absence of patients with moderate or severe sleep apnea prior to treatment may explain the lack of statistical significance. Nevertheless, the clinically relevant reduction in AHI suggests that further studies with larger patient cohorts may provide valuable contributions to the literature.

Papanikolaou" Hospital, Respiratory Failure Unit, Aristotle University of Thessaloniki, Thessaloniki, Greece, 3Hippokration General Hospital of Thessaloniki, ICU, Thessaloniki, Greece

sections.Objectives: Sleep disorders among people with cystic fibrosis (PwCF) appear to be an important comorbidity that is frequently underdiagnosed. The aim of this study is to evaluate sleep disorders in adult, stable PwCF and to correlate these disorders with their lung function, as well as other clinical parameters.
sections.Methods: Stable adult PwCF who are systematically examined in the Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre of the Respiratory Department of AUTH were included. The participants underwent pulmonary lung function testing, evaluation of their vital signs and 6 minute walking test. Also, they were asked to answer the following sleep related questionnaires: Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI), Epworth Sleepiness Scale (ESS) and Athens Insomnia Scale (AIS). They also answered the mMRC questionnaire, in order to evaluate their dyspnea. The Kerem questionnaire was also completed evaluating the quantity of cough during the day and the night.
sections.Results: 18 adult PwCF (13 men and 5 women) in a stable condition were included in this study. Their mean age was 30.17±7.21 years and their mean value of BMI was 21.36±2.95 kg/m2. As far as their respiratory function is concerned, the mean value of % predicted FEV1 and FVC was 58.93± 25.07 and 75.37± 26.14 respectively. The average score of ESS was 6.61±3.05, of PSQI 5.06±4.15 and of AIS 4.39±4.67. However, 38,8% of the perticipants reported an abnormal score at leat in one questionnaire. A statistically important negative correlation between SpO2% and PSQI score (p=0.03) was found. Furthermore, there was a statistically important positive correlation between nocturnal cough and AIS (p=0.008) and PSQI score (p=0.001). Finally, the score in the Kerem nighttime questionnaire differed importantly, depending on chronic infection with P.aeruginosa.
sections.Conclusion: Sleep disorders are common among PwCF and therefore, quality of sleep should be systematically evaluated. Lung function and nocturnal cough seem to correlate with sleep quality of PwCF.
