Methods: We reviewed the medical records of children with CF who underwent polysomnography (PSG) at the Hacettepe University Paediatric Pulmonology Sleep Laboratory between 01/01/2017 and 25/03/2025 due to snoring or hypoxia. Data included demo...

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a chronic multisystemic disease characterised by malnutrition and progressive lung disease. Sleep-related breathing disorders (SRBD) can negatively affect quality of life and disease progression. Data regarding SRBD prevalence in the paediatric population remain limited. This study retrospectively evaluates nocturnal oxygen requirements and the relationship between SRBD and pulmonary function test (PFT) parameters in children with CF.
sections.Methods: We reviewed the medical records of children with CF who underwent polysomnography (PSG) at the Hacettepe University Paediatric Pulmonology Sleep Laboratory between 01/01/2017 and 25/03/2025 due to snoring or hypoxia. Data included demographic characteristics, pulmonary function test (PFT) parameters (FEV1 z-score), genotypes, and PSG parameters.Correlation between the apnea-hypopnea index(AHI) and PFT results was examined.
sections.Results: Fifteen children with CF were included (median age 13.5 years; range 3–17.5 years). The median AHI was 0.7 (0-3.5). While 53.3% of patients had no sleep apnea, 46.7% exhibited mild obstructive sleep apnea.Median nocturnal oxygen saturation was 89% (83%–95%), and 73.3% of patients required nocturnal oxygen supplementation.Hypoventilation was not observed.Median FEV1 z-score was -6.87 (-8.75;2.78).No significant difference was found between children with and without sleep apnea regarding PFT parameters (p=1.000) or oxygen needs (p=0.87).Children with mild sleep apnea underwent PSG at a significantly younger age (median 10vs14.5 years;p=0.042).No significant correlation was found between AHI and PFT parameters (r=0.077; p=0.786).
sections.Conclusion: Mild sleep apnea was detected in nearly half of the children. High nocturnal oxygen requirements (73.3%) likely reflect the selection of patients with suspected respiratory failure. In this cohort, PFT results were not significantly associated with sleep apnea. Larger studies are needed to further elucidate these relationships.

presentations.title: Sleep quality in adults with cystic fibrosis: preliminary findings from the CF-Tracker study
presentations.summary: Background: Sleep disturbance is common in adults with cystic fibrosis (CF) and is likely multi-factorial. Both subjective and objective assessments demonstrate impaired sleep quality, but data is limited on which individuals with CF are more affected and how sleep relates to clinical parameters. Methods: Clinically stable adults with CF were recruited as part of the CF-Tracker study, an ongoing longitudinal observational study investigating and profiling pulmonary exacerbations. Subjective sleep quality was assessed using the Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI; good is ≤5). Objective sleep was measured at home over one month using wrist actigraphy. sections.Background: Sleep disturbance is common in adults with cystic fibrosis (CF) and is likely multi-factorial. Both subjective and objective assessments demonstrate impaired sleep quality, but data is limited on which individuals with CF are more affected and how sleep relates to clinical parameters.
sections.Methods: Clinically stable adults with CF were recruited as part of the CF-Tracker study, an ongoing longitudinal observational study investigating and profiling pulmonary exacerbations. Subjective sleep quality was assessed using the Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI; good is ≤5). Objective sleep was measured at home over one month using wrist actigraphy.
sections.Results: Data are available from the first 44 study participants; 25 (57%) were female, median age of 39 years (range 20-77). Median FEV1 (FEV1pp) was 59.7% predicted (range 25.2-103.7). Subjective sleep quality was impaired, with a median global PSQI score of 6 (range 1-14); 23 (52%) reported PSQI > 6. The median PSQI sleep efficiency component score was 1 (75-84% sleep efficiency). No statistically significant associations were observed between PSQI score and sex (Mann–Whitney U test), age, BMI, FEV1pp, or reported breathlessness (Spearman’s rank correlation). To date 11 participants have completed actigraphy monitoring for mean duration of 30 days (IQR range 14-43). Mean sleep duration was 6.9 hours per night, and median actigraphy-derived sleep efficiency was 85.3% (IQR range 74.9-90.4%).
sections.Conclusion: Adults with CF report impaired subjective sleep quality, with objective measures indicating reduced sleep duration and efficiency. Ongoing recruitment and longitudinal follow-up within CF-Tracker will enable evaluation of relationships between sleep, disease severity, and pulmonary exacerbations. Funding: LifeArc/Cystic Fibrosis Trust Innovation Hub Network.

sections.Objectives: 2789+5G>A variant is reported most frequently in Türkiye among European countries in ECFSPR 2023 annual data report.This study aimed to evaluate the demographic and clinical characteristics of patients carrying the 2789+5G>A variant registered in the CF Registry of Türkiye (CFRT) .
sections.Methods: 2024 annual data of patients carrying 2789+5G>A variant were retrospectively analyzed within this cohort. Findings were compared with 282 patients homozygous for the F508del variant registered in the same database.
sections.Results: 96 patients carrying 2789+5G>A variant (median age:10.37 years) were included.Compared with F508del homozygotes,patients with 2789+5G>A were diagnosed at a later age (median 0.33 vs 0.25 years;p<0.0001).Nutritional status was significantly better in the 2789+5G>A group,with higher BMI z-scores in patients under 18years (median 0.14vs−0.70; p<0.0001). Lung function was also superior (median FEV1:94.5%vs90.0%;p=0.044),and sweat chloride levels were significantly lower (84.76±23.37 vs 92.44±22.49 mmol/L; p=0.005). F508del homozygotes experienced more pulmonary exacerbations(19.1%vs9.4%;p=0.026) and had higher rates of Pseudomonas aeruginosa colonization (29.1%vs18.7%; p=0.044). Pancreatic sufficiency was significantly more common in 2789+5G>A patients (p<0.0001); also, acute pancreatitis occurred more frequently in this group as expected (2.1%vs0%; p=0.012). CF-related diabetes (11% vs 1%) and liver disease (34.7% vs 9.4% )were significantly more prevalent in F508del homozygotes (p<0.0001).
sections.Conclusion: The 2789+5G>A variant is associated with a milder CF phenotype, including later diagnosis, better nutritional status, and preserved lung function. Despite lower therapeutic burden and higher pancreatic sufficiency,these patients have an increased risk of acute pancreatitis, highlighting the need for variant-specific clinical follow-up.

presentations.authors_text: J. Rodman Berlot1,2, Z. Jagodic1, B. Salobir3, S. Škrgat3,2, U. Krivec1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University Children's Hospital Ljubljana, Department of Paediatric Pulmonology, Ljubljana, Slovenia, 2Faculty of Medicine, University of Ljubljana, Ljubljana, Slovenia, 3University Medical Centre Ljubljana, Department of Pulmonary Diseases and Allergy, Ljubljana, Slovenia

sections.Objectives: The transition from paediatric to adult cystic fibrosis (CF) care is a vulnerable period associated with decreased adherence and clinical deterioration. During the initial rollout of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) in Slovenia, a temporary strategy was implemented whereby adolescents approaching transition received ETI initiation within their established paediatric team to preserve therapeutic relationships.
sections.Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted on adolescents who received ETI initiation in paediatric care prior to transition between 2021 and 2024. We analysed ppFEV₁ and BMI one year before transition, during the transition year, and one year post- transition. The Friedman test was employed for three-group comparisons, and the Wilcoxon signed-rank test for two-group comparisons. Adherence to therapy and pregnancy outcomes were documented.
sections.Results: Thirteen young adults with CF (62% female) were included in the study. Mean age at transition was 22.8±1.7 years. No significant changes were observed in the median ppFEV₁ (98 before vs 90 during vs 87 % after transition; p=0.679) or median BMI (21.7 vs 21.7 vs 22.0 kg/m2; p=0.739). The change in ppFEV₁ before and after transition was not significant (2 vs 0 %; p=0.814). ETI adherence remained excellent, consistent with real- world adolescent data. Three of eight females (38%) became pregnant during the transition year (one planned, two unplanned).
sections.Conclusion: Initiating ETI in adolescents whilst under paediatric care facilitated a smooth transition without clinical deterioration. High adherence likely reflected the preservation of therapeutic relationships. The 38% pregnancy rate highlights the urgent need for earlier reproductive health counselling in adolescents receiving ETI, as pregnancy rates have increased substantially in the ETI era. Family planning discussions should become routine in adolescent CF care and transition preparation, ideally beginning well before the transition occurs.

We aimed to evaluate the clinical and genetic features of CFSPID in patients followed at our center under the IRT/IRT protocol. Methods: CFSPID patients followed between 2017 and 2025 were retrospectively reviewed. Age, sex, clinical findings, sweat chloride, CFTR genetics, and pancreatic status were recorded. Results: Nineteen patients were included, of whom 12 (63.2%) were female. The mean age was 12±11.2 (1–56) months and the mean sweat chloride was 38.1±7 mmol/L (20.6–58.2). No patient had pseudo-Bartter syndrome, panc...
presentations.authors_text: T. Unlu1, T. Sismanlar Eyuboglu1, A.T. Aslan1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Gazi University Medicine Faculty, Pediatric Pulmonology, Yenimahalle, Turkey

sections.Methods: CFSPID patients followed between 2017 and 2025 were retrospectively reviewed. Age, sex, clinical findings, sweat chloride, CFTR genetics, and pancreatic status were recorded.
sections.Results: Nineteen patients were included, of whom 12 (63.2%) were female. The mean age was 12±11.2 (1–56) months and the mean sweat chloride was 38.1±7 mmol/L (20.6–58.2). No patient had pseudo-Bartter syndrome, pancreatic insufficiency, or additional clinical findings. The mean follow-up time was 12.4±11.8 (2–48) months. The mean daily weight gain at presentation was 29.8±5.6 (21–39) g/day, and growth percentiles remained normal. Six patients had no CFTR variant. Nine had one variant (3 CF-causing, 1 CFTR-related disorder–causing, 2 VUS, 2 non–disease-causing, 1 not in databases) and four had two variants (2 VUS/non–disease-causing, 1 non–disease-causing/non–disease-causing, 1 CFTR-related disorder–causing/not in databases). The mean follow-up duration was 16.41±18.13 (1–60) months. During follow-up, 2 patients (10.5%) could not be classified because their variants were not found in the database, whereas 17 patients (89.4%) continued to be followed with a diagnosis of KFKOT.
sections.Conclusion: Most CFSPID patients remain asymptomatic; therefore, longitudinal follow-up is crucial to allow timely identification of clinically significant manifestations while minimizing unnecessary medical interventions.

A survey was also sent out to assess the confidence patients and families o...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Wirral University Teaching Trust, Paediatrics, Wirral, United Kingdom, 2Wirral University Teaching Hospital, Paediatrics, Wirral, United Kingdom, 3Wirral University Trust Hospital, Wirral, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Local paediatric DGH hospital in the North west caring for 34 children with cystic fibrosis. Most of the care for these children is done by the specific CF team, however out of hours the on-call general paediatric team are responsible for them. Incidents occurred where out of hours prescribers did not alter the dosing for antibiotics when the patient was on modulator therapy. This project aims to improve the prescribing in out-of-hour care for patients with CF.
sections.Methods: A survey of non-CF specialist resident doctors, nurse practitioners and consultants was carrier out to assess confidence in prescribing for patients with CF. A survey was also sent out to assess the confidence patients and families of those with CF had in the prescribers out-of-hours.
sections.Results: 13 prescribers responded with 76% being aware of modulator therapy. Confidence in prescribing for children on modulator therapy was low with a median result of 3.5/10 and 38% of prescribers reporting 0 or 1/10 for confidence. No prescribers felt confident in altering doses of modulator therapy. 17 patients responded with 23% being unaware of modulator therapy interacting with commonly used medications. All reported were happy to carry alert cards to improve awareness of modulator therapy and the impact this can have on other medications. Outcome: A teaching program has been implemented in department to update the non-CF doctors and prescribers. A repeat survey following this is expected to show improved confidence in prescribing. A medication alert card has been issued to all children on modulator therapy with cystic fibrosis to emphasize the need for consideration in prescribing additional medication.
sections.Conclusion: Care for CF patients remains largely managed by specialist CF teams however in emergency and out-of -hours situations non-specialist prescribers need to be aware of the impact of their prescribing. Education and alert cards can improve the confidence of both prescribers and families.

sections.Objectives: To present the regional experience of the Republic of Tatarstan in the use of ivacaftor+tezacaftor+elexacaftor in children with cystic fibrosis (CF) with a change in the brand name of the drug.
sections.Methods: Safety and efficacy of the therapy in 52 CF patients were monitored 14 days after the start of therapy, 1 month, 3 months, 6 months and 12 months.
sections.Results: A comprehensive assessment of the child's functional capacity was conducted before initiating and when changing triple therapy. During the triple targeted therapy, patients showed improvement in respiratory function parameters (FEV1 and FVC), as well as positive weight and height dynamics. When changing drugs within the same international nonproprietary name (INN), respiratory function parameters did not decrease. Three patients lost weight during the first 14 days, but after 3 months of treatment, all children gained weight. During the 6-month follow-up period, there were no CF exacerbations requiring hospitalization. During the assessment at 6 months. When changing therapy within the same INN, a local decrease in lens transparency was detected in 1 patient.
sections.Conclusion: Observation of children receiving triple therapy ivacaftor+tezacaftor+elexacaftor showed a good safety profile. Changing the drug within the same INN does not reduce the clinical efficacy of triple therapy; there was no significant increase in the incidence of adverse reactions in patients.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Hacettepe İhsan Doğramacı Children Hospital, Pediatric Pulmonology Department, Ankara, Turkey

sections.Objectives: Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Regulator Protein-Related Disorder (CFTR-RD) is a clinical entity that results from dysfunction of the Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Regulator (CFTR) protein but does not fully meet the diagnostic criteria for Cystic Fibrosis (CF).
sections.Methods: In this study, we retrospectively reviewed the data of 21 pediatric patients diagnosed with CFTR-RD who were followed up at Hacettepe İhsan Doğramacı Children's Hospital.
sections.Results: Regarding the presentation characteristics of the patients: 4 (19%) presented with elevated immunoreactive trypsinogen (IRT) levels on newborn screening, 4 (19%) presented with recurrent pancreatitis, 9 presented with pseudobartter syndrome (PBS) (42.9%), 4 presented with respiratory complaints such as chronic cough and pneumonia (19%), and 1 patient presented because their sibling had been diagnosed with CFTR-RD (4.8%). The mean sweat test result in the diagnosis was 38.81 ± 14.1 mmol/L. Aquagenic palmoplantar keratoderma (APK) testing was performed in 12 patients (57.1%), and the test was positive in 5 of these patients (41.6%).
sections.Conclusion: Re-evaluation of patients, particularly those with intermediate sweat test results, based on clinical diagnostic criteria and databases is crucial for confirming this diagnosis. This is important because CFTR modulators are not indicated for treatment in CFTR-RD group. Pseudo-Bartter syndrome (PBS) was observed in 42.9% of our cohort; although PBS is not currently included among the universally accepted clinical diagnostic criteria for CFTR-RD, recent studies have demonstrated that variant analyses in such patients are consistent with CFTR-RD, moreover, physiological evidence supports that this subgroup should also be considered within the CFTR-RD spectrum.

Patients using systemic corticosteroids and smokers were excluded. Cardiovascular parameters were assessed using the Mobil-O-Graph®, which non-invasively estimates central vascular and hemodynamic parameters. The augmentation index adjusted to a heart rate of 75 bpm (AIx@75) was calculated as the pressure difference between the peak of the reflected wave (P2) and the...
presentations.authors_text: B. Pessoa1, M. de Fuccio2, I. Carvalho-Ribeiro1, A. Guedes1, I. Vasconcelos1, R. Borges1, M. Velloso3, M.d.G. Rodrigues-Machado1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Faculdade Ciências Médicas, Belo Horizonte, Brazil, 2Júlia Kubitschek Hospital, Belo Horizonte, Brazil, 3Universidade Federal de Minas Gerais, Belo Horizonte, Brazil

sections.Objectives: To compare arterial stiffness, arterial compliance and vascular resistance indices between adults with cystic fibrosis (CF) and age and sex matched healthy controls.
sections.Methods: This was a controlled, cross-sectional observational study involving clinically stable adults with a confirmed diagnosis of CF and no known history of cardiopathy. Patients using systemic corticosteroids and smokers were excluded. Cardiovascular parameters were assessed using the Mobil-O-Graph®, which non-invasively estimates central vascular and hemodynamic parameters. The augmentation index adjusted to a heart rate of 75 bpm (AIx@75) was calculated as the pressure difference between the peak of the reflected wave (P2) and the peak of the incident wave (P1), expressed as a percentage of central pulse pressure (PP) [(AIx@75 = [P2–P1]/cPP × 100)]. Arterial compliance (stroke volume index/PP) and vascular resistance (PP/stroke volume index) were derived from hemodynamic parameters.
sections.Results: A total of 82 adults with CF and 82 controls were evaluated, with mean ages of 31.09±11.74 and 31.21±11.59 years, respectively. AIx@75 (29.11±10.22 vs. 18.48±7.93%, p<0.0001), reflection coefficient (62.44±6.54 vs. 57.71±6.24, p<0.0001), and augmentation pressure (6.52±2.89 vs. 5.30±2.39 mmHg, p=0.0045) were significantly higher in the CF group compared with controls. Central and peripheral vascular compliance were significantly lower in the CF group (1.16±0.29 and 0.84±0.21 mL/m²/mmHg) compared with controls (1.29±0.30 and 0.92±0.23 mL/m²/mmHg). Central and peripheral vascular resistance were significantly higher in the CF group (0.91±0.22 and 1.25±0.31 mmHg/ml/m2) compared with controls (0.82±0.24 and 1.14±0.28 mmHg/ml/m2).
sections.Conclusion: CF in adults is associated with central and peripheral vascular alterations consistent with accelerated vascular aging. These findings indicate an increased cardiovascular risk and reinforce the need for early cardiovascular screening in this population.

It is done by climbing up and down a step board in an gradually increasing velocity to detect cardiopulmonary performance It has previously been shown to be applicable in a pediatric population in our clinic, however the previous work has not been applied within the required standards, and did not investigate its correlation with muscle strength, other field tests and quality of life(QoL). This study has applied A-STEP as intended and aims to investigate its correlation with these parameters. Methods: Children and adults above the age of 10 diagnosed with stable CF who were followed up i...
presentations.authors_text: T. Sözer1, O. Kenis Coskun1, B. Yıldız1, C. Bilekyiğit Kurt1, E. Karadağ Saygı1, P. Ergenekon2, E. Erdem Eralp2, Y. Gökdemir2, B. Karadağ2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Marmara University, Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation Department, Istanbul, Turkey, 2Marmara University, Pediatric Pulmonology, Istanbul, Turkey

sections.Objectives: A-STEP is a novel exercise test used to monitor the performance of patients diagnosed with cystic fibrosis (CF). It is done by climbing up and down a step board in an gradually increasing velocity to detect cardiopulmonary performance It has previously been shown to be applicable in a pediatric population in our clinic, however the previous work has not been applied within the required standards, and did not investigate its correlation with muscle strength, other field tests and quality of life(QoL). This study has applied A-STEP as intended and aims to investigate its correlation with these parameters.
sections.Methods: Children and adults above the age of 10 diagnosed with stable CF who were followed up in our center were tested with the help of a metronome, step board and appropriate tracking devices. Reaching level 9 on the Borg scale was considered to be the achievement of at least one maximum effort criterion. Spearman correlation analyzes were used to ensure compatibility of A-step test results with clinical parameters such as FEV1, quality of life measured with AWESCORE test, performance parameters such as 6 minute walk test (6MWT) and biceps curl and gluteus and quadriceps strength as measured by hand-held dynamometer.
sections.Results: A total of 40 patients were included in this study. The average age of the patients was 17.20 (SD: 5.21) (minimum 11 - maximum 33) and their average FEV1 level was 80.90 (SD: 20.43) (minimum 30 - maximum 122). Median A-STEP level of the patients was 7 (IQR: 4.25-8). Only 2 patients have reached the maximum level. The A-STEP levels of the patients showed a significant correlation with 6MWT (rho=0.49, p=0.001), biceps curl (rho=0.53 p=0.001), FEV1 levels (rho=0.41 p=0.008) and AWESCORE (rho=0.39 p=0.13). No correlations were found with muscle strength.
sections.Conclusion: A-STEP test is applicable in pwCF above the age of 10 and shows medium correlation with other performance parameters, clinical parameters and quality of life.

presentations.title: Standardized exercise testing in the cystic fibrosis (CF) clinic – feasibility and relevance

sections.Objectives: Routine assessment of physical fitness is recommended for people with cystic fibrosis (PwCF). The objective of this retrospective single center study was to describe feasibility and impact of a simple incremental cycle ergometry test in outpatient care.
sections.Methods: Between 01.01.2025 and 31.12.2025 77 PwCF (36 female, 41 male, age 21±11 [7-55] years, FEV1 92±21 [40-129] %predicted) completed a standardized incremental cycle ergometry test (ErgoSelelct 900, Ergoline) following the protocol of the ERS (Radtke et al. ERR 2019) and using work rate increments proposed by Godfrey et al. (Clin Sci 1971). All tests were performed during routine outpatient visits in the clinic room. In 67 PwCF corresponding measures of body composition analyses (InBody 770) were available.
sections.Results: Exercise testing was feasible during routine outpatient visits in all PwCF and required only 15 min of extra time per test. FEV1 %predicted (FEV1%), peak heart rate, minimal oxygen saturation (SpO2) and peak work rate expressed as %predicted (Wpeak%, Hebestreit et al. Respiration 2015) did not differ between male and female PwCF. Average Wmax% of the 77 PwCF was 98.1±18.4% and did not correlate with age, sex, peak heart rate or minimal SpO2. Seven PwCF (five female) had a Wpeak% ≤73%. These seven PwCF did not differ – as a group – from the more fit PwCF in FEV1%, peak heart rate and minimal SpO2, but had a higher percentage of body fat (%BF). However, three PwCF with poor fitness were among those with lowest FEV1% and one male had the highest %BF of all male PwCF.
sections.Conclusion: A cycle ergometry test is easily implemented in the CF clinic routine and confirmed that most PwCF at our center have a good to excellent aerobic fitness. However, seven PwCF had a Wpeak% ≤73% associated with a decreased 10-year survival (Hebestreit et al., AJRCCM 2019), some of whom unexpectedly. Thus, the test is informative indicating additional work-up and targeted care for these seven PwCF.

presentations.authors_text: R. Fortune1, B. Morrow1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Cape Town, Department of Paediatrics and Child Health, Cape Town, South Africa

sections.Objectives: Physical activity (PA) is an essential component of cystic fibrosis (CF) management, yet PA levels and exercise capacity in African paediatric CF populations are largely unexplored. This study aimed to describe PA levels, muscle strength, and exercise tolerance in South African children and adolescents with CF.
sections.Methods: A cross-sectional study involved clinically stable children and adolescents with CF aged 7-18 years. All participants completed modified Physical Activity Questionnaires for children and adolescents (PAQ-CA), handgrip dynamometry, and the 10-meter 25-level modified shuttle test (MST-25). Clinical data including lung function (FEV₁% predicted), genotype, BMI, and Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Conductance Regulator (CFTR) modulator use were extracted from participant’s medical records. Descriptive statistics and correlation analyses were performed.
sections.Results: Thirty participants (age 12.4±3.1 years; 53.3% male; FEV₁% 83.6±20.2%) were enrolled. Mean PAQ-CA score was 2.92±0.76, indicating low to moderate PA levels. Mean handgrip strength was 16.35 ± 5.73 kg, below age-predicted norms for healthy children. Mean MST-25 distance was 400±117.6 metres (range 150 -560), substantially lower than reported MST values in other CF paediatric cohorts. Self-reported PA levels (r=0.52; p=0.003), FEV₁ (r=0.44; p=0.02), and combined handgrip strength were significantly associated with MST-25 distance (r=0.55, p=0.002). Participants receiving CFTR modulator therapy (40%) demonstrated better exercise tolerance (432.50±108.40 vs 381.10±123.40 metres on MST-25).
sections.Conclusion: South African children and adolescents with CF demonstrate low to moderate PA levels, reduced muscle strength, and impaired exercise tolerance compared to published data and healthy norms. These findings highlight the importance of structured exercise assessments and individualized exercise prescription to improve outcomes in resource-limited CF care settings.

presentations.authors_text: I. Waller1, C. Fordyce1, R. Bright-Thomas1, J. Mitchell1, P. Barry1, A. Horsley1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Manchester Foundation Trust, Cystic Fibrosis, Manchester, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Lung Clearance Index (LCI) is now a routine test in many CF paediatric clinics, but use in adults to monitor early disease progression is more limited. It has added potential to detect and follow lung disease in those with CFTR-related disorders. Implementation requires MBW equipment, trained staff, clinical buy-in, and effective clinic integration.
sections.Objectives: To summarise clinical data and practical experience since implementing LCI testing in a routine clinic at a large adult CF centre.
sections.Results: 31 Adults with CF (mean age 35yrs, M:F 18:13) have completed at least one LCI assessment. Mean FEV1 was 94% predicted (range 76-131%). In 2 cases (6%), reliable LCI measurement could not be obtained. Median session time was 25mins (IQR 20-29 mins, n=20). Mean LCI was 8.0 (range 5.7-14.1), mean LCI z score was 2.5 (range -1.9 to 10.6). 17 adults (55%) had elevated LCI (z score >2), compared to 39% with FEV1 <90% predicted.CFTR-RD patients (n=8) had a mean LCI 7.1 (mean z score 1.5, n=7). Mean FEV1 was 97% predicted. All CFTR-RD had normal LCI except for a single patient (LCI z score 3.8) with a known diagnosis of asthma.
sections.Conclusion: LCI testing is feasible in routine clinics with the right infrastructure and training. Allowance must be made by staff and patients for an additional 30mins for test completion. Challenges include equipment, clinic structure, and the availability of trained testing staff. CFTR-RD patients have predominantly normal LCI. Data collection is ongoing to define this population further, but LCI could provide a sensitive and practical method of tracking longer term changes.

Methods: Following PRISMA scoping review methodology, we included studies assessing BH and PA or providing PA interventions in pwC...
presentations.authors_text: Z. A. Alharbi1,2, D. Vlachopoulos1, M. Bianchim3, C. A. Williams1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Children's Health and Exercise Research Centre , University of Exeter, Faculty of Health and Life Sciences, Exeter, United Kingdom, 2Jouf University, Faculty of Applied Medical Sciences, Al Qurayyat, Saudi Arabia, 3Bangor University, School of Health Sciences, Bangor, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: With modulator therapy, People With Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF) live longer but face increasing age-related comorbidities. Osteoporosis is multifactorial and increasingly prevalent in pwCF, with risk factors including reduced physical activity (PA). PA is part of routine care; yet remains inconsistent and poorly characterised. Previous reviews show PA's favourable effect on health outcomes, but evidence examining PA's role in bone health (BH) among pwCF is scarce. We aimed to map existing literature on PA's role in BH, assess measurement methods, and identify knowledge gaps.
sections.Methods: Following PRISMA scoping review methodology, we included studies assessing BH and PA or providing PA interventions in pwCF (children and adults), excluding reviews, and animal studies. We systematically searched ten databases using a set of keywords. Two independent reviewers checked eligibility and extracted data.
sections.Results: From 3,662 references, 29 studies were included (24 cross-sectional, four longitudinal and one interventional). PA assessment was heterogeneous and included: questionnaires (n=18), interviews (n=2), diary (n=1), combined methods (n=4), and device-based measures (n=4). Studies assessed BH using dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry, accompanied by computed tomography–based imaging (n=5), quantitative ultrasound, single X-ray absorptiometry (n=1 each), and bone biomarkers (n=13). PA–BH associations were reported in 23 studies: no significant associations were found in children (n=7); six of 12 studies reported positive associations in young adults/adults; and two of four studies reported positive associations in mixed-age samples.
sections.Conclusion: Evidence shows potential benefits of PA for BH in adults with CF, but conclusions are constrained by an over-reliance on self-reported PA, especially in children. Evidence for PA's role in CF bone health is limited and equivocal in adults. More robust studies with comprehensive PA and BH assessments are needed.

presentations.authors_text: L. Lechtenberg1, W. Gruber2, F. Stehling2, J.-S. Schermaul1, J.G. Ortiz1, C. Taube1, M. Welsner1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University Hospital Essen - Ruhrlandklinik, Department of Pneumology, Essen, Germany, 2University of Duisburg-Essen, Pediatric Pulmonology and Sleep Medicine, Essen, Germany

sections.Objectives: To asses enjoyment of physical activity and motivation in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) along with socioeconomic variables.
sections.Methods: The study was investigator initiated and conducted in a cross-sectional design at the Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre at the Ruhrlandklinik Hospital in Essen, Germany. During routine out-patient visits, we recruited pwCF aged ≥ 18 years with a genetically confirmed diagnosis of Cystic Fibrosis. Informed written consent was obtained. We assessed forms of motivation and enjoyment using the Physical Activity Enjoyment Scale (PACES). The participants were also asked to provide information on marital status, level of education, job qualification and employment situation. Data was assessed for normality and non parametric tests were performed.
sections.Results: Group wise comparisons showed significant differences between pwCF of differing levels of education and job qualification (total n=173). PwCF with university qualification scored lower in amotivation (p=0.03) and higher in identified (p=0.5) and intrinsic (p=0.02) regulation as well as relative autonomy index (p=0.41). With regards to job qualifcation, PwCF with university degrees scored lower in amotivation (p=0.48) with a higher relative autonomy index (p=0.41) compared to pwCF with finished job training. There were no significant differences between pwCF grouped by marital status or employment situation.
sections.Conclusion: There were significant differences in enjoyment of physical activity between pwCF grouped by socioeconomic factors. In our sample, higher levels of education and higher job qualification were significantly associated with enjoyment of physical activity and forms of motivation associated with sustained physical activity. Further studies are required to assess the effects of socioeconomic factors on health outcomes in pwCF, specifically as mediated by physical activity.

sections.Objectives: A retrospective analysis of CPET in three CF patients, either homozygous or heterozygous for a CFTR mutation, with normal performance on 6MWT.
sections.Methods: All patients underwent CPET using a cycle ergometer and spirometry in accordance with ERS/ATS recommendations, as well as the 6MWT in accordance with ATS guidelines, before and after initiation of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI).
sections.Results: Three CF patients receiving optimised standard therapy, excluding CFTR modulators, demonstrated normal 6MWT results despite obstruction in spirometry and bronchiectasis and mucus plugging on chest imaging. All patients exhibited reduced aerobic capacity due to pulmonary limitation, characterised by dynamic hyperinflation (a reduction in inspiratory capacity greater than 150 mL) and expiratory flow limitation, with tidal volume enveloping the expiratory flow–volume loop. Once ETI became available in the Brazilian public health system, all three patients initiated treatment. CPET was repeated after a mean follow-up of 12 months, demonstrating significant improvement in aerobic capacity, as evidenced by increases in peak oxygen uptake (VO₂ peak) and maximal work rate (WR). However, dynamic hyperinflation and expiratory flow limitation persisted. Table 1. Spirometry, 6MWT, and CPET parameters before and after ETI. sections.Conclusion: CPET may be useful when the 6MWT yields normal results and is likely indicated for assessing the response to ETI therapy.

presentations.authors_text: E. Baran1, E. Zattera1, S. Gastal1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Hospital Rossi, La Plata, Argentina

sections.Objectives: To evaluate the correlation between functional capacity (assessed via the 1-minute Sit-to-Stand Test (STST)) and lung function parameters (absolute FEV1 and FEV1%) in a cohort of patients with Cystic Fibrosis (CF), determining if this field test serves as a reliable predictor.
sections.Methods: A cross-sectional descriptive study was conducted analyzing data from 40 adults patients. The following variables were recorded: lung function (absolute FEV1 (L) and FEV1 percentage (FEV1%) predicted; functional capacity (total number of repetitions achieved in the STST. Statical analysis: Pearson correlation coefficient (r) was used to determine the strength of the association between variables.
sections.Results: A moderate positive correlation (r=0.55) was found between VEF1% vs STST. Patients with higher FEV1% values generally demonstrated better performance in the STST. It was observed that patients with severe FEV1% (< 30%) did not exceed 20 repetitions, whereas those with normal lung function frequently performed above 25 repetitions. STST repetitions ranged from 16 to 41. We identified that muscular strength (STST) does not always follow a strictly linear relationship with FEV1, suggesting that other factors—such as nutritional status or peripheral muscle conditioning—significantly influence functional capacity.
sections.Conclusion: There is a significant correlation between FEV1% and performance in the STST. These results suggest that the STST is a valuable, low-cost, and easy-to-implement clinical tool for monitoring functional capacity in CF patients. However, the observed variability indicates that functional assessment should complement spirometry, as some patients maintain relatively high muscle strength despite moderate declines in lung function.

We compared absolute measures of muscle function and body composition in awCF receiving modulator therapy with age-, sex- and BMI-matched healthy controls (HC). Methods: Fifteen male awCF and nine HC were assessed. Maximal hand grip strength and isometric knee extensor strength were measured using dynamometry. Whole-body fat mass (FM) and fat-free mass (FFM) were quantified by dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry. Functional capacity was assessed using 6...
presentations.authors_text: B. Griffiths1, I. Clifton2, R. Watson2, G. Sploletini2, C. Webster2, H. Saumtally2, D. Peckham2,3, O. Price1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Leeds, School of Biomedical Sciences, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Leeds, United Kingdom, 2St James's University Hospital, Leeds Adult Cystic Fibrosis Unit, Leeds, United Kingdom, 3University of Leeds, Leeds Institute of Medical Research, Leeds, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Highly effective CFTR modulator therapy has improved lung function and body mass in adults with cystic fibrosis (awCF). However, whether these improvements translate into preserved skeletal muscle mass, strength and functional capacity remains unclear. We compared absolute measures of muscle function and body composition in awCF receiving modulator therapy with age-, sex- and BMI-matched healthy controls (HC).
sections.Methods: Fifteen male awCF and nine HC were assessed. Maximal hand grip strength and isometric knee extensor strength were measured using dynamometry. Whole-body fat mass (FM) and fat-free mass (FFM) were quantified by dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry. Functional capacity was assessed using 6-min walk distance (6MWD). Data are presented as median (IQR) and compared using Mann–Whitney U tests.
sections.Results: Despite similar age (awCF: 38 [34–44] vs. HC: 34 [31–37] years, P=0.074) and BMI (25.5 [23.8–27.6] vs. 24.8 [23.2–26.0] kg·m-2, P=0.420), awCF had lower FEV₁ (%predicted) than HC (70 [57–80] vs. 109 [101–119], P<0.001). Hand grip strength was reduced in awCF (43.0 [35.5–47.5] vs. 51.0 [44.0–58.5] kg, P=0.014), with numerically lower knee extensor strength that did not reach statistical significance (117 [85–142] vs. 132 [115–177] Nm, P=0.057). awCF exhibited lower FFM (53.6 [48.3–61.8] vs. 61.6 [60.9–65.9] kg, P=0.025), with trends toward higher FM. Functional capacity was reduced in awCF, evidenced by lower 6MWD (657 [627–687] vs. 775 [693–834] m, P=0.004), despite comparable free-living physical activity between groups.
sections.Conclusion: In the post-modulator era, awCF demonstrate reduced absolute muscle mass, strength and functional capacity compared with BMI-matched HC. Preserved BMI may therefore mask clinically relevant deficits in muscle quantity and force-generating capacity, supporting the rationale for targeted non-pharmacological interventions to optimise musculoskeletal health as the CF population ages.

presentations.summary: Objectives: Handgrip Strength (HGS) is an assessment of forearm grip strength and is often used alongside a nutritional assessment, with lower results often linking to negative health outcomes, whilst also acting as a proxy for exercise function and musculoskeletal health. As part of a Cystic Fibrosis (CF) patients annual review, HGS was introduced alongside other regular exercise tests. This study sought to assess whether there is a relationship between HGS, Pulmonary Function Tests (PFT), Body Mass Index (BMI) and Peak Power Output (PPO). Methods: Forty-nine adult patients with CF (PwCF) (19 females, 30 males) with a mean (SD) age of 36.4 ± 13.1 years, and FEV1 of 3.09 ± 1.15 L (82 ± 22%) performed a HGS tes...
presentations.authors_text: T. Kent1, G. Walker1, C. Williams1,2, L. Dobson1, P. Mitchelmore1,2, O. Tomlinson1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Royal Devon University Healthcare NHS Foundation Trust Hospital, Cystic Fibrosis, Exeter, United Kingdom, 2University of Exeter Medical School, Faculty of Health and life Science, Faculty of Health and life Science, Exeter, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Handgrip Strength (HGS) is an assessment of forearm grip strength and is often used alongside a nutritional assessment, with lower results often linking to negative health outcomes, whilst also acting as a proxy for exercise function and musculoskeletal health. As part of a Cystic Fibrosis (CF) patients annual review, HGS was introduced alongside other regular exercise tests. This study sought to assess whether there is a relationship between HGS, Pulmonary Function Tests (PFT), Body Mass Index (BMI) and Peak Power Output (PPO).
sections.Methods: Forty-nine adult patients with CF (PwCF) (19 females, 30 males) with a mean (SD) age of 36.4 ± 13.1 years, and FEV1 of 3.09 ± 1.15 L (82 ± 22%) performed a HGS test on a Jamar Plus handheld dynamometer, completing a total of six tests (three on left hand, three on right hand). The best result from each patient was then correlated against PFT, BMI and PPO using Pearson's correlation coefficients.
sections.Results: Mean maximal HGS was 42.4 ± 14.2 kg (99.6 ± 22.2 %) ranging from 19.0 – 79.6 kg (52 – 145%). Correlations between absolute HGS and PFT were significant (r = 0.45, p < 0.01) as were HGS and PPO (r = 0.59, p < 0.01). When correlating HGS against BMI data, there was not a significant association (r = -0.00, p = 0.98) however, HGS was correlated against body weight (BW) (r = 0.30, p < 0.05).
sections.Conclusion: The significant correlation between HGS, FEV1 and PPO, indicate its use as a proxy for disease status and exercise function. It also, however, calls into question the validity of HGS for use as a nutritional assessment tool in PwCF given varied associations between HGS, BMI and BW.

presentations.summary: Objective: Exercise is a well-established therapy for children with cystic fibrosis (CF). This study aims to compare substrate oxidation during exercise in children with CF who were short-term and long-term users of ETI. Methods: Children aged 8 to 17 y, were divided into short-term (G1) or long-term (G2) ETI therapy groups using a median split of ETI duration. Basic anthropometric measurements were collected. Aerobic fitness, defined as the highest 20-sec oxygen uptake (VO2peak), was measured on a treadmill using a...
presentations.authors_text: S.M da Silva1, L. Pedder2, B.W Timmons1,3, J. Obeid1,3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1McMaster University, Child Health & Exercise Medicine Program, Pediatrics, Hamilton, Canada, 2McMaster University, Division of Respirology, Pediatrics, Hamilton, Canada, 3McMaster University, Kinesiology, Hamilton, Canada

sections.Objectives: Exercise is a well-established therapy for children with cystic fibrosis (CF). As CF health continues to evolve with elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor (ETI) therapy, understanding fuel metabolism during exercise is increasingly important to support a healthy, active lifestyle. This study aims to compare substrate oxidation during exercise in children with CF who were short-term and long-term users of ETI.
sections.Methods: Children aged 8 to 17 y, were divided into short-term (G1) or long-term (G2) ETI therapy groups using a median split of ETI duration. Basic anthropometric measurements were collected. Aerobic fitness, defined as the highest 20-sec oxygen uptake (VO2peak), was measured on a treadmill using a Bruce protocol. Participants completed a 20-minute steady-state treadmill test at an intensity equivalent to 45% of VO2peak. Rates of fat and carbohydrate (CHO) oxidation were calculated at 5-10 and 15-20 minutes of exercise using indirect calorimetry. Basic descriptive statistics, t-tests, and repeated-measures ANOVAs were performed.
sections.Results: Six children (4 female, mean±SD: 13.2±1.6 y) were included in G1; G2 included 7 children (2 female, 14.3±3.2 y). ETI therapy spanned 29 to 181 weeks (G1: 93.6±42.6, G2:159.0±17.7). Rates of fat oxidation (G1: 3.8±2.0 mg/kg/min, G2: 2.3±1.7 mg/kg/min) and CHO oxidation (G1: 21.2±9.3 mg/kg/min, G2: 25.8±7.3 mg/kg/min) were similar between groups, with no effect of exercise time. Children in G2 oxidized higher amounts of CHO (21.8±8.7 g) compared with G1 (13.9±3.0 g), p<0.05. The percent of total energy expenditure (EE) from fat (G1: 36.7±14.5, G2: 18.4±15.6) and CHO (G1: 63.3±14.5, G2: 81.6±15.6) were similar between groups.
sections.Conclusion: Children with longer ETI therapy oxidized higher amounts of CHO, but no differences were found in the rate or contribution to EE between groups. Future research with a larger, longitudinal sample is needed to monitor changes over time as children with CF continue ETI therapy.

sections.Objectives: The objective of this study was to evaluate longitudinal changes in skeletal muscle mass and physical activity levels in adults with cystic fibrosis before and after initiation of CFTR modulator therapy, and to explore the association between physical activity and changes in body composition.
sections.Methods: A retrospective longitudinal study was conducted in adult patients with cystic fibrosis treated at the Cystic Fibrosis Center at University Hospital Centre Zagreb, Croatia. Skeletal muscle mass and body fat percentage were assessed using bioelectrical impedance analysis at the last visit before CFTR modulator initiation and at annual follow-up visits thereafter. Physical activity was recorded from clinical interviews and classified according to the type of activity and categorized by intensity. Changes over time were analyzed in relation to time since treatment initiation.
sections.Results: An increase in skeletal muscle mass was observed during the first year of CFTR modulator therapy, with further stabilization or gradual improvement during subsequent follow-up. Patients reporting moderate to high levels of physical activity demonstrated greater increases in muscle mass and a more favorable body composition profile compared with less active individuals, who showed a higher proportion of fat mass gain. Physical activity level was positively associated with longitudinal changes in skeletal muscle mass.
sections.Conclusion: CFTR modulator therapy is associated with beneficial changes in skeletal muscle mass in adults with cystic fibrosis, particularly in those who remain physically active. These findings support the importance of promoting regular physical activity and structured physiotherapy as an integral part of care in the era of CFTR modulator treatment.

Treatment compliance is important to help maintain health and combat respiratory infections. Technology now makes gamification and monitoring of ACT possible. Playphysio® was developed as a social venture with the goal of supporting ACT and making a positive impact on the lives of children and their families. This pilot study aimed to assess feasibility of the technology within clinical practice. Secondary outcomes around treatment quality, compliance and quality of life (CFQ-R) were also captured. Method: Single site, investigator single blinded randomised controlled feasibility trial....

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Alder Hey Children’s Hospital, Physiotherapy Department, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 2University of Liverpool, School of Allied Health Professionals, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 3Playphysio C.I.C, Cambridge, United Kingdom, 4University of Liverpool, Department of Health Data Science, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 5Alder Hey Children’s Hospital, Respiratory Department, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 6University of Liverpool, School of Medicine, Liverpool, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Airway clearance techniques (ACT) contribute to a high burden of care for young people with Cystic Fibrosis (CF). Treatment compliance is important to help maintain health and combat respiratory infections. Technology now makes gamification and monitoring of ACT possible. Playphysio® was developed as a social venture with the goal of supporting ACT and making a positive impact on the lives of children and their families. This pilot study aimed to assess feasibility of the technology within clinical practice. Secondary outcomes around treatment quality, compliance and quality of life (CFQ-R) were also captured.
sections.Methods: Single site, investigator single blinded randomised controlled feasibility trial. Participants were provided with a physioPal™ (sensor), that detected oscillations from the users ACT adjunct and a paired smart device, preloaded with the Playphysio® games app. Children were randomised (1:1) to ‘gamification’ or ‘blank’ mode group for their daily ACT. Baseline compliance was captured over 28 days, gamification was then activated for the intervention & the control was presented with a blank display. Both assignments remained this way throughout the trial (112 days). An open label extension with gamification for all was then unlocked.
sections.Results: 59 patients (aged 5-11) were recruited. Both compliance [mean (sd): 40 (7) Vs 10 (2); p-value = 0.001)] and treatment quality [mean(sd): 37.6 (5.42), 12.6 (3.15); p<0.001] demonstrate improved performance with gamification which was statically significant during the intervention phase vs the control. No significant changes were observed in CFQ-R.
sections.Conclusion: The intervention was well tolerated and feasible for implementation within clinical practice. While CFQ-R scores showed no significant changes during the study period, gamification resulted in significant improvements in both treatment compliance and quality - key factors for maintaining long-term respiratory health in children with CF.

Methods: A national benchmarking survey was distributed September-December 2025 to all UK CF centres. The questionnaire was circulated to physiotherapists via the Association of Chartered Physiotherapists in CF (ACPCF), and direct em...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Royal Brompton Hospital, Guy's and St Thomas' NHS Foundation Trust, Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, London, United Kingdom, 2Imperial College London, National Heart and Lung Institute, London, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Sputum induction (SI) is used in CF when patients cannot provide a spontaneous sample; however, SI practice varies widely across UK CF centres in terms of protocols and devices used. Although the European Cystic Fibrosis Society Clinical Trials Network (ECFS-CTN) has published a standard operating procedure (SOP) for SI, recommending jet or ultrasonic (US) nebulisers for use in clinical trials, the extent to which this informs routine clinical practice remains unclear.
sections.Methods: A national benchmarking survey was distributed September-December 2025 to all UK CF centres. The questionnaire was circulated to physiotherapists via the Association of Chartered Physiotherapists in CF (ACPCF), and direct email contact. Responses were analysed descriptively to benchmark current practice and identify variation across centres.
sections.Results: 30 centres responded, 60% adult (table). The primary indication for SI was clinical need (93%). Most centres performed more than one SI per month. 97% of centres used 7% hypertonic saline. eFlow®rapid were most used (55%), followed by jet (48%), US (21%), and Aerogen® (10%) devices, with several centres using more than one device. SI typically lasted 16–30 minutes. Only 17% of centres used a protocol with repeated cycles of nebulisation, airway clearance and spirometry per procedure. SI was undertaken predominantly by specialist physiotherapists (97%). While most centres reported having a local SOP, only 23% had formal competencies in place and 47% were aware of the ECFS-CTN SOP. Table Centre TypeAdult18 (60%)Paediatric9 (30%)Adult & Paediatrics 3 (10%)Indication for SI*Unable to expectorate spontaneously - routine17 (57%)Clinical need 28 (93%)Deprescribing nebulised antibiotics15 (50%)Other (clinical trials, annual review) 5 (10%)Competencies for SI Yes7 (23%)No 23 (77%)SOP in place at centreYes26 (87%)No4 (14%)Nebuliser device used for SI*Jet neb13 (43%)E-flow16 (53%)Aerogen5 (17%)US neb6 (20%)Walled oxygen3 (10%)Whichever nebuliser the patient has2 (7%)Repeated cycles of nebulisation > airway clearance > spirometry method per procedureYes5 (17%)No25 (83%)*Values are presented as percentages of respondents. Respondents were able to select more than one option.
sections.Conclusion: This benchmarking survey demonstrates substantial variation in SI practice across the UK, with wide differences in nebuliser devices and protocols. It highlights gaps in standardised competencies and shows that the ECFS-CTN SOP is not inclusive of devices currently used, supporting the need for further research to inform SOPs aligned with contemporary practice.

Nevertheless, routine surveillance of bronchopulmonary pathogens via sputum samples remains central to the clinical management of cystic fibrosis (CF). This study aimed to compare the success rates of induced sputum (IS) collection before and after the initiation of ETI in a real-world cohort. Methods: Clinical data and IS success rate were retrospectively analysed over a 3-year period. Sputum frequency and estimated volume were recorded on the day of IS collection using a standardized scale. All IS procedures were performed by trained physiotherapists in accorda...
presentations.authors_text: D. Appelt1, J. Eder2, K. Niedermayr2, I. Geißler2, A. Nöstlinger2, T. Grinschgl-Leis2, O. Eberharter2, H. Ellemunter3, T. Fuchs1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Medical University of Innsbruck, Department of Child and Adolescent Health, Paediatrics III, Innsbruck, Austria, 2Tirol Kliniken, Department of Child and Adolescent Health, Paediatrics III, Innsbruck, Austria, 3Cystic Fibrosis Centre Innsbruck, Medical Research Affiliate, Innsbruck, Austria

sections.Objectives: The introduction of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) therapy has been associated with a substantial reduction in sputum production. Nevertheless, routine surveillance of bronchopulmonary pathogens via sputum samples remains central to the clinical management of cystic fibrosis (CF). This study aimed to compare the success rates of induced sputum (IS) collection before and after the initiation of ETI in a real-world cohort.
sections.Methods: Clinical data and IS success rate were retrospectively analysed over a 3-year period. Sputum frequency and estimated volume were recorded on the day of IS collection using a standardized scale. All IS procedures were performed by trained physiotherapists in accordance with local protocols.
sections.Results: 581 IS samples one year prior and 966 samples obtained during the subsequent two years from 103 people with CF (62% adults, 52% female, mean age 22 years [range 6–48]) were analysed. Both adult and paediatric cohorts exhibited sustained and significant reductions in patient-reported sputum frequency and volume following ETI initiation (p<0.001 and p<0.05, respectively). Despite this, IS collection remained highly successful, with an overall success rate of 97–100% across all age groups, even though 18% of adults and 43% of paediatric patients reported no spontaneous sputum production on the day of sampling. Following ETI initiation, clinically relevant pathogens were identified in 32% of IS samples, prompting the initiation of antibiotic therapy.
sections.Conclusion: This real-world study demonstrates that routine sputum induction is safe and feasible. Despite substantially reduced sputum production, IS samples were successfully obtained in nearly all cases and enabled the detection of clinically relevant pathogens that might otherwise have remained undetected. Routine implementation of sputum induction two to four times per year should therefore be considered, particularly in patients without chronic bronchopulmonary colonisation.

presentations.authors_text: A. Delaney1, M. Doumit1, B. Prentice2, B. McMullan3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Sydney Children's Hospital Randwick, Physiotherapy, Randwick, Australia, 2Sydney Children's Hospital Randwick, Respiratory Medicine, Randwick, Australia, 3Sydney Children's Hospital Randwick, Infectious Diseases, Randwick, Australia

sections.Objectives: Children with cystic fibrosis (CF) often have difficulty spontaneously expectorating sputum. Induced sputum (IS), oropharyngeal swabs (OPS) and cough swabs (CS) are commonly used. Evidence for diagnostic accuracy of samples in the post modulator setting is limited and no study has compared OPS to CS. It is also unknown if clinical features (cough quality and sample appearance) are useful in determining best sampling approach.
sections.Methods: In this prospective study, children undergoing clinically indicated IS provided paired IS, OPS and CS. Diagnostic accuracy of OPS and CS was calculated with IS treated as the reference standard. Feasibility was assessed using collection time and distress ratings. Associations between cough quality (wet/dry), sample appearance (sputum/saliva) and pathogen detection were examined.
sections.Results: Seventy-four participants (mean age 12, range 4-18yrs) provided 102 complete sample sets. IS, OPS and CS were pathogen positive in 39%, 40% and 9% of samples respectively. OPS had superior diagnostic accuracy compared to CS. Sensitivity for Pa detection was 100% (95%CI 51-100) for OPS and 0% (95%CI 0-49%) for CS. For Sa, sensitivity for OPS was 57% (95%CI 39-73) and CS 18% (95%CI 8-36). Samples from children with dry cough versus wet cough on presentation were pathogen positive in 53% and 54% of cases respectively. Samples which appeared to contain sputum and samples that were salivary in appearance were pathogen positive in 42% and 67% cases respectively. All three sampling methods were acceptable with low levels of distress, however induced sputum was most time/resource consuming.
sections.Conclusion: Among swab-based methods, OPS showed the closest agreement with IS and CS showed low pathogen detection. Neither cough quality nor sample appearance reliably identified samples likely to yield pathogens, indicating a dry cough on presentation or a sample of salivary appearance should preclude induced sputum being collected and analysed.

These changes have raised concerns about the feasibility of sputum-based endpoints in clinical studies. presentations.affiliations_text: 1Manchester Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Wythenshawe, Manchester, United Kingdom, 2University of Manchester, Manchester, United Kingdom, 3Queen's University Belfast, School of Pharmacy, Belfast, United Kingdom, 4Cardiff and Vale University Health Board, Cardiff, United Kingdom, 5The Newcastle upon Tyne Hospitals NHS FT, Newcastle upon Tyne, United Kingdom, 6Leeds Institute of Medical Research, University of Leeds, Leeds, United Kingdom, 7Adult CF Centre, Liverpool Heart and Chest Hospital NHS Foundation Trust, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 8University of Liverpool, Liverpool, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Sputum is a key biofluid in cystic fibrosis (CF) clinical studies. However, sputum volume is often markedly reduced following initiation of CFTR modulator therapies. Many people with CF (pwCF) experience decreased cough and sputum production, and some report being unable to expectorate at all following treatment. These changes have raised concerns about the feasibility of sputum-based endpoints in clinical studies.
sections.Methods: Sputum was collected from pwCF as part of the CF-Tracker study (www.pulse-cf.com/tracker-study). Eligible participants must have experienced a pulmonary exacerbation within the past 12 months; those a priori known to be unable to produce sputum, even with saline induction, are excluded. Sputum samples were collected at each face-to-face visit and weighed prior to processing. A minimum sputum weight of 500 mg at baseline is required for study participation; participants who were unable to spontaneously expectorate this volume underwent enhanced physiotherapy manoeuvres and/or sputum induction with 7% hypertonic saline.
sections.Results: Forty-eight pwCF were recruited across the United Kingdom, of whom 46 (95.8%) are prescribed CFTR modulator therapy. Mean FEV1 was 1.87 litres (52.9% predicted). Across all study visits (n=96), 70 sputum samples were obtained. Eight participants (17%) required induced sputum during the study period. The mean sputum weight was 2,140 mg, with a median of 1,651 mg (range 466–8,783 mg). Two participants (4.16%) were unable to provide a baseline sputum specimen and were excluded. Discussion: These findings demonstrate that sputum-based clinical studies remain feasible in the era of CFTR modulator therapy. Careful population selection and engagement of respiratory physiotherapists are important to optimise sputum collection using airway clearance techniques and sputum induction when required. Funding: LifeArc / Cystic Fibrosis Trust Innovation Hub Network.

This study aimed to evaluate the incidence and characteristics of dysfunctional breathing patterns identified on cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET) in this population. Methods: We retrospectively analysed CPETs performed between January 2022 and December 2025 in children and young people with CF attending a tertiary centre. Inclusion...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1University Hospitals of Leicester, Paediatric Respiratory, Leicester, United Kingdom, 2University of Leicester, Respiratory Sciences, Leicester, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Breathing patterns may reflect physiological responses to respiratory disease; however, in the absence of organic pathology they may represent dysfunctional breathing. Dysfunctional breathing can occur in children and young people (CYP) with cystic fibrosis (CF), where symptoms may overlap with disease-related limitation and be difficult to distinguish clinically. This study aimed to evaluate the incidence and characteristics of dysfunctional breathing patterns identified on cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET) in this population.
sections.Methods: We retrospectively analysed CPETs performed between January 2022 and December 2025 in children and young people with CF attending a tertiary centre. Inclusion criteria were age 8–17 years, height ≥130 cm, and completion of a maximal CPET as part of routine annual assessment; tests with incomplete data or premature termination were excluded. CPET reports were reviewed following combined clinical and physiological assessment.
sections.Results: Ninety five CPETs from 36 CYP with CF were analysed. 2 patients had submaximal CPET data. Dysfunctional breathing was identified in 28 CPETs (29.47%), representing 20 CYP (55.5%). Of these CPET, 13 (46.4%) demonstrated exercise-induced hyperventilation. Within the dysfunctional breathing cohort, VO₂ peak was normal in 13 CYP (72.2%) and reduced in 5 CYP (27.7%). Abnormal breathing patterns occurred at variable stages of exercise testing. All affected CYP were referred for physiotherapy.
sections.Conclusion: Dysfunctional breathing was identified in a substantial proportion of CYP with cystic fibrosis undergoing routine cardiopulmonary exercise testing, with exercise-induced hyperventilation present in nearly half of affected tests. These findings suggest that dysfunctional breathing may coexist with CF-related respiratory limitation. CPET may be a useful objective tool to identify dysfunctional breathing and support targeted physiotherapy intervention.

presentations.authors_text: I. Ahmed1, D. Stevens2, D. Urquhart3, T. Radtke4, H. Hebestreit5
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University Hospitals of Leicester, Leicester, United Kingdom, 2Dalhousie University, Halifax, Canada, 3Royal Hospital for Children and Young People, Edinburgh, United Kingdom, 4University of Zurich, Zurich, Switzerland, 5University Children's Hospital, Wuerzburg, Germany

sections.Objectives: Dysfunctional breathing (DB) is increasingly recognised in cystic fibrosis (CF), yet no internationally standardised definition, diagnostic pathway, or treatment framework exists. The objective was to describe international clinical practice in DB assessment, diagnosis, and management in CF, and to identify priorities for standardisation.
sections.Methods: An international, cross-sectional, anonymous online survey of healthcare professionals working in CF centres was conducted. Developed by the European Cystic Fibrosis Society (ECFS) Exercise Working Group, the survey explored terminology, diagnostic and management approaches, and perceived challenges. Data were analysed descriptively.
sections.Results: 64 responses were received from centres, mainly in Europe. DB assessment or management was reported by 59% of centres, with marked variation in terminology, classification and diagnostic approaches. DB was most commonly assessed at rest and during exercise (85%). Diagnostic methods included observation (97%), spirometry (74%), questionnaires (62%), and cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET) (50%). Questionnaires were widely viewed as poorly validated and focused on hyperventilation. CPET use varied considerably, with key challenges including reliance on clinician experience (89%) and lack of diagnostic criteria (72%). Management was predominantly physiotherapist-led and individualised, with few centres using formal protocols. Barriers included limited specialist expertise, resource constraints, and low service prioritisation. Over 90% supported the need for international standardised definitions, assessment frameworks, CPET criteria and treatment pathways.
sections.Conclusion: DB practice in CF is highly variable, with limited specialist expertise and inconsistent diagnostic approaches, particularly for CPET. These findings will inform ongoing work by the ECFS Exercise Working Group to develop international consensus standards to support equitable, evidence-based DB care.

sections.Objectives: Urinary incontinence (UI) is an underreported yet clinically relevant concern in adults with cystic fibrosis (CF). Previous studies have shown high prevalence rates in CF. However, most were conducted prior to the widespread implementation of CFTR modulator therapy. The aim of this study was to determine the prevalence, severity and associated factors of UI in adults with CF, and to evaluate its relationship with CFTR modulator use.
sections.Methods: This retrospective cross-sectional study included adults with CF from the CF Centre of Ghent University Hospital. Using the validated International Consultation on Incontinence Questionnaire (ICIQ), UI was assessed during annual reviews from January to December 2024. Multivariable logistic regression was performed with UI as dependent variable and CFTR modulator use, sex, age and FEV₁ as covariates. Sex-specific subgroup analysis was conducted to assess the relationship between parity and UI in women.
sections.Results: A total of 134 adults with CF were included (age 18–67 years, 43% female). The overall prevalence of UI was 22.4% (95% CI 15.6–30.4%). In women, UI was significantly more frequent than in men (34.5% vs 13.2%), and women reported greater symptom severity. CFTR modulator use was not associated with UI (OR 0.97; 95% CI 0.28–3.41), whereas male sex was associated with lower odds of UI (OR 0.29; 95% CI 0.12–0.69). Age and FEV₁ were not significantly related to UI. In women with one or more children, UI was more prevalent than in nulliparous women (55.6% vs 25.0%).
sections.Conclusion: In adults with CF, UI remains a common extra-pulmonary symptom, particularly among (multiparous) women. In this study, no association was found between CFTR modulator use and UI. These findings suggest the need for systematic screening and management of UI in adult CF care, irrespective of CFTR modulator use. However, further research may be warranted in this field to contribute to a more targeted and individualised approach.

Despite robust evidence supporting pelvic floor muscle training in the general population its effectiveness and other treatment strategies in individuals with chronic respiratory conditions remain underexplored. This scoping review aimed to map and critically appraise interventions for PFD in this population. Methods: A comprehensive search of CINAHL, PubMed, Cochrane Library, MedLine, and grey literature was conducted up to April 2025, including studies of any design addressing PFD management in adults with chronic respiratory...
presentations.authors_text: A. Russell1, Z. Johnstone2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Queen Margaret University, Edinburgh, United Kingdom, 2Royal Hospital for Children and Young People, Edinburgh, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Chronic respiratory conditions, such as cystic fibrosis, increase the risk of PFD due to persistent coughing and altered breathing patterns that strain pelvic floor muscles. Despite robust evidence supporting pelvic floor muscle training in the general population its effectiveness and other treatment strategies in individuals with chronic respiratory conditions remain underexplored. This scoping review aimed to map and critically appraise interventions for PFD in this population.
sections.Methods: A comprehensive search of CINAHL, PubMed, Cochrane Library, MedLine, and grey literature was conducted up to April 2025, including studies of any design addressing PFD management in adults with chronic respiratory disease. Data extraction followed PRISMA-ScR guidelines, and findings were synthesised narratively.
sections.Results: Ten studies met inclusion criteria, including randomised controlled trials, observational studies, case series and literature reviews. UI, particularly stress incontinence, was the most frequently investigated dysfunction. PFMT consistently demonstrated improvements in symptoms, muscle endurance and pelvic floor contraction, although small sample sizes, heterogeneity, and methodological limitations reduced generalisability. Other interventions included tension-free vaginal tape, duloxetine and Chinese herbal medicine, with mixed or limited evidence. Key barriers to treatment adherence included embarrassment, recruitment difficulties, physical limitations and inadequate integration of continence care into respiratory services.
sections.Conclusion: The evidence highlights potential benefits of PFMT but underscores substantial research gaps, including limited studies in men, short-term follow-up, and insufficient investigation of non-urinary aspects of PFD. Future research should focus on long-term trials and integrated interdisciplinary approaches to optimise management of PFD in individuals with chronic respiratory conditions.

Where robust evidence was unavailable, Good Practice Points (GPPs) were developed from the collective clinical experience of chapter authors and document editors. Objective: To systematically validate the updated GPPs among ACPCF members, ensuring high clinical consensus and maximising their applicability in CF physiotherapy care. Method: A sequential, four-part survey was deployed among ACPCF members to mitigate survey fatigue (maximum member pa...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1West Midlands Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Birmingham, United Kingdom, 2Regional Paediatric Cystic Fibrosis Unit, Leeds General Infirmary, Leeds, United Kingdom, 3University Hospitals of Leicester NHS Trust, Leicester, United Kingdom

sections.Background: The dynamic evolution of Cystic Fibrosis (CF) care necessitated an update to the CF Trust and Association of Chartered Physiotherapists in CF (ACPCF) Standards of Care and Good Clinical Practice for the Physiotherapy Management of Cystic Fibrosis document. Where robust evidence was unavailable, Good Practice Points (GPPs) were developed from the collective clinical experience of chapter authors and document editors.
sections.Objectives: To systematically validate the updated GPPs among ACPCF members, ensuring high clinical consensus and maximising their applicability in CF physiotherapy care.
sections.Methods: A sequential, four-part survey was deployed among ACPCF members to mitigate survey fatigue (maximum member participation in a single phase: 39). Members evaluated statements using a four-point Likert scale. Acceptance required ≥80% agreement among those providing a substantive response. Reviewer comments guided the refinement of statements below this threshold and GPPs receiving ≥20% disagreement were rejected.
sections.Results: 321 discrete GPP statements were submitted for review. Based on the consensus threshold: 298 statements were immediately accepted as presented. 19 statements required editing and adaptation based on feedback before inclusion. 4 statements were rejected. In total, 317 GPP points were successfully validated and formally incorporated into the final updated Standards of Care document.
sections.Conclusion: This systematic, consensus-driven process successfully validated a comprehensive set of CF Physiotherapy GPPs. The resulting guidelines offer essential, expert-grounded clinical direction in emerging areas where research evidence is lacking, including: individualised exercise protocols, routine musculoskeletal and pelvic health screening, and specialised care provision in the post-CFTR modulator era (e.g., inhaled therapy post CFTR modulators).

presentations.title: Global collaboration to develop a physiotherapy outcome measure toolkit
presentations.summary: Background: An endeavour to improve harmony of data collection in physiotherapy resulted in global collaboration and development of an outcome measure (OM) toolkit for clinicians. Developing recommendations for OMs in physiotherapy, with global consensus, will enable quality research, inform treatment decisions, and improve service delivery in cystic fibrosis (CF). Methods: Through a Delphi process key statements which underpin data used by physiotherapists in clinical practice and research were identified. A broader survey was circulated across a global physiotherapy network to determine current clinical use of OMs, self-rated importance of these, and identification of additional and emerging OMs. An internat...
presentations.authors_text: J. Hauser1,2, L. Morrison3,4, S. Pollock5, E. Coulter5, Global Data Harmony Working Group, REACH for CF
presentations.affiliations_text: 1CFPhysio.com Inc, Hobart, Australia, 2Tasmanian Health Service, Tasmanian Adult Cystic Fibrosis Unit, Hobart, Australia, 3Queen Elizabeth University Hospital, Glasgow, United Kingdom, 4West of Scotland Adult CF Unit, Glasgow, United Kingdom, 5Glasgow Caledonian University, Department of Allied Health Professions, Glasgow, United Kingdom

sections.Background: An endeavour to improve harmony of data collection in physiotherapy resulted in global collaboration and development of an outcome measure (OM) toolkit for clinicians. Developing recommendations for OMs in physiotherapy, with global consensus, will enable quality research, inform treatment decisions, and improve service delivery in cystic fibrosis (CF).
sections.Methods: Through a Delphi process key statements which underpin data used by physiotherapists in clinical practice and research were identified. A broader survey was circulated across a global physiotherapy network to determine current clinical use of OMs, self-rated importance of these, and identification of additional and emerging OMs. An international working group completed evidence-based literature reviews for OMs relevant to current physiotherapy research and clinical care.
sections.Results: 167 responses were collated and analysed in the broader survey. Of the respondents, from countries including UK, Europe, Australia, New Zealand, USA and Canada, 97% rated spirometry/lung function as an essential outcome measure in CF care, followed by sputum assessment (70%), participation in physical activity (65%), quality of airway clearance (65%) and exercise testing (65%). These 5 core areas align with the key statements identified in the Delphi. A toolkit is in development to support clinicians in OM selection.
sections.Conclusion: Key statements underpinning physiotherapy research and clinical practice have been identified. OMs relevant to these statements were collated, and evidence-based standardised recommendations identified. A toolkit will provide access to standardised OMs supporting clinical decision-making, patient-centred care, and improving CF clinical practice. Future work, with co-design to develop a complementary resource for people with CF to ensure relevance, understanding, and value of these measures is of importance.
