sections.Background: Ongoing professional development (PD) is vital for clinicians in the ever-changing landscape of cystic fibrosis (CF) care. Collaboration across the globe on research initiatives and clinical service development priorities is essential. In 2021 a team of five clinicians from the UK, Australia and US combined to develop REACH for CF (a Research, Education and Collaboration Hub for Cystic Fibrosis), a global consortium to promote education, collaboration, and virtual discussion forums.
sections.Methods: In 2022, a survey was sent out to physiotherapists, physical and respiratory therapists to identify PD priorities in CF practice. REACH for CF developed an education program to meet these priorities, delivering eight virtual education sessions to the REACH membership from 2022-2025. Post-session feedback surveys were completed to assist development of events and capture participant satisfaction. Additional events were hosted to support collaborative discussions to support physiotherapists in the ever-evolving CF modulator era.
sections.Results: The survey was completed by 84 clinicians. The top three priorities were exercise testing and prescription, airway clearance, and musculoskeletal interventions. Post-session feedback surveys (n=90) indicate 96% of respondents plan to attend future REACH sessions and 85% rated the session(s) as extremely easy to access. The majority of respondents (98%) rated the content extremely relevant to their current practice.
sections.Conclusion: In the evolving CF landscape, the role of physiotherapy is adapting. An understanding of clinician needs in PD and research support is essential. Creating a space for virtual connection, collaboration, and ongoing PD has been met by the REACH for CF initiative. Future work in fostering global clinical and research development will remain a priority for the REACH for CF membership. Initiative was supported by an IME grant from Vertex Pharmaceuticals, and in collaboration of Beam Feel Good and CFPhysio.com Inc.

presentations.title: Finding the measurement of frailty in Adult Cystic Fibrosis
presentations.summary: Objectives: Historically, shorter life expectancy in people with cystic fibrosis (PWCF) limited the emergence of age-related comorbidities such as frailty. Frailty represents a geriatric syndrome generally recognized as a state of vulnerability due to adverse health outcomes including an increased risk of falls and fractures, hospitalization, decreased quality of life, physical disability, and early mortality. Advances in Cystic Fibrosis (CF) care, particularly Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Regulator (CFTR) modulators, have significantly improved survival, with many PWCF now expected to live into older age. Despite this, there is limited evidence to guide assessment and management of frailty in an ageing CF po...
presentations.authors_text: C. Reilly1, C. O'Connor1, S. Carter1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1St Vincent's University Hospital, National Referral Centre for Adult Cystic Fibrosis, Dublin, Ireland

sections.Objectives: Historically, shorter life expectancy in people with cystic fibrosis (PWCF) limited the emergence of age-related comorbidities such as frailty. Frailty represents a geriatric syndrome generally recognized as a state of vulnerability due to adverse health outcomes including an increased risk of falls and fractures, hospitalization, decreased quality of life, physical disability, and early mortality. Advances in Cystic Fibrosis (CF) care, particularly Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Regulator (CFTR) modulators, have significantly improved survival, with many PWCF now expected to live into older age. Despite this, there is limited evidence to guide assessment and management of frailty in an ageing CF population.
sections.Methods: Two frailty assessment tools were applied to clinically stable PWCF at hospital discharge. The Clinical Frailty Phenotype (CFP) classified frailty as ≥3/5 criteria, pre-/intermediate frailty as 1–2/5, and robust as 0/5. The Rockwood Clinical Frailty Scale (CFS) was applied retrospectively using a 9-point score for comparison.
sections.Results: Twenty-three PWCF were included (14 male, 9 female; mean age 31 years). Mean FEV1 was 62.5% predicted (range 32–93%). Cystic Fibrosis Related Diabetes (CFRD) was present in 43%, Pseudomonas aeruginosa colonisation 57%, and impaired Bone Mineral Density (BMD) in 52%. Using the CFP, 3 participants were frail, 11 pre-/intermediate frail and 10 robust. Scoring contributors included exhaustion (n=8), slowness (n=5), low activity (n=3), weight loss (n=3), and reduced grip strength (n=2). Using the CFS, 7 (30%) scored as mildly-moderately frail, while 16 (70%) were rated as managing well or very fit.
sections.Conclusion: There is no standardized frailty tool for CF. Both tools identified emerging frailty. Frail individuals also demonstrated impaired BMD, raising concerns regarding falls. Timing of assessment post-exacerbation may have influenced scores; however, emerging frailty patterns in PWCF warrant clinical attention. presentations.title: What’s the use? Provision of consumable nebuliser equipment – what we can learn.
presentations.summary: Introduction: Nebulised therapy is a cornerstone of routine Cystic Fibrosis (CF) care, and the consumables this requires generate a considerable cost burden. We audited this in our large adult CF centre over a 12-month period to explore potential cost saving. Methods: We retrospectively reviewed the use of PARI compressor and eflow consumables between March 2024-February 2025, comparing this with expected use based on individual patients’ prescriptions and manufacturer recommendations for replacement of equipment. Usage was then categorised as either: as expected, under expected use, over expected use or significantly over expected use. Findings were used to develop a standard operating procedure (SOP) to stan...
presentations.authors_text: K.-M. Flaherty1, T. Penrose1, R. Thornton1, M. Ledson1, M. Walshaw1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Liverpool Heart & Chest Hospital, Liverpool, United Kingdom

sections.Methods: We retrospectively reviewed the use of PARI compressor and eflow consumables between March 2024-February 2025, comparing this with expected use based on individual patients’ prescriptions and manufacturer recommendations for replacement of equipment. Usage was then categorised as either: as expected, under expected use, over expected use or significantly over expected use. Findings were used to develop a standard operating procedure (SOP) to standardise equipment provision.
sections.Results: We provided 375 pieces of equipment to 102 patients costing £15414.16, with most attributed to e-flow handsets. 54% of equipment usage fell within expected amounts, 28% below expected levels and 18% above expected levels, with only 2% falling into the “significantly over” expected category. The most over-used equipment individually was eflow handsets with the most under-used equipment being filter pads.
sections.Conclusion: An improvement in equipment supply processes is needed to ensure that provision is standardised, within manufacturer guidelines and reduces over-expenditure. Based on these findings we have developed an SOP to reduce the replacement of eflow handsets and aerosol heads through patient education around disinfection and cleaning. Further review is needed following the implementation of the SOP to assess the efficacy of the change. The underuse of filter pads could indicate reduced adherence to nebulised antibiotics, or lack of awareness around the need to filter. This presents further scope to improve patient education and possibly an alternative way to monitor adherence in the post-ineb era.

Results: Eight studies met the inclusion criteria, spanning qualitative, observational, Delphi, and survey methodologies. Conducted in the UK, Italy, the US, and Canada, they captured perspectives from people with cystic fibrosis, caregivers, and healthcare professionals. Three themes emerged: (1) a marked reduction in the perceived necessity and burden of airway clearance techniques f...
presentations.authors_text: H. Sheddon1, T. Craig1, Z. Johnstone2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Queen Margaret University, Edinburgh, United Kingdom, 2Royal Hospital for Children and Young People, Edinburgh, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: To map the available evidence on how airway clearance techniques are being used, reduced, or replaced in people with cystic fibrosis receiving modulators.
sections.Methods: A comprehensive search of MEDLINE, PubMed, CINAHL, PEDro, and the Cochrane Library was conducted for studies published between October 2019 and March 2025.
sections.Results: Eight studies met the inclusion criteria, spanning qualitative, observational, Delphi, and survey methodologies. Conducted in the UK, Italy, the US, and Canada, they captured perspectives from people with cystic fibrosis, caregivers, and healthcare professionals. Three themes emerged: (1) a marked reduction in the perceived necessity and burden of airway clearance techniques following modulator initiation, (2) increasing recognition of exercise as a complementary and potential alternative to traditional airway clearance, and (3) a cautious clinical approach.
sections.Conclusion: Evidence indicates that people with cystic fibrosis are reducing their use of airway clearance techniques in the modulator era. While clinicians may support patient-led adjustments through shared decision-making and close monitoring, caution is essential given the lack of long-term outcome data on the safety of airway clearance reduction. Larger, multi-centre trials are urgently required to establish whether ACTs can be safely reduced or replaced and to inform future clinical guidelines.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Liverpool Heart and Chest, Liverpool, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Guidelines recommended inhalation challenges for new inhaled antibiotics, but their role is unclear in switches of preparations of the same drug. We therefore explored outcomes of inhalation challenges in all switches from Promixin to generic Colistimethate at our large adult CF centre.
sections.Methods: A retrospective analysis of inhalation challenges was undertaken for consecutive inhalation challenges between January-December 2025. Lung function changes and subsequent treatment initiation decisions were captured.
sections.Results: Inhalation challenges completed in 41 pwCF (30 male (71%), median age was 32yrs [range 18-60yrs], median ppFEV1 of 76% [range 30-124]) were performed and included. Median FEV1 drop after the inhalation challenge was –1.8%. Nine pwCF (22%) had a drop of <5% FEV1 from baseline. Thirty Eight pwCF (92.7%) had a drop of <10% from baseline and were initiated on nebulised Colistimethate. Three (7.3%) experienced a drop in FEV1 >10%, in all 3 cases a clinical decision to initiate Colistmethate was made andall were safely initiated with modified regime or monitoring. Discussion: Clinically relevant bronchoconstriction responses when switching Colistimethate preparations are rare across wide range of ppFEV1 in pwCF. Inhalation test dosing did not alter clinical management even when FEV1 drops were seen. The role for inhalational test dose challenges in the post-ETI era warrants further evaluation.

presentations.authors_text: C. Morby1, S. Denniston1, L. Hull1, C. Wogan1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University Hospitals Birmingham NHS Foundation Trust, Paediatrics, Birmingham, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Mucus, phlegm, secretions, and sputum are terms used interchangeably within the Cystic Fibrosis (CF) multidisciplinary team (MDT) alongside descriptions of wet and productive cough. These are understood to have the same meaning. It is unclear if parents and their child with CF (CwCF) share this understanding and whether using one or two standardised terms would improve communication.
sections.Objectives: To understand parent and CwCF’s interpretation of terms and identify preferred terms.
sections.Methods: Two questionnaires were devised, one sent to 30 parents and one sent to 16 CwCF (aged 6-16 years). Results were analysed to determine if the MDT language was understood and if there was a preferred term.
sections.Results: 40% of parents and 50% of CwCF completed the questionnaires. Of those who replied, 58% of parents and 63% of CwCF understood the terms to be the same. Terminology used in clinicCwCF preference of term (%)Parent preference of term (%)Term parent believes CwCF best understands (%)Sputum151413.5Secretions01113.5Phlegm352524Mucus202516Other – not disclosed05.50Wet cough201422Productive cough105.511 50% of parents felt it was important for the CF MDT to adapt their language to a CwCF preference.
sections.Conclusion: The results show parents and CwCF predominantly understood the terms to be the same. CwCF prefer the word phlegm with this also being one of the top two preferences of parents. Phlegm is the term parents felt is best understood by their CwCF. When describing a cough, wet cough was preferred by both. Despite the small sample size, the CF MDT should consider using CwCF preferred terms. Nevertheless only 50% of parents felt it was necessary to adapt language to their CwCF’s preferences. Further research of a larger cohort of CwCF may be beneficial and MDT views on adopting standardised terms should be investigated.

The aim of this study was to investigate if hypertonic saline inhalation-related treatment burden decreases during the first 12 months of ETI treatment in pwCF followed at Stockholm CF Center. Methods: Data was collected from the Stockholm ETI Task Force study database, from the patient charts and from the National Swedish CF registry before ETI initiation and three and twelve months after initiation of ETI treatment. Statistical analysis was performed with GraphPad. Descriptive sta...
presentations.authors_text: S. Wilhelmsson1,2, A. Kowalik1,2, T. Pincikova1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Karolinska Institutet, Department of Paediatrics, CLINTEC, Stockholm, Sweden, 2Karolinska University Hospital, Stockholm CF Center, Stockholm, Sweden

sections.Objectives: Treatment with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has proven effective in many ways for patients with CF but there is a lack of real-world data regarding effects on treatment burden in Swedish people with CF (pwCF). The aim of this study was to investigate if hypertonic saline inhalation-related treatment burden decreases during the first 12 months of ETI treatment in pwCF followed at Stockholm CF Center.
sections.Methods: Data was collected from the Stockholm ETI Task Force study database, from the patient charts and from the National Swedish CF registry before ETI initiation and three and twelve months after initiation of ETI treatment. Statistical analysis was performed with GraphPad. Descriptive statistics, paired sample t-test and correlation analysis with Pearson correlation coefficient were used.
sections.Results: The cohort consisted of pwCF (n=29) with median age of 32 years (IQR 20.5-48). 51.7 % were male and FEV1 ranging from 39-117 precent predicted. Eleven and 14 pwCF were homozygotes and heterozygotes for delF508, respectively, and three had other CFTR variants. Number of inhalation occasions per week decreased from mean 7.8 at inclusion to 6.5 at three months with a mean difference of –1.9 (p=0.025). At 12 months after ETI initiation, number of inhalation occasions per week decreased to 6.1 with a mean difference of –2.27 (p=0.019). No significant difference in number of inhalation occasions was found between three and 12 months (p=0.69). There was no significant change in the amount of ml of hypertonic saline inhaled per inhalation occasion at either time of measurement. No correlation was found between FEV1 and number of inhalation occasions per week either at inclusion, three or 12 months.
sections.Conclusion: Inhalation-related treatment burden decreased during the first three months of treatment with ETI and remained unchanged at 12 months after ETI initiation. Further studies are needed to determine the long-term safety of reduced inhalation treatment.

Standardised PFTs we...
presentations.authors_text: A. Leeney1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1St. Vincents University Hospital, Physiotherapy, Dublin, Ireland

sections.Objectives: Pulmonary Function Tests (PFTs) are the gold standard of monitoring Cystic Fibrosis, used to assess lung disease severity, track progression, evaluate treatment efficiency and predict prognosis. However, technical errors occur with many patients, such as inconsistent effort, glottis closure, cough and tongue obstruction, adversely affecting their results. Technical errors were observed in 3 patients attending the National Referral Centre for Adult Cystic Fibrosis here at St. Vincents University Hospital.
sections.Methods: We identified 3 patients in our unit who were clinically stable but had either chronically poor PFT technique with substantial variability or unusually low PFT results. Standardised PFTs were performed by the CF Clinical Nurse Specialist (CF CNS) “take a deep breath in, put the mouthpiece in your mouth and seal your lips tightly, then blast all of your air into the tube as hard and as fast as you can in one long complete breath, and keep going until I tell you to stop”. The Senior Physiotherapist assessed the patients Forced Expiratory Technique (FET) to ascertain involvement of the upper airways, then re-educated the flow of air over upper airways/relaxed vocal chords and glottis, to achieve unobstructed flow required for good PFT technique. PFTs were then repeated by the CF CNS.
sections.Results: Patient 1 Patient 2 Patient 3 Pre Post % change Pre Post % change Pre Post % change FVC 6.74 6.25 -7.3% 4.74 4.53 4.4% 3.99 4.2 5.2% FEV1 4.07 5.18 27.27% 3.29 3.48 5.77% 2.6 2.73 5% PEF 7.17 11.81 64.7% 7.71 9.81 27.2% 7.82 7.86 0.5% In all 3 patients, Forced Expiratory Volume in 1 second (FEV1) improved back to the patients known baseline or was of better quality/volume than the initial test.
sections.Conclusion: Identification of poor PFT technique (through analysis of graphs and observation of technique) or unusually low FEV1 despite lack of symptoms may warrant physiotherapy input to help re-educate patients to achieve an open upper airway. This may help patients perform consistent, optimal PFTs, whilst concurrently acknowledging the variability of results achieved when testing PFT’s.

presentations.authors_text: H. Morgan1, Z. Jennings1, A. Schmidt1, W. Smith1, D. Rigler1, L. Thia1, J. Forton1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Noah's Ark Children's Hospital for Wales, Paediatric Respiratory Unit, Cardiff, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Spirometry is critical to monitoring lung health in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) . It is commonly performed during outpatient clinic visits, where children are often fatigued, distracted, or preoccupied. Formal laboratory-based spirometry offers a quieter, more focussed setting and may provide more accurate results.
sections.Methods: Formal laboratory-based spirometry (Jaeger Masterscreen PFT) was obtained in 25 pwCF aged 6-18 years, and compared to matched routine clinic-based spirometry (Spirolab, MIR, Italy) from preceding and subsequent scheduled clinics. All testing took place in clinically well patients. Pairwise non-parametric tests (Wilcoxon Signed Rank Test) were used to compare percent predicted FEV1 (ppFEV1) and percent predicted FVC (ppFVC), measured at different timepoints and clinical settings.
sections.Results: Mean age was 10.5 years (SD 3.8). Baseline clinic spirometry at preceding clinic showed a mean ppFEV1 of 85%, SD 7.8; and mean ppFVC of 91.3%, SD 10.3. Pairwise comparison of preceding clinic spirometry with formal spirometry (n=25) showed a mean increase in both ppFEV1 (9.2%; SD 7.9 (WSRT p<0.001)), and ppFVC (7.9%; SD 8.5 (WSRT p<0.001)). 44% of patients (11/25) demonstrated an increase in ppFEV1 of ≥10%. Pairwise comparison of formal spirometry with subsequent clinic spirometry (n=22) showed a mean decrease in both ppFEV1 (-7.6%; SD 8.3 (WSRT p<0.001)) and ppFVC (9.1%; SD 9.5 (WSRT p<0.001)). Pairwise comparison of preceding and subsequent clinic spirometry showed no significant difference in either ppFEV1 (1.6; SD 8.1 (WSRT NS) or ppFVC ( -1.0%; SD 11.1 (WSRT NS)
sections.Conclusion: Formal laboratory-based spirometry demonstrated clinically important improvements in both ppFEV1 and ppFVC, compared with matched clinic-based measurements. Longitudinal clinic-based spirometry consistently underestimated lung function in this study suggesting a busy clinic with many distractions may not be the optimal environment for testing.

sections.Objectives: In people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) diabetes is common and prevalence of obesity is increasing. Both are known to affect lung function. Glucagon-like peptide 1 agonist receptor (GLP1) medications are proven therapies for both conditions in the general population and not contraindicated in pwCF. Our aim was to explore the impact of GLP1 on pwCF’s lung function, BMI and glycaemic control in our adult population.
sections.Methods: Adult pwCF commencing a GLP1 were identified. Pre and post GLP1 data included BMI, HbA1c, and ppFEV1. Data was gathered retrospectively and statistical analysis using excel included change pre and post GLP1 in ppFEV1, BMI and HbA1c respectively. Correlation of change in ppFEV1 was explored. The relationship between change in ppFEV1 was compared with change in BMI and HbA1c, respectively.
sections.Results: 10 (6 males) pwCF were prescribed a GLP1 (5 through the NHS and 5 privately funded). 5 (4 males) were known diabetic. Median age 38 years. Data was collected from January 2024 to September 2025. Median pre ppFEV1 was 62% and post 68%. 5 pwCF had ≥10% increase in ppFEV1, with median change in ppFEV1 6.4%. Median BMI was 32.4 pre and 32.3 post; median percent change in BMI was -7%. Median HbA1c (n=9) pre was 48 and post 42 and median percent change was -13%. sections.Conclusion: GLP1 improved all measured outcomes and ppFEV1 increased significantly in 50% of pwCF. Decrease in BMI and HbA1c improved ppFEV1 in a similar relationship. The greater the decrease in BMI and HbA1c respectively, the greater the increase on ppFEV1. GLP1 significantly improves the lung function of pwCF although the main mechanisms for this need’s further exploration and is likely multifactorial. In the future larger and more complete data sets should be analysed and the inclusion of exercise testing outcomes should be explored.

sections.Objectives: Pulmonary exacerbations remain a major driver of morbidity and mortality in cystic fibrosis (CF), yet the mechanisms underpinning them are poorly understood. We used national registry data to identify patient factors associated with higher exacerbation risk.
sections.Methods: UK CF Registry data from 4,654 adults were analysed. Demographic, socioeconomic, and clinical factors associated with exacerbation were examined using negative binomial regression, reported as incidence rate ratios (IRR) with 95% confidence intervals.
sections.Results: Analysis was conducted on 2,507 individuals with complete data (2021–2023 pooled and 2023 cross-sectional). Higher exacerbation rates were strongly associated with female sex, smoking, disease severity, and comorbidity. These include chronic airway infection (P. aeruginosa, Aspergillus species, S. aureus, or NTM), insulin-treated diabetes, liver disease, and use of oxygen or non-invasive ventilation (IRRs range: 1.26-2.18, all p<0.05). Socioeconomic disadvantage, including unemployment and higher Townsend deprivation (quintiles 4–5), was associated with significantly higher exacerbation rates. Higher FEV1pp was independently associated with lower exacerbations, with each 1% increase corresponding to an approximate 2% reduction in exacerbation rate (IRR 0.98, 95% CI 0.98-0.99; p<0.001). Increasing age was also associated with a reduction in exacerbation frequency (IRR 0.99 per age year). No robust associations were observed for ethnicity, body mass index, vaccination status, or allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis. CFTR modulator use was associated with a lower exacerbation rate, although this did not reach statistical significance (IRR 0.77, 95% CI 0.59–1.00; p = 0.052).
sections.Conclusion: These findings highlight the multifactorial nature of exacerbation risk and underscore the need for holistic approaches that integrate both clinical and socioeconomic factors in risk stratification.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Vertex Pharmaceuticals Incorporated, Boston, MA, USA, Boston, United States, 2Royal Brompton Hospital, part of Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust and NHLI, Imperial College, London, UK, London, United Kingdom, 3Department of Respiratory Medicine, NHS Greater Glasgow and Clyde, Queen Elizabeth University Hospital, Glasgow, UK, Glasgow, United Kingdom, 4Cystic Fibrosis Trust, London, UK, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: LONGITUDE is a retrospective cohort study using UK Cystic Fibrosis (CF) Registry data to evaluate real-world outcomes in people with CF initiating elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) treatment. We report demographic and clinical characteristics for children with CF (cwCF) aged 2-5 years at ETI initiation.
sections.Methods: Analyses included children aged 2-5 years initiating ETI (index) between August 2019 (early access program) and December 2024 (latest data available) with ≥1 F508del allele and ≥1 annual review within 14 months pre-index. Baseline characteristics were obtained from the latest annual review pre-index. Genotype, prior CF transmembrane conductance regulator modulator (CFTRm) treatment, fecal elastase, co-morbidities, lung infections, and medications are described.
sections.Results: In total, 663 cwCF aged 2-5 years were included. Mean (SD) age at index was 3.3 (1.2) years; 52.3% were male; 55.4% had prior CFTRm treatment (among which 89.9% lumacaftor/ivacaftor), and 60.3% were homozygous F508del. At baseline, <1% had CF-related diabetes, 3.0% had liver disease, and 88.4% pancreatic insufficiency. Of those with a measure (N=463), 88.8% had a fecal elastase <200 mcg/g. The prevalence of lung infections at baseline annual review was 27.6% Staphylococcus aureus, 21.1% Pseudomonas aeruginosa, <1% Mycobacterium abscessus and <1% Burkholderia. Regarding medications at baseline annual review, 90.0% were taking pancreatic enzymes, 38.3% DNase, 34.7% hypertonic saline, 25.6% IV antibiotics, 29.0% inhaled antibiotics, 25.2% oral antibiotics and 11.3% inhaled corticosteroids.
sections.Conclusion: Among cwCF in the UK aged 2-5 years initiating ETI, clinical characteristics at baseline show a significant burden of CF at a young age from pancreatic insufficiency, lung infections, and medication use. The multi-system impact of CF, known to progress without intervention, is clearly present early in life.

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI), a triple CFTR modulator therapy licensed in the UK in 2020, has been shown to be a highly effective treatment for people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). Some groups may be ineligible for CFTR modulators, impacting health inequalities in treatment access and outcomes. This study aimed to assess social inequalities in eligibility and use of ETI amongst pwCF in the UK.
sections.Methods: We conducted an observational cohort study using data from the UK Cystic Fibrosis Registry (2020–2024). ETI eligibility was defined as meeting National Health Service (NHS) commissioning criteria for licensed or off-label use during each year, and ETI use as being prescribed ETI in that year. The Cochran–Armitage test for trend assessed social inequalities in eligibility, overall use, and use conditional on eligibility across years. Mixed-effects models further evaluated adjusted associations between deprivation quintiles and ETI eligibility.
sections.Results: Data from 11,486 pwCF contributed 50,219 observations between 2020 and 2024. ETI eligibility increased from 62.6% (n = 6,362) in 2020 to 93.2% (n = 9,475) in 2024, while ETI use rose from 6.2% (n = 627) to 79.3% (n = 8,061). Social deprivation gradients were seen in eligibility and overall ETI use (from 2021), but not in ETI use among eligible pwCF. Mixed-effects models showed that pwCF living in the least deprived areas were more likely to be eligible for ETI than those in the most deprived areas (OR 1.50, 95% CI 1.24–1.82), with younger individuals in deprived areas particularly disadvantaged.
sections.Conclusion: The current UK healthcare system ensures equitable ETI use among pwCF once eligibility criteria are met. However, individuals in more deprived areas are significantly less likely to be eligible, accounting for ethnicity and genotypes. These findings highlight the urgent need to reduce social inequalities in treatment eligibility and ensure that therapeutic advances benefit all pwCF.

sections.Objectives: Real-world data on CFTR modulator effects on ventilation inhomogeneity and structural lung disease in children remain scarce. This study evaluated short- and long-term effects of lumacaftor/ivacaftor (LUM/IVA) and elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) in children and adolescents with Cystic Fibrosis (CF) using chest computed tomography (CT) analyses and lung function tests.
sections.Methods: This retrospective cohort study included CFTR modulator–naïve individuals with cystic fibrosis followed at the Gothenburg CF centre between 2018 and 2025 who had undergone at least one chest CT scan. Chest CT scans were analysed using AI-based methods, and lung function data were retrieved from the Swedish National CF Registry. Longitudinal analyses were performed using linear mixed-effects models
sections.Results: Seventy individuals with cystic fibrosis (mean [SD] age 9.2 [4.6] years) initiating CFTR modulators were followed for a mean of 6.1 [1.2] years, during which 196 chest CT scans and 751 multiple-breath washout examinations were included. Mean baseline for bronchiectasis was 2.23% [3.41], with −0.21%-points (95% CI −1.04 to 0.62; p=0.61) change after LUM/IVA and −1.03%-points (95%CI −1.67 to −0.38; p=0.002) after ETI. Baseline mucus plugging was present in 49% (n=34); no significant change followed LUM/IVA (−7; p=0.33), whereas ETI reduced mucus plugging (−21; 95% CI −35 to −7; p=0.005). Mean baseline lung clearance index (LCI) was 8.14 (1.23) with a annual change of −0.04 (95% CI −0.11 to 0.02). LCI decreased after LUM/IVA and ETI with −0.57 LCI units (95% CI −0.91 to −0.24; p<0.0001) and −0.99 LCI units (95% CI −1.28 to −0.70; p<0.001), with no significant effect on annual LCI progression for either treatment.
sections.Conclusion: ETI, but not LUM/IVA, was associated with improvements in structural lung disease, while both treatments improved ventilation inhomogeneity, highlighting the complementary value of chest CT and lung function measures in monitoring CF lung disease. presentations.title: Lost in transcription: Bringing structure to free-text genotype data in the UK Cystic Fibrosis Registry
presentations.summary: Objectives: The UKCFR is a secure, centralised database. Containing demographic and routinely collected health data for consenting people with CF. Genotype data are entered by CF centres via a predefined drop-down list, which was expanded in 2024 to include 1,167 CFTR variants using CFTR2. As this list is not exhaustive and users are not necessarily genetics specialists, a free-text option allows variants to be entered when required. Routine cleaning of free-text entries is undertaken to improve data quality and standardisation. Methods: Following extension of the UKCFR list of CFTR variants, free-text entries were analysed to identify variants that could be matched to the updated variant database. An automate...
presentations.authors_text: K. Laidlaw1, M. Cerkvenik1, E. Gunn1, J. Osmond1, S. Charman1, S. Clarke1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Cystic Fibrosis Trust, Registry, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: The UKCFR is a secure, centralised database. Containing demographic and routinely collected health data for consenting people with CF. Genotype data are entered by CF centres via a predefined drop-down list, which was expanded in 2024 to include 1,167 CFTR variants using CFTR2. As this list is not exhaustive and users are not necessarily genetics specialists, a free-text option allows variants to be entered when required. Routine cleaning of free-text entries is undertaken to improve data quality and standardisation.
sections.Methods: Following extension of the UKCFR list of CFTR variants, free-text entries were analysed to identify variants that could be matched to the updated variant database. An automated cleaning programme scans free-text entries to identify legacy, cDNA, and protein names, as well as common misspellings, and other terms that map to existing Registry variant codes and re-categorises these entries accordingly. Free-text entries that cannot be mapped automatically are exported for further review.
sections.Results: A total of 11,302 patients have at least one recorded genotype, and 641 alleles from free-text entries were analysed. 57% alleles matched existing UKCFR variant options and were updated at source. 23% were identified as known CFTR variants not currently listed in the Registry, using external resources including the SickKids CFTR database. 20% require clarification and will be reviewed with CF centres and analysed further The analysis also provided insight into user behaviour informing future training provision.
sections.Conclusion: The cleaning programme successfully reduced the number of free-text entries by 80%, improving data quality and accuracy within the UKCFR. Regular and diligent cleaning ensures the ongoing reliability of genotype data. For the CF community, this ensures that every CFTR variant is captured, supporting robust, actionable insights that can be confidently used in clinical care, access to medicines, and eligibility for research.

sections.Objectives: To evaluate real-world effectiveness of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) using Italian CF Registry data, comparing outcomes over 24 months before and after treatment initiation.
sections.Methods: PwCF aged ≥6 years initiating ETI between October 2019 and December 2022 with ≥3 months of continuous treatment were included (n=2,276; 51% male; 35.8% F508del homozygous). Annual best % predicted FEV₁ (ppFEV₁), body mass index (BMI) or BMI z-score were assessed at baseline and at 12 and 24 months before and after ETI initiation. Chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection, complications, medication use, and hospital days were analysed.
sections.Results: Mean ppFEV₁ increased from 74.6±25.5 one year before ETI to 84.6±25.6 at 12 months (Δ 9.93; 95% CI 9.44–10.41, p<0.0001), then declined to 81.4±24.8 at 24 months, remaining above baseline. Similar trends were observed across genotype, sex, age, and P. aeruginosa or CF-related diabetes (CFRD) status. PwCF with ppFEV₁ <40 one year before ETI showed sustained improvement (31.4±7.7 to 38.0±8.9), while those with ppFEV₁ ≥40 mirrored the overall pattern. Adult BMI increased by 1.05 kg/m² (95% CI 0.97–1.14, p<0.0001) and paediatric BMI z-score by 0.21 SD (95% CI 0.16–0.26, p<0.0001), both sustained at 24 months. Use of inhaled therapies and antibiotics declined, particularly in pwCF with ppFEV₁ ≥40. Mean annual hospital days decreased by ~65%. Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis and massive haemoptysis decreased; CFRD remained stable. Non-cirrhotic liver disease declined at year 1 but increased at year 2; cirrhosis showed minimal change.
sections.Conclusion: ETI produced substantial improvements in lung function, nutrition, and hospital utilization. The decline in ppFEV₁ at 24 months among pwCF with baseline ppFEV₁ ≥40, coinciding with treatment de-escalation, warrants further evaluation, particularly regarding optimal therapy reduction strategies.

sections.Objectives: Survival data from Cystic Fibrosis (CF) registries demonstrate improved prognosis for individuals with CF. This study estimates the projected survival age of people living with CF in South Africa.
sections.Methods: A retrospective cohort study was conducted using prospectively collected data from CF centres participating in the South African Cystic Fibrosis Registry (SACFR) from 2018 to 2024. A period survival analysis, utilising five-year rolling windows and the Kaplan-Meier method, was used to calculate the median age of survival for 2019–2023 and 2020–2024. A Cox proportional hazards model was used to assess impact on survival.
sections.Results: Among 611 individuals followed by the SACFR, 62 (10.1%) deaths and 11 (1.8%) lung transplants were recorded. Most participants (64.2%) were diagnosed before the age of 2 years, with 109 (17.8%) diagnosed by six weeks of age. The overall median age of survival for 2024 was estimated at 30.9 years (95% CI: 22.0–41.2). Females had a lower estimated hazard of death than males (HR 0.78); though this was not statistically significant (95% CI 0.45–1.34, p = 0.37).
sections.Conclusion: The survival rate is improving for people living with CF in South Africa. Outcomes may be enhanced by measures including newborn screening, targeted interventions and improved access to new therapies.

sections.Objectives: Life expectancy of people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) has improved over recent decades, but comprehensive population-level data comparing survival gains before and after introduction of CFTR modulators (CFTRm) in Europe are limited.
sections.Methods: Data from 4,048 pwCF of three Central European countries (the Netherlands, Switzerland and Austria) were analysed using national CF registry data. Median survival age and key demographic characteristics were evaluated across three periods (2010–2014, 2015–2019, 2020–2024), with survival analysis further stratified by relevant cofactors, including genotype, age at diagnosis and selected clinical features.
sections.Results: Median survival age of the CF population increased significantly from 56.3 years in 2010–2014 to 67.1 years in 2020–2024 (p < 0.05). More than 70% of pwCF were diagnosed before their first birthday, and 49.5% were F508del homozygous. Survival gains were most pronounced among F508del homozygous individuals and pwCF with a FEV1pp below 40%. Differences in survival estimates across the different periods were observed regarding CF-related diabetes, chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection, use of pancreatic enzymes, frequency of antibiotic use, and exposure to CFTR modulators.
sections.Conclusion: Central European CF registry data demonstrate substantial gains in median survival age over 10 years, associated with evolving standards of care, especially introduction of CFTRm. Reliable up-to-date life expectancy estimates are crucial for public health care planning as well as for individual life plans, including education, employment, family, and social security considerations for pwCF. Pronounced improvements in younger birth cohorts and cofactor-defined subgroups underscore the value of ongoing population-based monitoring to guide future therapeutic strategies.

sections.Objectives: To evaluate changes in adherence to inhaled antibiotics and mucolytics following elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) initiation and associations with clinical outcomes in cystic fibrosis (CF).
sections.Methods: Retrospective study including 81 adult CF patients (43 females) from the Greek Cystic Fibrosis Patient Registry. Adherence to inhaled antibiotics (inh) and mucolytics (muc) was assessed using composite Medication Possession Ratio (cMPR) from the national electronic prescription system during the year pre-ETI and up to four years post-ETI. Outcomes included percent predicted FEV₁ (ppFEV₁), percent predicted FVC (ppFVC), intravenous (IV) antibiotic days, and non-hospitalized pulmonary exacerbations, comparing patients with low (cMPR ≤50%) versus higher adherence.
sections.Results: Inhaled antibiotic adherence declined from pre-ETI by 11% in the first year (p=0.004), 12.7% in the second (p=0.02), and 15.7% in the third (p=0.006), with a slight increase of 1.7% in the fourth year (p=0.70). Mucolytic adherence decreased by 9.2% in the first year (p=0.04), 7.6% in the second (p=0.26), 16% in the third (p=0.001) and minimal change in the fourth year (p=1). Neither differences in exacerbations requiring IV antibiotics nor in those with oral antibiotics between low and high adherence patients reached statistical significance. As shown in Table 1, similar results were obtained for ppFEV₁ and ppFVC. Table 1. sections.Conclusion: ETI was associated with reduced adherence to inhaled antibiotics and mucolytics, without differences on lung function or exacerbations between high and low adheres, supporting re-evaluation of inhaled treatments in the post- ETI era.

sections.Objectives: To compare longitudinal clinical trends in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) treated or not treated with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) using Italian Cystic Fibrosis Registry data over the two years before and after 2022.
sections.Methods: Registry data from 2022 were analyzed for pwCF not treated with ETI (No ETI; n=1830; 53% male; 44.5% pancreatic insufficient (PI)) and pwCF initiating ETI in 2022 (ETI; n=621; 47% male; 75% PI). Clinical parameters were assessed at four time points: two pre-2022 and two post-2022 evaluations. Outcomes included lung function (ppFEV₁), nutritional status (BMI, BMI z-score), medication use, and hospital days.
sections.Results: In the ETI cohort, mean ppFEV₁ increased from 86.62±21.66 at baseline to 95.31±21.56 at 12 months (mean difference 8.68; 95% CI: 7.71 to 9.65, p<0.0001) and was sustained at 24 months. Adult BMI increased by 0.76 kg/m² (95% CI: 0.57 to 0.95, p<0.0001), while pediatric BMI z-score remained stable. In the No-ETI cohort, ppFEV₁ remained stable over two years (90.66±22.23 to 90.01±23.18); adult nutritional parameters were stable, whereas pediatric BMI z-score declined modestly (−0.11; 95% CI: −0.18 to −0.04). Use of inhaled DNase, antibiotics, and oral azithromycin declined after ETI initiation but remained stable or increased in untreated pwCF. Mean annual hospital days decreased from 4.64±10.49 to 1.70±5.06 in ETI-treated pwCF, compared with an increase from 3.14±10.72 to 4.93±12.57 in untreated pwCF.
sections.Conclusion: Initiation of ETI in 2022 was associated with sustained improvements in lung function, nutritional status, and hospital utilization, contrasting with stable or worsening outcomes in untreated pwCF.

sections.Background: The N1303K variant is one of the most second frequent variant and is associated with a severe cystic fibrosis (CF) phenotype. Longitudinal real-world data on clinical outcomes in patients carrying the N1303K variant remain limited, particularly in the context of evolving regulatory approval and access to CFTR modulator therapies.
sections.Objectives: To describe long-term clinical characteristics and pulmonary function changes in patients with the N1303K CFTR variant and to compare these findings with F508del homozygous patients.
sections.Methods: This retrospective registry-based cohort study included patients with genetically confirmed CF recorded in CFRT, between 2020 and 2024. Patients were classified as F508del homozygous, N1303K homozygous, N1303K/F508del compound heterozygous, or N1303K/other CFTR mutation. Changes in anthropometric and spirometric parameters between 2020 and 2024 were evaluated using paired analyses.
sections.Results: During follow-up, CFTR modulator treatment became available for F508del homozygous patients starting in 2021, N1303K homozygous patients did not receive modulator therapy during the study period. Between 2020 and 2024, weight and BMI z-scores did not change significantly in the overall cohort (p=0.71 and p=0.14). Significant improvements were observed in pulmonary function, including absolute FEV₁ (1.93±0.95 to 2.15±0.98 L, p<0.001), percent predicted FEV₁ (83.08±25.6 to 87±25.24, p=0.039), absolute FVC (2.32±1.13 to 2.57±1.21 L, p<0.001), and percent predicted FVC (83.38±20.7 to 92.3±22.51, p<0.001). Improvements were most consistent among F508del homozygous patients (ppFEV₁ p=0.002), whereas patients carrying the N1303K variant showed more limited and heterogeneous changes across genotype subgroups.
sections.Conclusion: In this national registry-based cohort, patients with the N1303K CFTR variant exhibited limited and heterogeneous pulmonary function changes over time compared with F508del homozygous patients receiving modulator therapy.

Methods: A multicenter descriptive study, combining retrospective and prospective data, was...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Mohammed VI University of Health Sciences, Casablanca, Morocco, 2Mohammed V Military Hospital of Rabat, Rabat, Morocco

sections.Background: Cystic fibrosis (CF) has long been considered rare in Morocco; however, its apparent scarcity largely reflects underdiagnosis linked to the absence of newborn screening, limited access to sweat testing, and restricted availability of molecular analyses. The lack of a national registry hampers accurate assessment of disease burden and standardization of pediatric care.
sections.Objectives: To characterize the clinical, genetic, and epidemiological profiles of patients followed in five Moroccan centers and to assess the operational feasibility of implementing a national CF registry aligned with international standards.
sections.Methods: A multicenter descriptive study, combining retrospective and prospective data, was conducted from 2006 to 2024. Clinical, anthropometric, microbiological, and molecular data were collected using a standardized, harmonized form. Statistical analysis included parametric and non-parametric tests according to variable distribution.
sections.Results: Eighty-two patients were included. Most diagnoses were made during childhood, with substantial diagnostic delays. Respiratory symptoms were the most common initial presentation, frequently associated with malnutrition. Chronic colonization was dominated by Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus. Genetic analysis revealed marked heterogeneity, with predominance of F508del and several rare variants specific to the Moroccan population. The study demonstrated the technical and organizational feasibility of establishing a structured national registry.
sections.Conclusion: CF is underdiagnosed in Morocco and presents predominantly with severe pediatric manifestations exacerbated by delayed diagnosis. This study provides the first standardized national dataset and represents a foundational step toward implementing a Moroccan CF registry, critical for improving health equity, harmonizing care protocols, and strengthening pediatric management at national and regional levels.

sections.Objectives: The Cystic Fibrosis Registry of Türkiye(CFRT) was established in 2017 and contains longitudinal demographic and clinical data on people living with cystic fibrosis(CF).This study aimed to evaluate the quality of records in the CFRT through on-site data verification visits within the framework of the European Cystic Fibrosis Society Patient Registry project,which aims to implement measures to improve data reliability for registry based research.
sections.Methods: The 2024 data recorded in CFRT was on-site validated by CFRT board members and data quality managers.During these visits, parameters with the highest number of data-entry errors(mutations,gender,date of birth, FEV1%, body weight and height, treatments, and complications) were compared with medical records.The accuracy,consistency and completeness of variables were reviewed.
sections.Results: A total of 8 in 43 pediatric and adult CF centers were planned to be on-site validated for 2024 data.However,5 center visits were conducted between October-December 2025.A total of 229 patient records were reviewed for data validation.Informed consent was successfully obtained from the majority of patients(89.5%),with the remaining cases mainly due to the absence of renewed consent.Agreement between CFRT entries and medical records ranged from 86% to 100% across variables.Accuracy exceeded 90% for gender, date of visit, genetic data,anthropometric measurements, infections, medications, and CF-related complications.An overall accuracy of 86% was observed for FEV1%, and identified differences were primarily related to variations in data entry practices.
sections.Conclusion: On-site data validation visits are a core component of assessing and improving the data quality of CF registry.These findings confirm the overall high quality and reliability of CFRT data.Importantly,the validation process not only supports the robustness of existing data but also provides valuable insights to guide targeted improvements.https://www.datawrapper.de/_/glAfC/

sections.Objectives: To describe the demographic, educational, employment, and family characteristics of adult patients with cystic fibrosis (CF) attending a specialized center in Argentina during 2025.
sections.Methods: Cross-sectional descriptive study analyzing the annual registry of all adult patients (≥15 years) with CF followed at the Adult Cystic Fibrosis Center Hospital Rossi during 2025. Data collected included age, gender, age at diagnosis, educational level, employment status, marital status, living arrangements, family planning, health insurance coverage, and vaccination status.
sections.Results: Forty-six adult patients were included: 43.5% aged 15-29 years, 37% aged 30-44 years, 17.4% aged 45-59 years, and 2.2% >60 years. The cohort was predominantly male (63%). Age at diagnosis revealed 52.2% before 6 years, 17.4% between 6-15 years, and 30.4% after 15 years. Educational achievement: 76% completed secondary education and 15.2% obtained university degrees, with 39% currently pursuing higher education. Employment rates: 63% were employed (41.3% full-time, 13% part-time, 17.4% self-employed), only 4.3% reported inability to work due to health status. Most patients lived with family (56.5%) or partners (32.6%); 8.7% lived alone. Fifty percent expressed desire to have children, and 10 biological children had been born. Health insurance coverage was nearly universal (97.8%). Vaccination rates: 30.4% received complete schedules, 26.1% VRS vaccine, 47.8% influenza, and 82.6% COVID-19.
sections.Conclusion: The high proportion of late diagnoses (30.4% after age 15) underscores the need for increased CF awareness. Significant vaccination gaps, particularly for respiratory pathogens, represent modifiable risk factors requiring immediate intervention. These findings provide valuable insights for developing targeted care strategies addressing specific needs of adult CF patients in Latin America, where registry data remains limited compared to European and North American cohorts.

sections.Background: Since 2023, data has been collected to maintain the CF Registry in Kazakhstan in accordance with ECFSPR standards.
sections.Objectives: To present the clinical characteristics of adult pwCF in Kazakhstan in 2025.
sections.Methods: By the end of 2025, 252 pwCF were registered, 37 of them (14.68%) were 18 years of age and older. The diagnosis of CF in all cases was confirmed by a positive sweat test and/or genetic testing.
sections.Results: 37 adults (20 men), aged 23.94 ± 4.95 (min 18, max 45), median age at diagnosis 13.2 years (min 1, max 36 years). Six patients reached the age of 18 in 2025. One patient returned to treatment after a long break, one patient traveled outside of Kazakhstan and was lost to follow-up, and one patient was diagnosed with CF for the first time at the age of 21. Data for 27 patients are available for analysis. The F508del allele frequency in the group is 57.4%, BMI is 19.9 kg/m2, FEV1 is 59.2 ± 72.8%, and FVC is 68.1 ± 6.9%. Chronic P. aeruginosa infection was found in 85.2% of patients, and S. aureus infection in 14.8%. CF complications are diabetes mellitus (2 patients, 7.4%), liver disease (5 patients, 18.5%), and respiratory failure (40.7%). Therapy: 70.4% of patients were treated with dornase alfa, 55.6% with bronchodilators, 55.6% with inhaled steroids, 67.0% with hypertonic saline, 66.7% with intravenous antibiotics, 62.3% with oral antibiotics, 81.4% with inhaled antibiotics, and 74% with pancreatic enzymes. Only 31% do physiotherapy, and 25.9% receive long-term low-flow oxygen. Currently, 22 patients (81.5%) receive CFTR-modulator therapy.
sections.Conclusion: The proportion of adult patients with CF in Kazakhstan remains low. The patient cohort is characterized by late diagnosis, significant decrease in pulmonary function and nutritional status, high incidence of chronic P. aeruginosa infection, and other complications. This initiative aims to improve the CF diagnosis and treatment in the country.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1BPS, Pulmonology, Montevideo, Uruguay, 2BPS, Gastroenterology, Montevideo, Uruguay, 3BPS, Genetics, Montevideo, Uruguay, 4Bps, Pulmonology, Montevideo, Uruguay, 5BPS, Nursing, Montevideo, Uruguay, 6BPS, Nutrition, Montevideo, Uruguay

sections.Background: CFTR modulators have significantly changed the clinical course of cystic fibrosis (CF), however, there is limited evidence in the Latin American pediatric population
sections.Objectives: To evaluate the clinical effects of lumacaftor/ivacaftor and elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor after one year of treatment in children with CF in Uruguay.
sections.Methods: A retrospective observational study was conducted at a CF referral center in Uruguay. Fifty-three patients aged 2 to 16 years treated with CFTR modulators for ≥12 months were included. The mean age at baseline was 7.7 years; approximately one-third were under 6 years old. Sweat tests, FEV1, nutritional status (weight/height or BMI according to WHO standards), and pancreatic function using human fecal elastase were analyzed. Genetic distribution: homozygous F508del 32.1%, heterozygous F508del 54.7%, and other class II variants 13.2%. Statistical analysis was performed using JAMOVi v2.3.
sections.Results: Sweat chloride significantly decreased from baseline to 12 months, with a median reduction of 36 mmol/L (Student’s t = 17.8; p<0.001). Mean FEV1 increased from 76.8% to 88.1% predicted (p<0.001). No patient achieved normalization of pancreatic insufficiency, although a mean increase of 25.9 µg/g in fecal elastase was observed. Nutritionally, 69% had normal status; overweight risk and overweight increased from 10% to 12%, with no cases of obesity.
sections.Conclusion: CFTR modulators were associated with significant improvements in sweat chloride and lung function in pediatric CF patients, including those under 6 years of age. Pancreatic insufficiency persisted, highlighting the need for long-term digestive follow-up.

presentations.authors_text: K. Ocskay1, M. Imrei1, I. Gönczi1, L. Barlai1, D. Takács1, V. Csuzdi1, A. Gál1, A. Párniczky1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Heim Pál National Pediatric Institute, Budapest, Hungary, 2University of Pécs, Institute for Translational Medicine, 7624, Hungary

sections.Background: Long-term clinical trials of ETI have demonstrated sustained improvements in lung function, nutritional status, symptoms, and sweat chloride levels in people with cystic fibrosis (CF). However, real-world data suggest that lung function may decline over time in some patients despite ongoing ETI therapy, highlighting the need to assess long-term outcomes in borader, unselected paediatric populations.
sections.Methods: This single-centre prospective registry-based study enrolled children with CF on ETI therapy. Key parameters were prospectively collected at baseline (initiation of ETI) and after 24 months, including sweat chloride concentration (ChloroChek Chloridometer), fecal elastase-1 (FE-1), ppFEV1, BMI z-score, body fat %, and skeletal muscle % (using InBody 770). Wilcoxon signed-rank test was used.
sections.Results: Data from 22 children were assessed (male 7/22 (32%), age at ETI initiation 11.0 (IQR: 8.4-12.7) Over 24 months of ETI therapy, sweat chloride concentration decreased markedly from a median of 79 mmol/L (IQR: 68.3–95.3) to 40 mmol/L (IQR: 23.8–53.5; p<0.001), indicating substantial improvement in CFTR function. FE-1 showed a non-significant increasing trend from 19 ng/g (IQR: 15–42) to 51 ng/g (IQR: 15–321.5; p=0.055), suggesting potential partial recovery of exocrine pancreatic function. Lung function improved significantly, with ppFEV1 rising from 89.5% (IQR: 78.5–102.3) to 97.5% (IQR: 88.3–114; p=0.015). BMI z-score increased from -0.3 (IQR: -0.8–0.3) to 0.2 (IQR: -0.4–0.6; p=0.016), and body fat % rose from 17.4% (IQR: 11.6–21.7) to 18.9% (IQR: 15.3–24.3; p=0.025). Skeletal muscle % remained stable (42.1% [IQR: 39–44.7] to 43.3% [IQR: 40.2–45]; p=0.483).
sections.Conclusion: In this real-world paediatric cohort, ETI therapy was associated with significant and sustained clinical benefits over two years, including marked reductions in sweat chloride, improvements in lung function and BMI z-score, and favourable changes in body composition.

sections.Methods: This retrospective study analyzed microbiological, clinical and lung function data from CF patients followed at the national outpatient CF center during 2024.
sections.Results: A total of 60 patients were included, of whom 52 were actively followed (36 patients <18 years, 16 patients ≥18 years); chronic respiratory infection was highly prevalent. At least one pulmonary exacerbation treated with IVAs occurred in 77.8% of children and 56.3% of adults. Among those treated, multiple exacerbations were common: ≥2 IV-treated exacerbations in 39.3% of children and 55.6% of adults. Mean total days on IV antibiotics ranged from 14 to 17 days in children and 12 days in adults, with a substantial proportion requiring hospitalization. Airway microbiology revealed a high prevalence of chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection (52.8% of children and 87.5% of adults); Staphylococcus aureus (children 83.3%; adults 93.8%). Stenotrophomonas maltophilia was infrequently isolated and limited to paediatric patients and Aspergilus fumigatus was found in 18 patients. Despite intensive standard therapy, including inhaled antibiotics in 69.2%, rhDNase in 65.3%, and hypertonic saline in 78.8% of patients, infection burden remained substantial. Mean lung function values reflected moderate pulmonary impairment with a mean FEV₁ of 73.1% predicted and mean FVC of 84.9% predicted. Two deaths due to respiratory causes were recorded during the study period.
sections.Conclusion: CF patients in the RM experience a very high respiratory diseases burden. These findings highlight the urgent need for strengthened access to comprehensive CF care and advanced therapies in underrepresented Eastern European settings.

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has transformed cystic fibrosis (CF) care, representing a major therapeutic milestone. While short-term benefits are well documented, long-term outcomes beyond one year remain limited. Five-year follow-up (5y-FU) data are scarce, and no studies have compared baseline parameters with those obtained after five years of continuous therapy.
sections.Methods: In the Ruhrlandklinik we included 87 people with CF (pwCF) who initiated ETI in 2020 and completed 5y-FU in 2025. Analysed parameters were demographics such as gender, age, genotype, Body Mass Index (BMI), percent predicted forced expiratory volume in one second (ppFEV₁), six-minute walk test (6MWT), sweat chloride levels, and the respiratory domain of the Cystic Fibrosis Questionnaire-Revised (CFQ-R). The results are presented as mean with standard deviation (SD).
sections.Results: In our cohort, 56.3% of pwCF were male and the age at therapy initiation was 32.4±10.4 years. With regard to genotype, 52 pwCF (59.8%) were homozygous for F508del, while 35 (40.2%) were F508 del heterozygous. Over 5y-FU, BMI increased from 20.7±2.92 to 22.5±3.03 kg/m² (p<0.001), and ppFEV₁ improved from 45.5±18.9 to 63.2±22.4 (p<0.001). The 6MWT distance was higher at 5y-FU (512.3±115.6 m vs. 577.3±95.2 m, p=0.003). Sweat chloride decreased from 104.3±16 mmol/L to 50.9±15.9 mmol/L (p<0.001). CFQ-R respiratory scores rosed from 54.5±21.6 to 81.2±18.5 (p<0.001).
sections.Conclusion: Five years of ETI therapy led to sustained improvements in nutrition, lung function, exercise capacity, and quality of life. These real-world results show that ETI is a highly effective long-term treatment for adult pwCF.

sections.Objectives: To describe the characteristics of patients (pts) with cystic fibrosis and the scope of therapy.
sections.Methods: The 2024 Registry included data on 4,355 pts: 3,550 alive and 23 deceased observed in the current year.
sections.Results: The average age of pts was 13.6 (8.3-20.5) years, with 32.5% being over 18 years of age. The age at diagnosis was 0.3 (0.1-2.4) years. The diagnosis was made by neonatal screening in 56.7% of pts. The proportion of pts with 2 identified genetic variants was 95.7%. Respiratory function according to FEV1 was 85.7±27.6%, FVC was 91.5±22.2%. BMI Me (25th–75th pctl) was 37.4 (14.8-66.6) among children; 20.2 (18.4-22.5) among adults. Sa was detected in the respiratory tract of 66.5% of pts, MRSA– in 6%, Pa -38%, Bcc-4.3%. Chronic sinusitis with nasal polyps was diagnosed in 36%, without polyps in 31.6%, allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis-2.9%, pneumothorax requiring drainage-0.2%, liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and hypersplenism-3.7%, without portal hypertension /hypersplenism-2.4%, diabetes mellitus with insulin use-5.6%, osteoporosis (low bone mass)-3.5%, pulmonary hemorrhage-0.7%, electrolyte disorders-1.8%, cancer-0.3%, amyloidosis-0.4%, depression-4.4%, pancreatitis-2.7%, distal intestinal obstruction syndrome-1.3%, arthritis-1.7%, gastroesophageal reflux disease -12.3%, bronchial asthma-4.4%. Dornase alfa therapy was received by 92.1%, hypertonic saline-69.8%, mannitol- 12.3%, physiotherapy-72%, inhalation antibacterial drugs-39%, intravenous-39.1%, oral-60.4%, azithromycin in a subinhibitory dose-16.9%, UDCA-84.1%, pancreatic enzymes-89.2%, fat-soluble vitamins-89%, oxygen therapy-3.3%. CFTR modulator therapy was used in 2262 pts (60.5%), including 684 adult (55.1%) and 1614 children (63%).
sections.Conclusion: 90% of Russian pts are included in the registry, an increase in lung function to normal levels and a high percentage of gram-negative infections are noted, while the number of pts receiving CFTR modulators increases to 60.5%.

sections.Methods: We analyzed clinical data before initiation, at one year and 2,5years after receiving ETI for 7 adult pwCF with at least one F508del allele and severe lung disease at CF center Kozle, University clinic for respiratory diseases in children, Skopje, R.N. Macedonia. Assessments included FEV1%, weight, BMI, sweat chloride, use of pancreatic enzymes and insulin, and incidence of DIOS over 1 and 2.5 years after ETI initiation.
sections.Results: 7 adult pwCF mean age 37.1y (43-31y) were included. Sweat chloride concentration decreased from 105.6 mmol/L (CI 130-75) before to 51.1 mmol/L (CI 90-30) after first year, and 58 mmol/l (100-30 CL) at 2, 5 years (p<0.01). BMI z-scores increased slightly but with no statistical significance (baseline 23.6, first year 24.7 and after 2,5y BMI was 24).Pancreatic enzyme use remained with no decrease in use. Insulin utilization was equally unchanged, and DIOS incidence remained low (around 2.5%). This cohort experienced significant improvements in FEV1% (p<0.01) from baseline of 32.6% (20-40FEV1%), at first year was 49.6 %( 22-65%) and 46.9 % ( 23-65%) at 2.5 years. 5 pw CF discontinued inhalator therapy on their own, one had no effect of ETI due to double complex alleles L467F-508F, 2 had depression and one patient died because of cardiorespiratory failure.
sections.Conclusion: ETI significantly improved sweat chloride and FEV1% over a period of 2, 5 years, whereas no significant changes were observed for prevalence of pancreatic enzymes or insulin and for incidence of DIOS.

presentations.summary: Objectives: Cystic fibrosis(CF) is a multisystem genetic disease associated with significant morbidity and mortality, mainly due to progressive lung damage and infectious complications. Despite therapeutic advances and the introduction of CFTR modulator therapies, death still occurs at young ages. Mortality analysis provides valuable insight into the natural course of the disease and its clinical and imaging prognostic factors. The aim is to evaluate mortality characteristics among patients with cystic fibrosis in the Republic of Moldova. Methods: A retrospective descriptive study was conducted, including deceased CF patients followed at the National Cystic Fibrosis Centre of the Republic of Moldova between 20...
presentations.authors_text: C. Conica1,2, C. Tomacinschii1,2, D. Efros1,2, L. Rusu3, N. Buga2, A. Cotoman1, R. Selevestru2, S. Sciuca2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Mother and Child Institute, Pediatrics, Chișinau, Moldova, Republic of, 2State University of Medicine and Pharmacy „Nicolae Testemitanu”,, Chișinau, Moldova, Republic of, 3Children Emergency Hospital, Cluj Napoca, Romania

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis(CF) is a multisystem genetic disease associated with significant morbidity and mortality, mainly due to progressive lung damage and infectious complications. Despite therapeutic advances and the introduction of CFTR modulator therapies, death still occurs at young ages. Mortality analysis provides valuable insight into the natural course of the disease and its clinical and imaging prognostic factors. The aim is to evaluate mortality characteristics among patients with cystic fibrosis in the Republic of Moldova.
sections.Methods: A retrospective descriptive study was conducted, including deceased CF patients followed at the National Cystic Fibrosis Centre of the Republic of Moldova between 2022 and 2025. Data regarding causes of death, pulmonary imaging findings, comorbidities, and paraclinical parameters were analysed, acknowledging limitations related to irregular medical follow-up.
sections.Results: Seven patients were included, with a mean age at death of 14.29 ± 9.5 years (range 1–26 years); 71.4% were female. The mean age at diagnosis was 2 years, with 57% diagnosed during the first year of life. Death was predominantly associated with advanced lung disease and severe infectious complications. Sepsis was the leading cause of death in 57% of cases. CT findings suggestive of advanced lung disease were identified in 85,71% of patients, and bronchiectasis in 71,43%. P. aeruginosa and S. aureus were detected in 100% of sputum cultures. The delF508 genotype and severe pancreatic insufficiency were present in all patients; related diabetes in 28,57% and pulmonary aspergillosis in 57,14%, including 1 case of invasive bronchopulmonary aspergillosis.
sections.Conclusion: Mortality in cystic fibrosis in the Republic of Moldova occurs predominantly at young ages and is strongly associated with advanced structural lung disease, as evidenced by thoracic CT abnormalities in 85,71% of cases. Severe infectious complications, particularly sepsis, were the main causes of death.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Ivan Franko National University of Lviv, Lviv, Ukraine, 2West Ukrainian Specialised Children Hospital, Lviv, Ukraine, 3Regional Clinical Children's Hospital, Department of Pulmonology, Zaporizhzhia, Ukraine, 4Shupyk National Healthcare University of Ukraine, Kyiv, Ukraine, 5Regional Children's Clinical Hospital, CF Center, Mukachevo, Ukraine, 6Lviv Regional Hospital of Veterans of Wars and Repressed Named after Yu.Lypa, Lviv, Ukraine, 7Scientific Medical Genetic Center LeoGENE, Lviv, Ukraine, 8Lviv Regional Clinical Perinatal Center, Regional Center of Neonatal Screening, Lviv, Ukraine

sections.Objectives: Nutritional status and lung function (LF) are key prognostic factors in cystic fibrosis (CF). The global shift towards highly effective CFTR modulators has transformed CF care, with rapid weight gain reported as early treatment effects. This study aimed to establish a pre‑modulator baseline of nutritional and pulmonary status in Ukrainian patients with CF and to examine how BMI and CFTR genotype relate to LF prior to widespread ETI therapy.
sections.Methods: We conducted a cross‑sectional analysis of 512 Ukrainian patients with CF recorded in the 2023 European Cystic Fibrosis Society Patient Registry (ECFSPR). Patients were grouped into three CFTR genotype categories: F508del homozygous, F508del heterozygous and other genotypes. Associations between BMI and LF were assessed using Spearman’s rank correlation and multivariable linear regression adjusting for age and sex. Genotype differences were evaluated using the Kruskal–Wallis test with Bonferroni‑adjusted Dunn post hoc comparisons.
sections.Results: Among 512 patients (51.2% male), the median age was 12.5 y. and the median BMI z-score was −0.8 (Q1 −1.7, Q3 −0.03). Analyses were performed in 278 patients with complete anthropometric and LF data. BMI z-score was positively correlated with FEV₁% (ρ=0.21, p<0.001) and FVC% (ρ=0.21, p<0.001). Higher BMI z-score was associated with higher FEV₁% (β=5.97, 95% CI 3.02–8.92, p<2.7×10⁻⁹) and FVC% (β=5.63, 95% CI 2.78–8.48, p<3.1×10⁻⁸), while age showed a strong negative effect. BMI z-score did not differ between genotype groups. In contrast, FEV₁% and FVC% were higher in F508del homozygous and heterozygous patients compared with other genotypes (p<0.002; p<0.001), partly reflecting differences in age distribution (median age 10.4 vs 12.0 years).
sections.Conclusion: Better nutritional status, reflected by higher BMI z-scores, was consistently associated with better LF, as is well established. Further analysis will assess nutritional and pulmonary association following ETI introduction.

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) exhibits heterogeneous lung disease progression, which may be worsened by environmental exposures such as air pollution. We aimed to evaluate the non-linear and age-specific effects of PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, and NO₂ on pulmonary exacerbations in children with CF using data from the French CF Registry.
sections.Methods: Data from 909 children (ages 6–12) were analyzed. Pulmonary exacerbations were modeled with a negative binomial generalized linear mixed model accounting for nested clustering. Non-linear pollutant effects and age interactions were assessed using departmental air quality data.
sections.Results: Predicted exacerbation counts increased non-linearly with PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, and NO₂, showing clear age-specific patterns. Six- and 8-year-olds exhibited substantial increases with PM₁₀ and NO₂ (p<0.001), whereas 10- and 12-year-olds were less affected. PM₂.₅ effects were pronounced in 12-year-olds (p<0.001) but minimal in younger children. These findings highlight strong age-dependent vulnerability to air pollution.
sections.Conclusion: Air pollution is associated with higher pulmonary exacerbations in CF, especially among younger children. Considering non-linear and age-specific effects is critical for risk assessment and supports targeted interventions to reduce exposure in vulnerable populations. Acknowledgements: We thank Vaincre la Mucoviscidose, CF centres, and all patients for participation. This work is part of the REMEDIA project, funded by EU Horizon 2020 (Grant No. 874753).

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a key indication for lung transplantation (LT). Despite medical advances, many adults develop end-stage lung disease and require LT. The geographical impact on access to LT is unclear. We aimed to quantify differences in waitlist outcomes by geographical region, as defined by the United Network for Organ Sharing (UNOS).
sections.Methods: We utilized the UNOS database, including adults listed for single-organ LT from January 2005 to November 2023. We analyzed baseline characteristics with Chi-square and Kruskal-Wallis tests. Waitlist outcomes (transplant, death/deterioration) were modeled using Fine-Gray competing risks; subdistribution hazard ratios (sHRs) were reported as 95% confidence intervals. Models adjusted for age, BMI, lung allocation score (LAS), diabetes history and sex. Summary data was reported as overall median [IQR] and the median [IQR] of the medians of each region.

After adjustment, the sHRs for death/deterioration on the waitlist were significant in Great Lakes (Region 10) with sHR 0.553[0.308-0.995] and Southern (Region 11) regions with sHR 0.538[0.309-0.939], indicating independent association of the Great Lakes and Southern regions with better waitlist outcomes.
sections.Conclusion: Geographic disparities in CF LT waitlist access and outcomes exist across UNOS regions. Policy and allocation strategies should aim to reduce regional variation and promote equitable access to donor lungs for patients with CF.

While triple-therapy CFTR modulators have improved clinical outcomes for many, CF remains a lifelong, multisystem condition requiring intensive specialist care. Significant financial pressures, treatment burden, and psychosocial challenges persist. Your Life and CF builds on the 2022 Cost of CF baseline to track these experiences over time. Objectives: To describe the scale of need, year-on-year trends, and changes in lived experience reported by people affected by CF between 2022 and 2025. Methods: Between 2022 and 2025, CFT condu...
presentations.authors_text: K. Earlam1, A. Billin1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Cystic Fibrosis Trust, Policy and Public Affairs, London, United Kingdom

sections.Background: For four years, Cystic Fibrosis Trust (CFT) has run Your Life and CF, an annual survey capturing the lived experiences of people with cystic fibrosis (CF) across the UK. While triple-therapy CFTR modulators have improved clinical outcomes for many, CF remains a lifelong, multisystem condition requiring intensive specialist care. Significant financial pressures, treatment burden, and psychosocial challenges persist. Your Life and CF builds on the 2022 Cost of CF baseline to track these experiences over time.
sections.Objectives: To describe the scale of need, year-on-year trends, and changes in lived experience reported by people affected by CF between 2022 and 2025.
sections.Methods: Between 2022 and 2025, CFT conducted annual surveys with around 40 mixed-method questions covering physical and mental health, cost of living, treatment burden, neurodiversity, and access to care. Core measures were repeated annually to monitor trends.
sections.Results: Across four years, findings show persistent financial and treatment burden despite clinical progress. In 2022, 87% worried about money, three in ten had run out of food, and one in three missed essential appointments due to cost. Subsequent surveys show continued cost-of-living pressures affecting food, travel, energy, and household essentials. In 2025, respondents spent almost four hours per day on treatments. Mental health concerns remained high: 70% felt lonely at least some of the time and 90% worried about the future. Nearly 29% identified as neurodiverse in 2025, with most reporting an impact on their CF care.
sections.Conclusion: The longitudinal data demonstrate that, despite the advent of transformative therapies, people with CF continue to face substantial financial, mental health, and treatment burdens that shape daily life and future prospects. These insights underline the importance of holistic policy responses that extend beyond clinical benchmarks to include social, economic, and psychological dimensions of living with CF.
