presentations.authors_text: T. Kazmerski1, O. Stransky1, M. Lee2, R. Jain3, A. Georgiopoulos4, J. Billings5, E. Dellon6, K. Ramos7, N. West8, J. Taylor9
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Pittsburgh School of Medicine, Pediatrics, Pittsburgh, United States, 2University of Texas Houston, Houston, United States, 3University of Texas Southwestern, Dallas, United States, 4Massachusetts General Hospital, Boston, United States, 5University of Minnesota, Minneapolis, United States, 6University of North Carolina, Chapel Hill, United States, 7University of Washington, Seattle, United States, 8Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, United States, 9National Jewish Health, Denver, United States

sections.Objectives: Using Health Outcomes of Parents with CF (HOPeCF) retrospective data, we examined pulmonary exacerbation (PEx) rates among parents and nonparents with CF (PwCF; NPwCF).
sections.Methods: We surveyed PwCF who became new parents between 2012-2022 and NPwCF and linked results to their 2010-2023 patient registry data. We assessed PEx for two cohorts (2012-2018; 2019-2022). We designated time points as year -1 and -2 (pre-parenthood), year 0 (parenthood onset; assigned as 2015 and 2021 for NPwCF), and year 1-5 (post-parenthood). We evaluated longitudinal change in PEx rate using multivariable longitudinal negative binomial regression models adjusted for age, race, lung function, ever ivacaftor or elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor (ETI) use, insurance status, number of children, and years of parenthood.
sections.Results: We recruited 970 participants (253 PwCF/717 NPwCF). PwCF were 59% female, 91% White, 17% Medicaid-insured, and 90% ever used ivacaftor/ETI. In the 2012-2018 cohort, longitudinal change in PEx rate differed significantly between PwCF and NPwCF(p=0.02). PwCF had a significant increase in PEx rate year -1-0 (adjusted rate ratio =1.38[1.09,1.76]; p<0.01). In year 1-2, PwCF had a decrease in PEx rate (0.81[0.64,1.02]; p=0.07) and NPwCF a significant increase (1.21[1.08,1.35]; p<0.01). Both groups experienced a significant decrease in PEx year 4-5 (likely due to widespread ETI use). In the 2019-2022 cohort, longitudinal change in PEx rate did not significantly differ between PwCF and NPwCF(p=0.11). Both experienced a significant decrease in PEx from year -2 to -1 while only PwCF had a significant decrease in PEx from year -1-0 (likely due to ETI). Comparing mothers vs. fathers, we found no significant differences in PEx rates over time.
sections.Conclusion: Parenthood largely does not impact longitudinal PEx rates among people with CF especially in the ETI era. Furthermore, we found no differences in PEx rates by parental sex.

presentations.title: Pregnancy care and outcomes in patients with cystic fibrosis at a UK specialist centre
presentations.summary: Objectives: Advances in cystic fibrosis (CF) therapies, including highly effective modulator therapy, have resulted in increased pregnancies among people with CF (pwCF). Currently there are no clear guidelines on the management of pregnancy in pwCF. The Oxford Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre (OACFC) provides regional specialist care for pwCF. We aimed to evaluate pregnancy management, and maternal and infant outcomes in pwCF under our care. Methods: Pregnancies between 2005 and 2025 were identified using the UK Cystic Fibrosis Registry and cross-referenced with electronic patient records. Data were collected on maternal characteristics, pregnancy complications, delivery outcomes, infant outcomes, and clinician ma...
presentations.authors_text: A. Yue1, H. Caterson1, A. Gates1, N. Hough1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Oxford Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Oxford University Hospitals, Oxford, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Advances in cystic fibrosis (CF) therapies, including highly effective modulator therapy, have resulted in increased pregnancies among people with CF (pwCF). Currently there are no clear guidelines on the management of pregnancy in pwCF. The Oxford Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre (OACFC) provides regional specialist care for pwCF. We aimed to evaluate pregnancy management, and maternal and infant outcomes in pwCF under our care.
sections.Methods: Pregnancies between 2005 and 2025 were identified using the UK Cystic Fibrosis Registry and cross-referenced with electronic patient records. Data were collected on maternal characteristics, pregnancy complications, delivery outcomes, infant outcomes, and clinician management practices.
sections.Results: Data were available for 15 pwCF, comprising 24 pregnancies. 15 pregnancies resulted in live births (11 vaginal deliveries and four caesarean sections), 4 miscarriages and 5 terminations. Twelve pregnancies occurred in individuals receiving CFTR modulator therapy. Mean baseline lung function (FEV1) was 74.7% (range 18-118%). 5 pregnancies were identified as high risk by lung function and BMI; 3 resulted in a live birth at term, 2 required pre-term Caesarean section. Pulmonary exacerbations occurred in 11 pregnancies. Obstetric complications included pre-eclampsia (n=2) and gestational diabetes (n=4), with 5 pregnancies in patients with pre-existing diabetes. Low birth weight was documented in 3 infants (1115-2410kg). Most pregnancies were managed jointly with maternal medicine specialists. Gaps in data were identified, particularly lung function, maternal weight change, and infant health outcomes.
sections.Conclusion: Pregnancy in pwCF is increasingly common and often complicated by CF-related and obstetric comorbidities. Despite this, outcomes are generally favourable highlighting the need for specialist multidisciplinary care. Further work is required to establish standards of care, formalise clinical pathways, and improve data collection.

Maternal nutritional and pulmonary status was described using the closest availab...

sections.Background: The introduction of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has improved health and fertility in women with cystic fibrosis (CF). However, real-world data on pregnancy frequency and outcomes remain limited, particularly outside large registries.
sections.Objectives: To describe pregnancy frequency, maternal characteristics and pregnancy outcomes in women with CF receiving ETI, and to assess the impact of these findings on clinical practice.
sections.Methods: We performed a retrospective review of adult women with CF treated with ETI at a single centre from June 2021 to January 2026. Women of reproductive age (<40 years) were identified. Maternal nutritional and pulmonary status was described using the closest available post-ETI measurements prior to pregnancy. Pregnancy outcomes were recorded.
sections.Results: Among 15 women receiving ETI, 11 were of reproductive age. Five women (range 45.5%) experienced at least one pregnancy. Median time from ETI initiation to conception was 8.4 months (2.3–40.1). Only one pregnancy was planned.Maternal status prior to pregnancy was heterogeneous (BMI 19.1–27.0 kg/m²; ppFEV1 27–90%), including advanced lung disease, with two women requiring oxygen therapy and one listed for transplantation.ETI was continued throughout gestation.At analysis, five pregnancies had reached an outcome: one live birth at 36 weeks, one extremely preterm birth at 27 weeks with neonatal death, one fetal demise at 36 weeks due to hydrops, one early miscarriage, and one elective termination at 11 weeks. Two additional pregnancies were ongoing.
sections.Conclusion: In this experience, nearly half of women of reproductive age receiving ETI conceived, most without prior planning. Outcomes were heterogeneous, precluding causal inference. These findings prompted changes in clinical practice, including systematic reproductive counselling, proactive contraceptive guidance at ETI initiation, and multidisciplinary management of pregnancies within high-risk obstetric care pathways.

presentations.authors_text: E. McClenaghan1, J. Rouette2, E. Granger1, G. Davies3, R. Keogh1, J. Tazare1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine, Department of Medical Statistics, London, United Kingdom, 2GSK, Global Epidemiology, Montreal, Canada, 3UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health, Population, Policy & Practice Dept, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) remain commonly prescribed in cystic fibrosis (CF) despite limited evidence of benefit and substantial treatment burden. A randomised controlled trial (CF-WISE, PMID:1655669) concluded discontinuation of ICS appears safe. We aimed to estimate the causal effect of discontinuing ICS on clinical outcomes using real-world data, applying a target trial emulation framework.
sections.Methods: We conducted a population-based cohort study using UK CF Registry data (2016-2018), emulating a target trial based on CF-WISE. Eligible individuals were aged ≥6 years, had FEV1 ≥40% predicted, and received ≥3 months ICS therapy. Two innovative causal study designs were implemented: (i) prevalent new-user design, and (ii) sequential trials design. ICS discontinuation was compared with continued use, defined using exact prescription start and stop dates in the Registry. Primary outcome was time to first pulmonary exacerbation, defined as intravenous antibiotic treatment. Secondary outcome was change in percent predicted FEV1 over six months. Analyses adjusted for measured confounding using propensity-score based weighting and regression adjustment for methods (i) and (ii) respectively.
sections.Results: Among 3516 people meeting eligibility criteria for the emulated trial, 423 (12%) discontinued inhaled corticosteroids during the study period, while 3093 (88%) continued treatment. Median (IQR) length of ICS use prior to eligibility was 55 months (27-145), and 67% experienced at least one pulmonary exacerbation during the study period. Effect estimates for pulmonary exacerbation and lung function will be presented and compared across study designs, with benchmarking against the CF-WISE trial.
sections.Conclusion: This study applies and compares robust causal inference methods to address a clinically important question in CF. Findings will inform safety of ICS discontinuation and demonstrate the utility of target trial emulation for evaluating treatment strategies in CF.

While their efficacy has been established in randomized controlled trials, Real-World Evidence (RWE) is key to understanding long-term effectiveness in clinical practice. Real-world data collected through disease registries can help inform the optimal use of ETI in clinical practice, if effectiveness is assessed using robust methods like Target Trial Emulation (TTE). We aimed to assess whether the Dutch CF Registry is suitable for estimating ETI effectiveness via TTE. Methods: We compared two strategies: switching from dual modulator therapy to ETI versus sta...
presentations.authors_text: D.D. Zomer-van Ommen1, J. Stoelinga2, D.L. Weir2, C. Leopold2, R.W. Hofland3, H.M. Janssens4, J.J. Noordhoek-van der Staay1, R. Okhuijsen-Kos1, K.M. de Winter-de Groot5, W.G. Goettsch2,6, Dutch CF Registry Steering Committee
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Dutch CF Foundation, Soest, Netherlands, 2Utrecht University, Pharmaceutical Sciences, Utrecht, Netherlands, 3University Medical Center Utrecht, Pulmonary Medicine, Utrecht, Netherlands, 4Erasmus University Medical Center, Sophia Children's Hospital, Pediatrics, Rotterdam, Netherlands, 5Wilhelmina Children's Hospital, University Medical Center, Utrecht University, Pediatric Pulmonology, Utrecht, Netherlands, 6National Healthcare Institute, Diemen, Netherlands

sections.Objectives: CFTR modulator therapy, such as elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI), has greatly improved treatment of people with Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF). While their efficacy has been established in randomized controlled trials, Real-World Evidence (RWE) is key to understanding long-term effectiveness in clinical practice. Real-world data collected through disease registries can help inform the optimal use of ETI in clinical practice, if effectiveness is assessed using robust methods like Target Trial Emulation (TTE). We aimed to assess whether the Dutch CF Registry is suitable for estimating ETI effectiveness via TTE.
sections.Methods: We compared two strategies: switching from dual modulator therapy to ETI versus staying on dual therapy. Outcomes included lung function, sweat chloride concentration, and quality of life (CFQ-R). Data were obtained from the Dutch CF registry, which extracts data from electronic patient records. Data quality was evaluated using the European Medicines Agency data quality framework.
sections.Results: The Dutch CF Registry contains longitudinal data (2010-2024) of 1,735 pwCF (~95% coverage). Of these, 1,312 initiated ETI, of whom 831 previously received dual modulator therapy. Among the 932 dual therapy users, the number eligible to serve as a comparator group was limited (approximately 101 individuals at specific time points). We noticed changing measurement guidelines and evolving clinical practice over the years, which can impact the consistency of some outcome variables measured.
sections.Conclusion: Despite high coverage, estimating real-world effectiveness from a national CF registry remains challenging. Limitations of the current dataset may impact the use of TTE to guarantee methodological rigor. Our findings suggest future adaptations to the Dutch CF Registry to enable more reliable RWE using TTE on CFTR modulator effectiveness.

Logistic regression models were used to assess temporal trends. Results: In 2023, the prevalence of SM (chronic and intermittent) ranged from 0.26% in Romania to 19.3% in Serbia. Overall, 2556/51566 pw...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Pediatric Pulmonology Institute, Schneider Children’s Medical Center, Petah Tiqwa, Israel, 2Faculty of Medical and Health Sciences, Tel Aviv University, Tel Aviv, Israel, 3Laboratory of Medical Statistics, Biometry and Epidemiology “G. A. Maccacaro”, University of Milan, Department of Clinical Sciences and Community Health, Dipartimento di Eccellenza 2023-2027, Laboratory of Medical Statistics, Biometry and Epidemiology “G. A. Maccacaro”, University of Milan, Milan, Italy

sections.Methods: In this longitudinal study based on the European Cystic Fibrosis Society Patient Registry (ECFSPR), we analyzed the prevalence and incidence of SM airway infection and its association with demographic and clinical characteristics. Logistic regression models were used to assess temporal trends.
sections.Results: In 2023, the prevalence of SM (chronic and intermittent) ranged from 0.26% in Romania to 19.3% in Serbia. Overall, 2556/51566 pwCF across 42 European countries were infected (5.0%; 95% CI: 4.8 to 5.2). SM was associated with older age, increased treatment burden, and more complications (including ABPA and CFRD); chronic SM was also associated with higher steroids and antibiotics use. The prevalence of SM rose markedly between 2018 and 2023, with an inflection point around 2019—showing an upward trend beforehand and a gradual decline thereafter. Among 32,724 pwCF, the most common infection patterns were as follows: 75.2% (24,594) had no infections, 24.8% (8,130) had SM infection in at least one year, and 11.1% (3,640) had SM infection for at least two years.
sections.Conclusion: This large analysis offers a detailed view of SM infection in the European CF population, revealing that while most individuals remained uninfected, nearly one-quarter experienced infection between 2018 and 2023. Chronic infection was increased with age and was consistently linked to greater co infection burden. Temporal patterns—rising until 2019 and gradually declining in the CFTR modulator and post COVID era—indicate that medical advancements and shifts in healthcare practice influence SM epidemiology. These findings highlight the need for continued surveillance to guide care and research.

sections.Objectives: Access to bacterial isolates and clinical data is essential for advancing research, yet researchers often face challenges. The University of North Carolina (UNC) developed a Research Electronic Data Capture (REDCap®)-based Automated Microbial Pipeline (RAMP) that identifies clinical bacterial isolates and combines clinical and bacterial data. Here, we describe the use of RAMP for identifying nontuberculous mycobacteria (NTM) positive cultures at UNC in people with and without Cystic Fibrosis (CF).
sections.Methods: The IRB-approved, consent-waived RAMP protocol automatically screens microbiology data uploaded to the electronic health record (EPIC) and extracts relevant isolate and clinical data. Clinical data from defined cohorts, including NTM cohort, are retrieved from the Carolina Data Warehouse for Health (CDWH) using computable phenotypes, transformed into REDCap format, and uploaded into the RAMP project via REDCap API. Within the NTM cohort, RAMP provides demographics, AFB smear and culture results, chronic NTM infection status and antimicrobial susceptibility disclosing minimal protected health information. Isolates are collected, sequenced and banked in a de-identified manner while preserving linkages to identified data.
sections.Results: NTM cohort data extraction began in mid-December 2025. Within the first month, RAMP identified 108 NTM isolates from 89 unique subjects. Most specimens were sputum (84.2%) followed by bronchial lavages (15%). NTM species distribution included Mycobacterium abscessus (41%), M. intracellulare (36.7%) and M. avium complex (17.3%). Specimen and data collection are ongoing.
sections.Conclusion: RAMP is an efficient tool for researchers, facilitating rapid access to isolates and data without disrupting clinical workflows. Early implementation revealed minor limitations requiring manual extraction. These findings demonstrate RAMP’s potential to enhance CF-related NTM research and expand collaboration without major infrastructural changes.

Methods: This observational cohort study used previously collected secondary anonymised Canadian Cystic Fibrosis (CF) Registry data. 6-17-year-old pwCF with ≥1 F508del, available Registry data June 1, 2020-December 31, 2023, initiating ETI June 2021-December 2022, and ≥1 CF respiratory culture in the 12 months pre- and post-ETI initiation were included. P. aeruginosa or MSSA outcomes were defined positive if present in any density on ≥1 culture. Individual data fo...
presentations.authors_text: K. Ebbert1,2, M. Njoroge3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, Epidemiology, London, United Kingdom, 2University of Calgary, Pediatrics, Calgary, Canada, 3London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, Medical Statistics, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: To assess whether elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) is associated with decreased odds of respiratory pathogens P. aeruginosa and methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) in 6-17-year-old persons with cystic fibrosis (pwCF).
sections.Methods: This observational cohort study used previously collected secondary anonymised Canadian Cystic Fibrosis (CF) Registry data. 6-17-year-old pwCF with ≥1 F508del, available Registry data June 1, 2020-December 31, 2023, initiating ETI June 2021-December 2022, and ≥1 CF respiratory culture in the 12 months pre- and post-ETI initiation were included. P. aeruginosa or MSSA outcomes were defined positive if present in any density on ≥1 culture. Individual data for discordant pairs comparing the 12 months pre- and post-ETI initiation were assessed using McNemar's Χ2 test, estimating an odds ratio (OR) for the association between ETI initiation and P. aeruginosa and MSSA.
sections.Results: A total of 591 pwCF were analysed. P. aeruginosa was cultured pre-ETI in 30.4% pwCF, and 14.5% post-ETI; McNemar’s Χ2 OR was 0.19 (95% CI: 0.11-0.30, p<0.001). The risk difference was 15.9% (95% CI: 12.1-19.8%) lower post-ETI. MSSA was cultured in 76.8% pre-ETI and 72.4% post-ETI. McNemar’s Χ2 OR was 0.69 (95% CI: 0.49-0.98, p=0.029). The risk difference was 4.4% (95% CI: 0.2-8.5%) lower post-ETI.
sections.Conclusion: Odds of P. aeruginosa positive culture in children were reduced by 81% with ETI treatment initiation. ETI was strongly associated with reduced identification of P. aeruginosa on CF culture, which is important given its impact on early CF lung disease and disease progression. Despite reduced odds of MSSA culture positivity, the small risk difference suggests limited clinical significance. Limitations of this paired analysis include lack of adjustment for time-varying confounders and reduced sputum availability on ETI. Further adjusted analysis comparing this cohort with pwCF eligible for, but not on ETI, in this time frame is also planned.

presentations.title: Antimicrobial coatings on adhesive copper tapes as a method for preventing hospital-acquired infections and cross-contamination of patients
presentations.summary: Nosocomial infections (NIs) remain a major healthcare challenge and pose a major threat to cystic fibrosis patients. The spread of pathogens occurs not only from patients but also via contaminated surfaces. This leads to cross-infection. Innovative approaches, such as the use of antimicrobial coatings on high-touch surfaces, are being developed to reduce the infection risk. Objective: To evaluate the effectiveness of antimicrobial coatings on adhesive copper tapes, developed at the A.N. Frumkin Institute of Physical Chemistry and Electrochemistry (Moscow), in preventing NIs. Materials and Methods: A 5-month clinical study was conducted in a hospital setting. 1,440 swabs were taken from 24 touch surfaces (eleva...
presentations.authors_text: M. Chernukha1,2, L. Avetisyan1, E. Domblides1, A. Emelyanenko2, L. Boinovich2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Federal State Budgetary Institution «National Research Center for Epidemiology and Microbiology named after the Honorary Academician N.F. Gamaleya, Moscow, Russian Federation, 2Frumkin Institute of Physical Chemistry and Electrochemistry, Russian Academy of Sciences, Moscow, Russian Federation

sections.Objectives: To evaluate the effectiveness of antimicrobial coatings on adhesive copper tapes, developed at the A.N. Frumkin Institute of Physical Chemistry and Electrochemistry (Moscow), in preventing NIs. Materials and
sections.Methods: A 5-month clinical study was conducted in a hospital setting. 1,440 swabs were taken from 24 touch surfaces (elevator buttons, switches, door handles). 4 surface types were compared: K (control) – stainless steel, A – copper oxide/titanium oxide composite on laser-textured copper foil, T – laser-textured copper foil, M – smooth copper foil. Bacteriological methods and MALDI-TOF were used, with positive cultures from surfaces recorded (grant 23-73-30004 Russian Science Foundation).
sections.Results: Control surfaces showed 64.63% positive samples, whereas laser-textured copper had only 22.67%. Smooth copper and the composite demonstrated 28–29% positive results. A total of 40 bacterial species were isolated from all surfaces, with Staphylococcus epidermidis, Micrococcus luteus, and Staphylococcus aureus being dominant. Notably, copper and composite surfaces exhibited lower microbial diversity and significantly reduced pathogen detection rates, especially for S. aureus (cell count ratio for surfaces K/M/T/A = 43/2/1/1.4). The overall microbial load on textured copper was 26.8 times lower than on stainless steel (p<0.05). Composite coatings also displayed strong antimicrobial properties.
sections.Conclusion: Antimicrobial coatings, particularly textured copper and its composite derivatives, demonstrate high efficacy in reducing bacterial contamination and can be recommended for preventing NIs.

presentations.summary: Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a lifelong genetic disease in which respiratory infections substantially contribute to morbidity and mortality. Immunisation is a key preventive strategy in CF care. This study evaluated vaccination status and attitudes among patients with CF and presents baseline data from an ongoing quality improvement initiative. Methods: A retrospective review was conducted among 465 patients with CF followed at a tertiary cystic fibrosis centre. Completeness of the national mandatory immunisation schedule was assessed in patients with available national digital immunisation records (n=317), whereas uptake of influenza and polysaccharide pneumococcal vaccines was evaluated in the entire...

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a lifelong genetic disease in which respiratory infections substantially contribute to morbidity and mortality. Immunisation is a key preventive strategy in CF care. This study evaluated vaccination status and attitudes among patients with CF and presents baseline data from an ongoing quality improvement initiative.
sections.Methods: A retrospective review was conducted among 465 patients with CF followed at a tertiary cystic fibrosis centre. Completeness of the national mandatory immunisation schedule was assessed in patients with available national digital immunisation records (n=317), whereas uptake of influenza and polysaccharide pneumococcal vaccines was evaluated in the entire cohort (n=465) using digital records, supplemented by patient/caregiver reports. Vaccination attitudes were assessed using the Vaccine Attitudes Examination (VAX) scale. Clinical and demographic data were obtained from the centre database.
sections.Results: Of the cohort (n=465), 53.5% were male, with a median age of 12.7 years (7.6–19.7). Median FEV1% was 87.4 (67.9–100.4), and mean BMI z-score was −0.17± 0.05. Among patients with available digital records (n=317), 53.6% had a fully completed mandatory immunisation schedule. Uptake of influenza vaccine during the last season and polysaccharide pneumococcal vaccine was low (12.7% and 4.9%, respectively). Parents/caregivers demonstrated higher preference for natural immunity scores on the VAX scale compared with adult patients (p=0.002). During follow-up, influenza vaccination coverage increased to approximately 45%.
sections.Conclusion: Mandatory immunisation was completed in a proportion of patients; however, important gaps remained, especially in influenza and polysaccharide pneumococcal vaccination. On the basis of these findings, a reminder- and education-based quality improvement initiative was initiated and is ongoing.

presentations.title: Positive airway pressure initiation is associated with improved lung function in persons with cystic fibrosis
presentations.summary: Objectives: It is uncertain if treating sleep breathing disorders in the setting of cystic fibrosis (CF) improves pulmonary outcomes. Among persons with CF, our purpose was to evaluate the relationship between incident positive airway pressure (PAP) receipt and respiratory-related hospitalizations and lung function trajectory. Methods: This retrospective, data-linkage, population-based, self-controlled interval risk study linked data from the Canadian CF Registry and Ontario health administrative databases. All persons with CF newly receiving PAP between January 1, 2009-December 31, 2020, who remained alive and did not receive lung transplantation during follow-up, were included. Rate of respiratory-related ho...
presentations.authors_text: N. Vozoris1, I. Sharpe2, J. Sykes1, C. Ryan3, E. Tullis1, A. Stephenson1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1St. Michael's Hospital, University of Toronto, Respirology, Toronto, Canada, 2Institute for Clinical Evaluative Sciences, Toronto, Canada, 3University Health Network, University of Toronto, Respirology, Toronto, Canada

sections.Objectives: It is uncertain if treating sleep breathing disorders in the setting of cystic fibrosis (CF) improves pulmonary outcomes. Among persons with CF, our purpose was to evaluate the relationship between incident positive airway pressure (PAP) receipt and respiratory-related hospitalizations and lung function trajectory.
sections.Methods: This retrospective, data-linkage, population-based, self-controlled interval risk study linked data from the Canadian CF Registry and Ontario health administrative databases. All persons with CF newly receiving PAP between January 1, 2009-December 31, 2020, who remained alive and did not receive lung transplantation during follow-up, were included. Rate of respiratory-related hospitalizations and the rate of change in %predicted forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV1) were evaluated in the 6-months before vs. 6-months after PAP receipt. We separately evaluated our outcomes amongst incident continuous PAP (CPAP) recipients and incident bi-level PAP (BPAP) recipients. Because each participant served as his/her own control, we only adjusted for changes in receipt of CF-specific treatments in the pre- vs. post-PAP periods.
sections.Results: We identified 28 incident CPAP recipients and 44 incident BPAP recipients. In adjusted analyses, non-significant reduced rates of respiratory-related hospitalizations were observed post- vs. pre-PAP for both PAP groups (CPAP: rate ratio [RR] 0.97, 95% confidence interval [CI] 0.56-1.69; BPAP: RR 0.75, 95% CI 0.53-1.08). In adjusted analyses, rate of change in %predicted FEV1 was significantly improved post- vs. pre-PAP for BPAP recipients (+3.2 vs. -12.9 %predicted/year, p<0.0001), but not for CPAP recipients (+2.4 vs. +0.2 %predicted/year, p=0.45).
sections.Conclusion: Amongst persons with CF newly receiving BPAP (but not CPAP), a significant improvement in lung function trajectory post-PAP receipt was observed, accompanied by non-significant trends towards lower rates of respiratory-related hospitalizations.

presentations.title: Investigation of the correlation between changes in sweat chloride concentration and clinical improvement following initiation of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor
presentations.summary: Objectives: It is well-established that higher Sweat Chloride (SwCl) concentrations are associated with more severe cystic fibrosis (CF) phenotypes, and SwCl has been used as a surrogate marker of CFTR function restoration in clinical trials. After the introduction of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI), clinical outcomes have improved dramatically. In this study we aimed to examine the association between SwCl concentrations and improvements in clinical outcomes during ETI treatment. sections.Objectives: It is well-established that higher Sweat Chloride (SwCl) concentrations are associated with more severe cystic fibrosis (CF) phenotypes, and SwCl has been used as a surrogate marker of CFTR function restoration in clinical trials. After the introduction of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI), clinical outcomes have improved dramatically. In this study we aimed to examine the association between SwCl concentrations and improvements in clinical outcomes during ETI treatment.
sections.Methods: We conducted a nationwide observational study using the Danish CF Cohort, including individuals aged ≥6 years with at least one F508del mutation who have initiated ETI. Pre-ETI baseline was defined as the 12 months prior to initiation; follow-up was 2 years for individuals aged ≥12 years and 1 year for those aged 6–11 years. Associations between SwCl and key outcomes (ppFEV1, BMI/BMI-z, LCI2.5, and CFQ-R scores) were assessed with ANCOVA models adjusted for age, sex, CF center, and baseline values of outcome measure and SwCl.
sections.Results: A total of 58 individuals aged 6–11 years and 269 aged ≥12 years old were included. Mean ages were 9 and 29 years, respectively. Both groups had approximately 80% F508del homozygous individuals. In the ≥12-year group, delta ppFEV1 (95% CI) were 10.6 (8.6–12.6) for SwCl<30 mmol/l, 12.7 (11.5–13.9) for 30≤SwCl<60, and 9.6 (7.6–11.5) for SwCl≥60 between treatment and baseline period. In the 6–11-year group, delta estimates were 7.7 (5.2–10.2) for <30 mmol/L and 7.5 (5.5–9.5) for ≥30 mmol/L. All other outcomes showed similar patterns, with no differences between SwCl categories.
sections.Conclusion: In this cohort, the prespecified SwCl categories during ETI treatment were not associated with changes in clinical outcomes following ETI initiation. Clinical benefits of ETI appear independent of changes in SwCl concentrations. Further research is needed to elucidate the mechanisms underlying this discordance.

sections.Background: Proton pump inhibitors (PPI) are widely used in people with CF (pwCF); while improving airway surface liquid (ASL) pH, they may increase susceptibility to bacterial infections. We assessed factors associated with their use, as well as association with FEV1 and infection with Pseudomonas aeruginosa (PsA).
sections.Methods: European Cystic Fibrosis Society Patient Registry (ECFSPR) data (2019-2024) were used. PwCF were grouped by longitudinal PPI exposure: never used (reference), use from 2020 with 3–5 consecutive years of treatment (cohort 1), and persistent use across all years (cohort 2). Annual ppFEV1 and PsA over time were analyzed with mixed-effect regression models. Multiple logistic regression assessed factors associated with starting PPI.
sections.Results: A total of 39,767 pwCF were included (reference n=17367; cohort 1 n=957; cohort 2 n=6471); annual PPI use was 31–35%. Adjusted ppFEV1 was consistently higher in never-users (reference) than persistent users (cohort 2): 2019: 76.8 vs 71.9; 2024: 83.8 vs 77.9; overall p<0.001. Adjusted chronic PsA infection was lower in never-users than persistent users at baseline (2019: 34.9% vs 40.1%, p<0.001) and declined over follow-up, with narrowing between-cohort differences (2024: 25.2% vs 23.9%, p=0.029). In the logistic model (n=15,774), odds of starting PPI were higher with pancreatic enzyme use (OR 2.21, 95%CI 1.80–2.70), oral steroids (2.38, 1.69–3.34), chronic PsA (1.27, 1.17–1.38), insulin-treated diabetes (1.37, 1.13–1.66) and female sex (1.20, 1.06–1.36); higher ppFEV1 reduced odds (OR 0.87, 0.78–0.98); age/BMI effects were non-linear, with higher odds around age 20 years, and at both low and high BMI z-scores.
sections.Conclusion: In ECFSPR, PPI use is common and identifies pwCF with more severe disease. Initiation is strongly linked to pancreatic enzyme treatment and markers of advanced disease, including chronic PsA. Ongoing work will evaluate disease trajectories by PPI use and by duration of PPI exposure.

sections.Objectives: An aging cystic fibrosis (CF) population means that diseases of aging, including cardiovascular disease (CVD), are emerging as new concerns and may become more prevalent in people with CF (PwCF). PwCF are at an increased risk of adverse cardiovascular (CV) events, such as heart attack, stroke, and diabetes. However, CVD risk profiles remain poorly described in this group.
sections.Methods: A retrospective observation cohort study was performed utilising TriNetX global health network to determine CV risk profiles in adults with CF and observe 1-year CV outcomes (myocardial infarction, atrial fibrillation, heart failure, and a composite endpoint). Cardiometabolic disease codes were explored and compared in adults with rheumatoid arthritis (RA), systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and migraine. Analyses were conducted before and after propensity score matching.
sections.Results: In total, 3,558 PwCF aged ≥ 16 years who had a recorded outpatient appointment between 1 and 4 years ago. The CF cohort had a mean age of 29.3 (SD = 13.4) and were 49.5% female. The CF cohort was less likely to be female compared to the RA cohort (49.5% vs 73.7%, p<0.001), the SLE cohort (49.5% vs. 88.0%, p<0.001), and the migraine cohort (49.5% vs. 81.8%, p<0.001). PwCF had higher prevalence of diabetes (39.1%) than RA (20.2%, p<0.001), SLE (11.0%, p<0.001), and migraine (11.3%, p<0.001), but lower prevalence of hypertension was seen in CF (14.1%, p<0.001) compared to RA (52.4%, p<0.001), SLE (39.3%, p<0.001), and migraine (28.5%, p<0.001).
sections.Conclusion: PwCF have markedly different CVD risk profiles compared to people living with other inflammatory diseases considered high-risk for CVD. This highlights the importance of specific CV risk prediction models.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1UMC Utrecht, Department of Pediatrics, Regenerative Medicine Center Utrecht, Utrecht, Netherlands, 2Wilhelmina Children's Hospital, Department of Pediatric Respiratory Medicine, Utrecht, Netherlands, 3UMC Utrecht, Department of Pulmonary Medicine, Utrecht, Netherlands

sections.Objectives: People with Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF) are living longer, primarily due to the introduction and optimization of CFTR modulator therapy. However, ageing in pwCF is associated with long-term complications, such as colorectal cancer (CRC) and polyps. PwCF aged 40-49 show a CRC prevalence of ~23%, compared to 2-5% in age-matched non-CF individuals. Thus, pwCF undergo surveillance colonoscopies for early detection of colorectal polyps and cancer. This study aims to improve understanding of CF-related CRC by combining epidemiological data with molecular analysis of corresponding patient-derived polyp tissue.
sections.Methods: Clinical data from pwCF who underwent surveillance colonoscopies was extracted from patient files at the University Medical Centre Utrecht (the Netherlands) and was expanded with national data acquired by the Dutch Cystic Fibrosis Foundation (NCFS). The dataset includes demographic variables, CF-specific information (e.g., mutation type, CFTR modulator use) and polyp pathology reports. An appropriate control group is currently being established. Furthermore, a biorepository of polyps from pwCF and people without CF was set up for spatial transcriptomics.
sections.Results: Colorectal polyp incidence among 213 screened pwCF is 38%. Subgroup analysis will be performed stratified by demographic factors, lung transplantation status and modulator use, among others. The subgroup, polyp characteristics and spatial analyses are expected to be finalized in the spring of 2026.
sections.Conclusion: This study will provide insights into colorectal polyp incidence and risk and protective factors for CRC in pwCF. Spatial analysis will explore molecular pathways leading to polyp formation and CRC progression, which may reveal new therapeutic targets.

Associations between covariates and hearing severity across all frequencies were analysed using multinomial ordinal regression with a repeated-measures random effect. Results: Age was significantly associated with hearing loss severity; for each additional year of age, participants were 19% more likely to be classified in a worse hearing threshold cat...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Department of Speech & Hearing Sciences, School of Clinical Therapies, University College Cork, Cork, Ireland, 2Cork Centre for Cystic Fibrosis (3CF), Cork University Hospital, Ireland, Cork, Ireland, 3HRB Clinical Research Facility, University College Cork, Cork, Ireland, 4Cystic Fibrosis Registry of Ireland, University College Dublin Belfield, Belfield, Dublin, Ireland, 5College of Medicine & Health, University College Cork, Ireland, Cork, Ireland, 6Department of Otolaryngology, Head and Neck Surgery, South Infirmary Victoria University Hospital, Cork, Ireland

sections.Methods: A total of 104 adult pwCF were recruited. Baseline demographic data were collected, followed by comprehensive audiological assessment, including extended high-frequency pure-tone audiometry (250 Hz–20 kHz). Associations between covariates and hearing severity across all frequencies were analysed using multinomial ordinal regression with a repeated-measures random effect.
sections.Results: Age was significantly associated with hearing loss severity; for each additional year of age, participants were 19% more likely to be classified in a worse hearing threshold category (OR = 1.19, p < 0.001). Lung function was significantly associated with hearing level. A 10% absolute decline in ppFEV1 corresponding to a 64% increased likelihood of being in a worse hearing threshold category (OR = 1.64, p = 0.006), across all tested frequencies. Participants identified pre-test as having hearing loss, demonstrated a higher probability of more severe hearing loss at frequencies ≤10 kHz. Cystic fibrosis related diabetes (CFRD) was associated with hearing loss at 2 and 4 kHz. Genotype, CFTR modulation, maintenance macrolide and aminoglycoside exposure (in previous 4 years) showed no significant association with hear loss severity.
sections.Conclusion: Hearing loss in pwCF is significantly associated with increasing age and declining lung function, with effects observed across both standard and extended high frequencies. These findings suggest that hearing impairment in this population reflects cumulative disease burden and aging rather than ototoxic exposure alone. Frequency-specific associations with pre-existing hearing loss and CFRD further highlight the need for individualized risk assessment. Routine comprehensive audiological monitoring, may support earlier identification of hearing loss in pwCF.

sections.Objectives: We examined body mass index (BMI) between parents and nonparents with CF (PwCF; NPwCF) using retrospective data from the Health Outcomes of Parents with CF (HOPeCF) study.
sections.Methods: We surveyed PwCF who became new parents between 2012-2022 and NPwCF and linked results to 2010-2023 patient registry data. We created 2012-2018 and 2019-2022 cohorts and designated time points as year -1 and -2 (pre-parenthood), year 0 (parenthood onset or 2015 and 2021 for NPwCF), and year 1-5 (post-parenthood). We evaluated longitudinal BMI change using multivariable longitudinal linear regression models adjusted for age, race, lung function, ever ivacaftor or elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor(ETI) use, insurance status, number of children, and parenthood years.
sections.Results: We recruited 970 participants (253 PwCF/717 NPwCF). PwCF were 59% female, 91% White, 17% Medicaid-insured, and 90% ever ivacaftor/ETI use. In the 2012-2018 cohort, longitudinal BMI change differed significantly between PwCF and NPwCF(p<0.01). PwCF had a significant BMI increase from year -2 to -1 (adj mean change=0.36[0.12,0.59];p<0.01); NPwCF had no significant change. From year -1-0, NPwCF had a significant BMI increase (0.15[0.01,0.29];p=0.04); PwCF had no significant change. From year 0-1, PwCF had a significant BMI decrease (-0.26[-0.49,-0.03];p=0.03) and NPwCF had a significant BMI increase (0.27[0.13,0.41];p<0.01). Both groups experienced significant BMI increases years 3-4, 4-5, and 5-6 (likely due to widespread ETI use). In the 2019-2022 cohort, longitudinal BMI change also differed significantly between groups(p<0.01). Both had significantly increased BMI from year -2 to -1 and only NPwCF had a significant increase year -1-0 (likely due to ETI).
sections.Conclusion: Before widespread ETI use, PwCF experienced a decrease in BMI in the first year of parenthood; however, in the modern era, parenthood largely does not impact BMI over time. Analyses are ongoing to investigating pregnancy and parental sex impacts.

presentations.authors_text: L. Kirwan1, N. Adhikari2, R. Bhatnagar2, R. Doherty1, G. Fletcher1, B. Linnane3, S. Gambazza4,5, P. Gkolia5, A. Zolin4,5, P. Fitzpatrick2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Cystic Fibrosis Registry of Ireland, Dublin, Ireland, 2University College Dublin, UCD School of Public Health, Physiotherapy and Sport Science, Dublin, Ireland, 3University Hospital Limerick, Limerick, Ireland, 4University of Milan, Milan, Italy, 5European Cystic Fibrosis Society Patient Registry, Karup, Denmark

sections.Objectives: Newborn screening (NBS) for cystic fibrosis (CF) enables early diagnosis and prompt initiation of treatment. This study compares clinical outcomes in children with CF diagnosed through NBS with those diagnosed clinically.
sections.Methods: Data were obtained from the European Cystic Fibrosis Society Patient Registry (ECFSPR), which in 2023 included over 56,000 people with CF from 42 countries. To account for temporal changes in care and outcomes, screen-detected (SD) and clinically diagnosed (CD) patients were compared across three five-year birth cohorts: 2008–2012 (SD n=4,118; CD n=3,755), 2013–2017 (SD n=5,292; CD n=2,803), and 2018–2022 (SD n=5,282; CD n=1,181). Outcomes included lung function (ppFEV1), weight-for-age z-scores, and chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) infection. Generalised linear mixed models were fitted adjusting for age, cohort, cohort-by-age interaction, sex, genotype, country income status, and use of CFTR modulators, long-term antibiotics, azithromycin, and pancreatic enzymes, with random effects for repeated measures.
sections.Results: A total of 22,431 children with CF were included. Preliminary analysis shows that SD patients generally demonstrated superior outcomes. In the 2008–2012 cohort, lung function remained stable in the SD group, whereas the CD group declined by 0.43 ppFEV1 per year (p<0.001). In the 2013–2017 cohort, lung function improved in both groups, with greater improvement in SD patients (difference 0.32 ppFEV1; p=0.040). Weight trajectories similarly favoured SD patients, with sustained advantages in the earlier cohort and lower weight z-scores in CD patients at early age in the most recent cohort. CD patients were approximately 2.5 times more likely to have chronic Pa infection at ≤2 years (p<0.001), although higher acquisition rates over time in SD patients led to convergence with age.
sections.Conclusion: Across three European birth cohorts, diagnosis through NBS was associated with improved early clinical outcomes in children with CF.

presentations.summary: Introduction: Alder Hey Children’s Hospital (AHCH), UK, receives several notifications from the new-born screening team every year. Most of the children identified are diagnosed with cystic fibrosis (CF) and follow clear care pathways. Fewer children are diagnosed with cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator-related metabolic syndrome/cystic fibrosis screen positive, inconclusive diagnosis (CRMS/CFSPID). In 2021 Barben, Jürg et al published ‘Updated guidance on the management of children CRMS/CFSPID’ in the Journal of Cystic Fibrosis, to help standardise care in this smaller population. Objectives: To identify all children diagnosed with CFSPID across the network since the introduction of new-born...

sections.Objectives: To identify all children diagnosed with CFSPID across the network since the introduction of new-born screening in 2007 and evaluate how the care they received compared to the standards described by Barben et al.
sections.Methods: The population was selected from new-born screening notifications. After identifying the children diagnosed with CFSPID, medical notes were reviewed to highlight if key care standards were obtained. Key standards were taking from the published guidance by Barben et al in 2021. If care differed from the guidance, then a comment was made as to why.
sections.Results: In total 297 children were identified to AHCH via new-born screening since 2007. From those identified, 19 were diagnosed with CFSPID. Other diagnosis includes CF (n=242), CF gene carrier (n=14), false positive test results (n=11), other (n=11). The median age of CFSPID children at the time of the audit was 11 years (IQR 6-15). The children’s care was managed across 9 different centres, with a median number of 2 patients (IQR 1-3) with CFSPID per centre.
sections.Conclusion: Children with CFSPID make up 6% of all new-born screening referrals. Many of the network hospitals look after only one child with CFSPID. Having a small population in network hospitals makes consistency of care difficult to obtain. However, they are an important group to monitor, so regular review of the population is required.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Inserm UMR 1078, Brest, France, 2Laboratory of Molecular Genetics, University Hospital, Brest, France, 3CF Centre, Ildys Foundation, Roscoff, France, 4CF Centre, University Hospital, Rennes, France, 5CF Centre, South Hospital, Rennes, France, 6CF Centre, Chubert Hospital, Vannes, France

sections.Objectives: Brittany (western France) is known to have one of the highest incidences of cystic fibrosis (CF) worldwide. The very early implementation of newborn screening (NBS) for CF in this region - 35 years ago - offers the opportunity to accurately monitor long-term trends in incidence and detection of initial potential changes due to the advent of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI). The aim of this study was to explore temporal and spatial trends in the incidence of CF in Brittany since NBS for CF was set up.
sections.Methods: This study included all newborns with CF detected in Brittany since the implementation of NBS in 1989 (n=358). Temporal and spatial changes in incidence rates were modelled using Poisson regression and summarised as the percentage of change between periods or departments. Spatial disparities were mapped using Philcarto software.
sections.Results: Since the introduction of NBS for CF in Brittany, the incidence rate has declined significantly by 1.4% per year (p=0.013). The rate decreased significantly from 1/3,102 to 1/4,361 between the 1990-99 and the 2015-24 decades (p=0.024). Beyond this overall decrease, the west-to-east gradient in incidence persists nowadays and even appears to have strengthened. In the 1990s, the incidence rate was 60.9% higher in western Brittany (1/2,339 in Finistère vs. 1/3,763 in Ille-et-Vilaine; p=0.046). Currently, it is 84.9% higher (1/3,120 vs. 1/5,769; p=0.035). No significant variation in incidence has been noted since the advent of ETI.
sections.Conclusion: This study reports a significant decline in the incidence of CF in Brittany over the past 35 years and highlights the persistence of spatial disparities. Due to its peninsular geography and more limited population mixing, western Brittany continues to exhibit one of the highest incidences worldwide, whereas eastern Brittany shows an incidence similar to the French average. Ongoing monitoring of incidence remains essential to assess the potential effect of the advent of ETI.

sections.Objectives: Variation in the organization and delivery of cystic fibrosis (CF) care may affect patient experience and clinical outcomes. Previously, we demonstrated that patient-driven quality of care improvement is possible (Noordhoek, JCF, 2023). We aimed to benchmark Dutch CF centres in the CFTR-modulator era, using patient‑reported experience measures (PREMs), and inform revision of the Dutch CF standard‑of‑care guidelines.
sections.Methods: All Dutch CF centres (7 adult, 7 paediatric) completed a survey in 2025 on team composition, diagnostics, treatments, and organization. Parallel online questionnaires collected PREMs and overall care ratings from people with CF (pwCF) and parents of children with CF. Analyses were descriptive.
sections.Results: All centres reported multidisciplinary teams, online consultations and supported home spirometry. In concordance with the standards of care: annual comprehensive assessments in ≥80% of pwCF were reported by 92% of centres; quarterly cultures by 25%; annual NTM/fungal cultures by 58%; and annual P. aeruginosa susceptibility testing by 83%. Questionnaires were completed by 303 adults with CF and 166 parents, representing 27% and 30% of the Dutch CF population. Mean overall care ratings were high (children 8.9/10; adults 8.4/10). Satisfaction with pulmonologist expertise was 97% (children) and 96% (adults). Information clarity was rated positively by 89% (children) and 84% (adults). Annual access to psychological care differed substantially (children 33% vs adults 6%). Satisfaction with paediatric‑to‑adult transition preparation was 28%. Satisfaction with shared decision‑making was 78% (children) and 90% (adults).
sections.Conclusion: Dutch CF care is characterized by strong multidisciplinary organization and high patient‑reported satisfaction, with inter‑centre variation in access to psychological care and transition to adult care. Integrating centre‑level metrics with PREMs provides a patient‑centred evidence base for national CF care standards.

To ensure international standardization, the registry integrated Global Lung Function Initiative (GLI)...
presentations.authors_text: H. Gutierrez1, B. Joy2, M. Cestari3, C. Mims4, D. Panjwani2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Alabama at Birmingham, Pediatrics, Birmingham, United States, 2University of Alabama at Birmingham, Information Technology, Department of Medicine, Birmingham, United States, 3Hospital Pediatrico Humberto Notti, Mendoza, Argentina, 4The Children Hospital of Alabama, Birmingham, United States

sections.Background: Patient registries are key to improving cystic fibrosis (CF) care and enabling outcomes research. Many low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) lack access to national CF registries due to financial, infrastructure, and technical constraints, resulting in underrepresentation of these populations in global CF data.
sections.Methods: We aimed to develop a CF registry on the REDCap platform, designed for low-cost deployment and rapid implementation. The registry was activated in June 2022 and currently includes 11 participating centers from two countries, with 554 unique patients and 72 active clinical users. To ensure international standardization, the registry integrated Global Lung Function Initiative (GLI) reference equations via an application programming interface (API) for spirometry interpretation. We incorporated automated Z-score and percentile calculations for height-for-age, weight-for-age, weight-for-length (0-2 years), and BMI-for-age, with the option to select WHO or CDC standards based on point-of-care anthropometric values. Custom reporting tools were also created to extend beyond standard REDCap functionalities.
sections.Results: The architecture enabled real-time, standardized interpretation of lung function and nutritional status without relying on local reference tables, proprietary software, or advanced statistical expertise. Automated calculations reduced data-entry errors and ensured methodological consistency at the point of capture. Custom reporting supported longitudinal follow-up, QI, and benchmarking.
sections.Conclusion: A REDCap-based CF registry with automated GLI and WHO/CDC standardization and custom reporting is a scalable, equitable solution for CF data capture in LMICs. It complements existing national registries, addresses a gap in global CF data infrastructure, and enables broader participation in CF research and QI efforts. Funded by the CF Foundation.

sections.Objectives: CF team-based care may reduce burnout. We examined moderators of the relationship between Burnout and Professional Satisfaction.
sections.Methods: CF clinicians completed Maslach Burnout Inventory (MBI), Professional Quality of Life Scale (ProQOL). Principal Factoring Analysis (PFA) with promax rotation identified which subscales formed composite factors. PFA and general linear models evaluated moderation.
sections.Results: N=569: 24.9% physicians, 21.9% nurses/advanced practitioners, 29.4% allied health, 23.8% mental health providers (MH); 48.3% pediatric, 47.8% in CF >10 years. PFA yielded 2 factors: 1)“Burnout:” MBI Depersonalization (0.72), MBI Emotional Exhaustion (0.94), ProQOL Burnout (0.90), ProQOL Secondary Traumatic Stress (0.65); and 2) “Professional Satisfaction:” MBI Personal Accomplishment (0.77), ProQOL Compassion Satisfaction (0.89). Higher Burnout was associated with lower Professional Satisfaction (r =-0.55, p<.001). Professional Role was associated with “Burnout” [F(3, 558)=4.27, p=0.005] but not “Professional Satisfaction” [F(3, 558)=2.16, p=0.092]. Physicians had higher Burnout vs. MH (M Difference=0.35, 95% CI = 0.08, 0.62). Years in CF was associated with “Burnout” [F(2, 564)=3.65, p=0.027] and “Professional Satisfaction” [F(2, 564)=6.84, p=0.001]. Those in CF 10-14 years reported higher “Burnout” vs. <10 years (M Difference=0.24, 95% CI=0.01, 0.47), and lower “Professional Satisfaction” vs. <10 (M Difference=-0.28, 95% CI =-0.54, -0.03) or 15+ years (M Difference=-0.41, 95% CI=-0.68, -0.14). Neither Professional Role nor Years Working in CF moderated the direct effects between Burnout and Professional Satisfaction (p’s 0.48-0.62).
sections.Conclusion: CF clinicians reported high burnout, low-moderate Professional Satisfaction. Professional Role and Years Working in CF did not moderate this relationship. Next steps: replicate in Europe and test potential mediators.

A dedicated Executive of CF professionals and community representatives was set up to co-create an initial model, which was then refined with input from the CF Medical Association and Cystic Fibrosis Trust Clinical A...

sections.Objectives: Clinical peer review is a formal process whereby health professionals evaluate a service or colleague’s practice to improve care and facilitate shared learning. In a resource restricted health system, we sought to develop a pragmatic, cost-neutral approach to peer-led review of multidisciplinary cystic fibrosis (CF) care in the United Kingdom (UK).
sections.Methods: An initial online consultation was carried out with CF professionals to gather views on value and preferred format of peer review. A dedicated Executive of CF professionals and community representatives was set up to co-create an initial model, which was then refined with input from the CF Medical Association and Cystic Fibrosis Trust Clinical Advisory Group.
sections.Results: The online consultation received 108 responses, showing a clear appetite among CF professionals for peer review. They suggested an approach focused on service improvement and shared learning that leverages existing data and reduces burden on those involved. The proposed Peer-led Evaluation for Shared learning and Quality Improvement (PESQI) model is entirely virtual, to minimise costs and burden. It is led by a panel of clinicians and community members, consisting of three phases: Baseline Assessment: Existing insights on outcomes, patient experiences and staffing are collated. Service completes a self-reflection, and additional patient feedback is gathered. These insights are shared with the panel. Review Day: Virtual conversations between panel members and staff at the service under review, followed by an end-of-day debrief. Reporting and Feedback: Panel outline existing best practices and actionable improvement ideas in a report that is shared with the service.
sections.Conclusion: Based on stakeholder feedback, we developed a new model for CF peer review. The programme will be piloted to test feasibility of a virtual-only approach and to explore capacity needed, as well as value gained, to ensure this is a worthwhile and ethical enterprise.

A steering committee was formed that had representation from across Canada and all roles. Each member was responsible for...
presentations.authors_text: M. Richmond1, J. Kocourek2, E. Tullis3, P. Eckford2, M. Chilvers1, Canadian Consensus Guidelines Steering Committee
presentations.affiliations_text: 1BC Children's Hospital, Vancouver, Canada, 2Cystic Fibrosis Canada, Toronto, Canada, 3St. Michael's Hospital, Toronto, Canada

sections.Objectives: To describe the integration of the multi-disciplinary team roles into the Canadian cystic fibrosis (CF) consensus guidelines
sections.Methods: In 2022, CF Canada began developing the Canadian CF consensus guidelines. Informed by updated standards from the European CF Society and CF Foundation, CF Canada sought to develop a holistic set of guidelines that integrated the role of the multi-disciplinary team, encompassed care across the lifespan for people with CF (pwCF) and reflected changes in practice since the introduction of modulator therapies; all from a uniquely Canadian perspective. A steering committee was formed that had representation from across Canada and all roles. Each member was responsible for liaising with their profession group for feedback. Discipline-based groups met to discuss the guidelines and ensure that their role was represented across all clinical contexts.
sections.Results: The completed guidelines were circulated to Canadian CF teams for initial implementation in late 2025. The work highlights each team member’s role and responsibilities across the lifespan of pwCF and different co-morbidities/scenarios (Table 1). Given the vast Canadian landscape and different practice settings (e.g. urban vs. rural, large vs. small clinics), it is recognized that not all CF teams have the full complement of team members. By identifying overlapping responsibilities smaller teams can be strengthened with flexibility in their roles while still providing thorough care. The tool can also be used to advocate for funding/clinical needs in under-resourced clinics. Table 1 Canadian consensus guidelines: outline of the multi-disciplinary roles & clinical scenarios Multi-disciplinary roles physician, nurse practitioner, nurse coordinator/registered nurse, registered dietitian, pharmacist, physiotherapist, psychologist, respiratory therapist, social worker Clinical scenarios Age based (infant, preschool, child, adolescent, young adult, adult – minimal/mild/moderate lung disease) Transition to adult care Advanced lung disease Rapidly declining lung function Pregnancy CF related diabetes CF hepatobiliary involvement Emotional wellness/mental health Post-lung transplant
sections.Conclusion: The integration of the multi-disciplinary team into the Canadian guidelines represents the collaboration of Canadian CF teams. This is the first time roles have been thoroughly reviewed to identify opportunities for cross scope to improve clinic efficiencies. Ongoing projects include the development of a web-based tool that allows filtering by profession for a role-tailored document.

A longitudinal evaluation of the pancreatic parenchyma included in the caudal portions of chest HRCT scans was performed using available pre- and post-therapy examinations (mean follow-up 88 months; 774 CT scans analyzed). Pancreatic parenchyma was independently segmented by two readers, and...
presentations.authors_text: H. Gutierrez1, N. Charles2, L. Hearld2, C. Mims3, S. Self1, K. Hearld2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Alabama at Birmingham, Pediatrics, Birmingham, United States, 2University of Alabama at Birmingham, Department of Health Services Administration, Birmingham, United States, 3The Children Hospital of Alabama, Birmingham, United States

sections.Objectives: To evaluate the potential influence of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) therapy on the progression of pancreatic imaging abnormalities in patients with Cystic Fibrosis.
sections.Methods: A total of 196 patients with Cystic Fibrosis treated with ETI and who underwent High-Resolution Computed Tomography (HRCT) were included (93 males, 103 females; mean age at first CT 15 years; mean age at start of therapy 24 years). A longitudinal evaluation of the pancreatic parenchyma included in the caudal portions of chest HRCT scans was performed using available pre- and post-therapy examinations (mean follow-up 88 months; 774 CT scans analyzed). Pancreatic parenchyma was independently segmented by two readers, and Hounsfield Unit (HU) density histograms were computed using dedicated research image analysis software (Mango, Research Imaging Institute, UTHSCSA). A multivariate analysis using a generalized linear mixed model was conducted to assess the effect of ETI therapy on changes in the distribution of acinar (18–70 HU) and adipose (–34 to –190 HU) pancreatic components before and after treatment.
sections.Results: A statistically significant time-related increase in the adipose component (β = 9.04; p = 0.0036) and a reduction in the acinar component (β = –12.85; p = 0.0031) were observed. Although pancreatic involution continued over time, a significant difference in the temporal response slope (β) was found post-treatment compared to pre-treatment for both the adipose (β = –0.40; p = 0.0022) and acinar components (β = 0.63; p = 0.0011) (p < 0.001). These findings indicate that ETI therapy induces significant changes in both intercept and slope for pancreatic adipose and acinar components.
sections.Conclusion: Despite the ongoing loss of acinar tissue and increase in adipose tissue, ETI therapy appears to significantly influence the progression of pancreatic structural changes in patients with Cystic Fibrosis.

presentations.title: Advancing cystic fibrosis care in Canada: developing a national quality improvement model
presentations.summary: Objectives: Rapid advances in cystic fibrosis (CF) therapy have reshaped clinical needs in Canada. In response, Cystic Fibrosis Canada collaborated with clinicians and community members (2023–25) to develop new Canadian Consensus Guidelines for CF Care. Evidence shows that guideline implementation declines without structured planning and monitoring. To address this, Cystic Fibrosis Canada is developing a national quality improvement (QI) program to support uptake, adherence, and identification of care gaps. Methods: An environmental scan and literature review were conducted to identify comparable QI models, indicators, and outcomes. Interviews will be conducted with clinicians to explore the role of QI and imp...
presentations.authors_text: J. Kocourek1, P. Eckford2,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Cystic Fibrosis Canada, Mission- Healthcare program, Toronto, Canada, 2Cystic Fibrosis Canada, Toronto, Canada

sections.Objectives: Rapid advances in cystic fibrosis (CF) therapy have reshaped clinical needs in Canada. In response, Cystic Fibrosis Canada collaborated with clinicians and community members (2023–25) to develop new Canadian Consensus Guidelines for CF Care. Evidence shows that guideline implementation declines without structured planning and monitoring. To address this, Cystic Fibrosis Canada is developing a national quality improvement (QI) program to support uptake, adherence, and identification of care gaps.
sections.Methods: An environmental scan and literature review were conducted to identify comparable QI models, indicators, and outcomes. Interviews will be conducted with clinicians to explore the role of QI and implementation science, processes needed to support QI in CF care, links to the new guidelines, and proposed implementation and outcome indicators. We also aim to determine how Cystic Fibrosis Canada can best facilitate QI efforts nationally.
sections.Results: The environmental scan identified adaptable QI frameworks such as audit and feedback cycles, outcome dashboards, and multidisciplinary learning collaboratives. Common indicators included guideline adherence, care process reliability, patient reported outcomes, and clinic level variation. Anticipated interview findings include strong support for embedding QI and implementation science into routine CF care, and the need for clear, measurable indicators aligned with the new guidelines. Clinicians are expected to highlight the value of practical tools, site visits, and national learning networks. These insights will inform a draft QI program integrating guideline aligned indicators, structured improvement cycles, and continuous evaluation.
sections.Conclusion: A national QI program aligned with the new guidelines offers an opportunity to strengthen consistency, equity, and evidence based CF care in Canada. Early findings highlight both the need and readiness for a structured approach to guideline implementation and evaluation.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1SEI «Avicenna Tajik State Medical University», Department of Children’s Diseases № 2, Dushanbe, Tajikistan, 2SEI «Republican Scientific Clinical Center - Paediatrics and Children's Surgery», Dushanbe, Tajikistan, 3Research Centre for Medical Genetics, Moscow, Russian Federation

sections.Objectives: In recent decades, advances in the diagnosis and treatment of cystic fibrosis (CF) have significantly increased the life expectancy and quality of life of people with CF (pwCF) in developed countries. However, in countries without specialised CF centers, there are a number of acute problems that make our study very important. Aim: To study the clinical features of of children with CF (cwCF) in our hospital and determine the role of the paediatrician in managing cwCF in the absence of a specialised center.
sections.Methods: Eight cwCF, from 1 to 6 years old (all diagnosed in the Russian Federation (RF)), were monitored by a paediatrician, of whom 6 were repeatedly hospitalized in the National Medical Center of the Republic of Tajikistan's paediatric pulmonology department. Two cwCF were observed on an outpatient basis by a local paediatrician in conjunction with video consultations with CF specialists from the RF. The following data were analysed: clinical manifestation of CF, numbers of lungs’ exacerbations and hospitalizations, nutritional status, the paediatrician's management tactics at the outpatient level.
sections.Results: Seven cwCF suffered from chronic cough with viscous sputum, including 6 with frequent bronchial obstruction and 3 with sinusitis and nasal polyposis. Five cwCF (62.5%) had steatorrhea, 4 (50%) had severe malnutrition (-3 and -4 on the Z-score scale). Three cwCF had bronchiectasis, and one had liver fibrosis, salt loss syndrome and diabetes mellitus. One of the cwCF died at 6.3 years old.
sections.Conclusion: In regions without specialised CF centers, paediatricians become mentors to families, combining medical, psychological and social assistance. Limited access to modern drugs for treating CF complicates the prognosis, but proper organization of patient care can significantly improve the course of the disease. Educational programs and international cooperation are needed to improve the quality of care for cwCF in countries with limited resources.

Our goal is to build, implement, and adapt tools to support quality improvement projects through rigorous data capture systems and enhanced communication...
presentations.authors_text: B. Horton1, R. List2, D. Brighton1, S. Shen1, D. Albon2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Virginia, Department of Public Health Sciences, Charlottesville, United States, 2University of Virginia, Department of Medicine, Charlottesville, United States

sections.Objectives: The United States Cystic Fibrosis Learning Network (CFLN) is a maturing, collaborative, multisite, multi-stakeholder, data-driven network that aims to improve outcomes for all people with CF (PwCF) and their families. The structure of CFLN relies on timely and reliable data summaries that are transparently distributed to the network to enhance their quality improvement aims. The University of Virginia Data Core (UVADC) is a collaborative group of professionals with diverse backgrounds aiming to support quality improvement initiatives for the CFLN. Our goal is to build, implement, and adapt tools to support quality improvement projects through rigorous data capture systems and enhanced communication for the learning network, with high-quality and transparent reports for all CF clinical care programmes and their patient and family partners in the learning network.
sections.Methods: The data core was initiated as a multidisciplinary team with expertise in CF clinical care, quality improvement science, database development and management, and data analysis and visualisation. UVADC members meet weekly as a team and with CFLN leaders and quality improvement support group and project leaders.
sections.Results: From August 2023 to January 2026, the UVADC has supported CFLN activities and collaborative projects bridging quality improvement initiatives with data from the Cystic Fibrosis Foundation Patient Registry. The UVADC employs innovative, transformative, and agile tools to create outcome reports, a web application to identify missing data and data errors in real-time, and provides manuscript support for all activities.
sections.Conclusion: Curating a diverse team of UVADC members is essential to ensure rigorous data collection, analysis, reporting, and interpretation to support multicentre learning and improvement. The approach used to develop UVADC to support the CFLN can be translated to support other learning networks, both in cystic fibrosis as well as other clinical areas.

Three rounds of eDelphi survey are being conducted to gain consensus among stakeholders about the most important outcomes to measure in CF trials. In rounds 1 and 2, participants rate the importance of outco...

sections.Objectives: There is a need for greater consensus on the outcomes used in cystic fibrosis (CF) clinical trials to enable results to be compared and synthesised across trials. The development of a universal Core Outcome Set for CF (COS-CF) aims to address this need by defining outcomes that should be measured in all late-phase interventional trials in CF, regardless of their specific focus.
sections.Methods: Our protocol is registered on Figshare (DOI: 10.6084/m9.figshare.28044917) and the COMET initiative database. Three rounds of eDelphi survey are being conducted to gain consensus among stakeholders about the most important outcomes to measure in CF trials. In rounds 1 and 2, participants rate the importance of outcomes on a 9-point Likert scale. Outcomes are deemed ‘consensus out’ and dropped after rounds 1 and 2 if ≤50% of participants in each stakeholder group score them 7-9. Outcomes scored 7-9 by ≥70% of participants and 1-3 by ≤15%, among all stakeholder groups, are considered ‘consensus in’. The third round, translated into multiple languages, will ask participants to rank their top 5 outcomes from those remaining.
sections.Results: The eDelphi process is ongoing; the first round has concluded, with 187 people rating 49 outcomes. Participants were people with CF or families (n=105), healthcare professionals (n=62) or researchers (n=20). After round 1, 7 outcomes were voted ‘consensus out’ and excluded from round 2. Seven outcomes were voted ‘consensus in’, with the remaining 35 classified as ‘no consensus’.
sections.Conclusion: The large number of outcomes deemed ‘no consensus’ indicates substantial variation in the views of individuals and between stakeholder groups. In the second round, participants will review their own scores alongside the average scores of each stakeholder group to promote greater consensus. Following completion of the eDelphi process, remaining outcomes will be reviewed and ratified during an online multi-stakeholder workshop in Summer 2026.

presentations.title: Measuring what matters: physiotherapy outcomes in cystic fibrosis care
presentations.summary: Background: Cystic fibrosis (CF) registries underpin population surveillance, service planning and evaluation of therapeutic outcomes. Registry datasets infrequently capture functional or physiotherapy-related outcomes central to contemporary care. This study considered the heterogeneity of physiotherapy outcome-measures (OMs) and the value of clinically relevant OMs for possible inclusion in expanding registry datasets. Methods: An online survey was distributed to physiotherapists working in CF across UK, Europe, Australia, New Zealand, USA and Canada. Quantitative data summarised the frequency and consistency of OM use, while qualitative data explored the contextual and organisational factors shaping OM deci...
presentations.authors_text: L. Morrison1, J. Hauser2,3, E. Coulter4, S. Pollock4, Global Data Harmony Working Group, REACH for CF, International Virtual Network
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Queen Elizabeth University Hospital, West of Scotland Adult CF Unit, Glasgow, United Kingdom, 2Royal Hobart Hospital, Tasmanian Adult Cystic Fibrosis Unit, Tasmania, Australia, 3CFphysio.com Inc, Hobart, Australia, 4Glasgow Caledonian University, Department of Allied Health Professions, Glasgow, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Cystic fibrosis (CF) registries underpin population surveillance, service planning and evaluation of therapeutic outcomes. Registry datasets infrequently capture functional or physiotherapy-related outcomes central to contemporary care. This study considered the heterogeneity of physiotherapy outcome-measures (OMs) and the value of clinically relevant OMs for possible inclusion in expanding registry datasets.
sections.Methods: An online survey was distributed to physiotherapists working in CF across UK, Europe, Australia, New Zealand, USA and Canada. Quantitative data summarised the frequency and consistency of OM use, while qualitative data explored the contextual and organisational factors shaping OM decisions, either for research or clinical practice.
sections.Results: Responses from 167 experienced physiotherapists were collated. Results demonstrated clear convergence in OMs routinely assessed. Respiratory monitoring was almost universal where 97% of respondents routinely recorded spirometry, with 98% rating it as clinically valuable. Auscultation (79%), cough assessment (81%), and sputum assessment (92%) were widely embedded and considered clinically relevant. Exercise testing, showed variation across regions with a large majority of respondents in Australia and New Zealand (86%) but a smaller proportion in some European centres (69%).Similarly, physical-activity participation ranged from 90% to 76%.
sections.Conclusion: Clinically relevant physiotherapy OMs are standardised, widely used and routinely collected. Their variable inclusion in CF registries can limit the ability to benchmark practice, evaluate service models and demonstrate long term clinical impact. A construct-based physiotherapy dataset, supported by feasible documentation workflows, would allow registries to capture functional and behavioural OMs increasingly relevant in the modulator era. Registry evolution is necessary to maintain relevance, sensitivity and completeness of population-level CF surveillance.
