sections.Objectives: Newborn screening for cystic fibrosis (CF) was introduced in Ireland in 2011. The ICOS national cohort study compares children with CF (CWCF) diagnosed through newborn screening with those diagnosed clinically. We examined risk factors for hospitalisation due to pulmonary exacerbations and other CF-related conditions in first five years.
sections.Methods: Study includes CWCF born between July 2008 - June 2023. Hospitalisation data were collected on both screen-detected and clinically diagnosed cohorts from birth to 60 months from CF Registry, Ireland and hospital charts. Logistic regression was used to identify demographic and clinical factors associated with hospitalisation in first 60 months of life in CWCF ≥ 60 months.
sections.Results: Of 300 (≥ 60 months) CWCF recruited, 225 were screen-detected (born 2011 - 2018) and 75 clinically diagnosed (born 2008 - 2011). Being diagnosed clinically (adjusted OR: 3.80, 95% CI: 1.07-13.43, p=0.038) and ΔF508 homozygous mutation (adjusted OR: 14.34, 95% CI: 1.74-118.33, p=0.013) were significantly associated with hospitalisation for pulmonary CF exacerbations when adjusted for gender, sibling with CF and modulator type. Use of CF modulators was highly protective, reducing risk of hospitalisation for both any CF-related issues (Orkambi/Symkevi adjusted OR: 0.21, 95% CI: 0.07-0.67, p=0.008; Kaftrio/Ivacaftor adjusted OR: 0.10, 95% CI: 0.04-0.24, p<0.001) and also pulmonary exacerbations (Orkambi/Symkevi adjusted OR: 0.07, 95% CI: 0.007-0.58, p=0.015; Ivacaftor/Kaftrio adjusted OR: 0.001, 95% CI: 0.00-0.011, p<0.001).
sections.Conclusion: Clinical diagnosis and ΔF508 homozygous mutation are associated with increased early childhood hospitalisations, while modulator therapy reduces this risk, highlighting benefits both screening combined with targeted treatment in CF care.

presentations.summary: Objectives: Developed in 2000, the Cystic Fibrosis Questionnaire-Revised (CFQ-R) has been used extensively in clinical and behavioral studies and considered the “gold standard” health-related quality of life (HRQoL) measure for people with cystic fibrosis (PWCF) ages 2+ years. With the advent of highly effective modulators (ETI, VTD), substantial improvements in lung function, body mass index, and HRQoL have been documented. Thus, the lived experience of PWCF has changed dramatically, requiring an expansion of items and scales on the CFQ-R to reflect this clinical progress. This study aims to conduct qualitative research with PWCF to elicit concepts for a modulator-specific version of the CFQ-R (CFQ-M) and eva...
presentations.authors_text: A. Quittner1, A. Foster2, K. Kelly2, F. Sowell2, S. Taneja2, E. van Roil2, M. Kosinski2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Joe DiMaggio Cystic Fibrosis, Pulmonary and Sleep Center, Pulmonary Department, Hollywood, United States, 2IQVIA Patient Centered Solutions, Durham, United States

sections.Objectives: Developed in 2000, the Cystic Fibrosis Questionnaire-Revised (CFQ-R) has been used extensively in clinical and behavioral studies and considered the “gold standard” health-related quality of life (HRQoL) measure for people with cystic fibrosis (PWCF) ages 2+ years. With the advent of highly effective modulators (ETI, VTD), substantial improvements in lung function, body mass index, and HRQoL have been documented. Thus, the lived experience of PWCF has changed dramatically, requiring an expansion of items and scales on the CFQ-R to reflect this clinical progress. This study aims to conduct qualitative research with PWCF to elicit concepts for a modulator-specific version of the CFQ-R (CFQ-M) and evaluate the comprehensibility and relevance of new items. After finalizing the CFQ-M, a crosswalk study is planned to calibrate items from both CFQ-R and CFQ-M on a common latent variable using item-response theory (IRT).
sections.Methods: The study team will use a 4-phase approach: (1) qualitative interviews with up to 75 PWCF to elicit new and relevant concepts (e.g., symptoms and impacts) to be included in the age-appropriate CFQ-M; (2) development of the CFQ-M informed by a concept mapping exercise of the phase 1 findings; (3) cognitive debriefing interviews with up to 62 PWCF to assess the appropriateness of the CFQ-M; and (4) application of IRT to calibrate the new CFQ-M item content with the CFQ-R.
sections.Results: Phase 1 interviews will aim to expand the range of functioning measured by CFQ-R scales with new item content and define key concepts reflecting patient experience with modulators. Findings from this phase will guide the implementation of subsequent phases.
sections.Conclusion: In line with regulatory guidance, qualitative and psychometric research will be conducted to capture and measure concepts that are relevant and meaningful for PWCF on new modulators, producing a reliable and valid CFQ-M while maintaining comparability of scores with previous studies using CFQ-R.

While there has been recent interest in the use of PROMs as outcome measures in clinical trials, there is limited long-term real-world data published by registries. Longitudinal PROMs data can track change, help inform clinical practice and empower patients to better understand and manage their condition. Patient registries play an important role to support these goals given their longitudinal nature and rich clinical outcome datasets. To explore the feasibility of the inclusion of PROMs within a...
presentations.authors_text: R. Doherty1, L. Kirwan1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Cystic Fibrosis Registry of Ireland, Dublin, Ireland, 2UCD School of Public Health, Physiotherapy and Sport Science, Dublin, Ireland

sections.Objectives: The use of patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs), including symptom scores, wellbeing measures, and Health-Related Quality of Life measures, are of increasing interest to help measure the burden of CF. While there has been recent interest in the use of PROMs as outcome measures in clinical trials, there is limited long-term real-world data published by registries. Longitudinal PROMs data can track change, help inform clinical practice and empower patients to better understand and manage their condition. Patient registries play an important role to support these goals given their longitudinal nature and rich clinical outcome datasets. To explore the feasibility of the inclusion of PROMs within a registry context, the objectives of this study are to: Define current practice with respect to the use of PROMs in clinical CF practice in Ireland Define current practice with respect to the inclusion of PROMs in international CF registries
sections.Methods: This study adopts a multi-method approach to gather data including the following: A pseudo-anonymous online survey of clinical staff at any CF-specialist centre in Ireland to map the use of and attitudes towards PROMs in clinical CF practice in Ireland A desk-based international review of Cystic Fibrosis Registry practice in the use of PROMs utilising publicly available/shared documents (e.g., variable lists and annual reports).
sections.Results: Results of the survey and desk-based review are descriptively analysed and presented to understand current practices and preferences in the use of PROMs in clinical practice in Ireland and in international registries.
sections.Conclusion: Findings from this study will inform the feasibility for the inclusion of PROMs in the Irish CF registry in terms of existing data availability and accessibility. This study will make recommendations on current practice, the potential for future work on the implementation of PROMs in CF registries, and inform discussions for international harmonisation.

sections.Background: The Twinning Expansion Project aims to develop a long-term centre-to-centre partnership in which an established cystic fibrosis (CF) centre guides and supports another site to optimise care for people with CF. Istanbul-Cerrahpasa and King’s College London were matched as mentii and mentor in 2024 spring.
sections.Objectives: To assess the early phase of this partnership using SWOT analysis.
sections.Methods: Some team members first met in person during the ECFS Conference in Glasgow. An online meeting followed in July 2024 to introduce both teams and plan the process. Cerrahpasa presented the organisation of the centre and patient characteristics. To date, four cross-site clinical and teaching meetings have taken place. In November 2024, the mentee centre visited the mentor centre in London, observed both paediatric and adult CF teams in inpatient and outpatient settings, and engaged MDT meetings. The process was evaluated using a SWOT framework.
sections.Results: The main Strength was the open sharing of treatment approaches and clinical experiences. Staff issues limited the planned sub-groups and were identified as a Weakness. Opportunities included ongoing collaboration, discussion of complex cases, and future education. High workloads at both centres created scheduling difficulties and were considered a Threat. Other strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats were presented in Table 1.
sections.Conclusion: The early phase of the project created a useful link between the two CF centres and supported communication, shared learning, and reviewing practice. This shows that twinning can help both clinical and organisational development. Continued collaboration may increase the long-term impact of this partnership. Table 1. SWOT analysis S W O T Strengths Weaknesses Opportunities Threats Early engagment of full Sharing clinical practice Online+in-person communication Meeting with expertise Case-based discussion Staff turnover Limited sub-groups Consultation on complex CF cases Joint education Development of paediatric-to-adult transition pathways for Cerrahpasa Sharing experience with other CF centres High workload Scheduling difficulties for meetings and visits Busy clinical services

It took a year before regular virtual meetings could take place. The first on-site...
presentations.authors_text: C.E. Szabo1,2, O. Voivod3, M.P. Chira2, G. Feron4, S. Braun4, F. Heyvaert4, S. Teerlinck4, E. De Wachter4
presentations.affiliations_text: 1"Iuliu Hațieganu" University of Medicine and Pharmacy, Mother and child, Cluj-Napoca, Romania, 2Children's Emergency Clinical Hospital, Romania, Cluj-Napoca, Romania, 3Patient's representative, CF Europe, Cluj-Napoca, Romania, 4UZ Brussels, CF Clinic, Pediatric Pulmonology, Brussels, Belgium

sections.Background: The goal of ECFS-Twinning project is to set up a site-to-site partnership, aiming to fill the gap between European countries regarding care and clinical outcomes in people with CF. Twinning combines mentorship-led knowledge transfer with practical implementation to the selected mentee site. In 2020, collaboration between the CF centre of the Pediatrics Clinic 1 Cluj, Romania, (mentee) and UZ Brussel, Belgium, (mentor) started as part of this project.
sections.Methods: First online meetings were exploring and showed an important language barrier, complicating real conversations. Consequently, Dr Szabo followed English course. It took a year before regular virtual meetings could take place. The first on-site visit (15 - 16 May 2023) was in Cluj-Napoca, by the CF doctor, physiotherapist and CF-nurse from UZ Brussel. Presentations were given, CF patients were seen, mainly focusing on physiotherapy sessions. Barriers for optimal CF-care were identified and discussed. Since then, genuine and committed cooperation has been possible. Virtual meetings were interspersed with personal contacts during ECFS activities. Recently, the Cluj team (CF doctor, physiotherapist, patient representative) visited the centre of Brussels (14 -16 Oct 2025).
sections.Results: Improved multidisciplinary competencies at the mentee site, adoption of harmonized CF protocols and infection control practices. Due to emerging changes in the organization of CF-care at the Pediatrics Clinic 1 Cluj, from 1st January 2026, the CF centre is facing challenging times, in both personnel and infrastructure. The Brussels' mentor site and ECFS supported the actions undertaken by the local staff of the CF clinic in Cluj and follow-up further developments closely.
sections.Conclusion: Providing courses in English for mentee sites prior to active involvement could be helpful. Twinning has been a phantastic journey so far, allowing both sites to learn a lot, highlighting the slogan “together stronger”.

Methods: A Public Patient Involvement (PPI) team of 8 members (5 PwCF, 3 parents of PwCF) developed a survey for the Irish CF community to explore their understanding of DHL and how best to deliver this training and resources. This survey was digitised and made available onlin...
presentations.authors_text: T. Vagg1,2,3, M.J. O’Sullivan4, A.-M. Shier4, C. Byrne4, C.-A. O’Rielly4, C. Fleming1, M. McCarthy1, J. Dorgan1, K. Cronin1, F. Holmes1, E. Madden1, S. Twohig1, V. Matthews1, C. Quigney1, H.I. Ibrahim1,2,3, B.J. Plant1,2,3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Cork University Hospital, University College Cork, Cork Centre for Cystic Fibrosis (3CF), Cork, Ireland, 2University College Cork, The HRB Funded Clinical Research Facility, Cork, Ireland, 3University College Cork, Department of Medicine, Cork, Ireland, 4Cork University Hospital, University College Cork, Digital Health Literacy (DHL) Public Patient Involvement (PPI) Team, 3CF, Cork, Ireland

sections.Background: Digital Health Literacy (DHL) combines health literacy and digital skills to safely and confidently use digital health tools. CF Digital Health Tools (DHT) such as telehealth, AI searches, remote monitoring, and online forums are increasingly common in CF care, creating a need to build DHL knowledge. Aim &
sections.Objectives: To identify effective modes for delivering DHL training and resources to PwCF and their caregivers in Ireland.
sections.Methods: A Public Patient Involvement (PPI) team of 8 members (5 PwCF, 3 parents of PwCF) developed a survey for the Irish CF community to explore their understanding of DHL and how best to deliver this training and resources. This survey was digitised and made available online using Qualitrics. Ethical approval was granted from the Clinical Research Ethics Committee at Cork. The survey was advertised via social media through the national patient association, Cystic Fibrosis Ireland. The survey was made available for 8 months.
sections.Results: 37 participants responded to the survey. 21 were PwCF, 16 were family/carers of a pwCF (Age range 16-60, mean age-range 36-40, 25 Female). 8 had knowledge of health literacy, 10 of digital skills, and 6 of DHL. 37 actively sought out CF health information through known CF medical websites. 21 used social media to locate information (mainly Instagram). Preferred training resources included the development of an online multimodal platform (website/app) using text, video, and images for each topic. Topics should be grouped for easier understanding and social media sharing. Additionally, paper-based and email resources are recommended to support users with lower digital skills.
sections.Conclusion: Within Ireland, DHL does not appear to be well known and needs to be an area for future educational focus. When delivering resources to PwCF and family/carers, online multimedia with accompanying paper resources is preferrable. Funding: Supported by a Vertex CF-RIA award.

This study evaluated treatment adherence, patient satisfaction, the accuracy of home spirometry (HS) versus stationary spirometry (SS), and whether lung function remained stable during DHH. Methods: Of 61 adults with CF (≥17 years) in the Norwegian CF Registry with forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV₁%) >40 and receiving full care at OUH, 14 participants were randomly selecte...
presentations.authors_text: B.A. Groeterud1, P.L. Finstad1, M. Kolsgaard1, E. Bakkeheim1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Oslo University Hospital, Norwegian Centre for Rare Diseases, Unit for Pulmonology and Cystic Fibrosis, Oslo, Norway

sections.Objectives: Digital Home Hospital (DHH) solutions can transform cystic fibrosis (CF) care by enabling remote monitoring, reducing clinic visits, and providing personalised support. At Oslo University Hospital (OUH), the Dignio platform, a cloud-based mobile system, was introduced to track patient-reported data and clinical metrics. This study evaluated treatment adherence, patient satisfaction, the accuracy of home spirometry (HS) versus stationary spirometry (SS), and whether lung function remained stable during DHH.
sections.Methods: Of 61 adults with CF (≥17 years) in the Norwegian CF Registry with forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV₁%) >40 and receiving full care at OUH, 14 participants were randomly selected for one year of DHH follow-up (10 male; 13 with ≥1 F508del variant; 11 pancreatic insufficient; mean age 30.3 years, range 19.2–58.1). Adherence was calculated as completed sessions out of 12 scheduled. Satisfaction was assessed via forms. Agreement between HS and SS was analysed using paired measurements within ≤3 days. Registry data from the study year and the next were compared using paired tests to assess lung function stability.
sections.Results: One participant did not engage. Among the remaining 13, HS adherence ranged 42–100% (mean 76%). One relied exclusively on Dignio; others combined digital monitoring with limited consultations. Most had 1–2 consultations; only one attended both two hospital visits and two phone consultations. Satisfaction was high: 10 of 11 respondents were largely or very satisfied, and all would choose digital follow-up again. HS and SS showed strong agreement (mean difference 0.01 L; range −0.6 to 0.5 L; p=0.90), confirming HS reliability. Lung function remained stable (mean FEV₁ 3.6 L both years; p=0.25), indicating DHH did not negatively impact outcomes.
sections.Conclusion: DHH follow-up for people with CF demonstrated good adherence, high satisfaction, accurate HS, and stable lung function, supporting its feasibility in routine care.

presentations.title: Mapping current practices of digital health in respiratory disease management: national survey
presentations.summary: Background: Chronic respiratory diseases are long-term, irreversible disorders associated with substantial treatment burden and ongoing care needs. Digital health technologies are increasingly promoted to support self-management, monitoring, and care delivery in long-term conditions. However, evidence on their actual use, implementation, and perceived impact within respiratory care remains limited. Objectives: This study aimed to (1) describe current use (or non-use) of digital health technologies in the management of respiratory diseases among patients and healthcare professionals, (2) identify reported barriers, enablers, and potential inequalities influencing its use and implementation within NHS settings,...
presentations.authors_text: H. Alshuwaikhat1, C. Williams1, S. van Beurden1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1The University of Exeter, Exeter, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Chronic respiratory diseases are long-term, irreversible disorders associated with substantial treatment burden and ongoing care needs. Digital health technologies are increasingly promoted to support self-management, monitoring, and care delivery in long-term conditions. However, evidence on their actual use, implementation, and perceived impact within respiratory care remains limited.
sections.Objectives: This study aimed to (1) describe current use (or non-use) of digital health technologies in the management of respiratory diseases among patients and healthcare professionals, (2) identify reported barriers, enablers, and potential inequalities influencing its use and implementation within NHS settings, and (3) explore perceived impacts on day-to-day care, self-management, quality of care.
sections.Methods: A cross-sectional survey was conducted with patients with respiratory diseases and healthcare professionals involved in respiratory care. The surveys captured digital health use, reasons for use or non-use, and perceived impacts on care delivery and experience. Surveys were distributed via six NHS Trusts, professional and charitable organisations, social media, and community channels. To support accessibility and inclusion, participants could, if preferred, complete the survey via a paper-based format.
sections.Results: At the time of abstract submission, the survey was ongoing and scheduled to conclude in February 2026. To date, the survey had been initiated online by 354 patients with respiratory diseases and 41 healthcare professionals. Preliminary data indicates substantial variation in digital health use across patient and healthcare professional groups. Full analyses will examine patterns of use, perceived impacts on care and service provision, and reported barriers and inequalities following completion of data collection.

presentations.title: Feasibility and acceptability of a digital, cognitive behavioural therapy-based group intervention for adolescents with chronic physical illness and their parents: a Swedish adaptation of On Track
presentations.summary: Objectives: digital treatment formats are essential to improve equitable access to healthcare and reduce treatment burden. Around 20% of children in western countries live with chronic physical illness (CPI), such as cystic fibrosis. CPI limits daily functioning and entails psychosocial challenges, across different CPI, including anxiety and depression. Parents play a key role in adolescents’ well-being and coping with illness-related stress, underscoring the need for interventions involving both adolescents and parents. To address this, the psychosocial support program On Track (OT), a digital, transdiagnostic group intervention based on Cognitive Behavioural Therapy, was developed in the Netherlands, to prov...
presentations.authors_text: E. Bergenmar Ivarsson1, J. Strand1, S. Weineland1, E.-L. Bratt2, L. Scholten3, S. Buratti1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Gothenburg, Psychology, Gothenburg, Sweden, 2University of Gothenburg, Institute of Health and Care Sciences, Gothenburg, Sweden, 3Amsterdam University, Medical Center for Research, Amsterdam, Netherlands

sections.Objectives: digital treatment formats are essential to improve equitable access to healthcare and reduce treatment burden. Around 20% of children in western countries live with chronic physical illness (CPI), such as cystic fibrosis. CPI limits daily functioning and entails psychosocial challenges, across different CPI, including anxiety and depression. Parents play a key role in adolescents’ well-being and coping with illness-related stress, underscoring the need for interventions involving both adolescents and parents. To address this, the psychosocial support program On Track (OT), a digital, transdiagnostic group intervention based on Cognitive Behavioural Therapy, was developed in the Netherlands, to provide targeted support. This study examines feasibility and acceptability of a Swedish adaptation of OT in a clinical setting, focusing on implementation and participant experiences.
sections.Methods: semi-structured interviews were conducted with two adolescents and six parents who completed OT. Recruitment challenges limited the sample size. Inductive thematic analysis identified four themes: mutual exchange and understanding; digital format as an enabler; improved ability to regulate emotions; and enhanced family communication.
sections.Results: sharing experiences within the group was highly valued. The digital format was seen as accessible and flexible. The transdiagnostic approach was well received, although careful group composition appears important for engagement. The CBT framework combined with group interaction supported the adoption of new coping strategies, particularly for emotional regulation. Parents reported improved illness-related communication. Recruitment was slower than anticipated and required substantial effort.
sections.Conclusion: findings indicate that OT is a feasible and acceptable intervention for addressing psychosocial difficulties. Future research should focus on optimizing recruitment strategies and evaluating OT’s effectiveness in the Swedish context.

presentations.title: Virtual clinical reviews in paediatric CF care - development of a service based on patient experience and clinical parameters
presentations.summary: Introduction: Advances in CF care have resulted in lower morbidity in children with CF (CwCF). Telehealth has the potential to allow clinical monitoring of CwCF with less disruption to daily life. Adoption of telehealth in CwCF has been slow, due to factors such as unreliability of equipment in younger children, reliance on parents to recognise subtle changes in their child’s health, safeguarding concerns & parental wishes. Within our centre we aimed to ascertain parental views on the development of a virtual clinic (VC) and develop a clinical checklist to stratify patient suitability for a VC appointment based on their annual assessment (AA) results. Methods: A PREM was sent to all families within the CF cent...
presentations.authors_text: C. Dawson1, C. Edmondson2, A. Turnbull2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Great Ormond Street Hospital NHS Trust, Cystic Fibrosis Unit, London, United Kingdom, 2Great Ormond Street Hospital NHS Trust, London, United Kingdom

sections.Methods: A PREM was sent to all families within the CF centre to explore views on the development of a virtual clinic. All members of the CF MDT were asked to identify AA triggers or results that would constitute a cause for concern, forming the basis of the checklist.
sections.Results: 109/180 (61%) of families responded, with a higher proportion of responses from families with CwCF between 13 & 14yrs (14%) and lowest response from families of CwCF aged below 1 year (1%). 68% were in favour of VC,19% ambivalent, & only 10% disagreed. 98% used public transport as part of their journey to hospital. Respondents’ concerns included: no physical examination (80%) & no spirometry (68%). Benefits included less travel expense (80%), less disruption to education/employment (88%/80%), & reduced clinic wait (95%). The developed checklist comprises 14 domains including respiratory health, anthropomorphic data & social concerns.
sections.Conclusion: Most families were in favour of VCs as an option, but a significant number raised concerns regarding the lack of examination or investigations. These concerns may be addressed in the future with the growth of remote technology. Our checklist is currently being utilised within our service to stratify patients, with PREMs being sent after the VC to assess feedback from families.

presentations.summary: Objectives: Sweat chloride is a validated biomarker of CFTR function and response to CFTR modulators, yet its use beyond diagnosis is limited by logistical barriers. Emerging point-of-care (POC) technologies may enable rapid assessment of CFTR modulation in routine care. We evaluated clinician perceptions of the realism and clinical utility of POC sweat chloride testing across contemporary cystic fibrosis (CF) care scenarios. Methods: We conducted a cross-sectional survey of CF clinicians evaluating seven scenarios in which rapid (10–15 minute) POC sweat chloride results could inform care, including CFTR modulator adherence, drug–drug interactions, atypical modulator response, exercise-related hydration manage...
presentations.authors_text: M. Jain1, R. Nelson1, S. McColley2, T. Cybulski3, M. Sala3, M. Prickett3, J. Rogers4, R. Ghaffari5, A. Aranyosi5, N. Mayer-Hamblett6, C. Goss6
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Northwestern University Feinberg School of Medicine, Medicine, Pulmonary Critical Care, Chicago, United States, 2Ann & Robert H. Lurie Children's Hospital of Chicago, Chicago, United States, 3Northwestern University Feinberg School of Medicine, Chicago, United States, 4Northwestern, University Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Evanston, United States, 5Epicore Biosystems, Inc, Cambridge, United States, 6Seattle Children's Research Institute, Seattle, United States

sections.Objectives: Sweat chloride is a validated biomarker of CFTR function and response to CFTR modulators, yet its use beyond diagnosis is limited by logistical barriers. Emerging point-of-care (POC) technologies may enable rapid assessment of CFTR modulation in routine care. We evaluated clinician perceptions of the realism and clinical utility of POC sweat chloride testing across contemporary cystic fibrosis (CF) care scenarios.
sections.Methods: We conducted a cross-sectional survey of CF clinicians evaluating seven scenarios in which rapid (10–15 minute) POC sweat chloride results could inform care, including CFTR modulator adherence, drug–drug interactions, atypical modulator response, exercise-related hydration management, modulator dose reduction due to hepatic impairment or mental health adverse effects, and monitoring during pregnancy. Respondents rated each scenario for realism and clinical utility.
sections.Results: A total of 105 CF clinicians responded, representing pediatric, adult, and mixed practices, with most having over five years of CF experience. Assessment of adherence and drug–drug interactions were considered realistic by 59% and 69% of respondents, respectively. Modulator dose reduction due to hepatic impairment or mental health adverse effects was viewed as realistic by ~71%. Pregnancy-related monitoring and exercise-related hydration management were considered realistic by 46% and 31%. POC sweat chloride testing was perceived as clinically useful across all scenarios, with highest utility for modulator dose modification due to hepatic impairment (87%) and mental health adverse effects (88%). Adherence assessment and drug–drug interactions were also frequently identified as useful (82–84%).
sections.Conclusion: CF clinicians perceive POC sweat chloride testing as a clinically useful biomarker across diverse real-world care scenarios, supporting prospective evaluation of its role in individualized CFTR modulator management and physiologic monitoring.

presentations.title: Maintaining skills, evolving roles, enhancing knowledge: physiotherapy in cystic fibrosis
presentations.summary: Introduction: With highly effective modulators and specialised multidisciplinary care reshaping cystic fibrosis (CF) management, physiotherapy roles continue to evolve. While many individuals experience reduced symptom burden, others face complex exacerbations, or limited access to modulators, and increased complexity with ageing. To ensure ongoing access to evidence-based resources, Australian physiotherapists (with ongoing input from global clinicians) established CFPhysio.com Inc (CFP Inc), a not-for-profit organisation supporting professional development (PD) through digital technology. Method: Specialist physiotherapists translated evidence-based guidelines into an online learning platform offering free,...
presentations.authors_text: J. Hauser1, J. Bishop2, R. Cobb3, N. Ward4, B. Button
presentations.affiliations_text: 1CFPhysio.com Inc, Hobart, Australia, 2Adult CF Unit, Westmead Hospital, Sydney, Australia, 3Adult CF Centre, The Prince Charles Hospital, Brisbane, Australia, 4Heart and Lung Program Central Adelaide Local Health Network, Adelaide, Australia

sections.Methods: Specialist physiotherapists translated evidence-based guidelines into an online learning platform offering free, self-paced modules in written, video, and illustrated formats. Initiatives such as REACH for CF (Research, education and collaboration for CF) and the Chronic Suppurative Lung Disease Education series extend the site into a collaborative hub for connection and learning. Outcomes: Analytics of the website, since launch in 2019, report site is accessed by over 2700 clinicians. Registration surveys (n= 846) highlight only 18% of users have completed CF specific PD 12 months prior to visiting the website. Across 9 domains of physiotherapy skills in CF care, self-rating of knowledge and confidence was between low and average, with 50% of respondents rating their knowledge in airway pathogens, inhalation therapy, and autogenic drainage as low or very low. All respondents in a snapshot review of the airway clearance modules (n=113) rated content helpful and improved their knowledge and confidence.
sections.Conclusion: CFP Inc provides sustainable, responsive physiotherapy education for clinicians. By leveraging technology, the organisation continues to overcome barriers in maintaining knowledge, fostering empowerment and supporting the CF community. Acknowledgements: CFPhysio.com Inc executive committee and clinicians contributing to content and education resource delivery.

presentations.summary: Objectives: Optimising the delivery of inhaled therapies for CF requires an understanding of the impact of structural and functional effects of CF lung disease on particle deposition. Methods: In one volunteer with CF (M, age = 44.8 yrs, ppFEV1 = 64.9), ultrashort echo time (UTE) 1H-MRI was used to segment visible airways and then further generate an idealised model of airway structure. From their 129Xe ventilation MRI, ventilation defects were linked to the corresponding airways in the model. These airways were then constricted to achie...
presentations.authors_text: J. Brady1, Y. Brown2, A. Biancardi2, D.-J. Jakymelen2, L. Smith2, J. Wild2, A. Horsley1, C. Whitfield3, Magnify Strategic Research Centre
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Manchester, Division of Immunology, Immunity to Infection and Respiratory Medicine, Manchester, United Kingdom, 2University of Sheffield, POLARIS, University MRI, Division of Clinical Medicine, Sheffield, United Kingdom, 3University of Manchester, Department of Mathematics, Manchester, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Optimising the delivery of inhaled therapies for CF requires an understanding of the impact of structural and functional effects of CF lung disease on particle deposition. These are not adequately represented in current computational deposition models. Our aim is to develop a personalised lung deposition model for pwCF based on 1H and 129Xe-MRI.
sections.Methods: In one volunteer with CF (M, age = 44.8 yrs, ppFEV1 = 64.9), ultrashort echo time (UTE) 1H-MRI was used to segment visible airways and then further generate an idealised model of airway structure. From their 129Xe ventilation MRI, ventilation defects were linked to the corresponding airways in the model. These airways were then constricted to achieve optimal agreement between the 129Xe MR image and the ventilation distribution in the model lung after a simulated inspiration. We then simulated inspiration of a 100ml bolus of 3µm diameter particles at the centre of a 1L tidal breath, in both the unconstricted and constricted models, to mimic aerosol bolus dispersion (ABD) experiments.
sections.Results: The simulated 129Xe MR manoeuvre in the constricted model reproduces the low ventilation percentage (LVP) (37.2% vs. 41.2% in the 129Xe MR image), but overestimates ventilation defect percentage (VDP) (31.8% vs. 22.4%). Deposition increased with airway constriction from 50% of particles inhaled, to 57%, with the greatest increase occurring in airway generation 5 (from 5.7% to 9.0%). The mean percentage change in dose delivered to each airway generation with constrictions introduced was 41%.
sections.Conclusion: The predicted small increase in airway deposition due to constrictions is consistent with previous clinical reports. However, this model also captures the resulting spatial heterogeneity in delivered dose which is significant for drug delivery applications. The ability to generate patient-specific testable predictions, such as ABD experiments, will be used to validate and further improve the model in future studies.

The utilisation of Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) has resulted in improvements in lung function measurements in adults with CF but less is known on its sustained impact on exercise capacity.Aim:Cystic fibrosis (CF) is associated with progressive decline in lung function and reduced exercise tolerance. However, long-term, real-world changes in exercise capacity after treatment remain limited particularly during and after Kaftrio and a globa...
presentations.authors_text: I. Waller1, R. McVean1, S. Kiernan1, N. Hodgson1, J. Mitchell1, P. Barry1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Manchester Foundation Trust, Cystic Fibrosis, Manchester, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Prior to the introduction of CFTR modulation CF lung disease was characterised by progressive decline in lung functionCardio-pulmonary exercise testing (CPET) has been reported to provide additional prognostic information beyond lung function measurements. The utilisation of Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) has resulted in improvements in lung function measurements in adults with CF but less is known on its sustained impact on exercise capacity.Aim:Cystic fibrosis (CF) is associated with progressive decline in lung function and reduced exercise tolerance. However, long-term, real-world changes in exercise capacity after treatment remain limited particularly during and after Kaftrio and a global pandemic. MethodsA retrospective review of CPET data was collected during routine clinical monitoring in adults with CF. Data from a CPET prior to ETI were compared to data from two tests conducted post ETI Results Data from 78 CPETs in 26 adults (median age 27.0 [23.5-34.5] years) were analysed. Baseline tests were conducted a mean of 543±275 days prior to ETI with the first post ETI test being conducted a mean of 694±320 days post ETI and the third test conducted a mean of 735±366 days following this. Mean FEV1 percent predicted increased significantly between post ETI test 1 and baseline (59.4±18.9 vs 69.9±18.6, p<0.001) and was sustained at post test 2 (70.6±20.8, p=0.623). Median pO2 was significantly higher at the both post ETI tests compared to baseline (p<0.001). VO2peak was not significantly different between baseline and any of the post ETI tests but a significant increase was seen between baseline and post test 2 when corrected to percent predicted (75.6±13.7 vs 82.9±15.8, p=0.013). VO2 percent predicted at anaerobic threshold was not significantly different post ETI
sections.Conclusion: VO2peak is maintained over time in adults with CF treated with ETI. Maintaining aerobic capacity may be important in preserving long term health for adults with CF

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 01 -Targeting pseudomonas in cystic fibrosis: from mechanisms to new therapies

sections.Objectives: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) airway colonization in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) requires close monitoring due to its association with increased pulmonary exacerbations, progressive lung damage and accelerated decline in pulmonary function. Prior studies from our group identified pwCF who produced highly neutralizing antibodies against the type III secretion system (T3SS) of Pa. The aim of this work is to investigate the clinical relevance of these antibodies and immunological mechanisms driving chronic Pa infection.
sections.Methods: We quantified serum T3SS-neutralizing activity in 99 pwCF and 12 healthy individuals. Neutralization capacity was integrated with immunophenotyping, including FACS-based characterization and bulk RNA sequencing of peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs), as well as clinical parameters. In the CF group, blood samples were collected twice with a time interval of two years.
sections.Results: As expected, high neutralization capacity (Pa-Abhigh) was associated with chronic Pa infection. Immunophenotyping of pwCF with high and low neutralization revealed distinct cellular profiles including specific differences in monocyte and lymphocyte subpopulations, with more non-classical monocytes and fewer naïve CD8+ T-cells in Pa-Abhigh. These cellular alterations were reflected in transcriptomic differences, particularly within cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction and adaptive immunity pathways. Spirometry data showed that Pa-Abhigh individuals had reduced forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV1). Moreover, an increase of Pa neutralization over time was linked to a progressive decline in FEV1.
sections.Conclusion: Our findings offer a mechanistic insight into the development of potent neutralizing anti-T3SS antibodies and reveal a unique immunological profile linked to chronic Pa infection and reduced lung function. Incorporating this immune signature into clinical practice may enable early detection of patients at risk of disease progression.

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 02 - Rare genotypes & challenging phenotypes

sections.Background: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) improves F508del-CFTR function and leads to substantial clinical benefits. However, people with CF and the F508del mutation and the complex allele L467F showed limited clinical response to ETI. Recently, vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaftor (VTD) was approved as an alternative modulator therapy for people with CF and at least one non-class I mutation. However, the effects of VTD in patients carrying the complex allele L467F have not been investigated. Within an ECFS Twinning project in collaboration with the CF center in Skopje, North Macedonia, we therefore assessed the pharmacological rescue of CFTR activity by ETI and VTD in primary nasal epithelial cells (pHNEC) of people with at least one F508del-L467F allele.
sections.Methods: Highly differentiated pHNEC were established from 4 F508del-L467F-homozygous people with CF, 3 people with CF compound heterozygous for F508del-L467F and a minimal function mutation and 3 healthy controls. CFTR activity was assessed by transepithelial short-circuit currents in Ussing chamber after VTD/ETI/DMSO incubation and expressed as % of healthy CFTR function.
sections.Results: Treatment with ETI did not improve CFTR-mediated chloride currents, determined by forskolin peak response (P = 0.16) and CFTRinhb172-inhibited currents (P = 0.53), compared to vehicle-treated control in pHNECs of the 7 patients with at least one F508del-L467F allele. Interestingly, VTD treatment increased CFTR-mediated chloride currents following forskolin/IBMX stimulation compared to vehicle control (P < 0.001) and ETI (P < 0.05). Moreover, CFTR activity was inhibited by CFTRinhb172 in comparison to vehicle control (P < 0.01) and ETI (P < 0.05). PHNEC’s of the whole cohort showed a response to VTD with a mean correction of CFTR activity of about 7% of the healthy controls.
sections.Conclusion: VTD but not ETI treatment improves CFTR function in vitro in people with CF carrying at least one F508del-L467F allele.

We also compared the data to the Welsh national statistics for young people (16-24) not in education, employment or training (NEET) in 2015 and 2025. Results: Data is summ...

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 03 - From breakthrough to burdens: mental health in modern cystic fibrosis care

sections.Objectives: The past decade has seen growth in the number of young people with CF (YPwCF) in the All Wales Adult CF Centre, as well as the introduction of CFTR modulators earlier in life. Given these changes, we compared YPwCF’s involvement in education, employment and training in 2025 with 2015.
sections.Methods: Employment and education data collected at annual review in 2015 (n=71, 44 M, 27 F, mean age 21, mean FEV1 76.7%) were compared to 2025 data (n=91, 48 M, 39 F, 4 TG, mean age 20, mean FEV1 93.4%) for patients in the adult service aged 16-24. We also compared the data to the Welsh national statistics for young people (16-24) not in education, employment or training (NEET) in 2015 and 2025.
sections.Results: Data is summarised in the table below. %NEET Full-time work (FTW) Part-time work (PTW)Education Education and PTWReceiving health benefits 2015 26.8 23.9 23.933.8 8.587.3 Welsh National Average 2015 16.0 2025 14.3 26.4 28.648.4 17.652.8 Provisional Welsh National Average 2025 15.1 Alongside the rise in YPwCF in education, the proportion studying at a higher level e.g. degree increased. No YPwCF were parents/carers in 2015, but in 2025, a small number (6/91) were parents/carers, of whom 3/6 were classed as NEET (or economically unavailable), 2/6 worked part-time and 1/6 combined study and PTW.
sections.Conclusion: The proportion of YPwCF who are NEET has declined between 2015 and 2025, showing more engagement in work and study. A rise in FEV1 and drop in health-related benefits indicate improvement in health. Whilst unable to draw firm conclusions it is likely that CFTR modulators contributed to improving health, ability and expectations of YPwCF to pursue study and work. Given likely continued improvements in health, we should consider the support needed for YPwCF to manage health alongside work and study.

sections.Objectives: CFTR modulators have transformed cystic fibrosis (CF) care; however, variability in patient responses highlights the need for more predictive and physiologically relevant functional assays. Patient-derived intestinal organoids (PDIOs) enable high-throughput, yet indirect assessment of CFTR activity through the forskolin-induced swelling (FIS) assay.1 Here, we aim to establish fluorescence-based assays for direct, quantitative, real-time readouts of CFTR activity in PDIO-derived systems using genetically encoded ion-sensitive fluorescent sensors, such as HS-YFP-mKate2 for iodide flux and ClopHensor3 for chloride and pH measurements.
sections.Methods: HS-YFP-mKate and Clop3 constructs were cloned into lentiviral backbones via Gibson Assembly, and lentiviral particles were produced in HEK293T cells to transduce wild-type and F508del/F508del CFTR PDIOs. Fluorescent organoids were fluorescence-activated cell sorted (FACS) to generate homogeneous reporter populations.
sections.Results: Both three-dimensional organoid cultures and two-dimensional monolayer assays are under development to support automated high-throughput microscopy of CFTR-dependent halide and bicarbonate fluxes. Stable fluorescent PDIO populations expressing HS-YFP-mKate or Clop3 were successfully generated, and preliminary data demonstrate CFTR-dependent HS-YFP quenching upon pharmacological activation. Protocol optimisation for combined chloride and pH measurements using ClopHensor is ongoing.
sections.Conclusion: Together, these fluorescent intestinal cell models provide a robust platform for real-time CFTR functional testing and drug screening, with potential to improve the accuracy of patient-specific response assessment to CFTR modulators. presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 05 - Fuel, frame and function in cystic fibrosis nutrition

sections.Objectives: People with cystic fibrosis (CF) have historically been advised to eat an unrestricted high-calorie, high-fat “legacy CF diet”, which is associated with poor diet quality, and may exacerbate gastrointestinal symptoms (GiS). This study examined associations between diet, dietary patterns and GiS.
sections.Methods: Adults from three UK CF centres completed GiS questionnaires to derive clusters using Latent Class Analysis. This provided four distinct phenotypes mild, moderate-diarrhoea, moderate-constipation and severe. Participants completed at least one 24-hour dietary recall using Intake24. Following quality checks, mean intake across two days was calculated. Diet quality and inflammatory potential were assessed using the Diet Quality Index-International (DQI-I) and Dietary Inflammatory Index (DII). Symptom phenotypes and diet were analysed using multinomial logistic regression.
sections.Results: 221 adults were included (50.2% male; median age 30 years, range 16-67 years). Median energy intake was lowest in the severe phenotype (1608 kcal/day), although intake was broadly comparable across phenotypes. Fibre intake was also the lowest in the severe phenotype (14 g/day) compared with mild and both moderate groups 16 g/day). Fat intake contributions were broadly similar across phenotypes (median 32–35% energy). The moderate-diarrhoea phenotype exhibited the lowest mean DII score (1.1, SD 1.9), whereas the severe phenotype had the highest (1.9, SD 1.9). Regression analyses showed no significant associations between fat intake, fibre intake, DQI-I score or DII score and symptom phenotypes.
sections.Conclusion: Dietary intake, diet quality and inflammatory potential were not significantly associated with GiS phenotypes. Lower fibre intake and high DII score was observed among participants in the severe phenotype, but associations were not statistically significant. The high DII score in the severe phenotype may suggest a more pro-inflammatory dietary profile.

At T1, 52(59.1%) cases had no liver disease, 33(37.5%) had CFHBI and 3(3.4%) aCFLD versus 28(42.4%), 32(48.5%) and 6(9.1%) in control population. From T0 to T1, median weight increased by 11.6kg in control group versus 19.9kg in cases and median FEV1 declined by 7.9% in controls with no decline in cases. sections.Objectives: Antibiotic-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa poses a major challenge in chronic airway disease, particularly in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). Carbapenem-resistant P. aeruginosa is considered a highest-priority bacterial threat, with resistance frequently driven by loss-of-function mutations in the carbapenem uptake porin OprD. However, the broader consequences of these mutations for bacterial physiology and infection dynamics remain poorly understood.
sections.Methods: Clinical P. aeruginosa strains isolated longitudinally from pwCF were analyzed to assess the emergence of oprD mutations. The effects of OprD loss were evaluated using an air–liquid interface airway epithelial model, measuring early bacterial attachment and translocation across epithelial barriers. Biophysical analyses were performed to assess changes in outer membrane properties and mucus interactions due to OprD loss across diverse clinical genomic backgrounds.
sections.Results: Here we show that oprD mutations can arise in clinical strains isolated from pwCF in the absence of carbapenem treatment, suggesting selective pressures beyond direct antibiotic exposure. In the airway epithelial model, oprD mutants displayed enhanced early attachment to epithelial surfaces and increased translocation across the epithelial barrier, a phenotype conserved across diverse clinical genomic backgrounds. Biophysical analyses indicated that loss of OprD alters outer membrane charge and reduces mucus entrapment, thereby facilitating epithelial colonization independently of antimicrobial pressure.
sections.Conclusion: These findings demonstrate that antibiotic resistance mutations can directly modulate host–pathogen interactions during airway infection, reshaping colonization dynamics beyond their impact on antibiotic susceptibility. This highlights the need to consider the wider biological effects of resistance-associated mutations when evaluating infection dynamics and clinical outcomes.

sections.Background: People with Cystic fibrosis carrying two class I CFTR mutations are generally considered non-responsive to CFTR modulators. According to recent literature [1], the genotype G542X/622-2A>C is predicted as Class I/I, that would result in no protein production, precluding benefit from CFTR modulators. However, 622-2A>C is a very rare CFTR undercharacterized variant.
sections.Methods: Patient-derived rectal organoids (PDROs) from a woman with with CF carrying the G542X/622-2A>C genotype were analyzed via RT-PCR/sequencing of complete CFTR cDNA, immunoblot, and forskolin-induced swelling (FIS) assays with ivacaftor(I), tezacaftor/I (TI), and elexacaftor/TI (ETI). An off-label treatment was also conducted.
sections.Results: RNA analysis showed 622-2A>C caused alternative splicing, skipping of the first 9-nucleotides of exon 5 leading to the deletion of 3 amino acids in the first intracellular loop (ICL1). Protein analysis showed core-glycosylated CFTR (band B) partially rescued to full-glycosylated (band C) after ET treatment. PDRO theratyping showed significant CFTR function restoration with ETI (0.8µM FIS AUC 2779.44; SD 67.29), supporting ETI sensitivity. Off-label initiation of ETI led to substantial clinical improvement over 6 months: FEV₁ rose from 2.24L (75%) to 2.50L (84%), BMI from 21.9 to 22.7 kg/m², and exacerbations/hospitalizations fell to zero. Sweat chloride improved from 103 to 69 mmol/L. Sweat chloride concentration improved from 103 to 69 mmol/L.
sections.Conclusion: PDRO-based precision medicine identified a misclassified class I mutation, 622-2A>C, and demonstrated in vitro and in vivo responsiveness to ETI. This case shows personalized in vitro CFTR functional testing can uncover unexpected modulator sensitivity in supposed Class I genotypes and reinforces the role of precision-medicine approaches in extending modulator access for patients with very rare CFTR variants. [1] eClinicalMedicine. 2025 Aug 30;88:103476. Study funded by KUL C2 grant (C24M/21/041)

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 05 - Fuel, frame and function in cystic fibrosis nutrition

sections.Background: Historically, people with cystic fibrosis (CF) have been advised to consume a high-sodium diet to compensate for excessive sweat sodium losses caused by CFTR dysfunction. Highly effective CFTR modulator therapy improves CFTR function and reduces sweat sodium losses, potentially altering sodium balance. Persistently high sodium intake increases risk of hypertension, an emerging concern as the CF population ages.
sections.Objectives: To investigate whether adults with CF receiving CFTR modulator therapy exhibit high urinary sodium concentrations [uNa] as a proxy for high dietary sodium intake.
sections.Methods: Retrospective cohort study using routinely collected spot urinary sodium and sodium/creatinine concentrations. Analysed for patients receiving CFTR modulators and a contemporaneous control group not receiving modulators. Independent t-tests and Mann–Whitney U tests were used, with p<0.05 considered statistically significant.
sections.Results: Sixty-one patients provided urinary samples; 53 were receiving CFTR modulator therapy and 8 formed the control group. Mean urinary sodium concentration was higher in the modulator group (81.2 mmol/L) than controls (67.6 mmol/L), though this difference was not statistically significant. Marked intra- and inter-individual variability was observed. The use of urinary sodium/creatinine ratios (to reduce impact of renal water reabsorption) did not significantly reduce variability; there was still no difference between groups. Patients in receipt of sodium supplementation did not have significantly higher urinary sodium concentrations. Nearly 70% of patients receiving modulators had mean urinary sodium concentrations >50 mmol/L.
sections.Conclusion: Although no statistically significant difference in urinary sodium excretion was demonstrated between groups, many adults with CF receiving CFTR modulator therapy exhibit relatively high [uNa]. These findings raise questions regarding the appropriateness of longstanding high-sodium dietary recommendations in the CFTR modulator era. More robust prospective studies incorporating 24-hour urine collections, dietary assessment, and blood pressure monitoring are required to inform individualised sodium guidance and long-term cardiovascular risk management in CF.

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 06 - Gastrointestinal and hepatic trajectories in cystic fibrosis from early life to adulthood

sections.Background: Pancreas disease is one of the first disease complication in Cystic Fibrosis (CF) reflected by high immunoreactive trypsin (IRT) at birth used as CF screening test. Although a postnatal decline in serum trypsin has previously been described, longitudinal trypsin trajectories across childhood have not been well characterized. We leveraged the longitudinal clinical data and serum trypsin measurements in the Toronto CF Database (TCF) to estimate trypsin trajectories and their association with clinical outcomes.
sections.Methods: Retrospective analysis with data from 1984 to 2017. Longitudinal trajectories of trypsin measured in serum as trypsinogen using a radioimmunoassay (RIA) were modeled using repeated-measures linear regression.
sections.Results: Trypsin trajectories were estimated in 265 individuals with at least one measurement <1y and at least one >1y (15 CFSPID, 19 CFPS, and 227 CFPI). Estimated trypsin level at birth was significantly higher in CFPI than CFPS and CFSPID (p < 0.0001 for both comparisons), and higher in CFPS than CFSPID (p = 0.0003). Individuals with CFPI exhibited a steep decline in trypsin levels, crossing the threshold for pancreatic insufficiency by 4.2y. In contrast, CFPS and CFSPID demonstrated more gradual declines, with levels remaining within the pancreatic-sufficient range. The presence of meconium ileus was not associated with trypsin decline (p = 0.6), whereas the MIP score, a measure of CFTR variant severity, was associated with steeper declines (p = 0.011). Analyses assessing the predictive value of trypsin trajectories for clinical outcomes are ongoing.
sections.Conclusion: Longitudinal trypsin decline differs markedly between mild and severe CF phenotypes and genotypes. With recent validation of IRT as a reliable surrogate for serum trypsin (Gummadi et al, 2025), this biomarker is widely accessible, but longitudinal reference ranges for IRT beyond the newborn period need to be established.

sections.Methods: Human nasal epithelial cells obtained via the nasal brushing technique were used to generate three-dimensional spheroid cultures. The primary aim was to assess the feasibility of establishing 3D cultures, while the secondary aim was to investigate in vitro growth kinetics during spheroid formation using live imaging. Cells were embedded in 3D Matrigel to establish spheroid cultures1. Time-lapse images were acquired using an inverted microscope to analyze growth kinetics.
sections.Results: The study population consisted of 7 children with cystic fibrosis (cwCF), 8 children with a preliminary diagnosis of primary ciliary dyskinesia (cwpdPCD), and 1 child with cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator–related disorder (cwCFTR-RD). After 7 and 14 days of culture, spheroid number and size were evaluated using microscopic imaging and morphological analysis. Spheroid formation was successfully achieved in all samples. Spheroids derived from cwpdPCD patients were generated in significantly higher numbers compared with those obtained from cwCF/cwCFTR-RD patients. Moreover, spheroids from cwpdPCD patients exhibited significantly larger diameters at both time points.
sections.Conclusion: This study reports the first successful generation of three-dimensional airway epithelial models from pediatric nasal epithelial cells in Türkiye. These findings provide foundational evidence and represent a major step forward in advancing translational and personalized research in pulmonology nationwide. 1Brewington JJ et al.J Vis Exp.2018;134:57492

sections.Objectives: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (PA) causes chronic lung infections in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). Small molecules known as modulators partially restore the defective protein causing CF. Despite several patients experience sputum load decreases and apparently resolve chronic bacterial infection, PA may persist through adaptation. We investigated how PA adapts to the changed environment in pwCF during modulators therapy by integrating phenotypic and genomic data.
sections.Methods: We investigated 563 PA strains isolated from 35 chronically colonised pwCF collected from a maximum of 15 years pre-treatment and up to 3 years post-treatment with elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor (ETI) or previous modulators. Phenotypic resistance and biofilm production under conditions mimicking the CF lung environment were confronted with Whole Genome Sequencing (WGS) data to elucidate changes under modulators' selective pressure.
sections.Results: 31 out of 35 pwCF retained the same Sequence Type (ST) pre-ETI, despite 1 case of prolonged negativity; 9 acquired new STs (even High-Risk Clones) without loss and 4 had clone replacement. Changes observed under modulators involved loss of non-mucoid morphotype, biofilm hyperproduction (up to 18-fold), and changes in antibiotic resistance. Hypermutability appeared under 3 different modulators (8 cases, ETI included) but decreased only under ETI (4 cases). Evolution of colonising PA into novel STs was also observed under ETI. Indeed, in 3 pwCF, ST274 evolved in novel STs, with convergent evolution giving rise to the same novel ST in 2 of them.
sections.Conclusion: Our data indicate variability in the response to modulators, which drive adaptation through phenotypic and genomic changes with ambivalent effects, requiring attention in terms of their clinical implications. Some STs demonstrate reduced persistence in the face of pressure exerted by modulators. This suggests that modulator therapy could have a greater impact on lung colonisation in certain pwCF.

sections.Objectives: Although clinical studies have reported positive effects of ETI in people with cystic fibrosis (PWCF), longitudinal data are limited. These results are part of an ongoing longitudinal study on the global effects of ETI in adolescents and adults.
sections.Methods: PWCF aged ≥12 years eligible for ETI completed longitudinal assessments at baseline (T0), 1-mos (T1), 3-mos (T2), 6-mos (T3), 12-mos (T4), 18-mos (T5), 24-mos (T6) after ETI. Measures: a) physical parameters (FEV₁, BMI, GI Symptom Tracker); b) HRQoL (CFQ-R); c) depression (PHQ-9) & anxiety (GAD-7); d) cognitive processing (SDMT); e) ad hoc neurocognitive side-effects questionnaire.
sections.Results: A total of 128 PWCF were recruited (F/M=67/61; M age=26.8 yr). FEV₁ increased at T1 and remained stable (T0<T1–T6; p<.001). BMI increased at 18-mos (p<.05). Improvements for GI symptoms: Abdominal (T0>T5; p<.05), Stools (T0>T4=T5; p<.005), Eating (T0>T1–T6; p<.001), Adherence (T0>T4–T6; p<.001). Improvements in HRQoL: Physical, Health, Respiratory and Role (T0<T1–T6; p<.001); Vitality (T0<T1–T4; p<.001); Emotional (T0<T4–T6; p<.05), Eating and Social (T0<T3–T6; p<.001); Treatment Burden (T0<T2=T4=T5=T6; p<.005), Body Image (T0<T4–T6; p<.001), Weight (T0<T1–T6; p<.001); Digestive (T0<T4=T6; p<.001). Treatment Burden remained low (range 54–66). Depression improved (T0>T2–T5; p<.05), anxiety was higher in females (p<.05). Cognitive processing improved at T1 and T2 (p<.001) and was positively correlated with FEV₁ (p<.05). Neurocognitive effects: insomnia (13–34%), headaches (32–39%), memory problems (18–34%), concentration problems (15–38%), brain fog (7–14%). Worsening trends were observed for insomnia (T1<T5=T6; p<.001; F>M, p<.05), memory and concentration problems (T1<T5=T6; p<.005 - T1<T2–T6; p<.001; M>F, p<.001).
sections.Conclusion: Overall, ETI was associated with significant improvements across multiple physical and mental health domains, while neurocognitive side effects persisted, particularly in females. An improvement in GI symptoms was observed after 12 months.

sections.Methods: We performed a retrospective, single-center analysis at the adult and pediatric Montpellier CF Center. Salt requirements were assessed under standardized conditions by comparing patients’ salt intake with French High Authority for Health recommendations and urinary Na/K ratio (A urinary Na/K ratio < 1 may indicate insufficient sodium intake, particularly under high salt loss conditions such as heat or exercise). Analyses were conducted in winter and repeated in summer, incorporating ambient temperature and physical activity data.
sections.Results: In winter, only 0.9% of 112 adults required additional salt at breakfast beyond recommended intakes. Breakfasts were generally low in sodium, with 67% below recommendations, and 6.2% showed a urinary Na/K ratio <1. Among 61 pediatric patients, 21.3% needed salt supplementation despite sodium intakes exceeding age-specific recommendations. In summer (ambient temperature >30 °C), 47 usable urinary analyses (22 children, 3 adults) revealed that 87% consumed more than 150% of the recommended salt intake. Nevertheless, 21% had a urinary Na/K ratio <1, mainly associated with intense physical activity.
sections.Conclusion: Despite HEMT, certain pwCF continue to require sodium supplementation beyond standard population guidelines. Routine ionogram monitoring can help prevent both hypernatremia and hyponatremia. Sodium management should remain. individualized, with heightened vigilance in pediatric patients—even during winter—and should incorporate age, environmental conditions, physical activity level, urinary Na/K ratio, and baseline dietary intake.

CF-related diabetes (CFRD) is a prominent comorbidity in pwCF, yet its impact upon the gut microbiome is not known. We investigated the gut microbiota, metabolome, and lipidome across pwCF with/without CFRD and control participants.
sections.Methods: Stool samples from pwCF with (n=33) and without (n=33) CFRD, alongside controls (n=19), were subjected to full-length 16S rRNA microbiota sequencing. LC/MS analyses (n=57) were conducted on CF stool samples to investigate differences in metabolite and lipid composition. Multivariate approaches and generalised linear models were used to investigate relationships with clinical data.
sections.Results: Microbiota diversity and composition significantly differed between controls and pwCF, including across CF groups (q<0.05). A higher prevalence of gut pathobionts, alongside reduced prevalence of beneficial taxa was observed in CFRD. Strikingly, an increased prevalence of lung pathogens (including Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus) was also observed in CFRD. Additional clinical factors significantly explained microbiota variation. Modulator therapy was not significant. Importantly, after adjusting for such confounders, CFRD remained a significant driver of microbiota composition across pwCF (q=0.024). Lipidomic profiles also significantly clustered based on the presence of CFRD following covariate adjustment (q=0.030). Immunomodulatory lipid species, including increased hexosylceramides (q=0.030) and oxidised phosphatidylcholines (q=0.073), alongside decreased sulphonolipids (q=0.054), were observed across the CFRD group.
sections.Conclusion: We demonstrate for the first time a distinct and worse microbiome dysbiosis in CFRD. The causative nature and clinical consequences of such host-microbe alterations warrant additional research of the CF gut utilising multiomics approaches.

sections.Objectives: Widely used cystic fibrosis (CF) in vitro models, including cell lines, primary airway epithelial cultures and organoids are essential for testing CFTR modulators (CFTRm), but fail to capture the complexity of native lung tissue. Precision-cut lung slices (PCLS) from healthy or explanted CF lungs retain three-dimensional architecture and cellular diversity, providing a highly relevant ex vivo translational system. Our aim was to develop and validate an optimized human PCLS protocol supporting prolonged tissue viability, preservation of key structural and functional features, and reproducible response to infectious, inflammatory and pharmacological stimuli.
sections.Methods: Tissue handling, agarose infusion, Compresstome slicing and cultures conditions were optimized to minimize tissue injury and maximize serial slice yield. Model performance was assessed in fresh and cryopreserved PCLS through viability, cellular composition and functional readouts following exposure to bacterial virulence factors, bronchoalveolar lavages from infected patients and clinically relevant CFTRm. The optimized workflow generates hundreds of serial slices within 1-2 working days and supports both fresh use or cryostorage. PCLS sustained native multicellular organization and ciliary activity, and remained viable for up to 35 days, enabling longitudinal studies. The model allows standardized infectious/inflammatory challenges and CFTRm treatments, with multimodal readouts including tissue integrity, cytokine production and CFTR rescue.
sections.Results: Tissue handling, agarose infusion, Compresstome slicing and cultures conditions were optimized to minimize tissue injury and maximize serial slice yield. The optimized workflow generates hundreds of serial slices within 1-2 working days and supports both fresh use or cryostorage. The model allows standardized infectious/inflammatory challenges and CFTRm treatments, with multimodal readouts including tissue integrity, cytokine production and CFTR rescue.

The optimized workflow generates hundreds of serial slices within 1-2 working days and supports both fresh use or cryostorage. PCLS sustained native multicellular organization and ciliary activity, and remained viable for up to 35 days, enabling longitudinal studies. The model allows standardized infectious/inflammatory challenges and CFTRm treatments, with multimodal readouts including tissue integrity, cytokine production and CFTR rescue.
sections.Conclusion: This human PCLS model preserves the native lung microenvironment for extended ex vivo studies and represents a versatile preclinical tool to examine host-pathogen interaction, inflammatory mechanisms, and inter-individual variability in responses to CFTRm. presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 01 -Targeting pseudomonas in cystic fibrosis: from mechanisms to new therapies

sections.Objectives: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (PA) is a key pathogen in cystic fibrosis (CF), linked to high morbidity and mortality. Early antibiotic therapy may eradicate new PA, but if it reappears, eradication may not have happened. Distinguishing between strain persistence and new acquisition is crucial for guiding treatment strategies to prevent chronicity. This is also important for CF patients on elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI), re-presenting PA after negativity. Using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), we aimed to assess the clonality of PA strains, focusing on isolates responsible for 1st and 2nd infections, intermittent colonisation and re-emergence on ETI.
sections.Methods: A total of 103 PA isolates causing infection in 36 people with CF (pwCF) were compared by FTIR with post-treatment isolates. Two were chronically colonized and 2 intermittently colonised; 12 had a history of chronic colonization followed by PA negativity after starting modulator treatment; and 20 had received eradication therapy for a first PA infection, in 10 cases either before or after initiating modulators. Whole Genome Sequencing (WGS) was used to validate FTIR results.
sections.Results: FTIR and WGS revealed same-strain persistence in 24 patients, confirming a 100% predictive value for FTIR. Persistence occurred in intermittently and chronically colonised pwCF: 9 on modulators and 11 underwent eradication therapy (6 on modulators). WGS identified 3 FTIR misclassifications (3 pairs of isolates deemed as different), due to phenotypic differences and spectral variability among PA isolates. Significant temporal overlap between new infections and failed eradications demonstrates timing’s unreliability as sole differentiator.
sections.Conclusion: FTIR is an easily implementable diagnostic tool that can define the success of eradicating therapy and optimise therapy. Our study proves its ability to inform appropriate therapeutic decisions for pwCF with recurrent PA infections.

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 02 - Rare genotypes & challenging phenotypes

sections.Objectives: People with Cystic Fibrosis (CF) are achieving successful pregnancies with CF transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) modulator therapy; predominantly elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI). Evidence suggests that exposure to ETI in utero or by breastmilk may partially restore CFTR function in the CF fetus, which can lead to falsely low immunoreactive trypsinogen (IRT) levels. Infants exposed to ETI may be at risk for falsely negative screens with British Columbia's (BC) IRT-DNA-IRT CF newborn screening (NBS) model. As part of a quality improvement initiative, a pathway to identify infants at risk and to monitor those with ETI exposure was developed.
sections.Methods: A working group of registered nurses (RNs), physicians and geneticists developed a process to identify ETI-exposed pregnancies and ensure CF Canada monitoring guidelines were met. An automated alert was integrated into the BC CF registry in October 2025. At any BC CF clinic, new pregnancies recorded in the electronic medical record trigger an email to notify the RNs, linking to a secure dashboard with patient information. RNs then request CFTR genotyping on the infants, regardless of IRT level. RNs coordinate counseling with the families and set up monitoring of liver function tests and an eye exam.
sections.Results: In BC, 26 ETI-exposed infants were born from 2016-2025. Prior to pathway implementation, 50% of these infants had no CFTR genotyping on NBS; 46% had no monitoring. Since implementation, three pregnancies triggered the pathway and 100% of infants have had CFTR genotyping on NBS and completed monitoring. Families reported appreciation for proactive communication and clear guidance.
sections.Conclusion: An automated pregnancy alert is critical to reduce missed ETI-exposed infants. This initiative strengthened communication within the NBS program and leveraged the provincial registry to reduce operational burden. Collaboration with other Canadian CF clinics to create a national pathway is underway.

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 03 - From breakthrough to burdens: mental health in modern cystic fibrosis care

sections.Objectives: The aim of the study was to examine the relationship between stress coping and post-traumatic growth (PTG) in fathers of children with cystic fibrosis (CF).
sections.Methods: The sample consisted of 67 fathers of children and adolescents with cystic fibrosis (average age 37.25 years, SD = 6.50) recruited from the CF Foundation MATIO in Poland. The COPE inventory for Measuring Coping with Stress by Ch. S. Carver, in the Polish adaptation by Juczyński and Ogińska-Bulik (2009) and The Post-traumatic Growth Inventory (PTGI) by Tedeschi and Calhoun, adapted into Polish by Ogińska-Bulik and Juczyński (2010) were used.
sections.Results: The most frequent coping strategies used by fathers of children and adolescents with CF were: Planning, Active coping, and Positive Reinterpretation and Growth. More than 55 % of the examined fathers demonstrated average or high levels of PTG. Coping strategies were found to be correlated with the PTGI total score and all its dimensions. The strategies significantly associated with PTG included: Restrained coping, Focus on and Venting of Emotions, Behavioral Disengagement, Alcohol/substance use, Humour, Seeking Emotional Social Support, Religion, and Acceptance - most of which are classified as emotion-focused ones. Positive correlations were observed for strategies such as: Seeking Instrumental Social Support, Seeking Emotional Social Support, and Religion.
sections.Conclusion: The results of the study may constitute a basis for defining the scope of psychosocial support addressed to fathers of children and adolescents with CF and may inspire further research into correlates of the post-traumatic growth and other positively oriented variables in the studied population.

sections.Methods: A questionnaire was distributed in clinic from June 2024 to February 2025, consisting of 9 multiple choice or likert scale questions on bone health monitoring and related nutrition topics. ‘Importance’ and ‘personal concern’ rankings were scored 1 to 10(1 being not important, 10 most important). PwCF were divided into BMD group based on most recent DXA reports.
sections.Results: 113 pwCF, 58 men(51%), mean age 33.5yrs, 93(82%) pancreatic insufficient, 52(46%) DF508 homozygous completed the questionnaire. 87(77%) correctly identified that DXA assesses BMD. Table 1 shows the breakdown by BMD group. Table 1:PwCF perceived importance and concern BMD Group Total Identified DXA assesses BMD Mean importance score for DXA (out of 10) Mean personal concern score for bone health (out of 10)Normal BMD68 (60%)50 (74%)6.864.1Osteopenia34 (30%)29 (85%)6.974.76Osteoporotic11 (10%)8 (73%)7.26.36 Bone important nutrients identified included vitamin D(n=84) and protein(n=39) whilst 36 identified vitamin K. Dairy foods were identified most as calcium rich foods(milk n=106, yoghurt n=98, cheese n=96), whilst 27 chose fortified milk alternatives. 59 responders declined additional resources, whilst 24 requested more information on ‘lifestyle and bone health’ and ‘what DXA looks at’. Email was preferred to receive resources(n=71).
sections.Conclusion: Results identified positive correlations between importance of monitoring, personal concern and pwCF with worsening BMD. Gaps in bone health knowledge appear to exist and addressing these are crucial with an ageing CF population. Therefore, bespoke resources have been developed and are available for our patients.

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has been shown to significantly and clinically relevantly reduce abdominal symptoms (AS) in people with Cystic Fibrosis (CF) aged ≥6 years using the CFAbd-Score© and CFAbd-Score.kid©, respectively. In November 2023, ETI was approved for pre-school children with CF (cwCF), aged 2 to 5 years. This study aimed to capture changes in the burden of AS in cwCF aged 2–5 years during therapy with ETI.
sections.Methods: In this multicentric study, proxies of cwCF aged 2–5 years who received a new ETI-therapy completed the CFAbd-Score.kid© before and during therapy, on behalf and with active involvement of cwCF. The CFAbd-Score.kid© is a patient-reported outcome measure specifically designed for cwCF aged <12 years. It includes CF-specific gastrointestinal (GI) items, such as the Bristol stool scale and a stool colour chart. Pictograms, plain language, and child-friendly response strategies are implemented. The scoring algorithm, developed according to FDA guidelines, weights items and domains differently, reaching a maximum of 100 points.
sections.Results: A total of n=53 cwCF (n=27 female), with a median age of three years, were recruited in five German CF centres. They completed a total of n=191 questionnaires, according to a median of three questionnaires per participant over the entire observation period. The mean total CFAbd-Score.kid© declined significantly (-15.51%/p<0.05) during ETI therapy, as well as the "disorders of bowel movement" domain (-23.64%, p≤0.01). The other domains revealed positive trends without reaching significance, which we attribute to limitations in by-proxy rating of some AS.
sections.Conclusion: As one of the first real-world studies to examine the effects of ETI on GI symptoms of pre-school children, this study also reveals the effectiveness of ETI for preschool-children. As with schoolchildren and adults, ETI significantly reduces the burden of AS in children aged 2 to 5 years, when the CFAbd-Score© is applied by-proxy.
