presentations.summary: Objectives: P. aeruginosa is a major pathogen in cystic fibrosis (CF) patients, causing chronic lung infections associated with strong inflammation. In chronically infected patients, persistent P. aeruginosa form biofilm, which confers protection against host immunity and antibiotics, making infections refractory to treatments. In addition, co-infections with S. aureus are commonly observed in CF patients. To better control chronic infections, we developed a model in zebrafish embryo to study in vivo the bacterial persistence of clinical CF isolates in the context of mono or co-infections. This work aims to characterize the host inflammatory response associated with persistent bacteria in the context of mono-...
presentations.authors_text: F. Costes1, F. Nilly1, K. Moreau2, A.-B. Blanc-Potard1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Laboratory of Pathogens and Host Immunity (LPHI), Montpellier, France, 2Centre International de Recherche en Infectiologie (CIRI), Lyon, France
presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 01 -Targeting pseudomonas in cystic fibrosis: from mechanisms to new therapies

sections.Objectives: P. aeruginosa is a major pathogen in cystic fibrosis (CF) patients, causing chronic lung infections associated with strong inflammation. In chronically infected patients, persistent P. aeruginosa form biofilm, which confers protection against host immunity and antibiotics, making infections refractory to treatments. In addition, co-infections with S. aureus are commonly observed in CF patients. To better control chronic infections, we developed a model in zebrafish embryo to study in vivo the bacterial persistence of clinical CF isolates in the context of mono or co-infections. This work aims to characterize the host inflammatory response associated with persistent bacteria in the context of mono- or co-infections and to determine the localization of persistent bacteria in vivo, assessing whether they adopt a biofilm-like state.
sections.Methods: Zebrafish embryos were infected using a wound infection method with CF isolates. Various fluorescent tools were used: il1b:GFP fish line to assess the inflammatory response, mfap4:mCherry fish line to assess the intra-macrophagic localization of bacteria by confocal microscopy, and a fluorescent biofilm reporter to assess a biofilm-like state of bacteria.
sections.Results: We observed a strong il-1b response during persistent infection by P. aeruginosa CF isolates, which can be modulated in the context of co-infection with S. aureus (Costes et al., 2025). Persistent bacteria were visualized as intra-macrophagic clusters. Preliminary results showed the activation of a biofilm reporter for persistent P. aeruginosa.
sections.Conclusion: The zebrafish embryo is a relevant and powerful in vivo model to study P. aeruginosa persistence in mono- and co-infection. Notably, we have translated main parts of our findings in mammalian models. The visualization of intracellular, biofilm-like bacterial aggregates, associated with a pro-inflammatory response paves the way to identify and test therapeutic strategies efficient against chronic infections.

sections.Objectives: Quantitative pilocarpine iontophoresis sweat testing is essential for diagnosis of cystic fibrosis (CF), but requires specialised equipment and technical proficiency, limiting widespread availability and contributing to diagnostic delays, especially in less resourced areas or countries.
sections.Methods: We developed and tested a system of two sticker-like flexible, silicone-encased patches, measuring 6.0 × 2.8 × 0.75 cm. The stimulator patch uses a button battery and a pilocarpine hydrogel to induce sweat. The analyser patch uses microfluidic channels, reservoirs, and chemical reagents to enable colourimetric measurement of sweat chloride concentration. Colourimetric analysis is performed via an application on an iOS device. We compared sweat chloride concentrations between this system and the standard of care Macroduct™ system in children and young adults with CF or who were CF Screen Positive, Inconclusive Diagnosis (CFSPID), and in healthy adults.
sections.Results: Of 55 participants, 42 had CF (20 on modulators), 10 CFSPID, and 3 were healthy controls. Demographics were representative of the CF population seen at our Centre's pediatric program (Table 1). There were no quantity not sufficient tests. Sweat chloride ranges were consistent with clinical categorisation. Macroduct™ and colourimetric mean differences were small (Table 2). There was strong correlation between system sweat chloride values (Pearson's r = 0.98, R2 = 0.96). Comparisons between systems were similar to those from previously published comparing right vs. left arm Macroduct values¹ (data not shown). Participant/guardian acceptability was high (data not shown).
sections.Conclusion: Our flexible microfluidic sweat chloride system shows high correlation with the standard of care system and strong potential for use in diagnosis of and pharmacodynamic studies in CF. Inclusion of more infants in future studies is essential for further development. Reference: LeGrys, VA, et al. Both mothers and fathers were invited. A Theory of Planned Behavior–based questionnaire assessed attitude, subjective norm, and perceived control related to attendance (1–10 scale). Attendance at inpatient, physical outpatient...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Queen Silvia´s childrens´hospital, Cystic fibrosis, Gothenburg, Sweden, 2Institution of Molecular and clinical Medicine, Gothenburg, Sweden, 3Department of Paediatrics, Queen Silvia´s childrens´hospital, Gothenburg, Sweden
presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 03 - From breakthrough to burdens: mental health in modern cystic fibrosis care

sections.Objectives: In pediatric cystic fibrosis (CF) care, Swedish registry data show that mothers attend medical visits more frequently than fathers, with no indication of a narrowing gap. This study quantified gender differences in caregiver attendance and explored factors influencing parental participation.
sections.Methods: We performed a study combining survey and registry data from parents of children with CF with scheduled medical visits in 2023 at the pediatric CF centers in Gothenburg and Lund. Both mothers and fathers were invited. A Theory of Planned Behavior–based questionnaire assessed attitude, subjective norm, and perceived control related to attendance (1–10 scale). Attendance at inpatient, physical outpatient, and digital visits was analyzed using factor analysis and multilevel modelling.
sections.Results: Of 140 eligible parents, 123 completed the survey; 65 (53%) were mothers. In 2023, mothers attended in-person visits in 76% (95% CI 71 to 81%) of cases compared with 56% (95% CI 51 to 61%) for fathers (difference 20%-points; 95% CI 13 to 27; p<0.0001). For digital visits, attendance was 80% (95% CI 72 to 89%) for mothers and 51% (95% CI 41 to 61%) for fathers (difference 29 %-points; p=0.0002). A one-unit increase in attitude score was associated with 13% higher attendance among fathers (95% CI 7 to 20%; p<0.001) but not among mothers (−2%; 95% CI −12 to 9%; p=0.77). Higher subjective norm scores were associated with lower attendance among fathers (−7%; 95% CI −7 to −7%; p<0.001) and mothers (−8%; 95% CI −12 to −4%; p<0.001). Higher perceived control was associated with increased attendance among fathers (16%; 95% CI −1–32%; p=0.058) and mothers (8%; 95% CI 1–14%; p=0.019).
sections.Conclusion: Marked gender inequality persists in caregiver attendance for children with CF. Fathers’ attendance seems more strongly influenced by attitudinal and perceived control factors, indicating that targeted interventions addressing these factors may promote more equitable parental participation.

presentations.title: CFTR dysfunction and epithelial plasticity in patient-derived intestinal organoids
presentations.summary: Background: Emerging studies indicate that people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) have a substantially increased risk (~5-fold) of colorectal cancer (CRC) compared with the general population. presentations.authors_text: C.S. Rodrigues1, I. Pankonien1, M.S. Fragoso1, M.D. Amaral1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1BioISI—Biosystems & Integrative Sciences Institute, Faculty of Sciences, University of Lisboa, Lisboa, Portugal
presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 04 - Models and mechanisms in CFTR biology

sections.Background: Emerging studies indicate that people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) have a substantially increased risk (~5-fold) of colorectal cancer (CRC) compared with the general population. Additionally, loss of CFTR function has been shown to promote partial epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT), a cellular program that may contribute to cancer progression.
sections.Objectives: To elucidate how loss of CFTR impacts epithelial differentiation and promotes EMT and cancer-associated processes.
sections.Methods: Intestinal organoids (IOs) generated from healthy and CF donors were analysed for epithelial and mesenchymal markers and cell-type composition by immunofluorescence and Western blot. CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing is used to introduce p.Phe508del and p.Gly551Asp in homozygosity in non-CF IOs to generate two novel isogenic IO lines.
sections.Results: Both CF and non-CF IOs differentiated into specific cell types, such as, goblet and enteroendocrine cells. Interestingly, the expression of the epithelial marker CK-18 was found to be reduced in CF vs non-CF, whereas expression of the stemness marker LGR5 was increased in CF IOs. Gene edited IOs are currently under clonal selection and will be used to investigate the direct effect of these CFTR variants on intestinal epithelial differentiation and EMT.
sections.Conclusion: Results indicate that defective CFTR alters epithelial differentiation, reflected by reduced CK-18 expression in CF IOs. The novel isogenic IO lines provide a unique platform to study the direct role of CFTR on cancer-related processes. Acknowledgements: Work supported by UID/04046/2023 UID/04046/2023 centre grant (to BioISI UID/00100), from FCT/MCTES Portugal, and research grants: “HIT-CF” (H2020-SC1-2017-755021), “ORGESTRA” (EC HORIZON–MSCA-2023-DN-JD- 101120108), both from EU; SRC-2024-026 from CF Trust-UK and FFC#1/2025 from Fondazione Fibrosi Cistica Ricerca; CR recipient of PhD fellowship SFRH/BD/153053/2022 from FCT (Portugal).

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 05 - Fuel, frame and function in cystic fibrosis nutrition

sections.Background: People with cystic fibrosis (CF) often require supplementation of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E) due to malabsorption. CFTR modulators (CFTRm), particularly elaxacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI), may alter vitamin absorption and metabolism by correcting the underlying CFTR defect. Aim: To evaluate changes in vitamin D, A and E levels among children with CF treated with CFTRm.
sections.Methods: Single-centre prospective registry data were analysed. Vitamin concentrations were measured at the start of CFTRm therapy (Vitamin D only) and annually thereafter. Pancreas exocrine status was assed using faecal elastase levels.
sections.Results: Vitamin D levels were measured in 76 children with a median follow-up of 3 years (IQR: 2–3.9). Levels declined slightly during follow-up, from 30.4 ng/mL (IQR: 25.8–38.3) to 29.2 ng/mL (IQR: 22.5–35.5) (p=0.030), and the proportion of children with insufficient vitamin D levels increased from 9% to 18%. Participants who showed improvement in pancreatic exocrine function (n=9) had sufficient or elevated vitamin D levels at baseline (8 vs 1) and maintained normal levels during therapy. Vitamin A and E levels were measured in 42 children after 12–48 months of CFTR modulator therapy. The median vitamin E and A levels were 1.6 mmol/L (IQR: 1.1-1.9) and 18 mmol/L (IQR: 13-24.9), respectively. Most patients had levels within the normal range (vitamin E: 21 [50%], vitamin A: 22 [52%]). Both insufficient (vitamin E: 7 [17%], vitamin A: 6 [14%]) and elevated levels (vitamin E: 14 [33%], vitamin A: 14 [33%]) were observed, but no symptomatic hypervitaminosis occurred.
sections.Conclusion: Regular assessment of fat-soluble vitamin levels is necessary during CFTR modulator therapy to ensure adequate vitamin status while avoiding hypervitaminosis. Funding: National Research, Development and Innovation Fund (NKFIH 138929)

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 06 - Gastrointestinal and hepatic trajectories in cystic fibrosis from early life to adulthood

sections.Objectives: People with cystic fibrosis have an increased risk and earlier onset of colorectal cancer (CRC). Despite CF-specific screening guidelines published in 2018, real-world outcomes are poorly characterised. We aimed to describe colonoscopy completion, pathology detection, repeat colonoscopy and bowel preparation quality.
sections.Methods: A multi-centre retrospective cohort study was conducted across three CF centers (2014–2024). Outcomes included referral-to-procedure completion, pathology (adenoma(s) or ileocolonic malignancy) and bowel preparation quality. Yield was assessed at the patient level (first colonoscopy) and procedure level (all colonoscopies). Guideline yield was assessed on current criteria: aged ≥40 years or ≥30 years and post-solid organ transplant at procedure.
sections.Results: Among 284 referred, 242 (85.2%) completed at least one colonoscopy, 42 (14.8%) did not proceed. At first colonoscopy (n=242), median age 39.9 years [IQR 33.2-46.5], 124 (51.2%) male, 46 (19.0%) post-transplant, adenomas were detected in 30.6% (74/242), and colorectal malignancy in 1.7% (4/242). Across all 353 colonoscopies, pathology was detected in 32.9% (116/353), 5 malignancies (4 colorectal, one terminal ileal). Second colonoscopy occurred in 34.7% (84/242), and ≥3 in 11.2% (27/242), for symptoms, routine re-screening, or inadequate preparation, with sustained yield; 35.7% (30/84) second; 34.8% (8/23), third. 125 procedures occurred outside guidelines (ages 25-39 years), with pathology identified in 25 (20%), including one malignancy. Bowel preparation was adequate, good/excellent in 71.4% (252/353) while 13.6% (48/353) were poor or unsatisfactory.
sections.Conclusion: In real-world practice, colonoscopy demonstrated high adenoma burden, clinically important malignancy rate and persistent pathology detection. Suboptimal referral-to-procedure completion rate was observed. Findings support robust surveillance and consideration of earlier CF-specific screening thresholds.

presentations.title: Resistance to colistin in Pseudomonas aeruginosa biofilms is modulated by the arn operon and pH
presentations.summary: Colistin, a last-resort antibiotic against multidrug resistant Gram-negative bacteria (doi:10.1189/fcimb.2022.851784), targets the bacterial outer membrane (OM) by electrostatic interactions. Bacteria may counteract this mechanism by OM remodeling which in P. aeruginosa is mainly due to lipid A aminoarabinosylation mediated by the arn operon. Based on previous observations showing that P. aeruginosa biofilms are tolerant to colistin (doi: 10.1371/journal.ppat.1000213), we asked whether the arn operon is involved in this phenomenon. presentations.affiliations_text: 1Charles University - 2nd Faculty of Medicine and University Hospital Motol and Homolka, Department of Biology and Medical Genetics, Prague, Czech Republic, 2Charles University - 2nd Faculty of Medicine and University Hospital Motol and Homolka, Department of Paediatrics, Prague, Czech Republic
presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 02 - Rare genotypes & challenging phenotypes

sections.Objectives: Complex CFTR alleles, defined as two or more variants in cis (i.e., on the same CF allele), complicate diagnostic evaluation and genotype–phenotype correlations and may negatively affect the response to CFTR modulator therapy. In Czech pwCF, several complex alleles have been identified, with [L467F;F508del] most frequent, followed by [E292X;R709Q]. Novel complex alleles harbouring two CF-causing variants, including E831X, have recently been detected. This study aimed to describe these clinical cases.
sections.Methods: Data were obtained from the Prague CF Newborn Screening Centre and the Prague CF Centre.
sections.Results: We identified two cases in which the E831X variant occurred within a complex allele harbouring two additional CF-causing variants. The first patient had a positive newborn screening (NBS) and was heterozygous for two CF-causing variants, E831X and R851X. Parental DNA analysis showed that both variants were inherited from the father, whereas no CFTR variants were detected in the mother, indicating that E831X and R851X are in cis. The infant had a negative sweat chloride test. The second case involved an eight-year-old boy with a clinical diagnosis of CF and an initial finding of a single CF-causing variant, N1303K. Subsequent massively parallel sequencing (Devyser CFTR NGS assay; Sweden) identified two additional CF-causing variants: 711+5G>A and E831X. The N1303K variant was detected in the father, while maternal DNA was unavailable. The patient is thus likely a compound heterozygote with the genotype N1303K/[711+5G>A;E831X].
sections.Conclusion: Finding of two CF-causing variants within a complex allele, either during NBS or through postnatal genetic analysis of clinical CF cases, is very rare. Despite their detection by NBS, parental segregation analysis and sweat testing remain essential to establish the final screening/diagnostic outcome.

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 03 - From breakthrough to burdens: mental health in modern cystic fibrosis care

sections.Objectives: This study aimed to evaluate 1) the impact of ETI on psychological wellbeing and quality of life and 2) how post-ETI changes in health and social circumstances influence psychological wellbeing in people with CF (pwCF).
sections.Methods: Semi-structured one-to-one interviews were conducted face-to-face or via Microsoft Teams. Four questions explored physical and mental health, quality of life, contact with the CF service, family life and relationships, education/work, social activities, and the impact of COVID-19. Transcripts were analysed using Grounded Theory.
sections.Results: Twenty-one pwCF on ETI were interviewed between March 2023 and September 2024; 8 males, mean age 36.5 (SD 8.25) years, mean interview length 32 minutes. Four themes emerged: 1) positive effect on mental health post-ETI dose adjustment, 2) adjusting to changes in health, 3) conflicted emotions, 4) impact on relationships, education, career and finances. Dose adjustment of ETI, which occurred in 8 pwCF, improved negative mental health side effects. The CF team were praised for listening to patients and prioritising both physical and mental health. PwCF express gratitude and relief as they adapt to a new normal marked by stable health and accomplishing tasks once thought impossible. Managing ETI health expectations has been vital, and many had forgotten how much treatment previously dominated their lives. Reduced hospital contact led some to grieve the loss of their CF “extended family”. Whilst ETI has fostered hope for the future, it has also caused survivor guilt, uncertainty regarding long-term prognosis, as well as concerns about career trajectories and financial security, leaving some pwCF feeling “stuck”.
sections.Conclusion: While ETI has had a positive impact on psychological wellbeing, it also associated with survivor guilt, uncertainty regarding long-term prognosis, career trajectories, and financial security. Sustained, targeted support is essential to optimise both physical and mental health.

presentations.title: Weight is not the win: bioimpedance reveals what BMI misses after ETI in adults with cystic fibrosis
presentations.summary: Background and aims: CFTR modulation with elexacaftor–tezacaftor–ivacaftor (ETI) has transformed clinical trajectories in cystic fibrosis (CF). Yet, in the post‑modulator era, weight and BMI may no longer be the best metrics. We asked a clinically critical question: what tissue drives ETI‑associated weight gain, and where does it accumulate, when assessed with bioelectrical impedance analysis (BIA) in routine care? Methods: In a single‑centre observational cohort, 51 adults with CF underwent BIA before and after ETI initiation. BIA quantified whole‑body fat and lean mass, trunk composition, and phase angle at 50 Hz; anthropometry and spirometry were recorded at matched/nearest timepoints. Results: ETI increase...
presentations.authors_text: K. Ballinger1, L Culliford2, C. Pearson1, Z. Saynor2, L. Marino3, T. Daniels4
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University Hospitals Southampton NHS Foundation Trust, Wessex Cystic Fibrosis Department, Southampton, United Kingdom, 2University Hospital Southampton, Southampton Academy of Research, Southampton, United Kingdom, 3South West Yorkshire Partnership NHS Foundation Trust, Research & Development, Wakefield, West Yorkshire, United Kingdom, 4University Hospital Southampton NHS Foundation Trust, Wessex Cystic Fibrosis Department, Southampton, United Kingdom
presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 05 - Fuel, frame and function in cystic fibrosis nutrition

sections.Methods: In a single‑centre observational cohort, 51 adults with CF underwent BIA before and after ETI initiation. BIA quantified whole‑body fat and lean mass, trunk composition, and phase angle at 50 Hz; anthropometry and spirometry were recorded at matched/nearest timepoints.
sections.Results: ETI increased BMI by +2.3 ± 2.0 kg/m² and weight by +6.6 ± 5.8 kg. At a median follow‑up time of 8 months absolute gains were similar, fat mass (+3.5 ± 3.7 kg) and lean mass (+3.2 ± 2.5 kg), indicating mixed tissue accretion consistent with nutritional restoration. However, fat contributed a larger proportion of total weight change, elevating fat mass percentage and fat mass index. Distribution mattered: adipose gain followed a predominantly central pattern, with trunk fat mass rising by +2.1 ± 2.2 kg. The proportion classified as overweight, obese, or normal‑weight obesity increased from 37% to 57% (p=0.04). Phase angle at 50 Hz did not change significantly.
sections.Conclusion: In adults with CF, ETI‑associated weight gain reflects both muscle and fat accrual while BIA reveals a shift in composition and distribution that BMI alone cannot show. The clinical message is simple and actionable: measure beyond weight. Integrating body composition into CF care enables a transition from weight‑centric goals to composition‑informed, heart‑healthy, and function‑focused nutrition as this population ages.

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a risk factor for colorectal cancer (CRC). Since 2018, colonoscopy screening has been recommended for people with CF (pwCF) from age 40, and from age 30 for organ transplanted (TX). This retrospective registry study evaluated CRC screening coverage, procedure-related risks, and pathology frequency in the screening-eligible Scandinavian CF population.
sections.Methods: We analyzed data from the CF registries in Denmark, Norway and Sweden for the period 2019-2024. Colonoscopies performed due to symptoms were included, as these replaced screening. Coverage, complications, and pathology frequencies were reported as absolute numbers and proportions.
sections.Results: Among 493 eligible pwCF (31% TX), 284 underwent colonoscopy, resulting in 58% coverage (55% in TX). A total of 311 colonoscopies were performed, with 61 (20%) due to symptoms. Macroscopic findings were registered for 261 procedures (84%): 126 normal (48%), 106 polyps (41%), 24 other (9%) and 5 cancer (2%). Adenoma was the most frequent polyp type (n=64, 60%), followed by hyperplastic polyps (n=20, 19%). Polyps were removed in 106 procedures (34%); histology results were available for 88 (83%), describing no dysplasia in 17 (19%), low-grade dysplasia in 68 (77%) and high-grade dysplasia in 3 (3%). CRC was found in 5 pwCF (1.8%), of these one TX, all among individuals having colonoscopy by clinical indication. Complications were recorded for 5 procedures (1.6%), with bleeding in 2 (0.6%).
sections.Conclusion: CRC screening for pwCF was rapidly adopted in Scandinavia, achieving 58% coverage in six years. Polyps, mainly adenomas, were common findings. Colorectal cancer was found in 5 pwCF (1.8%), all in symptomatic individuals. Procedure-related complications were infrequent.

presentations.title: Investigation of CFTR function and epithelial barrier properties at single cell resolution using multi-electrode array technology
presentations.summary: Objectives: Current assays lack the high spatial resolution and/or throughput to study differences in CFTR rescue between cells. We set out to study the effect of CFTR treatments in individual cells using imec’s high density multi-electrode array chip. Methods: CFTR ion channel function and local barrier integrity were studied using electrical impedance recordings on chips with >16,000 subcellularly sized (8 µm) electrodes. We used HEK293T cells, intestinal colon cancer CaCo2 cells, and 16HBE to represent increasing model complexity. Impedance was recorded at 1 kHZ before and after 20min incubation with CFTR activator forskolin (10 µM) and/or CFTRinh-172 (50 µM). CFTR function was determined as the %change. Ba...
presentations.authors_text: M.M. Ensinck1, A. Venz2, A.S. Ramalho3, G. Amatngalim4, S. Ebrahimi Takalloo2, F. Vermeulen5, D. Braeken2, M.S. Carlon1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1KU Leuven, Department of Chronic Diseases and Metabolism, Leuven, Belgium, 2imec, Leuven, Belgium, 3KU Leuven, Department of Development and Regeneration, Leuven, Belgium, 4UMC Utrecht, Department of Cellular Disease Models and Regenerative Medicine, Utrecht, Netherlands, 5UZ Leuven, Department of Pediatrics, Leuven, Belgium
presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 04 - Models and mechanisms in CFTR biology

sections.Objectives: Current assays lack the high spatial resolution and/or throughput to study differences in CFTR rescue between cells. We set out to study the effect of CFTR treatments in individual cells using imec’s high density multi-electrode array chip.
sections.Methods: CFTR ion channel function and local barrier integrity were studied using electrical impedance recordings on chips with >16,000 subcellularly sized (8 µm) electrodes. We used HEK293T cells, intestinal colon cancer CaCo2 cells, and 16HBE to represent increasing model complexity. Impedance was recorded at 1 kHZ before and after 20min incubation with CFTR activator forskolin (10 µM) and/or CFTRinh-172 (50 µM). CFTR function was determined as the %change. Barrier integrity was determined using impedance, immunocytochemistry for tight junction protein ZO-1 and permeability with FITC-dextran (4/20 kDa).
sections.Results: Restoration of F508del-CFTR function upon elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor treatment (p=0.0087) was shown in HEK293T. We observed a CFTR-specific impedance drop of ~20% upon CFTR stimulation by forskolin in CaCo2 cells (vs ~3% in controls; p=0.0003), inhibited by co-incubation with Inh-172 (~1% drop; p=0.0047; vs mock: p=0.96), while tight junctions were unaffected. In first 16HBE experiments, CFTR activation and inhibition resulted in ~4% decrease and ~8,5% increase in impedance, respectively. In 16HBEgeG542X, both increased impedance by ~1%, showing again specificity for CFTR. We are optimizing growth of primary rectal organoid-derived monolayers and submerged differentiated airway epithelia on the chips. For the latter we could follow-up growth and differentiation through impedance for >50 days. Future perspectives: Electrical impedance recordings allow to study simultaneously barrier integrity and CFTR channel function at single cell resolution. We are optimizing the assay to primary cell models and envision this novel model will contribute to unravelling the full effects of CFTR-targeted therapies.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Imperial College London, National Heart and Lung Institute, London, United Kingdom, 2Institut Für Biologie, Freie Universität Berlin, Berlin, Germany, 3Aarhus University, Department of Biomedicine, Aarhus, Denmark, 4Royal Brompton Hospital, Department of Paediatric Respiratory Medicine, London, United Kingdom
presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 01 -Targeting pseudomonas in cystic fibrosis: from mechanisms to new therapies

sections.Objectives: Chronic infection with Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) in the lungs of people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) is challenging to eradicate. Development of new drugs faces many barriers, so repurposing existing drugs is increasingly appealing. Our previous work showed the ability of glatiramer acetate (GA), an approved drug for use in multiple sclerosis, to synergise with tobramycin, an aminoglycoside antibiotic, against clinical Pa from pwCF. This effect is likely due to antimicrobial peptide-like bacterial membrane damage. Here, we test the effect of GA addition to multiple classes of antibiotics to evaluate possible potentiation.
sections.Methods: Thirty clinical Pa isolates from pwCF at the Royal Brompton Hospital were selected from the UK CF Infection Biorepository. Isolates represented various antimicrobial sensitivities and differing mucoid status. Checkerboard assays assessed the effect of two concentrations of GA on the growth inhibitory impact of clinically relevant antibiotics.
sections.Results: Addition of 200 µg/ml GA significantly reduced the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC50) of aminoglycosides amikacin (p<0.001, n=11) and gentamicin (p=0.001, n=12) for 100% of isolates tested, of which 64% and 60% were sensitive to gentamicin and amikacin respectively. Preliminary data indicated a strain-specific variability of GA's potentiation with the antibiotics aztreonam, ciprofloxacin, piperacillin/tazobactam, ceftazidime and colistin (n=10). GA does not potentiate meropenem (n=10). Further isolates are being screened and data will be available for the conference.
sections.Conclusion: GA shows an extended ability to potentiate other aminoglycosides against antibiotic sensitive and resistant Pa. The effect of GA on non-aminoglycoside agents varies, which may aid exploration of the incompletely understood mechanism of action of GA. These results enhances evidence of the promising effect of GA with antimicrobial treatments in pwCF. Funders: LifeArc, CF Trust

presentations.title: Clinical outcomes of former CFSPID adolescents and young adults with Cystic Fibrosis (CF) or CFTR-Related Disorder
presentations.summary: Objectives: Although the knowledge about the outcomes of CFSPID has expanded, a gap remains in understanding evolution in adolescence or adulthood. We retrospectively evaluated clinical data and management of former CFSPID adolescents and young adults after a diagnosis of CF or CFTR-RD. We also evaluated the clinical status of CFSPID adolescents. sections.Objectives: Although the knowledge about the outcomes of CFSPID has expanded, a gap remains in understanding evolution in adolescence or adulthood. We retrospectively evaluated clinical data and management of former CFSPID adolescents and young adults after a diagnosis of CF or CFTR-RD. We also evaluated the clinical status of CFSPID adolescents.
sections.Methods: This is a retrospective, observational, descriptive and multicenter study including Italian subjects born before 2011, defined as CFSPID at birth. They were followed by 6 CF Regional Italian Centers and in follow up at 30 June 2025 (study end), either retaining the CFSPID denomination or progressing to a CF or CFTR-RD diagnosis. Data were collected at first and last evaluation.
sections.Results: Among the 75 subjects included (median age at study end: 19.6 years; IQR: 16.3-23.8), 30 received a diagnosis of CF at a median age of 8.6 years (IQR: 1.8–13.2), 29 (96.7%) for pathological sweat chloride (SC) values and 1 (3.3%) for multi-organ symptoms and pancreatic insufficiency. 13 were labelled as CFTR-RD, in presence of normal or intermediate SC and mono-organ involvement: bronchiectasis in 6(46.2%), nasal polyposis in 4 (30.8%), sinusitis and recurrent pancreatitis in 1(7.7 %). CF and CFTR-RD subjects had normal BMI and lung function (ppFEV1>90% in 86.2% and 92.3%, respectively), with rare complications (sinusitis: 16.1%, male infertility in 7 out of 21 males tested, dehydration: 6.4%, non-cirrhogenic liver disease: 3.2%) in the CF group. 31 subjects (median age: 20.9; IQR: 16.4-24.2) were asymptomatic still with an inconclusive diagnosis, although abnormal LCI was observed in 26.1%. 25 (80.6%) were treated with PEP mask and 20 (60.5%) received salt replacement. Among 9 eligible subjects with CF and at least one F508del, 1 received ETI therapy.
sections.Conclusion: CF or CFTR-RD adolescents and adults former CFSPID grow up healthy and are almost always asymptomatic. Unnecessary therapies are still prescribed to CFSPID subjects.

sections.Objectives: Transition from paediatric to adult care is an important step for young people with cystic fibrosis (CF) and their families. We worked with a national charity to explore our transition process and generate change ideas to enhance it.
sections.Methods: A dedicated group was formed with key internal stakeholders and a representative from the charity Cystic Fibrosis Trust. The group carried out scoping work to better understand the current transition process, including following a patient journey through transition clinic (Gemba method) and a root cause analysis to identify barriers to successful transition. To complement insights from scoping and ensure young people’s and families’ views guided the work, Cystic Fibrosis Trust facilitated focus groups to gather unbiased feedback on transition experiences and ideas for improvements which were analysed thematically. A prioritisation matrix brought ideas together to identify priority actions.
sections.Results: Focus groups showed young people and parents wanted clear communication and information before and throughout the process. They emphasised the importance of individualised approaches to transition. Additionally, joined-up care and access to resources and peer support were highlighted. From additional scoping work, the team identified factors perceived to impact the transition process and generated ideas to tackle these. To address patients’ priorities of communication and individualisation, two immediate actions were identified and delivered: Update the service’s information booklet, ensuring this can be personalised Implement an online information evening for ‘Transition Class of 2025’
sections.Conclusion: Improvement projects are often run by busy clinicians with limited time to consult service users. Working with an external charity enabled us to gather and analyse additional feedback from those receiving care. The resulting activities have expanded our transition offer, with evaluation ongoing and further projects planned.

sections.Objectives: Body composition is a key indicator of nutritional status in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) is considered the reference standard for assessing body composition, while bioelectrical impedance analysis (BIA) is commonly used in routine clinical practice. Discrepancies in body fat percentage (BF%) estimates between BIA and DXA have been reported in healthy populations. However, data in children with CF, particularly those receiving cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator modulators (CFTRm), are limited. This study aimed to assess the agreement and relationship between BF% estimates obtained using BIA and DXA in children and adolescents with CF.
sections.Methods: Participants underwent two body composition assessments under standardised conditions on the same day, both conducted as part of routine care or if medically indicated. BIA measurements were performed using the Bodystat 1500MDD™ analyser, and DXA scans were acquired and analysed using the Hologic Horizon A™ bone densitometer. Agreement between BF% estimates obtained by the two methods was assessed using Bland–Altman analysis.
sections.Results: Twenty-four participants (15 males, mean age 13.2±2.8 years; mean BMI 17.6±2.0 kg/m2) were included. Mean BF% measured by BIA and DXA were 16.7±5.8 % and 25.6±5.1 % respectively. BIA underestimated BF% compared with DXA by a mean of 8.9%, with wide 95% limits of agreement (-17.2 % to - 0.6 %). Twenty-two patients showed at least moderate increases in extracellular water, which may have played a role in BIA estimates.
sections.Conclusion: BIA showed poor agreement with DXA for the assessment of BF% in children and adolescents with CF. These findings suggest that BIA may be of limited reliability for body composition assessment in this population.

sections.Objectives: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT), and the GGT–to-platelet ratio (GPR) are commonly used to assess advanced cystic fibrosis-related liver disease (aCFLD). However, ALP levels also reflect bone turnover during linear growth. This study evaluated ALP in relation to GGT, GPR, and linear growth in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) following elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) initiation to disentangle the contributors to ALP levels.
sections.Methods: Using data from the Dutch Cystic Fibrosis Foundation Registry from 2018 to 2023, we assessed ALP, GGT, GPR, linear growth, and aCFLD classification in pwCF from ETI initiation up to 12 months thereafter. To account for linear growth, pwCF aged 12-17 years (adolescents) were analyzed separately from pwCF≥18 years (adults). In total, 202 adolescents and 867 adults were included.
sections.Results: aCFLD was present in 6% (n=13) of the adolescents and 8% (n=71) of the adults. In adolescents, ALP levels at 6 and 12 months correlated strongly with linear growth since ETI initiation (ρ=0.67 and ρ=0.77; both p<0.001), but not with GGT or GPR. In adults, there was no linear growth, but ALP correlated significantly with GGT and GPR at both 6 (ρ=0.33 and ρ=0.35; both p<0.001) and 12 months (ρ=0.34 and ρ=0.38; both p<0.001). In pwCF with aCFLD, these patterns were consistent: ALP correlated with linear growth in adolescents at 6 months (ρ=0.65, p=0.04), while in adults ALP correlated with GGT and GPR at both 6 (ρ=0.74 and ρ=0.65; both p<0.001) and 12 months (ρ=0.69 and ρ=0.50; both p<0.001).
sections.Conclusion: In adolescents, ALP reflects linear growth rather than aCFLD. In contrast, in adults ALP correlates with established aCFLD markers. These findings indicate that elevated ALP levels in adolescents should be interpreted in the context of linear growth, whereas in adults ALP remains a clinically relevant marker for aCFLD.

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 04 - Models and mechanisms in CFTR biology

sections.Objectives: CFTR expression is heterogeneous among the specialised cell types composing the respiratory epithelium. Its transcriptional regulation is tissue-specific and involves the interaction of regulatory elements within the CFTR locus, that is organized into a topologically associating domain (TAD). We setup a patient-specific cellular system of nasal epithelial stem cells (CF-CRC-AESC) inducible to respiratory differentiation. To understand mechanisms controlling CFTR expression, the study aimed to assess mRNA levels and the TAD DNA methylation pattern. A therapeutic epigenetic strategy was also evaluated in vitro by a DNA hypomethylating approach.
sections.Methods: The analyses were conducted in nasal brushing samples and in patient-specific cellular models derived from wild-type and F508del/F508del individuals. The hypomethylating agent 3-deazaadenosine (3-DZA) was tested in differentiated CF-CRC-AESC. mRNA levels were evaluated by digital droplet PCR, while CG, CHG and CHH DNA methylation analysis was performed by bisulfite transformation and next-generation sequencing in 7 regulatory regions of CFTR TAD, located from -80.1kb to +48.9kb.
sections.Results: All the analysed CHG and CHH sites of CFTR TAD resulted to be demethylated regardless of mRNA levels. Rather, some CG sites of the -80.1kb, -35kb, -20.9kb, 5’-flanking and intron 26 CFTR gene regions revealed a DNA methylation pattern correlated to its expression. These CG sites showed a reduce methylation after 3-DZA treatment, correlating with an increase of CFTR expression.
sections.Conclusion: The presence of methylated CG sites in the CFTR TAD, modulated accordingly to CFTR expression and susceptible to drug-induced hypomethylation, suggests the possibility of a transcriptional epigenetic control. Indeed, 3-DZA treatment was able to induce simultaneously an hypomethylating effect on CG sites and an enhancement of CFTR mRNA levels. These results could provide new insights for developing epigenetic therapeutic strategies in CF.

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 01 -Targeting pseudomonas in cystic fibrosis: from mechanisms to new therapies

sections.Objectives: Chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa infections in people with cystic fibrosis feature persistent inflammation and oxidative stress, yet the mechanisms enabling bacterial persistence remain unclear. We investigated whether mutations in the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDHc) drive pyruvate secretion that promotes persistence, and whether analogous mutations occur in other airway pathogens.
sections.Methods: We analyzed clinical isolates by comparative genomics to identify PDHc mutations, quantified extracellular pyruvate, and used airway epithelial cell at the air-liquid interface and macrophage infection models to assess host responses. We tested oxidative‑stress scavenging (H₂O₂), immune‑activation readouts, and phagocytosis survival to determine functional consequences of pyruvate secretion.
sections.Results: Clinical P. aeruginosa isolates harbored PDHc mutations that increased pyruvate secretion, establishing a more tolerogenic infection microenvironment and favoring bacterial persistence. Pyruvate scavenged reactive oxygen species (H₂O₂), dampened immune activation in epithelial cells and macrophages, and enhanced bacterial survival during phagocytosis. Analogous mutations were also identified in Staphylococcus aureus, Haemophilus influenzae, and Stenotrophomonas maltophilia.
sections.Conclusion: Pyruvate functions as a bacterial immunometabolite that mimics host antioxidant defenses, reshaping the infection niche to enable long‑term colonization, consistent with the concept of disease tolerance. These findings highlight secreted metabolites as key mediators of host–pathogen interactions and suggest therapeutic strategies targeting metabolic pathways to manage chronic infections.

sections.Objectives: Since 2023, we have run an adult diagnostic clinic to improve detection of Cystic Fibrosis (CF), CFTR‑related disease (CFTR‑RD), and Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia (PCD). Despite universal newborn screening (NBS) in Wales since 2007, we continue to see late diagnoses and CFTR‑RD remains under-recognised; CFTR modulators may be a treatment option in future, and we are aiming to improve our detection and follow-up of these.
sections.Methods: We reviewed 2 years of demographic, clinical and lab data from our diagnostic service. Alongside clinical assessment, we can perform on-the-day lab testing, lung function, FeNO (nasal and lower airway), radiography, sweat testing and CFTR genotyping (CF50 gene panel with option of send-out extended CFTR genotyping where indicated).
sections.Results: 45 individuals have attended, (most referred from respiratory clinics) with bronchiectasis the commonest feature. DemographicsClinic populationTotal45 (100%)Male19 (42%)Mean age (years) (n)(range)41.4 (18-90)FEV1 (% predicted)81%Pseudomonas Aeruginosa carriage7 (16%)Bronchiectasis33 (73%)Sino-nasal symptoms30 (67%)Chronic Gastrointestinal symptoms14 (31%) Of 35 completed sweat tests: 22/35 (63%) were <30mmol/L 11/35 (31%) were 30-60mmol/L 2/35 (6%) were >60mmol/L CF50 testing (n=41) identified CFTR mutations in 11; extended genotyping (n=26) detected no additional variants. Diagnoses included: 1 adult CF 7 CFTR‑RD 3 had abnormal CFTR function (2 x sweat chloride >30mmol/L) but no identifiable CFTR mutations. Onward referrals: 4 for additional CFTR function testing 3 for PCD testing Fourteen remain under our care with the remainder discharged or referred to other services.
sections.Conclusion: Adult assessment for CF and CFTR-RD remains necessary despite NBS. Our model aids with the detection of disease and reduces demand on national services for second‑line testing. High prevalences of CFTR mutations and abnormal CFTR function tests in symptomatic adults aligns with prior observations suggesting wider significance in respiratory disease. Strengthening links with the NBS team is a future goal to identify referrals with a label of Cystic Fibrosis Screen Positive, Inconclusive Diagnosis (CFSPID) as an added marker of CFTR dysfunction.

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 03 - From breakthrough to burdens: mental health in modern cystic fibrosis care

sections.Background: Highly effective CFTR modulators, such as the triple therapy ivacaftor/tezacaftor/elexacaftor (ETI), have transformed clinical outcomes in people with CF(pwCF) carrying responding mutations. While improvements in lung function are well documented, there is still a dearth of data regarding the impact on the socio-professional and psychosocial situation.
sections.Objectives: To assess the impact of ETI on family, professional and social status, leisure activities, quality of life, care satisfaction and confidence in the future in adult pwCF.
sections.Methods: This monocentric observational study included all CF patients aged over 23 years at the time of ETI initiation at the Roscoff CF center. Primary outcomes included changes in family, professional, social situations, and engagement in leisure activities. Secondary outcomes included CF-specific quality of life (CFQR-14+), satisfaction with care (0–10 visual analog scale), confidence in the future (0–5 emoji scale), and participation in daily life activities.
sections.Results: Of the 43 eligible patients, 40 participated in the study. Median age was 38.5 years [32–45], and the median duration of ETI treatment at the time of questionnaire completion was 41 months [38–44]. Changes in personal circumstances were frequently reported: 47.5% (n=19) of patients experienced a change in family, 25% (n=10) in professional, 32.5% (n=13) in social situation, and 40% (n=16) in leisure activities. Quality of life assessed using the CFQ-R 14+ showed highly significant improvements. Support during initiation of ETI within the CF center was rated highly, with median scores of 9 [8–10] for physical follow-up, 8 [7–10] for psychological support, and 9 [8–10] for social support. The mean confidence-in-the-future score was 4.3 ± 0.6.
sections.Conclusion: This study provide novel data on the social and quality-of-life since ETI treatment in pwCF, contributing to a better understanding of their evolving needs in the context of a major shift in disease prognosis.

sections.Objectives: Obesity is a complex, chronic issue and prevalence of obesity in people with CF (pwCF) is rising. Recent interventions include glucagon-like peptide 1 agonist receptors (GLP1’s). GLP1’s aid weight loss and improve glycaemic control. Evidence in the general population shows weight loss is predominantly fat mass, however, body composition change in pwCF has not been studied. Our aim was to explore the effect of GLP1’s on body composition in adult pwCF.
sections.Methods: Adult pwCF commencing a GLP1 and undergoing body composition assessment before and after starting a GLP1 were identified. Data recorded included weight and BMI, fat mass index (FMI) and fat free mass index (FFMI), using a multi-frequency BIA device. sections.Results: 4 pwCF (1 female, 2 pancreatic sufficient (PS), 2 insufficient (PI)) underwent body composition assessment pre and post starting a GLP1 (all tirzepatide, 2 to treat diabetes 2 to treat obesity, 2 NHS funded, 2 privately funded) between February 2024 and May 2025. sections.Objectives: The multicentric COMET-CF-IBD (Comprehensive Omic and SyMptom Evaluation, a Translational study) in Cystic Fibrosis (CF) and Inflammatory Bowel Diseases (IBD) aims to improve understanding of chronic gastrointestinal (GI) inflammation and its clinical impact. We analysed metabolomic profiles in serum from people with CF (pwCF) and compared them with those from people with IBD (pwIBD) and from a healthy control (HC) group. Both diseases are characterised by chronic inflammation and an enhanced burden of abdominal symptoms. Subsequently, we conducted a pilot study in sub-cohorts, analysing faecal fatty acid profiles. The aim was to provide novel insights into shared and disease-specific mechanisms of chronic GI inflammation.
sections.Methods: Metabolomic profiling of serum samples was performed using nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy and the lifespin profiling software. Faecal fatty acid profiles were assessed using liquid gas chromatography flame-ionization detection.
sections.Results: A total of 155 serum samples from 50 pwCF, 51 pwIBD and 54 HC were analysed. Compared to HC, both pwIBD and pwCF showed several metabolic alterations, including elevated levels of inflammation-associated metabolites, such as glycoprotein acetyls B and catabolic metabolites, such as alanine. However, significant metabolic differences were observed between pwCF and pwIBD. Faecal fatty acid profiles were assessed from 51 donors, including 14 pwCF, 20 pwIBD, and 17 HC. Both, pwCF and pwIBD shared a pattern of increased long-chain fatty acids compared with HC, predominantly affecting omega-6 and omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid species.
sections.Conclusion: Compared to HC, pwCF and pwIBD exhibited significant metabolic alterations including enhanced inflammation. Detailed analysis of coherences and differences in both specific pathologies may pave the way for further understanding of chronic inflammation, which in both diseases, is known to enhance risks for sequelae like colorectal carcinoma.

sections.Objectives: Mutations in gyrA and gyrB, encoding the two subunits of a type II topoisomerase, are frequently detected in clinical isolates of Pseudomonas aeruginosa from people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). This study aims to explore how mutations in these genes contribute to bacterial adaptation during chronic lung infection.
sections.Methods: We generated a panel of single gyrA and gyrB mutants using CRISPR-Cas9, representing pathoadaptive mutations commonly identified in clinical isolates. The ciprofloxacin (CIP) minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) was determined in Mueller–Hinton medium using the broth microdilution method. The impact of these mutations during infection was investigated using air–liquid interface (ALI) models that closely mimic the cystic fibrosis (CF) airway environment, both in the absence and presence of antibiotic pressure.
sections.Results: Mutations in gyrA and gyrB are commonly associated with CIP resistance. Interestingly, we observed that not all mutations identified in isolates from pwCF lead to an increased CIP MIC. This suggests that some of these mutations may play additional roles beyond antibiotic resistance, potentially contributing to host adaptation, affecting bacterial fitness, or acting as compensatory changes.
sections.Conclusion: Overall, this work improves our understanding of the role of gyrA and gyrB mutations in the adaptation of P. aeruginosa during CF airway infection. These insights may have clinical relevance by helping to predict bacterial evolutionary trajectories and optimise treatment strategies, ultimately supporting more personalised therapeutic approaches.

sections.Objectives: Lung disease progression is well studied in cystic fibrosis, but there is few data in CFTR-RD. We investigated longitudinal changes in ppFEV1 & incidence of abnormal chest imaging in patients in our CFTR-RD clinic.
sections.Methods: Single-centre retrospective review of patients diagnosed with CFTR-RD from 2017-2023, followed up to 2025. Baseline data at diagnosis: age, sex, sweat chloride concentration(SCC), BMI, chronic airway colonisation(AC). Longitudinal data(≥3 serial ppFEV1 measurements, ≥2 chest CTs, minimum 12 months apart) were obtained where available. A linear mixed‑effects model with random intercepts & slopes for each patient examined ppFEV1 change over the 9‑year period, adjusting for age, gender, SCC, BMI, AC(data analysis in Jamovi 2.6.44).
sections.Results: Of 44 patients included (79%male; median age[range] 40.5[19-62]yrs; SCC 37.5[12.5-89]mmol/l; BMI 24.4[18.9-34.7]kg/m2; ppFEV1 98[44-126]%), the main clinical phenotype was bacterial AC(n=22, 50%), 4(9%) had bronchiectasis(BX). Serial ppFEV1 & CT data were available for 28 and 17 patients respectively. The model estimated a not-significant annual ppFEV1 change of -0.8%(SE 0.5; 95%CI -1.8, 0.2; p=0.15) but a statistically significant lower ppFEV1 in patients with chronic AC (-21.6%; SE 2.5; 95%CI -26.4, -16.6; p<0.001). A subgroup analysis did not find annual ppFEV1 decline in this group(-0.5%; SE 0.9; 95%CI -2.2, 1.3; p=0.65). Among patients with serial CTs, 1(6%) progressed from bronchial wall thickening(BWT) to BX, 2(12%) showed resolution of prior BWT, 1(6%) developed new BWT, and 13(76 %) had no abnormalities throughout.
sections.Conclusion: Over 9 years, ppFEV1 remained stable and only one new case of BX developed, suggesting stable pulmonary disease. AC was associated with lower ppFEV1, but no annual decline. Registry capture, more sensitive functional & imaging modalities (lung clearance index, magnetic resonance imaging) will be required to detect subtle disease progression to determine long-term prognosis.

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 03 - From breakthrough to burdens: mental health in modern cystic fibrosis care

sections.Objectives: To examine changes in illness perceptions and illness identity from pre-ETI baseline to 3-year follow-up and assess associations with mental wellbeing (psychosocial health, anxiety and depression), CF-related quality of life (QoL), lung function, and intravenous (IV) antibiotic use.
sections.Methods: This observational cohort followed pwCF aged ≥12 years up to 3 years after ETI initiation. Questionnaires assessed IP, II, mental wellbeing, and QoL. Lung function and IV antibiotic courses were extracted from medical records. Longitudinal changes were analysed using covariance pattern models, and associations using Pearson and Spearman correlations. IP and II scores were compared by prior IV antibiotic use using the Mann–Whitney U test.
sections.Results: Of 177 participants at baseline, 117 (66.1%) completed 3-year assessments. IP scores decreased from 4.67 (95% CI 4.48–4.87) to 3.94 (3.72–4.17; p<0.001). II rejection decreased from 2.55 (2.42–2.69) to 2.27 (2.14–2.41), and II engulfment from 2.22 (2.08–2.35) to 1.92 (1.79–2.04; both p<0.001). II acceptance (p=0.192) and enrichment (p=0.167) did not change. Baseline IP correlated strongly with II engulfment (R=0.80; ρ=0.81; p<0.001) and other mental health indicators (R=0.47–0.74; ρ=0.52–0.76; all p<0.001), while associations with ppFEV₁ were weaker (R=–0.40; ρ=–0.42; p<0.001). Correlations remained consistent at 3 years. No baseline differences were observed by IV antibiotic use.
sections.Conclusion: IP and II show sustained improvement up to 3 years after ETI and are strongly associated with long-term mental wellbeing. Addressing illness adaptation in clinical care may support mental health alongside physical improvement.

sections.Background: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has markedly improved clinical outcomes in cystic fibrosis (CF), yet its effects on gastrointestinal (GI) symptoms, nutrition, and pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) in children remain incompletely characterised. This study aimed to evaluate GI-related changes during the first year of ETI treatment in Swedish children with CF.
sections.Methods: In this prospective multicentre study, children aged 6–17 years initiating ETI at three paediatric CF centres were assessed at baseline and after 6 and 12 months. Outcomes included GI symptoms, health-related quality of life (CFQ-R), nutritional status, PERT dose, and faecal elastase. Longitudinal changes were analysed using generalised least squares regression.
sections.Results: Sixty-two children (mean age 11.7 years) completed 12-month follow-up. The mean total number of reported GI symptoms decreased from 1.5 to 0.9 (P = 0.005), although no individual symptom showed a statistically significant change. A reduction in the number of GI symptoms was reported by 46% (n = 27) of participants, 32% (n = 19) reported no change, and 22% (n = 13) reported an increase (P = 0.003). CFQ-R Digestive scores improved by a mean of 7.8 points (P = 0.002). The Eating domain increased from a mean (SD) of 83.4 (20.5) to 88.0 (22.2) (mean change 4.5 points; 95% CI −0.1 to 9.2; P = 0.055), and the Weight domain from 72.8 (28.2) to 79.8 (31.5) (mean change 6.9 points; 95% CI 0.0 to 13.7; P = 0.051). Mean PERT dose decreased by 1.3 × 10³ units/kg/day (P < 0.001), while BMI z-scores and faecal elastase levels remained unchanged.
sections.Conclusion: During the first year of ETI therapy, children with CF experienced an overall reduction in gastrointestinal symptom burden and improved digestive-related quality of life, accompanied by reduced PERT use, without measurable changes in pancreatic function or growth.

sections.Background: Emerging evidence indicates that CFTR modulators (CFTRm) may restore exocrine pancreatic function in some children with cystic fibrosis (CF), yet predictors of recovery remain poorly defined. Aim: To determine the rate of conversion from pancreatic exocrine insufficiency (PEI) to pancreatic exocrine sufficiency (PES) in children on CFTRm treatment and identify clinical and demographic variables associated with the recovery of pancreatic function.
sections.Methods: In this single-centre prospective pediatric cohort study, children with CF and PEI (baseline fecal elastase-1 [FE-1] <200 μg/g stool) initiated CFTR modulator therapy and underwent serial FE-1 measurements. Groups were compared using Mann-Whitney U and Fisher exact tests. Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis evaluated age at modulator initiation.
sections.Results: Of 59 children, 44 remained PEI and 15 transitioned to PES. Follow-up duration was longer in PEI (median 2.06 years [IQR 1.96-3.00] vs. 1.96 [0.98-2.04]; p=0.020). 29 were treated with lumcaftor/ivacaftor 21 with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor and 9 switched between the two CFTRm during the follow-up. Median age at baseline was 7.4 years (IQR 4.3-12.3). PES children started CFTRm at a younger age (5.0 years [2.6-7.8] vs. 8.6 [5.8-12.4]; p=0.013) and showed higher proportion of detectable baseline FE-1 (>15 μg/g; 47% vs. 25%). The proportion of males did not differ significantly between groups (47% in PES vs. 46% in PEI). ROC curve analysis of age at CFTRm initiation yielded an area under the curve (AUC) of 0.724. An optimal threshold of 6.5 years predicted recovery of exocrine pancreatic function with 70% sensitivity and approximately 70% specificity. Below this age threshold, 44% of children regained PES, compared to 14% above this age.
sections.Conclusion: Younger age at CFTR modulator initiation and higher baseline FE-1 levels are associated with recovery of exocrine pancreatic function. These findings support individualized CF management and may guide future decisions on PERT discontinuation. Long-term safety and outcomes require further investigation. Funding: National Research, Development and Innovation Fund (NKFIH 138929)

sections.Objectives: Chronic airway infections caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) remain a substantial burden for people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). The persistent chronic infection and the alarming rise in antibiotic resistance make Pa difficult to treat, highlighting the urgency for alternative treatment strategies. Non-digestible oligosaccharides (NDOs) have emerged as promising compounds with antimicrobial properties. Aim: To investigate the antimicrobial activity of the NDOs chito- and alginate-oligosaccharides (COS and AOS) against both antibiotic-resistant and -sensitive Pa strains.
sections.Methods: Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC), Minimum Bactericidal Concentration (MBC), and time-kill kinetic (TKK) assays demonstrated that COS and AOS inhibited growth and killed antibiotic sensitive and resistant Pa in a concentration-dependent manner within 24h. Adding COS to ceftazidime or tobramycin or AOS to ceftazidime, reduced the required antibiotic concentration 4- to 8-fold, restoring antibiotic efficacy in clinical isolates and shifting resistant strains into a susceptible range (MIC 16  2 µg/ml). In an in vitro lung infection model, 2% COS and 4% AOS prevented Pa attachment and internalization without cytotoxicity to respiratory epithelium, while 2% COS alone and in combination with tobramycin eradicated intracellular Pa. Mechanistic studies showed that COS, but not AOS, disrupted bacterial membrane integrity, explaining its antibiotic-potentiating effect. Neither of the NDOs promoted resistance development within 7 days of incubation, yet they maintained antimicrobial activity in artificial sputum medium (ASM).
sections.Results: Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC), Minimum Bactericidal Concentration (MBC), and time-kill kinetic (TKK) assays demonstrated that COS and AOS inhibited growth and killed antibiotic sensitive and resistant Pa in a concentration-dependent manner within 24h. Adding COS to ceftazidime or tobramycin or AOS to ceftazidime, reduced the required antibiotic concentration 4- to 8-fold, restoring antibiotic efficacy in clinical isolates and shifting resistant strains into a susceptible range (MIC 16  2 µg/ml). Mechanistic studies showed that COS, but not AOS, disrupted bacterial membrane integrity, explaining its antibiotic-potentiating effect. Neither of the NDOs promoted resistance development within 7 days of incubation, yet they maintained antimicrobial activity in artificial sputum medium (ASM).
sections.Methods and results: Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC), Minimum Bactericidal Concentration (MBC), and time-kill kinetic (TKK) assays demonstrated that COS and AOS inhibited growth and killed antibiotic sensitive and resistant Pa in a concentration-dependent manner within 24h. Adding COS to ceftazidime or tobramycin or AOS to ceftazidime, reduced the required antibiotic concentration 4- to 8-fold, restoring antibiotic efficacy in clinical isolates and shifting resistant strains into a susceptible range (MIC 16  2 µg/ml). In an in vitro lung infection model, 2% COS and 4% AOS prevented Pa attachment and internalization without cytotoxicity to respiratory epithelium, while 2% COS alone and in combination with tobramycin eradicated intracellular Pa. Mechanistic studies showed that COS, but not AOS, disrupted bacterial membrane integrity, explaining its antibiotic-potentiating effect. Neither of the NDOs promoted resistance development within 7 days of incubation, yet they maintained antimicrobial activity in artificial sputum medium (ASM).
sections.Conclusion: COS and AOS are both promising new antimicrobials against Pa, that act synergistically with existing antibiotics, restoring antibiotic sensitivity in resistant strains.

sections.Objectives: Newborn screening (NBS) for Cystic Fibrosis (CF), has led to an increasing number of infants being labelled as Cystic Fibrosis Screen Positive Inconclusive Diagnosis (CF-SPID). Such infants carry two gene variants but show no clinical evidence of CF disease. A positive newborn screening result does, however carry a potential future risk for the development of CF. Current CF-SPID guidelines (Barben et al. J Cyst Fibros 2021) advise consideration of discharge from follow-up after aged 6 years if sweat tests remain normal and no clinical conversion to CF.
sections.Methods: We collated data on all children born between 2006 and 2019 labelled as CF-SPID within our service. Data collected included genotype, sweat chloride at diagnosis, subsequent sweat test results, as well as clinical symptoms and lung function.
sections.Results: Eighteen children with CF-SPID were identified via NBS, with no clinical symptoms or evidence of disease. Four (22%) were later reclassified as having CF due to developing symptoms and/or sweat chloride (sweat Cl-) >60mmol.L-1. These data are summarised in Table 1. Lung function has remained near-normal for this cohort. Table 1 Patient characteristics of CF-SPIDs who converted to a diagnosis of CF Age at conversion SexGenotypeInitial sweat Cl- as infantRepeat sweat Cl- (age) Clinical symptomsLatest sweat Cl- (reason) Latest FEV1 (%pred) 12MaleG551D/Q1291H 4760 (aged 2)Asymptomatic79 (pre-modulator) 10525Female G542X/P988R 5378 (aged 5) Sino-nasal symptoms, recurrent cough 78 (pre-modulator) 97313Female V456A/c.1705T>G 3959 (aged 8)Sino-nasal symptoms 65 (when diagnosed) 100415MaleF508del/C866Y 2153 (aged 9) DIOS/small bowel obstruction 71 (when diagnosed) 100
sections.Conclusion: Four (22%) of our CF-SPID cohort converted to a diagnosis of CF, with 75% these having clinical symptoms prior to re-designation. Two (11%) were teenagers when re-designated, and would have gone undetected if using current guidelines that recommend discharge at aged 6 if asymptomatic. Our findings strongly suggest the need for long-term follow-up of young people labelled as CF-SPID.

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 05 - Fuel, frame and function in cystic fibrosis nutrition

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) has traditionally been associated with malnutrition, with nutritional optimisation linked to improved lung function and survival. Clinical management has been focused on weight gain and achieving target body mass index (BMI). The introduction of highly effective CFTR modulator therapy (HEMT) has altered this paradigm, leading to improvements in pulmonary function and nutritional outcomes. This study aimed to assess lung function and nutritional status in paediatric CF patients in the HEMT era.
sections.Methods: Retrospective analysis of medical records of paediatric CF patients followed at 2 national reference centers. Data collected included demographics, genotype, CFTR modulator therapy, pancreatic status, lung function and anthropometric measurements.
sections.Results: Fifty-seven patients were included (49.1% female), with a median age of 11.8 years (P25–P75: 6.8–15.9). The most common genotype was heterozygous F508del (38.6%), and 80.7% had pancreatic insufficiency. Most patients (75.4%) were receiving CFTR modulators, mainly elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (51.2%). Median FEV₁ was 98.2% predicted (P25–P75: 87.8–110.9). Mean BMI z-score was −0.3 (±1.1; min −3.1, max 1.9) and mean height z-score was −0.8 (±1.2; min −3.7, max 1.4). Mean arm circumference z-score (n=34) was -1.1 (±0.9). Median triceps skinfold thickness z-score (n=19) was 0 (P25-75: -1.1-0.4), and mean subscapular skinfold thickness z-score was -0.1 (±0.9). Fat mass percentage (n=29) was normal in 58.6% and high in 27.6%. Skeletal muscle mass percentage was normal in 58.6% and low in 34.5%. Nineteen patients underwent DEXA scanning, with a mean whole-body z-score of −0.5 (±0.7).
sections.Conclusion: In the HEMT era, paediatric CF patients demonstrated preserved lung function and generally adequate nutritional status. With increasing survival and a shift towards overweight and obesity, updated nutritional management strategies are required.

sections.Objectives: Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) are commonly prescribed in children with cystic fibrosis (CwCF), despite concerns regarding their impact on gut microbiome development. Utilising data arising from the PEARL-CF Study, associations between PPI exposure and gut microbiota development were examined.
sections.Methods: Clinical data and stool samples from CwCF (aged 0-8 years) and age-matched healthy controls (HC) enrolled in PEARL-CF (https://doi.org/10.64898/2025.12.04.25341620) were utilised. Stool samples underwent shotgun metagenomic sequencing and analysed using MetaPhlAn4. All CwCF receving the PEARL-CF probiotic/placebo were included as a single group. Linear mixed-effects models were utilised to explore longitudinal associations between PPI use (at time of stool sampling) and observed bacterial species richness from all study samples. Community structure was assess using Aitchison distance.
sections.Results: Seventy CwCF (age 2.9 years [2.4–4.8]; 57% male) and 67 HC (age 2.2 years [0.9–3.9]; 54% male) were included. At the time of sampling, PPIs were used by 14.3% of CwCF and 4.1% of HC. In CwCF, PPI exposure was not associated with altered observed bacterial richness (alpha diversity) trajectories (β −1.7 [SE 5.4], p=0.75). Healthy controls exhibited consistently higher richness compared with CF, with a trend towards a differential PPI-associated effect between groups (β −42.8 [SE 25.3], p=0.09). Within CwCF, Aitchison distance analyses (beta diversity) demonstrated no significant association between PPI exposure and gut community structure after adjusting for age (β 0.63; 95% CI −2.6 to 3.8; p=0.7), indicating substantial inter-individual variability but no consistent PPI-driven compositional shift.
sections.Conclusion: In CwCF, PPI exposure was not associated with measurable changes in gut bacterial richness or community structure. Persistent differences between CwCF and HC highlight disease-specific effects on microbiome development outweighing PPI exposure during early life.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 01 - Clinical and functional impact of highly effective modulators

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) results from pathogenic CFTR variants that impair epithelial chloride and bicarbonate transport. CFTR modulators such as elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) have transformed CF care, and the next‑generation regimen vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaftor (VTD) has recently been introduced on the market. VTD offers once‑daily dosing and expanded regulatory approval, yet its functional advantages remain not fully characterized across a broad set of genotypes. Consequently, we set out to compare CFTR functional rescue of ETI and VTD in primary patient-derived intestinal organoids (PDIOs) harbouring a broad range of CFTR variants.
sections.Methods: We evaluated ETI and VTD efficacy in 62 PDIO lines representing 42 rare CFTR genotypes and reference groups (F508del/F508del, F508del/class I, class I/class I). CFTR rescue was assessed by CFTR maturation via western blot and CFTR function by steady‑state lumen area quantification and forskolin‑induced swelling assays.
sections.Results: VTD increased CFTR maturation more effectively than ETI, yet demonstrated similar potentiator activity. Across rare variants, CFTR functional rescue by ETI and VTD was strongly correlated, with modest but consistent VTD advantages in a subset of folding‑defective genotypes (e.g., , V520F). Three PDIOs showed disproportionately high responses to VTD.
sections.Conclusion: VTD provides enhanced CFTR maturation and modest genotype‑specific functional advantages but is largely comparable to ETI across rare variants. PDIO‑based screenings performed in this study underline again their potential for theratyping, which is particularly critical for guiding decisions on accessibility for rare variants in an era with a variety of modulators, and combinations thereof.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 02 - From escalation to optimisation: real-world treatment changes in the CFTR-modulator era

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has been shown to improve lung function in people with CF (pwCF), with recent clinical trials suggesting sustained long-term benefits. However, pulmonary function decline remains a concern, and long-term real-world data are limited. We aimed to analyze data from the European Cystic Fibrosis Society Patient Registry (ECFSPR) to describe long-term FEV₁ trajectories following ETI initiation and to evaluate factors associated with post-treatment decline.
sections.Methods: We analyzed percent predicted FEV₁ (ppFEV₁) trajectories in adult pwCF with at least 3 years of follow-up following ETI initiation. Longitudinal analysis employed linear mixed-effects models with random intercepts and slopes, comparing ppFEV₁ trends 3 years before and 3 years after ETI initiation. Models were adjusted for demographics and disease characteristics.
sections.Results: A total of 10,214 adults were included (mean age 31.6±10.1 years; 53.4% male, 94% with ≥1 F508del allele). Crude analyses showed that ppFEV₁ increased from 69.2(±23.8)% at before ETI to 74.1(±23.9)% after 1 year of ETI, with values remaining largely stable at 73.7% and 73.2% at years 2 and 3, respectively. In adjusted mixed‑effects models, the annual ppFEV₁ decline in the 3 years prior to ETI initiation was –1.45% (95% CI: –1.57 to –1.34), compared with –0.43% per year (95% CI: –0.54 to –0.32) in the 3 years following ETI initiation (p < 0.001). Before ETI, low BMI, chronic P. aeruginosa infection, and CF-related diabetes were associated with greater FEV₁ decline. After ETI, these associations were no longer significant.
sections.Conclusion: Data from the ECFSPR demonstrate long-term improvement in ppFEV₁ following ETI initiation. Nevertheless, a decline in lung function still persists. Moreover, traditional predictors of FEV₁ decline lose prognostic value in the post-ETI period suggesting that strategies to monitor and manage disease progression in pwCF may need to be reconsidered in the modulator era

presentations.session_title: Workshop 04 - From calories to composition: the new story of nutrition in cystic fibrosis

sections.Objectives: Essential fatty acids have been hypothesized to play a role as a disease-modifier in cystic fibrosis (CF). We aimed to assess fatty acid-status in serum phospholipids before and after CFTR modulator (CFTRm) therapy.
sections.Methods: The National Swedish CF registry was used to retrieve data on serum phospholipids (in mol%) before and after the initiation of CFTRm treatment in children (n=92) and adults (n=119) with CF followed at Stockholm CF Center (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor: n=104, lumacaftor/ivacaftor: n=86, ivacaftor: n=8, tezacaftor/ivacaftor: n=10, other: n=3). Paired T-test was used for statistical analyses.
sections.Results: Eicosapentaenoic and docosahexaenoic acid decreased after CFTRm initiation in both children (1,38 -> 1,09; 3,42 -> 2,90; p<0,001 in both) and adults (1,62 -> 1,40; p=0,004; 3,29 -> 3,09; p=0,027). In addition, linoleic acid (LA) decreased in children (19,6 -> 18,3; p=0,002). In adults, LA remained unchanged (18,3 -> 17,9; p=0,29). In line with that, oleic acid (OLA), OLA/LA and Mead acid increased in both children (12,9 -> 14,1; 0,68 -> 0,81; 0,40 -> 0,59; p<0,001 in all) and adults (13,6 -> 14,5; p<0,001; 0,77 -> 0,86; p=0,003; 0,50 -> 0,58; p=0,025). Retinol (1,40 -> 1,56; p<0,001) and 25-hydroxy-vitamin D (72,0 -> 75,8 nmol/L; p=0,042) increased in the whole cohort. Tocopherol remained unchanged (p=0,3). Z-BMI (-0,19 -> 0,05; p<0,001) and BMI (22,2 -> 23,2; p<0,001) increased in children and adults, respectively. Both in children and adults, FEV1 (% predicted) increased (93,0 -> 96,7; p=0,036 and 71,9 -> 76,6; p<0,001). PwCF with low LA (LA < 18 mol%, n=88) after CFTRm initiation had higher total leucocyte count in peripheral blood, compared with pwCF with LA > 18 mol% (n=100; 7,3 vs. 6,4; p=0,002).
sections.Conclusion: The results indicate that omega 3 and 6 fatty acid status measured in mol% unexpectedly decreased after CFTRm initiation despite unchanged dietician recommendations (except in obese). We speculate that a possible fatty acid deficiency could be a disease-modifier in CF that remains unaffected by CFTRm.

sections.Objectives: Assess safety, pharmacokinetics (PK) and efficacy of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ELX/TEZ/IVA) in children with CF aged 12-<24 months with ≥1 F508del or ELX/TEZ/IVA-responsive CFTR allele.
sections.Methods: This 24-week, phase 3, open-label study of ELX/TEZ/IVA enrolled children aged 12-<24 months with responsive genotypes, including F/MF and F/F. Primary endpoint was safety and tolerability. Secondary endpoints were PK and absolute change in sweat chloride.
sections.Results: 54 children enrolled and received ELX/TEZ/IVA. 53 children (98.1%) had adverse events (AE); majority were mild (42.6%) or moderate (46.3%). 6 children (11.1%) had serious AEs, none considered related to ELX/TEZ/IVA. PK exposures of ELX, TEZ, and IVA were within target adult ranges. Mean change from baseline in sweat chloride was -71.8 mmol/L (95% CI: -75.4, -68.2; P<0.0001) through Week 24; 98.0% of children achieved concentrations <60mmol/L and 68.6% <30 mmol/L. Observed annual rate of PEx was 0.67. Weight and length z-scores remained in normal range. Mean fecal elastase-1 concentration increased by 68.7 µg/g (SD, 98.5) at Week 24 from baseline mean of 56.5 µg/g; 14.6% of children (7/48) transitioned from pancreatic insufficiency at baseline (<200 µg/g) to pancreatic sufficiency at Week 24. In the 26 children that had sedated LCI measurements, which is the largest LCI dataset in this age group from a CFTRm trial, the mean change in LCI2.5 through Week 24 was -0.44 (SD, 1.26).
sections.Conclusion: Over 24 weeks, ELX/TEZ/IVA was generally safe and well tolerated in this young population. Children had rapid and statistically significant decreases in sweat chloride, indicating improved CFTR function, with ~70% of children reaching normal levels. The sweat chloride reduction was the largest observed in any CFTRm trial to date. There were low rates of PEx, normal growth, improved fecal elastase-1, and decreased LCI2.5 values. These unprecedented results support the value of early treatment in CF.
