presentations.session_title: Workshop 02 - From escalation to optimisation: real-world treatment changes in the CFTR-modulator era

sections.Objectives: To determine the 12-month cost-effectiveness of reducing treatment burden by discontinuing nebulised muco-active therapies in people with cystic fibrosis (CF) receiving ETI.
sections.Methods: CF STORM (ISRCTN=14081521) was a multicentre, registry-based randomised controlled trial enrolling people with CF aged ≥6 years established on ETI and using at least one daily nebulised muco-active therapy. Participants were randomised 1:1 to STOP or CONTINUE their muco-active therapy (dornase alfa, hypertonic saline, or both). This within-trial economic evaluation compares healthcare use, costs, and health-related quality of life (HRQoL) over 12 months. Resource-use (medications, pulmonary exacerbations, hospitalisations, and outpatient encounters) were collected via the UK CF Registry. HRQoL was measured using EQ-5D-5L at baseline and follow-up; EQ-5D-3L index scores were derived using a validated crosswalk to calculate QALYs for the cost-effectiveness analysis.
sections.Results: 591 participants were enrolled from 40 UK CF centres (mean [SD]: age 21.7 [13.2] years; baseline ppFEV₁ 89.4 [18.2]). Matched baseline and follow-up HRQoL data were available for n=354. Baseline EQ-5D-3L index scores were high (maximum =1.0), with 64% reporting no problems across any EQ-5D-5L domains. Unadjusted baseline EQ-5D-3L index scores were lower in STOP than CONTINUE (mean [SD]: 0.89 [0.15] vs 0.93 [0.11]; p=0.005). Index scores remained high at follow-up in both groups, and there was no significant difference in adjusted 1-year QALYs between STOP and CONTINUE (mean difference −0.0041; 95% CI −0.013 to 0.005; p=0.4).
sections.Conclusion: In people with CF receiving ETI, stopping nebulised muco-active therapies was not associated with a difference in adjusted 1-year QALYs. High baseline EQ-5D scores and ceiling effects were observed, potentially limiting sensitivity to detect health-related quality of life changes. Full cost-effectiveness and sensitivity analyses will be presented at the conference.

sections.Objectives: Treatment for Mycobacterium abscessus pulmonary disease (MABSC-PD) is characterised by prolonged use of multiple, expensive antibiotics that exhibit substantial toxicity and poor effectiveness. The Finding the Optimal Regimen for Mycobacterium abscessus Treatment (FORMaT) adaptive platform trial (APT) aims to find the best treatment for MABSC-PD. There is an intensive treatment phase using intravenous and oral antimicrobials followed by a consolidation phase each with separate randomisations. An Observational Cohort (OC) simultaneously recruits participants with positive cultures who do not meet criteria for treatment.
sections.Methods: Inclusion criteria for the APT: positive MABSC-PD diagnosis based on American Thoracic Society criteria; any age and sex; no MABSC-PD treatment in 12 months prior; and for OC: ≥1 MABSC positive respiratory culture; any age and sex; no MABSC-PD treatment in 12 months prior. The first interim analysis (IA) in April 2026 will analyse data from 60 APT participants who have completed 6 weeks short intensive therapy (SI) using the Sequential Multiple Assignment Randomised Trial approach, thus providing the first randomised assessment of standard of care treatments. Following IA a new treatment arm using combined B-lactam antimicrobials will be initiated. The APT has 3 nested trials – the first compares efficacy and tolerance at 6 weeks. The primary outcome measures are: MABSC clearance – defined as 3 negative respiratory samples (sputum/induced sputum) or 1 negative bronchoalveolar lavage collected at Week 4; and Treatment tolerance – defined as no adverse events (AEs) or AEs possibly/probably/definitely related to study drugs graded 1 or 2 based on the Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events Version 5.0. sections.Background: Advances in CF care and the widespread use of highly effective modulator therapies (HEMT) have shifted the nutritional profile of adults with CF, with rising prevalence of overweight, obesity and altered body composition. The cardiometabolic implications of increased adiposity on lipid profiles remains unclear.
sections.Objectives: To characterise body composition and lipid profiles in adults with CF, and to examine how Body Mass Index (BMI), and fat mass percentage (FM%) relate to atherogenic lipid profiles.
sections.Methods: This cross-sectional retrospective chart review was conducted at a single UK adult CF centre. Routine annual assessment data included anthropometry, bioelectrical impedance analysis (BIA) and fasting lipid profiles (total cholesterol [TC], triglycerides [TG], high-density lipoprotein [HDL], direct low-density lipoprotein cholesterol [LDL-C], non-HDL C, TC: HDL C ratio). World Health Organisation BMI categories were applied. FM% was classified as pre-obese (25-29.9% men; 30-34.9% women) and obese (≥ 30% men; ≥ 35% women). Nine combined BMI–FM phenotypes were derived. Associations between FM% and lipid profiles were examined.
sections.Results: 223 participants were included (64% male); median age 35 years (IQR 27-42), median FEV1 74% predicted (IQR 54-93) and median BMI 24.1kg/m2 (IQR: 22.3-26.6). HEMT use was reported in 94%. Based on FM%, 28% were pre-obese and 30% were obese, affecting 72% of women and 47% of men. Elevated TC, TG, LDL-C, non-HDL-C C and low HDL-C were observed in 21%, 15%, 23%, 11% and 15% participants respectively (p<0.01). Higher FM% was associated with greater prevalence of dyslipidaemia and showed moderate positive correlations with TC, direct LDL C, non-HDL C and TC: HDL C.
sections.Conclusion: Higher FM%, including those with a normal BMI, is associated with more atherogenic lipid profiles in adults with CF. Incorporating BIA alongside BMI and routine lipid screening may improve identification of emerging cardiometabolic risk.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 01 - Clinical and functional impact of highly effective modulators

sections.Objectives: To assess long-term safety and efficacy of VNZ/TEZ/D-IVA (VTD) in children with CF aged 6-11yrs, with ≥1 elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor(ETI)-responsive allele who completed the pivotal study RIDGELINE 105.
sections.Methods: Study VX22-121-106 is an ongoing phase 3, open-label study of VTD consisting of Parts A and B (96 wks each); Part A is reported here. Primary endpoint is safety/tolerability. Secondary endpoints include absolute change in sweat chloride (SwCl), ppFEV1, and number of pulmonary exacerbations (PEx). Other endpoints include proportion of participants with SwCl <60 and <30 mmol/L and absolute change in LCI2.5.
sections.Results: 75 participants (pts) were enrolled and dosed; 69 completed treatment in Part A. 98.7% of pts had an AE; most were mild (34.7%) or moderate (54.7%) in severity and consistent with manifestations of CF. Event rates of rash, transaminase and creatine kinase elevations, and cataracts were consistent with the VTD safety profile. Neuropsychiatric events/rates were consistent with those observed in Study 105. Absolute change from baseline on ETI in Study 105 in SwCl was -6.2 (95%CI: -9.9,-2.6) mmol/L; proportion of pts with SwCl <60 and <30mmol/L were 88.9% and 50.8%, respectively. Lung function (ppFEV1 and LCI2.5) was normal at baseline and remained stable. Additional efficacy results were maintained from Study 105.
sections.Conclusion: VTD was generally safe and well tolerated for 96 wks of treatment in pts aged 6-11yrs, consistent with Study 105 and the established VTD safety profile. VTD demonstrated further improvement in CFTR function compared to ETI baseline, with sustained improvement in SwCl and majority of pts maintaining SwCl <60 and <30mmol/L. Lung function (which was normal at baseline) remained stable, demonstrating the sustained clinical impact of CFTR restoration, which has been shown to correlate with better clinical outcomes. These findings suggest the impact of additional CFTR restoration in this age group is maintained.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 02 - From escalation to optimisation: real-world treatment changes in the CFTR-modulator era

sections.Background: Fewer pulmonary exacerbations are being diagnosed in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) since the introduction of the highly effective modulator therapy (HEMT) elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI). We assessed current antibiotic (ABX) treatment responses to acute drops in FEV1 and their effect in the post ETI era.
sections.Methods: Using CF Foundation Patient Registry data (2021–2023), we identified the first relative decline from baseline of 5%-50% in PwCF aged 6–40 years with baseline FEV1pp >50%, stratified by consistent vs no HEMT use. ABX treatment was determined within -14 to +28 days of decline. Multivariable logistic regression assessed odds of recovery to 100% baseline FEV1pp within 180 days post decline, adjusting for baseline FEV1pp, decline magnitude, HEMT, age, asthma, prior Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa), CF-related diabetes.
sections.Results: Among 22,798 eligible pwCF, 9,841 (43%) had eligible declines; 55% of those on HEMT, 24% not on HEMT. Only 20% received ABX (HEMT: 18%, non-HEMT: 27%). ABX treatment increased the OR for recovery to baseline for all levels of decline and for those on or off HEMT (all p<0.001), albeit less so in the HEMT group (HEMT: OR 1.92; 95% CI 1.66, 2.22; non-HEMT: OR 2.66; 95% CI 2.12, 3.33; interaction p=0.042). Both modes of ABX therapy significantly increased the OR for return to baseline, but recovery with IV was 1.92 (95% CI 1.51, 2.42) times more likely than with oral/inhaled.
sections.Conclusion: While HEMT leads to fewer acute drops in FEV1pp, when they occur, those as small as 5% predicted are still less likely to be recovered if ABX treatment is not prescribed. Nonetheless, acute drops in PWCF using HEMT are treated half as often as the low levels we found in the pre-ETI era (PMID: 38995116) and those occurring in PWCF not using HEMT are treated ¾ as often. These findings suggest the continued need for more aggressive antimicrobial treatment of acute drops in FEV1pp even for those with relatively small declines on HEMT.

sections.Background: When children with cystic fibrosis (cwCF) are clinically stable, but grow methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) on airway culture, the benefit of antibacterial therapy is unknown. The Antibiotic Treatment Of Staphylococcus aureus In Stable People With CF study (ASAP-CF; NCT04553419) was a two-centre, randomised, placebo-controlled study to assess the effect of oral cephalexin in clinically stable cwCF who grow MSSA on airway culture.
sections.Methods: Key inclusion criteria included age 3-16 years, airway culture growth of MSSA in the past 24 months and ability to perform multiple breath nitrogen washout testing (MBW). Exclusion criteria included chronic Gram-negative bacterial growth, allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis or systemic corticosteroid exposure. Individuals were followed at routine CF clinic visits and randomised (up to 2 times each) to receive oral cephalexin (50 mg/kg TID) or placebo for 14 days if deemed at clinical baseline with a positive airway culture for MSSA.
sections.Results: Between October 2020 and June 2024, 56 children were recruited, with 81 total randomisations. Mean age at randomisation was 10.2 (SD 3.95) years, mean baseline lung clearance index (LCI) was 6.93 (SD 1.17) and mean baseline ppFEV1 was 105.0% (SD 11.8). As previously reported in abstract form, antibiotic treatment did not improve spirometry or MBW outcomes, nor respiratory symptoms (Rayment et al., NACFC, 2025). Here, we report new analyses showing that time to next pulmonary exacerbation (HR 0.39 [95%CI 0.18, 0.85], p=0.017) and time to next MSSA growth (HR 0.34 [95%CI 0.20, 0.59], p<0.001) were significantly longer in those in the treatment group, analysed in a Cox Proportional Hazard model.
sections.Conclusion: While administration of oral cephalexin to clinically stable cwCF who have MSSA on surveillance airway culture does not result in improvement in lung function, it does impact time to next pulmonary exacerbation and subsequent MSSA growth.

sections.Methods: Data on all cwCF aged 2–17 years recorded in the UK CF Registry between 2007-2023 were analysed. CwCF were categorised by BMI z score following ESPEN-ESPGHAN-ECFS 2024 definitions; underweight, target range, overweight, and obese. Linear models were used to analyse the associations of BMI and BMI change with lung function, by modulator use.
sections.Results: Data on 8,246 cwCF from 62,940 annual reviews was available. Between 2007 and 2023, cwCF in target BMI range fell from 80.4% to 74.0%, overweight rose from 10.8% to 12.2%, obesity rose from 3.5 to 11.0%, and underweight fell from 5.3% to 2.9%. Higher BMI is associated with higher FEV1. Increasing BMI in underweight cwCF saw the largest increase in FEV1. Much smaller gains were seen in cwCF in target range. CwCF on modulators had a higher FEV1 and a different association between BMI gain and FEV1 (p < 0.001). In cwCF on modulators FEV1 was 3.77 units higher, and initial increases in BMI in all NS categories showed a smaller, but still positive, effect up to a threshold of diminishing positive associations.
sections.Conclusion: This large, registry-based study is the first to examine trends and associations in NS in UK cwCF. Findings broadly align with other national registries data highlighting the priority for proactive management of excess weight. Monitoring is essential for understanding and re-evaluating evolving nutritional trends, management, and targets. This is the first study showing historic BMI–lung function associations differ in cwCF on modulators.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 01 - Clinical and functional impact of highly effective modulators

sections.Background: EMA approved CFTR modulators use for the treatment of cystic fibrosis patients (pwCF) aged ≥ 2 years with at least one non-class I CFTR. Nevertheless, almost 10% of pwCF have two non responsive CFTR variants and remain ineligible for highly effective modulator therapy (HEMT). Aims: This study describes this group’s phenotype and evaluates eligibility for emerging targeted trials.
sections.Methods: Using the French Cystic Fibrosis Registry, we compared 573 HEMT - ineligible pwCF (defined by the absence of a F508del variant and not included in the official list of eligible variants (506 variants) nor in the French compassionate use program (additional 53 variants)) with 6,539 pwCF eligible to HEMT (≥1 F508del variant). Analyses included a cross-sectional approach (2024) and longitudinal follow-up (ineligible cohort: 2020–2024; eligible cohorte pre-HEMT: 2015–2019).
sections.Results: In 2024, ineligible patients had significantly poorer outcomes: mean FEV1 79% vs. 87% (p<0.01), BMI 19 vs. 21 kg/m² (p<0.01), exacerbations 2.7 vs. 1.8 (p<0.01), hospitalizations 0.8 vs. 0.2 (p<0.01), mortality 1.22 vs. 0.47 (p=0.04). Longitudinally, their clinical trajectory mirrored that of the pre-HEMT eligible cohort, with a steeper FEV₁ decline (−3.8% vs −1.1%, p < 0.01), more hospitalization days (6.73 vs 5.13, p < 0.01), and a comparable exacerbation rate (2.36 vs 2.24, p = 0.09). Only 30% met major common trial inclusion criteria (FEV1 >40%, BMI >17, no Burkholderia cepacia complex infection, no transplant) and we will discuss the other inclusion/exclusion criteria.
sections.Conclusion: pwCF carrying two CFTR variants ineligible to modulators are as severe as those eligible for HEMT prior to initiation of Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor — and may even experience worse outcomes. Only a few met major common trial inclusion criteria of current trials. These findings highlight the urgent need to update clinical trial eligibility criteria, develop alternative therapeutic approaches, and understand patient motivations to support the design of more inclusive trials for a population that remains as vulnerable as those currently eligible.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 02 - From escalation to optimisation: real-world treatment changes in the CFTR-modulator era

sections.Objectives: The impact of starting ETI (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor) on maintenance therapy prescribing in the UK is largely unknown. We aim to examine prescribing trends and factors influencing deprescribing over a 4-year period.
sections.Methods: A retrospective analysis of chronic medications obtained from the UK CF registry, between the date of ETI initiation until 31st December 2023. The relationships between starting ETI and documented maintenance therapies (oral and inhaled antibiotics, mucolytics and insulin) were analysed. Risk factors associated with stopping maintenance therapies were determined by logistic regression models, adjusted for age, sex and percentage predicted FEV1. Socioeconomic status (SES) was determined by Townsend score quintiles.
sections.Results: Data were available from 5965 individuals aged ≥ 12 years who were prescribed ETI. From the year of initial ETI prescription to 3 years after starting ETI, 4.2 to 8.0% stopped inhaled antibiotics per year, 1.5 to 12.8% stopped mucolytic therapies per year, and 4.1 to 5.2% stopped azithromycin per year. Over the same time period, overall prevalence of prescribed insulin increased from 27.1 to 34.0%. Stopping inhaled antibiotics and azithromycin was associated with younger age, lower body mass index and higher lung function. Those with higher SES, as defined as quintile 5, were significantly more likely to remain on any mucolytic (OR:1.34; CI:1.04-1.74), inhaled antibiotics (OR:1.47; CI:1.08-2.02), azithromycin (OR:1.53; CI:1.05-2.25) and flucloxacillin (OR:1.68; CI:1.07-2.64), compared to the lowest SES, quintile 1.
sections.Conclusion: There are sustained yearly reductions in prescribed nebulised antibiotics, mucolytics and azithromycin post ETI initiation. Age, body mass index, lung function and SES are associated with changes in chronic prescribing in those on ETI. These data warrant consideration in future clinical trial designs examining treatment withdrawal.

sections.Objectives: To characterise the antimicrobial activity, mechanism of action and potential for resistance emergence of OMN51, a novel bioengineered antimicrobial peptide derived from Capitellacin, against Pseudomonas aeruginosa, a major driver of pulmonary disease in cystic fibrosis. To evaluate its suitability for inhaled delivery in patients with cystic fibrosis.
sections.Methods: OMN51 was characterised in vitro for antimicrobial activity, bactericidal kinetics and propensity for resistance development. The mechanism of action was investigated by fluorescence microscopy, and interactions with standard-of-care (SOC) antibiotics, which usually are given as inhaled therapy, were assessed by checkerboard analysis. Inhalation suitability was evaluated through nebuliser screening, including aerosol characterisation and peptide stability.
sections.Results: OMN51 demonstrated high efficacy and potent activity against diverse Pa strains with varying resistance phenotypes, with minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) values within a narrow range (4-16 µg/ml). Rapid, concentration-dependent bactericidal activity was observed, with comparable killing kinetics in antibiotic-susceptible and multidrug-resistant (MDR) strains. Mechanistic studies indicate a membrane-disrupting mode of action. No resistance development was observed over 20 days of serial passaging, in contrast to SOC antibiotics. Combination studies showed no interference of OMN51 with SOC drugs, where additive effects and lack of cross-resistance were demonstrated. OMN51 generated stable, inhalable aerosol across multiple nebulisers while preserving its antimicrobial activity.
sections.Conclusion: Omnix introduces OMN51 as a game-changing inhaled anti-infective, targeting Pa, offering high efficacy with no observed resistance and enabling convenient at-home administration. Once approved, OMN51 will be positioned as a first-line inhaled treatment in CF, non-bronchiectasis and COPD.

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator modulator (CFTRm) therapies have resulted in a notable shift in the clinical presentation of CF. There is a lack of robust, real-world longitudinal data on CFTRm therapy in children under five years of age, where extrapulmonary outcomes such as growth, micronutrient status and pancreatic function are the key focus.
sections.Methods: PaNC is a prospective cohort study involving all eight tertiary paediatric CF centres in Australia. Infants and children four months to five years of age who are eligible for Alyftrek®, Trikafta® or Kalydeco® meet the inclusion criteria. The primary outcomes are change in weight-for-length (WFL)/body mass index (BMI) z score and change in serum micronutrient status, at 6–12 monthly intervals, during the first two years of treatment. Secondary outcomes include change in exocrine pancreatic function, measured by faecal elastase-1 (FE-1). The study protocol has been published: https://bmjopen.bmj.com/content/15/7/e097071
sections.Results: A total of 208 children are enrolled in PaNC. At baseline, 192 (92%) children were eligible and due to commence Trikafta®, 16 (8%) Kalydeco® and nil Alyftrek®. The median (IQR) age at baseline was 3.34 (2.33;4.63) years. Ninety-five (46%) children were on a previous modulator at baseline. Twelve month interim results from 122 children reveal a mean (SD) change in WFL z score of 0.12 (0.29) and a mean (SD) change in BMI z score of -0.12 (0.01). At 12 months, 44 paired FE-1 results were available: the mean (SD) change in FE-1 was 89.40 ug/g (39.33) and eight (22%) of the 36 children who were pancreatic insufficient at baseline reverted to pancreatic sufficiency at 12 months, as defined by FE-1 >200 ug/g. Non-significant changes were noted in serum micronutrient status.
sections.Conclusion: Early life CFTRm therapy demonstrates minimal growth and serum micronutrient changes however does reveal potential for restoration of exocrine pancreatic function.

sections.Objectives: Discuss outcomes, ethics, and emerging data, short & long term, from U.S. & European cases of prenatal elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor (ETI) exposure in cystic fibrosis pregnancies, including treatment timing, access, postnatal management, disease & neuro-development.
sections.Methods: Case data were gathered via standardized tools, including REDCap, from published & ongoing reports. Clinical outcomes, prenatal and postnatal findings,& complications were analyzed.
sections.Results: In U.S. cases abnormal prenatal ultrasounds occurred in 89.4% of cases, most showing echogenic/dilated bowel loops. These resolved in most cases after a mean ETI treatment duration of less than 7 weeks. Mean gestational age at delivery was 38 5/7 weeks -15.7% delivered late preterm. Postnatal complications included NICU admission (56%), transient feeding or meconium passage issues. Sweat chloride averaged 62 mmol/L, & 42% of breastmilk-fed infants were found to be pancreatic sufficient. Among European cases, abnormal prenatal ultrasounds also were noted in the majority of cases, however a smaller proportion of infants had finding resolution prior to delivery. Multiple cases of postnatal meconium ileus are described. Adverse maternal effects remain rare, such as rash or mild transaminase elevation. Further data reflecting long term outcomes on pancreatic sufficiency, growth, sweat chloride, cataracts & presence/absence of the vas deferens continue to be collected; most infants from U.S. with early pancreatic sufficiency have maintained this status. Data available from U.S. cases on developmental milestones is within normal limits & development of cataracts has not been reported.
sections.Conclusion: Prenatal ETI exposure shows promise in improving outcomes for CF infants, resolving meconium ileus, & supporting early pancreatic function. Cumulative reports from known cases suggest complications are uncommon. Standardized protocols, equitable access, & further research are needed to address unresolved cases & ensure long-term safety.

sections.Background: Chronic inhaled antibiotics (iAB) are recommended as suppressive therapy for chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) infection. Yet, better lung function of people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) may affect the benefits associated with chronic therapies.
sections.Objectives: To evaluate risk of iAB associated infections, if lung function stabilization associated with iAB differs by Pa infection status and if this association changed between 2011 and 2019.
sections.Methods: Use of US CF-Foundation Patient Registry to define chronic, intermittent and Pa negative status per Leeds Criteria and chronic iAB use (≥3/12 months). Survival modeling defined risks of Aspergillus and Gram-negatives between antibiotic-treated and untreated pwCF by Pa status. Differences in lung function associated with iAB were modeled using generalized estimating equations, with testing if the mean effect of antibiotics differed across CFTR-modulator eras (ivacaftor, lumacaftor/ivacaftor, tezacaftor/ivacaftor). Analyses included inverse probability treatment weighting.
sections.Results: From 2011 to 2019 the number of pwCF analysed increased from 12,000 to 16,041 with decreasing prevalence in chronic Pa, increasing age and better lung function. Risk of Aspergillus was higher in antibiotic prescribed vs. non-prescribed pwCF: HR 1.33 (CI95% 1.26, 1.41), and was significantly higher in Pa negative compared to Pa chronic pwCF. Similarly, iAB were associated with higher risk of Stenotrophomonas maltophilia and Achromobacter: HR 1.21 (CI95% 1.14, 1.29) and 1.26 (CI95% 1.14, 1.39), respectively. iAB were associated with higher lung function in chronic Pa but lower lung function in Pa negative pwCF. The antibiotic-associated treatment effect on lung function decreased over the three eras of CFTR-modulators.
sections.Conclusion: Whereas Pa negative pwCF had a higher risk of treatment emergent infections they had the least benefit. For chronic Pa infection the antibiotic-associated benefit decreased as CFTR-modulator use increased.

sections.Objectives: Phage therapy shows promise against antibiotic-resistant infections, but host immune responses may compromise efficacy. We aimed to describe the adaptive immune response to phage therapy and investigate its potential impact on treatment outcomes.
sections.Methods: A 14-year-old CF patient ineligible for CFTR modulators received three cycles of nebulized phages C4001 & C4004 with systemic antibiotics for chronic pan-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa respiratory infection. We assessed serum and sputum phage pharmacokinetics, neutralization activity, and phage-specific IgA, IgG, and IgM antibodies.
sections.Results: Initial improvements in dyspnea and sputum production during cycles 1-2 did not persist, and Pseudomonas titers remained unchanged despite no obvious phage resistance. Phages were detected in sputum up to two weeks post-cycle 1 but became markedly reduced or undetectable in subsequent cycles, including within 30 minutes of administration during cycle 3. Serum neutralization against C4001 appeared one month post-treatment, intensifying during cycle 2 and achieving complete neutralization by cycle 3. For C4004, neutralization developed rapidly by day 8 of first exposure, reaching complete neutralization during the subsequent cycle. Critically, phage-specific serum and sputum IgA, IgG, and IgM antibodies were already pressent at baseline, with titers increasing during each cycle and persisting throughout follow-up.
sections.Conclusion: In this case, host immune response substantially limited phage therapy efficacy, representing a critical barrier to successful treatment. Rapid neutralizing antibody development against multiple phages fundamentally challenges current treatment protocols. These findings call for careful protocol design regarding treatment duration, sequential versus concomitant phage administration, and repeated exposure avoidance. Understanding baseline neutralizing antibody prevalence is essential for patient selection and warrants further investigation.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 04 - From calories to composition: the new story of nutrition in cystic fibrosis

sections.Objectives: ETI has significantly altered the nutritional trajectory of cwCF. Rollout has outpaced the development of nutritional guidance. This study aimed to: 1. systematically review the literature on ETI and nutrition in cwCF 2. develop expert consensus-based guidance 3. highlight research gaps.
sections.Methods: This project was registered on PROSPERO (ID: 587618). A global panel of 22 dietitians, gastroenterologists, respirologists, endocrinologists, and a parent representative was convened. Research questions were developed using the PICO framework across multiple domains including anthropometry, body composition, vitamins, minerals, cholesterol, diet, endocrine and exocrine pancreatic status and ETI administration. A literature search was conducted across Medline, Embase, CINAHL, and Cochrane (up to and including April 2025). Studies were screened, assessed using SIGN criteria, and summarised in evidence tables. Draft statements were refined through iterative discussion, with final consensus achieved (≥80% agreement) using a real-time Delphi.
sections.Results: In total 67 statements reached consensus. Here we present statements in the subsection related to diet and ETI. In the absence of direct evidence, all statements were designed as Good Practice Points (GPP), informed by the collective clinical expertise of the panel.
sections.Conclusion: These GPP’s provide interim guidance for cwCF on ETI. Prospective studies are urgently needed to define optimal energy requirements, macronutrient composition, and dietary patterns. Children with CF should focus on nutrient-dense, minimally processed, fibre-rich diets that emphasise fruits, vegetables, legumes, whole grains, nuts, and lean protein. GPP94.74%Focus on monounsaturated and preferably polyunsaturated fats while minimising saturated and trans fats. GPP 100%Minimise processed meats. GPP 90%Red meat should be consumed in alignment with population-level dietary recommendations. GPP 100%Prioritise lean, minimally processed protein sources. GPP 90%Minimise refined carbohydrates and sugar-sweetened beverages. GPP 90%Choose wholegrain carbohydrates. GPP 100%Consume fibre in line with population recommendations. GPP 100%Assess energy requirements individually, considering lung function, pancreatic status, and overall growth needs. Dietitian-led assessment and ongoing monitoring are recommended. GPP 100%For cwCF who continue to have elevated energy requirements, nutritional intake should be optimised to meet needs while maintaining diet quality. High energy needs can be met through nutrient dense foods rather than relying on energy-dense, nutrient-poor options. GPP100%

sections.Objectives: Pregnancies among people with cystic fibrosis (PwCF) have increased in the CFTR modulator (CFTRm) era. However, pregnant PwCF were excluded from CFTRm trials, limiting evidence on offspring outcomes after in utero and breast milk exposure. Follow-up practices for exposed children vary, and no widely accepted recommendations exist. We aimed to develop the first international recommendations for clinical follow-up of children exposed to CFTRm in utero and/or via breastfeeding.
sections.Methods: An international survey of follow-up practices and a literature review informed item generation for a Delphi consensus survey. We recruited panellists from four clinician groups (paediatric CF, adult CF, non-CF physicians, and other healthcare professionals) and a group of PwCF. An a priori consensus threshold was set at ≥70% overall agreement and ≥50% agreement within each group, with up to three survey rounds. The Imperial College Research Ethics Committee granted ethical approval. The protocol was prospectively registered (DOI:10.17605/OSF.IO/VJ2Z8).
sections.Results: Completed responses were submitted by 106, 101 and 107 panellists in rounds 1–3, respectively, representing 26 countries. Clinician panellists had a median of 18 years’ clinical experience (IQR 10–25). Eight PwCF participated. Consensus was reached on 73 items, including follow-up setting, liver function monitoring, cataract screening, and potential false-negative CF newborn screening. In total, we received 941 free-text comments which we used to refine existing items and generate new ones for subsequent rounds.
sections.Conclusion: International consensus recommendations are now available to support consistent, implementable follow-up of children exposed to CFTRm in utero and/or via breastfeeding, adaptable to local practice.

sections.Objectives: To define a subgroup of short-term ppFEV1 decliners after elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) and to evaluate their long-term ppFEV1 trajectories.
sections.Methods: Using the European Cystic Fibrosis Society Patient Registry, we evaluated pwCF initiating ETI and having lung function data from 1 year pre ETI to 4 years post ETI. Short term decline (−1 to +1 year) was defined with variability anchored metrics: reduction of 1 point in FEV1Q in adults and conditional change in FEV1Z score < -1.96 in children. Multivariable logistic regression assessed predictors of pwCF with short-term decline. Long-term (−1 to +4 year) trajectories of ppFEV1 were summarized using piecewise mixed-effect model, adjusted for demographics and clinical factors.
sections.Results: At 1 year, short term ppFEV1 decline was uncommon (340/9,980, 3.4% children and 239/14,019, 1.7% adults). In children, decline was less likely among those aged 14–18 years (OR 0.32, 95% CI 0.19–0.53), those with more IV antibiotic days (1–14 vs 0: OR 0.70, 95% CI 0.52–0.93), and males (OR 0.58, 95% CI 0.48–0.70; all p<0.0001). In adults, older age (OR 0.76, 95% CI 0.68–0.86), higher baseline ppFEV1 (OR 0.16, 95% CI 0.12–0.21), and more IV antibiotic days (15–365 vs 0: OR 0.44, 95% CI 0.31–0.61; all p<0.0001) were associated with lower odds of decline. The adjusted mean (95%CI) of long-term change depends on baseline ppFEV1. In children, it was 15.7 (9.7 to 21.7) for <40%, and 3.0 (1.5 to 4.5) for >90%; in adults, 8.3 (7.6 to 9.0) for <40%, and 3.9 (2.9 to 4.8) for >90%. Sensitivity analyses in 116 decliners with 4 years follow-up showed that apparent short-term decline often reflected delayed benefit following a transient decline: most pwCF (100/116, 86.2%), reached the expected 1 year post ETI value at ≥ 2 years after starting ETI.
sections.Conclusion: Although a small subgroup meets criteria for short term ppFEV1 decline, most improve or stabilize over longer follow-up, underscoring the need for long-term assessment.

presentations.summary: Objectives: Bacteriophages (phages) – viruses that selectively infect bacteria – are a promising option for personalized therapy of CF infections. Clinical implementation in many countries worldwide, however, faces multiple hurdles, including a lack of consensus on general principles for phage therapy, infrastructural requirements, procedures for quality-assured phage selection and preparation, clinical administration, monitoring, and documentation. These gaps impede safe and transparent clinical use and effective regulatory over...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1University Hospital Frankfurt, Goethe University Frankfurt, Department of Internal Medicine II, Infectious Diseases, Frankfurt am Main, Germany, 2Faculty of Medicine and University Hospital of Cologne, University of Cologne, Department I of Internal Medicine, Division of Infectious Diseases, Cologne, Germany

sections.Objectives: Bacteriophages (phages) – viruses that selectively infect bacteria – are a promising option for personalized therapy of CF infections. Clinical implementation in many countries worldwide, however, faces multiple hurdles, including a lack of consensus on general principles for phage therapy, infrastructural requirements, procedures for quality-assured phage selection and preparation, clinical administration, monitoring, and documentation. Existing guidance provides limited practical direction across the entire translational pathway and lacks inspection-ready specifications to support both pharmacies and clinical sites. These gaps impede safe and transparent clinical use and effective regulatory oversight. Likewise, there are no established processes to identify research questions that will be key to advancing clinical phage research in the future.
sections.Methods: To address these needs, this consensus-based guideline was developed within the methodological framework of the Association of the Scientific Medical Societies of Germany. It was created through a collaborative effort involving 20 professional societies, 18 international experts, patient advocacy groups, and regulatory authorities.
sections.Results: The guideline provides over 60 recommendations on core principles, infrastructure, preparation and quality control, administration, and future research. Recommendations have been endorsed by leading international societies and stakeholders.
sections.Conclusion: By providing clear and practice-oriented recommendations, this consensus statement paves the way for the safe and standardized use of personalized phage therapy.

presentations.title: A multi-centre randomised non-inferiority trial of withdrawal of nebulised muco-active therapies in people with CF on regular elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI); the CF STORM study

presentations.session_title: Workshop 05 - Further evolving our understanding of ETI impacts

sections.Objectives: To determine, for people with CF on regular ETI, if reducing treatment burden by stopping daily nebulised muco-active therapies results in a change in spirometry at 52 weeks.
sections.Methods: CF STORM (ISRCTN, 14081521) was a multicentre open-labelled parallel registry-randomised controlled trial. People with CF, aged over 6 years, established on ETI and at least one daily nebulised muco-active therapy were enrolled across 40 UK CF centres, and randomised 1:1 by a central system (registry based) using computer generated tables, to either STOP or CONTINUE muco-active nebulised treatments (dornase alfa, hypertonic saline, or both) for 52 weeks. The trial was designed to determine that change from baseline in the primary outcome (ppFEV1) would be less than the pre-defined non-inferiority margin of 3%. Secondary clinical outcomes included hospital admissions, pulmonary exacerbations and quality of life.
sections.Results: 591 people (STOP = 297, CONTINUE = 294) were randomised (Mean age (SD), 21.7 (13.2) years, ppFEV1 89.4 (18.2) at baseline). The STOP and CONTINUE groups were well matched at baseline. 201 participants were excluded from the primary analysis with data collected outside protocol-defined windows, leaving 390 (STOP = 195, CONTINUE = 195) for the ITT analysis. The mean difference (STOP – CONTINUE) in ppFEV1 at 52 weeks was 0.97 (95% CI, -0.51 to 2.45). A pre-determined sensitivity analysis using expanded time periods was consistent with the primary analysis (STOP = 270, CONTINUE = 258), mean difference 1.06 (95% CI, -0.31 to 2.43). Safety data were comparable across the two arms.
sections.Conclusion: Discontinuation of muco-active nebulised therapy for people with CF established on ETI was not shown to be inferior to continuation with respect to change in respiratory function at 52 weeks. Analysis of secondary outcomes will be presented at the conference and will provide further important context. Funding: NIHR UK [HTA 131889]. GD is funded by UKRI [MR/T041285/1].

presentations.session_title: Workshop 06 - Integrating mental health and neurocognitive care in contemporary cystic fibrosis practice

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has substantially improved pulmonary function and survival for people with cystic fibrosis. While ETI is generally well tolerated, post-marketing reports and observational data have described neuropsychiatric adverse effects, including anxiety, depression, sleep disturbance, and mood changes, in a subset of patients. In randomized clinical trials comparing ETI with vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaftor (VTD), rates of reported neuropsychiatric adverse events did not differ significantly between treatment groups, although such trials may be limited in detecting patient-specific intolerance or delayed effects. Given the unique components and distinct pharmacokinetic properties of VTD, it remains possible that individual patients who do not tolerate ETI may experience improved mental health outcomes after switching therapy. Our aim was to assess neuropsychiatric tolerability of VTD in patients with documented ETI-related neuropsychiatric adverse effects.
sections.Methods: We performed a single-center retrospective chart review of people with cystic fibrosis prescribed VTD following mental health–related adverse drug reactions to ETI. Demographic and clinical data were collected. Mental health outcomes included changes in PHQ-9 and GAD-7 scores before and after transition to VTD, when available, and clinician-documented patient-reported symptom changes. Descriptive statistics were used for analysis.
sections.Results: As of January 2026, 106 patients have been prescribed VTD, with 86 patients currently on therapy. Of these 86 patients, 23 reported ETI-related neuropsychiatric adverse effects as one of the primary reasons for their desire to switch to VTD. Of these 23 patients, 10 have had confirmed improvement in their mental health symptoms.
sections.Conclusion: Transition from ETI to VTD was associated with improvement in mental health symptoms among patients with prior ETI-related neuropsychiatric adverse effects

presentations.session_title: Workshop 07 - Physiotherapy in the CFTR modulator era: integrating exercise capacity, strength, sleep and daily participation

sections.Objectives: Characterise thermoregulatory and perceptual responses to exercise in the heat in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) on Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) versus healthy controls, as well as patient-reported experiences and hydration practices. ​
sections.Methods: Mixed-methods design: i) laboratory study where pwCF (n=6) and healthy controls (n=6) completed two 30-minute cycling bouts in the heat (35°C, 30% relative humidity) at metabolic heat productions of 150 W·m⁻² and 250 W·m⁻². Measures: whole-body and local sweat rate, deep body (Tbody) and skin temperature (Tskin), forearm and finger skin blood flow (SkBF), heart rate (HR), sweat sodium chloride concentration ([Na⁺Cl⁻]), and perceptual responses (exertion, thermal sensation and comfort, thirst, and heat-illness symptom index (HISI)); ii) global e-survey (n=30) exploring free-living sweating, thermoregulation and hydration/salt replacement practices during exercise and heat exposure.
sections.Results: In the laboratory, no between-group differences were observed for sweat [Na⁺Cl⁻] (pwCF: 75 (59–92) mmol·L⁻¹, controls: 45 (36–106) mmol·L⁻¹; P=0.45), sweating rate, Tbody or Tskin, HR, SkBF, perceived exertion, thermal sensation or comfort, or HISI (all P>0.05). PwCF reported greater thirst at baseline (P=0.045) and after 30 minutes of exercise in the heat (P=0.01), despite similar hydration status. Survey data suggested that some pwCF perceive altered sweating and salt loss on ETI, though salt-supplementation practices are mostly unchanged.
sections.Conclusion: PwCF on ETI show thermophysiological and perceptual responses to exercise in the heat similar to healthy controls, suggesting reduced heat vulnerability via historical mechanisms. However, emerging cardiovascular abnormalities may confer risk through other pathways. Updated, evidence-informed guidance on hydration and salt intake is needed to support safe exercise without increasing cardiovascular risk. Funding: Cystic Fibrosis Warriors Charity (Charity No. 1178063).

sections.Objectives: Meconium ileus represents one of the most severe neonatal manifestations of cystic fibrosis (CF) and is associated with substantial morbidity. Surgical intervention, including procedures such as ileostomy and end-to-end anastomosis, is frequently required and is followed by an increased risk of gastro-intestinal complications. This study aimed to evaluate whether early off-label administration of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) is associated with improved post-operative clinical and gastro-intestinal outcomes.
sections.Methods: A retrospective cohort study was conducted including children with CF treated surgically for meconium ileus at Wilhelmina Children's Hospital between 2007 and 2025. Four patients received post-operative off-label low dose ETI, while seven patients did not. Clinical and gastro-intestinal outcomes were compared between both groups during hospital admissions in the first year of life.
sections.Results: The most notable results were observed in clinical outcomes. The median duration of NICU admission for ETI versus non-ETI was 2 versus 9 days (IQR 5.25 vs. 22.5). The median total length of hospital stay for ETI versus non-ETI was 38 versus 55 days (IQR 8.25 vs. 52.5). All ETI patients and four non-ETI patients underwent ileostomy surgery, with a median time to ileostomy reversal surgery of 88 versus 94 days respectively (IQR 21.5 vs. 40.8). Lastly, the median duration of total parenteral nutrition (TPN) for ETI versus non-ETI was 14 versus 18 days (IQR 9.5 vs. 56.5). No side effects of ETI were reported.
sections.Conclusion: Off-label low dose ETI as additive therapy for meconium ileus poses careful positive effects on clinical outcomes. Of course, interpretation of these findings is limited by the small sample size and the retrospective design. These preliminary findings support the need for further investigation.

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has markedly improved lung function in people with cystic fibrosis (CF), raising questions about the need for twice-daily inhalation and airway clearance therapy. This study evaluated the effects of reducing inhalation and airway clearance from twice to once daily in children with CF receiving ETI.
sections.Methods: Children aged 6–17 years at the paediatric CF centres in Gothenburg and Uppsala, treated with ETI for ≥9 months and performing inhalation and airway clearance twice daily, were offered reduction to once daily for 12 months when clinically stable. Outcomes included spirometry, lung clearance index (LCI), airway microbiology, antibiotic use, and respiratory symptoms. Data were obtained from the Swedish National CF Registry (December 2022–November 2025). Mixed-effects models compared outcomes during twice- versus once-daily therapy, adjusting for repeated measures, missing data, antibiotic use, and airway colonisation.
sections.Results: A total of 44 of 53 (83%) eligible participants were included. Mean (SD) age was 12.3 (3.3) years. Participants were followed for a mean of 12 (1.5) months during twice-daily and 12 (1.4) months during once-daily therapy. Mean FEV₁% predicted was 99.4 (95% CI 94.8 to 104.0) during twice-daily therapy and 98.5 (95% CI 94.0 to 103.1) during once-daily therapy, with an adjusted mean difference of −1.1 FEV1%-points (95% CI −2.8 to 0.6; p=0.22). Mean LCI decreased from 6.6 (95% CI 6.5 to 6.8) to 6.3 (95% CI 6.2 to 6.5), with an adjusted mean difference of −0.2 (95% CI −0.4 to −0.1; p<0.001). Mean annual total antibiotic use decreased from 45.0 days (95% CI 34.5 to 58.8) to 27.7 days (95% CI 21.1 to 36.2; p<0.001) per year.
sections.Conclusion: In children with CF treated with ETI, reducing inhalation and airway clearance therapy to once daily was associated with lower LCI and reduced antibiotic use.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 06 - Integrating mental health and neurocognitive care in contemporary cystic fibrosis practice

sections.Objectives: Since the introduction of CFTR modulators, there have been increasing reports of psychological and neurocognitive side effects, particularly with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI). A UK drug safety update in May 2025 highlighted infrequent reports of psychological side effects including anxiety, low mood, sleep disturbance, poor concentration, and forgetfulness. While these risks have been formally documented for ETI, they may also apply to other modulators. The European Cystic Fibrosis Society (ECFS) Standards of Care recommend screening in accordance with CF mental health guidelines prior to and within three months of commencing modulators. The UK rollout of vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaftor (VTD) has provided an opportunity to implement and evaluate a more proactive, coordinated approach to screening.
sections.Methods: The adult psychology subgroup of the UK Psychosocial Professionals in CF (UKPPCF) developed recommendations for a consistent, nationwide approach to psychological screening for those starting VTD, allowing flexibility for service-level variation while aiming to capture the variety of potential side effects. Suggested screening measures included: General Mental Health Concerns Side Effects Related to Modulators (GEMS-ModSE); Generalised Anxiety Disorder (GAD) and Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ); Patient Reported Outcome Measurement Information System (PROMIS) Cognitive Function. Five UK adult CF centres came together to review the implementation and utility of these recommendations.
sections.Results: To date, 187 individuals across the five centres have been screened prior to VTD initiation. 3-month follow-up measures have been completed for 10 patients, with screening ongoing.
sections.Conclusion: It is hoped that a coordinated, systematic screening approach will help provide a useful framework for early detection of possible psychological side effects linked to CFTR modulators. This can be used to support clinical discussions and decision making.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 07 - Physiotherapy in the CFTR modulator era: integrating exercise capacity, strength, sleep and daily participation

sections.Objectives: We aimed to track longitudinal changes in aerobic capacity after ETI initiation in paediatric and adult CFwith patients (pwCF) and identify key determinants of VO₂ max, including lung function and muscular conditioning.
sections.Methods: Retrospective, longitudinal study of paediatric and adult pwCF eligible for ETI at the CF Centre of Montpellier. All included pwCF underwent ≥2 CPETs, with ≥1 pre-ETI. Individual VO₂ max trajectories before and after ETI were analysed using mixed linear regression models. Detailed CPET and plethysmography data were collected.
sections.Results: 115 pwCF were included. Before ETI, VO₂ max declined at −0.88 ml/min/kg/year (95 % CI [−1.11; −0.65], p < 0.001). ETI significantly modified this trajectory, with a slope change of +1.93 ml/min/kg/year (95 % CI [1.26; 2.60], p < 0.001), yielding a positive slope of +1.05 ml/min/kg/year. Lung function improved, with FEV₁ increasing by +9.74 % (95 % CI [7.08; 12.40], p < 0.001). VO₂ at the first ventilatory threshold (VT1) declined pre-ETI (−0.60 %/year, p = 0.035) and improved after ETI (+2.15 %/year, 95 % CI [0.51; 3.79], p = 0.010), reflecting better submaximal exercise tolerance. Marked inter-individual variability was observed.
sections.Conclusion: ETI improves aerobic capacity and overall respiratory and muscular function in CF, yet individual VO₂ max responses vary widely. Identifying physiological drivers of this variability could reveal biomarkers to guide targeted rehabilitation alongside modulator therapy. These findings reinforce the essential role of CPET and VO₂ max for comprehensive monitoring in the modulator era.

sections.Objectives: The terminal ileum (TI) is commonly impacted in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF), with earlier surgical reports describing a dilated TI, filled with clay-like material. However, quantitative data on TI dimensions in pwCF is limited. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) enables non-invasive assessment of the small bowel morphology, though no data on TI diameters in pwCF is available. This study aimed to: (1) measure the diameter of the distal TI in pwCF and healthy volunteers (HVs) using MRI; (2) test the hypothesis that the TI diameter is increased in pwCF compared to HVs.
sections.Methods: MRI imaging of the TI was completed in 26 adult pwCF (23 on CFTR modulator therapy) and 30 adult HVs. A commercial image analysis platform (Entrolytics, Motilent, UK) was used to measure long and short axes diameters on cross-sectional images, along the most distal 5cm of the TI. Cross sectional area of the TI was also calculated assuming an elliptical shape.
sections.Results: (mean ±SEM) The TI diameter was successfully measured in all participants. pwCF had significantly larger TI dimensions compared with HVs. This was true for both the long axis TI diameter (2.9±0.1cm for pwCF versus 1.6±0.1cm for HVs, p<0.0001), the short axis TI diameter (2.2±0.1cm for pwCF versus 1.2±0.1cm for HVs, p<0.0001) and the TI cross-sectional area (4.9±0.5cm2 for pwCF versus 1.6±0.1cm2 for HVs, p<0.0001).
sections.Conclusion: The axial diameters of the TI of pwCF were approximately 80% larger than in HVs, consistent with historical surgical observations and despite modulator therapy. Increased chyme viscosity and/or impaired motility may lead to accumulation of chyme in the terminal ileum in pwCF. Such MRI methods may provide further understanding of the physiopathology of the TI in pwCF. New treatments correcting these abnormalities such as secretagogues could also be evaluated.

presentations.title: Assessing CFTR modulator efficacy via urine bicarbonate excretion following a short therapy interruption
presentations.summary: Objectives: Renal CFTR function is essential for urinary HCO3- excretion and is markedly impaired in people with CF (pwCF). sections.Objectives: Renal CFTR function is essential for urinary HCO3- excretion and is markedly impaired in people with CF (pwCF). Urinary HCO3- excretion following an oral NaHCO3 load may serve as a simple in vivo biomarker of CFTR function, reflecting both the extent of CFTR dysfunction and therapy-induced rescue. This study investigates challenged urine HCO3- excretion in pwCF treated with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI), before, during, and after a short therapy interruption, and examines the association with venous acid-base parameters and ETI plasma concentrations.
sections.Methods: PwCF underwent a 90-min oral NaHCO3- challenge test before, during, and after a randomized ETI interruption (12, 36, or 60 hours). Baseline urine and venous blood samples, and post-challenge urine were collected at each visit. Urine and venous acid-base parameters, and ETI plasma concentrations were measured. Currently, 24 pwCF have completed all three visits, and 27 have completed the first two visits. In addition, seventeen healthy control participants completed the challenged urine test.
sections.Results: Mean challenged urine HCO3- excretion was markedly lower in pwCF compared to healthy controls (3.5 vs. 11.2 mmol/90 min.). In pwCF, ETI interruption was associated with a reduced challenged urine HCO3- excretion (∆: 1.16 mmol/90 min., 95% CI: 0.2 to 2.1) and a concomitant increase in plasma base excess (∆: 0.52 mmol/L, 95% CI: -0.02 to 1.07). Higher pre-challenge plasma base excess was associated with a 27% higher urine HCO3- excretion per mmol/L, whereas higher baseline spot urine NH4 was associated with a 53% lower HCO3- excretion per 2-fold increase. No significant differences were found across pause lengths. ETI plasma concentration analyses are pending.
sections.Conclusion: Challenged urine HCO₃- excretion is highly sensitive to short-term ETI therapy changes, supporting its dynamic reflection of CFTR function and its use as an in vivo biomarker in pwCF.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 05 - Further evolving our understanding of ETI impacts

sections.Objectives: Without CFTR modulators, cystic fibrosis (CF) is associated with an accelerated decline in percent predicted forced expiratory volume in one second (ppFEV1), at a rate of approximately -1.5% per year. Although treatment with elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor (ETI) initially increases ppFEV1, little is known about its impact on subsequent FEV1 decline. Recent analyses of the extension phases of the Phase 3 ETI pivotal studies (Daines et al., AJRCCM, 2025) found no evidence of ppFEV1 decline over four years in patients aged 12 and older who were treated with ETI. Our objectives was to examine the decline in ppFEV1 in a large real-world cohort of adults with CF.
sections.Methods: Data from adults (≥18 years) with CF included in the French prospective national observational ETI study were analyzed over 3 years. Eligible participants were those with at least 3 measures of ppFEV1 between 3 months and 3 years following ETI initiation. The reference ppFEV1 was the best ppFEV1 during the first 3 months of ETI treatment and subsequent decline in ppFEV1 was analyzed using a linear mixed-model effect.
sections.Results: The analysis was based on data from 2321 adults with CF (male, 54.4%; median [IQR] age, 29.2 [23.1;36.7]) ; ppFEV1 prior to ETI initiation 59.7 [39.8;79.1]) treated with ETI for three years. Following ETI initiation, ppFEV1 increased by a median [IQR] + 14.5 [7.9;22.7]% within the first 3 months. The subsequent mean (95% confidence interest) decline in ppFEV1 was -0.70 (-0.83 ;-0.57) per year. No significant difference in ppFEV1 decline was found by age quartiles or sex at birth; ppFEV1 decline appeared greater in participants with higher ppFEV1.
sections.Conclusion: Our results suggest a persistent yet reduced ppFEV1 decline in adults with CF who are treated with ETI. Subgroup analyses will clarify the impact of these findings, particularly for individuals with advanced lung disease.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 06 - Integrating mental health and neurocognitive care in contemporary cystic fibrosis practice

sections.Objectives: CF-CBT is an 8-session intervention to address depression and anxiety in adults with CF (AWCF). Between 2022-25, >200 US and Canadian CF clinicians completed an 8-hour CF-CBT training. We conducted a focus group examining clinician experiences of CF-CBT implementation as part of a mixed-methods randomized implementation trial.
sections.Methods: Nine CF-CBT trained clinicians participated in a focus group in July 2025: 8 (89%) women, 7 (78%) white, 8 (89%) heterosexual; 7 (78%) social workers, 1 psychologist, 1 nurse practitioner; working in CF 0-17 years. The focus group was audio recorded, transcribed, and analyzed in NVivo 14 with a codebook based on the RE-AIM (Reach, Effectiveness, Adoption, Implementation, Maintenance) framework (Table 1).
sections.Results: Clinicians endorsed positive outcomes for AWCF and families, institutional support, group and individual consultation provided by the CF-CBT team, and professional satisfaction (Table 1). Clinicians suggested additional resources such as skills handouts to augment the patient workbook. Several clinicians voiced support for making an asynchronous online CF-CBT training available for new clinicians and to help trained clinicians refresh their CF-CBT delivery skills.
sections.Conclusion: CF-CBT implementation has positively impacted AWCF, clinicians, and institutions. Clinician feedback guided development of the online CF-CBT training and will inform the creation of additional resources to support teams implementing CF-CBT. Funding: This work was supported by the CF Foundation [FRIEDM22K0] Table 1. Key themes and exemplar quotes from CF-CBT clinician focus group RE-AIM ThemeSubthemeExemplar QuoteReach: How do I reach those who need this intervention? Process of identifying and approaching AWCF for CF-CBT“I do the mental health screening and if they have mild or moderate scores, I offer the CBT to them…But even if they have [lower scores]…but they talk about feeling anxious or feeling frustrated at work or just not coping very well, I’m starting to offer it to more patients, not just relying on the number.”Challenges in engagement“The main place I’ve been having push back is patients that are wanting in person visits. We don’t offer that because of the infection controls.”Effectiveness: How do I know my intervention is working? Positives for AWCF“A patient…verbalized to me that the CF-CBT was really helpful in getting her through this past hospitalization…the new perspective of looking at hospitalizations, how to be prepared, how to look at her automatic negative thinking.”Positives for families and caregivers“My patient had mentioned that his grandmother…noticed that he had become much more social. That was a big driving force for him to choose to continue to participate after the initial sessions.” Adoption: How do I develop organizational support to deliver my intervention? Perceptions of CF-CBT implementation from institutions“Our clinic director and team are all extremely supportive and see CF CBT as a welcome and needed part of our mental health care of patients.”Center / institutional barriers for CF-CBT implementation"Unfortunately, there is not the institutional support around extra time or protected time for this work...Also, private space is always an issue.”Implementation: How do I ensure the intervention is delivered properly? Effect of CF-CBT implementation on clinicians“The training had really lifted me out a feeling of being burnt out...I find this to be very rewarding...It's really helped me to feel definitely more eager to show up to work and be more involved in [patients’] emotional well-being in a more meaningful way.”Experience with CF-CBT consultation “I feel something ongoing is really great. It's all in the group setting. It's always great to connect with colleagues, having different experiences, and cross pollinate, and then have the facilitator there with their expertise.”Sessions requiring more training or information“For session 7...I never quite found like the right way to get through it cohesively, though that was something that we talked about a fair amount in my consultation group as well.”Maintenance: How do I incorporate the intervention, so it is delivered over the long term?Enduring CF-CBT training materials“I would appreciate [the online CF-CBT training] too. Being able to go back and hear a little bit, be reminded of the trainings, or seeing actual role plays of what it looks like could be useful and helpful.”Additional CF-CBT resources“It would be nice to have things like cheat sheets…that summarizes some of the ideas that you can send home with [patients]. Like some of the automatic negative thinking ideas, thinking traps, things like that.”

presentations.session_title: Workshop 07 - Physiotherapy in the CFTR modulator era: integrating exercise capacity, strength, sleep and daily participation

sections.Objectives: To investigate the relationship between peak exercise capacity and enjoyment of, and motivation for, physical activity (PA) and exercise participation (EP) in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF).
sections.Methods: 56 pwCF (31f/26m, age 18.3±10.5yrs, ppFEV1 84.8±16.9,) participated. A cycle-ergometry was performed to determine VO2peak and Wpeak. German versions of the Physical Activity Enjoyment Scale (PACES-D) was used to assess PA and EP enjoyment, and the Behavioural Regulation in Exercise Questionnaire Version 2 (BREQ-2-D) assessed motivational aspects. Data were collected during an inpatient rehabilitation course.
sections.Results: While participants showed normal exercise capacity (VO₂peak; 80.1±16.2%pred., Wpeak 85.5±17.5% pred.), they reported high enjoyment of PA and EP (67.0±10.2). Their motivation was characterised by high intrinsic (4.10±1.0) and identified regulation (3.6±0.8) versus low introjected (2.1±0.9), low external regulation (1.7±0.8), and amotivation (1.5±0.7). The Relative Autonomy Index (RAI: 9.58 ± 6.11), a weighted composite of all BREQ-2-D subscales, where higher values indicate stronger autonomous motivation, was notably positive. Regression analysis revealed that adding motivational variables to demographic/clinical factors increased explained variance in PA/EP enjoyment from 18% to 62% (ΔR²=.437, p<.001). Intrinsic motivation (β=.824, p<.001), lung function (β=.722, p=.019) and age (β=.766, p=.015) were found to be significant predictors of enjoyment, while exercise VO₂peak/Wpeak showed no association (ΔR²=.002, p=.931).
sections.Conclusion: PA/EP enjoyment was predicted by intrinsic motivation (strongest predictor), lung function and age but not by exercise capacity. The discrepancy between physical capability and of PA/EP enjoyment highlights psychological factors. Promoting an active lifestyle could be achieved by shifting from exercise testing alone to a more holistic physical literacy assessment which incorporates motivational profiling.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 08 - The hidden burden of cystic fibrosis: liver diseases and the gastrointestinal ecosystem

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor–tezacaftor–ivacaftor (ETI) was recently introduced in the Netherlands, including for persons with advanced cystic fibrosis-related liver disease (aCFLD). This retrospective study examined the potential therapeutic and hepatotoxic effects of ETI in persons with aCFLD.
sections.Methods: We compared persons with aCFLD and those without CF-related hepatic involvement (noCFHBI) upon ETI initiation and up to 12 months thereafter, adjusting for prior CFTR modulator use. Using the Dutch Cystic Fibrosis Foundation registry, we analysed between the two groups the liver enzyme levels and fibrosis indices (APRI and GPR) during the 12 months study period.
sections.Results: We included 85 subjects with aCFLD and 613 noCFHBI controls. A significant transient increase in median aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine transaminase (ALT), and gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) levels was observed in both groups after ETI initiation. Median AST, ALT, and GGT levels were consistently higher in the aCFLD group than in controls at all time points, most pronounced in the first month. Unexpectedly, significant increases in fibrosis indices were observed in the aCFLD group between baseline and 12 months (APRI 0.681 vs. 0.931; GPR 0.807 vs. 1.385; both p<0.05).
sections.Conclusion: Based on fibrosis indices, no hepatic therapeutic improvement of aCFLD was observed 12 months after ETI initiation. Patients with aCFLD demonstrated higher post-ETI elevations in liver enzymes, suggesting a distinct hepatic response to ETI in this group. While longer treatment duration or earlier initiation may still mitigate disease progression, these findings underscore the need for cautious and closely monitored ETI use in patients with aCFLD.

presentations.title: Shared MSD2 binding site but distinct potency of elexacaftor and vanzacaftor in correcting F508del-CFTR
presentations.summary: Many individuals with cystic fibrosis (CF) benefit from Trikafta™, a triple CFTR modulator composed of the correctors: elexacaftor (VX-445) and tezacaftor (VX-661), and the potentiator: ivacaftor (VX-770), a.k.a. ETI). In cryo-EM studies, the two correctors have been shown to bind to two different regions on the F508del-CFTR to act in an additive or synergistic manner to induce near-Wt assembly via allostery. ETI treatment has been found to improve lung function in patients with at least one F508del-CFTR mutation. However, a subset shows limited benefit or adverse effects. presentations.summary: Background & Objectives: We demonstrated (Liessi JCF 2023, Ciobanu JCF 2024) that Tezacaftor inhibits the enzyme (DEGS) which converts dihydroceramides (dHCer) into ceramides (Fabrias Prog.Lip.Res. 2012), thus producing increase of dHCer in cells. sections.Objectives: We demonstrated (Liessi JCF 2023, Ciobanu JCF 2024) that Tezacaftor inhibits the enzyme (DEGS) which converts dihydroceramides (dHCer) into ceramides (Fabrias Prog.Lip.Res. 2012), thus producing increase of dHCer in cells. Since DEGS malfunctioning is often associated with severe neurological impairments linked with aberrant myelin composition and structure (Karsai JCI 2019, Pant JCI 2019), we conducted an in vivo drug safety study, by administering ETI to CD-1 mice during pregnancy and breastfeeding. The aim is to evaluate the potential impact of DEGS inhibition on the formation of the peripheral and central nervous systems (Doretto Plos 2011).
sections.Methods: ETI was incorporated as powder into food, in a high-fat diet regimen. Besides recording weight and size, we also investigated pups' behavior by means of dedicated tests. ETI and dHCer levels as well as changes in the global lipidome were measured by tandem mass spectrometry coupled to liquid chromatography.
sections.Results: Ten days after birth (P10), a significant accumulation of dHCer in the brains of pups born from ETI-fed dams compared to controls occurs. No accumulation was observed in the sciatic nerve of these animals. We also conducted a global lipidomics survey, which revealed other alterations associated with exposure to ETI. During breastfeeding, when exposure to the drug decreases, these alterations revert and virtually disappear at P28, together with other differences in the phenotype and behaviour of the pups observed earlier (Squarzoni JCF 2025).
sections.Conclusion: We have now demonstrated that exposure of dams to ETI induces an increase in dHCer in the pups brain. Despite a few experimental difficulties, which we are overcoming using also a bit of creativity, we are progressing toward a better understanding of the practical implications of these findings. Fundings: Cystic Fibrosis Foundation (grant ARMIRO24G0 to AA), Fondazione per la Ricerca sulla Fibrosi Cistica (grant FFC#2-2024 to LN).

presentations.session_title: Workshop 05 - Further evolving our understanding of ETI impacts

sections.Objectives: Several countries reported a reduction in lung transplants for people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) after elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor (ETI) was introduced. We sought to evaluate how lung transplant indications evolved in relation to CFTR genotypes and the use of ETI.
sections.Methods: All lung transplants performed on pwCF in France between 2015 and 2024 were identified using data from the French Agence de la Biomédecine. Individual-level patient data, including CFTR genotypes and use of ETI at the time of transplant, were obtained from the French CF reference network centers.
sections.Results: Between 2015 and 2024, a total of 538 lung transplants were performed, including 461 first-time transplants and 77 re-transplants. The rate of first-time lung transplant was 13.4 per 1,000 non-transplanted patients/year versus 1.8 per 1,000 non-transplanted patients/year for 2015-2019 (prior to ETI) and 2020-2024 (with ETI), respectively (P<0.0001); the rate of lung re-transplant was 10.2 per 1,000 transplanted patients/year versus 7.7 per 1,000 transplanted patients/year (P=0.70). Treatment with ETI was prescribed prior to transplant in all F508del pwCF who received a first-time transplant since 2021 and in all those with non-F508del responsive variants since 2022. Eleven patients treated with ETI required lung transplant due to adverse effects leading to ETI discontinuation, concomitant severe liver disease or insufficient improvement of respiratory failure.
sections.Conclusion: ETI has reduced, but not eliminated, the need for a first-time lung transplant in pwCF. Indications for a first-time lung transplant persist in a subset of pwCF who respond to ETI and in those with nonresponsive CFTR genotypes. Re-transplant remains an option for selected transplant recipients with chronic lung allograft dysfunction.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 06 - Integrating mental health and neurocognitive care in contemporary cystic fibrosis practice

sections.Objectives: A 5 year follow-up of US participants in The International Depression Epidemiological Study (TIDES) found a significant increase in mortality among people with CF (PWCF) who screened positive for depression, especially those with severe symptoms. We investigated intermediary outcomes that may contribute to this finding.
sections.Methods: We included untransplanted TIDES participants ages 12+ years followed at 45 collaborating US CF care centers who completed depression screening at a stable visit in 2006-2010 and linked clinical characteristics from the CF Foundation Patient Registry. We compared the association of a positive screen with incidence/duration of IV antibiotic treated pulmonary exacerbations (PEx), decline in FEV1, and regular clinic attendance (≥1x/quarter) at 1, 3 and 5 years for PWCF who screened negative vs. positive (HADS≥8 and/or CES-D≥16) and severely positive (HADS≥11 and/or CES-D≥26) for depression. We adjusted for gender, age, FEV1, CF-related diabetes and insurance status.
sections.Results: Of 999 PWCF, 156 screened in the mild-moderate and 95 in the severe range. Those with severe symptoms had an increased risk of PEx at 3 (IRR 1.3, 95%CI 1.1, 1.6) and at 5 years (IRR 1.3, 95%CI 1.1, 1.6); those with mild-moderate symptoms did not evidence a statistically increased risk. PWCF with severe symptoms had an increased number of IV antibiotic days at 1 (7.33, 95%CI 0.1, 14.6), 3 (19.2, 95%CI 2.1, 36.3), and 5 years (28.7, 95%CI 1.8, 55.5). Overall FEV1 decline was greater by 0.53% predicted/year (95%CI .07, .99) in PWCF with severe depressive symptoms. No difference was seen in quarterly outpatient clinic attendance.
sections.Conclusion: PWCF who screen positive for severe but not mild-moderate depression have an increased risk of PEx and IV antibiotic days and an increased rate of FEV1 decline. Previous research suggests an association between depression and poor adherence, but this is not reflected in outpatient clinic attendance. Funding: CFF GEORGI22A1-LAD

presentations.session_title: Workshop 07 - Physiotherapy in the CFTR modulator era: integrating exercise capacity, strength, sleep and daily participation

sections.Objectives: As reduced exercise capacity and muscle weakness are common in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF), and associated with worse life prognosis and quality of life, we aimed to determine the impact of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) on exercise capacity and peripherical muscle strength in pwCF.
sections.Methods: The 6-Minute Walk Test (6MWT), 1-Minute Sit-to-Stand Test (1STST) and measurement of Maximal isometric Voluntary Contraction of the Quadriceps (MVCQ) with a dynamometer were performed before initiating ETI and after one and two years of ETI.
sections.Results: 82 pwCF (≥12y) were recruited. The 6MWT distance increased significantly after one and two years of ETI compared with baseline (605±73m at baseline; 638±75m after one year of ETI, p<0.0001; 641±72m after two years of ETI, p<0.0001). The number of repetitions of 1STST showed a significant enhancement after one and two years of ETI compared with baseline (52 [39-66] at baseline; 55 [45-66] after one year of ETI, p=0.0242; 57 [46-66] after two years of ETI, p=0.0012). MVCQ also showed a significant improvement after one and two years of ETI compared with baseline (64 [44-79Nm] at baseline; 73 [59-90Nm] after one year of ETI, p=0.0037; 79 [65-95Nm] after two years of ETI, p<0.0001). In contrast, no significant change was noticed while comparing results of 6MWT, 1STST and MVCQ between one and two years of ETI.
sections.Conclusion: 6MWT, 1STST and MVCQ improved after one year of ETI and those improvements persisted at two years, suggesting that restoring CFTR function could enhance exercise capacity and quadriceps strength.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 08 - The hidden burden of cystic fibrosis: liver diseases and the gastrointestinal ecosystem

sections.Objectives: Advanced cystic fibrosis liver disease (aCFLD) is a major cause of morbidity and mortality in people with cystic fibrosis (CF). Identification of those at risk of aCFLD may facilitate earlier management and potentially avoid complications. The objective was to validate a previously published predictor model for aCFLD in a prospective dataset.
sections.Methods: This study is a prospective cohort of children with CF, with follow-up conducted every 5 years. Liver-focused data are collected at each visit. Primary outcome is the development of aCFLD. The prediction algorithm from the PUSH study (Siegel et al. 2023) was applied to this cohort. Model performance was assessed using measures of discrimination (AUC) and calibration plots, followed by model updating and internal validation using bootstrapping.
sections.Results: The analysis cohort comprised 517 patients: 56% M, median age 7yrs(IQR 3,12). 29 developed aCFLD over the 10-year period. Patients who developed aCFLD had higher baseline levels of AST, ALT, GGT, GGT to Platelet Ratio (GPR), and AST to Platelet Ratio Index (APRI) versus those who did not develop aCFLD. External validation of the model yielded an AUC of 0.712(95% CI:0.66-0.764). Calibration assessment demonstrated poor calibration, with an intercept of -4.926(95% CI:-5.371-4.481) and a slope of 0.423(95% CI:0.21-0.636). The updated model showed improved performance, with an apparent AUC of 0.745(95% CI:0.717-0.773) and an optimism-corrected AUC of 0.729(95% CI:0.63-0.828). Calibration was substantially improved, with an intercept of -0.002(95% CI:-0.393-0.389) and a slope of 1(95% CI:0.565-1.435), both of which showed minimal change following internal validation.
sections.Conclusion: This study externally validated and updated a previously published prediction model for aCFLD. The updated model demonstrated moderate discrimination and excellent calibration, supporting utility for early risk stratification and targeted monitoring of children at risk for aCFLD.
