presentations.session_title: Workshop 09 - Deciphering the mechanism(s) of action of modulators

sections.Objectives: To investigate whether elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) improves hippocampal function in humanized cystic fibrosis (CF) mice, by modulating chloride homeostasis and synaptic activity. Our study provides a potential insight for the neuropsychiatric symptoms reported in people with CF.
sections.Methods: Hippocampal tissue was obtained from humanized CFTR mice (male, 13-18 weeks) expressing either wild-type CFTR (hWT) or homozygous F508del CFTR (hCF). ETI exposure was either in vitro (48 hours) or in vivo (4 days). We performed patch-clamp electrophysiology on cultured neurons and hippocampal slices to assess synaptic function, plasticity, chloride homeostasis, and neurotransmission in response to excitatory (AMPA, NMDA) and inhibitory (GABA) stimulation. Relevant protein quantification (GluA1, NKCC1, KCC2) and hippocampal slice histology was also performed.
sections.Results: In the hippocampus of hCF mice we found impaired synaptic strength and excitation, accompanied by elevated intracellular chloride concentration. The ETI treatment restored normal hippocampal functions. Protein analysis suggested postsynaptic weakness was mediated by reduced phosphorylation of the GluA1 AMPA subunit and decreased activity of the KCC2 cotransporter.
sections.Conclusion: Our findings uncovered a CNS phenotype in hCF mice, which was highlighted by impaired hippocampal synaptic function and altered chloride homeostasis. The observed phenotype may potentially be the direct result of CFTR dysfunction, as ETI treatment was able to restore synaptic and chloride homeostasis disruption in hCF mice toward hWT levels. Therefore, these results provide the comprehension of CNS involvement in CF and the need for more investigation into the neurological effects of ETI therapy.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 05 - Further evolving our understanding of ETI impacts

sections.Objectives: The aim of this study was to evaluate the effects of highly effective modulator therapy (HEMT) on retinal microvasculature, corneal and ocular surface parameters, and electrophysiological findings.
sections.Methods: This cross-sectional study was conducted between August 2025 and December 2025. Demographic, clinical data, and comprehensive ophthalmologic examinations—including corneal, retinal, and electrophysiological evaluations—were recorded. Patients were categorized according to use of HEMT, and analyses were performed using right eye data.
sections.Results: Patients charecteristics are given in Table 1. Best-corrected visual acuity was logMAR 0 in all patients. Based on non-invasive tear breakup time (NIBUT) measurements, 76.2% of patients had normal tear film stability (≥10s), 11.9% had borderline values (5–9s), and 11.9% exhibited tear film instability (NIBUT<5 s), with no significant differences between the groups. Meibography score, endothelial cell density, and central corneal thickness were not different between the groups. Optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) analysis showed significantly higher superficial foveal vessel density in patients receiving HEMT (p=0.014), whereas parafoveal and deep capillary plexus parameters did not. Increased retinal vascular tortuosity was identified on color fundus photographs in 5 patients (16.7%). Foveal avascular zone (FAZ) area and perimeter (PERIM) values were similar between groups. Electrophysiological assessments revealed trends toward increased Dark-Adapted 0.01 blue electroretinography (ERG) b-wave amplitudes and reduced N95/P50 ratios in the modulator group; however, these changes did not reach statistical significance.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 06 - Integrating mental health and neurocognitive care in contemporary cystic fibrosis practice

sections.Objectives: Neurodiversity refers to differences in brain function and behavioural traits, including conditions such as attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and autism. Emerging studies suggest higher rates in pwCF than in the general population and extra burden/ barriers to healthcare. We developed a care pathway to help navigate support for neurodivergent patients who may experience additional challenges to daily life, function and CF management.
sections.Methods: Since January 2025, 248 patients were seen at annual review clinic and alongside routine assessment, were asked Do you have a diagnosis/ difficulty linked to neurodiversity e.g. ADHD, autism? Patients who responded positively were asked to complete a survey and measures of depression (PHQ9) and anxiety (GAD7), offered screening and dependent on outcome, referred to local diagnostic services and offered psychological support.
sections.Results: Forty (16%; 18 M, 18 F, 3 TG and 1 NB) patients reported neurodiversity. Of these 4 had a diagnosis of ADHD and 2 a diagnosis of autism. Sixteen were screened using the ASRSv1.1 or AQ50 and 13 referred on for diagnostic assessment. A further 7 opted in for psychological support. Thirty (75%) patients reported low mood and anxiety within clinical range (Table 1) compared to just 47% (N=192) of patients not reporting neurodiversity. Notably, mean scores on the PHQ9 (4.9) and GAD7 (4.3) were in the non-clinical range for non-reporters. Survey feedback indicated that neurodiversity has an important bearing on CF healthcare, but it is ‘overshadowed’ and given little or no consideration by CF professionals. Table 1: Scores for patients reporting neurodiversity N=40Age (Years)FEV1 %predPHQ9GAD7Mean26.783.69.8 Mild depression9 Mild anxietyRange17-4429-1100-21 0-19
sections.Conclusion: Around a sixth of patients reported neurodivergence, associated impact on CF care, and poorer mental health. Whilst fewer have a formal diagnosis, screening indicates the likelihood of more receiving a diagnosis in the future. Further work is needed to improve and adapt care for this patient group.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 07 - Physiotherapy in the CFTR modulator era: integrating exercise capacity, strength, sleep and daily participation

sections.Background: Low exercise capacity is associated with increased morbidity and mortality in the general population and people with Cystic fibrosis (pwCF). In previous studies, reduced sleep led to decreased lung functions in pwCF, while sleep disorders were associated with exercise intolerance and pulmonary exacerbations. Our aim was to assess effects of sleep time and disturbed sleep (nocturnal cough and snoring) on exercise capacity using cardio-pulmonary exercise testing (CPET) in young pwCF compared to healthy controls.
sections.Methods: Prospective study assessing sleep time, disturbed sleep (questionnaire) and CPET in a group of young pwCF (pediatric & young adults) compared to healthy controls.
sections.Results: Thirty-two CF patients (mean age 15.9± 4.9 y/o, 18 males, BMI%tile 47.0±26.9) and ninety-nine healthy controls were evaluated. FEV1, peak oxygen uptake (peak VO2) and anaerobic threshold (AT) were lower in pwCF vs. controls (FEV1 75.2±22.4 vs. 96.9±13.2%, p=0.001; 83.2±20.9 vs. 98.1± 19.2%, p=0.001; 40.6±11.8 vs. 47.4±11.8%, p=0.007, respectively). Sleeping hours were similar between the groups (CF: 7.95±1.6, controls: 8.1±1.4, p=0.7), and a higher proportion of pwCF reported nocturnal cough (18.8% CF vs. 5.1% controls, p=0.015). In the control group, sleep time was correlated with peak VO2 (specific (ml/kg/min) and % pred: r=0.248, p=0.016 and r=0.212., p=0.035, respectively). In CF, sleep time was correlated with AT (r=0.44, p=0.013). PwCF with nocturnal cough had lower FEV1 compared to those without cough (56.8±24.2 vs. 79.4±20.1% pred, respectively).
sections.Conclusion: PwCF demonstrated lower exercise capacity compared to controls, with a positive correlation between sleep time and submaximal exercise (AT); patients with nocturnal cough exhibited lower pulmonary function. Our findings highlight the important effect of sleep time and quality on lung health in PwCF. Further long-term studies will elucidate the effect of improving sleep hygiene on long term outcomes in pwCF.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 08 - The hidden burden of cystic fibrosis: liver diseases and the gastrointestinal ecosystem

sections.Objectives: Gut microbial dysbiosis is common in cystic fibrosis but is typically assessed using non-specific diversity metrics or study‑specific taxonomic signatures, limiting comparability across studies and interventions. We developed a metagenomic, species-level Cystic Fibrosis Microbial Dysbiosis Index (CF-MDI) to enable quantitative assessment of gut microbial imbalance in children with CF (CwCF).
sections.Methods: Stool metagenomic data from the PEARL-CF study (ACTRN12616000797471), including CwCF and age- and gender-matched healthy controls (HC), were analysed using MaAsLin2 to identify taxa enriched or depleted in CF (q<0.05, |coefficient| ≥ 4). CF-MDI was defined as the log10 ratio of summed relative abundances of CF-enriched taxa to those enriched in HC. Reproducibility was evaluated in the independent EARTH cohort (PMID:32295774). Responsiveness to therapeutic modulation was assessed using publicly available metagenomic data from an Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) study (PMID: 38275294).
sections.Results: PEARL-CF data (70 CwCF, 67 HC; 0-6 years) was used to derive CF-MDI. CF-MDI scores were significantly higher in CF than HC (p<0.001), inversely correlated with species richness (ρ= -0.74, p<0.001), and higher in pancreatic insufficient vs sufficient CwCF (p=0.01). In the EARTH cohort (57 CwCF, 56 HC; 0-18 years) key ecological and clinical associations were replicated, confirming robustness. In the ETI study (39 CwCF; 6-18 years), CF-MDI decreased significantly at 6 and 12 months post-ETI, while alpha-diversity metrics, previously reported as unchanged, remained non-significant.
sections.Conclusion: CF-MDI is a robust, CF-specific metric that captures pathophysiologically relevant microbial restructuring not detected by standard diversity measures. CF-MDI differentiates disease and pancreatic status and sensitively tracks therapeutic modulation of the CF gut microbiome, supporting its use as a novel gut microbiome endpoint for monitoring intervention effects in CF trials.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 09 - Deciphering the mechanism(s) of action of modulators

sections.Objectives: ETI restores anion transport in the most common F508del CFTR mutant; however, some cystic fibrosis (CF) patients continue to experience lung infection and inflammation after 3.5 years of treatment. The variability in phenotype-genotype correlations in patient lung suggests that additional genetic or environmental factors influence clinical outcomes. Here, we aim to identify genome and epigenome changes in airway epithelial cells following CFTR rescue.
sections.Methods: Primary Human Bronchial Epithelial Cell (HBEC) cultures were generated from 6 CF patients and 5 non-CF (NCF) donors. 1 CF patient (referred to as CF1) carried the “2184ΔA/W128A” mutation, while 5 patients were homozygous for F508del (referred to as CF2). CFTR channel function was assessed by Ussing chamber assay after 72 h of ETI treatment. Single nuclei (sn) multi-omics sequencing was performed to profile the epigenome and transcriptome changes of cells before and after ETI treatment.
sections.Results: ETI restored CFTR channel transport function in CF2 cells to 46.3% of wide-type CFTR activity, whereas no restoration was observed in CF1. snRNA-seq analysis revealed significant transcriptional alterations in CF2 cells compared with NCF cells: dysfunction in the “extracellular matrix organization” and “cell cycle and senescence” pathways in basal cells; alteration in the “RNA, protein, and lipid metabolism” pathways in goblet cells; and disruption in the “membrane trafficking” and “cholesterol biosynthesis” pathways in ciliated cells. ETI tended to normalize abnormal pathways in CF2 cells, suggesting metabolic reprogramming. Nevertheless, across all cell types, only a minimal proportion (8.4%) of CF2 abnormally regulated genes was significantly restored following ETI treatment.
sections.Conclusion: We started to uncover the effects of ETI to the different cell types in CF airway epithelium that will be deepened with snATAC-seq data to map the epigenetic landscape underlying ETI-responsive and uncorrected genes.

sections.Objectives: measure the quality and quantity of sleep before and after starting elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) and its relationship to exercise, energy, mood and general health at baseline function.
sections.Methods: before and after starting ETI measure the 5 domains of wellness using the Alfred Wellness Score (AWESCORE), a recently developed patient reported outcome measure. This comprises 10 wellness items each valued 0-10 and Total Wellness Score 0-100. Higher scores denote greater wellness. We analysed changes in the AWESCORE on ETI. then the relationship between sleep and cough, sputum, energy, exercise participation, appetite, weight, mood and general health.
sections.Results: at study baseline the characteristics of 123 well adults with CF were (mean and std. deviation): age 36.9 (10.7) years; FEV1 63.5 (23.1)% pred.; BMI 24.0 (6.5); AWESCORE Total Score 67.3 (12.6); sleep 6.4 (2.0); cough 6.2 (1.9); sputum 6.2 (1.9); energy 6.2 (1.9); exercise participation 5.9 (2.3); appetite 7.8 (2.1); weight 7.9 (2.1); mood 6.9 (2.3); anxiety 6.9 (2.3); general health 6.8 (2.0). There was an increase in total AWESCORE following treatment with ETI, mean difference 18 (95%CI 15-20, p<0.0001; and a significant increase in sleep score, mean difference 1.3 (95% CI 0.9-1.8), p<0.0001. Table 1: Correlation of change seen between sleep and selected individual scores. These values were analysed using Pearson's correlation. sections.Conclusion: Treatment with ETI resulted in significant increases in all items of the AWESCORE including sleep. There were highly significant correlations between sleep and scores on energy, exercise participation, mood and sense of general health. Reference: The AWESCORE, a patient-reported outcome measure: development, feasibility, reliability, validity and responsiveness for adults with CF. presentations.title: Synonymous CFTR variants contribute to functional variability in human nasal epithelial cells
presentations.summary: Objectives: Human nasal epithelial (hNE) cells are a reliable ex-vivo model to study CFTR function. Defining reference CFTR activity in non-CF individuals is crucial to identify subtle functional alterations and to evaluate CFTR rescue strategies. This study aimed to characterize CFTR functional variability in hNE cultures and explore potential genetic determinants of reduced activity. Methods: hNE cells were collected from ~50 non-CF donors and differentiated in air–liquid interface. CFTR-dependent chloride secretion was measured by short-circuit current under symmetrical chloride (SC) and after imposing a chloride gradient (GC) to enhance CFTR-mediated anion transport. CFTR gene analysis by NGS was performed...
presentations.authors_text: V. Capurro1, E. Pesce1, V. Tomati1, C. Pastorino1, A. Dighero1, R. Pisani1, A. Quarato1, M. Galli2, L.J.V. Galietta3,4, F. Cresta5, C. Castellani5, N. Pedemonte1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1IRCCS Istituto Giannina Gaslini, UOC Genetica Medica, Genova, Italy, 2IRCCS Istituto Giannina Gaslini, Laboratory of Molecular Genetics and Biobanks, Genova, Italy, 3Telethon Institute of Genetics and Medicine (TIGEM), Pozzuoli, Italy, 4University of Naples Federico II, Department of Translational Medical Sciences (DISMET), Napoli, Italy, 5IRCCS Istituto Giannina Gaslini, UOSD Centro Fibrosi Cistica, Genova, Italy

sections.Objectives: Human nasal epithelial (hNE) cells are a reliable ex-vivo model to study CFTR function. Defining reference CFTR activity in non-CF individuals is crucial to identify subtle functional alterations and to evaluate CFTR rescue strategies. This study aimed to characterize CFTR functional variability in hNE cultures and explore potential genetic determinants of reduced activity.
sections.Methods: hNE cells were collected from ~50 non-CF donors and differentiated in air–liquid interface. CFTR-dependent chloride secretion was measured by short-circuit current under symmetrical chloride (SC) and after imposing a chloride gradient (GC) to enhance CFTR-mediated anion transport. CFTR gene analysis by NGS was performed for most subjects, with sequencing ongoing for a subset.
sections.Results: Under SC, CFTR activity ranged 10–35 µA/cm², with marked inter-individual variability. GC induced ~two-fold increases in most cultures. Among samples <20 µA/cm², one subgroup responded strongly (~three-fold in GC) and another modestly (~1.5-fold), including obligate and unrecognized carriers. NGS revealed synonymous variants potentially affecting protein folding or expression: p.Thr854= (modulating local translation), p.Glu528= (splicing/mRNA structure), p.Glu146= (rare, possible mRNA effects), p.Gly91= (splicing, reduced protein), and a missense p.Arg31Cys (impacting conformation/trafficking). These findings suggest even synonymous changes can contribute to inter-individual variability.
sections.Conclusion: hNE cell coltures robustly detect subclinical CFTR dysfunction. Combined functional and genetic analysis highlights a potential role of synonymous variants in modulating CFTR folding and activity, supporting the use of this ex-vivo model for personalized CFTR assessment. Supported by Fondazione FFC grants FFC #9/2019 and FFC #10/2021, and Italian MoH grant PNRR-MR1-2023-12378412.

presentations.title: Functional characterization of rare variants c.4055A>C and c.3352T>C in the CFTR gene using an intestinal organoids model
presentations.summary: Objective: To define the functional impact and therapeutic potential of two uncharacterized rare CFTR variants, c.4055A>C (p.Q1352P) and c.3352T>C (p.S1118P), using a patient-derived intestinal organoid model. These specific variants have not been previously described in major cystic fibrosis databases. Method: We utilized the forskolin-induced swelling assay in intestinal organoids from patients with genotypes c.1392+1G>A/c.4055A>C and c.3528delC/c.3352T>C. Organoids homozygous for F508del were used as a control. Functional response was quantified before and after treatment with CFTR modulators: ivacaftor (IVA), lumacaftor/ivacaftor (LUM/IVA), tezacaftor/ivacaftor (TEZ/IVA), and elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaft...
presentations.authors_text: M. Krasnova1, A. Efremova1, N. Kashirskaya2, E. Kondratyeva2, D. Goldshtein1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Research Centre for Medical Genetics, Stem Cell Genetics Laboratory, Moscow, Russian Federation, 2Research Centre for Medical Genetics, Moscow, Russian Federation

sections.Objectives: To define the functional impact and therapeutic potential of two uncharacterized rare CFTR variants, c.4055A>C (p.Q1352P) and c.3352T>C (p.S1118P), using a patient-derived intestinal organoid model. These specific variants have not been previously described in major cystic fibrosis databases.
sections.Methods: We utilized the forskolin-induced swelling assay in intestinal organoids from patients with genotypes c.1392+1G>A/c.4055A>C and c.3528delC/c.3352T>C. Organoids homozygous for F508del were used as a control. Functional response was quantified before and after treatment with CFTR modulators: ivacaftor (IVA), lumacaftor/ivacaftor (LUM/IVA), tezacaftor/ivacaftor (TEZ/IVA), and elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ELX/TEZ/IVA).
sections.Results: Both variants demonstrated high residual CFTR function, with organoid swelling significantly exceeding the F508del/F508del control, classifying them as mild (Class III-VI) mutations. Organoids carrying the c.4055A>C variant responded robustly to all modulators. Similarly, the c.3352T>C variant showed extreme residual function and high efficacy for all tested drugs, including the triple combination. In contrast, F508del/F508del organoids required corrector combinations (LUM/IVA, TEZ/IVA) for rescue, with ELX/TEZ/IVA being most effective.
sections.Conclusion: The rare variants c.4055A>C and c.3352T>C are mild CFTR mutations with significant residual function. Their strong response in organoids to CFTR modulators, particularly the triple-combination ELX/TEZ/IVA, predicts high clinical efficacy of targeted therapy for patients carrying these genotypes.

presentations.authors_text: E. Altay Tanyer1, E. Yalçın1, T. Kantemir1, B. Çapraz Yavuz1, N. Emiralioğlu1, D. Doğru1, U. Özçelik1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Hacettepe University School of Medicine, Department of Pediatric Pulmonology, Ankara, Turkey

sections.Objectives: The D110H mutation is a rare variant on exon 4 of the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) gene. It is more prevalent in Southern Europe, Turkey, Italy, and Iran, and has generally been associated with pseudo-Bartter syndrome (PBS) in the literature. While D110H is defined as "CF-causing" in the CFTR2 database, it is classified as "disease-causing with varying clinical consequence" in CFTR-Fr. This study evaluates the clinical and laboratory findings of cystic fibrosis (CF) patients with the D110H mutation to assess its impact on disease progression.
sections.Methods: We retrospectively analysed 24 years of data from 21 patients carrying the D110H mutation.
sections.Results: Median age at diagnosis was 5 months; 95.2% were diagnosed with CF and 4.8% with Cystic Fibrosis Screen Positive Inconclusive Diagnosis (CFSPID). At presentation, 38.1% of patients exhibited PBS. Other reasons for admission included failure to thrive (28.6%), elevated immunoreactive trypsinogen (IRT) (9.5%), and pancreatitis (9.5%). During a median follow-up of 11 years, 80% maintained their CF diagnosis, while 19% were reclassified as CFTR-related disorder (CFTR-RD). Mean FEV1 was 101.78±12.33% and 47.6% were colonised with methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA). Pancreatic sufficiency was preserved in 8 of 9 patients tested. While 47.6% showed no systemic involvement, 28.6% had PBS, 9.5% pancreatitis, 9.5% recurrent infections, and 4.8% pancreatic insufficiency. In the CFTR-RD group (n=4), second alleles carried residual function or unidentified mutations. In the CF group (n=17), 8 had residual function and 8 had minimal function mutations on the second allele.
sections.Conclusion: D110H is frequently associated with PBS, preserved pancreatic sufficiency, and normal FEV1 values. The clinical phenotype and disease severity are significantly determined by the accompanying mutation. Long-term follow-up and genetic characterisation are critical for optimal patient management.

Gülen12, V. Şen13, S. Pekcan14, E. Çakır15, Y. Canıtez16, N. Emiralioğlu2, T. Şişmanlar Eyüboğlu17, G. Cinel8, D. Doğru Ersöz2, U. Özçelik2, N. Çobanoğlu1

sections.Objectives: Large deletions and duplications (copy number variations, CNVs) in the CFTR gene are rare in cystic fibrosis (CF) compared with common mutations. This study aimed to evaluate the frequency of large CFTR CNVs in Türkiye and their clinical impact using data from the National Cystic Fibrosis Registry (NCFR).
sections.Methods: NCFR data from 2024 were retrospectively analyzed. Among 2,150 CF patients followed in 36 centers, those with identified large CFTR CNVs were included. Demographic, clinical, microbiological, and treatment-related characteristics were recorded.
sections.Results: A total of 43 patients (2.00%) had large CNVs, 53.4% of whom were male. Median (min-max) age at diagnosis was 0.33 (min-max: 0.08-9) years. Newborn screening was positive in 44.1%, and 9.30% had a history of meconium ileus. Mean±SD sweat chloride levels were 85.31±25.97 mEq/L and 93.00±22.11 mEq/L. Inhaled antibiotic therapy was used by 34.8% of patients, while advanced respiratory support was required in three patients. CFTR modulator therapy was used by two patients with heterozygous F508del mutations. Median (min-max) BMI Z-score was −0.67 (-2.73-2.72), and the mean±SD FEV₁ value in the spirometry test was 76.81%±26.00. Ten patients (23.2%) experienced a mean±SD of 2.10±1.10 pulmonary exacerbations per year. Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Methicillin-resistance Staphylococcus aureus were detected in 41.8% and 13.9% of patients, respectively. Pancreatic insufficiency and liver disease were present in 23.2% and 20.9% of patients, respectively. ABPA was recorded in 3 patients, diabetes in 2 patients, and Dios in 1 patient.
sections.Conclusion: Children with large CFTR CNVs constitute a small but clinically significant subgroup with increased disease burden, characterized by chronic airway infection, and frequent organ involvement. Systematic evaluation of large CFTR CNVs may improve risk stratification and support genotype-based follow-up strategies in CF.

presentations.title: Clinical significance of 5T polymorphism genotype in a cohort of cystic fibrosis Portuguese patients
presentations.summary: Objectives: A common genetic variant among patients with cystic fibrosis (CF) is 5T. This variant exhibits a highly variable clinical phenotype, particularly when present in combination with other CFTR alleles. The aim of this study was to characterize the clinical and genetic features of Portuguese patients carrying this variant. Methods: We conducted a retrospective analysis of children and adults followed in 5 CF- Reference Centers in Portugal, who carried at least one 5T variant in trans with another CFTR variant. Results: Fifteen patients were included (7 males), with a median age 22 years [IQR 13–45], and median age at diagnosis of 13 years [IQR 3–33]; two patients had a positive neonatal screening. Eigh...

sections.Objectives: A common genetic variant among patients with cystic fibrosis (CF) is 5T. This variant exhibits a highly variable clinical phenotype, particularly when present in combination with other CFTR alleles. The aim of this study was to characterize the clinical and genetic features of Portuguese patients carrying this variant.
sections.Methods: We conducted a retrospective analysis of children and adults followed in 5 CF- Reference Centers in Portugal, who carried at least one 5T variant in trans with another CFTR variant.
sections.Results: Fifteen patients were included (7 males), with a median age 22 years [IQR 13–45], and median age at diagnosis of 13 years [IQR 3–33]; two patients had a positive neonatal screening. Eight patients had 5T in trans with F508del (1 of whom also had another CF-causing (CFc) variant in cis with the F508del mutation), four had 5T in trans with a non-F508del CFc variant, and three had F508del and another CFc variant in cis with 5T. Seven patients had a 5T;12TG genotype, while TG repeat length was unknown in seven. Median sweat chloride was 60 mmol/L [IQR 43–75] at diagnosis. Thirteen patients were diagnosed with CF, one with CF related disorder, and one with CF screen positive inconclusive diagnosis. Chest CT abnormalities were observed in 11 patients. Pancreatic insufficiency was present in two patients, and six reported gastrointestinal symptoms. Respiratory symptoms were present in 11 patients, and five had Pseudomonas aeruginosa isolates. Median FEV₁ was 87% [IQR 66-105]. Three patients initiated highly effective modulator therapy, one of whom discontinued treatment due to adverse effects.
sections.Conclusion: This is the first multicentre Portuguese cohort describing patients carrying the CFTR 5T variant. Most patients showed clinically relevant respiratory involvement. These findings reinforce the importance of comprehensive genetic evaluation to understand the impact of the 5T variant in the disease and its genotype-phenotype correlations.

sections.Objectives: To conduct an association study of PPARG, PPARD, UCP2, and UCP3 gene variants with nutritional status and body composition in children with cystic fibrosis (CF).
sections.Methods: Patients with CF (F508del/F508del) (n=478) aged 0 to 18 years (11.8 ± 3.8 years). Body composition was analyzed before and after 1 year of targeted therapy. Polymorphisms (SNPs) in the PPARG, PPARD, UCP2, and UCP3 genes were tested using PCR and RFLP analysis.
sections.Results: After 1 year of targeted therapy, body weight in boys significantly increased by 5.3 (±2.5) kg and height by 5.7 (±1.7) cm (p<0.01), while in girls, body weight increased by 4.5 (±1.9) kg and height by 4.8 (±1.3) cm (p<0.01). In boys, %FM increased from -0.23 to 0.13 SD (p=0.04). In girls, %FM increased from -0.15 to 0.08 SD (p=0.02) and %SMM decreased from 0.79 to 0.6 SD (p=0.04). The TT genotype of the UCP2 gene is associated with excess BMI (+1.1 to +2.0 Z score) and class 2 obesity (+3.0 Z score) (OR=9.583, p=0.044). The CC genotype of the UCP2 polymorphism is associated with normal SMM (-1 to +1 Z score) (OR=6.769; p=0.031). The CC genotype of the UCP3 gene is associated with excess BMI and class 2 obesity (OR=5.926; p=0.050). The CC genotype of the UCP3 gene is associated with a decrease in ACM (<-1.1 Z score) (OR=12.857; p=0.039). The TT genotype of the UCP3 gene is associated with low body fat (OR=8.000; p=0.016) and high SMM (+1.1 to +2.0 Z score) (OR=7.143 (95% CI: 1.352-37.746; χ2=6.334; p=0.042). The GG genotype of the PPARG gene is associated with decreased body fat (<-1.1 Z score) (OR=15.500; p=0.015), and the CC genotype of the PPARD gene is associated with decreased body fat (OR=17.500; p=0.016).
sections.Conclusion: Revealed an increase in the proportion of FM after 1 year of targeted drug treatment. Carriage of the TT genotype of the UCP2 gene and the CC genotype of the UCP3 gene is associated with a violation of body composition.

sections.Objectives: 400 patients (mean age 11.1±3.8 years) receiving ivacaftor/lumacaftor and elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor therapy (ETI) in 2022-2025 were examined.
sections.Methods: study of phase-I: CYP2C9*3, CYP2C9*2, CYP2C19*2, CYP2C19*3, CYP2D6*4, CYP3A4*3, CYP3A4*1B, CYP1A1, CYP1A2 and phase-II drug metabolizing enzymes genes polymorphism: GSTT1 и GSTM1; GSTP1, GCLC, GCLM, NAT2 (c.191G>A; c.282C>T, c.341T>C, c.434A>C, c.481C>T, c.590G>A, c.803A>G, c.845A>C, c.857G>A) was performed by PCR followed by RFLP (restriction fragment length polymorphism) or AFLP (amplified fragment length polymorphism) analysis.
sections.Results: Patients with the AC genotype of the CYP2C9*3 had higher weight (p=0.05), height (p=0.05), and better decrease sweat test (p=0.040) compared to carriers of the CC genotype, indicating a higher efficacy of targeted therapy. Better nutritional status indicators after one year of therapy were observed in patients with the GG genotype of the GSTP1 gene compared to the AA and AG genotypes (p=0.040), as well as in patients with the II genotype of the GSTM1 gene compared to the DD genotype (p=0.002 and p=0.004). FVC, % pred., FEV1, % pred. after one year of targeted therapy were significantly higher in patients with the TT genotype of the GSLM gene compared to patients with the CC and CT genotypes (p=0.002 and p=0.004). Carriage of the AA genotype of the CYP2C19*2 genetic variant in CF with adverse events (AE) was recorded 4.4 times more often compared to children without AE (p=0.013). Carriage of the CC genotype of the CYP1A2 gene in CF with AE was recorded 2 times more often compared to children without HP (p=0.008). Carriage of the AA genotype of the CYP2D6*4 genetic variant in CF with AE was recorded 2.8 times more often compared to children without AE (p=0.007).
sections.Conclusion: Pharmacogenetic testing allows to identify risk groups for developing AE to targeted therapy for CF.

sections.Objectives: Homozygosity for the complex allele p.Leu467Phe-p.Phe508del (L467F-F508del) is associated with failure to respond to elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) in vitro and a below average response in vivo. Clinical and functional CFTR response to the newly available vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaftor (VTD) in vivo in homozygous carriers of these mutations is unknown. We report clinical and functional responses towards ETI and VTD of two L467F-F508del homozygous children.
sections.Methods: An evaluation by clinical symptoms, ppFEV1, sweat chloride concentration (SCC), sweat secretion test (SST), and nasal potential difference (nPD) measurement was assessed in two children prior CFTR modulator, during ETI and during VTD therapy.
sections.Results: The diagnosis of F508del homozygous CF was made in early childhood. Lack of pronounced decrease in sweat chloride after initiation of ETI (- 18mmol/l to 85mmol/l and -15 mmol/l to 93 mmol/l, respectively) led to resequencing of CFTR, which revealed L467F homozygosity in both children. Both children showed low clinical responses to ETI with the girl experiencing continued decline in lung function and the boy demonstrating an above average number of pulmonary exacerbations during ETI therapy. They received nPD measurements prior and with ETI therapy, which showed below average to no responses. After switch to VTD, ppFEV1 increased by 10% in the girl. The boy did not experience any exacerbations anymore and stopped coughing. Results of nPD, SST, and SCC are pending and will be available soon.
sections.Conclusion: Two children with L467F-F508del homozygosity suspect a greater clinical response to VTD compared to ETI. Data on CFTR function will complement clinical results. The investigation of complex alleles on CFTR modulator responsiveness is important.

sections.Objectives: To evaluate the potential added benefit of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) and vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (VTI) compared to ivacaftor in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) carrying a class III and class I mutation, and to assess whether patient-derived intestinal organoids (PDIOs) can inform individualised modulator therapy decisions.
sections.Methods: PDIOs were generated from 4 pwCF carrying a CFTR class III mutation in trans with a class I mutation: G178R/Q493X (case 1), S1251N/G542X (case 2), R347H/2183AA>G (case 3) and Q359R/1717-1G>A (case 4). CFTR function was quantified using the forskolin-induced swelling (FIS) assay, following exposure to CFTR modulators (3 μM). Swelling responses (at 0.8 μM forskolin) were quantified as area under the curve (AUC ± SD) and compared between treatment conditions.
sections.Results: Ivacaftor resulted in substantial rescue of CFTR function in all PDIOs. ETI produced the highest swelling responses across all cases, indicating incremental CFTR rescue compared with ivacaftor treatment alone. In cases 2–4, considerable residual CFTR function was observed. FIS responses are summarised in Table 1. Individual case-level comparisons were underpowered, but the multilevel analysis showed a significant overall treatment effect favouring ETI (p = 0.001).
sections.Conclusion: Rectal organoid-based functional testing suggests that ETI could further increase CFTR function compared to ivacaftor treatment alone in pwCF with a combination of a class III and class I mutation. As ETI is now approved for these genotypes, the key question is whether greater in vitro CFTR function translates into meaningful clinical benefit to justify potential risks. presentations.affiliations_text: 1Meyer Children Hospital IRCSS, Florence, Italy, 2Emory University School of Medicine, Pediatrics, Center for Cystic Fibrosis & Airways Disease Research, Atlanta, United States, 3Meyer Children's Hospital, IRCCS Department of Paediatric Medicine, Cystic Fibrosis Regional Reference Center, Florence, Italy

sections.Background: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has revolutionized cystic fibrosis (CF) care; however, its efficacy in pediatric subjects carrying one ETI-responsive and one non-responsive CFTR variant remains incompletely characterized. This study compared the realworld response to ETI using sweat chloride concentration (SCC) as a key biomarker of CFTR function.
sections.Methods: In this retrospective, single-center study, pediatric subjects aged 6–18 years, carrying at least one p.Phe508del variant and on ETI, were included. Participants were stratified by genotype into those with two responsive variants or one responsive variant. SCC, percent predicted FEV1 (ppFEV1), BMI z-score, and pulmonary exacerbations were assessed at baseline (T0), 3–6 months (T1), 12 months (T2), and 24 months (T3). Longitudinal analyses were performed using mixed-effects models.
sections.Results: Among 80 pediatric participants, those with two responsive variants had significantly lower median SCC at all follow-up time points (T1: 31.0 vs 46.5 mmol/L; T2: 26.0 vs 41.0 mmol/L; T3: 32.0 vs 45.0 mmol/L; all p<0.01) and were more likely to achieve SCC <30 mmol/L. Regression models confirmed that carrying two responsive variants was independently associated with a 30% lower SCC (coefficient −0.36, 95% CI −0.54 to −0.19) and a 97% reduction in odds of elevated SCC (OR 0.03). No significant differences were observed in ppFEV1, BMI z-score, or pulmonary exacerbation rates.
sections.Conclusion: Children and adolescents with CF carrying two ETI-responsive variants exhibit a superior biochemical response to ETI. The absence of differences in clinical outcomes likely reflects preserved baseline status, reinforcing SCC as a sensitive biomarker of CFTR modulation in pediatric individuals.

sections.Background: X-ray Velocimetry (XV) is a functional X-ray imaging method that measures local lung ventilation during breathing. XV technology has been validated in animal models and is FDA approved for use in adult humans. In Australia elexacaftor+tezacaftor+ivacaftor (ETI) was approved for children aged 2-6 years in August 2024. The aim of this study was to assess how XV ventilation parameters change in response to ETI.
sections.Methods: This prospective, single-center cohort study aimed to recruit children that were eligible to commence ETI. Children had XV imaging and pulmonary function testing (spirometry and nitrogen multiple breath washout) performed prior to starting ETI, and at 1-3 months post-ETI commencement.
sections.Results: To date eight children have been recruited, with paired lung function and XV scans completed. All were stable and asymptomatic during study visits, although four children (57%) had a mild exacerbation requiring oral antibiotics after commencing ETI. Children were compliant, with 98% of Trikafta doses taken overall. There were no significant differences in the pulmonary function metrics over time, but the Inspiratory Ventilation Defect Percentage (VDP) was significantly higher at follow up compared to baseline. All CF children with matched lung function and XV reports had normal spirometry and LCI. However, XV visualizes regional airflow dynamics at each point in the breath, and showed substantial ventilation abnormalities exist in some of these children.
sections.Conclusion: Our preliminary results indicate that repeated XV analysis can be applied in children and may be of use in evaluating new medications in young children. In this cohort of young children with cystic fibrosis who have normal spirometry and LCI, XV may provide early evidence of altered ventilation following ETI commencement. Acknowledgements: This study was supported by a seed grant from 4DMedical. MD received funding from MRFF Grant RFRHPSI000013 and AEA Grant IV240100090.

sections.Objectives: To use 129Xe ventilation MRI and pulmonary function tests to assess lung function in people with cystic fibrosis (CF) receiving highly effective modulator therapy (HEMT).
sections.Methods: Ten children (M=6, F=4) and 18 adults with CF (M=10, F=8) were assessed using Xe-MRI, spirometry and lung clearance index (LCI). Ventilation defect percent (VDP) from Xe-MRI was calculated as the proportion of the lung without ventilation. Adults also had pre-HEMT measurements ~5.5 years earlier using the same methods. Data are presented as mean (SD) or median [IQR].
sections.Results: In children, age, height and weight= 11.4 years [7.7,14.6], 145.6cm [132,156.3] and 35.5kg [28.8,46.1]. In adults, age, height and weight= 29.9 years (11.6), 167.9cm (9.2) and 66.5kg (9.5). All children had an FEV1 within the normal range (FEV1= 105.1% [97.4,113.4]). On average LCI and VDP were normal (LCI=6.3 [6.0,7.1], VDP=1.6% [1.4,1.9]), however 3 children had clear Xe-MRI ventilation defects, 2 of which also had abnormal LCI. In comparison adults had a significantly worse FEV1=78.9% [54.8,102.4] (p=0.002), FEV1/FVC=74.9% (19.3) (p=0.001), LCI=12.7 (5.6) (p=0.0002) and VDP= 25.1% [3.9,35.9] (p<0.001). Age correlated (p<0.001) with FEV1 (r=-0.76), FEV1/FVC (r=-0.79), LCI (r=0.78) and VDP (r=0.82). Compared with pre-HEMT findings, adults on average showed increases in FEV1 and FVC% (mean difference [95% CI] in % =6.9 [3.8–10.0] and 8.2 [4.3–12.0]; p<0.001). LCI deteriorated (median difference [95% CI] =0.9 [0.04–2.8]; p=0.03). VDP showed no significant change, with ventilation abnormalities largely persisting.
sections.Conclusion: Children receiving HEMT had normal lung function and mostly homogeneous ventilation on 129Xe-MRI, contrasting with pre-HEMT studies where ventilation abnormalities on MRI were common. Adults with prior disease had poorer lung function with persistent ventilation abnormalities, despite an FEV1 increase. Xe-MRI provides a powerful and sensitive means of monitoring lung health over time.

Current guidance recommends pre-treatment (baseline) ophthalmology screening and repeat screening after one year. We aimed to assess adherence with baseline and follow-up ophthalmology screening in children commenced on ivacaftor-containing modulators in a tertiary paediatric CF centre. Methods: Between September and October 2025 a retrospective audit was conducted of children with CF commenced on ivacaftor-cont...
presentations.authors_text: O. Moran1, C. Ring1, W. Naqvi1, L. Perrem1, B. Elnazir1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Children’s Health Ireland at Tallaght, Respiratory Department, Dublin, Ireland

sections.Objectives: Ivacaftor-containing CFTR modulators from infancy are standard care for most children with cystic fibrosis (CF). Lens opacities following ivacaftor exposure have been reported and congenital cataracts described following in utero and breast milk exposure to elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor. Current guidance recommends pre-treatment (baseline) ophthalmology screening and repeat screening after one year. We aimed to assess adherence with baseline and follow-up ophthalmology screening in children commenced on ivacaftor-containing modulators in a tertiary paediatric CF centre.
sections.Methods: Between September and October 2025 a retrospective audit was conducted of children with CF commenced on ivacaftor-containing therapies at a tertiary level CF centre. Charts were reviewed for baseline and follow-up ophthalmology screening. Families were contacted for any unavailable data.
sections.Results: A total of 86 children commenced 134 ivacaftor-containing modulators. Baseline screening was performed in 120/134 (89.5%) modulator initiations. 20/134 (14.9%) counted a previous screen, from prior to commencing a different modulator, as their baseline. Patients who had baseline screening were older (mean 5.73 years) than those who did not (mean 2.46 years). Of the 120 follow-up screenings due (12 were excluded as on modulator for less than 1 year), 2 were excluded as outcomes could not be confirmed. Follow-up screening was completed in 82/118 (69.5%). 28/118 (23.7%) didn’t attend follow-up ophthalmology review until switching to a new modulator. One patient (0.7%) was found to have cataracts; baseline screening had not been performed and cataracts were detected nine months after initiation.
sections.Conclusion: While baseline ophthalmology screening prior to ivacaftor-containing modulator initiation was high, adherence to recommended follow-up screening after one year was substantially lower. Carers reported cost and access difficulties, particularly for younger children.

presentations.title: Cystic fibrosis newborn screening pilot study in Estonia
presentations.summary: Introduction: Until 2023, Cystic Fibrosis (CF) Newborn Screening (NBS) was not available in Estonia and diagnosis was often delayed. To improve early diagnosis and access to treatment, a national pilot CF NBS programme was initiated on 1 April 2023. Objective: To evaluate and summarise the outcomes of the Estonian CF NBS pilot programme. Methods: We retrospectively analysed data from the CF NBS pilot conducted in 2023–2025. Screening followed a three-tier strategy: immunoreactive trypsinogen (IRT), pancreatitis-associated protein (PAP), and CFTR gene sequencing; in cases of extremely high IRT values (≥120 ng/mL), a safety-net was applied.The IRT cut-off was set at the 97th percentile in year 1 and adjusted to...
presentations.authors_text: E. Albin1, T. Kahre1,2, K. Õunap1,2, K. Muru1,2, K. Künnapas1, M.-M. Lass1, M. Vasar3, A. Viidebaum3, S. Plado4, S. Pajusalu1,2, K. Reinson1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Tartu University Hospital, Genetics and Personalized Medicine Clinic, Tartu, Estonia, 2University of Tartu, Institute of Clinical Medicine, Tartu, Estonia, 3Tartu University Hospital, Children's Clinic, Tartu, Estonia, 4Tallinn Children`s Hospital, Tallinn, Estonia

sections.Objectives: To evaluate and summarise the outcomes of the Estonian CF NBS pilot programme.
sections.Methods: We retrospectively analysed data from the CF NBS pilot conducted in 2023–2025. Screening followed a three-tier strategy: immunoreactive trypsinogen (IRT), pancreatitis-associated protein (PAP), and CFTR gene sequencing; in cases of extremely high IRT values (≥120 ng/mL), a safety-net was applied.The IRT cut-off was set at the 97th percentile in year 1 and adjusted to the 98th percentile thereafter. PAP cut-offs were 1.8 ng/mL for samples with IRT <100 ng/mL and 0.6 ng/mL for samples with IRT ≥100 ng/mL. The third tier comprised sequencing of all CFTR coding exons. Newborns with ≥1 CF-causing CFTR variant or meeting the safety-net criterion were considered screen positive (SP) and referred for sweat chloride testing and paediatric assessment.
sections.Results: A total of 26,425 newborns were screened. IRT exceeded the cut-off in 876 cases, triggering PAP measurement. CFTR sequencing was performed for 302 newborns. Thirty-one newborns were classified as SP due to ≥1 CF-causing variant and/or safety-net IRT. CF diagnosis was confirmed in three patients. Two infants with a pathogenic variant and a variant of varying clinical consequence were classified as CF Screen Positive, Inconclusive Diagnosis (CFSPID). We identified one false negative patient with a PAP concentration below the cut-off.
sections.Conclusion: Data from the pilot programme indicate that the applied three-tier algorithm can effectively identify newborns with CF. A nationwide screening workflow was successfully implemented, and effective collaboration between the screening centre and CF care centres was established.

In order to improve the positive predictive value (PPV), genetic testing was incorporated into the screening algorithm in 2023. In 2024, the currently used algorithm was finally established. This study aims to analyse the results of CF newborn screening since 2024, and to compare the performance of the current algorithm with that of previous screening strategies.
sections.Methods: A total of 172,339 newborns were screened for CF using an IRT/PAP/DNA/IRT strategy. Dried blood spot samples were predominantly collected between the 3rd and 6th days of life. Genetic analysis included the detection of 68 CFTR mutations commonly found in the European population.
sections.Results: Between 2023 and 2024, newborn screening identified 14 patients with CF. One additional patient, presenting meconium ileus, and who had a normal IRT value in the first sample, came to our knowledge. In six cases referred for clinical evaluation at specialized centers, CF was not confirmed. In eight patients (57%) both CF-causing alleles were identified with the genetic study included in the screening algorithm, four of them were homozygous for the F508del mutation. The currently implemented screening algorithm demonstrates superior performance compared with previous algorithms, as indicated by the following estimates (95% confidence intervals): sensitivity 93.3% (68.1–99.8), specificity 99.997% (99.993–99.999), PPV 70,0% (45.7–88.1), and NPV 99.999% (99.996–100).
sections.Conclusion: Since 2013, 1,037,332 newborns have been screened for CF in Portugal, leading to the identification of 100 affected patients and an estimated prevalence of 1:10,478 newborns. Based on the results presented, the current screening algorithm appears to be well adapted to the Portuguese population and will remain in use.

presentations.title: Thirty years of newborn screening for cystic fibrosis in Buenos Aires Province, Argentina: evolution of incidence in a long-term population-based experience
presentations.summary: Introduction: Newborn Screening (NBS) for Cystic Fibrosis (CF) is a cornerstone of public health, enabling early diagnosis, timely treatment, improved quality of life and while facilitating genetic counselling. Although CF NBS has been mandatory in Argentina since 1994, its regulation in Buenos Aires Province (BA) was implemented only in 2008. In July 2010, CF NBS was included in the Programme for the Diagnosis and Treatment of Congenital Diseases (PRODyTEC), Ministry of Health - BA. Objective: To describe the evolution of CF NBS in BA over a 30-year period, focusing on cumulative incidence trends up to 2024. Materials and Methods: Immunoreactive trypsinogen (IRT) was measured using AutoDELFIA technology, with...
presentations.authors_text: V. D'Alessandro1, J. Finocchiaro1, A. Marianelli2, G. Borrajo3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Children's Hospital "Sor María Ludovica", Pulmonary, La Plata, Argentina, 2Children's Hospital "Sor María Ludovica", Central Lab, La Plata, Argentina, 3Fundación Bioquímica Argentina, La Plata, Argentina

sections.Objectives: To describe the evolution of CF NBS in BA over a 30-year period, focusing on cumulative incidence trends up to 2024. Materials and
sections.Methods: Immunoreactive trypsinogen (IRT) was measured using AutoDELFIA technology, with a cut-off value of 70 ng/mL. NBS strategy was based on IRT/IRT, with sweat testing as confirmatory diagnosis. Molecular analysis was not part of the NBS strategy, was therefore performed only after sweat test confirmation.
sections.Results: During the initial period (1995–2007), when NBS coverage was limited and 636.134 newborns (NBs) were screened, cumulative incidence was 1:5,437. Following regulatory implementation in 2008, a sustained decrease in cumulative incidence was observed, reaching 1:6,541 up to 2010 in 968.062 NBs tested. After incorporation into PRODyTEC, the cumulative incidence continued to decline steadily, reaching its lowest historical value of 1:9,000 in 2019 out of a population of 2,636,878 screened NBs. In subsequent years (2020-2024), cumulative incidence remained stable, ranging between 1:8,467 and 1:8,862 in more than 3,2 million NBs tested, reflecting long-term consolidation of the NBS programme.
sections.Conclusion: Over 30 years, CF NBS in BA evolved from limited implementation to a consolidated public health programme. Regulatory support and integration into a structured health system were key to achieving sustained coverage and stable cumulative incidence, reinforcing the value of long-term NBS programmes for equitable early CF diagnosis.

presentations.title: Critical assessment of newborn screening data in a low-income country: insights from a Brazilian reference center
presentations.summary: Newborn screening (NBS) for cystic fibrosis (CF) is crucial to ensure early diagnosis and timely treatment. However, the multistep process involving IRT1, IRT2, sweat chloride testing may compromise its effectiveness in large and resource-limited countries such as Brazil with centralized screening process. Objectives: To analyze NBS performance in a public CF center between 2022 and 2025, focusing on diagnostic timelines, nutritional status, early symptoms, and microbiological findings at first assessment. Methods: A retrospective observational study was conducted with confirmed CF cases identified through NBS. Variables included the age at IRT1, IRT2, sweat test, genotyping, final diagnosis, and first clinica...
presentations.authors_text: J. Horn1, E. Procianoy1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Hospital de Clinicas de Porto Alegre, Pediatric Pneumology, Porto Alegre, Brazil

sections.Objectives: To analyze NBS performance in a public CF center between 2022 and 2025, focusing on diagnostic timelines, nutritional status, early symptoms, and microbiological findings at first assessment.
sections.Methods: A retrospective observational study was conducted with confirmed CF cases identified through NBS. Variables included the age at IRT1, IRT2, sweat test, genotyping, final diagnosis, and first clinical visit. Clinical features such as steatorrhea, cough, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) colonization were recorded. Data analysis was descriptive and performed using Excel.
sections.Results: Thirty-eight patients were diagnosed via NBS from 2022 to 2025, including 10 with meconium ileus. IRT1 was collected at 3–20 days of life (median 5 days); IRT2 at 7–27 days (median 13). Sweat test was performed at 15–262 days (median 37), and genotyping results were available between 35–441 days (median 117). In 9 cases, diagnosis was based solely on genotyping due to absence of sweat testing. The final diagnosis was confirmed between 16–262 days of life (median 41). The first visit occurred at a median age of 74 days (range 38–275). At first evaluation, 49% of patients were underweight, 59% had steatorrhea, 49% had cough, and 2 had Pa in their first oropharyngeal culture.
sections.Conclusion: Delays in key steps of the IRT1/IRT2/sweat test NBS remain a significant challenge within Brazilian public health system. The high proportion of infants with CF presenting with nutritional deficits and early clinical manifestations at their first CF center visit underscores the urgent need for streamlined diagnostic workflows to ensure earlier intervention.

Independently of IRT, meconium ileus (MI) was an indication for ST...
presentations.authors_text: M. Thimmesch1, S. Marie2, J. Dewulf2, L. Marcelis3, G. Luis4, S. Berardis5, L. Hanssens6, C. Quentin6, H. Boboli7, F. Boemer4
presentations.affiliations_text: 1CHC MontLégia, Department of Pediatric Pulmonology and Cystic Fibrosis, Liège, Belgium, 2Universitary Hospital Saint-Luc, Biochemical Genetics and Newborn Screening Laboratory, Brussels, Belgium, 3Universitary Hospital Erasme, Biochemical Genetics and Newborn Screening Laboratory, Brussels, Belgium, 4University Hospital of Liège, Biochemical Genetics and Newborn Screening Laboratory, Brussels, Belgium, 5Universitary Hospital Saint-Luc, Department of Pediatric Pulmonology and Cystic Fibrosis, Brussels, Belgium, 6Queen Fabiola Children’s University Hospital, Department of Pediatric Pulmonology and Cystic Fibrosis, Brussels, Belgium, 7Universitary Hospital of Liège, Department of Pediatric Pulmonology and Cystic Fibrosis, Liège, Belgium

sections.Objectives: To evaluate the performance of the cystic fibrosis newborn screening (CF-NBS) program using an IRT–DNA algorithm, officially implemented in the Wallonia–Brussels Federation (WBF) since January 2020.
sections.Methods: Immunoreactive trypsinogen (IRT1) was measured on dried blood spots collected 48–96 hours after birth. DNA analysis using a 12-mutation CFTR panel was performed when IRT1 was ≥ 99th percentile (P99). Infants with one or two CFTR variants were referred for a sweat test (ST). When IRT1 was ≥P99.9 with negative mutation screening, a failsafe strategy was applied using a repeat IRT at day 21. ST was performed if IRT21 remained > P99. Independently of IRT, meconium ileus (MI) was an indication for ST. Full CFTR gene sequencing was performed when required.
sections.Results: From 2020 to 2025 (results ongoing), 312.623 newborns were screened. CF-NBS was positive and led to ST in 282 infants: 230 due to elevated IRT1 with at least one CFTR mutation, 25 through the failsafe pathway, and 27 with IRT ≥P99.9 and negative mutation screening without repeat IRT at day 21. Nineteen infants were lost to follow-up. CF was diagnosed in 56 newborns: 43 through CF-NBS, 5 with MI, 4 with family history (FH), and 4 missed cases. CF incidence was 1/5.583 live births. Among CF-NBS cases, 40 were identified through IRT ≥P99 with at least one mutation and 3 via the failsafe pathway; one infant died before ST. The program identified 171 healthy CFTR carriers and 17 Cystic Fibrosis Screen Positive Inconclusive Diagnosis (CFSPID) cases. Median age at first CF center visit was 22 days. Sensitivity was 91%, positive predictive value (PPV) 15.3%, and CF/CFSPID ratio 2.5.
sections.Conclusion: This six-year evaluation demonstrates good sensitivity of the CF-NBS program and enables CF diagnosis before one month of age. However, PPV and CF/CFSPID ratio remain suboptimal, highlighting the need for continuous evaluation and optimization of the screening strategy.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Manchester Foundation Trust, Paediatric Respiratory Medicine, Manchester, United Kingdom, 2Manchester Foundation Trust, Biochemistry, Manchester, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Universal Cystic fibrosis (CF) newborn screening (NBS) was introduced in the UK in 2007. However, a small proportion of cases are missed. We aim to characterise a single-centre cohort of children with CF not detected by NBS and identify patterns of missed diagnoses.
sections.Methods: A retrospective cohort study used laboratory NBS and clinical data from 2007 to 2025.
sections.Results: Twenty-two children born between 2009-2025 were identified. Recorded ethnicity was White British/Irish in 15, South Asian in four, and mixed ethnicity in three children. One case was due to post-analytical delay; the remaining cases were protocol-related false negatives. Data on diagnostic indication were available for 20/22 children: 19/20 were diagnosed following clinical symptoms, three of which were meconium ileus, and one following parental carrier testing. All except four were pancreatic sufficient at diagnosis (see Table 1). Both CF-causing alleles were identified in 21/22, and 18 of 22 children had at least one CFTR variant included in the initial four-mutation (CF4) panel, including three children homozygous for p.Phe508del. Table 1: Case characteristics CharacteristicTotal numberPercent (%)Age at diagnosis Median [Q1-Q3] Range 10 months [4.25-34.5m) 8 days – 14 years Reason for missed cases (n=22) Pre-analytical issues IRT <cut off B (99.5th centile) No variants detected on DNA analysis / IRT<120ug/L 1 variant detected, repeat IRT <cut off A (99.5th centile - 10 ug/L). Post analytical issues 0 12 3 6 1 0 54.5 13.7 27.3 4.5 Symptoms at diagnosis (n=20) Respiratory Failure to thrive Gastrointestinal (meconium ileus) Gastrointestinal (other) Family history Other 13 4 3 5 3 1 65 20 15 25 15 5 Pancreatic status at diagnosis (n=20) Insufficient 4 20Sweat test results (n=21) Positive Equivocal Negative 10 6 5 47.6 28.6 23.8 Genetic profile (n=22) Both alleles identified One or more CFTR variants included in the CF4 panel Second CFTR variant in the CF-EU2 panel Neither allele in screening panels 21 18 10 3 95 81.8 45.5 13.6 Four groups were identified according to mechanism of missed detection (Table 2): Group A (IRT<cut off B); Group B (IRT>cut off B, no pathogenic variants detected on CF4 panel and IRT <120ug/L); Group C (CF4 panel screen-positive, negative CF-EU2 panel analysis and repeat IRT <cut off A); and Group D (post-analytical delayed diagnosis). Table 2: Groups according to mechanism of missed detection Group (n)Diagnostic indication (n)Number positive sweat testGenetic information (n)Recent clinical status (n)A (n=12)Meconium ileus (3/10) Absent vas deferens (1) Respiratory symptoms (7/10) Other gastrointestinal symptoms (4/10) Failure to thrive (3/10) 6/111 or more variants in CF4 panel (11/12) 1 or more variants in CF-EU2 panel (7/12) No active management (1/10) Active management (9/10)B (n=3)Respiratory symptoms (3/3) Gastrointestinal symptoms (1/3) 1/31 or more variants in CF-EU2 panel (1/3) Active management (3/3) C (n=6)Asymptomatic, genetic diagnosis (1/6) Respiratory symptoms (5/6) Failure to thrive (1/6) 3/6 No active management (2/5) Active management (3/5) D (n=1)Respiratory symptoms (1/1) 0/1Identified in screening No active management (1/1)
sections.Conclusion: CF cases missed by NBS represented a clinically heterogeneous group. Missed diagnoses were primarily protocol-related false negatives. They were not predominantly associated with Non-White ethnicity or rare CFTR genotypes. These findings emphasise the importance of clinical vigilance and support a low threshold for diagnostic testing despite a negative NBS result.

presentations.title: Genome sequencing for discovery of a hard‑to‑detect CFTR gene mutations
presentations.summary: Introduction: A patient was diagnosed with cystic fibrosis after positive newborn screening (Immunoreactive trypsinogen 153 ug/L, Pancreatitis associated protein 4.6 ug/L). Sweat-chloride was 86 mmol/L and the patient had exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (faecal elastase <15 ug/g). Only one pathogenic CFTR mutation was found (p.Phe508del). Father had congenital bilateral absence of the vas deferens due to CFTR variants p.(Phe508del) and p.(Arg117His). For the mother, the Inno LiPa kit with 37 most common European variants was negative. No residual risk for carriership could be given due to non-Caucasian background. Analysis of the 53 most common European CFTR variants (Elucigene CF-EU2V1 kit), Sanger sequenci...
presentations.authors_text: J. Roukema1, G.M. Schobers2, M.T. Schepens2, D. Westra2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Radboud University Medical Centre, Amalia Children's Hospital, Pediatric Pulmonology, Nijmegen, Netherlands, 2Radboud University Medical Centre, Human Genetics, Nijmegen, Netherlands

sections.Methods: Short read genome sequencing was performed, after which analysis of variants in the complete CFTR gene (exons and introns) was executed.
sections.Results: A heterozygous likely pathogenic mobile element insertion of an alu-element (AluYa5) just upstream of exon 9 on the CFTR gene (NM_000492.4) was detected: c.1117-20_1117-19insAlu r.spl? p.?. This insertion of 281 bp most probably places the branchpoint too far from the splice acceptor site of exon 9, and likely disrupts correct mRNA splicing, potentially leading to the creation of a new cryptic splice acceptor site, the introduction of a premature stop codon, or the loss of functional protein domains. This variant was also found in the mother.
sections.Conclusion: By applying the newest genomic sequencing techniques and expanding the analysis to intronic regions and mobile element insertions, a second hard-to-detect compound heterozygous likely pathogenic variant was found and the genetic etiology was elucidated.

sections.Methods: All patients registered in CFRT as of 2024 were included in the study and divided into two groups based on age at CF diagnosis: ≤2 months (Group 1) and >2 months (Group 2). Demographic, clinical, and laboratory data were compared.
sections.Results: A total of 2239 CF patients were recorded; 47.2% were female. Of these, 691 (30.8%) were diagnosed at ≤2 months of age. In Group 1, females were more prevalent (p=0.037).No differences in growth z-scores were observed between the groups (p>0.05).The median current age was lower in Group 1 (p<0.05).The second sweat test was lower in Group 2 (p<0.001). FEV1 and FVC values were higher in Group 1 (p=0.001, p=0.024, respectively).Use of UDCA was higher in Group 1, while use of oxygen and mannitol was higher in Group 2(p < 0.05).CF-related liver disease, pancreatic insufficiency, meconium ileus, and pseudobartter were more common in Group 1, whereas hemoptysis was more frequent in Group 2 (p <0.05).MSSA and Haemophilus influenzae colonization were more common in Group 1(p <0.05).No difference in mortality was detected between the groups(p=0.973).
sections.Conclusion: Diagnosing CF within the first two months of life is linked to better lung function and significant differences in disease features.Improving early detection methods could enhance long-term results.

presentations.summary: Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a genetically inherited disease caused by mutations in the CFTR gene. The Russian Federation (RF) is home to more than 190 ethnic groups, including small indigenous and autochthonous nations. Aim: Genetic testing of people with CF (pwCF) from different ethnic groups was performed to assess the possibility of employing existing VST across the RF. sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a genetically inherited disease caused by mutations in the CFTR gene. Marked variation exists in the prevalence of CFTR gene variants among different ethnic groups, leading to a wide range of clinical manifestations and responsiveness to variant-specific therapy (VST). The Russian Federation (RF) is home to more than 190 ethnic groups, including small indigenous and autochthonous nations. Aim: Genetic testing of people with CF (pwCF) from different ethnic groups was performed to assess the possibility of employing existing VST across the RF.
sections.Methods: Genetic analysis was performed on 1530 blood samples, obtained between 1995 and 2025. The ethnic origin of the corresponding pwCF (up to the third generation) was determined via direct interview.
sections.Results: The five most common CFTR variants among pwCF of Russian nationality (n=1384) were F508del (54.99%), CFTRdele2,3 (7.59%), 2143delT (2.71%), 3849+10kbC-T (2,35%) and 2184insA (2,24%). A high frequency of E92K was found in the Turkic-speaking groups of the Volga region (VR) and North Caucasus (NC): Chuvash (86.36%), Bashkirs (30%), Tatars (16.3%), Chechens (15.28%), Udmurts (6.8%); W1282X - in Turkic-speaking groups: Karachais (90%), Nogais (100%), Abazins (50%) and Ossetians (57.33%); 1677delTA - in autochthonous nations as the Abkhaz-Adygean (Abazins and Circassians over 30%) and Nakh groups: Chechens (81.3%), Terek Kumyks (50%) and Ingush (100%). 1677delTA has also been detected in other ethnic groups from the NC region: Armenians (25%) and Georgians (42,7%). Interestingly, no pwCF have been identified so far in the indigenous Yakuts, Buryats and Kalmyk populations.
sections.Conclusion: Our results demonstrate significant interethnic differences in the spectrum of CFTR gene variants across the RF. Among the variants common to both Russians and other ethnic groups, Сlass I CFTR variants (CFTRdele2,3, 2143delT, 2184insA, 1677delTA, and W1282X) are widespread, limiting the use of available VST.

sections.Objectives: The prevalence of cystic fibrosis (CF) remains unclear in many resource-limited countries. Morocco has a predominantly Caucasian population and a high consanguinity rate (≈24%). Genetic and clinical data on CF in this country are limited.
sections.Methods: This observational study included all CF patients seen between January 2018 and July 2025 by a single paediatrician at Abderrahim El Harouchi Children’s Hospital, Casablanca.
sections.Results: A diagnosis of CF was confirmed in 39 children (16 males/23 females, median age at first consultation : 1.72 years , 95% CI: 0.71–3.10; range: 0.08–11.47). All patients presented with exocrine pancreatic insufficiency. Two CFTR mutations were identified in each patient, with homozygosity observed in two thirds of the patients (26/39 patients), mainly for F508del (31%), E1104X (13%) and 711+1G>T (8%). Over the study period, 5 patients (13%) died between 4 and 6.5 years of age, and 5 others were lost to follow-up. At the last visit , the mean age (±SD)of the cohort was 6.3 (±4.3) years, and the mean BMI z- score was −1.15 (±1.72).Pseudomonas aeruginosa was detected in sputum samples in 83% of the patients. Mean FEV1 of 17 patients aged ≥6 years was 81.7 % (±24.1) of predicted value (GLI).
sections.Conclusion: Among 78 alleles, the three most frequently identified mutations were F508del (33/78; 42%), E1104X (11/78; 14%), and 711+1G>T (9/78; 11.5%). These proportions differ significantly from those reported in CFTR2 (65%, 0.02%, and 0.2%, respectively; p<0.0001). Based on their genotype, barely half of the patients in this cohort (49%) would be eligible for modulator therapy at age 6 in Belgium. Clinical outcomes were very poor and highlight the urgent need in Morroco to develop national strategies that include improving access to diagnosis, structuring of specialized reference centers and ensuring equitable access to both symptomatic and innovative treatments.

sections.Methods: The 2024 Registry included data from 3,573 patients and 271 patients who were not followed up that year.
sections.Results: The overall cumulative frequency of identified alleles was 95.7%. 97.1% of alleles were identified in children and 92.5% in adults. A total of 304 variants were identified, 159 of which were detected repeatedly. The most frequent variants were: F508del, CFTRdele2.3, E92K, 1677delTA, 3849+10kbC->T, 2143delT, 2184insA, W1282X, L138ins, N1303K. 33 variants are not included in international databases. Two variants of the CFTR were detected in 94.9% of children and 89.5% of adults, one variant in 4.6% and 6.0%, and none in 0.6% and 4.5% of children and adults, respectively. "Severe" genotypes predominate among both children and adults, but their frequency is 79.5% in those under 18 years of age and 65.9% in those over 18. The "mild" genotype was detected in 20.0% of patients under 12 years of age and in 59.2% of patients over 36 years of age. Of the 3,844 patients, F508del/F508del was found in 28.5%, F508del/not F508del in 46.0%, and not F508del/not F508del in 25.5%. In the Central (CFD) and Northwestern (NWFD) Federal Districts, genetic testing was conducted more frequently than in other regions – 98.4% and 98.2% of patients, respectively. The first three positions in frequency were occupied by severe genetic variants: F508del - 52.9% (CFD) and 54.4% (NWFD), CFTRdele2.3 - 7.6% (5.0%), 2143delT - 2.7% (2.8%). In the Southern (SFD) and North Caucasian FD genetic testing was conducted in 96.5% and 95.6% of patients. In the SFD, the F508del variant was found in 54.9% of cases, CFTRdele2.3 - 6.9%, and 3849+10kbC->T - 3.3%.
sections.Conclusion: Thus, the RF is characterized by a diversity of genetic variants, and their frequency varies depending on the region of residence and its ethnic composition.

sections.Background: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is an autosomal recessive disease caused by pathogenic variants in the CFTR gene. While ΔF508 is the predominant mutation in European populations, the distribution of CFTR mutations exhibits significant geographic and ethnic variability.
sections.Objectives: To characterize the genetic epidemiology of CF in Lebanon and compare it to patterns reported in European and Arab populations.
sections.Methods: In this national cross-sectional study, 94 patients with CF were genetically characterized for common CFTR mutations. Demographic data included age, sex, and adult status (>21 years). Allelic frequencies for the three most common mutations — ΔF508, N1303K, and W1282X — were calculated. Consanguinity prevalence was previously reported at 50%1 in Lebanese CF families which suggests the possibility of undiagnosed patients. Comparative reference data from European and Arab cohorts were synthesized from published mutation frequency studies.
sections.Results: Among 94 Lebanese CF patients (47 males; 24 adults), the allelic distribution was: ΔF508 50%, N1303K 22%, and W1282X 14%. Eight patients have only one identified mutation and three patients are missing their genetic information. The cohort demonstrates a relatively elevated contribution of N1303K and W1282X compared with European registry data where these mutations individually represent ~1–3% of alleles. In some Arab populations, N1303K has been reported at higher frequencies (up to ~27%). This genetic heterogeneity contrasts with the classical European predominance of ΔF508 and highlights a distinct mutation distribution in Lebanon.
sections.Conclusion: The Lebanese CF mutation spectrum reveals substantial genetic heterogeneity with a lower relative ΔF508 frequency and higher representation of N1303K and W1282X compared to European cohorts. This underscores the importance of tailored genetic testing panels and suggests population-specific priorities for screening strategies. sections.Background: CFTR genotyping has become especially important recently due to the possibility of targeted therapy for CF patients. The aim: To study the features and frequency of pathogenic CFTR gene variants in adult CF patients in Kazakhstan.
sections.Methods: 29 adult CF patients were tested. Both mutations were identified in all the patients.
sections.Results: 29 pwCF (13 males) were included. The age of the patients in 2025 was 22.5 years (min 18, max 45 yrs.). 9 (31%) of the patients were ethnic Kazakhs, 9 (31%) were Russians, 5(17,2%) were multinational, 3 (10,3%) Ukrainian, 1 (3,4%) Polе, 1 (3,4%) Korean and 1 (3,4%) Turkish. Severe mutations (class I–III) accounted for approximately 81% of all detected alleles, while mild mutations (class IV–V) represented 19%. The most frequent mutation was F508del (51.7%), it was detected in 20 patients (69%) (10 in homozygous and 10 in heterozygous state) followed by 1677delTA (8.6%) and 2143delT (5.2%). Fourteen other CFTR gene variants were identified, forming 13 different genotypes among adult CF patients. 6 genotypes included at least one “mild” CFTR variant and were therefore classified as “mild” genotypes:1677delTA / 3849+10kb C>T; 1677delTA / V392G; 3849+10kb C>T / R668C; R792X / H1054; F508del / L1335P; R117H / F508del. 7 genotypes represented combinations of two “severe” CFTR variants: F508del / F508del; F508del / N1303K; F508del / 2143delT; F508del / c.1766+2T>C; R1066C / c.4111_4113dupGAA; G542X/F508del; 2184insA/F508del. Most identified genotypes are eligible for targeted therapy. Only 3 patients (≈10%) had genotypes not eligible for approved targeted therapy.
sections.Conclusion: The CFTR variant spectrum in adult pwCF in Kazakhstan is characterized by genetic heterogeneity and a predominance of severe mutations, with F508del remaining the most frequent variant but occurring at a lower rate compared to global data. 90% of adult patients are eligible for CFTR modulators treatment.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Scientific Center of Pediatrics and Pediatric Surgery, Pediatric Pulmonology, Almaty, Kazakhstan, 2Charles University and Motol University Hospital, Department of Biology and Medical Genetics, 2nd Faculty of Medicine, Prague, Czech Republic, 3Astana Medical University, Department of Internal Medicine with a Course of Nephrology, Hematology, Allergology, Immunology, Astana, Kazakhstan

sections.Background: CFTR genotyping is essential for accurate diagnosis of cystic fibrosis (CF) and for identifying patients eligible for mutation-specific therapies. Aim: To characterize the spectrum and frequency of CFTR gene variants in a pediatric CF cohort in Kazakhstan.
sections.Methods: Children and adolescents under 18 years of age with confirmed CF and available CFTR genetic testing results were included. Variant type, zygosity, and allele frequency were analyzed.
sections.Results: Genetic testing was performed in 180 (85%) of 212 registered CF patients. Two pathogenic CFTR variants were identified in 163 patients (91%), while a single pathogenic variant was detected in 17 patients (9%). In total, 52 pathogenic CFTR variants were identified across 360 alleles, demonstrating marked allelic heterogeneity. The most frequent variant was F508del, with an allele frequency of 47.5%, detected in 111 patients (62%), including 41 homozygous and 70 heterozygous individuals. All other variants occurred at substantially lower frequencies. In eight patients, three CFTR variants were detected, which may indicate the presence of complex alleles; however, family segregation analysis was not performed, and variant phase could not be determined.
sections.Conclusion: The CFTR mutation spectrum in Kazakhstani pediatric CF patients is highly heterogeneous. Standard CFTR screening panels may miss clinically relevant variants, potentially limiting access to CFTR modulator therapy. Population-specific expansion of mutation panels may improve diagnostic yield and personalized treatment strategies.

sections.Objectives: Cystic Fibrosis, CF is usually diagnosed in children and adolescents, but rarely in adults. Some infertile men with obstructive azoospermia (OA) have undiagnosed mild, atypical CF or CF-related disorders (CF-RD), particularly congenital bilateral aplasia of vas deferens (CBAVD). The aim of study was to evaluate a prevalence of undiagnosed CF and the CFTR genotypes in infertile men with OA/CBAVD. Material and
sections.Methods: 150 Russian patients with CBAVD were examined using by comprehensive clinical, andrological and genetic evaluation. Semen analysis was done in accordance with WHO laboratory manual (2010; 2021). CFTR gene was analyzed using by PCR-AFLP and MLPA, whole exome sequencing (WES) and Sanger sequencing.
sections.Results: In 14 (9.3%) patients, two the CFTR gene variants were found with following CFTR genotypes: F508del/L138ins (n=5), F508del/Met952Ile, F508del/CFTRdele2,3, F508del/Leu159Ser, L138ins/L138ins, 2184insA/E92K, c.2749T>C/Glu993Ala, L138ins/N1303K, 3849+10kbC>T/E92K, Asp1152His/Asp1152His. Eight (F508del, СFTRdele2,3, L138ins, 2184insA, 3849+10kbC>T, E92K, N1303K) of 13 variants were common in Russian populations, five ones (Asp1152His, c.2749T>C, Glu993Ala, Leu159Ser, Met952Ile) were rare, detected by WES. The F508del and L138ins mutations were the most frequent. The Asp1152His variant, when in combination with another one known to cause CF-RD, is associated with an extremely variable phenotype ranging from asymptomatic to CF. Missense variant c.2856G>C(p.(Met952Ile)) has unknown significance, c.2749T>C variant is novel. Positive sweat test and/or bronchiectasis, sinusitis, pancreatitis, were found at least in 10% patients.
sections.Conclusion: About 10% of CBAVD patients can have undiagnosed CF. Rare CFTR mutations are detected by WES/WGS. This should be taken into account during reproductive and genetic examinations. Also, it is very important to evaluate the anamnesis and the phenotype, specially the respiratory, digestive, and reproductive systems.
