sections.Background: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is an autosomal recessive genetic disease caused by mutations in the CFTR gene, with varied clinical expression, which presents a diagnostic challenge. In Ecuador, genetic testing or a standardized protocol remains a limitation for initial diagnosis in pediatric patients. The objective of this study was to determine the mutational profile of the CFTR gene in patients with suspected CF treated at the Vicente Corral Moscoso Hospital in Cuenca, Ecuador.
sections.Methods: A descriptive study was conducted that included 18 patients with clinical suspicion of CF, analyzing a pediatric population.The diagnosis was based on the determination of sweat chloride test values and genetic testing.
sections.Results: In the pediatric patients (N=18), sweat chloride levels above 60 mg/dL predominated in 15 patients, consistent with classic forms of CF. Genetic testing was performed in 14 pediatric patients, in which the H609R variant was the most prevalent (n=2) in non-sibling patients, followed by F508del/N1303K and[HS1] R334W/F508del, observed in siblings. sections.Conclusion: This study constitutes the first description of the mutational spectrum of the CFTR gene in patients with suspected cystic fibrosis treated at this healthcare facility. The predominance of variants ineligible for modulators highlights the need to expand diagnostic testing and develop strategies to standardize genetic testing, perform accurate newborn screening, and avoid late diagnoses. This Quality Improvement (QI) project aimed to standardise diagnostic practice for new referrals by implementing the European Cystic Fibrosis Society (ECFS) standards of care. Objective: A key goal was to develop and implement a patient-centred diagnosis and management pathway to sensitively and accurately differentiate CF and CFTR-RD, inte...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1West Midlands Regional Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Birmingham Heartlands Hospital, University Hospitals Birmingham NHS Foundation Trust, Birmingham, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Accurate diagnosis of Cystic Fibrosis (CF) versus CFTR-Related Disorder (CFTR-RD) is complex, given the spectrum of CFTR protein dysfunction. CFTR-RD is defined as evidence of CFTR protein dysfunction without meeting the diagnostic criteria for CF. Misdiagnosing those with milder CFTR dysfunction as CF, can cause increased health anxiety and over-medicalisation. This Quality Improvement (QI) project aimed to standardise diagnostic practice for new referrals by implementing the European Cystic Fibrosis Society (ECFS) standards of care.
sections.Objectives: A key goal was to develop and implement a patient-centred diagnosis and management pathway to sensitively and accurately differentiate CF and CFTR-RD, integrating psychological support early in the process.
sections.Methods: A multidisciplinary CFTR-RD working group was established, and using the Plan-Do-Study-Act (PDSA) framework, an evidence-based Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) aligned with ECFS guidelines was developed. The SOP involved a staged assessment, including a history, genetic testing, sweat tests, sputum sampling, and radiology, culminating in a review at a specialist CF/CFTR-RD Multidisciplinary Team (MDT) meeting.
sections.Results: Between October 2019 and December 2025, 36 cases (69% male, mean age 37yrs) were referred to our service. Each case was reviewed and assessed using the new SOP. The most frequent routes to referral were infertility (N=17) and respiratory clinics (N=13). A diagnosis was given in 29 of the 36 cases: 16 CF diagnoses (44%) 2 CFTR-RD diagnoses (6%) 11 patients found to be ‘Not CF’ and discharged (31%) Seven remain under investigation via the new pathway.
sections.Conclusion: The QI initiative established a standardised CFTR-RD pathway, promoting patient-centred care while minimising psychological harm. New referrals and those awaiting investigations will continue to be assessed using the SOP to sustain diagnostic consistency, with follow-up care provided in consultant-led clinics as indicated.

sections.Objectives: Population genetic carrier screening (PGCS) for cystic fibrosis (CF) has been offered in Israel since 1999 and funded by the Ministry of Health since 2008. We previously demonstrated increased use of preimplantation genetic diagnosis among CF carriers. This study evaluated the impact of PGCS on CF incidence and on the genetic and clinical characteristics of affected children.
sections.Methods: This retrospective national study included children with CF born in Israel between 2000 and 2023. Data were obtained from the national CF registry and medical records, including demographic and genetic characteristics, prenatal parental carrier screening, ethnic origin, and religion.
sections.Results: CF incidence per 100,000 live births declined from 12.17 in 2000 to 2.90 in 2023 (R²=0.760,p<0.0001). Between 2010 and 2023, 118 children were born with CF; median age at diagnosis was 4.2 months (range 0–111). Muslim Arabs (31%) and Orthodox Jews (23%) were overrepresented, whereas secular Jews, comprising ~60% of the population, accounted for 41% of cases. PGCS was performed by 46 couples; in 27 screen was negative, while 19 were both identified as carriers and continued the pregnancy. 18 couples had an older child with CF. Overall, 37 couples were known carriers prior to birth; 15 (41%) carried at least one residual function variant, mainly D1152H or 5T. Pancreatic sufficiency was present in 41/118 (35%) patients. Despite national availability, 70/118 couples did not undergo PGCS. Even if all had been screened, only 45 would have been identified as carrier couples. PGCS uptake was lower among Orthodox Jews (5/27) and Muslims (8/36) than among secular Jews (24/47-51%).
sections.Conclusion: Since implementation of PGCS, CF birth incidence in Israel has markedly decreased, with a higher prevalence of residual function variants and pancreatic sufficiency. Increased screening uptake, particularly in specific populations, and the expanded genetic panel introduced in 2025 may further reduce CF births.

CF is generally linked to elevated sweat chloride levels and pancreatic insufficiency, whereas PCD is linked with normal sweat chloride levels and intact pancreatic activity although have similar respiratory symptoms. This overlap can create significant diagnostic challenges for clinicians and researchers. We report a diagnostically challenging case with classical CF features, later reclassified as PCD through whole-exome sequencing (WES). M...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Precision Genomics Research Lab, Centre for Applied Molecular Biology, University of the Punjab, Lahore, Pakistan, 2Gulab Devi Teaching Hospital, Lahore, Pakistan, 3School of Biochemistry and Biotechnology, University of the Punjab, Lahore, Pakistan, 4Centre for Applied Molecular Biology, University of the Punjab, Lahore, Pakistan

sections.Objectives: Cystic Fibrosis (CF) and Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia (PCD) are both recessive genetic disorders with overlapping clinical manifestations including mucociliary dysfunction leading to chronic respiratory infections, sinus disease, and early-onset of bronchiectasis. CF is generally linked to elevated sweat chloride levels and pancreatic insufficiency, whereas PCD is linked with normal sweat chloride levels and intact pancreatic activity although have similar respiratory symptoms. This overlap can create significant diagnostic challenges for clinicians and researchers. We report a diagnostically challenging case with classical CF features, later reclassified as PCD through whole-exome sequencing (WES).
sections.Methods: A 23-year-old female with chronic cough, bronchiectasis, pancreatic insufficiency (fecal elastase:101 µg/g), gastrointestinal complications, and elevated sweat chloride level (95 mmol/L) was enrolled for genetic evaluation. Patient’s DNA was analyzed for targeted Sanger sequencing (exons 11,13,19, and poly-TG/T intron 9–exon10 region) of CFTR gene followed by WES.
sections.Results: The targeted sequencing predicted no pathogenic variants. WES confirmed the absence of CFTR mutations, while identified a homozygous pathogenic frameshift variant in CCDC40 gene [c.1416del (p.I473Ffs*2)], consistent with PCD. The segregation analysis predicted both parents and two female siblings as heterozygous carriers, while one homozygous younger female sibling with similar but less severe symptoms. On clinical follow-up, the patient reported symptomatic improvement after initiation of Trikafta.
sections.Conclusion: This case describes CF-like symptoms in a patient genetically diagnosed with PCD. It underscores the limitations of phenotype-driven and targeted genetic testing and highlights the essential role of comprehensive genomic analysis in resolving CF–PCD diagnostic overlap. The unexpected symptomatic response to CFTR modulator therapy warrants further investigation.

Most patients with CFSPID remain well and healthy, but for some Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Regulator-Related Disorder signs may be present or develop as they mature. This uncertainty may lead to emotional turmoil and impact on patient’s ability to follow their desired career choice. sections.Conclusion: As patients diagnosed as CFSPID are now approaching working age, we need to be mindful of the impact this designation may have on a patient’s mental health and ability to follow their desired career choice. For most children with CFSPID, their health will not be affected. A small number of children with CFSPID may develop a form of CF as they grow older. However, CFSPID is not a condition or a disease. Awareness of this needs to be increased to avoid unnecessary exclusion of these patients into certain career pathways.

sections.Objectives: To examine a case series of patients designated as Cystic Fibrosis Screen Positive Inconclusive Diagnosis (CFSPID) and consider signs, symptoms and test results in determining if diagnosis remains inconclusive or if Cystic Fibrosis Related Disease would be a more appropriate designation.
sections.Methods: The Royal Wolverhampton Hospitals NHS Trust provide shared care to approximately 40 patients with Cystic Fibrosis (CF). At the point of identification through Newborn Screening 11 of these received the designation of CFSPID. We took the information from those patients and compared their genotype, phenotype and clinical course.
sections.Results: The 11 patients who were designated as CFSPID on Newborn Screening. Patient Number Genotype 1 Genotype 2 Screening IRT 1st sweat chloride Highest sweat chloride1st faecal elastaseLowest faecal elastase1p.Phe508delp.Arg117HisNo result4244No resultNo result2p.Phe508del p.Arg117His No result 3946No resultNo result3621+1G>Tp.Arg117His No result 2738>500Not applicable4p.Phe508del p.Phe1099Leu672841>500Not applicable5p.Phe508del p.Phe1099Leu No result 2349>500Not applicable 6p.Phe508del p.Phe1099Leu No result 2525>500Not applicable 7p.Phe508del p.Arg117His 703239>500<158p.Gly551Aspp.Phe575Tyr1073941>500Not applicable 9p.Phe508del p.Arg117His 712344484Not applicable 10p.Phe508del p.Arg117His 1334244232Not applicable 11p.Phe508del p.Arg117His 654063134Not applicable Table 1: Cases of patients designated as CFSPID on Newborn Screening. The clinical course of these patients has been variable with different phenotypic presentations. Patients one and two (siblings) had consistent and evolving changes on chest x-rays with a Chrispin-Norman (CN) score of nine; the computed tomography (CT) scans have not demonstrated bronchiectasis. Patient three has isolated Pseudomonas Aeruginosa on respiratory sampling with associated respiratory symptoms. .Patient four presented acutely with seizures due to Pseudo-Bartter Syndrome. Following gastro-intestinal symptoms patient seven has been diagnosed with severe pancreatic insufficiency. Patient 10 has had conductive hearing loss requiring hearing augmentation and significant nutritional concerns. Re-designation has been made in patient 11 after a diagnostic sweat chloride.
sections.Conclusion: The patient cohort has been managed in accordance with Network guidance, and with the exception of the patient with a diagnostic sweat chloride have continued be monitored and treated in line with the original designation, CFSPID. Given the variability in phenotype, the question remains whether this is the correct course, or if re-designation as CF related disease would be more appropriate?

sections.Objectives: To describe respiratory manifestations and clinical course in a Spanish cohort of children with Cystic Fibrosis Screen Positive, Inconclusive Diagnosis (CFSPID).
sections.Methods: Retrospective observational study including children with CFSPID born between July 2009 and June 2024 and followed at two CF Units in Madrid, Spain. Data collected included respiratory manifestations, hospitalizations, treatments, sweat chloride test (SCT) results, and spirometry from 5 years of age. CFTR variants were verified using CFTR2 and CFTR-France/Montpellier databases. Pulmonary exacerbations (PEx) were defined according to modified Fuchs’ criteria.
sections.Results: Seventy-five children were included (57.3% female; median age at end of follow-up 3.59 years [IQR 1.73–5.43]). Respiratory symptoms occurred in 28 children (37.3%). PEx were the most frequent event (38 episodes in 14 children, 18.7%), followed by pneumonia (8%), bronchiolitis (8%), recurrent bronchospasm (8%), and asthma (2.7%). Six hospitalizations occurred due to pneumonia or hypoxemic bronchospasm; none were related to PEx. All antibiotic courses prescribed for PEx (n=38) were oral. Inhaled corticosteroids were the most frequently used treatment (15.8%); inhaled antibiotics and chronic azithromycin were used in only one girl who progressed to CFTR-related disorder. Airway clearance therapy was infrequently prescribed (11.8%), and dornase alfa was not used. Spirometry (n=11) showed preserved lung function (median FEV₁ z-score −0.06 SD [IQR −1.3 to +0.3]). Three children progressed to CF after a positive SCT and had a higher frequency of PEx and oral antibiotic use than non-converted CFSPID (p=0.0019).
sections.Conclusion: Children with CFSPID show a generally mild respiratory course, with few antibiotic-treated pulmonary exacerbations and infrequent hospital admissions, consistent with reports from international cohorts.

The metabolic background of elevated IRT in non-CF neonates has not been fully understandable. The objective of this study was to investigate metabolic differences, with a focus on amino acid and acylcarnitine profiles, in newborns with elevated IRT who were confirmed not to have CF, and to examine the potential impact of maternal and perinatal factors on these metabolic changes. Methods: A retrospective cohort study was conducted with targeted metabolomic profiling. Neonat...

sections.Objectives: Increased immunoreactive trypsinogen (IRT) concentrations detected through newborn screening (NBS) are commonly associated with cystic fibrosis (CF) but may also occur in infants without CF, resulting in false-positive findings. The metabolic background of elevated IRT in non-CF neonates has not been fully understandable. The objective of this study was to investigate metabolic differences, with a focus on amino acid and acylcarnitine profiles, in newborns with elevated IRT who were confirmed not to have CF, and to examine the potential impact of maternal and perinatal factors on these metabolic changes.
sections.Methods: A retrospective cohort study was conducted with targeted metabolomic profiling. Neonates with elevated IRT levels but without a CF diagnosis were compared with newborns with normal IRT levels. Data were obtained from the CF NBS programme at the Children’s Clinical University Hospital of Latvia between July 2021 and June 2022. Following inclusion and exclusion criteria, 57 infants were included to the elevated IRT group and 53 infants to the control group. Associations between maternal characteristics and neonatal metabolite levels were also analysed.
sections.Results: Newborns with elevated IRT demonstrated increased concentrations of glutamine, acetylcarnitine, propionylcarnitine and asparagine, whereas reduced levels of glutamate, lysine, threonine and serine compared with controls. Evaluation of maternal variables revealed maternal COVID-19 infection as the only factor significantly associated with neonatal metabolic alterations, including higher alanine and lower aspartic acid and hydroxyproline levels.
sections.Conclusion: Non-CF newborns with elevated IRT exhibit alterations in their metabolic profiles, suggesting that transient metabolic responses related to perinatal stress or maternal conditions may contribute to increased IRT levels. These non-CF influences may help refine CF newborn screening strategies and reduce false-positive results.

presentations.summary: Introduction: Sweat testing (ST) is a key diagnostic test for cystic fibrosis. ECFS guidelines 2022 recommend bilateral arm ST to reduce rates of ‘quantity not sufficient’ (QNS), aiming for <15% samples. We performed this study to quantify QNS frequency at our adult centre and explore associated clinical characteristics (sex, age, and ethnicity). The study period spanned a switch from the Macroduct® Collection System (MCS) to the Macroduct® Advanced Collection System (MACS). All operators were experienced at ST and samples were collected in line with ECFS guidelines. Method: All patients (≥16y) who underwent bilateral diagnostic ST over 62 months (MCS 2017-2019; MACS 2023-2025) were included and data collected...
presentations.authors_text: R. Robinson1, L. Weitnauer1, E. Russell-Jones1, A. Downes1, A. Peres1, D. Office1, R. Dunk1, E. Diable1, B. O'Connor1, A. Jones1, I. Felton1,2, N.J. Simmonds1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Royal Brompton Hospital, part of Guys and St Thomas' NHS Foundation Trust, Department of Cystic Fibrosis, London, United Kingdom, 2National Heart and Lung Institute, Imperial College London, London, United Kingdom

sections.Methods: All patients (≥16y) who underwent bilateral diagnostic ST over 62 months (MCS 2017-2019; MACS 2023-2025) were included and data collected from medical records. Patients were grouped by QNS sample at first visit: 0 QNS vs ≥1 QNS. Chi2 and Mann Whitney U test were used to assess for statistical difference (p>0.05).
sections.Results: The rate of QNS samples was no different between MCS (9%) and MACS (10%; p=0.73) so results for both devices were combined. 261 patients (median age [IQR]: 39[25-50]yr, 157(60%) female) were included. There was no significant difference between sexes, 92 (88%) males 0 QNS vs 135 (86%) females, p=0.56). Age difference did not reach statistical significance (0 QNS: median age [IQR]= 38 [26-50] vs ≥1 QNS: 40.5 [29-56], p=0.17). Comparison between those identified as Asian 36 (14%) vs White 155 (59%), confirmed a higher proportion of QNS in Asian patients (22% vs 8%; p=0.03). Due to small numbers and/or lack of disclosure 47 (18%) further analysis was not possible for other ethnicities (Black 2 (1%), Mixed 6 (2%), Other 15 (6%)).
sections.Conclusion: Bilateral ST at our adult centre ensured QNS rates within recommended standards. Of particular importance is the Asian population as they are more likely to have ≥1 QNS and may require repeat ST. Whilst not statistically significant, older age may also be associated with ≥1 QNS. Further, large-scale prospective studies are required to confirm these findings.

sections.Background: The sweat test remains the gold standard for confirming cystic fibrosis (CF) diagnosis, requiring experienced personnel (≥80 tests/year), approved equipment, and adherence to (inter)national guidelines. We report our center's experience to identify failure modes and protocol improvements.
sections.Methods: Retrospective analysis of sweat tests performed at the CF Centre for Paediatrics and Adults, Zagreb, using Macroduct™ collection by nurses whose experience exceeds 80 tests/year each. Data spanned March 2024–December 2025 (n=304 tests in children).
sections.Results: Of 304 tests, 14 (4.6%) yielded insufficient sweat (<15 μL) and 2 (0.7%) had analytical errors. Among 16 insufficient samples, CF was diagnosed in 2/16 via repeat testing. Age distribution: 4/16 <1 year, 9/16 aged 1–3 years, 2/16 aged 4–5 years, 1/16 ≥6 years. Primary failure cause was inadequate patient preparation (poor hydration; 9/16). Collector-arm size mismatch affected 2/16 (neonates >2000 g, appropriate gestational age), with performance errors (loose collector/air leak; 1/16) and absent sweat (1/16). Repeat tests used arm (13/16) or leg (3/16). Our quantity non sufficient rates was significantly below Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute C34—A4 limits.
sections.Conclusion: This audit identified hydration as the leading cause of insufficient sweat collection, prompting protocol revisions emphasizing pre-test hydration. Neonatal and infant collector-arm disproportion warrants industry and Society focus to develop appropriately sized devices, enhancing diagnostic reliability in young infants.

sections.Objectives: To identify the features of MGC in families with a high genetic risk of CF and to evaluate the effectiveness of its implementation.
sections.Methods: The results of a retrospective MGC were analyzed for the period from 2001 to 2024. 72 families in which 81 children (43 boys and 38 girls) suffered with CF. The methods of obtaining fetal material for prenatal DNA diagnostics were chorionic biopsy at 9 – 11 weeks of pregnancy or amniocentesis at 16-18 weeks of pregnancy.
sections.Results: The share of "informative" families in the analysis of the CFTR gene in the studied families was 66.7% (48 families). At the same time, both mutations were identified in 27.8%, and 1 mutation in the CFTR gene was identified in 25.9%. In 33.7% of families, both mutations of the CFTR gene were not detected. 38.8% (28) of the families which are informative decided to continue childbearing, 15 of them used the method of prenatal diagnosis (one family used prenatal diagnosis in three pregnancies). In all these families, the birth of a child with CF was prevented: in 3 cases, 2 mutations in the CFTR gene were detected and the pregnancy was terminated. 46.4% of families (13 out of 28) did not consult a geneticist when planning pregnancy, 8 families(11.1%) out of 72 consulted had children with CF, the total effectiveness of MGC in the surveyed families with CF was 81.9%.
sections.Conclusion: MGC is an essential part of medical care for families with hereditary CF, its effectiveness is 81.9%.

presentations.title: Discontinuation of cystic fibrosis clinical studies: a cross-sectional analysis
presentations.summary: Introduction: Clinical studies in cystic fibrosis are susceptible to early discontinuation and non-publication, contributing to research waste and limiting evidence generation. This study aimed to characterize registered clinical studies in cystic fibrosis and identify factors associated with trial discontinuation and non-dissemination. Methods: We searched ClinicalTrials.gov for all clinical studies related to cystic fibrosis up to December 2025. Data on enrollment size, study type, study location, and funding source were extracted and analyzed using binomial logistic regression. Results: Among 1,279 eligible clinical studies, 1,100 (85.5%) were completed and 179 (14.5%) were discontinued. Studies enrolling m...
presentations.authors_text: Y.R. Alnomani1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Faculty of Medicine, Benha University, Benha, Egypt

sections.Methods: We searched ClinicalTrials.gov for all clinical studies related to cystic fibrosis up to December 2025. Data on enrollment size, study type, study location, and funding source were extracted and analyzed using binomial logistic regression.
sections.Results: Among 1,279 eligible clinical studies, 1,100 (85.5%) were completed and 179 (14.5%) were discontinued. Studies enrolling more than 100 participants had significantly lower odds of discontinuation (OR = 0.35, 95% CI 0.20–0.61; P < 0.001). Observational studies had decreased odds of discontinuation compared with interventional studies (OR = 0.53, 95% CI 0.33–0.88; P = 0.013). Trial discontinuation did not differ significantly between industry-funded and non–industry-funded studies (OR = 1.13, 95% CI 0.74–1.73; P = 0.059), nor between single-center and multicenter studies (OR = 1.08, 95% CI 0.72–1.64; P = 0.70)
sections.Conclusion: A notable proportion of cystic fibrosis clinical studies are discontinued or remain unpublished, indicating persistent non-dissemination bias. These findings highlight ethical concerns regarding participant involvement without corresponding knowledge gain and underscore the need for improved trial feasibility and stronger dissemination practices in cystic fibrosis research.

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) was developed to improve F508del-CFTR function in people with CF. Recently, ETI was approved for people with CF and at least one non-class I mutation. However, the efficacy of many rare variants is unknown. Therefore, we tested the rescue of CFTR activity by ETI in vitro and in vivo in non-F508del people with CF to improve understanding of mutation-specific CFTR modulator responses.
sections.Methods: Highly differentiated primary nasal epithelial cells (pHNEC) were established from 54 people with CF and at least one rare variant and no F508del allele and 29 healthy controls. CFTR activity was assessed by Ussing chamber measurements after ETI/DMSO incubation and expressed as % of healthy CFTR function. In 12 people with CF, who received ETI therapy, we assessed lung function as well as sweat chloride concentration (SCC), intestinal current measurements (ICM) and nasal potential difference (NPD) at baseline and with ETI.
sections.Results: 37 people with CF showed no increased CFTR activity in pHNEC with ETI compared to DMSO. However, 17 people with CF showed ETI response with a mean correction of CFTR activity of 20% of healthy in pHNEC. Of these, 12 people with CF received ETI therapy and showed a mean improvement in ppFEV1 of 12% and reduced SCC of -36mmol/L with ETI. In ICM, the cAMP-dependent chloride secretory response increased from -6µA/cm2 to 98µA/cm2 after ETI initiation and the total chloride secretory response increased from -18µA/cm2 to 130 µA/cm2. In a subgroup of 7 people with CF treated with ETI, total chloride response in NPD increased from 2.8mV to -12mV with ETI. ​
sections.Conclusion: We identified non-F508del CFTR variants that show a response to ETI in vitro and result in improvements in ppFEV1%, SCC, ICM and NPD after initiation of ETI. Our data show that in vitro CFTR modulator testing in pHNEC with confirmation of therapeutic effects with in vivo CFTR biomarkers provides a promising approach for personalized medicine.

We evaluated CFTR function in human nasal epithelial cell (HNEC) cultures from CFSPID/CRMS individuals using Ussing chambers and assessed their response to elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) to explore whether in vitro functional profiling could stratify disease risk. Methods: HNECs fro...
presentations.authors_text: L. Borek Dohalska1, T. Dousova2, Z. Varenyiova2, J. Bartosova2, P. Drevinek1
presentations.affiliations_text: 12nd Faculty of Medicine, Charles University and Motol University Hospital, Department of Medical Microbiology, Praha 5, Czech Republic, 22nd Faculty of Medicine, Charles University and Motol University Hospital, Department of Pediatrics, Praha 5, Czech Republic

sections.Objectives: Infants with positive newborn screening for cystic fibrosis (CF) but low or intermediate sweat chloride levels and/or inconclusive CFTR genotypes are classified as CFSPID (CF screen-positive, inconclusive diagnosis) or CFTR-related metabolic syndrome (CRMS). While most individuals remain healthy, 10–40 % later develop CF or CFTR-related disorders (CFTR-RD). Reliable predictors of disease progression are lacking. We evaluated CFTR function in human nasal epithelial cell (HNEC) cultures from CFSPID/CRMS individuals using Ussing chambers and assessed their response to elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) to explore whether in vitro functional profiling could stratify disease risk.
sections.Methods: HNECs from 30 CFSPID/CRMS individuals (four individuals later reclassified as CF) and 10 healthy controls (WT) were analysed. HNECs were studied untreated or after 48 h pretreatment with 3 μM E and 3 μM T. Short-circuit current (ISC) was measured following sequential stimulation and inhibition, and CFTR function expressed as a percentage of WT activity. Changes after ET pretreatment were compared with those observed in CF-reclassified individuals.
sections.Results: CFTR function in CFSPID/CRMS-derived HNECs was highly heterogeneous, ranging from 22 % to 100 % of WT. Thirteen exhibited residual CFTR function comparable to or lower than that observed in CF-reclassified subjects. When ranked by decreasing in vitro responsiveness to ETI, individuals later reclassified as CF clustered within the upper half (positions 4, 7, 10, 12). The three highest-ranked individuals have been followed for only 2 – 8 years and may not represent true negative cases.
sections.Conclusion: In vitro assessment of ETI responsiveness in HNECs may help stratify CFSPID/CRMS individuals and identify those at higher risk of progression to CF or CFTR-RD. Validation in larger cohorts with longer follow-up is needed. Supported by Ministry of Health of the Czech Republic, grant no NU23-07-00079.

sections.Methods: The degree of EMT was compared between CFBE 41o− cells carrying the p.Phe508del-CFTR and wt-CFTR through the level of epithelial and mesenchymal markers by fluorescence microscopy, RT-qPCR and ELISA. EMT was further induced by TGFβ1 in p.Phe508del-CFTR CFBE cells from 24-120h to investigate the relationship between the development of EMT and HE4/MMP9 expression. CFTR dysfunction was restored by VX-445/VX-661/VX-770 to monitor EMT phenotype. The direct effect of HE4 on EMT and MMP9 expression was analyzed after HE4 level was artificially reduced by transfection with HE4 specific siRNA. Cell proliferation was evaluated via Ki67 positivity.
sections.Results: CFBE cells expressing p.Phe508del-CFTR were prone to be more mesenchymal and showed higher basal HE4 and MMP9 expression than wt-CFTR CFBE cells. In response to TGFβ1, even lower E-cadherin with higher N-cadherin and MMP9 levels were observed in p.Phe508del-CFTR CFBE cells vs untreated cells. In contrast, HE4 expression decreased after 48h and further reduced up to 120h. The EMT phenotype could be reversed by the CFTR modulator treatment causing normalized HE4 and MMP9 levels. Finally, downregulated HE4 promoted EMT with induced MMP9 expression and reduced cell proliferation in p.Phe508del-CFTR CFBE cells.
sections.Conclusion: EMT is accompanied with decreasing HE4 expression and upregulated MMP9 expression in the airway epithelial cells of CF. Grants: This study was supported by FK-135327 grant (BN) and UID/04046/2025 centre grant in MDA lab (to BioISI) from FCT/MCTES Portugal.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Maternal Child Centre of the North (CMIN), Santo António Local Health Unit (ULSSA), Department of Pediatrics, Porto, Portugal, 2Santo António Local Health Unit (ULSSA), Department of Internal Medicine, Porto, Portugal, 3Maternal Child Centre of the North (CMIN), Santo António Local Health Unit (ULSSA), Cystic Fibrosis Reference Centre, Porto, Portugal

sections.Background: As survival improves in cystic fibrosis (CF), pregnancy has emerged as a clinical scenario with heterogeneous maternal and fetal outcomes.
sections.Objectives: To compare maternal and neonatal outcomes in four pregnancies in women with CF.
sections.Methods: Retrospective case series from a CF centre.
sections.Results: Four women (P1–P4) with heterogeneous genotypes and disease severity were included. P1 (F508del/W1282X; FEV₁ ~85–90% predicted) had severe exocrine pancreatic insufficiency requiring long-term gastrostomy feeding and poor nutritional reserve. She remained on elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) during pregnancy with stable respiratory status but inadequate weight gain; intrauterine fetal death occurred at 32+3 weeks despite unremarkable fetal investigations. P2(F508del/F508del; advanced CF lung disease) had frequent pulmonary exacerbations, chronic airway infection and prior lung transplant assessment. Treatment adherence was poor, with uncertain ETI exposure. She developed severe hypoxaemic respiratory infection and sepsis at 33–34 weeks requiring intensive care. Urgent caesarean section at 34 weeks resulted in a preterm neonate with favourable short-term outcome. P3 (F508del/A561E; FEV₁ ~55–60% predicted) continued ETI throughout pregnancy, remained clinically stable and delivered at term by caesarean section; neonatal outcome was favourable, with transient respiratory adaptation. P4 (N1303K/R334W; FEV₁ ~60–65% predicted) wasn't receiving ETI during pregnancy and had a stable maternal course; caesarean delivery resulted in a low-birthweight infant with good neonatal adaptation.
sections.Conclusion: Maternal respiratory complications were associated with advanced lung disease, severe nutritional compromise and poor treatment adherence, while fetal outcomes were variable and not clearly related to CFTR genotype or modulator exposure. Baseline disease severity, nutritional status and treatment adherence are key factors when counselling pregnancy in women with CF.

sections.Objectives: Direct measurement of lipolysis, proteolysis and amylolysis in chyme from different gastrointestinal compartments could ensure a natural environment to measure efficacy and stability of enzymes from pancreatic enzymes replacement therapy (PERT) in the upper small intestine. Stability and efficacy of a novel broad spectrum microbially-derived product (ANG003) and porcine-derived pancrelipase was estimated in chyme from exocrine pancreatic insufficient (EPI) pig model.
sections.Methods: Five EPI pigs were tested. Four weeks after pancreatic duct ligation, cannulas for chyme collection were placed in the stomach, duodenum and ileum. Pigs were fed a high fat meal (1) without enzymes, (2) with two doses of ANG003 (lipase 80-120mg, protease 50-75mg, amylase 120-160mg and (3) with 50,000 pancrelipase units; Kreonâ. Chyme was collected during 360 minutes after feeding and enzyme activity was estimated at pH 6.0 by measuring the release of oleic acid, tyrosine and glucose.
sections.Results: ANG003 showed significantly higher activity of lipase and amylase in stomach and duodenal chyme than Kreon. ANG003 lipase maintained activity from the stomach to the duodenum demonstrating its high stability. Kreon lipase activity in the stomach and duodenum didn’t differ from no enzyme condition likely due to the enteric coating. ANG003 protease was released in the upper small intestine whereas Kreon protease appeared in essential amounts in the ileum.
sections.Conclusion: The ANG003 enzymes had an earlier and more physiological activity profile compared to Kreon. The stabilization of ANG003 lipase makes it immediately active in the stomach and duodenum. The earlier activity of all three ANG003 enzymes stands in contrast to Kreon activity, which revealed minimal lipase and amylase activity in upper small intestine, while excessive protease activity in the ileum. Measuring enzymes activity in chyme from EPI pigs can be a useful tool to assess PERT efficacy in natural prandial conditions.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1The Children's Hospital for Wales, Paediatric Respiratory Medicine, Cardiff, United Kingdom, 2University Hospital of Wales, All Wales Adult CF Centre, Cardiff, United Kingdom, 3Public Health Wales, Microbiology Department, Cardiff, United Kingdom, 4Cardiff University, Cardiff, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: The CF-SpIT trials in children show sputum-induction to be an effective approach to pathogen detection both in clinic and at home. CF-Adult_HomeSpIT [ISRCTN86523335] is a prospective internally-controlled interventional trial in 100 adults comparing home-sampling using spontaneous early morning cough-clear (emCC) and home sputum-induction (hSI) with matched same-day clinic sampling using cough swab (CS), spontaneous clinic cough-clear (cCC) and clinic sputum-induction (cSI). Here we present preliminary qualitative data exploring feasibility & acceptability of home sampling in adults with CF.
sections.Methods: Patients at the All Wales Adult CF Centre already using hypertonic saline (HTS) were eligible for recruitment. emCC was obtained on waking, using 3 rounds of standardised expectoration: COUGH, HUFF, COUGH, CLEAR THROAT (hawk), and SPIT. hSI was obtained using HS with normal home physiotherapy routine, using the same standardised expectoration. Same-day clinic samples (cCC and cSI) were taken after routine CS by a physiotherapist using standardised procedures and expectoration.
sections.Results: To date 25 patients have performed sampling events. Median age 29yrs. Median FEV% 81%. 88% were on modulator therapy. Median sweat Cl- 55mmol/L. 4% had a wet cough. All patients produced emCC and hSI samples (median duration 15 minutes). 16/25 (64%) grew pathogens from all samples combined. 46% of samples were described as mucoid, purulent or mucopurulent on lab processing. 23/25 (92%) and 20/25 (80%) of patients would do emCC and hSI again respectively, with a median chosen frequency of 4/year. Ease of both home procedures on clinic day were rated independantly at median of 0 on a Likert scale of 0-10 (0=easy).
sections.Conclusion: Home-based sputum-sampling on clinic day is feasible and acceptable to adults with cystic fibrosis and could be implemented into routine care. Pathogen detection from home samples compared to clinic samples will be presented at the end of the trial.

Here, we aimed to evaluate the effects of ETI on Pa burden and innate immune responses in children with CF. Sputum concentrations of antimicrobial peptides (including Secretory leukocyte protease inhibitor (SLPI)) were measure...
presentations.authors_text: F. Kanoun1, Y. Sereme2, I. Jeguirim1, M. Kelly-Aubert1, I. Haï1, F. Zavala1, O. Tabary1, I. Sermet-Gaudelus1,3,4
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Institut Necker Enfants Malades, Paris, France, 2Institut National de la Santé et de la Recherche Médicale, Paris, France, 3Service de Coprologie Fonctionnelle, Hôpital Universitaire Pitié Salpétrière, Paris, France, 4European Rare Disease Network, Lung, Frankfurt, Germany

sections.Objectives: P. aeruginosa (Pa) lung infections are the leading cause of mortality in Cystic Fibrosis (CF), typically chronic and punctuated by exacerbations. The advent of triple combination therapy (ETI) has reshaped the clinical course of CF, with substantial airway microbiome changes, including a marked reduction in Pa. Here, we aimed to evaluate the effects of ETI on Pa burden and innate immune responses in children with CF.
sections.Methods: Longitudinal sputum cultures were analysed at baseline and at 1 year of ETI therapy allowing classification of patients as chronic, intermittent, or negative. Sputum concentrations of antimicrobial peptides (including Secretory leukocyte protease inhibitor (SLPI)) were measured before and after therapy. To complement these in vivo experiments, primary nasal epithelial cells from healthy donors, Phe508del individuals, and patients carrying a Premature Termination Codon (PTC) were cultured at the air-liquid interface, treated with ETI or vehicle and infected with Pa. Bacterial growth was assessed across genotypes. Experiments were repeated in the presence of a neutralizing anti-SLPI antibody.
sections.Results: After 1 year of ETI therapy, and in the absence of concurrent antibiotic treatment, a subset of patients cleared Pa from their airways. In sputum, concentrations of SLPI increased after treatment (p<0.01). The pattern observed in sputum cultures data was recapitulated ex vivo in Pa-infected primary epithelial cells. Incubation with ETI reduced bacterial growth in cells from CF patients (p<0.05), but not in cells from healthy donors or individuals carrying a PTC. The observed reduction was not due to a direct antibiotic effect, indicating a host-mediated effect. SLPI neutralization reduced the ETI-mediated inhibition of bacterial growth, suggesting a key role for SLPI in controlling Pa under therapy.
sections.Conclusion: ETI enhances host antimicrobial defences, modulating infection burden and airway innate immunity in children with CF.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Queen's University Belfast, School of Pharmacy, Belfast, United Kingdom, 2University of Liverpool, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 3University of Manchester, Manchester, United Kingdom, 4Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust, Manchester, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Accurate detection of pathogens in cystic fibrosis (CF) lung infection is essential and traditionally relied on sputum culture. Saliva represents a non-invasive, easily obtainable alternative sample for people with CF (pwCF). This study aimed to determine the limit of detection (LoD) of Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) in spiked saliva samples using real-time quantitative PCR (qPCR).
sections.Methods: Spiking assays (n=4) were performed using Pa reference isolate (ATCC 27853) and a clinical isolate (0293A) across a dilution range of 1 x 108 – 1 x 101 CFU/mL in pooled saliva from healthy volunteers (n=6). Total viable counts (TVC) were determined by culture on Pseudomonas selective agar. Genomic DNA was extracted using the MagNA Pure 96 system (Roche), and qPCR targeting oprL performed in triplicate. The LoD was defined as the lowest concentration producing Cp values below those of negative controls across replicates.
sections.Results: In spiked saliva, the qPCR LoD was determined as any sample yielding a Cp value below that of the negative control, and corresponded to a mean concentration of 1.18x104 CFU/ml. Above the LoD, no significant differences were observed between total viable counts (CFU/mL) and oprL qPCR copy numbers for corresponding serial dilutions (p > 0.05). qPCR achieved 100% detection of both Pa isolates at concentrations above the LoD.
sections.Conclusion: This work demonstrates the feasibility of detecting Pa in saliva, supporting its potential use as a non-invasive matrix for infection detection in pwCF. Future studies will focus on evaluation in clinical saliva samples. Funded through PULSE-CF by the CF-Trust and LifeArc.

Traditional antibiotic susceptibility testing (AST) is typically performed on a very limited number of individual isolates and therefore often fails to accurately predict clinical responses in chronic infections, as it does not capture population-level behaviour. The aim of this project was to investigate the extent to which individual isolates reflect the antibiotic susceptibility o...
presentations.authors_text: H. Richardson1, M. Shepherd1, R. Wright1, C. Igler1, M. Brockhurst1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Manchester, Manchester, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Chronic lung infection with Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a major cause of morbidity in individuals with chronic lung infections like cystic fibrosis (CF). Extensive phenotypic and genotypic adaptation during chronic infection results in highly heterogeneous bacterial populations with varying susceptibility to antibiotics. Traditional antibiotic susceptibility testing (AST) is typically performed on a very limited number of individual isolates and therefore often fails to accurately predict clinical responses in chronic infections, as it does not capture population-level behaviour. The aim of this project was to investigate the extent to which individual isolates reflect the antibiotic susceptibility of heterogeneous P. aeruginosa populations within chronically infected patients.
sections.Methods: A unique global collection of P. aeruginosa isolates obtained from patients with bronchiectasis prior to a phase III clinical trial of inhaled liposomal ciprofloxacin was used to assess resistance behaviour in heterogeneous populations. For each patient, 90 P. aeruginosa isolates were analysed, enabling characterisation of within-host diversity through growth curve analysis and determination of minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) for ciprofloxacin, colistin, levofloxacin, tobramycin, and aztreonam.
sections.Results: The results demonstrate that individual isolate behaviour can predict antibiotic susceptibility of pathogen populations. A mathematical model shows that increasing the number of isolates sampled leads to resistance prediction for populations that approach the maximum observed resistance among isolates of the population, but mean resistance remains a better predictor of population behaviour.
sections.Conclusion: These findings highlight the limitations of using single isolates for AST and emphasise the importance of accounting for population-level heterogeneity when guiding antibiotic therapy in CF care.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Galway, School of medicine, Galway, Ireland, 2Queens University Belfast, Belfast, United Kingdom, 3Galway University Hospital, Galway, Ireland

sections.Objectives: This longitudinal study aimed to compare conventional culture and targeted quantitative Polymerase Chain Reaction (qPCR) for the early detection of CF-associated pathogens and to characterise airway microbial community composition.
sections.Methods: Fifteen paediatric CF patients attending Galway University Hospital (GUH) provided 4 sputum samples each during clinically stable visits over a one-year period. Samples (n=60) were tested using culture and qPCR to detect and quantify Pseudomonas aeruginosa (PA), Haemophilus influenzae (HI) and Staphylococcus aureus (SA). Airway microbial community composition was assessed using 16S rRNA gene sequencing. Ethical approval was obtained from the GUH Clinical Research Ethics Committee.
sections.Results: Of 15 patients, 60% male, mean (SD) age 11.7 (2.7) years, FEV1 95.3 (17) %. While culture did not detect PA in any sample, targeted qPCR identified PA in 4/15 patients (26.7%) who were culture-negative. Out of the 4 patients, 3 were qPCR-positive on all 4 samples and for 1 patient PA was not detected in the initial sample but was detected in all 3 patient subsequent samples. Bacterial loads were consistently low (10⁰–10¹ copies), indicating low-level molecular detection that may represent early or transient airway presence rather than established colonisation and was not detected by culture. Furthermore, qPCR detected HI in 43.3% (26/60) and SA in 21.7% (13/60) samples, compared with 3.3% (2/60) and 36.7% (22/60) by culture; respectively. 16S rRNA gene sequencing revealed a diverse and patient-specific airway microbiome dominated by Streptococcus, Veillonella, Haemophilus and Prevotella species, with marked inter-individual variability and temporal stability across visits.
sections.Conclusion: In paediatric CF patients, targeted qPCR detected PA DNA in patients where PA was not detected by culture. The significance of this as a guide to treatment and prognosis is unclear but merits further investigation.

sections.Objectives: Despite advances with CFTR modulators, children with cystic fibrosis (CF) continue to experience recurrent bacterial pulmonary exacerbations, increasingly complicated by reduced β-lactam susceptibility. Our aim was to evaluated pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) target attainment following implementation of a therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) service, using free trough-to-MIC ratios (fCmin/MIC) ≥1 and ≥4 as targets.
sections.Methods: A center-specific β-lactam TDM protocol was introduced alongside multidisciplinary education. Meropenem and piperacillin–tazobactam were initially administered as 60-minute infusions. When free drug concentrations failed to meet PK/PD targets, infusion duration was prolonged or switched to continuous infusion, accounting for individual tolerability.
sections.Results: Five pediatric patients were included (four received meropenem, one piperacillin–tazobactam). Standard 1-hour infusions failed to achieve PK/PD targets in all cases. Prolonged infusion was not tolerated in one patient. Among three patients receiving prolonged meropenem infusions (3 hours), trough concentrations were unavailable in two cases. In contrast, the patient treated with continuous piperacillin–tazobactam infusion via an elastomeric pump achieved the predefined PK/PD target.
sections.Conclusion: TDM-guided β-lactam dosing is feasible and clinically informative in pediatric CF care but presents practical challenges. Achieving PK/PD targets may require individualized infusion strategies, particularly in the setting of reduced antimicrobial susceptibility. sections.Background: Cystic fibrosis is a multisystem genetic disorder in which progressive pulmonary involvement remains the leading cause of morbidity and mortality. Pulmonary exacerbations accelerate lung function decline. Early diagnosis through newborn screening enables timely intervention and systematic follow-up. This study evaluated the frequency of pulmonary exacerbations and associated microbiological findings in children diagnosed through newborn screening.
sections.Methods: A retrospective observational study included 19 children diagnosed via national newborn screening and followed at the paediatric cystic fibrosis centre, University Children’s Clinic, Skopje, North Macedonia. All pulmonary exacerbations recorded between January 2019 and December 2024 were analysed. Exacerbations were defined as clinical deterioration requiring inpatient intravenous antibiotic therapy. Clinical and microbiological data from respiratory samples were extracted from medical records.
sections.Results: Nineteen patients experienced 83 pulmonary exacerbations, with a median of 3 exacerbations per patient per year. Increased cough was the most frequent presenting symptom. One exacerbation occurred in 17.6% of patients, one to 2 in 23.5%, and more than 3 in 58.8%. Staphylococcus aureus was the most frequently isolated pathogen, followed by Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Escherichia coli. Recurrent exacerbations were more often associated with persistent bacterial colonisation.
sections.Conclusion: Despite early diagnosis through newborn screening, children with cystic fibrosis continue to experience a high burden of pulmonary exacerbations, frequently linked to persistent airway infection. Early detection of pathogens such as P. aeruginosa and methicillin-resistant S. aureus allows timely treatment to prevent chronic colonisation and progressive lung damage. Systematic microbiological surveillance and regular follow-up remain essential to optimise long-term respiratory outcomes.

Reducing treatment burden is a key CF care priority, with recent studies reporting non-inferiority from stopping nebulised mucolytics. Long-term value of inhaled antibiotics in people taking ETI is unclear, with some opting to discontinue therapy. sections.Objectives: Improvements in lung function (ppFEV1), airway microbiology and pulmonary exacerbations (PE) are established benefits of ETI. Reducing treatment burden is a key CF care priority, with recent studies reporting non-inferiority from stopping nebulised mucolytics. Long-term value of inhaled antibiotics in people taking ETI is unclear, with some opting to discontinue therapy. This retrospective, single-centre observational study assessed impact of inhaled antibiotic adherence on ppFEV1 and PE frequency during the first 60 months of ETI treatment in CF patients.
sections.Methods: CF patients prescribed ETI and inhaled antibiotics at King’s College Hospital (2020-2025) were classified as adherent or poor/discontinued treatment based on self-reporting and clinical assessment. A subgroup was assessed by electronic nebuliser data and prescription refills for grouping validation. Data were extracted from clinical records and CF Registry database. Baseline characteristics were taken at annual review prior to ETI, with ppFEV1 and PE collected annually up to 60 months. Severity of PE was pre-defined, with severe events requiring IV antibiotics.
sections.Results: This study included 81 patients. Sustained improvements in ppFEV1 across 5-years was greater in patients with good (n=25) compared to poor (n=56) adherence, though this did not reach significance (p=0.052) (table 1). No difference was found in the number of mild/moderate (p=0.38), severe (p=0.45) and total (p=0.30) PE between groups. AdherenceBaseline Pre-ETI ppFEV1 (%)Change in ppFEV1 from baseline to Year 1 post-ETI (%)Change in ppFEV1 from baseline to Year 5 post-ETI (%) Rate of Exacerbation per person (per year)Good (n=25)60.0+9.93+7.911.8Poor (n=56)66.3+9.78+3.941.9
sections.Conclusion: Patients taking ETI with good adherence to inhaled antibiotics showed a trend towards maintaining gains in ppFEV1. Adherence had no impact on PE across 5 years. These pilot data support the need for a randomised clinical trial to assess the role for long-term inhaled antibiotics in CF patients taking ETI.

presentations.authors_text: A. Chan1, L. Rossiter1, D. Nazareth1,2, M. Ledson1, F. Frost1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Liverpool Heart and Chest Hospital, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 2University of Liverpool, Liverpool, United Kingdom

sections.Background: The Antibiotic Spectrum Index (ASI) is a measure of antimicrobial breadth and potential antimicrobial resistance (AMR) burden in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). Following the introduction of elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor (ETI), hospitalisations for pulmonary exacerbations have fallen substantially, but the impact on antibiotic selection and spectrum remains unclear. This study aim to review intravenous (IV) antibiotic use at a large adult CF centre, focusing on ASI before and after ETI initiation.
sections.Methods: All inpatient IV antibiotic courses between 1 June 2013 and 30 September 2025 were reviewed. Data collected included admission and discharge dates, ETI initiation dates, and CF-specific ASI scores based on Cogen et al. (JCF, 2024). Antibiotics not routinely used in the United States (temocillin and teicoplanin) were assigned ASI scores using published spectrum data. Analyses were limited to pwCF receiving ETI, with comparisons made pre- and post-ETI at population and individual levels.
sections.Results: In total, 2,830 IV antibiotic admissions in 322 pwCF were analysed. Annualised admissions decreased significantly after ETI initiation (326 per year pre-ETI vs 94 post-ETI, p = 0.002). Median ASI scores were unchanged (9.0 pre-ETI vs 9.0 post-ETI,median difference= 0, p = 0.13). In a paired analysis of 116 individuals, median ASI was 8.6 [8.1–9.0] pre-ETI and 8.8 [8.0–9.0] post-ETI (p = 0.51). ASI was not associated with time to next hospitalisation, with similar median scores between bottom and top quartiles (9 vs 9, median difference = 0, p = 0.49).
sections.Conclusion: Despite a marked reduction in hospitalisations following ETI, the antimicrobial spectrum of IV antibiotic regimens, as measured by ASI, remained unchanged and was not associated with short-term clinical outcomes. As life expectancy for pwCF increases, antimicrobial stewardship should prioritise reducing cumulative lifetime AMR burden rather than admission-level antibiotic spectrum alone.

presentations.title: Quantification of antimicrobial resistance in the CFTR modulator era: a retrospective analysis using the relative resistance index
presentations.summary: Introduction: Little is known about the dynamics of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) in P.aeruginosa (Pa) with long-term elexacaftor/ivacaftor/tezacaftor (ETI) treatment. The Relative Resistance Index (RRI) is a validated AMR quantification tool in Pa. This study therefore explored the impact of ETI on RRI. Study design: A retrospective, microbiological surveillance study was conducted at our large regional adult CF centre including consecutive isolates from people living with CF (pwCF) who started ETI. RRI was calculated from antibiograms of all Pa isolates using scores of 0,0.5,1 for reported S/I/R respectively and scored cumulatively for ciprofloxacin, ceftazidime, meropenem, piperacillin/tazobactam and tobra...
presentations.authors_text: H. Myat-Noe1, L. William1, L. Rossiter1, D. Nazareth1, G. Hyde1, M. Ledson1, M. Walshaw1, F. Frost1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Liverpool Heart and Chest Hospital, Liverpool, United Kingdom

sections.Results: We identified 207 pwCF receiving ETI with history of Pa at any time. A total of 8762 Pa isolates were available for analysis for this group. Overall, at the cohort level, median [IQR] RRI for isolates in the pre-ETI era was 0.3 (IQR 0.1-0.7). In the post-ETI era, the median RRI was 0.6 [0.4-0.8]. The median difference between the pre- and post-ETI RRI scores was +0.3, (Mann-Whitney test p < 0.0001). Of 207 pwCF with a Pa isolate pre-ETI, 80/207 did not have a positive post-ETI (38.7%). At the individual level 97 pwCF were positive for Pa before and after ETI and had available antibiogram, including 5551 isolates. Pre-ETI, median [IQR] RRI was 0.3 [0.2-0.5], post-ETI median RRI was 0.6 [0.4-0.8], median difference +0.26 (p<0.0001).
sections.Conclusion: A significant proportion of individuals are no longer culture positive for Pa, but in those that are, RRI has increased significantly in the post-ETI era. Whether this reflects general temporal increases in AMR, a modulator-specific effect or a subset of sputum-producing patients acting as reservoirs of resistance warrant further evaluation.

We report four cases of clinically significant swelling and fluid-associated weight gain following CFTR modulator therapy. Methods: We retrospectively reviewed four patients with CF or CF-related disorder who developed significant fluid-associated weight gain after CFTR modulator initiation. Data collected included demographics, CFTR genotype, pre- and post...
presentations.authors_text: E. Tillman1, C. Brown1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Indiana University, Medicine, Indianapolis, United States

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) modulators improve lung function and nutritional status in cystic fibrosis (CF). Weight gain is commonly attributed to improved appetite and metabolism, yet sustained hypervolemic edema or fluid-associated weight gain has not been described in clinical trials and remains under-recognized. We report four cases of clinically significant swelling and fluid-associated weight gain following CFTR modulator therapy.
sections.Methods: We retrospectively reviewed four patients with CF or CF-related disorder who developed significant fluid-associated weight gain after CFTR modulator initiation. Data collected included demographics, CFTR genotype, pre- and post-modulator body mass index (BMI), timing and pattern of weight change, clinical evidence of edema, laboratory evaluation, and, when available, cardiac and pharmacokinetic data. CFTR modulator dosing, dose modifications, treatment interruptions and responses were documented. The Naranjo Adverse Drug Reaction Probability Scale was used to assess causality.
sections.Results: All patients developed marked edema and rapid BMI increase temporally associated with CFTR modulator exposure. Mean pre-modulator BMI was 26.3±2.83 kg/m², increasing to 33.45±2.99 kg/m² post-exposure (p=0.05). Three patients required dose reduction and one discontinued therapy to improve swelling. No alternative cardiac, renal or hepatic cause of fluid retention was identified. Naranjo scores ranged from 8–9, indicating probable to definite association with CFTR modulator therapy.
sections.Conclusion: Sustained, fluid-associated weight gain may occur in a minority of patients receiving CFTR modulators and can necessitate dose reduction or discontinuation. Clinicians should consider this adverse effect when evaluating rapid weight gain or unexplained edema following CFTR modulator initiation. presentations.affiliations_text: 1Liverpool Heart and Chest, Liverpool, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Little is known about the consequences of inhaled antibiotic discontinuation for people receiving CFTR modulators. Within our large adult CF Centre, we reviewed the patient outcomes following the discontinuation of their nebulised antibiotic therapy.
sections.Methods: Completing a retrospective analysis at our large regional adult CF centre we reviewed all the cases of nebulised antibiotic discontinuation between 2000 and 2025. We categorised them into 3 groups; Group 1 (Patient choice), Group 2 (Low adherence but clinically stable), Group 3 (Clinician led due to clinical stability). The outcomes between the 3 groups, with a focus on the re-initiation of a nebulised antibiotic.
sections.Results: Of the 420 pwCF at our centre, 70 discontinued nebulised antibiotics between 2020 and 2025 (35 male, median FEV1 87% predicted, 20 Liverpool Epidemic Strain Pseudomonas aeruginosa). Overall, 16/70(22.9%) patients restarted their nebulised antibiotic due to clinical deterioration or new pathogen growth. Restart rates were similar between group 1 (26.7%) and group 3 (25%). Restart rates were significantly higher in those with FEV1 <80% predicted (41% vs. 15%, p=0.03). There was no difference in the restart rates between those with Liverpool epidemic strain P. aeruginosa compared to other P. aeruginosa strains (10% vs. 28%, p=0.12), and restart rates were no similar in those with and without recent P. aeruginosa growths prior to discontinuing their antibiotic nebuliser (31% vs. 20%, p=0.56).
sections.Conclusion: Nebulised antibiotics were discontinued without the need for restarting in over 75% of cases. Recent P. aeruginosa growth or stain was not associated with differential rates of restarting; however, restart rates were higher in those with lower lung function. These findings can inform discussions and decisions with patients considers inhaled antibiotic discontinuation.

We aimed to assess prescription trends at the University of Washington adult CF center. We hypothesized that there would be an initial drop in oral antibiotic use with ETI and the Covid pandemic, but that it would slowly rebound in the adult population. Methods: We performed a longitudinal single center cohort study assessing the temporal impact of ETI on the use of oral antibiotics from December 2014 to December 2024. We included patients who received an ETI prescription during this period. To assess temporal trends, we calculated monthly oral antibiotic prescription rates per patient encounte...
presentations.authors_text: J. Hernandez1, A. Pichler2, O. McElvaney2, S. Kapnadak2, K. Ramos2,3, S. Heltshe4,3, T. Ye1,3, C. Goss2,4,3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Washington, Biostatistics, Seattle, United States, 2University of Washington, Medicine, Seattle, United States, 3Seattle Children's Research Institute, Pediatrics, Seattle, United States, 4University of Washington, Pediatrics, Seattle, United States

sections.Objectives: ETI has been demonstrated to have a significant impact on lung function, symptoms and exacerbation rates. We aimed to assess prescription trends at the University of Washington adult CF center. We hypothesized that there would be an initial drop in oral antibiotic use with ETI and the Covid pandemic, but that it would slowly rebound in the adult population.
sections.Methods: We performed a longitudinal single center cohort study assessing the temporal impact of ETI on the use of oral antibiotics from December 2014 to December 2024. We included patients who received an ETI prescription during this period. To assess temporal trends, we calculated monthly oral antibiotic prescription rates per patient encounter.
sections.Results: We had a total of 376 CF individuals who fulfilled inclusion criteria. Uptake of ETI was quite rapid with 40% of the cohort receiving ETI by March 2020. During the study period, 82% of individuals received at least one prescription for oral antibiotics. The most common antibiotics used were levofloxacin (34%), trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (31%), ciprofloxacin (16%), amoxicillin-clavulanate (8%) and cephalexin (7%). When assessing prescription trends, we observed an initial decline in monthly prescriptions per encounter following ETI approval and the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic. Prescription rates later rebounded in 2023–2024. Average monthly antibiotic rate/100 encounters was 9.84 in 2019, 5.41 from 2020-22, and 7.56 from 2023-2024. Formal time series analysis will be presented for both oral and intravenous antibiotic use.
sections.Conclusion: Oral antibiotic use appears to have decreased with both the approval of ETI and the Covid pandemic only to rebound by the end of 2024 in this population.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Unidade Local de Saúde de Coimbra, Coimbra, Portugal, 2Unidade Local de Saúde de São José, Lisbon, Portugal, 3Unidade Local de Saúde de Santa Maria, Lisboa, Portugal, 4Unidade Local de Saúde de Santo António, Porto, Portugal, 5Unidade Local de Saúde de São João, Porto, Portugal

sections.Methods: Multicenter observational study including 16 CF patients with chronic Bcc infection followed at referral centers in Portugal. Patients under follow-up between 2020 and the present who initiated elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) were identified. Clinical data, respiratory cultures and spirometric parameters (FEV1 and FVC, % predicted) were retrospectively reviewed to assess the impact of ETI on microbiological status and lung function.
sections.Results: Mean age at Bcc identification was 24.8 ± 8.7 years, with a male predominance (56.3%). 8 patients were homozygous for F508del, 7 were heterozygous, and 1 carried two non–class I CFTR mutations. Pseudomonas aeruginosa was concurrently isolated in three patients. Intravenous antibiotic eradication therapy and inhaled suppressive antibiotic therapy were each used in 13 cases. After 12 months of ETI, mean FEV1 increased from 51.5 ± 21.0% to 72.1 ± 22.8%, and mean FVC from 69.8 ± 26.7% to 85.5 ± 16.6%. Microbiological eradication of Bcc was observed in 11 patients (69%), occurring at a mean of 29.0 ± 19.7 months (median 25.6 months, IQR 10.9–45.2) after ETI initiation.
sections.Conclusion: In this multicenter cohort, ETI initiation was associated with significant improvement in lung function and with microbiological clearance of chronic Bcc infection in a substantial proportion of patients. These findings suggest a possible link between CFTR modulation and Bcc eradication, although the relative contribution of ETI versus concomitant antimicrobial therapy remains uncertain and warrants further investigation.

This study aims to evaluate longitudinal changes in expectorating ability (EA) and respiratory microbiological profiles during the first three years of ETI. Methods: A prospective study was conducted at the Regional Cystic Fibrosis Center in Milan from 1st September 2020 to 30th September 2025. A cohort of pwCF starting ETI were followed for up to 36 months. EA and the prevalence of key respiratory pathogens (from sputum and nasopharyngeal aspirates) were evaluate...
presentations.authors_text: B.S. Orena1, F. Girardi1, E. Tomassini1, D. Girelli1, A. Teri1, M. D'Accico1, F. Blasi2,3, V. Daccò4, A. Callegaro1, L. Cariani1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Fondazione IRCCS CA’ Granda, Ospedale Maggiore Policlinico, Clinical Microbiology and Virology Unit, Milan, Italy, 2Fondazione IRCCS Ca’ Granda Ospedale Maggiore Policlinico, Internal Medicine Department, Respiratory Unit and Cystic Fibrosis Center, Milan, Italy, 3University of Milan, Department of Pathophysiology and Transplantation, Milan, Italy, 4Fondazione IRCCS Ca’ Granda, Ospedale Maggiore Policlinico, Mother and Child Department, Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Milan, Italy

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor–tezacaftor–ivacaftor therapy (ETI) significantly improves lung function and reduces pulmonary exacerbations in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). However, its long-term effects on airway colonization are incompletely defined. This study aims to evaluate longitudinal changes in expectorating ability (EA) and respiratory microbiological profiles during the first three years of ETI.
sections.Methods: A prospective study was conducted at the Regional Cystic Fibrosis Center in Milan from 1st September 2020 to 30th September 2025. A cohort of pwCF starting ETI were followed for up to 36 months. EA and the prevalence of key respiratory pathogens (from sputum and nasopharyngeal aspirates) were evaluated at baseline (T0) and after 12 (T12), 24 (T24), and 36 months (T36) of therapy. Statistical analisys was carried out using chi-square and Fisher’s exact tests.
sections.Results: A total of 367 pwCF (205 males; median age 25 years, IQR 14–36.5) were enrolled. Over three years of ETI therapy, EA significantly decreased (81.7% at T12 to 66% at T36; p < 0.001). Significant reductions were observed in Pseudomonas aeruginosa (33.8% → 15.2%, p < 0.001), mucoid P. aeruginosa (30.5% → 21.7%, p < 0.05), Achromobacter xylosoxidans (15.8% → 8.1%, p < 0.05), rapidly growing mycobacteria (RGM) (4.9% → 0.5%, p < 0.01), and Aspergillus spp. (19.1% → 2%, p < 0.001). Non-significant changes were seen for Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, and Burkholderia cepacia complex. Stenotrophomonas maltophilia decreased significantly until T24 (11.2% → 2.7%, p < 0.001) but not at T36 (7.1%, p > 0.05).
sections.Conclusion: After three years of ETI therapy, reduced expectorating ability likely reflects clinical improvement and lower mucus production. Microbiological trends suggest a shift toward a chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)-like profile, indicating that ETI remodels the respiratory microbiota in pwCF and highlighting the need for ongoing long-term monitoring. In addition, due to the increasing number of non-expectorating pwCF, it will be necessary to identify a new biological sample to serve as gold standard for respiratory sampling.

In the post-CFTR modulator era, some patients discontinue or do not initiate prophylaxis despite Pa infection, often by patient choice rather than clinical recommendation. This study examined the impact of this on lung function, antibiotic use, and antimicrobial resistance. This study aimed to compare outcomes in adults with CF and Pa receiving versus not receiving prophylactic antibiotics in a UK adult CF centre. Methods: Annual review data from 161 adults (2022 and 2024) were analysed using Mann–Whitney U tests for FEV₁% predicted, change in FEV₁% predicted,...
presentations.authors_text: J. Cunningham1, W.T. Cheong1, A. Higton1, C. Orchard1, A. Ewence1, A. Lillis1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Frimley Park NHS Foundation Trust, Cystic Fibrosis Unit, Frimley, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Inhaled prophylactic antibiotics remain recommended for people with cystic fibrosis (CF) with chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) infection. In the post-CFTR modulator era, some patients discontinue or do not initiate prophylaxis despite Pa infection, often by patient choice rather than clinical recommendation. This study examined the impact of this on lung function, antibiotic use, and antimicrobial resistance. This study aimed to compare outcomes in adults with CF and Pa receiving versus not receiving prophylactic antibiotics in a UK adult CF centre.
sections.Methods: Annual review data from 161 adults (2022 and 2024) were analysed using Mann–Whitney U tests for FEV₁% predicted, change in FEV₁% predicted, antibiotic use, and antimicrobial susceptibility, expressed as the percentage of isolates resistant to five antibiotics.
sections.Results: Significant differences were observed between Pa and non-Pa groups for FEV₁% predicted (median 68.4% vs 86.2%; p=0.0001), change in FEV₁% predicted (−1.73% vs +1.12%; p=0.004), annual oral antibiotic use (median 2 vs 1 courses; p=0.007), and IV antibiotic days (p=0.003). Among Pa patients, no significant differences were observed between those receiving prophylaxis versus not for FEV₁% predicted (60.5% vs 74.2%; p=0.139), change in FEV₁% (−2.1% vs −1.4%; p=0.49), oral antibiotic use (median 2 vs 1.5; p=0.219), or IV antibiotic days (p=0.296). Multidrug resistance was higher in patients receiving antibiotics (median 1.5 vs 0.0 resistant agents; p=0.028).
sections.Conclusion: Lung function and antibiotic burden differed between adults with and without Pa. Among Pa patients, outcomes did not differ significantly by prophylaxis status. Higher resistance in patients on prophylaxis may reflect cumulative antibiotic exposure or preferential treatment of established infection. These observational findings do not support withdrawing recommended therapy but highlight the need for ongoing review and shared decision-making in the post-modulator era.

Generalized and linear mixed-effects models were used to assess associations between ETI, time, age, sex, and culture positivi...
presentations.authors_text: S.O. Dekker1, B.C.H. de Bont1, H.G.M. Heijerman1, R. van der Meer2, M. Eggermont2, N. Vrisekoop1, L. Koenderman1, I. Bronsveld1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University Medical Centre Utrecht, Dept. of Respiratory Medicine, Utrecht, Netherlands, 2Haga Ziekenhuis, Dept. of Pulmonology, The Hague, Netherlands

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) reduces sputum production in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). Therefore, throat swabs are used more frequently to detect pathogens when exacerbations are absent. This study retrospectively examined longitudinal changes in pathogen detection in sputum and throat swab cultures before and up to three years after ETI initiation.
sections.Methods: Microbiological sputum and throat swab cultures of adult pwCF treated at two Dutch CF centres were collected from one year prior to three years after ETI initiation and grouped into six-month intervals. Generalized and linear mixed-effects models were used to assess associations between ETI, time, age, sex, and culture positivity for Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa), Staphylococcus aureus (Sa), and Aspergillus fumigatus (Af), as well as associations with ppFEV1.
sections.Results: ETI initiation was associated with significantly lower odds of positive sputum cultures for Pa (OR = 0.18, 95% CI 0.11-0.18), Sa (OR = 0.45, 95% CI 0.37-0.55) and Af (OR = 0.11, 95% CI 0.08-0.13; all p<0.001). Female sex and higher age were associated with increased odds for Pa positivity in sputum, while higher age was associated with lower odds for Sa positivity in sputum. In contrast, Sa was the only pathogen that showed consistently high predicted probabilities in throat swabs before and after ETI initiation. Moreover, Sa throat swab positivity increased modestly but significantly over time following ETI initiation (OR = 1.15 per six-month interval, 95% CI 1.05-1.27, p = 0.002). Pa positivity was associated with lower ppFEV₁ before and after ETI, whereas Sa positivity in throat swabs was associated with higher ppFEV₁.
sections.Conclusion: ETI is associated with reduced sputum culture positivity for key CF pathogens, while throat swab cultures, particularly for S. aureus, do not reflect these trends. These findings highlight important differences between sputum and throat swab cultures, and its association with ppFEV1.

sections.Objectives: To evaluate clinical characteristics,eradication success,and long-term microbiological outcomes in pediatric cystic fibrosis(CF)patients following first-time Pseudomonas aeruginosa(Pa)isolation and tobramycin-based treatment.
sections.Methods: We retrospectively analyzed 10-year data(2015-2025)of 100pediatric CFpatients(51%female).Patients were grouped by response: Group1 (successful eradication,n=43)and Group2 (recurrence,n=57). Group 2was subdivided:Late Eradication(2a,n=18),Chronic Colonization(2b,n=26),and Intermittent Colonization(2c,n=13).Demographics,BMI,FEV₁,Pa phenotype,and co-pathogens at first isolation were analyzed.
sections.Results: Median age at first Pa isolation was89months(IQR: 88.25); median diagnosis age was 2.25months(IQR: 3.00).At isolation,47%were symptomatic. Treatment included inhaled tobramycin alone(57%) or with oral ciprofloxacin(43%). MeanFEV₁ was 91.75%±22.80%and median BMIwas16.13(IQR: 2.91). Microbiologically,19%had a mucoid phenotype and 72%had co-isolations.Group2had significantly lower median BMI compared to Group1 (15.70,IQR:2.40vs.16.6,IQR:4.30;p=0.046).Mucoid phenotype at initial isolation was higher in Group2(26%)thanGroup1(9%)(p=0.032).Co-isolation was higher in Group1(83%) than Group2(63%)(p=0.023),with no significant difference in MRSA(p=0.100)orMSSA(p=0.381).No significant differences were found between Groups1 and2 in age at isolation(p=0.232),diagnosis(p=0.323),FEV₁(p=0.883),or treatment regimen(p=0.543).Subgroup analysis showed mucoid phenotype was most prevalent in group2b(42.3%)vs.2a(16.7%) and2c(7.7%)(p=0.037).Longitudinal analysis showed significant BMI decline during recurrence (t =-4.405, p<0.001). Group1 maintained better BMI than groups2b and 2c (p=0.023).
sections.Conclusion: Pa recurrence is common despite eradication. Lower BMI and mucoid phenotype at first isolation are linked to recurrence, necessitating nutritional optimization and close follow-up.

sections.Background: Paediatric patients provide respiratory samples for microbiology testing when symptomatic and at routine clinic visits. Eradication is attempted for all first isolates of Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa). First-line treatment is three-months oral ciprofloxacin plus nebulised colistimethate sodium but prescribing of ciprofloxacin has declined following safety warnings. Alternative regimens we use include intravenous (IV) antibiotics, nebulised tobramycin for 28 days or a novel extended regimen of alternate-month nebulised tobramycin for six months in younger children where nebuliser technique may be less effective. Treatment choice is patient-focused following multidisciplinary team review.
sections.Methods: We evaluated Pa eradication regimes for six patients treated between October 2024 and November 2025, assessing re-isolation rates for Pa six months post-treatment.
sections.Results: Two patients received IV antibiotics. One seven-year-old patient received three-months nebulised colistimethate sodium plus oral ciprofloxacin, and one six-year-old patient received 28 days of nebulised tobramycin. All four patients remained Pa free six months post-treatment. Three younger patients (aged six months, two years and three years) received the extended regimen of nebulised tobramycin cycles for six months; two remained Pa-free while the three-year-old reisolated Pa whilst still on nebulised tobramycin and required escalation to IV therapy.
sections.Conclusion: Effective nebuliser administration is likely to be age-dependant and appears to be a critical determinant for success in Pa eradication. An extended alternate-month regimen of nebulised tobramycin for six months may be more effective than 28 days treatment in younger patients. More data and longer follow-up are needed to assess the success and durability of these regimens.
