sections.Objectives: Chronic respiratory infection with methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) is associated with worse clinical outcomes in CF (higher mortality). Yet, uncertainty exists about early treatment of asymptomatic MRSA positive patients. Our prior randomized trial (STAR-too) using an intense eradication regimen showed higher rates of MRSA eradication in the treatment compared to no-treatment arm. This follow-up study evaluated the microbiologic outcome of oral antibiotic and topical decontamination in subjects with incident MRSA.
sections.Methods: Open label multi-center treatment study with comparison to control group from the STAR-too trial. Inclusion criteria were incident (first ever or at least 2 year negative and no prior chronic MRSA) MRSA positive culture, being at stable baseline, age 4-45 years. The medication regimen was TMP-SMX (or minocycline if allergic to TMP-SMX and >8 years) and oral gargle with 0.12% chlorhexidine BID for 14 days, and nasal mupirocin 2% nasal ointment for 5 days. This treatment was repeated after Day 28. The primary endpoint was MRSA culture status at Day 28.
sections.Results: Characteristics of the 37 subjects who completed the primary endpoint are in Table 1, 17 were on a CFTR-modulator. As in the prior trial the majority had at least one F508del mutation and were pancreatic insufficient. Oral study medication was TMP-SMX in 33 (89%) of subjects. 56 adverse events (AE) occurred between screening visit through visit 3 (Day 56) of the study, one AE was deemed study related and 3/7 severe AEs were deemed study related (with 2 SAE in the same subject). At Day 28, 11 subjects (30%) had MRSA negative respiratory culture. In comparison, 26% of subjects in the observation arm in STAR-too were MRSA negative at Day 28. At Day 56, 10 of 32 (29%) subjects were MRSA negative.
sections.Conclusion: The current data show limited benefit of a simplified eradication protocol for incident MRSA positive cultures even with a sequential course. Glatiramer acetate (GA) shares similarity to antimicrobial peptides and enhances tobramycin (TOB) activity against the Gram-negative Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa). GA’s proposed mechanism is via interaction with lipopolysaccharides (LPS). Thus, its effect on common co-infecting Gram-positive (G+) organisms like Staphylococcus aureus (Sa), is unclear. presentations.authors_text: A. Daly-Gourdialsing1, R.F Rollo1, A.C. Micallef1, M. Zhao1, R.A. Murphy2, T. Vorup-Jensen3, D.A. Hughes1,4, L.P. Allsopp1, J.C. Davies1,4
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Imperial College London, National Heart and Lung Institute, London, United Kingdom, 2Freie Universität Berlin, Institut Für Biologie, Berlin, Germany, 3Aarhus University, Department of Biomedicine, Aarhus, Denmark, 4Royal Brompton Hospital, Department of Paediatric Respiratory Medicine, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Identifying therapies to reduce the cumulative antibiotic burden in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) is a priority, particularly in chronic lung infections that drive adverse side effects and antimicrobial resistance. Glatiramer acetate (GA) shares similarity to antimicrobial peptides and enhances tobramycin (TOB) activity against the Gram-negative Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa). GA’s proposed mechanism is via interaction with lipopolysaccharides (LPS). Thus, its effect on common co-infecting Gram-positive (G+) organisms like Staphylococcus aureus (Sa), is unclear. Therapeutics for polymicrobial infections should be at least neutral to co-infecting pathogens, not antagonistic.
sections.Methods: We assessed the impact of co-treatment with GA on antimicrobial activity of TOB against pathogens found in polymicrobial CF lung environments. This work included 21 Sa, nine Achromobacter xylosoxidans (Ax), nine Stenotrophomonas maltophilia (Sm), and six Burkholderia cenocepacia (Bc) isolates from pwCF. Median minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC50) values for TOB were determined alone and with 200 µg/ml GA.
sections.Results: Addition of GA significantly enhanced TOB activity against Sa; GA reduced the median TOB MIC₅₀ by 40% across all Sa tested (p<0.0001), including a methicillin resistant isolate. Data showed increased TOB activity against other pathogens; co-administration of GA reduced the median TOB MIC50 by 56% against Ax isolates (p<0.01), and by 49% against Sm isolates (p=0.08). Bc isolates demonstrated variable, non-significant MIC50 responses. Screening of a larger panel of pathogens with relevant antibiotics is underway and data will be available at the conference.
sections.Conclusion: GA increases antimicrobial activity of TOB against G+ Sa from pwCF and certain other CF-relevant pathogens, expanding its antibiotic adjuvant role beyond Pa. This potentiation of TOB against Sa, which lacks LPS, raises compelling questions around GA’s mechanism of action. Funders: LifeArc, CF Trust.

presentations.summary: A key role in the pathogenesis of staphylococcal infections belongs to the "quorum-sensing" system, which includes the accessory gene regulator (agr), coordinating the expression of virulence factors. The functional state of agr determines the bacterial strategy — from acute aggression to chronic persistence. Objective: To define the population structure of S. aureus from cystic fibrosis (CF) patients by agr types and assess links to methicillin resistance. Materials and methods: 216 S. aureus isolates from CF patients were studied. The agr gene was detected and typed (I–IV) by PCR. Methicillin susceptibility was tested by disс diffusion with cefoxitin. MLST was used for genotyping. Results: The agr gene was f...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1N.F. Gamaleya National Research Center for Epidemiology and Microbiology, Moscow, Russian Federation, 2Research Centre for Medical Genetics, Moscow, Russian Federation, 3Federal Pulmonology Research Institute, Federal Medical and Biological Agency of Russia, Moscow, Russian Federation

sections.Objectives: To define the population structure of S. aureus from cystic fibrosis (CF) patients by agr types and assess links to methicillin resistance. Materials and
sections.Methods: 216 S. aureus isolates from CF patients were studied. The agr gene was detected and typed (I–IV) by PCR. Methicillin susceptibility was tested by disс diffusion with cefoxitin. MLST was used for genotyping.
sections.Results: The agr gene was found in 87.5% of isolates (189). Among agr‑positive, type I dominated (67.2%), then types II (23.8%), III (6.9%) and IV (2.1%). This pattern matches the global S. aureus epidemiology, where type I is prevalent. However, a key feature in CF patients is the elevated proportion of agr‑negative isolates (12.5%), suggesting active adaptation in chronic infection. Agr type I showed high genetic diversity (ST8, ST239, ST22, and novel ST6919, ST6972). Importantly, the dominant agr I type was closely linked to methicillin resistance (43.8% MRSA), typical for successful global clones like USA300 (ST8).
sections.Conclusion: Thus, in the population structure of S. aureus from CF patients, the highly virulent agr type I dominates, with 43.8% represented by MRSA, requiring strict epidemiological surveillance. Simultaneously, a substantial proportion (12.5%) of agr‑negative isolates was identified, indicating ongoing pathogen adaptation to chronic infection and highlighting the need for antibiotics effective in chronic settings. The predominance of a functional agr type I makes this virulence‑regulatory system a potential target for the development of novel drugs (e.g. quorum‑sensing inhibitors), and agr typing may serve as a scientific basis for the design of such therapeutic agents.

presentations.authors_text: L. Günther1, J.T.-N. Truong1, B.C. Kahl1, F. Dach1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University Hospital Münster, Institute of Medical Microbiology, Münster, Germany, 2University Hospital Münster, Department of Pediatrics, Münster, Germany

sections.Objectives: Staphylococcus aureus (Sa) is the predominant pathogen in people with Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF). Most isolates produce the carotenoid pigment staphyloxanthin (STX), which confers resistance to oxidative stress and neutrophil mediated killing. Clinically, Sa strains with and without pigmentation frequently coexist, suggesting an advantage of heterogeneity. The aim of this study was to demonstrate the role of STX at the population level and to explore the loss of pigmentation as a potential adaptive process.
sections.Methods: Prevalence of crt deficient Sa strains were analyzed using Whole Genome Sequence (WGS) data from CF samples. For functional analysis of the crt-operon, a yellow-pigmented JE2 wild type (WT), its non-pigmented transposon mutant, and a clinical CF strain pair carrying the same mutation were used. STX production was assessed by colony pigmentation and spectrometrically quantified by STX methanol extraction. Intraspecies/ spatial effects on STX synthesis were tested using a proximity assay. To determine whether non-pigmented Sa benefit from coexistence with STX-producing strains, hydrogen peroxide susceptibility assays were performed in mono- and coculture.
sections.Results: WGS showed ~4.7% of clinical isolates with inactivating mutations in the crt-operon. Independent of proximity to non-pigmented strains, pigmented colonies exhibited increased pigmentation over 14 days. Under oxidative stress, STX expressing Sa strains exhibited greater survival than non-pigmented variants in monoculture. However, when paired in coculture, the white variants showed increased viability, suggesting a protective effect mediated by STX. This effect was observed in both reference- and clinical strains.
sections.Conclusion: STX plays a vital role in heterogeneous Sa populations, providing oxidative stress relief to crt-deficient members. Further analyses are underway to analyze these interactions in a cell culture model and explore the metabolic implications of crt-deficiency.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Manchester, School of Medical Sciences, Manchester, United Kingdom, 2Royal Manchester Children's Hospital, Department of Paediatric Research, Manchester, United Kingdom, 3Manchester University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Depart of Biostatistics, Manchester, United Kingdom, 4Royal Manchester Children's Hospital, Department of Paediatric Respiratory Medicine, Manchester, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) affects over 10,000 in the UK. S. aureus, a common pathogen in CF, damages lung epithelium and worsens pulmonary decline. This study, conducted at a large tertiary centre, assessed S. aureus isolation and treatment.
sections.Methods: Royal Manchester Children’s Hospital (RMCH), a major tertiary CF centre in northwest England, cares for 330 children with CF, including 200 through direct care and 130 via shared arrangements. A ‘positive swab’ or ‘positive sputum culture’ was defined as lab confirmation of Staphylococcus aureus. Data from an electronic database included cough and sputum samples, positive S. aureus samples, and antibiotic treatments in 2024. For each positive S. aureus, notes from 7 days before and after culture date were reviewed, including clinic, encounter, and nurses’ notes, to confirm if antibiotics were prescribed and taken. Antibiotic treatment was recorded only when there was clear evidence of patient intake.
sections.Results: Complete data were available for 169 patients with CF, aged 1–17 years. On average, each patient submitted 7 swabs or 2 sputum samples in 2024. All patients included in the evaluation submitted at least one sample in 2024. A total of 1,128 cough swabs and 328 sputum samples were collected in 2024, with S. aureus identified in 15.4% and 34.8% of samples, respectively. 40.8% of patients had no positive S. aureus swabs or sputum cultures. Among patients who tested positive, 43% had not received any antibiotic treatment.
sections.Conclusion: This finding highlights strong infection control protocols, but variations in prescribing may cause over- or under-treatment. A decision-support tool could standardise antibiotic prescriptions.

sections.Objectives: Non-tuberculous mycobacteria (NTM) represent a significant disease burden in the cystic fibrosis (CF) population. Whilst the acquisition of NTM to the respiratory tract is predominantly attributed to environmental exposures, the contribution of the gut-lung axis to NTM pulmonary disease remains poorly understood.
sections.Methods: In this study, we retrospectively examined 1285 stool and 223 respiratory microbiomes from 145 children and 52 adults with CF, and 171 healthy control (HC) children, to assess the prevalence, abundance and clinical significance of NTM in gut.
sections.Results: We detected numerous species of low clinical concern in the stool of 2% of people with CF and 1% of HC children sampled, with longitudinal sampling revealing their occurrence to be sporadic and transient. Of the participants studied, only 11 had a history of prior clinical NTM respiratory colonisation or infection, but there was limited evidence to support ongoing persistence in the intestinal tract.
sections.Conclusion: This study suggests that the gut is not a meaningful reservoir for pulmonary NTM infections nor is it likely to serve as a transmission route within the CF population.

sections.Objectives: Mycobacterium abscessus complex (MABc) remains a challenging pathogen in cystic fibrosis (CF), given the heterogeneous disease course, lack of biomarkers to guide treatment decisions, and substantial treatment toxicity. We describe management and outcomes of MABc infection in people with CF (pwCF) at a Belgian NTM-CF centre.
sections.Methods: We performed a retrospective chart review of pwCF with ≥1 MABc isolation between 2015-2025. Disease characteristics and microbiological outcomes were evaluated, stratified by receipt of MAB-directed antibiotic therapy.
sections.Results: We included 13 pwCF (median age 31 years; 38% female; median BMI 20.3 kg/m²). All presented with nodular–bronchiectatic MABc pulmonary disease, with one patient having a cavity. Six of 13 pwCF initiated MABc therapy, three in preparation for lung transplantation; two additional patients were offered but refused treatment. Prior to MABc therapy, two patients were on highly effective CFTR modulator therapy (HEMT). Treated patients had lower baseline FEV1 than those not treated (median 51% vs 68% predicted) and more frequent smear positivity (50% vs 14%). ≥ 3 positive sputum cultures in the preceding year occurred in 100% treated vs 57% not treated. All treated patients had M. abscessus, apart from four non-treated patients with M. massiliense. Treatment was initiated at a median 3.2 months after first isolate. Culture conversion occurred in 5/6 (83%; including 3 transplanted) treated at a median 3.7 months (IQR 2.9–29.3) and in 6/7 (86%) not treated at a median 18.2 months (IQR 6.4–35.8), most following HEMT initiation. Recurrent positive cultures after treatment were noted in two (33%) treated patients.
sections.Conclusion: MABc-directed treatment was largely reserved for pwCF with more advanced disease and predated HEMT. Most non-treated cases converted over time following HEMT. Improved tools for stratification based on expected benefit from antimicrobial therapy are needed.

presentations.title: Mycobacterium abscessus clearance post initiation of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor in patients with cystic fibrosis
presentations.summary: Objectives: Mycobacterium abscessus (MABSC) isolation in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) can have major implications including reduced lung function, high treatment burden, transplant exclusion, and stricter infection control requirements. presentations.authors_text: H. Withers1, N. Bell1, S. Cowman1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Bristol Royal Infirmary, Bristol Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Bristol, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Mycobacterium abscessus (MABSC) isolation in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) can have major implications including reduced lung function, high treatment burden, transplant exclusion, and stricter infection control requirements. Case reports describe clearance of MABSC from sputum following elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) initiation, and observational data suggest decreased nontuberculous mycobacteria prevalence post CFTR modulators. We reviewed MABSC isolation patterns pre- and post-ETI initiation.
sections.Methods: Retrospective analysis of adult pwCF at Bristol Adult CF centre with ≥2 MABSC-positive sputum cultures between 2009 and 2025. Inclusion: regular full dose ETI for >2 year and ≥2 MABSC isolations in the five years pre-ETI initiation. Data were collected using our Centre’s clinical database.
sections.Results: Twenty-five pwCF met the inclusion criteria, sending median 2.8 (range 0.2-5.7) samples per year pre-ETI. Median follow up duration post-ETI was 61 months (range 25-70) with median 0.6 samples (range 0.2-3.3) being sent per year. Thirteen (52%) had no MABSC isolation post-ETI (median 0.5 [range 0.2-1.1] samples per year over median 60 [range 45-70] months). All patients remained productive post-ETI, with 21 (84%) patients having 2 or more samples sent. Three pwCF were undergoing MABSC treatment at time of ETI initiation which was subsequently discontinued; all continued to isolate MABSC post-ETI but remained clinically stable with no indication to restart treatment.
sections.Conclusion: ETI initiation was associated with apparent MABSC clearance in half of cases. It is possible that reduced mycobacterial sampling post-ETI could have led to under-detection of MABSC. All three pwCF on MABSC treatment were able to discontinue it having started ETI.

presentations.title: Signed, Sealed, Sputum: detecting non-tuberculosis Mycobacteria by post in cystic fibrosis
presentations.summary: Introduction: Sputum microbiological surveillance underpins Cystic Fibrosis (CF) care, but the move towards virtual clinics makes this challenging. Whilst postal sputum sampling has been validated for normal culture, its utility for Non-tuberculosis Mycobacteria (NTM) is unclear. To study this further, we prospectively compared NTM positivity in paired postal and fresh samples in our adult CF Centre. Methods: We looked at 13 pwCF with sputum samples positive for NTM stain or culture (NTM-PD) between 2023 and 2025. We then obtained paired sputum samples from these pwCF at face-to-face clinics or ward visits over a four-month period, and also a control group of five pwCF without NTM. One sample from each pair wa...
presentations.authors_text: H. Wood1, K.-M. Flaherty1, T. Penrose1, M. Walshaw2,3, L. Rossiter2, M. Ledson2, F. Frost2,3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Liverpool Heart and Chest Hospital, Physiotherapy Department, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 2Liverpool Heart and Chest Hospital, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 3University of Liverpool, Liverpool, United Kingdom

sections.Methods: We looked at 13 pwCF with sputum samples positive for NTM stain or culture (NTM-PD) between 2023 and 2025. We then obtained paired sputum samples from these pwCF at face-to-face clinics or ward visits over a four-month period, and also a control group of five pwCF without NTM. One sample from each pair was sent directly to the laboratory for culture, whilst the other was sent via the existing community postal sputum pathway. We recorded the final growth and NTM stain for each sample.
sections.Results: Of the 13 NTM-PD, for postal samples two were stain positive (one indeterminate) and three culture positive (one Mycobacterium abscessus). For fresh samples, only one was both stain and culture positive. In the control group, all fresh samples were negative but one was stain (indeterminate) and culture positive (Mycobacterium chelonae) in the postal group. Overall, postal samples had 100% negative predictive value and 25% positive predictive value for fresh culture.
sections.Conclusion: We have shown that NTM is recoverable from postal sputum in both patients with known NTM-PD and those without. Our data suggests a negative postal sputum sample is reassuring, but that a positive sample should be followed up with fresh samples given discordant positivity between sampling groups.

presentations.summary: Objectives: Non-tuberculous mycobacteria (NTM) are increasingly detected in adults with cystic fibrosis (CF). Results: Eight patients (10.3%) had NTM isolation. Mean age was 34.6 years and seven patients (87.5%) were female. Five patients (62.5%) carried at least one F508del...
presentations.authors_text: A.S. Gonçalves1, E. Fragoso2,3, P. Azevedo2,3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Funchal Central Hospital, Pulmonology, Funchal, Madeira, Portugal, 2North Lisbon University Hospital Centre, ULS Santa Maria, Pulmonology, Lisbon, Portugal, 3Faculdade de Medicina da Universidade de Lisboa, Lisbon, Portugal

sections.Objectives: Non-tuberculous mycobacteria (NTM) are increasingly detected in adults with cystic fibrosis (CF). The relationship between highly effective CFTR modulators and NTM isolation remains uncertain. Our aim is to characterize adult CF patients with NTM infection and to evaluate the timing of NTM isolation in relation to exposure to elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI).
sections.Methods: A cross-sectional study was performed in a single adult CF centre. Among seventy-eight adult CF patients, those with at least one NTM isolation were included (n=8).
sections.Results: Eight patients (10.3%) had NTM isolation. Mean age was 34.6 years and seven patients (87.5%) were female. Five patients (62.5%) carried at least one F508del allele and mean sweat chloride was 78.6 mmol/L. Mean FEV₁ was 63.1% predicted, with lung function classified as severe obstruction in one patient (FEV₁<40%), moderate in five (FEV₁ 40–70%) and mild in two (FEV₁>70%). Five patients had exocrine pancreatic insufficiency and two CF-related diabetes. Six patients (75%) had NTM without ETI exposure, while two patients (25%) experienced their first NTM isolation after initiating ETI. In ETI group, patients maintained mild to moderate lung function and chronic airway infection was limited to Staphylococcus aureus. One patient with long-standing Stenotrophomonas maltophilia isolation no longer had this microorganism detected following NTM identification. In contrast, patients without ETI exposure exhibited lower FEV₁ values, Aspergillus fumigatus colonization, and chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection, with two patients requiring long-term inhaled antibiotic therapy. Moreover, one patient developed Burkholderia cepacia infection following completion of NTM treatment.
sections.Conclusion: Adult CF patients with NTM infection present heterogeneous genetic and clinical characteristics. In this cohort, most NTM isolations occurred independently of ETI exposure. Continued microbiological surveillance remains essential in the modulator era.

presentations.title: Patient-focused Target Product Profiles for the development of phage therapies to treat lung infections in cystic fibrosis
presentations.summary: Objective: Despite advances in cystic fibrosis (CF) care, lung infections are a leading cause of morbidity and mortality for people with CF (pwCF). Phage therapy could help address this unmet need by specifically targeting pathogenic bacteria, including antibiotic resistant strains, with reduced toxicity compared to prolonged antibiotic use. The CF AMR Syndicate joined forces with the TRAILFINDER-CF Innovation Hub to deliver patient-focused Target Product Profiles (TPPs) to catalyse the development of phage therapies for CF lung infections. A TPP describes the characteristics a therapeutic must have to address a specific need Method: TPP development began with focus groups and one-to-one interviews with pwCF,...
presentations.authors_text: L. Jenkinson1, C. Takawira1, N. Gingles1, P. Sommer2, L. Allen2, L. Jackson3, H. Mok4, J. Fothergill3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Medicines Discovery Catapult, Macclesfield, United Kingdom, 2Cystic Fibrosis Trust, London, United Kingdom, 3University of Liverpool, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 4LifeArc, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Despite advances in cystic fibrosis (CF) care, lung infections are a leading cause of morbidity and mortality for people with CF (pwCF). Phage therapy could help address this unmet need by specifically targeting pathogenic bacteria, including antibiotic resistant strains, with reduced toxicity compared to prolonged antibiotic use. The CF AMR Syndicate joined forces with the TRAILFINDER-CF Innovation Hub to deliver patient-focused Target Product Profiles (TPPs) to catalyse the development of phage therapies for CF lung infections. A TPP describes the characteristics a therapeutic must have to address a specific need
sections.Methods: TPP development began with focus groups and one-to-one interviews with pwCF, followed by input from CF clinical care teams and phage therapy innovators, alongside a clinical trial landscape review. These interactions explored phage therapy awareness; integration into current treatment strategies; and barriers to clinical adoption. Clinical trial endpoints and outcome measures, preclinical data, plus regulatory and safety requirements were also explored. Outputs were used to outline an initial set of TPPs, now being refined through stakeholder engagement, consensus survey and a virtual symposium
sections.Results: Three TPPs have been developed for both conventional and modified phage therapy: (1) patient-focused TPP capturing the needs and priorities of pwCF that should be addressed by any phage therapeutic; (2) TPP for the treatment of chronic lung infections using phage therapy; (3) TPP for the eradication of newly acquired lung infections using phage therapy. The TPPs are pathogen-agnostic and applicable to pathogens in CF and other adjacent diseases e.g. bronchiectasis
sections.Conclusion: Stakeholder input is ongoing to refine the TPPs, aiming to deliver consensus-driven documents that define the desired characteristics of a phage therapy for CF lung infections and stimulate activity to address existing technical, regulatory, and clinical challenges

Methods: Antibacterial activity was assessed through MIC, MBC and checkerboard assays. Biofilm eradication was assessed by crystal violet and tetrazolium salt reduction assay. Transcription of selected virulence-related genes was verified by qRT-PCR. Protease activity, adhesion on abiotic surface and to epith...
presentations.authors_text: M. Stabile1, A. Esposito2, A. Migliaccio3, L. Sepe1, R. Artiano1, V.D. Iula4, R. Zarrilli3, A. Guaragna2, E. De Gregorio1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Naples Federico II, Department of Molecular Medicine and Medical Biotechnology, Naples, Italy, 2University of Naples Federico II, Department of Chemical Sciences, Naples, Italy, 3University of Naples Federico II, Department of Public Health, Naples, Italy, 4Ospedale del Mare - ASL Napoli 1 Centro, Department of Laboratory Medicine, U.O.C Patologia Clinica, Naples, Italy

sections.Objectives: Stenotrophomonas maltophilia (Sm) is an opportunistic Gram-negative MDR pathogen that causes life-threatening infections in cystic fibrosis patients. Sm has many virulence factors that contribute to host tissue damage and immune evasion. In this study, the synthetic iminosugar L-NPDNJ was tested against Sm strains to evaluate its ability to enhance antibiotic efficacy and reduce pathogenicity.
sections.Methods: Antibacterial activity was assessed through MIC, MBC and checkerboard assays. Biofilm eradication was assessed by crystal violet and tetrazolium salt reduction assay. Transcription of selected virulence-related genes was verified by qRT-PCR. Protease activity, adhesion on abiotic surface and to epithelial cells assays, macrophage infection assay,and an in vivo infection with Galleria mellonella larvae were also performed.
sections.Results: L-NPDNJ showed limited antibacterial activity but showed additive effects with aminoglycosides. It significantly disrupted preformed biofilms (70% reduction in biomass and metabolic activity). qRT-PCR revealed downregulation of genes encoding resistance determinants, biofilm regulators and virulence factors. These transcriptional changes led to reduced protease secretion, resulting in reduced epithelial damage by Sm supernatants, and impaired its adhesion to abiotic surfaces and to epithelial cells. L-NPDNJ treatment stimulates macrophage phagocytosis and enhances the intracellular clearance of Sm by macrophages, as well as enhancing macrophage viability upon bacterial infection. In addition, pretreatment of Sm cells with L-NPDNJ before infection significantly improved larval survival in the in vivo G. mellonella model, confirming its ability to attenuate virulence.
sections.Conclusion: Despite lacking direct bactericidal activity, L-NPDNJ effectively targets multiple virulence mechanisms in Sm, supporting its potential as a non-toxic antivirulence compound that could be used to complement antibiotic therapy and reduce pathogenicity.

presentations.title: ‘One Bronchiectasis’ in clinical trials of anti-pseudomonal agents: an evidence based approach?
presentations.summary: CFTR modulator treated (MT) cystic fibrosis (CF) is considered by some to be clinically similar to other causes of bronchiectasis (Bx); people with CF (pwCF) generally do not eradicate bacterial airway infection. Some recent trials of anti-inflammatory agents are cohorting MT-CF with other causes of Bx under the umbrella concept: ‘One Bx’. A ‘One Bx’ approach may be a worthwhile strategy to increase power in antimicrobial studies, a WHO priority. But are microbes from pwCF and people with Bx (pwBx) similar in phenotype? Methods: We performed detailed phenotyping of 70 Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) from pwCF in a pre-MT era and 66 from pwBx, including: antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST; 2019 European Comm...
presentations.authors_text: W.D Smith1,2, F.J Terpstra1,3, A. Cunanan1, O.L Fletcher1, R.A Murphy1, A. Simbo1, C. Hogg1,2, A. Filloux1, A. Bush1,2, J.C Davies1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Imperial College London, London, United Kingdom, 2Royal Brompton Hospital, London, United Kingdom, 3University of Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

sections.Methods: We performed detailed phenotyping of 70 Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) from pwCF in a pre-MT era and 66 from pwBx, including: antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST; 2019 European Committee on AST (EUCAST) disk diffusion method); ex vivo biofilm forming potential (crystal violet assay); motility and enzymatic activity (agar plate assays). Each assay had three technical replicates per strain. Inocula were consistent. EUCAST clinical breakpoints were used to identify antimicrobial resistance (R) and R to at least 1 of ≥ 3 different classes of antibiotic was defined multi-drug resistance (MDR).
sections.Results: A greater proportion of Pa strains from pwCF demonstrated MDR in comparison to Bx (86% vs. 55%), p <0.0001, X2. Small, statistically significant differences were seen in biofilm forming potential, twitching motility (greater in CF), and lipase activity (greater in Bx).
sections.Conclusion: These ex vivo assays show small, but statistically significant differences between Pa from pwBx and pwCF in the pre-MT era. Antibiotic exposure likely explains the MDR differences. The clinical significance of these data and how they reflect the in vivo environment is unknown. Assuming the in vivo environment in MT CF more closely resembles that of non-CF Bx, these data may support a One-Bx concept for antimicrobial trials. Supported by the British Lung Foundation. Affiliated to the UK CF Trust-funded Strategic Research Centre for Pseudomonas infection.

This study aimed to develop a loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) assay for the rapid detection of Pa in saliva samples. LAMP utilises four to six primers to amplify DNA at a single temperature, reducing assay time and enabling simpler workflows compared to PCR, whilst maintaining sensitivity. Methods: LAMP detection targeted the oprL gene in Pa, using an intercalating dye for real-time detection. Optimal assay performan...
presentations.authors_text: A.M. Kadoom1, G.G Einarsson1, E. Caamano Gutierrez2, R.W Lord3, R.E Maher4, A. Jones3, A. Horsley3, D.F Gilpin1, M.M Tunney1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Queens University Belfast, School of Pharmacy, Belfast, United Kingdom, 2University of Liverpool, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 3Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust, Manchester Adult CF Centre, Manchester, United Kingdom, 4University of Liverpool, Centre for Proteome Research, Liverpool, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) remains a key respiratory pathogen in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). Following the introduction of CFTR modulator therapies, sputum production has decreased in pwCF, highlighting the need to explore alternative sample types such as saliva. This study aimed to develop a loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) assay for the rapid detection of Pa in saliva samples. LAMP utilises four to six primers to amplify DNA at a single temperature, reducing assay time and enabling simpler workflows compared to PCR, whilst maintaining sensitivity.
sections.Methods: LAMP detection targeted the oprL gene in Pa, using an intercalating dye for real-time detection. Optimal assay performance was achieved using a one-step protocol incorporating proteinase K and thermal lysis, totalling 15 minutes. Using spiked saliva samples obtained from healthy volunteers (n=15), eight technical replicates per dilution were tested to determine the limit of detection (LoD; 95% detection rate) of the assay.
sections.Results: The LoD was shown to be approximately 2.4 x 103 cfu/ml (100% detection) when using a 30-minute threshold, with a mean (±SD) detection time of 14 minutes ±2.4 minutes. Pa detection was achieved in 94% of reactions at a concentration of 1.2 x 103 cfu/ml, with a mean (±SD) detection time of 16.5 minutes ±3.9 minutes. At 6.0 x 102 cfu/ml, Pa detection was observed in 80% of reactions, with a mean (±SD) detection time of 17.2 minutes ±3.9 minutes.
sections.Conclusion: At a bacterial load of 2.4 x103 cfu/ml in saliva, Pa was detected in 100% of spiked samples by LAMP, potentially providing rapid and sensitive results from a sample type easier to collect than sputum. This approach could improve surveillance and monitoring of Pa colonisation. Future work will validate the assay on clinical samples, comparing saliva and sputum detection. Funded through PULSE-CF by the CF-Trust and LifeArc.

presentations.title: Metabolic adaptation in sulfur-rich cystic fibrosis airways shapes pulmonary pathogen dynamics
presentations.summary: Objectives: Pulmonary infection in people with Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF) shifts from early Staphylococcus aureus (Sa) to later Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) dominance, exacerbating airway inflammation. Here, we examined how CFTR-driven airway metabolism and pathogen adaptation promote sulfur-dependent progression from Sa to Pa coinfection. Methods: CFTR-associated airway metabolic alterations were profiled by LC-MS metabolomics of sputum and airway cell supernatants (SN) from CF and non-CF donors. Adaptive mutations were identified by Whole-Genome Sequencing (WGS) of Sa isolates from CF respiratory samples (n=560). Functional studies used the JE2 wildtype (WT) strain and its cysM transposon mutant. Bacterial growth...
presentations.authors_text: J.T.-N. Truong1, Y.-T. Chen2, G.K. Lohia2, B.C. Kahl1, F. Dach1,3, S.A. Riquelme2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University Hospital Münster, Institute of Medical Microbiology, Münster, Germany, 2Columbia University, Department of Pediatrics, New York, United States, 3University Hospital Münster, Department of Pediatrics, Münster, Germany

sections.Objectives: Pulmonary infection in people with Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF) shifts from early Staphylococcus aureus (Sa) to later Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) dominance, exacerbating airway inflammation. Here, we examined how CFTR-driven airway metabolism and pathogen adaptation promote sulfur-dependent progression from Sa to Pa coinfection.
sections.Methods: CFTR-associated airway metabolic alterations were profiled by LC-MS metabolomics of sputum and airway cell supernatants (SN) from CF and non-CF donors. Adaptive mutations were identified by Whole-Genome Sequencing (WGS) of Sa isolates from CF respiratory samples (n=560). Functional studies used the JE2 wildtype (WT) strain and its cysM transposon mutant. Bacterial growth was assessed in LB and Artificial Sputum Medium (ASM)±sulfur-rich metabolites. Infection outcomes - bacterial burden (CFU) and cytokine secretion (ELISA) - were evaluated in non-CF and CF airway epithelial (F508del/W1282X) and differentiated myeloid cells (F508del/F508del), and validated in a mouse respiratory infection model.
sections.Results: CF sputum and airway epithelial cell SN were enriched in sulfur-containing metabolites. WGS revealed inactivating cysM mutations in ~78% of Sa isolates from pwCF. In LB, cysM inactivation confers resilience to the stress imposed by sulfur metabolites like cysteine. ASM provided similar protection to cysM-intact strains, supporting an adaptive convergence between bacterial genetics and the CF metabolic milieu. In CF epithelial cells, cysM inactivation enhanced IL-8 secretion. Although this mutation did not increase Sa persistence in neutrophils, during coinfection with Pa it markedly promoted Pa survival, an effect reproduced in vivo.
sections.Conclusion: CFTR-driven sulfur enrichment of the airway selects for cysM-deficient Sa strains, which are resilient to the CF metabolic milieu, enhance inflammation, and facilitate Pa persistence. Our data identify sulfur metabolism as a key driver of pathogen evolution and community dynamics in CF.

Sa typically establishes colonization early in life, whereas Pa becomes dominant as the disease progresses. However, growing evidence indicates that both pathogens often persist together, forming a polymicrobial environment that accelerates lung function decline. The prolonged residency in CF lung, coupled with recurrent hospitalizations and intensive antibiotic pressure, drives the evolutionary adaptation of these specie...
presentations.authors_text: I. Paris1, A. Pietrella1, S. Raimondi2, M. Rossitto3, G. Vrenna3, L. Selan1, M. Artini1, R. Papa1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Sapienza University, Department of Public Health and Infectious Diseases, Rome, Italy, 2Bambino Gesù Children’s Hospital, IRCCS, Microbiology and Diagnostic Immunology Unit, Rome, Italy, 3Bambino Gesù Children’s Hospital, IRCCS, Multimodal Laboratory Medicine, Rome, Italy

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) lungs are characterized by a dense mucus layer that promotes chronic colonization by opportunistic pathogens, where Staphylococcus aureus (Sa) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) are predominant pathogens strongly associated with increased morbidity and mortality. Sa typically establishes colonization early in life, whereas Pa becomes dominant as the disease progresses. However, growing evidence indicates that both pathogens often persist together, forming a polymicrobial environment that accelerates lung function decline. The prolonged residency in CF lung, coupled with recurrent hospitalizations and intensive antibiotic pressure, drives the evolutionary adaptation of these species toward multi-drug resistance (MDR). This has led to the alarming emergence of colistin-resistant Pa and methicillin-resistant Sa (MRSA), neutralizing last-resort therapeutic options. Polymer-based nanohydrogels (NHs) represent a promising platform to bypass the mucosal barrier, the biofilm matrix and potentially restoring antimicrobial potency against MDR pathogens.
sections.Methods: NHs were synthesized and characterized. Their intrinsic antibiofilm activity and synergistic interactions with conventional antibiotics used in therapy were investigated in MDR clinical Pa and Sa isolates from CF patients. Efficacy was evaluated through checkerboard and biofilm eradication assays.
sections.Results: NHs showed high stability and significant intrinsic antibiofilm properties, specifically targeting the initial adhesion phase. In combination with antibiotics, the systems exhibited a marked synergistic effect, significantly reducing MIC values for colistin-resistant Pa and MRSA. Furthermore, the nanocarriers demonstrated higher penetration into the dense biofilm matrix compared to free drugs.
sections.Conclusion: Nanohydrogel systems represent a promising tool to overcome mucosal and biofilm barriers, restoring the potency of last-resort antibiotics against challenging MDR infections in CF.

presentations.title: Decade-long dynamics in lung sampling and microbiological outcomes at a large adult cystic fibrosis centre
presentations.summary: Background: Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) has transformed microbiological landscape in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). However, the impact of ETI on sampling practices and culture outcomes is not well described. This study evaluated the dynamics of respiratory sampling in CF over the past decade at our large adult CF centre. Methods: We looked at all 11,697 samples from 384 pwCF currently receiving ETI [9330 sputum (SP), 2367 cough swabs (CS)]. Annualised sampling frequency and positivity for Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa), methicillin‑sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA), methicillin‑resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) and Burkholderia (Bcc) were recorded, comparing the pre‑ and post‑ETI eras....
presentations.authors_text: L. Rossiter1, G. Hyde1, D. Nazareth1, M. Wlshaw1, T. Penrose1, H. Wood1, F. Frost1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Liverpool Heart and Chest Hospital, Respiratory Medicine, Liverpool, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) has transformed microbiological landscape in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). However, the impact of ETI on sampling practices and culture outcomes is not well described. This study evaluated the dynamics of respiratory sampling in CF over the past decade at our large adult CF centre.
sections.Methods: We looked at all 11,697 samples from 384 pwCF currently receiving ETI [9330 sputum (SP), 2367 cough swabs (CS)]. Annualised sampling frequency and positivity for Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa), methicillin‑sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA), methicillin‑resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) and Burkholderia (Bcc) were recorded, comparing the pre‑ and post‑ETI eras.
sections.Results: Total respiratory sampling fell, with a marked reduction post-ETI (median samples: pre 1356/yr vs post 783/yr; mean difference 647, p<0.0001), driven by a 7.4% reduction in SP samples. CS sampling remained stable but accounted for a greater proportion post-ETI. We found a general decrease in Pa positivity rates post ETI (SP: pre 71% vs post 51%; CS: pre 17% vs post 7%; p<0.001). We also observed a reduction in BCC and MRSA for CS and SP (both p<0.02). However, although MSSA had reduced positivity for SP (24% vs 19%, p<0.0001), CS remained similar (5.4% vs 7.2%, p=0.29). Annualised discordance between CS and SP increased post-ETI with lesser agreement between CS and SP seen for Pa and to a lesser degree MSSA.
sections.Conclusion: In the ETI era, marked shifts in respiratory sampling from SP to CS are accompanied by lower culture positivity rates and reduced detection of most CF pathogens. Divergent effects on Pa and MSSA positivity and sampling concordance were seen, reinforcing the need for high-quality respiratory sampling to allow robust microbiological surveillance in pwCF on ETI.

presentations.summary: Objectives: Antibiotics have been a mainstay treatment for the pulmonary manifestations of cystic fibrosis (CF). Recurrent antibiotic use is associated with increased antibiotic-resistant genes (ARG) in bacteria, conferring a greater risk of antimicrobial resistance (AMR). Elexacaftor / Tezacaftor / Ivacaftor (ETI) therapy is associated with reduced intravenous (IV) antibiotic use and early data suggest some gut microbiotal modulation. We investigated whether ARG abundance changes pre and post ETI therapy in stool samples from adults with CF. Methods: This study was part of an observational cohort study (Igloo-CF). Antibiotic resistant genes were analysed from Whole Metagenome Sequencing data of paired stool s...
presentations.authors_text: L.R Caley1, H.M Wood1,2, P. Quirke1,2, D.G Peckham1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Leeds, Leeds Institute of Medical Research, Leeds, United Kingdom, 2University of Leeds, NIHR Leeds Biomedical Research Centre, Leeds, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Antibiotics have been a mainstay treatment for the pulmonary manifestations of cystic fibrosis (CF). Recurrent antibiotic use is associated with increased antibiotic-resistant genes (ARG) in bacteria, conferring a greater risk of antimicrobial resistance (AMR). Elexacaftor / Tezacaftor / Ivacaftor (ETI) therapy is associated with reduced intravenous (IV) antibiotic use and early data suggest some gut microbiotal modulation. We investigated whether ARG abundance changes pre and post ETI therapy in stool samples from adults with CF.
sections.Methods: This study was part of an observational cohort study (Igloo-CF). Antibiotic resistant genes were analysed from Whole Metagenome Sequencing data of paired stool samples collected from 57 participants pre and post ETI therapy. Samples were collected a median of 15 months apart (range 4 -21 months), with median duration of ETI being 5 months (IQR 3, 7 months) at follow up.
sections.Results: At baseline, median age and ppFEV1 were 33 years (IQR 29, 39.5) and 47 (IQR 34, 73) respectively, with ppFEV1 increasing to 59 (IQR 44, 85) at follow up (post-ETI). ARG relative abundance significantly decreased post-ETI therapy (p=0.0032). IV antibiotic use also significantly decreased following ETI therapy (p= <0.001).
sections.Conclusion: Following ETI therapy, the relative abundance of ARG in stool samples decreased, which may be owing to the concurrent decrease in IV antibiotics. Analysis is ongoing. Whether the change in AMG alters AMR risk requires further exploration. Funded by the UK CF Trust (SRC 012).

presentations.title: Microbiological changes in cystic fibrosis: the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic and CFTR modulators
presentations.summary: Objectives: The lung microbiota in people with Cystic Fibrosis (CF) is shaped by environmental and host factors. The COVID-19 pandemic altered healthcare delivery and social behaviour, while the introduction of Elexacaftor–Tezacaftor–Ivacaftor (ETI), in Norway from July 2022, transformed CF care. We examined changes in chronic lower airway microbiota pre- and post- the pandemic and after >2 years of ETI therapy. Methods: Data from the Norwegian CF Registry were analysed for 2019 (pre-pandemic), 2021 (post-pandemic, pre-ETI), and 2024 (>2 years post-ETI), excluding lung transplant receivers. sections.Objectives: The lung microbiota in people with Cystic Fibrosis (CF) is shaped by environmental and host factors. The COVID-19 pandemic altered healthcare delivery and social behaviour, while the introduction of Elexacaftor–Tezacaftor–Ivacaftor (ETI), in Norway from July 2022, transformed CF care. We examined changes in chronic lower airway microbiota pre- and post- the pandemic and after >2 years of ETI therapy.
sections.Methods: Data from the Norwegian CF Registry were analysed for 2019 (pre-pandemic), 2021 (post-pandemic, pre-ETI), and 2024 (>2 years post-ETI), excluding lung transplant receivers. Prevalence trends for chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) and Staphylococcus aureus (Sa) were assessed, and a cross-sectional logistic regression was performed for 2024 in pwCF aged ≥6 years.
sections.Results: Chronic Pa and Sa prevalence was 20% and 53% in 2019 (n=288), 18% and 47% in 2021 (n=309), and 13% and 31% in 2024 (n=353). Reductions from 2021 to 2024 were significant for Pa (p=0.041) and Sa (p<0.01), whereas changes from 2019 to 2021 did not reach statictical significanse. Change in Pa status from 2021 to 2024 was associated with ETI use (p=0.007), and a linear trend indicated greater stability with ≥2 years ETI exposure (p=0.003). A similar non significant trend for Sa was observed. In 2024 regression (n=298), older age increased chronic Pa odds (OR 1.05/year; 95% CI 1.03–1.08; p<0.001), while pancreatic sufficiency (OR 0.39; 95% CI 0.16–0.96; p=0.041) and chronic Sa (OR 0.29; 95% CI 0.11–0.76; p=0.012) were protective. Sex and ETI exposure duration showed no significant association.
sections.Conclusion: The pandemic did not significantly affect chronic infection rates. Prolonged ETI therapy was linked to marked reductions in chronic Pa in longitudinal analyses, suggesting sustained treatment may aid pathogen clearance. In the cross‑sectional 2024 model, host factors such as age and pancreatic status remained key determinants of chronic infection risk in the modulator era.

sections.Objectives: Triple CFTR modulator therapy with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has substantially changed CF management but its real-world impact on respiratory microbiology remains incompletely characterized. Therefore, the main objective of this study is to evaluate changes in routine respiratory culture positivity for P. aeruginosa, S. aureus, and H. influenzae, as well as P. aeruginosa morphotype diversity, before and after the introduction of ETI.
sections.Methods: A retrospective observational study was conducted including respiratory routine cultures collected from CF patients in our centre between 2017 and 2025. Cultures were compared between a pre-ETI period (2017–2021) and a post-ETI period (2022–2025). Proportions and morphotype diversity were analyzed using chi-square and Student’s t tests, respectively.
sections.Results: During the pre-ETI period, 2,915 respiratory cultures were analyzed. Overall, P. aeruginosa was isolated in 544 cultures (18.7%), S. aureus in 1,801 (61.8%), and H. influenzae in 455 (15.6%). The highest percentage of positive cultures for P. aeruginosa was observed in 2020 (29.8%). In the post-ETI period, 3,330 cultures were analyzed. A significant reduction of 56.6% in P. aeruginosa was observed (270 cultures, 8.1%; p <0.001), along with a 35.1% reduction in S. aureus isolates (1,335 cultures, 40.1%; p <0.001), being the decrease in S. aureus smaller than that observed for P. aeruginosa. In contrast, H. influenzae isolates showed a significant increase of 35.6% (705 cultures, 21.2%; p <0.001). The mean number of P. aeruginosa morphotypes per positive culture was higher in the pre-ETI stage (1.33 ± 0.08) compared with the post-ETI stage (1.16 ± 0.07; p <0.014).
sections.Conclusion: ETI therapy was associated with a marked shift in respiratory microbiology, including reduced prevalence and diversity of P. aeruginosa and S. aureus, and a relative increase in H. influenzae, suggesting a reorganization of the airway microbial ecosystem in the CFTR modulator era.

sections.Background: Pulmonary exacerbations (PEx) in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) are frequently complicated by multidrug-resistant (MDR) Gram-negative infection and advanced lung disease. Newer antipseudomonal β-lactams, including ceftolozane/tazobactam (CZ), ceftazidime/avibactam (CA) and cefiderocol (CD), are increasingly used; however, real-world data on objective clinical response and tolerability in pwCF remain limited. Aims: To describe inflammatory response, lung function change and tolerability following treatment with CZ, CA and CD for CF PEx.
sections.Methods: Retrospective review of pwCF treated with CZ, CA or CD for PEx at a large adult CF centre (Sept 2023–Jan 2026). Data included chronic airways microbial status, incident PEx sputum microbiology, concomitant intravenous antimicrobials, C-reactive protein (CRP), percent-predicted forced expiratory volume in one second (ppFEV₁), and adverse effects. Pre-treatment CRP and ppFEV₁ were the most recent prior to antibiotic initiation; post-treatment CRP was measured after treatment, and ppFEV₁ was the first available value within 60 days.
sections.Results: Sixteen treatment encounters in 11 patients were included (Table). Median baseline ppFEV₁ was low, reflecting advanced lung disease. In most encounters, the index antibiotic followed exposure to other intravenous regimens during the same hospitalisation. CRP improved in most encounters, with marked reductions in those with high baseline inflammation. Lung function response was heterogeneous. Tolerability was favourable, with few adverse effects and no treatment discontinuations.
sections.Conclusion: In pwCF with advanced lung disease and MDR infection, CZ, CA and CD were well tolerated and associated with improvement in systemic inflammation, with variable lung function response, supporting use in selected patients with severe or refractory CF PEx. sections.Objectives: Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA) is a hypersensitivity to Aspergillus fumigatus, mainly affecting those with Asthma or Cystic Fibrosis (CF). Early diagnosis reduces pulmonary complications. This study examined patterns of ABPA biomarkers to evaluate their use for early detection in paediatric CF patients.
sections.Methods: Royal Manchester Children’s Hospital (RMCH), a major tertiary CF centre in northwest England, cares for 330 children with CF, with 200 through direct input and 130 via shared care. A retrospective analysis used data from an electronic record. Patients under 1 year old or with data gaps over 3 years were excluded. Biomarkers, including eosinophil count, total IgE, and A. fumigatus-specific IgE and IgG, were measured and analysed using linear modelling and regression.
sections.Results: Data were available for 173 CF subjects. Among the biomarkers, A. fumigatus IgG showed the strongest correlation (r = 0.321, p < 0.001), identifying all confirmed ABPA cases at the provisional threshold. Total serum IgE and eosinophils showed weaker correlations with confirmed cases (r=0.131, r=0.046). In confirmed ABPA, biomarker levels lacked linear trends after diagnosis, limiting their use for monitoring.
sections.Conclusion: This evaluation supports the use of ABPA biomarkers within a broader diagnostic framework. However, their limitations in early detection and post-diagnosis surveillance highlight the need for further research to refine these tools and improve diagnostic accuracy and clinical outcomes.

presentations.summary: Background: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is characterized by chronic inflammation, gut-lung axis dysfunction, microbial dysbiosis, and systemic metabolic abnormalities. Human and animal studies suggest that CF-relevant microbiome and metabolomic alterations can arise early and may be influenced by environmental modifiers. Vaping, also known as electronic nicotine delivery system (ENDS) use, is increasingly prevalent, including among individuals with CF, yet its impact on systemic metabolism and gut-lung microbial ecology remains poorly understood. Methods: Wild-type mice were chronically exposed to flavored ENDS aerosol (N=8) or room air (N=7). Stool and lung microbiomes were analyzed by 16S rRNA sequencing to evaluat...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Morsani College of Medicine- University of South Florida, Tampa, United States, 2College of Nursing- University of South Florida, Tampa, United States

sections.Background: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is characterized by chronic inflammation, gut-lung axis dysfunction, microbial dysbiosis, and systemic metabolic abnormalities. Human and animal studies suggest that CF-relevant microbiome and metabolomic alterations can arise early and may be influenced by environmental modifiers. Vaping, also known as electronic nicotine delivery system (ENDS) use, is increasingly prevalent, including among individuals with CF, yet its impact on systemic metabolism and gut-lung microbial ecology remains poorly understood.
sections.Methods: Wild-type mice were chronically exposed to flavored ENDS aerosol (N=8) or room air (N=7). Stool and lung microbiomes were analyzed by 16S rRNA sequencing to evaluate diversity, taxonomic composition, and differential abundance. Plasma metabolomics were used to assess systemic metabolic remodeling.
sections.Results: Despite no significant differences in alpha or beta diversity, vaping exposure was associated with alterations of both gut and lung microbiomes. In stool, vaping was associated with increased Firmicutes and Proteobacteria and reduced Bacteroidetes, with depletion of Barnesiella which has been reported in CF. In lung tissue, vaping was associated with taxonomic shifts without significant diversity loss, including reduced Micrococcus, Muribaculum, and Clostridium IV, consistent with microbial remodeling that has been described in CF. Plasma metabolomics revealed vaping-associated systemic metabolic alterations consistent with oxidative stress and altered host-microbial metabolism.
sections.Conclusion: In mice, chronic ENDS aerosol exposure is sufficient to induce CF-relevant microbial and metabolic phenotypes independent of CFTR dysfunction. These findings suggest that vaping-associated oxidative stress may act as an environmental modifier capable of exacerbating gut-lung axis dysfunction and inflammatory vulnerability relevant to CF.

Gamaleya National Research Center for Epidemiology and Microbiology, Moscow, Russian Federation, 2Gamaleya National Research Center for Epidemiology and Microbiology, Moscow, Russian Federation, 3Federal Pulmonology Research Institute, Federal Medical and Biological Agency of Russia, Moscow, Russian Federation, 4Research Centre for Medical Genetics, Moscow, Russian Federation

sections.Objectives: To assess the detection rate of P. jirovecii and evaluate its association with clinical and laboratory parameters in Russian patients with cystic fibrosis (CF). Materials and
sections.Methods: In a pilot study, sputum samples from 31 CF patients (26 adults and 5 children) were analysed for P. jirovecii DNA using real-time PCR. Clinical data were collected from questionnaires completed by attending physicians.
sections.Results: P. jirovecii DNA was detected in 7 patients (22.6%; 95% CI: 10.5–39.6%). The mean threshold cycle (Cq) value was 25.9, indicating a moderate fungal load. No statistically significant differences were observed in microbial co-infections or lung function (FVC, FEV1) between colonised and non-colonised groups. However, exacerbation of chronic pulmonary infection occurred in 42.9% (3 of 7) of colonised patients versus 25.0% in non-colonised individuals. Notably, two of these three patients had high fungal loads (Cq 23.5 and 23.9), possibly implicating P. jirovecii in exacerbation pathogenesis, even in the absence of diagnosed pneumocystosis. Interestingly, 42.9% of colonised patients were receiving co-trimoxazole, which has anti-pneumocystis activity. Persistence of the pathogen despite therapy may suggest reduced susceptibility or suboptimal dosing.
sections.Conclusion: The prevalence of P. jirovecii colonisation (22.6%; median Cq 24.6) in Russian CF patients aligns with global data and shows a trend toward association with pulmonary exacerbations. Although no statistically significant differences emerged in this small cohort, the findings generate important hypotheses warranting validation in larger studies. Even asymptomatic colonisation may be clinically relevant, particularly in assessing risk and determining prophylactic needs in candidates for lung transplantation.

sections.Objectives: to assess the dynamics of microbiological parameters in cystic fibrosis patients in the Russian Federation over the period 2011–2024. Material: data from the Cystic Fibrosis Patient Registry in the Russian Federation for the period 2011–2014.
sections.Results: S. aureus is the dominant microbial species colonizing the respiratory tract of cystic fibrosis patients throughout the entire observation period. The prevalence of MSSA ranges from 53,8% (2013) to 70,0% (2023), with an average of 60,7%. There is a trend towards an increase in these figures in recent years. The proportion of MRSA ranges from 3,1% (2020) to 6,4% (2015), averaging 4,7%. The prevalence of P. aeruginosa averaged 47,1%, with the lowest infection rates of 41,7% observed in 2024, which may likely be attributed to the widespread use of targeted therapy among Russian patients. Chronic infection rates ranged from 30,8% to 34,3%, while the incidence of intermittent infection ranged from 9,6% to 16,9%. The lowest values for both indicators were also recorded in 2024. Over the 14‑year observation period, there has been a trend towards a decrease in the prevalence of Burkholderia cepacia complex‑associated infection, from a maximum of 7,9% in 2013 to 4,3% in 2024. The incidence of S. maltophilia reached its minimum value of 3,1% in 2024, with an average prevalence of 4,0%. The proportion of Achromobacter spp. averaged 5,9%. The average prevalence of non‑tuberculosis mycobacteria over the entire observation period was 1,0%.
sections.Conclusion: According to the Cystic Fibrosis Patient Registry data in the Russian Federation for 2011–2024, there has been an increase in the proportion of MSSA, a decrease in Burkholderia cepacia complex infections, and a trend towards a reduction in P. aeruginosa prevalence.

presentations.title: Intraspecific variation is key for predicting the outcome of bacterial species interactions in the cystic fibrosis lung
presentations.summary: Background: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a common recessively inherited disease characterised by consistent chronic lung infections. While S. aureus is the dominant pathogen infecting patients during childhood, P. aeruginosa often appears by early adulthood, displacing or occasionally co-existing with S. aureus. The ability of P. aeruginosa to outcompete S. aureus and dominate the CF lung is a worrying milestone in the life of a patient with CF, as P. aeruginosa infections are associated with worsening disease symptoms and mortality. Objectives: We aimed to understand the factors that allow P. aeruginosa to outcompete or...
presentations.authors_text: S. Dodd1, S. O'Brien1, J. Fothergill2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Trinity College Dublin, Microbiology, Dublin, Ireland, 2University of Liverpool, Clinical Infection, Microbiology & Immunology, Liverpool, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a common recessively inherited disease characterised by consistent chronic lung infections. Two key pathogens colonizing the CF lung are Staphylococcus aureus and Pseudomonas aeruginosa. While S. aureus is the dominant pathogen infecting patients during childhood, P. aeruginosa often appears by early adulthood, displacing or occasionally co-existing with S. aureus. The ability of P. aeruginosa to outcompete S. aureus and dominate the CF lung is a worrying milestone in the life of a patient with CF, as P. aeruginosa infections are associated with worsening disease symptoms and mortality.
sections.Objectives: We aimed to understand the factors that allow P. aeruginosa to outcompete or coexist with S. aureus, with the overall goal of managing disease progression.
sections.Methods: Here, we perform in-depth analysis of a collection of 40 P. aeruginosa isolates from a single CF patient sample. The methods employed include reciprocal supernatant assays, plaque assays, and comprehensive statistical data analysis.
sections.Results: We show that the interactions between S. aureus and P. aeruginosa are lineage-specific – so different within-patient lineages are affected by S. aureus in distinct and opposing ways. We have begun to further uncover these diverse outcomes by investigating phage production, auxotrophy, and within-population cross-feeding of P. aeruginosa.
sections.Conclusion: Our work suggests that the use of a wider range of clinical isolates is necessary to understand the role of interspecies interactions for predicting the evolution of P. aeruginosa in CF.

Recent studies have highlighted the complexity of the CF lung microbiome, where community composition and microbe–microbe interactions influence disease progression and antimicrobial susceptibility. CF polypharmacy and more specifically persistent antibiotic use contribute to this dysbiosis. However, the mechanistic effects of CF drug-microbiome interactions are not understood. Here, we validate high-throughput donor-based in vitro gut microbiome models representative of the CF and healthy gut environments to address this knowledge gap. Methods: Stool samples from CF (n=7) and healthy (n=7) participants were used to seed respective gut microbiome models. CF and healthy microbiome models were run up to 14 days under anaerobic conditions and subjec...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Northumbria University, Faculty of Science and Environment, Newcastle, United Kingdom, 2University of Nottingham, Translational Medical Sciences, School of Medicine, Nottingham, United Kingdom, 3NIHR Nottingham Biomedical Research Centre, Nottingham, United Kingdom, 4Newcastle University, Translational and Clinical Research Institute, Faculty of Medical Sciences, Newcastle, United Kingdom, 5Newcastle upon Tyne Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Paediatric Respiratory Medicine, Great North Children’s Hospital, Newcastle, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Gut symptoms and negative alterations to the gut microbiome in people with cystic fibrosis (CF) persist, despite the use of CFTR modulator therapies. CF polypharmacy and more specifically persistent antibiotic use contribute to this dysbiosis. However, the mechanistic effects of CF drug-microbiome interactions are not understood. Here, we validate high-throughput donor-based in vitro gut microbiome models representative of the CF and healthy gut environments to address this knowledge gap.
sections.Methods: Stool samples from CF (n=7) and healthy (n=7) participants were used to seed respective gut microbiome models. CF and healthy microbiome models were run up to 14 days under anaerobic conditions and subjected to the following analyses: total bacterial cell abundances (cells/ml) using flow cytometry, long read (full 16S rRNA gene) bacterial microbiome sequencing, and targeted metabolomics of short chain fatty acids (SCFA).
sections.Results: Once the gut microbiome models were established, cell abundances remained consistent for the duration. Microbiome characteristics of diversity and composition were consistently stable throughout. Microbiome abundance, diversity, and composition were significantly lower and distinct in the CF model when compared to the healthy model (p<0.05 in all instances using Kruskal-Wallis and PERMANOVA tests). Likewise, SCFA production and levels were also consistent over the 14-day period.
sections.Conclusion: Here, we validated the longitudinal use of CF gut microbiome models contrasted to healthy models. Subsequently, this tractable and representative model combined with multi-omic approaches, will allow for elucidation of drug-microbiome mechanistic interactions in the CF gut.

presentations.authors_text: S.E Weinberg1,2, G. Chalikias3, S. Ahmad1,3, E. Caamaño Gutiérrez4, R. Lord1,2, R.E Maher4, A.M Jones1,2, M.M Tunney5, A. Horsley1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Manchester, Manchester, United Kingdom, 2Manchester Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Wythenshawe, Manchester, United Kingdom, 3Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust, Department of Virology, Manchester, United Kingdom, 4University of Liverpool, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 5Queen's University Belfast, School of Pharmacy, Belfast, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Viral respiratory infections (VRIs) are a common potential trigger of pulmonary exacerbations in adults with cystic fibrosis (CF) and are associated with significant morbidity. However, prospective data on VRI incidence and clinical impact in people with CF (pwCF) remain limited.
sections.Methods: Adults with CF were recruited as part of CF-Tracker study, an ongoing longitudinal observational study of pulmonary exacerbations using detailed clinical and multi-modal phenotyping. To date, 46 pwCF have been followed prospectively for up to seven months. Nose-and-throat swabs for viral PCR were collected fortnightly via home sampling and at the onset of new pulmonary symptoms. Twenty-two healthy volunteers (HV) provided a single control swab. PCR assays targeted SARS-CoV-2, adenovirus, influenza A/B, human metapneumovirus, parainfluenza (types 1-3), respiratory syncytial virus, and human rhinovirus. Symptom scores and spirometry were recorded at each visit.
sections.Results: To date, 256 viral swabs have been collected, including symptom triggered samples. Twenty-two swabs (8.6%) tested positive (mean cycle threshold (Ct) of 30.57, range 21.19-42). Among symptom-triggered swabs, 6 / 20 (30.0%) were positive (mean Ct 30.66), compared with 16 positive results (6.8%) during scheduled stable visits (mean Ct 30.53). Among asymptomatic HV, 2 / 22 swabs (9.1%) were positive (mean Ct 37.67). Discussion: Viral positivity was substantially higher during new pulmonary symptoms than during stable visits or in HV, suggesting that VRIs may account for approximately one in four pulmonary exacerbations. Asymptomatic viral carriage in stable adults was similar to healthy controls. These findings demonstrate the feasibility of viral home sampling and reliable detection of viruses in postal samples. This ongoing study will further define the clinical relevance of these infections and their relationship to baseline lung health. Funding: LifeArc / Cystic Fibrosis Trust Innovation Hub Network.

Understanding viral involvement may guide targeted treatments and reduce unnecessary antibiotic use. We investigated how viral infections affect the airway microbiome and immune responses in children with CF (CwCF) during PExs, and explored bacteria-virus-host interactions in vitro. Methods: Throat swabs from CwCF (<5 years) were collected during routine visits and PExs for microbiome (16S rRNA sequencing), viral RNA (multiplex qPCR), and immune marker (Olink) analyses. In vitro, bacterial binding to RSV A2 was studied using FITC...
presentations.authors_text: J. Bastiaenssen1,2, N. Jouret3, E. Cauwenberghs1, P. Delputte4, S. Verhulst2,3, S. Lebeer1, I. De Boeck1, I. Spacova1, K. Van Hoorenbeeck2,3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Antwerp, Lab of Applied Microbiology and Biotechnology, Antwerp, Belgium, 2University of Antwerp, Lab of Experimental Medicine and Pediatrics, Antwerp, Belgium, 3University Hospital of Antwerp, Edegem, Belgium, 4University of Antwerp, Laboratory of Microbiology, Parasitology and Hygiene, Antwerp, Belgium

sections.Background: People with cystic fibrosis (CF) face greater clinical morbidity from viral infections, yet the role of these infections in pulmonary exacerbations (PExs) is understudied. Understanding viral involvement may guide targeted treatments and reduce unnecessary antibiotic use. We investigated how viral infections affect the airway microbiome and immune responses in children with CF (CwCF) during PExs, and explored bacteria-virus-host interactions in vitro.
sections.Methods: Throat swabs from CwCF (<5 years) were collected during routine visits and PExs for microbiome (16S rRNA sequencing), viral RNA (multiplex qPCR), and immune marker (Olink) analyses. In vitro, bacterial binding to RSV A2 was studied using FITC-labeled anti-RSV antibodies and flow cytometry. Indirect immune interactions were examined by measuring IRF and NF-κB immune signaling activation in THP1-Dual monocytes and A549-Dual lung epithelial cells.
sections.Results: 22 children were followed for one year, during which 16 PExs were included. Key respiratory viruses, including rhinovirus, RSV, and coronavirus, were detected in over 70% of PExs. During these events, the airway microbiome showed an increased relative abundance of pathogenic species such as Moraxella, while taxonomic richness declined following antibiotic treatment. Furthermore, PEx samples exhibited elevated cytokine levels (IL-1α, IFN-γ, IL-6), which decreased after antibiotic use. In vitro, Streptococcus pneumoniae showed strong RSV binding, unlike other isolates such as Staphylococcus aureus. Additionally, most isolates, e.g. S. aureus, induced high NF-κB and IRF activation, suggesting activation of inflammation and/or antiviral immune pathways.
sections.Conclusion: CwCF exhibit diverse airway microbiomes that shift during PExs, coinciding with frequent respiratory virus detection and elevated inflammation. Direct and indirect interactions between CF bacteria and viruses may have implications for improving management of viral and bacterial (co-)infections.

presentations.authors_text: U. Kavgacı1, E. Yalçın1, T. Kantemir1, D. Demirel1, N. Emiralioğlu1, D. Doğru1, U. Özçelik1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Hacettepe University, Pediatric Pulmonology, Ankara, Turkey

sections.Background: Socioeconomic status(SES) is increasingly recognized as an important determinant of health outcomes in cystic fibrosis(CF);however,data on its relationship with airway colonization remain limited in pediatric cohorts. Aim: To explore the association between SES,colonization and disease-related knowledge levels in children with CF(cwCF).
sections.Methods: This cross-sectional analysis included 45 cwCF.SES was summarized using a principal component analysis(PCA)–derived score incorporating household income,parental education,maternal employment,smoking exposure,and crowding index.Patients were categorized into two SES groups based on a median split of the PCA-SES score.Knowledge regarding infection control was assessed using a questionnaire(score range:0–15).
sections.Results: The study included 45 cwCF,with a median age of 13 years(IQR 9–15).The median knowledge score was 13(IQR 11.5–14.0).Based on the PCA-SES score, patients were split into lower(n=23)and higher(n=22)SES groups.Knowledge scores were significantly lower in the lower SES group compared with the higher SES group(median 12 vs 14; p< 0.001).Median knowledge scores were similar between patients with and without prior and current MRSA and Pseudomonas aeruginosa(Pa)colonization.Children in the lower SES group had higher prevalence of prior MRSA colonization compared to higher SES group(52.2% vs 27.3%),showing a trend toward significance(p=0.130).No significant differences were observed between SES groups with respect to current MRSA colonization, prior and current Pa colonization.
sections.Conclusion: Lower SES-score was associated with a higher rate of prior MRSA colonization,although statistical significance was not reached.SES was strongly associated with disease-related knowledge levels;however, knowledge scores were not associated with colonization status.These findings suggest that socioeconomic factors may influence infection risk through pathways beyond knowledge levels and underscore the need for larger studies.

Methods: Antimicrobial susceptibility was determined using disk diffusion and broth microdilution methods. β-lactamase genes were identified using immunochromatography and real-time PCR. Results: We analyzed 4,133 bacterial isolates obtained from patients with cystic fibrosis. The leading positions in the spectrum of microorganisms are occupied by S. aureus - 27% (n = 1108) and P. aeruginosa - 22% (n = 911). When determining the susceptibility of P. aeru...
presentations.authors_text: Z. Sadeeva1, A. Lazareva1, E. Samoilova1, Y. Gorinova2, O. Simonova2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1National Medical Research Center for Children's Health Federal State Autonomous Institution of the Ministry of Health of the Russian Federation, Laboratory of Microbiology, Moscow, Russian Federation, 2National Medical Research Center for Children's Health Federal State Autonomous Institution of the Ministry of Health of the Russian Federation, Pulmonology, Moscow, Russian Federation

sections.Objectives: To determine the spectrum of bacterial pathogens isolated from pediatric patients with cystic fibrosis between 2020 and 2024, determine the antimicrobial susceptibility of the main pathogens, and identify metallo-β-lactamase genes in P. aeruginosa.
sections.Methods: Antimicrobial susceptibility was determined using disk diffusion and broth microdilution methods. β-lactamase genes were identified using immunochromatography and real-time PCR.
sections.Results: We analyzed 4,133 bacterial isolates obtained from patients with cystic fibrosis. The leading positions in the spectrum of microorganisms are occupied by S. aureus - 27% (n = 1108) and P. aeruginosa - 22% (n = 911). When determining the susceptibility of P. aeruginosa, it was determined that resistance to meropenem and imipenem was 19% and 27%, respectively. Resistance to protected cefalosporins reached 25%. 23% of strains were resistant to aztreonam. Up to 40% of isolates were resistant to aminoglycosides. Resistance to protected penicillins and fluoroquinolones reached 30%. Particular attention was attracted by P. aeruginosa strains that showed resistance to colistin - 6%. Isolated cases of the MBL VIM- gene were detected in 9 isolates. Regarding S. aureus, all isolates studied were susceptible to tigecycline, linezolid, and vancomycin. Resistance to gentamicin, ciprofloxacin, and tetracycline was 7%, 12%, and 19%, respectively. Resistance to erythromycin and clindamycin was 31% and 25%, respectively. Notably, resistance to oxacillin (MRSA) reached 19% of strains during this period.
sections.Conclusion: S. aureus and P. aeruginosa are the main microorganisms isolated from the respiratory tract of patients with cystic fibrosis. P. aeruginosa has a relatively favorable resistance profile to antibiotics, but the emergence of colistin- resistant isolates is alarming. Among S. aureus isolates, increasing resistance to oxacillin and macrolides is noteworthy.
