presentations.authors_text: B. Van Gasse1, M. Somers1, J. Lomelino1, N. Jouret1,2, J. Bastiaenssen2, K. Van Hoorenbeeck1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Antwerp, Antwerp, Belgium, 2Antwerp University Hospital, Pediatric Pulmonology, Edegem, Belgium

sections.Objectives: The objective of this study is to assess if viral infections will trigger a Pulmonary Exacerbation (PEx) in children with Cystic Fibrosis (CF) and to identify patient-related risk factors.
sections.Methods: Children with CF, aged zero to five years, were included at the pediatric CF clinic of Antwerp University Hospital (UZA) between January 2024 and 2026. Oropharyngeal swabs for the detection of respiratory viruses by PCR were obtained during routine visits and at the time of PEx. Patients were divided into two groups (with and without PEx) and the following patient-related factors was recorded: age, gender, CFTR gene mutation, bacterial colonization, lung function, BMI and socioeconomic factors.
sections.Results: During the study period, 15 of the 21 included children experienced at least one PEx. Generalized Estimating Equations analysis showed an almost 12 times higher chance of detecting viral presence during PEx compared to routine visits (p=0.010; OR=11.66). The confounders female sex (p=0.046), lower BMI (p=0.013) and seasonal variation (p=0.032) were significantly associated with viral detection. The most prevalent virus detected during PEx was rhinovirus. Only parainfluenzavirus type 3 (PIV3) had a significantly higher presence during PEx compared to routine visits (p=0.027). Multivariate logistic regression showed no significance for patient related factors as a risk factor for the development of a PEx. Univariate analysis did show a significant effect of the patient’s CFTR mutation type (p=0.067). It showed a correlation between the Fdel580 mutation and more frequent exacerbations.
sections.Conclusion: Viral presence is almost 12 times higher during PEx compared to routine visits. Rhinovirus was the most prevalent virus during PEx, and PIV3 showed a significantly higher presence during PEx. There were no patient-related factors found that contribute to the development of PEx.

Both cohorts undergo comparable cardiovascular assessments. Cardiovascular risk profiling includes blood pressure, BMI, anthropometric measurements (chest, waist and hip circumference), a...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Wilhelmina Children's Hospital, University Medical Center Utrecht, Pediatric Pulmonology, Utrecht, Netherlands, 2University Medical Center Utrecht, Pulmonology, Utrecht, Netherlands, 3Wilhelmina Children's Hospital, University Medical Center Utrecht, Social Pediatrics, Utrecht, Netherlands, 4Sophia Children's Hospital, Erasmus Medical Center, Pediatric Cardiology, Rotterdam, Netherlands

sections.Objectives: With increasing life expectancy, people with CF may face a growing burden of cardiovascular disease (CVD). Insight into cardiovascular risk and early vascular changes from childhood into adulthood is currently limited. This study aims to assess cardiovascular risk factors and preclinical atherosclerosis across the life course in CF.
sections.Methods: The study consists of two complementary prospective cohort studies: SMART-Youth, including children with CF aged 8–18 years, and SMART-CF, including adults with CF (≥18 years). Both cohorts undergo comparable cardiovascular assessments. Cardiovascular risk profiling includes blood pressure, BMI, anthropometric measurements (chest, waist and hip circumference), abdominal visceral and subcutaneous adipose tissue (assessed by ultrasound), lipid profile, HbA1c and inflammatory markers. Lifestyle and psychosocial factors are collected using validated questionnaires. Physical activity is assessed by a physiotherapist using either a shuttle run test or bicycle ergometry. CF-related factors include CF-related liver disease, pancreatic insufficiency, CF-related diabetes, respiratory colonization, lung function, oxygen saturation, and transplantation history. Preclinical atherosclerosis is assessed using carotid intima-media thickness (cIMT), carotid distensibility, aortic pulse wave velocity (PWV) and endothelial function measurement. All assessments are repeated after two years to evaluate progression.
sections.Results: This abstract describes the study design. Enrolment is ongoing. An update on study progress will be available in June 2026.
sections.Conclusion: By applying a unified and harmonized cardiovascular assessment strategy in both pediatric and adult CF populations, our study enables evaluation of early vascular changes and cardiovascular risk trajectories across the life course. This approach may inform future timing and strategies for cardiovascular monitoring and prevention in CF.

sections.Objectives: Despite the advent of highly effective CFTR modulators, digestive symptoms (DS) remain frequently reported by patients with cystic fibrosis (CF). The French patient organisation Vaincre la Mucoviscidose, in collaboration with healthcare professionals, launched a project to better characterize and manage DS in CF.
sections.Methods: A core committee conducted two surveys. Questionnaire 1 collected patient-reported outcomes from people with CF or parents of children with CF over the previous four weeks, focusing on DS, their impact on quality of life, and current treatments. Questionnaire 2 gathered clinical experience and care management issues related to DS from CF care teams across 47 CF centers. Finally, a multidisciplinary seminar involving patients, healthcare teams, and researchers, including a gastroenterologist specialized in functional gastrointestinal disorders—was held.
sections.Results: DS were reported by 63% of the 458 respondents, 77% receiving modulators. Symptoms were more frequent in adults than in children (60% vs 40%) and in females compared with males (60% vs 40%). The most reported DS were flatulence, bloating, foul-smelling stools, and abdominal pain, affecting 80% of symptomatic patients. Fatigue was the main quality-of-life impact, reported by 65%. Although 80% of respondents were asked about DS during routine visits, only 38% of those with DS consulted a gastroenterologist. This finding is consistent with reports from CF care teams, highlighting limited involvement of gastroenterologists. Only two centers reported no issues. Major challenges included diagnostic complexity (45%) and limited therapeutic options (78%).
sections.Conclusion: The seminar facilitated discussion of survey findings with patients, care and research teams, identified priorities for improving care, including targeted training in CF for gastroenterologists, and enhanced unmet needs, which should be addressed through research.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1UZ Leuven, Leuven, Belgium, 2KU Leuven, Leuven, Belgium

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has substantially improved outcomes in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) but is increasingly associated with metabolic changes, including alterations in serum lipid profiles. The effects on lipid parameters remain unclear. We evaluated changes in serum lipid levels following initiation of ETI.
sections.Methods: All adults with CF from a single centre who initiated ETI and had serum lipid measurements (total cholesterol (total-c), low-density cholesterol (LDL-c), high-density cholesterol (HDL-c) and triglycerides (TG)) and body mass index (BMI) at baseline and follow-up were included. Measurements were obtained 0-2 months before ETI initiation and 10-18 months after initiation under routine, non-fasting conditions. Additional inclusion criteria were: ≥1 F508del mutation, ≥1 year of ETI treatment, no lung transplantation and documented ETI adherence.
sections.Results: 56 adults with CF were included (31 males; mean age 34.7±12.4 years; 44 with pancreatic insufficiency (EPI); 12 CF diabetes). Mean total-c increased from 140.7 mg/dL (range 59-219) to 153.0 mg/dL (range 81-248) (p<0.01), with significant increases observed in males and individuals with EPI. LDL-c increased from 63.9 mg/dL (range 18-132) to 76.6 mg/dL (range 29-145) (p<0.01), independent of sex and pancreatic status. TG levels decreased from 107.4 mg/dL (range 42-252) to 94.9 mg/dL (range 42-215) (p<0.05), while HDL-c remained unchanged. Mean BMI increased from 23.2 kg/m² to 24.0 kg/m².
sections.Conclusion: ETI initiation was associated with heterogeneous changes in serum lipid profiles. Total-c and LDL-c increased, whereas TG decreased unexpectedly. HDL-c remained unchanged. These changes likely reflect a normalisation of lipid metabolism. However, in an aging CF population, they may signal emerging cardiovascular risk profiles previously less prominent in CF care. Attention to a healthy diet with an appropriate fatty acid composition is advisable.

sections.Objectives: Cystic Fibrosis Liver Disease (CFLD) comprises a spectrum of hepatobiliary manifestations, including hepatic steatosis, biliary disease and cirrhosis, and is an independent risk factor for CFRD. This exploratory study aims to address the knowledge gaps in the link between early glucose abnormalities and hepatic steatosis.
sections.Methods: All CF patients (n= 252) attending annual review in 2024 at Ghent University Hospital were eligible, excluding CFRD (n=30), CFLD (n=18), and prior liver transplantation (n=2). Assessments included transient elastography (TE), controlled attenuation parameter (CAP), relative fat mass (FM%) and fat mass index (FMI) measured by BIA, HbA1c, and OGTT. Non-parametric statistics and linear regression were used. Results are reported as median [IQR], unless stated otherwise.
sections.Results: Patients’ characteristics are summarized in table 1. In adults, 32/116 (28%) had a CAP >238 dB/m and showed a higher HbA1c% (5.7[5.4; 5.9] vs 5.4[5.2; 5.7]p =.019), FM % (20.6[15; 25.9] vs 26.3[19.9; 32]p=.005) and FMI (kg/m2) 4.5[3.1; 5.5] vs 6.4[4.3; 8.2]p=.002. In children, 17/68 (25%) had a CAP >238 dB/m with higher FM% 26.5[24.2; 29.2] vs 19.5[13.5; 24.3],p=.042 and FMI (kg/m2) 4.3[3.7; 5] vs 3.3[2.2; 4.3],p=.017 and a non-significant higher HbA1c%. CAP was not a predictor of HbA1c%, whereas FM % and FMI were significantly predicted HbA1c% in adult men. The regression model with FMI explained a significant proportion of the variance in HbA1c% (R2=.357,F(1,44)=23.91,p<.001;(β=.60,t(44)=4.89,p<.001)). Specifically, each one-unit increase in FMI, was associated with 2.68% increase in HbA1c% (B=2.68, 95% CI [1.57,3.78]). sections.Conclusion: We observed higher FM%, FMI and HbA1c% levels in CF individuals with a higher CAP values. However, only FM% and FMI emerged as significant predictors of HbA1c% in adult men. Considering evidence from the non-CF literature, these findings warrant further research.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of South Florida Morsani College of Medicine, Pediatrics, Tampa, United States, 2University of South Florida, College of Nursing and Microbiomes Institute, Tampa, United States, 3University of Colorado Anschutz Medical Campus and Children’s Hospital Colorado, Pediatrics, Aurora, United States, 4University of South Florida, Molecular Pharmacology & Physiology Department, Tampa, United States, 5Johns Hopkins All Children’s Hospital, Pediatrics, St Petersburg, United States, 6Johns Hopkins University, Departments of Pediatrics and Medicine, St Petersburg, United States

sections.Objectives: Gastrointestinal (GI) manifestations remain burdensome for people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) as symptoms and inflammation persist despite elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) use. While stool calprotectin correlates with patient-reported outcomes, data on upper GI symptom burden and esophageal inflammatory markers remain unexplored. We evaluated correlations between proximal (PE) and distal esophagus (DE) inflammation using a novel minimally invasive esophageal string test (EST) and GI-related quality of life (QoL) in pwCF on and off ETI vs. healthy controls (HC).
sections.Methods: This cross-sectional study included pwCF aged 6–25 years on or off ETI and healthy controls. PedsQL™ Gastrointestinal Symptoms Scales (PedsQL GI) were reported (higher scores=better QoL). Biomarker concentrations were measured by multiplex immunoassay using exudates from the EST from the PE and DE (IL-1β, IL-33, IL-6, IL-8, MMP-9, REG3α, TNF-α, and calprotectin). Spearman correlations with mean PedsQL GI items scores were calculated (p<0.05).
sections.Results: A total of 36 individuals completed the study (19 on ETI, 5 off ETI, 12 HC). In the on ETI group, “stomach discomfort when eating” QoL negatively correlated with IL-8 (rho -0.52; p=0.031) and “gas and bloating” QoL negatively correlated with IL-1β (-0.51; p=0.038) in the PE; but positive correlations were observed in the DE. In contrast, those off ETI had no significant correlations. In HC, “trouble swallowing” QoL correlated negatively with IL-1β (-0.67; p=0.024) in the PE.
sections.Conclusion: Increased PE inflammation was associated with worse GI-symptoms in pwCF on ETI and in HC. The lack of similar findings in the DE could indicate that swallowed airway secretions may contribute to inflammation in the PE. No correlations were observed in pwCF off ETI, likely due to small sample size. This suggests that pwCF with upper GI symptoms may have persistent inflammation despite ETI which may be derived in part from airway inflammation.

Only a minority (27/159) of raised PTHs were paired with deficient...
presentations.authors_text: A. Derry1, H. Gledhill2, D. Waine2, D. Derry2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Sheffield Medical School, Sheffield, United Kingdom, 2Plymouth Adult CF centre, Plymouth, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: European guidelines recommend routine monitoring of PTH to optimise treatment with vitamin D (vitD). However we observed that PwCF with high PTH were often not vitD deficient. We sought other explanations.
sections.Methods: Blood results from Annual Review of adult PwCF between 2016-2025 were analysed. PTH was compared with paired vitD, calcium, phosphate (PO4) and magnesium (Mg). Oral calcium intake was assessed in a subgroup. PwCF with primary hyperparathyroidism were excluded.
sections.Results: PTH was raised above 7.2 pmol/l (laboratory ULN) in 159/560 (28%) samples from 47/83 PwCF. There was only weak correlation (r=-0.28) between PTH and vitD. Only a minority (27/159) of raised PTHs were paired with deficient VitDs (<25 ng/l; Table). Low serum calcium, Mg and raised PO4 explained a further 1,12 and 5 raised PTHs respectively. Deficient VitD (<25 ng/l)VitD 25<>74 ng/lOptimal VitD (>=75 ng/l) Raised PTH >7.2 pmol/l278349Chi square testNormal PTH 1.6<>7.2 pmol/l14173214p<0.0001Table: PTH level by Vitamin D status 20/83 (24%) patients contributed 30% of blood results, but 118/159 (74%) of raised PTHs. All 20 of these patients were pancreatic exocrine insufficient (PEI). Pancreatic sufficient patients (15/83) contributed 15% of results, but only 4/159 (2.5%) of raised PTHs. No other clinical parameter tested had a stronger association. Raised PTH did not correlate with low (<1 g/day) oral calcium intake (as assessed in 2025 in 47 patients), but this analysis was confounded as PwCF had previously been prescribed calcium supplements in response to a high PTH.
sections.Conclusion: Although PTH had a weak negative correlation with vitD, PTH was raised in 25% of non-vitD deficient samples, limiting its value as a marker of inadequate vitD replacement. Association was observed between raised PTH and PEI status. Testing the hypothesis that PTH increased in response to reduced calcium absorption (eg after sequestration with excess bile salts) was confounded by analysis not using contemporaneous calcium intake. The possibility of assay interference (ie false positives) is also being considered.

presentations.summary: Objective: Immune dysregulation in cystic fibrosis (CF) may increase susceptibility to autoimmune diseases (AID). Undiagnosed AID could worsen persistent inflammation, nutritional and endocrine dysfunction, treatment burden, and misinterpretation of CF disease progression. We assessed the prevalence of celiac disease (CD), hypothyroidism requiring thyroid hormone replacement, and pancreatic islet autoantibodies in a pediatric CF cohort. Methods: Single-centre prospective registry data (2019-2025) from 150 children were analysed. CD screening followed ESPGHAN guidelines. Thyroid function was monitored annually via TSH, with hypothyroidism requiring levothyroxine defined as persistently elevated TSH plus low fre...
presentations.authors_text: N. Farkas1, R. Molnár2, B. Varga3, M. Imrei2, A. Párniczky2, K. Ocskay2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Pécs, Medical School, Institute of Bioanalysis, Pécs, Hungary, 2Heim Pál Pediatrics Institute, Budapest, Hungary, 3University of Pécs, Medical School, Institute for Translational Medicine, Pécs, Hungary

sections.Objectives: Immune dysregulation in cystic fibrosis (CF) may increase susceptibility to autoimmune diseases (AID). Undiagnosed AID could worsen persistent inflammation, nutritional and endocrine dysfunction, treatment burden, and misinterpretation of CF disease progression. We assessed the prevalence of celiac disease (CD), hypothyroidism requiring thyroid hormone replacement, and pancreatic islet autoantibodies in a pediatric CF cohort.
sections.Methods: Single-centre prospective registry data (2019-2025) from 150 children were analysed. CD screening followed ESPGHAN guidelines. Thyroid function was monitored annually via TSH, with hypothyroidism requiring levothyroxine defined as persistently elevated TSH plus low free T4. Pancreatic islet autoantibodies (GAD, IAA, IA-2, ZnT8) were measured at least once. Autoantibody positivity was defined as ≥1 positive marker. Descriptive statistics and prevalence rates were calculated.
sections.Results: Median age of the cohort was 10.62 years [IQR 5.25–16.56], 48% male). CD was diagnosed in 7 patients (4.7%; 28.6% male), with age at diagnosis ranging from 1.9 to 9.1 years. Hypothyroidism was identified in 5 cases (3.3%; 80% male), with onset from infancy to adolescence. At least one islet autoantibody (AAB) was present in 21 children (16%; 29% male; median age 11.32 years [IQR 7.64–15.91]), among which, 7 (33%) had CF-related diabetes, 4 (19%) had impaired glucose tolerance, and 10 (48%) had normal glucose tolerance. Co-occurrence of autoimmune conditions was rare: one child had CD plus islet AABs with diabetes, and two had hypothyroidism plus islet AABs with diabetes. No patient presented with all three conditions simultaneously.
sections.Conclusion: In this pediatric CF cohort, the prevalence of celiac disease, hypothyroidism, and pancreatic islet AAB positivity appeared higher than reported rates in the general non-CF pediatric population. Funding: National Research, Development and Innovation Fund (NKFIH 138929) and PTE ÁOK-KA Grant (KA-2025-19).

In adults with CF, there is an increased incidence and earlier development of CRC, which includes an accelerated progression of adenomatous polyps. We aimed to determine the prevalence of pks+ E. coli in adults with CF and identify microbial factors that promote a tumorigenesis-permissive niche in the gut. ​Methods: Stool samples and DNA were analyzed from the SCREEN-CF study cohort (X18-0379 & HREC/18/RPAH/536). Intestinal inflammation was assessed via fecal calprotectin. Metagenomic taxonomic libraries were prepared using Illumina protocols...
presentations.authors_text: J. van Dorst1, N. Taylor2,3, B.L.D.U. Pushpakumara1, Z.T. Tan1, D. Buchanan4, P.S. Haber5,6,7, E. Nash5,7, S. Visser2, A. Volovets7, S. Sivam2,5, C.Y. Ooi1,8
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of New South Wales, School of Clinical Medicine, Discipline of Paediatrics & Child Health, Randwick, Australia, 2Royal Prince Alfred Hospital, Department of Respiratory and Sleep Medicine, Camperdown, Australia, 3University of New South Wales, School of Clinical Medicine, Medicine & Health, Kensington, Australia, 4University of Melbourne, Department of Clinical Pathology, Melbourne, Australia, 5University of Sydney, Syndey Medical School, Camperdown, Australia, 6Royal Prince Alfred Hospital, Drug Health Services, Camperdown, Australia, 7Royal Prince Alfred Hospital, AW Morrow Gastroenterology and Liver Centre, Camperdown, Australia, 8Sydney Children's Hospital Network, Department of Gastroenterology, Randwick, Australia

sections.Objectives: Polyketide synthase island-positive (Pks+) E. coli is a genotoxic gut bacterium linked to colorectal cancer (CRC) tumorigenesis via the genotoxin colibactin. In adults with CF, there is an increased incidence and earlier development of CRC, which includes an accelerated progression of adenomatous polyps. We aimed to determine the prevalence of pks+ E. coli in adults with CF and identify microbial factors that promote a tumorigenesis-permissive niche in the gut. ​
sections.Methods: Stool samples and DNA were analyzed from the SCREEN-CF study cohort (X18-0379 & HREC/18/RPAH/536). Intestinal inflammation was assessed via fecal calprotectin. Metagenomic taxonomic libraries were prepared using Illumina protocols and sequenced on the NovaSeq X Plus platform. Detection of the pks island was assessed with polymerase chain reaction (PCR) targeting a 283 base pair sequence in the clbB gene. Individuals were separated into three groups based on their colonoscopy results.
sections.Results: Of the 49 CF patients; 35 (71%) had no pathology (NORMAL), 12 (25%) had adenomatous polyps (AP), and 2 (4%) had malignancy (CRC). pks+ E. coli was detected in 1/35 (3%), 5/12 (42%), and 2/2 (100%) of these groups respectively. Individuals with either AP or CRC (ileocolonic neoplasia) were 34 times more likely to harbor pks+ E. coli than those with NORMAL colonoscopy findings (OR = 34.0, 95% CI 5.00-691, p = 0.002). The presence of pks+ E. coli correlated with higher overall E. coli burden (p = 0.0009), but not with fecal inflammation, bacterial composition, or the presence of other genotoxic bacterial species. ​
sections.Conclusion: Pks+ E. coli is not universally abundant among adults with CF, but its presence is strongly associated with ileocolonic neoplasia, indicating a potential role in pathogenesis. If validated in larger cohorts, pks+ E. coli could provide a clinically meaningful biomarker for early detection, risk stratification and a potential target for precision intervention.

presentations.title: Assessment of abdominal symptoms and intestinal inflammation in children and adolescents with cystic fibrosis before and after initiation of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor therapy
presentations.summary: Introduction: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a rare genetic disease caused by mutations in the CFTR gene that result in dysfunctional protein and affect several body systems, including the gastrointestinal tract. The changes caused by CF in the GI tract include dysmotility, dysbiosis, intestinal inflammation, and abdominal symptoms such as pain, changes in bowel habits, and distension, which are frequent in people with CF. The study aims to establish the prevalence of these complaints in children with CF and investigate associations between symptoms, clinical conditions, and marker of intestinal inflammation before and after the initiation of therapy with CFTR modulator drug (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor - ETI)....
presentations.authors_text: G.C. dos Santos1, L.C. Montera1, L.M. Genaro2, B.A.G. Rodrigues2, R.F. Leal2, A.F. Ribeiro3, J.D. Ribeiro3, E.A. Lomazi3, UNICAMP Modulators CFTR Study Group (UNIMCS Group)
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Campinas, Campinas, Brazil, 2University of Campinas, Surgery Department, Campinas, Brazil, 3University of Campinas, Department of Pediatrics, Campinas, Brazil

sections.Methods: Twenty-two pediatric patients with genetic diagnosis of CF, treated at a Brazilian reference center, were included. The methods involved systematic assessment of gastrointestinal symptoms using a questionnaire, biochemical analysis of fecal calprotectin (FC) as marker of intestinal inflammation, and collection of clinical and anthropometric data from medical records.
sections.Results: 86.3% of participants (n=19) showed elevated FC levels (>50 mcg/g), indicating high prevalence of intestinal inflammation. The most common abdominal symptoms were pain and flatulence, present in about half of the patients. No significant associations were observed between FC levels and clinical or symptomatic variables. After three months of ETI therapy, there was average reduction of 73% in FC levels, but no statistically significant difference in the frequency of abdominal symptoms.
sections.Conclusion: There is high prevalence of intestinal inflammation and abdominal symptoms in a sample of Brazilian children with CF, and therapy with ETI can reduce the level of intestinal inflammation.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1University Clinic for Respiratory Diseases in Children - Kozle, Skopje, North Macedonia, The Republic of, 2Ss Cyril and Methodius University, Faculty of Medicine, Skopje, North Macedonia, The Republic of

sections.Objectives: The novel triple modulatory therapy Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) has shown clinical efficacy in patients with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). Our aim was to evaluate the impact of ETI on basic serum liver and kidney function tests in pwCF in the University Clinic for Respiratory Diseases in Children - Kozle in Skopje, Republic of North Macedonia 18 months after initiation of the therapy.
sections.Methods: Data of 28 pwCF eligible for ETI was obtained. We have monitored aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), total bilirubin (TB), urea and creatinin on the start and after 18 months of the therapy with ETI. We have used a significant threshold of p<0.05. Sub analysis of age, sex and genotype was performed.
sections.Results: Median age was 29.03 years (range 16-45), 16 male (57%) and 12 female (43%), 11 (39%) homozygous for F508del and 18 (61%) heterozygous for F508del. Mean values at the start were: AST 26.33 U/L (±13.26), ALT 30.11 U/L (±21,27), TB 12.95 μmol/l (±7.77), urea 5.08 mmol/l (±1.13) and creatinin 68.82 μmol/l (±14.81). After 18 month of initiation of the therapy mean values were: AST 27.00 U/L (±13.87), ALT 28.50 U/L (±14.75), TB 20.30 μmol/l (±14.72), urea 5.13 mmol/l (±1.10) and creatinin 75.79 μmol/l (±15.11). At the beginning and after 18 months of treatment, there was no significant change in the serum levels of AST, ALT, urea, creatinin, p=0.396, p=0.944, p=0.278, p=0.108, respectively. There was significant increase in bilirubin levels p<0.05.
sections.Conclusion: Our study has shown that ETI is associated with increased bilirubin levels, but there is no impact on AST, ALT, urea and creatinin plasma levels 18 months after initiation.

presentations.authors_text: G. Putti1,2, A. Gramegna1,2, G. Alicandro1,3, M. Contarini2, S. Misuraca2, M.P. Adorni4, F. Zimetti5, M. Mantero1,2, M. Ruscica6,7, F. Blasi2,1

sections.Objectives: The aim of this longitudinal study was to describe changes in HDL Cholesterol Efflux Capacity (CEC) and Cholesterol Loading Capacity (CLC) in adults with CF (awCF) after starting therapy with Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI).
sections.Methods: Serum of awCF was collected before starting ETI therapy and 6 months later, a pool of healthy controls was also enrolled for healthy serum comparison. HDL was isolated and incubated for 6 hours with different cell lines, to evaluate their capacity to promote cholesterol efflux. ABCA1-mediated CEC was studied on J774A.1 cell line, while ABCG1-mediated CEC was studied using CHO-K1 and CHO-L3 cell lines. CLC was instead measured using macrophages derived from THP-1 monocytes. Intracellular cholesterol was determined with fluorimetric method and normalised for protein content.
sections.Results: Fifty eight awCF participated (males 61%; median age 41). Baseline awCF CEC was significantly lower than in health controls (−15% for ABCA1 and −11% for ABCG1). Comparing the two timepoints in awCF, after six months of ETI therapy CEC increased of +13% (ABCA1) and +11% (ABCG1) compared to baseline, reaching values comparable to healthy controls. Regarding CLC, no significant difference between awCF at baseline and controls was found. After 6 months of ETI, CLC decreased of 7%. Higher HDL CEC reduces the risk of foamy macrophages formation, possibly decreasing the risk of atherosclerotic plaques formation at endothelial level. Results also suggest that patients do not have an increased CLC per se, and therapy appears to reduce such phenomenon, reinforcing the hypothesis of a positive clinical net effect.
sections.Conclusion: Our results suggest that the correction of CFTR improves HDL function, possibly exerting a protective cardiovascular effect. Larger population studies with longer follow-up time should certainly be conducted to validate these results and provide a clinical confirmation of atherosclerotic risk reduction induced by ETI in awCF.

presentations.title: Gastrointestinal manifestations assessed by the CFAbd-Score in patients with cystic fibrosis
presentations.summary: Introduction: Gastrointestinal (GI) manifestations are common in patients with Cystic Fibrosis (CF). Their etiology can be attributed to three pathophysiological factors: inflammation, dysbiosis, and GI dysmotility. Better understanding the GI symptom context and developing more specific and early treatments are priorities for managing these patients. Objective: To evaluate the prevalence and factors associated to gastrointestinal manifestations in patients with Cystic Fibrosis. Methods: Cross-sectional observational study, conducted between April and September 2024, after approval from the Ethics Committee of the Institution. We included 59 children and adolescents with Cystic Fibrosis treated at the Pediatri...
presentations.authors_text: L.C.M. Wiezel1, J.D. Ribeiro1, A.F. Ribeiro1, E.A. Lomazi1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1UNICAMP, Campinas, Brazil

sections.Objectives: To evaluate the prevalence and factors associated to gastrointestinal manifestations in patients with Cystic Fibrosis.
sections.Methods: Cross-sectional observational study, conducted between April and September 2024, after approval from the Ethics Committee of the Institution. We included 59 children and adolescents with Cystic Fibrosis treated at the Pediatrics outpatient clinic of the Hospital das Clínicas at Unicamp. Gastrointestinal symptoms were assessed using a validated questionnaire in Portuguese, the CFAbd-Score, to investigate the presence, frequency, and correlation of gastrointestinal manifestations. Data analysis was performed descriptively and inferentially using chi-square tests and Pearson's correlation.
sections.Results: GI manifestations were reported in 53/59 patients. Symptoms of abdominal distension and nausea had a significant positive correlation with abdominal pain. The occurrence of abdominal pain was more frequent in patients experiencing exacerbations, and, although the statistical association was not significant, there was a positive correlation between abdominal pain and respiratory exacerbations.
sections.Conclusion: Gastrointestinal manifestations were reported in approximately 90% of the respondents. The report of abdominal pain was positively correlated to symptoms of abdominal distension, nausea, and respiratory exacerbation. CFAbd-Score worked as a useful tool for assessing GI manifestations in CF patients.

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis diabetes (CFD) is a common complication of cystic fibrosis (CF), affecting 40-50% of adults with CF (1). Guidelines state: “insulin may not always be the preferred therapy, and consideration should be given to dietary modification and other available anti-diabetic agents” (2). sections.Methods: A retrospective analysis of patients’ data pre- and post-metformin initiation was conducted. Changes in body mass index (BMI), HbA1c, Libre™ time in range (TIR), FEV1 and insulin requirements were evaluated and analysed using paired t-test or Wilcoxon’s signed rank test using SPSS v31.

presentations.authors_text: S. Rupčić1, I.K. Crnogorac2, I. Senečić-Čala2,1, I. Bambir3, L. Omerza3, I. Todorić3, A. Vukić Dugac3,1, D. Tješić - Drinković3

sections.Methods: Serum levels of 25-hydroxyvitamin D [25(OH)D], vitamin A, and vitamin E were measured before initiation of CFTRm therapy (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor) and after one year. Changes were analyzed using paired-samples t-tests; effect sizes were expressed as Cohen’s d (p<0.05). Statistical analyses were performed using R.
sections.Results: Thirty pediatric patients (7-18 years, median 15) were included. Before initiation of CFTRm therapy, mean serum levels were 81.29 ±28.61 nmol/L for vitamin D (reference value - rv.>75 nmol/L), 1.2±0.43 µmol/L for vitamin A (rv. 1.05-2.80 µmol/L), and 22.91±7.78 µmol/L for vitamin E (rv. 21.8-50.4 µmol/L). After one year of therapy, mean levels for vitamin D were 75.77±24.12 nmol/L, for vitamin A 1.50±0.53 µmol/L and for vitamin E 20.56±6.95 µmol/L. A statistically significant increase in vitamin A levels was observed (t(29)=2.79, p=0.009, Cohen’s d=0.51), while vitamin E concentrations showed a statistically significant decrease (t(29)=-2.42, p=0.022, Cohen’s d=-0.44). No statistically significant change was found for vitamin D (p=0.283). Ultimately, after one year of therapy, the overall number of patients with a deficiency of at least one vitamin remained unchanged, due to a deterioration in vitamin E status, despite an improvement in vitamin A status.
sections.Conclusion: CFTRm therapy was associated with increased serum vitamin A levels, unchanged vitamin D levels, and a redistribution of vitamin E values with a decreased mean after one year. Overall, CFTRm therapy was not associated with a reduced prevalence of fat soluble vitamin deficiencies, potentially due to suboptimal supplementation adherence related to perceived health improvement.

presentations.title: Appropriateness and acceptability of colonoscopy screening in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF)
presentations.summary: Objectives: Because of an increased risk of colonic cancer, colonoscopy screening is recommended for pwCF from the age of 40, and from the age of 30 years post-transplantation. A shared decision approach is advised as pwCF may have other health priorities, complications and co-morbidities. We reviewed the clinical reasons why some pwCF were considered unsuitable for colonoscopy and why some declined the procedure. Results: Of 366 pwCF at our Centre, 84 fell in the age category for considering screening colonoscopy; 17 (20%) were post lung transplant. Fifteen (18%) pwCF were considered unsuitable for screening colonoscopy for medical reasons (11 had advanced lung disease with mean FEV1 24% predicted; 4 had sign...
presentations.authors_text: A. Anderson1, J. Catlow1, A. Duffy1, S. Doe1, C. Echevarria1, S.J Bourke1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Royal Victoria Infirmary, Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Newcastle upon Tyne, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Because of an increased risk of colonic cancer, colonoscopy screening is recommended for pwCF from the age of 40, and from the age of 30 years post-transplantation. A shared decision approach is advised as pwCF may have other health priorities, complications and co-morbidities. We reviewed the clinical reasons why some pwCF were considered unsuitable for colonoscopy and why some declined the procedure.
sections.Results: Of 366 pwCF at our Centre, 84 fell in the age category for considering screening colonoscopy; 17 (20%) were post lung transplant. Fifteen (18%) pwCF were considered unsuitable for screening colonoscopy for medical reasons (11 had advanced lung disease with mean FEV1 24% predicted; 4 had significant co-morbidities as oesophageal cancer, ischaemic heart or cerebrovascular disease; one was currently pregnant). Four (5%) pwCF were aged over 75 years, above the recommended age in the general population, and 6 (7%) had a CF-related disorder and were not offered colonoscopy. Ten (12%) had already had recent colonoscopy for clinical reasons. Thirty-nine consented to screening colonoscopy although 5 wanted to postpone till next year. Ten declined screening colonoscopy. Reasons for declining colonoscopy included ‘don’t want colonoscopy’, ‘not prepared to take bowel preparation’, ‘not a priority for me’, ‘not key issue at present’, ‘enough medical problems already’, ‘no bowel problems’. To date no patient has been diagnosed with cancer at screening colonoscopy although 2 post-transplant pwCF have previously undergone hemicolectomy for cancer.
sections.Conclusion: Some pwCF are not suitable for screening colonoscopy because of advanced lung disease and co-morbidities. The uptake of screening colonoscopy is good. Other screening methods could be considered to define risk in those who are unsuitable or who decline colonoscopy.

sections.Objectives: Adequate bowel preparation is critical for effective colorectal polyp and cancer surveillance in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). CF-specific bowel preparation regimens require extended duration and higher cumulative laxative exposure. Real-world preparation quality and protocol standardisation are poorly described. This study aimed to characterise bowel preparation quality across CF centres and evaluate a CF-specific protocol at a single centre.
sections.Methods: A multi-centre retrospective cohort study of pwCF undergoing colonoscopy across three Australian adult CF centres between 2014–2024 was performed. Bowel preparation quality was categorised using the Aronchick scale (excellent, good/satisfactory, fair/adequate, poor/unsatisfactory or unknown) for all procedures. Overall bowel preparation quality was described across centres with pre/post-implementation analysis at a single intervention centre following protocol introduction in 2019, using procedure-level data with patient-level clustering. Pre/post analyses were limited to the intervention centre.
sections.Results: Across all centres, 353 colonoscopies were analysed. Bowel preparation quality was excellent in 15.3%, good/satisfactory in 54.7%, fair/adequate in 13.9%, and poor/unsatisfactory in 13.6%, unknown 2.5%. At the intervention centre 218 colonoscopies were analysed (48 pre-implementation; 170 post): poor/unsatisfactory preparation decreased from 18.8% (9/48) to 9.0% (15/170) (OR 0.43, 95% CI 0.19–0.99; p = 0.048). Fair/adequate (less than satisfactory) preparation decreased from 29.2% (14/48) to 15.3% (26/170) (OR 0.45, 95% CI 0.22–0.94; p = 0.034).
sections.Conclusion: In adults with CF, bowel preparation quality is variable, with approximately one-quarter of procedures demonstrating less than satisfactory preparation. Implementation of a CF-specific protocol was associated with improved quality, supporting standardised, CF-tailored bowel preparation protocols.

sections.Background: Gastrointestinal (GI) symptoms are common complications of cystic fibrosis (CF), contributing significantly to disease burden and impacting on quality of life. This has led to the development of a CF gastroenterology outpatient clinic within our adult CF service, led by a specialist gastroenterologist and CF dietitian.
sections.Objectives: To describe referral indications, investigations required, and diagnoses made within a specialist CF gastroenterology clinic.
sections.Methods: Clinical activity from the monthly hybrid CF gastroenterology clinic was retrospectively reviewed, using electronic medical records (Jan-Nov 2025).
sections.Results: Over 11 months, nine gastroenterology clinics with 84 appointments were scheduled. Of these, 67 appointments were attended (79.8%). Among 57 unique patients (35 female), median age was 37 years (range 17–64), and 45 were pancreatic insufficient. The predominant indications for referral to the clinic are summarised in table 1. Table 1. Table 2. CF gastroenterology clinic: clinical investigations and diagnoses Investigation (n)Coeliac screen (6) Hydrogen breath test (6) Colonoscopy (5) CT abdomen (4) Gastroscopy (4) Faecal calprotectin (3) Oesophageal manometry (3) pH impedance test (2) Wireless pH monitoring (2)Flexible sigmoidoscopy (2) Colonic transit study (1) CT colonography (1) Defecating proctogram (1) SeHCAT scan (1) Abdominal ultrasound (1) Faecal elastase (1) Faecal immunochemical test (1) Diagnosis (n) Irritable bowel syndrome (8) Constipation (6) Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (5) Functional gut disorder (4) Bile acid malabsorption (3) Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (2) Slow gut transit (2) Haemorrhoids (2)Hiatus hernia (1) Oesophageal dysmotility (1) Oesophageal hypersensitivity (1) Possible Dyssynergic defecation (1) Abdominophrenic Dyssynergia (1) Coeliac disease (1) Suspected post-swallow aspiration (1)
sections.Conclusion: Abdominal pain, reflux, bloating, and constipation were common indications for referral to the CF gastroenterology clinic. The breath of resultant investigations and diagnoses highlight the importance of a joint CF and specialist gastroenterology service to optimise treatment of the complex gastrointestinal manifestations in adults with CF. Future work will evaluate patient satisfaction through a structured survey of the clinic population.

presentations.title: Dynamics of fibrotic changes in the liver according to transient elastography at the start and after 2 years of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor therapy
presentations.summary: Hepatic involvement is frequent in patients with cystic fibrosis (CF), with focal biliary cirrhosis being the pathognomonic hepatic manifestation. Transient elastography (TE) (Fibroscan) is a non-invasive, user-friendly and quick technique that provides an objective and reproducible measure of liver stiffness. The aim is to assess the condition of the liver parenchyma according to TE at the start and after 2 years of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) therapy. Objectives: 24 children with CF (10.0±3.8 years), 8 boys and 16 girls were examined before and after 2 years of ETI therapy. Methods: In each case, transient elastography measurement was performed immediately after the routine annual ultrasound evalu...
presentations.authors_text: E. Zhekaite1,2, E. Kondratyeva1,2, A. Voronkova1,2, G. Tsurkin1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Research Centre for Medical Genetics, Cystic Fibrosis, Moscow, Russian Federation, 2Research Clinical Institute of Childhood Ministry of Health of the Moscow Region, Moscow, Russian Federation

sections.Objectives: 24 children with CF (10.0±3.8 years), 8 boys and 16 girls were examined before and after 2 years of ETI therapy.
sections.Methods: In each case, transient elastography measurement was performed immediately after the routine annual ultrasound evaluation of the liver. Sonographic staging of the liver was classified from 1 to 4 by the METAVIR score system. Ten validated TE measurements were performed in each patient with the result expressed in kilopascals (kPa). The median value was considered representative of the elastic modulus of the liver.
sections.Results: Median elasticity in CF patients was significantly higher at the start Me (Q1-Q3) 5.7 (4.7-8.7) kPa than after 2 years of ETI therapy 4.6 (4.0-6.5) (p < 0.001). Among the examined patients, 12 patients had no fibrosis (F0) at the start of therapy, 6 patients had F1, 0 patients had F2, 4 patients had F3, and 2 patients had cirrhosis (F4) by METAVIR. After 2 years of therapy, 19 patients had no fibrosis, 3 patients had F1 fibrosis, 1 child had F2, and 1 patient had cirrhosis (F4). One 17-year-old female patient, who had been diagnosed with cirrhosis for 6 years and who demonstrated progression of esophageal varices and hypersplenism during 2 years of ETI therapy, significantly reduced TE value from 15 to 7.7 kPa during the third year of therapy.
sections.Conclusion: The obtained results indicate the possibility of a reversible process not only in fibrosis stages 1-2 according to METAVIR, but also in the cirrhosis.

presentations.title: Analysis of fat-soluble vitamin levels during CFTR modulator therapy
presentations.summary: Patients with cystic fibrosis (CF) have an increased risk of fat-soluble vitamin deficiency, primarily due to malabsorption syndrome and pancreatic insufficiency. Both a deficiency of fat-soluble vitamins and their excess can have a negative impact on health. The aim of the study was to investigate the effect of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) administration in children with CF on levels of fat-soluble vitamins (FSVs) A, D, E, K. Objectives: Based on the results of examination of 117 children (9.4±4.6 years). Methods: Serum vitamin A, E, and K levels were determined before and after 1 year CFTR modulator therapy using HPLC-MS (high-performance liquid chromatography with mass-selective detection), and vi...
presentations.authors_text: E. Zhekaite1,2, E. Kondratyeva1,2, A. Voronkova2,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Research Centre for Medical Genetics, Cystic Fibrosis, Moscow , Russian Federation, Russian Federation, 2Research Clinical Institute of Childhood Ministry of Health of the Moscow Region, Moscow, Russian Federation

sections.Objectives: Based on the results of examination of 117 children (9.4±4.6 years).
sections.Methods: Serum vitamin A, E, and K levels were determined before and after 1 year CFTR modulator therapy using HPLC-MS (high-performance liquid chromatography with mass-selective detection), and vitamin D levels were determined using enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). All patients were advised to take FSVs from the moment they were diagnosed with CF.
sections.Results: A significant difference was observed in vitamin A and K levels at baseline and after one year of targeted therapy. Vitamin A levels increased from 0.42±0.17 to 0.45±0.18 μg/ml (p=0.010), remaining within the normal range. Vitamin K levels decreased from 0.6±12.4 to 0.59±0.14 ng/ml (p=0.05), also within the normal range. At the start of targeted therapy, 60% of children had normal vitamin D levels, while 40% had deficiency or insufficiency. Low vitamin A levels were established in 20% of patients, normal in 57%. Vitamin E levels were within normal limits in 73% of children, below normal in 2%. An increase in the proportion of children with high levels of vitamins A from 22% to 57% and E from 25% to 42% was observed in those treated with ETI. Normal vitamin D status was established in 52% of patients on targeted therapy.
sections.Conclusion: Treatment with ETI increased the proportion of patients with serum retinol levels above the norm, which can lead to adverse reactions. The study was carried out as part of the research RCIC (122013100175-3).

We analyzed BMD at the femoral neck and lumbar spine and stratified pwCF by variant severity: severe/severe (two Class I-III, s/s, n=31), mild/severe (one RF/one Class I-III, m/s, n=37), and mild/mild (two RF, m/m, n=10). Results: 78...
presentations.authors_text: M. Heching1, A. Lobechov1, L. Slomiansky1, J. Weinberg1, O. Shtraichman1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Rabin Medical Center, Pulmonary Department, Petach Tikva, Israel

sections.Objectives: Low bone mineral density (BMD) is a common comorbidity in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF), as CFTR dysfunction impairs osteoblastic bone formation. While the "recessive rescue" phenomenon—where a single residual function (RF) variant preserves pancreatic function—is well established, its impact on skeletal health is unclear.
sections.Methods: We retrospectively evaluated adult pwCF at our clinic who completed DEXA scans in the era of highly effective modulator therapy (HEMT). We analyzed BMD at the femoral neck and lumbar spine and stratified pwCF by variant severity: severe/severe (two Class I-III, s/s, n=31), mild/severe (one RF/one Class I-III, m/s, n=37), and mild/mild (two RF, m/m, n=10).
sections.Results: 78 (of 87) adult pwCF completed DEXA scans (mean age 40.2, 45% male, mean FEV1 71%). Mean lumbar Z-score was -0.60; 58% met criteria for osteopenia and 6% for osteoporosis. Age correlated negatively with BMD, with no significant correlation found for gender or vitamin D. m/m pwCF exhibited superior BMD (mean +0.43). In contrast, m/s pwCF had the lowest BMD (mean -1.04, p<0.003 vs m/m) despite having similar age (44 vs 41) and pancreatic function (80% vs 78%) to the m/m group. While HEMT duration trended positively with BMD, it did not normalize outcomes for the m/s group. Notably, despite having preserved pancreatic function and higher BMI (24.8 vs 22.4), the m/s group had lower BMD than the pancreatic-insufficient s/s group (mean -0.84). In particular, pwCF homozygous for Class I (n=10) had greater BMD (mean -0.59) compared to the HEMT-treated m/s group (-1.04).
sections.Conclusion: We observed that the "recessive rescue" phenomenon does not apply to BMD. Carrying a single RF variant fails to protect against bone loss, unlike its protective effect on the pancreas. Clinicians must be vigilant with pwCF carrying a single RF variant, as preserved BMI and pancreatic sufficiency do not imply skeletal protection; these patients represent a vulnerable population for bone disease.

Alternative screening methods include continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) already used locally. The “Cystic Fibrosis–related Diabetes: A First Canadian Clinical Practice Guideline” (2025) recommends an HbA1c-first screening strategy, with OGTT reserved for individuals at higher CFRD risk. This service evaluation assessed CGM-diagnosed CFRD and corresponding HbA1c values to determine whether an HbA1c-first strategy could identify all patients requiring treatment. Metho...
presentations.authors_text: R. Milner1, J. Snowball1, H. Caterson1, M. Matheou2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Oxford University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Oxford Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Oxford, United Kingdom, 2Oxford University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Oxford Centre for Diabetes, Endocrinology and Metabolism, Oxford, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Regular screening for cystic fibrosis related diabetes (CFRD) is essential in cystic fibrosis (CF) care. The oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) is the gold standard but is logistically difficult to deliver due to resource constraints. Alternative screening methods include continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) already used locally. The “Cystic Fibrosis–related Diabetes: A First Canadian Clinical Practice Guideline” (2025) recommends an HbA1c-first screening strategy, with OGTT reserved for individuals at higher CFRD risk. This service evaluation assessed CGM-diagnosed CFRD and corresponding HbA1c values to determine whether an HbA1c-first strategy could identify all patients requiring treatment.
sections.Methods: Retrospective data were collected from electronic health records and CGM platforms between January 2024 and December 2025. We included adults without a prior diagnosis of CFRD, who had both a serum HbA1c measurement and a CGM assessment within a six-month interval. Variables collected included HbA1c, % time above target glucose range (3.9–7.8 mmol/L), frequency of glucose excursions >11 mmol/L, CFRD diagnosis based on CGM, and recommended care plans from the diabetes team.
sections.Results: Of 102 patients requiring CFRD screening, 53 had a complete CGM assessment. Based on CGM results, 15 patients were diagnosed with CFRD. Among patients with HbA1c <37 mmol/mol (35/42), five met CGM criteria for CFRD; none required pharmacological treatment and all received dietary advice. Among those with HbA1c ≥37 mmol/mol (18/20), ten met CGM criteria for CFRD; three required medical therapy (insulin or oral agents), while the remainder received dietary advice.
sections.Conclusion: In this cohort, HbA1c would have identified all patients with CFRD requiring medical treatment supporting the feasibility of anHbA1c-first screening strategy for lower-risk patients with targeted CGM use and reducing clinical burden. A patient and multidisciplinary team survey will inform service redesign.

Methods: Fecal samples were collected from 14 children with CF (aged 1.9–6.6 years; all with ≥1 F508del variation; 5 with pancreatic insufficiency) before and ≥6 months after initiation of ETI. The inflammatory markers analyzed were calprotectin...
presentations.authors_text: C. Sæland1, E. Bakkeheim2, K. Størdal3, K. Solum4, M. Hellan Iversen3, C. Sachs-Strømmen2,4, V. Hovland2,4, A. Wathne2, W. Gjerde4, T. Nyenget5, R.A. Thomassen1, A.C. Brun1, M. Kolsgaard2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Oslo University Hospital, Department of Paediatric Medicine, Oslo, Norway, 2Oslo University Hospital, Norwegian Centre for Rare Diseases, unit for pulmonology and cystic fibrosis, Oslo, Norway, 3Oslo University Hospital, Department of Pediatric Research, Oslo, Norway, 4Oslo University Hospital, Children's Department of Allergy and Pulmonary Diseases, Oslo, Norway, 5Oslo University Hospital, Department of Paediatric Medicine, clinical trial unit, Oslo, Norway

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is associated with chronic inflammation affecting both the respiratory and gastrointestinal (GI) tract. CFTR modulators may alter intestinal inflammation, though evidence in young children remains scarce. In this study, we examined changes in three complementary fecal inflammatory markers before and during elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) therapy to explore whether this treatment is associated with reduced intestinal inflammation.
sections.Methods: Fecal samples were collected from 14 children with CF (aged 1.9–6.6 years; all with ≥1 F508del variation; 5 with pancreatic insufficiency) before and ≥6 months after initiation of ETI. The inflammatory markers analyzed were calprotectin (CAL), neopterin (NPT), and myeloperoxidase (MPO), which are markers of neutrophil-driven inflammation, macrophage activation via TNF-γ, and oxidative stress/vasculitis, respectively. Two children were excluded from the calprotectin analyses because of a change in analytical instrument between visit 1 and 2, leaving 12 children for this analysis. We analyzed change from baseline using a paired t-test.
sections.Results: There was a significant reduction in mean (Std. Deviation) NPT from 1355 (777) to 936 (669) nmol/L (p = 0.018). Mean CAL decreased from 240 (429) to 53 (68) mg/kg, and mean MPO from 860 (867) to 408 (605) ng/mL, showing a clear downward trend, even though not statistically significant.
sections.Conclusion: Fecal inflammatory marker levels decreased during ETI therapy, but a statistically significant reduction was only observed for NPT, indicating reduction in macrophage activity. These findings indicate that ETI may reduce intestinal inflammation in young children with CF. Larger studies stratified by pancreatic status are warranted to confirm these results and to further explore the relationship between reduced intestinal inflammation and clinically relevant outcomes, such as GI related quality of life.

presentations.authors_text: E. Molloy1, O. Holmes1, V. Lynch1, J. Rendall1, A. Nugent1

sections.Objectives: NICE guideline NG17 (2022) recommends that structured education be an integral part of diabetes care. Further research is needed to identify the requirements of structured educational programmes for people with CFD according to CF Trust guidelines (2022). We have created and implemented a structured CFD education programme in the adult CF unit in NI.
sections.Methods: Analysis of electronic care record (ECR) to illustrate most recent HbA1C levels prior to the CFD education sessions. These sessions were presented via Microsoft Teams in a group or on a one-to-one basis as per individual preference, with interaction encouraged. The HbA1C result post education sessions was analysed to help identify changes in glycaemic control.
sections.Results: A total of 50 (53%) adults with CFD participated in the CFD education sessions between December 2020 and December 2025. Twenty-five (50%) people had an improvement in HbA1C post education compared with before education. Three (6%) people maintained their HbA1C level and 17 (34%) showed a deterioration in HbA1C. Post education HbA1C results are pending for 5 (10%) participants. Of those with a deteriorating HbA1C, one (2%) person was non-adherent to treatments, including insulin and 13 (26%) people maintained their HbA1C within target range (NICE NG28 2022).
sections.Conclusion: CFD structured education sessions have identified positive effect on glycaemic control based on HbA1C readings pre and post education. This has supported the roll out of education sessions to the wider adult CFD community in NI. It is important to highlight that Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Conductance Regulator (CFTR) modulators were established before pre education HbA1C was recorded.

We aimed to compare changes in CMR factors over 3 yrs before and after ETI initiation and identify predictors of post-ETI changes. Methods: Ongoing systematic bioclinical data collection within the Montreal Cystic Fibrosis Cohort has allowed access to both pre- and post-ETI data in adult pwCF. In our analyses, we included pwCF w...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Montreal Clinical Research Institute, Montreal, Canada, 2Centre Hospitalier de l'Université de Montréal, Montreal, Canada, 3Université Paris Cité, Paris, France, 4Centre de Recherche CIUSSS Nord-de-l'île-de-Montréal, Montreal, Canada, 5Université de Montréal, Montreal, Canada

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has demonstrated significant improvements in respiratory function and weight gain among people living with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). Several studies have also shown changes in traditional cardiometabolic risk (CMR) factors. However, these were conducted over short time periods and often without longitudinal pre-ETI clinical measurements. We aimed to compare changes in CMR factors over 3 yrs before and after ETI initiation and identify predictors of post-ETI changes.
sections.Methods: Ongoing systematic bioclinical data collection within the Montreal Cystic Fibrosis Cohort has allowed access to both pre- and post-ETI data in adult pwCF. In our analyses, we included pwCF who initiated ETI prior to 2023 and with pre and post-ETI data available. Mixed effects models were conducted, with both months before and after ETI initiation, sex and age as fixed effects and subject as random effect. Interaction terms were subsequently added.
sections.Results: Sixty participants (median age 36 yrs; 52% female) were included. Glycated haemoglobin (hbA1c) increased pre-ETI (p=0.03) and decreased significantly post-ETI (p<0.001). Inclusion of interaction terms revealed significant interactions between post-ETI hbA1c slope and body mass index <25 kg/m2 (based on first available data during study period) (p=0.04) and between post-ETI slope and hbA1c <5.5% (based on first available data) (p<0.001). Similarly, fructosamine increased pre-ETI (p<0.001) and decreased post-ETI (p<0.001). There was a significant interaction between post-ETI fructosamine slope and pancreatic insufficiency (p=0.03). Systolic blood pressure (BP) and diastolic BP increased pre-ETI (p=0.02 and p<0.01, respectively), but remained stable post-ETI. With interaction terms included, there was a significant interaction between F508del heterozygous and systolic BP slope post-ETI (p=0.04).
sections.Conclusion: Pre-treatment clinical variables likely have an impact on CMR factors post-ETI initiation.

However, natural fluctuation of faecal elastase (FE) concentrations has also been observed in people with CF (pwCF), even without targeted treatment. We present a single-centre analysis evaluating longitudinal FE measurements in patients receiving CFTR modulators. presentations.affiliations_text: 1Medical University of Innsbruck, Department of Child and Adolescent Health, Paediatrics III, Innsbruck, Austria, 2Tirol Kliniken, Department of Child and Adolescent Health, Paediatrics III, Innsbruck, Austria, 3Cystic Fibrosis Centre Innsbruck, Medical Research Affiliate, Innsbruck, Austria

sections.Objectives: Pancreatic insufficiency (PI) is a characteristic of cystic fibrosis (CF) and traditionally considered irreversible once established. Cystic fibrosis transmembrane regulator (CFTR) modulator therapies have prompted reconsideration of this assumption, as emerging reports suggest potential improvement in exocrine pancreatic function. However, natural fluctuation of faecal elastase (FE) concentrations has also been observed in people with CF (pwCF), even without targeted treatment. We present a single-centre analysis evaluating longitudinal FE measurements in patients receiving CFTR modulators.
sections.Methods: We retrospectively analysed 2203 FE values in pwCF 5 years before and during CFTR modulator initiation. FE results were categorised as <100 µg/g, 100–200 µg/g, and >200 µg/g to define pancreatic status. pwCF were classified as sustained pancreatic insufficient (PI), pancreatic sufficient (PS), or undulating if at least one FE value was <200µg/g and one >16µg/g (lower level of detection in local lab).
sections.Results: Among 132 pwCF, 14 (10.6%) had undulating FE values during CFTR modulator therapy, while 8 (6.1%) remained PS and 110 (83.3%) persistently PI. In the undulating group, 8 pwCF (57.1%) became PS, enabling enzyme dose reduction or discontinuation in most cases. Median age in this subgroup was 5.2 years (range 0.2-67.4 years). Two pwCF received ivacaftor and 12 elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI); 5 had prior lumacaftor/ivacaftor exposure before commencing ETI.
sections.Conclusion: Our findings demonstrate that while most pwCF maintain persistent PI under CFTR modulator therapy, a subset of predominantly younger children may exhibit fluctuation or recovery in pancreatic exocrine function, with a trend to becoming PS. These findings suggest that, beyond natural variability of pancreatic status, improvement can occur under modulator therapy. Regular FE monitoring is recommended, particularly in children, or in adults with unstable FE prior to CFTR modulator initiation.

sections.Objectives: Highly effective modulator therapy (HEMT) has significantly improved pulmonary outcomes in pediatric patients with cystic fibrosis (PwCF), but its effect on gastrointestinal complications remains unclear. This study aims to evaluate changes in emergency department (ED) visits and hospitalizations for constipation and intestinal obstruction before and after the introduction of HEMT.
sections.Methods: We conducted a retrospective cohort study using the Pediatric Health Information System (PHIS®) database, including PwCF aged 2–18 years during the pre-HEMT (2014–2018) and post-HEMT (2020–2024) eras. ED visits and hospitalizations with primary or secondary diagnoses of constipation or intestinal obstruction were analyzed. Statistical comparisons were performed using Mann–Whitney Wilcoxon, chi-square, and Fisher’s exact tests, with significance defined as p < 0.05.
sections.Results: A total of 483 ED visits and 4,165 hospitalizations were identified. Significant demographic differences were observed between cohorts. ED visits for constipation declined in the post-HEMT era (295 pre vs. 188 post; p = 0.012), as did hospitalizations (2,631 vs. 1,534; p = 0.012). Encounters with constipation as the primary diagnosis also decreased significantly in both ED visits (252 vs. 152; p = 0.012) and hospitalizations (297 vs. 159; p = 0.012). ED visits and hospitalizations for intestinal obstruction declined post-HEMT, with hospitalizations reaching statistical significance (131 vs. 42; p = 0.047).
sections.Conclusion: HEMT is associated with significant reductions in ED visits and hospitalizations for constipation and intestinal obstruction among pediatric PwCF, suggesting beneficial gastrointestinal effects beyond pulmonary outcomes but may also warrant further investigation into evolving management patterns.

sections.Background: Liver disease is a relevant complication of cystic fibrosis (CF); however, the association between CFTR genotype and liver involvement remains unclear. This study evaluated the frequency of liver involvement in CF patients and its relationship with genotype using both classical and functional protein-based classifications.
sections.Methods: A total of 457 patients with genetically confirmed CF were included. Liver involvement was classified as cystic fibrosis-related liver disease (CFLD) or non-cirrhotic hepatobiliary involvement. Genotype–phenotype associations were first analysed according to classical CFTR variant classes. Variants were then reclassified based on functional impact as protein-truncating variants (PTV) or non-PTV, and patients were grouped as PTV/PTV, PTV/non-PTV or non-PTV/non-PTV. Clinical and demographic variables were analysed statistically.
sections.Results: Liver involvement was present in 12.9% (n=59) of patients, most commonly non-cirrhotic disease (10.1%). No association was observed with classical CFTR variant classes (p>0.05). Functional genotype analysis (n=455) showed liver involvement was significantly more frequent in the PTV/PTV group compared with the PTV/non-PTV and non-PTV/non-PTV groups (p=0.0038). Patients with liver involvement were diagnosed at a younger age, were more often male and had lower height Z-scores.
sections.Conclusion: While classical CFTR classification was not associated with liver involvement, functional genotype analysis demonstrated a strong association between the PTV/PTV genotype and hepatobiliary disease. Protein-level classification may better identify CF patients at risk and support closer clinical monitoring. Table 1. sections.Background: Neonatal jaundice is common and usually self-limiting; however, persistence may indicate an underlying disorder, including cystic fibrosis, whose role in prolonged jaundice remains unclear.
sections.Objectives: To evaluate the frequency of neonatal and prolonged jaundice in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) and to assess whether prolonged jaundice should be considered a typical early manifestation of the disease.
sections.Methods: We retrospectively compared 298 individuals with cystic fibrosis and 251 healthy children with regard to neonatal jaundice. Prolonged jaundice was defined as lasting more than 14 days in term infants and more than 21 days in preterm infants. CF-related clinical data were obtained from medical records and analysed using appropriate statistical methods.
sections.Results: Infants with cystic fibrosis demonstrated lower rates of neonatal jaundice than healthy controls (Table 1). Neonatal jaundice was significantly less frequent in pwCF compared with controls (30.5% vs 55.0%, p<0.05). Prolonged jaundice occurred in 7.0% of pwCF and 19.1% of controls (p<0.05), while jaundice persisting beyond one month was also less common in pwCF (3.4% vs 14.3%, p<0.05). The median duration of jaundice was shorter in pwCF (0 days [IQR 4]) than in controls (4 days [IQR 10], p<0.05). Phototherapy rates were comparable between groups. No association was observed between CFTR genotype and jaundice-related outcomes, whereas pancreatic insufficiency showed a clear genotype association. PwCF N=298Healthy group N= 251pGender; male 51.7 %, n=154 53.8 %, n=135 0.62Age (years) - Median; IQR 12.0; 11.5 5.6; 7.0 <0.05Neonatal jaundice %,n30.5 %, n=9155.0%, n=138<0.05Prolonged jaundice (>14 days) %,n 7%, n=21 19.1 %, n=48 <0.05Jaundice lasting more than a month 3.4%, n= 10 14.3%, n=36 <0.05Duration of jaundice (days) Median; IQR 0;4 4;10 <0.05Receiving phototherapy treatment 9.7%, n=29 13.5%, n=34 0.16
sections.Conclusion: In this cohort, neonatal and prolonged jaundice were less frequent and resolved earlier in pwCF than in controls, indicating that prolonged jaundice is not a typical early manifestation of CF. However, when present, prolonged or cholestatic jaundice remains clinically relevant and requires careful evaluation for early hepatobiliary disease.

Pancreatic status, CFTR modulator use, PERT requirements (lipase units/kg/day), and faecal elastase (FE) were assessed. Twelve patients were receiving CFTR modulators (9 elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor [ETI], 3 lumacaftor/ivacaftor [Orkambi]); three were not on modulators. Results: At diagnosis, 11/15 patients were pa...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust, Royal Manchester Childrens Hospital (Wythenshawe), Paediatrics, Manchester, United Kingdom

sections.Background: CFTR modulator therapy has been associated with improvements in pancreatic function in some children with cystic fibrosis (CF). This study evaluates changes in pancreatic status and pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) requirements following initiation of CFTR modulators.
sections.Methods: A retrospective review was conducted of 15 children with CF aged 0–16 years (10 male, 4 female). Pancreatic status, CFTR modulator use, PERT requirements (lipase units/kg/day), and faecal elastase (FE) were assessed. Twelve patients were receiving CFTR modulators (9 elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor [ETI], 3 lumacaftor/ivacaftor [Orkambi]); three were not on modulators.
sections.Results: At diagnosis, 11/15 patients were pancreatic insufficient (PI) and 4 were pancreatic sufficient (PS). Of the 12 patients on CFTR modulators, 11 were PI at diagnosis. Among those treated with ETI (n=9), one was PS at baseline; six remained PI; and two converted from PI to PS and discontinued PERT. Overall, 2/11 (18%) PI patients became PS, both in the younger age group. Among six patients assessed for changes in PERT dosing, five demonstrated a 20–48% reduction in lipase requirement per kg body weight per day, while one patient had a 44% increase. Repeat FE measurements in five of six patients continued to show severe pancreatic insufficiency (<5, <28, <15, <15, <8 µg/g), with no significant increase observed.
sections.Conclusion: Pancreatic sufficiency developed in 18% of pancreatic-insufficient children treated with CFTR modulators, consistent with published data (~28%), particularly in younger patients. Although faecal elastase remained low, a clinically significant reduction in PERT requirements was observed in most patients, suggesting improved pancreatic function not fully captured by FE measurements.

presentations.summary: Objective: Despite recommendation in national guidance, there is no structured education for people with cystic fibrosis related diabetes (CFRD). A patient discussion group was undertaken to explore what additional education formats and content are valued alongside existing CF-specific one-to-one (1:1) education clinic to inform development of supplementary. Method: Participants were recruited in CF clinics. Virtual focus group was facilitated by a CF Diabetes Nurse and CF Dietitian exploring five domains: format, content, peer support, family involvement and accessibility. Transcripts were analysed thematically using Copilot-assisted analysis with manual sense-checking. Results: Seven participants (25–52 year...
presentations.authors_text: H. Winterbottom1, A. Brennan1, L. Kinsey1, F. Lewis1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust, Manchester Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Manchester, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Despite recommendation in national guidance, there is no structured education for people with cystic fibrosis related diabetes (CFRD). A patient discussion group was undertaken to explore what additional education formats and content are valued alongside existing CF-specific one-to-one (1:1) education clinic to inform development of supplementary.
sections.Methods: Participants were recruited in CF clinics. Virtual focus group was facilitated by a CF Diabetes Nurse and CF Dietitian exploring five domains: format, content, peer support, family involvement and accessibility. Transcripts were analysed thematically using Copilot-assisted analysis with manual sense-checking.
sections.Results: Seven participants (25–52 years; 57% female), with a mean CFRD duration of 10 years (range 1–23). Six used insulin; one managed diabetes with diet and lifestyle. All were on elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor. Participants valued continued access to CF-specific one-to-one education, particularly for those with long-standing or complex CFRD. Short (5–10 minute), practical, on-demand videos were favoured to address specific issues or revisit information but were viewed as a supplement rather than a replacement for personalised care. Clear visuals, concise summaries, optional subtitles and practical tools were preferred; quizzes were unpopular. Topics of interest included long-term complications, future treatments, carbohydrate awareness, exercise, technology, stress management. Participants strongly emphasised the need for CF-specific content. Peer support was considered optional. Simple family resources were welcomed. A single platform for accessibility was considered essential.
sections.Conclusion: This small, self-selecting group with experience of personalised CFRD care, valued ongoing CF-specific one-to-one education. Supplementary video-based resources were viewed as useful adjuncts to clinical support, rather than alternatives. These findings will inform co-design of our CFRD education resources.

presentations.summary: Background: People with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) are at increased risk of colorectal cancer (CRC) with risk increasing following lung transplantation (LT). The Cystic Fibrosis Foundation recommends colonoscopy-based CRC screening with intensive bowel preparation. Altered gastrointestinal physiology and treatment-related factors may reduce bowel preparation quality after LT. Data on bowel preparation effectiveness in post-LT pwCF are limited. Methods: We evaluated bowel preparation quality in our CRC screening protocol for post–LT pwCF using the Boston Bowel Preparation Scale (BBPS) (scores ≥6 adequate) plus subjective assessment by the endoscopist. Extended CF-specific bowel preparation regimen comprised a low-r...
presentations.authors_text: A. Brennan1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Manchester University Foundation Trust, Manchester Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Manchester, United Kingdom

sections.Background: People with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) are at increased risk of colorectal cancer (CRC) with risk increasing following lung transplantation (LT). The Cystic Fibrosis Foundation recommends colonoscopy-based CRC screening with intensive bowel preparation. Altered gastrointestinal physiology and treatment-related factors may reduce bowel preparation quality after LT. Data on bowel preparation effectiveness in post-LT pwCF are limited.
sections.Methods: We evaluated bowel preparation quality in our CRC screening protocol for post–LT pwCF using the Boston Bowel Preparation Scale (BBPS) (scores ≥6 adequate) plus subjective assessment by the endoscopist. Extended CF-specific bowel preparation regimen comprised a low-residue diet for 3 days, followed by polyethylene glycol 2 sachets BD for 3 days (6 washes), and senna 15 mg the night before colonoscopy. Preparation adequacy and need for repeat colonoscopy due to poor preparation were assessed.
sections.Results: 18 colonoscopies were performed in 15 post–lung transplant pwCF. A repeat colonoscopy in pwCF with an initial BBPS 1 was excluded from analysis as a further intensified regime was prescribed and results not comparable. BBPS was documented 14 of 17 included studies. Mean BBPS was 6.2 (SD 2.0). In one procedure preparation was described as poor where BBPS was not recorded. Overall, 70.6% achieved adequate preparation. Repeat colonoscopy due to inadequate preparation was required in 2 patients (11%).
sections.Conclusion: In the general population inadequate bowel preparation is reported in approximately 20-25% colonoscopies. In this cohort of post-LT pwCF inadequate preparation occurred in approximately 30% of procedures despite use of an extended CF-specific bowel preparation regime. Nevertheless, acceptable colonoscopy quality was achieved with extended bowel preparation in the majority of cases which highlights the importance of tailored bowel preparation strategies to prevent missed polyps and repeat procedures in this high-risk group.

However limited data describe the prevalence, multiplicity, and histological characteristics of colonic polyps in post-LT pwCF. Outcomes included polyp detection rate, number of polyps per patient, and histological features. Findings were interpreted in the context of published CF screening cohorts. Results: Nin...
