presentations.affiliations_text: 1Manchester University Foundation Trust, Manchester Adult Cystic Fibrosis Center, Manchester, United Kingdom

sections.Background: In people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) post lung transplant (LT) the risk of colorectal neoplasia is 25-30 times the risk of the general population, likely due to immunosuppression and accelerated adenoma formation. However limited data describe the prevalence, multiplicity, and histological characteristics of colonic polyps in post-LT pwCF.
sections.Methods: We retrospectively reviewed colonoscopy findings in post-LT pwCF managed at a large adult CF center. Indications included CRC surveillance and symptom-driven assessment. Outcomes included polyp detection rate, number of polyps per patient, and histological features. Findings were interpreted in the context of published CF screening cohorts.
sections.Results: Nineteen post-LT pwCF were identified who were eligible for CRC; 15 (79%) underwent colonoscopy, 2 declined investigation, 2 were deemed unsuitable due to co-morbidities. A total of 18 procedures were performed including 2 repeat surveillance examinations. Eleven colonoscopies were performed for CRC screening and 7 for symptomatic indications. Median age at colonoscopy was 37 years (range 27–55). Twelve polyps were detected in 6/15 patients (40%). Three patients had a single polyp and 3 had multiple polyps. 5 polyps were right-sided, 3 transverse colon and 4 left-sided. Tubular adenoma was the most common histological finding among resected polyps (8/10 cases). Median age of first adenoma detection was 35.5 years (range 30-46 years). No high-grade dysplasia or malignancy was detected.
sections.Conclusion: Forty percent of post-LT pwCF undergoing colonoscopy had colonic polyps detected, often adenomatous and occasionally multiple. The adenoma detection rate was 33%. This is comparable to data from CF Foundation where 41.1% screened post-LT pwCF had adenomatous polyps detected. These results confirm a potential neoplastic burden several years after transplantation and support early, intensive, and ongoing colonoscopic surveillance in lung transplant recipients with CF.

presentations.title: HbA1c in the diagnosis of CFRD: potential for undetected cases
presentations.summary: Objective: With increasing life-expectancy, prompt diagnosis of CFRD is important to prevent long duration of untreated hyperglycaemia which can lead to microvascular and macrovascular disease. UK guidelines recommend OGTT as gold standard with CGM as a supported screening tool. Canadian guidelines use a two-step approach from age 10 with HbA1c as the initial screen and OGTT reserved for HbA1c 37–48 mmol/mol. We assessed whether this strategy could delay CFRD diagnosis. Method: We retrospectively reviewed adults diagnosed with CFRD over 4 years using OGTT (0,1,2h) plus CGM analysed with Brompton criteria. HbA1c at diagnosis was recorded to determine eligibility under Canadian guidelines. Outcomes included init...
presentations.authors_text: F. Lewis1, P. Barry1, S. Punt1, A. Brennan1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Manchester NHS Foundation Trust, Manchester Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Manchester, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: With increasing life-expectancy, prompt diagnosis of CFRD is important to prevent long duration of untreated hyperglycaemia which can lead to microvascular and macrovascular disease. UK guidelines recommend OGTT as gold standard with CGM as a supported screening tool. Canadian guidelines use a two-step approach from age 10 with HbA1c as the initial screen and OGTT reserved for HbA1c 37–48 mmol/mol. We assessed whether this strategy could delay CFRD diagnosis.
sections.Methods: We retrospectively reviewed adults diagnosed with CFRD over 4 years using OGTT (0,1,2h) plus CGM analysed with Brompton criteria. HbA1c at diagnosis was recorded to determine eligibility under Canadian guidelines. Outcomes included initiation of glucose-lowering therapy and evidence of microvascular complications.
sections.Results: 36 individuals were diagnosed with CFRD using OGTT plus CGM. 55.6% had HbA1c <37 mmol/mol and 44.4% ≥37 mmol/mol at diagnosis, meaning 55.6% would not have progressed to OGTT under Canadian guidance. None had diagnostic OGTT results at the time of testing. Patients with HbA1c ≥37 mmol/mol were more likely to commence medication (70% vs 15%; OR 13.2, p=0.005). Microvascular complications were uncommon. HbA1c < 37 mmol/mol HbA1c ≥ 37 mmol/mol Diagnosed with CFRD using OGTT & CGM 2016Commenced medication 37Microalbuminuria 21Background Retinopathy (R1) 12
sections.Conclusion: Reliance on HbA1c as the initial screening test would have delayed CFRD diagnosis in over half of these individuals. Our findings highlight discordance between HbA1c and CGM metrics in CFRD, where HbA1c frequently underestimates glycaemic exposure. These data support the use of CGM for early detection and clinical decision-making. They also raise concerns about the sensitivity of HbA1c-based screening and highlight the value of broader CGM use to identify dysglycaemia and prevent treatment delays. Although microvascular complications were uncommon in both groups, reliance on the Canadian approach may delay identification of early microvascular changes. Earlier and tighter glycaemic control substantially lowers microvascular risk.

sections.Conclusion: Transition to HCL systems was associated with a significant increase in bolus insulin delivery, suggesting enhanced engagement with treatment. Those with optimal pre-HCL control maintained good post-HCL glycaemic outcomes. Further research with larger cohorts and longer follow-up is warranted to assess the sustained clinical and treatment burden benefits of HCL use in CFRD.

presentations.title: PwCF satisfaction of Hybrid Closed Loop insulin delivery system for CFRD management
presentations.summary: Introduction: Cystic Fibrosis Related Diabetes (CFRD) is a common complication of CF, with treatment options including oral medication, insulin injections and Continuous Subcutaneous Insulin Infusion (CSII). Hybrid Closed Loop (HCL) CSII has recently become available in the UK, with evidence of improved glycaemic control and quality of life. At our large adult CF centre (n=420), 22 people with CF (pwCF) [13 male, mean age 37 years)] are using HCL; we evaluated their experience of transitioning from standard CSII to HCL. Method: An online anonymous survey was sent to 22 pwCF using HCL (13 male, mean age 36.7 years), with a 73% (n=16) response rate. The survey assessed treatment burden, glucose control and satis...
presentations.authors_text: V. Jackson1, R. White1, A. Ochota1, D. Nazareth1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Liverpool Heart and Chest Hospital, Liverpool, United Kingdom

sections.Methods: An online anonymous survey was sent to 22 pwCF using HCL (13 male, mean age 36.7 years), with a 73% (n=16) response rate. The survey assessed treatment burden, glucose control and satisfaction, and included free‑text questions about the transition to HCL.
sections.Results: All respondents agreed or strongly agreed that they were happy with glucose control on HCL. 75% agreed or strongly agreed that they spent less time managing CFRD and were less worried about their control on HCL. 94% would recommend HCL to others. Respondents found the transition easy, largely because they were already experienced with CSII, and valued support from the CFRD multidisciplinary team.
sections.Conclusion: Overall, reported outcomes show a strongly positive experience of transitioning from standard CSII to HCL in this cohort. We are working with our people with CF to increase uptake of HCL therapy.

presentations.title: Is there a role for C-peptide in the management of cystic fibrosis diabetes?
presentations.summary: Objectives: Measurement of C-peptide can be used to assess endogenous insulin secretion and extent of residual beta-cell secretion in diabetes. It has been used to identify diabetes type and guide suitable treatment options with non-CF populations, reducing unnecessary insulin therapy and episodes of hypoglycaemia. Little is known about its use in cystic fibrosis diabetes (CFD). We investigated C-peptide levels in our adult CF cohort and its association with treatment management of CFD. Methods: Ninety-six pwCF (mean age 35.2years [SD 11.8], 42% female) had a non-fasting C-peptide test alongside their routine annual screen. Clinical thresholds for serum C-peptide were extrapolated from type-1 diabetes with C p...
presentations.authors_text: A. Ochota1, L. Rossiter2, J. Daniels3, V. Jackson3, R. White1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Liverpool Heart and Chest Hospital, Dietitian Department, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 2Liverpool Heart and Chest Hospital, Respiratory Medicine, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 3Liverpool Heart and Chest Hospital, Cystic Fibrosis Nursing, Liverpool, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Measurement of C-peptide can be used to assess endogenous insulin secretion and extent of residual beta-cell secretion in diabetes. It has been used to identify diabetes type and guide suitable treatment options with non-CF populations, reducing unnecessary insulin therapy and episodes of hypoglycaemia. Little is known about its use in cystic fibrosis diabetes (CFD). We investigated C-peptide levels in our adult CF cohort and its association with treatment management of CFD.
sections.Methods: Ninety-six pwCF (mean age 35.2years [SD 11.8], 42% female) had a non-fasting C-peptide test alongside their routine annual screen. Clinical thresholds for serum C-peptide were extrapolated from type-1 diabetes with C peptide <200 pmol/L indicating insulin deficiency, ≥600 pmol/L suggesting preserved endogenous insulin secretion and >900 pmol/L significant insulin resistance. 37 patients had their diabetic treatment reviewed, as part of routine clinical care, and C-peptide levels were subsequently compared for these treatment groups.
sections.Results: 9.3% patients had C-peptide <200 pmol/L, 41.2% ≥200-600 pmol/L, 17.5% ≥600 pmol/L with 32% >900 pmol/L, and insulin treatment was independently used in 100%, 55%, 36% and 25% patients respectively. C-peptide was lowest in insulin-treated patients (median 155.5 pmol/L), while Non-CFRD (851 pmol/L), Sitagliptin (911 pmol/L), and Sitagliptin + Insulin (667 pmol/L) groups had higher levels (H = 25.41, p< 0.0001). Patients with C-peptide <600 pmol/L were three times more likely to require insulin (OR 3.38, 95% CI 1.34–8.62, P=0.004–0.008) and had higher HbA1c than those with C-peptide >600 pmol/L (median 47 vs 41, P=0.042).
sections.Conclusion: C-peptide is a useful marker, alongside other glycaemic parameters, to identify pwCF at risk of CFD, particularly those likely to require insulin. However, further studies are needed to clarify its role in assessing insulin resistance and guiding individualized treatment strategies.

presentations.title: Long-term trajectories of bone health in the CFTR modulator era
presentations.summary: Objectives: CF related bone disease is a significant comorbidity amongst adult patients with CF. This may prove more problematic with increased life expectancy and additional effects of aging. Pulmonary and nutritional complications have shown substantial improvement with CFTR modulators but the effect on bone health remains unclear. We reviewed the progression of bone density, alongside clinical parameters, in adults with CF following introduction of Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) therapy. Methods: Retrospective longitudinal data was collated for consecutive patients undergoing routine dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scans at our large adult CF centre. Participants with DEXA at 3 time points (...
presentations.authors_text: A. Ochota1, F. Frost2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Liverpool Heart and Chest Hospital, Dietitian Department, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 2Liverpool Heart and Chest Hospital, Respiratory Medicine, Liverpool, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: CF related bone disease is a significant comorbidity amongst adult patients with CF. This may prove more problematic with increased life expectancy and additional effects of aging. Pulmonary and nutritional complications have shown substantial improvement with CFTR modulators but the effect on bone health remains unclear. We reviewed the progression of bone density, alongside clinical parameters, in adults with CF following introduction of Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) therapy.
sections.Methods: Retrospective longitudinal data was collated for consecutive patients undergoing routine dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scans at our large adult CF centre. Participants with DEXA at 3 time points (baseline, pre-ETI and ≥2 years post-ETI initiation) were included. Trajectories over time and multivariable logistic regression for factors associated with increased bone mineral density (BMD) loss were explored.
sections.Results: In total we included 252 scan from 58 pwCF (mean age 28.6 years [SD 9.1], 38% female. Pre-ETI decline in femoral BMD was observed (0.96 to 0.92 p=0.001) and this decline continued post ETI (p<0.05). Consequently, proportion of pwCF with osteoporosis increased post-ETI. Conversely, no significant change in spinal BMD or Z-scores were seen in the pre-ETI or post- ETI periods (p>0.05). Multivariable logistic regression identified chronic steroid use OR [95%CI] 96 [2-1949], p<0.001 and male sex OR 0.11 [0.005 to 0.72] as factors associated with differential BMD loss.
sections.Conclusion: Loss of femoral BMD in CF is not slowed or reversed by ETI use in the short term. Our data suggest classical risk factors for low BMD in CF (steroid use and male sex) persist in the ETI era. Taken together, these results suggest ongoing identification and monitoring of BMD is vital in an ageing CF population.

presentations.title: Integrating elastography into annual clinical assessments for adults with cystic fibrosis
presentations.summary: Objectives: To describe our experience of integrating Vibration Controlled Transient Elastography (VCTE) into the CF annual clinical assessment pathway. Background: CF-related liver disease (CFLD) remains a major cause of mortality in pwCF. However, progression of CFLD remains poorly understood, with investigations such as ultrasonography and liver function often being the mainstay of monitoring. The advent of non-invasive VCTE provides measures of steatosis (controlled Attenuation Parameter (CAP)), and liver stiffness, both of which have been validated in non-CF liver disease. Methods: Three respiratory physicians at the Leeds Adult CF Unit were formally trained and certified in undertaking VCTE using Fibrosc...
presentations.authors_text: H. Saumtally1,2, I. Clifton1,2, D. Shimmin1, A. Sawant1, G. Spoletini1, A. Smyth3,4, D. Peckham1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Leeds Teaching Hospitals Trust, Leeds Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Leeds, United Kingdom, 2University of Leeds, Leeds Institute of Medical Research, Leeds, United Kingdom, 3Queen’s University Belfast, School of Medicine, Dentistry and Biomedical Sciences,, Belfast, United Kingdom, 4NIHR Nottingham Biomedical Research Centre, Nottingham, United Kingdom

sections.Background: CF-related liver disease (CFLD) remains a major cause of mortality in pwCF. However, progression of CFLD remains poorly understood, with investigations such as ultrasonography and liver function often being the mainstay of monitoring. The advent of non-invasive VCTE provides measures of steatosis (controlled Attenuation Parameter (CAP)), and liver stiffness, both of which have been validated in non-CF liver disease.
sections.Objectives: To describe our experience of integrating Vibration Controlled Transient Elastography (VCTE) into the CF annual clinical assessment pathway.
sections.Methods: Three respiratory physicians at the Leeds Adult CF Unit were formally trained and certified in undertaking VCTE using Fibroscan®. Patients were fasted prior to annual assessment bloods and scan. A retrospective review of the first eight months of VCTE testing was undertaken. A cut-off of 288 dB/m and 7.0 kPa was used to indicate steatosis and significant fibrosis respectively.
sections.Results: Eighty-nine scans were undertaken (53 male, Median age 36.0 years, range of 18-75) with a median BMI of 24.0 [IQR 5.3] kg/m2. The median CAP score was 204 [IQR 68] dB/m and median liver stiffness was 4.6 [IQR 1.9] kPa. 10/89 (11.2%) and 9/89 (10.1%) were found to have steatosis and significant fibrosis respectively.
sections.Conclusion: VCTE is an operator-dependent, non-invasive scan which has a substantial potential in the monitoring of CFLD during routine CF outpatient appointments. Further analysis is ongoing to correlate the above findings with respect to more conventional biomarkers.

presentations.title: Assessment of uptake and barriers to bowel cancer screening in patients with cystic fibrosis
presentations.summary: Objectives: People with cystic fibrosis (CF) have an increased incidence of colorectal cancer (CRC). Screening colonoscopies are currently the gold-standard screening test. Screening is commonly offered from 40yr in the UK, local practice is to offer screening from 30yr. We audited how many eligible patients at our centre had been offered screening, the proportion of patients undergoing screening, and reasons for patients declining screening. Methods: Audit of clinical notes (2017-2025) of CF patients ≥30yr at a single UK adult CF centre. The number of patients offered screening, completing screening, and declining screening were recorded. A sample of patients who did not consent to referral were asked why. Re...
presentations.authors_text: J. Kane1, B. Hayee1, M. Waller1, K.-L. Watson1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1King's College Hospital NHS Trust, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: People with cystic fibrosis (CF) have an increased incidence of colorectal cancer (CRC). Screening colonoscopies are currently the gold-standard screening test. Screening is commonly offered from 40yr in the UK, local practice is to offer screening from 30yr. We audited how many eligible patients at our centre had been offered screening, the proportion of patients undergoing screening, and reasons for patients declining screening.
sections.Methods: Audit of clinical notes (2017-2025) of CF patients ≥30yr at a single UK adult CF centre. The number of patients offered screening, completing screening, and declining screening were recorded. A sample of patients who did not consent to referral were asked why.
sections.Results: From 163 eligible patients, 120 (73.6%) were offered screening: 77 (64.2%) consented, 31 (25.8%) deferred a decision to a later date, 9 (7.5%) declined referral, 3 (2.5%) patients declining repeat screening. For those asked about barriers to consenting, common themes were low levels of concern related to CRC screening or concerns about the procedure itself.
sections.Conclusion: CRC screening uptake was comparable to other national screening programmes. The audit highlighted that 26.4% were not offered screening: most had recently turned 30, others were new to the service or patients with limited engagement. To address this, a section on screening has been added to the review proforma, these patients should be offered colonoscopy at their next review. Additionally, opportunities with less engaged patients must be utilised. For those who deferred the decision to a later date, documentation on how these patients will be followed up is required as it was lacking. The barriers to consenting identified that education on the importance of screening and the procedure is vital. We will repeat this audit to reassess screening uptake following these interventions.

presentations.title: Central precocious puberty in cystic fibrosis: an uncommon finding raising questions in the CFTR modulator era
presentations.summary: Cystic fibrosis (CF) has been associated with delayed puberty, mainly related to malnutrition, respiratory disease severity, and metabolic comorbidities. Over recent decades, improvements in comprehensive care and the introduction of CFTR modulators have allowed pubertal progression in many patients to more closely resemble that of the general population. However, central precocious puberty remains an exceptionally rare finding in this population, with limited available evidence and a lack of studies specifically evaluating its association with CF or CFTR modulator therapy. Case report: 9 year old female patient with a neonatal diagnosis of CF (homozygous F508del), pancreatic insufficiency, and moderate respir...
presentations.authors_text: M.V. Arenal1, M.I. Nabais Robalo1, N. Biancofiore2, M.F. Bernatene1, C. Venialgo1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Hospital del Torax Antonio. A. Cetrangolo, Cystic Fibrosis, Vicente Lopez, Argentina, 2Sanatorio Anchorena San Martin, Endocrinology, San Martin, Argentina

sections.Conclusion: The existing literature primarily describes delayed puberty in patients with CF, and there are no systematic studies or case series addressing precocious puberty in this population or its potential association with CFTR modulator therapy. Although this case does not allow causal inference, it highlights a significant knowledge gap and underscores the need for prospective studies to evaluate the impact of nutritional status, pulmonary function, and emerging therapies on pubertal development in individuals with CF.

presentations.title: Modified, activated and natural clinoptilolite might influence adsorption capacity and anti-inflammatory effect on the intestinal mucosa in CF
presentations.summary: Objectives: TOXAPREVENT® MEDI contains the active ingredient MANC® (modified, activated, natural clinoptilolite), which selectively binds harmful substances such as histamine, ammonium, heavy metals and mycotoxins in the gastrointestinal tract and can remove them from the body – without depriving it of nutrients. The combination of adsorption capacity and anti-inflammatory effect on the intestinal mucosa could be particularly beneficial for cystic fibrosis patients. Therefore, we evaluated the use of MANC® in people with CF (pwCF) who received the standard therapy in terms of their gastrointestinal complaints and disease. Methods: PwCF who received standard of care therapy due to gastrointestinal symptoms, did...
presentations.authors_text: C. Schwarz1,2, L.M. Bechinger1,2, J.G. Mainz3,4
presentations.affiliations_text: 1HMU University Potsdam, Internal Medicine, Potsdam, Germany, 2Clinic Ernst von Bergmann, CF Center, Potsdam, Germany, 3Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Brandenburg Medical School (MHB) University, Klinikum Westbrandenburgvel, Germany, Brandenburg and der Havel, Germany, 4Faculty of Health Sciences, Joint Faculty of the Brandenburg University of Technology Cottbus-Senftenberg, The Brandenburg Medical School Theodor Fontane and the University of Potsdam, Brandenburg an der Havel, Germany

sections.Objectives: TOXAPREVENT® MEDI contains the active ingredient MANC® (modified, activated, natural clinoptilolite), which selectively binds harmful substances such as histamine, ammonium, heavy metals and mycotoxins in the gastrointestinal tract and can remove them from the body – without depriving it of nutrients. The combination of adsorption capacity and anti-inflammatory effect on the intestinal mucosa could be particularly beneficial for cystic fibrosis patients. Therefore, we evaluated the use of MANC® in people with CF (pwCF) who received the standard therapy in terms of their gastrointestinal complaints and disease.
sections.Methods: PwCF who received standard of care therapy due to gastrointestinal symptoms, did not respond sufficiently or at all to therapy and took MANC® orally on an individual basis. Patients with an age >18 years, with abdominal symptoms and MANC® therapy over 2 months and more have been included in this observational, retrospective study. Primary outcome parameter was the CFAbd-Score after 2 months of treatment with MANC®.
sections.Results: 20 pwCF could be evaluated between 1 January 2024 and 31 December 2025. The mean age was 41.9 years (Range 26-60 years). 10 pwCF were female. 10 pwCF were homozygous for F508del/F508del, 7 heterozygous for F508del, and 3 other mutations than F508del. 16/20 patients responded with a decrease in the CFAbd-Score and 4 did not show a decrease or even showed an increase in CFAbd-Score.
sections.Conclusion: Interestingly, the observational study showed that people with CF can benefit from modified, activated and natural clinoptilolite therapy after they have already taken the standard therapy for the underlying gastrointestinal complaints. Further prospective studies should be implemented to further understand the benefits of this oral treatment.

presentations.title: Longitudinal impact of CFTR modulator therapy on hepatobiliary biochemistry and liver stiffness in children with cystic fibrosis: prospectively collected single-centre observational data
presentations.summary: Background: Clinically significant hepatobiliary involvement (HBI) affects 15–30% of children with cystic fibrosis (cwCF). It is unclear whether CFTR modulator therapy alters the natural course of CF-related hepatobiliary disease (CFHBI), and long-term effects on hepatobiliary biomarkers, including liver stiffness, remain largely unknown. We aimed to evaluate the impact of 36-month CFTR modulator therapy on CFHBI in cwCF. Methods: In this single-centre prospective study, liver biomarkers including aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT), AST to platelet ratio index (APRI), GGT to platelet ratio (GPR), and liver stiffness measurement by 2D-shear-wave el...
presentations.authors_text: M. Imrei1,2, K. Ocskay1,3, A. F. Kéri1, I. Gönczi1, É. Gács1, M. Polovitzer1, D. Molnár1, A. Párniczky1,2,3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Heim Pál National Pediatric Institute, Budapest, Hungary, 2University of Pécs, Institute for Translational Medicine, Medical School, Pécs, Hungary, 3Semmelweis University, Pharmaceutical Sciences and Health Technologies Division, Medical School, Budapest, Hungary

sections.Background: Clinically significant hepatobiliary involvement (HBI) affects 15–30% of children with cystic fibrosis (cwCF). It is unclear whether CFTR modulator therapy alters the natural course of CF-related hepatobiliary disease (CFHBI), and long-term effects on hepatobiliary biomarkers, including liver stiffness, remain largely unknown. We aimed to evaluate the impact of 36-month CFTR modulator therapy on CFHBI in cwCF.
sections.Methods: In this single-centre prospective study, liver biomarkers including aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT), AST to platelet ratio index (APRI), GGT to platelet ratio (GPR), and liver stiffness measurement by 2D-shear-wave elastography (Philips EPIQ 5 ElastQ) were collected prior to and 36 months after initiating modulator therapy.
sections.Results: Forty-five children were included (mean age at modulator initiation 9.4 years [SD 4.7]; 59% male). Thirty-eight received lumacaftor/ivacaftor and six received elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor. At baseline, APRI and GPR were elevated and decreased significantly after 36 months (APRI: 0.29 to 0.18, p<0.001; GPR: 0.20 to 0.11, p<0.001). FIB-4 remained stable (0.21 to 0.22; p=0.973). Significant reductions were observed in transaminase levels: AST decreased from 37.9 to 26.9 U/L (p<0.001), ALT from 36.6 to 23.1 U/L (p<0.001), and GGT from 20.2 to 12.1 U/L (p=0.007). Only two children had elevated transaminases at baseline; none remained elevated at 36 months. LSM data were available for 22 children (mean age 10.4 years [SD 4.2]; 72% male; all on lumacaftor/ivacaftor). Liver stiffness did not change significantly (7.02 kPa to 7.07 kPa; p=0.824).
sections.Conclusion: CFTR modulator therapy over 36 months improved hepatobiliary biochemical indices and transaminases substantially, but did not significantly alter liver stiffness, suggesting potential benefits on CF-related hepatobiliary disease. (NKFIH 138929, CF Trust NU-00600)

presentations.title: Effect of CFTR modulators on glucose tolerance in children with cystic fibrosis: an age- and sex-matched cohort study
presentations.summary: Objectives: Cystic fibrosis–related abnormal glucose tolerance (CF-AGT) is a progressive, age-dependent complication, with ~50% of patients developing cystic fibrosis–related diabetes (CFRD) by their forties. Despite widespread CFTR modulator use, the impact on glucose tolerance remains uncertain. Most studies use self-controlled pre–post designs that fail to disentangle treatment effects from natural CF-AGT progression. We hypothesised modulators mitigate age-related CF-AGT worsening. We aimed to isolate modulator effects in a prospective pediatric registry-based matched control analysis. Methods: We analysed prospectively collected registry data comparing CFTR modulator-treated children with untreated contro...
presentations.authors_text: S. Kiss1, P. Mátrai2, O. Varannai1, M. Imrei1, A. Kéri1, Z. Vajda1, K. Ocskay1, A. Párniczky1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Heim Pál National Pediatric Institute, Budapest, Hungary, 2Institute of Bioanalysis, University of Pécs, Medical School, Pécs, Hungary

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis–related abnormal glucose tolerance (CF-AGT) is a progressive, age-dependent complication, with ~50% of patients developing cystic fibrosis–related diabetes (CFRD) by their forties. Despite widespread CFTR modulator use, the impact on glucose tolerance remains uncertain. Most studies use self-controlled pre–post designs that fail to disentangle treatment effects from natural CF-AGT progression. We hypothesised modulators mitigate age-related CF-AGT worsening. We aimed to isolate modulator effects in a prospective pediatric registry-based matched control analysis.
sections.Methods: We analysed prospectively collected registry data comparing CFTR modulator-treated children with untreated controls. Oral glucose tolerance tests (OGTT) were performed per study protocol in every child able to cooperate. 1:1 nearest-neighbour propensity score matching was based on age and sex (age calliper ≤1 year; exact sex match). Pair-stratified linear models compared outcomes. Analyses used the MatchIt R package v4.5.3.
sections.Results: 80 treated children (29 males) were matched, 27 on LUM/IVA and 53 on ETI. OGTT data were available for 60 pairs. BMI z-scores did not differ across groups (Table 1 and Table 2). Characteristicn (controls/treated)ControlsTreatedp-valueAge, mean (SD), years80/8010.7 (4.6)10.7 (4.7)0.957Haemoglobin A1c, mean (SD), (%)76/765.3 (0.4)5.5 (0.6)0.034​BMI Z-score, mean (SD)80/80–0.4 (1.0)–0.3 (1.2)0.544Fasting glucose, mean (SD), mmol/L60/604.9 (0.5)5.0 (0.5)0.11560-min OGTT glucose, mean (SD), mmol/L60/609.2 (2.4)10.1 (3.0)0.109120-min OGTT glucose, mean (SD), mmol/L60/606.2 (1.7)6.3 (1.8)0.788Fasting insulin, mean (SD), µU/mL50/506.6 (3.6)7.3 (5.9)0.45760-min insulin, mean (SD), µU/mL52/5256.9 (32.5)55.0 (35.6)0.772120-min insulin, mean (SD), µU/mL51/5143.2 (29.8)35.2 (26.6)0.158Fasting C-peptide, mean (SD), nmol/L62/621.5 (0.8)1.6 (1.2)0.906​​​​​​​​​​ NGT vs AGT proportion was 49 (82%)/11 (18%) in controls vs 14 (23%)/46 (77%) in treated (p=0.500), reflecting historical timing and modulator initiation criteria. No statistically significant differences were observed for fasting and 60- or 120-minute glucose, insulin or C-peptide levels. HbA1c values showed no clinically significant difference between groups.
sections.Conclusion: We did not observe any statistically significant effects of CFTR modulator therapy on glucose metabolism. Modulator-specific in-depth analyses are warranted.

presentations.title: Determinants of vitamin D status in children with cystic fibrosis: a GEE-based analysis
presentations.summary: Objectives: Vitamin D insufficiency is common in children with cystic fibrosis (CF). Despite routine supplementation, maintaining adequate serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D (25(OH)D) levels remains challenging. The aim of this study was to evaluate determinents of vitamin D status, and examine the relationship between vitamin D supplementation dose and vitamin D status. Methods: Repeated measurements from 275 children with CF are used to classify vitamin D status as sufficient (>30), insufficient (20–30), and deficient (<20). For determinants of this outcome we applied a Generalized estimating equations (GEE)-based ordinal (cumulative logit) model with an exchangeable working correlation structure in order to account...
presentations.authors_text: H. Avcı1, Z. Özdemir Uslu2, M. Yıldız Kayaoğlu3, U. Kavgacı3, N.B. Çelik Ertaş2, N. Emiralioğlu3, E. Güneş Yalçın3, U. Özçelik3, D. Doğru3, Z.A. Ozon2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Hacettepe University Faculty of Medicine, Biostatistics Department, Ankara, Turkey, 2Hacettepe İhsan Doğramacı Children Hospital, Pediatric Endocrinology Department, Ankara, Turkey, 3Hacettepe İhsan Doğramacı Children Hospital, Pediatric Pulmonology Department, Ankara, Turkey

sections.Objectives: Vitamin D insufficiency is common in children with cystic fibrosis (CF). Despite routine supplementation, maintaining adequate serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D (25(OH)D) levels remains challenging. The aim of this study was to evaluate determinents of vitamin D status, and examine the relationship between vitamin D supplementation dose and vitamin D status.
sections.Methods: Repeated measurements from 275 children with CF are used to classify vitamin D status as sufficient (>30), insufficient (20–30), and deficient (<20). For determinants of this outcome we applied a Generalized estimating equations (GEE)-based ordinal (cumulative logit) model with an exchangeable working correlation structure in order to account for within-subject correlation in repeated measurements. The associations of sex, pancreatic enzyme replacement (PERT), ursodeoxycholic acid use, and body mass index (BMI) SDS with vitamin D status were also assessed in the model.
sections.Results: Female sex was associated with a lower risk of insufficient/deficient vitamin D levels (B = −0.474, p<0.001; OR = 0.62, 95% CI: 0.47–0.82). Interestingly, patients who were not prescribed PERT was associated with a higher risk of insufficient/deficient vitamin D status (B=0.660, p<0.001; OR = 1.93, 95% CI: 1.32–2.83). Ursodeoxycholic acid prescription and BMI SDS were not associated with the risk of insufficient/deficient vitamin D status (B=0.134, p=0.455 and B=−0.020, p=0.237). An approximate daily vitamin D dose of 4500 IU/day would be required to increase the probability of achieving 25(OH)D >30 ng/mL above 50% in children with CF.
sections.Conclusion: Patients deemed clear of pancreatic insufficiency based on clinical and laboratory criteria, and who didn't use PERT, had a higher risk of insufficient or deficient vitamin D status, suggesting a mild subclinical impairment in fat absorption. A daily intake of 4,500 IU of vitamin D or more may be necessary for adequate vitamin D levels.

presentations.title: Empowering people with cystic fibrosis (CF) related diabetes: preliminary findings from the rollout of CF specific structured diabetes education sessions
presentations.summary: Objective: Structured diabetes education is recommended by the UK National Institute for Health and Care Excellence, as it supports effective self-management and improves outcomes such as HbA1c. A third of people with CF aged ≥16 years receive treatment for CF-related diabetes (CFRD) in the UK. To promote equitable access to structured education for people with CFRD (pwCFRD), we developed a session modelled on existing courses for Type 2 Diabetes. Method: CF dietitians designed a structured, interactive session covering CFRD aetiology, monitoring, complications, treatments, carbohydrate awareness, glycaemic index (GI) and food labels. Since late 2023, sessions have been delivered online in small groups or indi...
presentations.authors_text: C. Bradshaw1, C. Merrells1, A. Higton1, C. Orchard1, A. Ewence1, A. Lillis1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Frimley Health NHS Foundation Trust, Cystic Fibrosis Unit, Frimley, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Structured diabetes education is recommended by the UK National Institute for Health and Care Excellence, as it supports effective self-management and improves outcomes such as HbA1c. A third of people with CF aged ≥16 years receive treatment for CF-related diabetes (CFRD) in the UK. To promote equitable access to structured education for people with CFRD (pwCFRD), we developed a session modelled on existing courses for Type 2 Diabetes.
sections.Methods: CF dietitians designed a structured, interactive session covering CFRD aetiology, monitoring, complications, treatments, carbohydrate awareness, glycaemic index (GI) and food labels. Since late 2023, sessions have been delivered online in small groups or individually in person. Anonymous feedback was collected and HbA1c levels pre- and post-education were compared using the paired t-test.
sections.Results: Eighteen pwCFRD completed the session; 14 provided feedback. All attendees found the session helpful and reported a ‘good understanding’ of what causes high blood glucose levels afterwards, representing a 50% shift from limited prior knowledge. Post-education, all participants also reported a ‘good understanding’ of what to eat with CFRD, with 71% improving from ‘no idea’ or ‘some idea’. Regarding managing their diabetes going forwards, 57% were ‘extremely confident’ and 43% ‘somewhat confident’. Learning about carbohydrate types, GI and food-related glucose responses were the most frequently cited benefits. Participants valued the interactive format, and 50% expressed interest in sessions exploring the emotional and psychological impact of CFRD. The median HbA1c change pre- and post-education was −2 mmol/mol (mean change −2.21 mmol/mol; range −7 to 2 mmol/mol; p=0.013).
sections.Conclusion: Bespoke structured education for pwCFRD enhances self-management and is associated with improvements in HbA1c. These findings support the development of a national structured education programme to ensure equitable access for all pwCFRD.

presentations.title: Prevalence of hypercalciuria in the era of highly effective CFTR modulators
presentations.summary: The introduction of Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) has significantly improved the prognosis for People with Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF). However, increased survival has shifted clinical attention toward long-term complications, including metabolic and renal disorders. Among these, hypercalciuria, defined as elevated urinary calcium excretion in the absence of hypercalcemia, may predispose to nephrocalcinosis, kidney stones, and potential impairment of renal function. In pwCF not treated with ETI, the reported prevalence of hypercalciuria ranges from 30% to 60%. In the era of highly effective CFTR modulators, its prevalence and clinical relevance remain largely unknown. Aim: To evaluate the prevalence of hyp...

sections.Methods: In this cross-sectional study, we included pwCF who had been receiving ETI therapy for at least 6 months. The presence of hypercalciuria was defined based on sex and age-specific normative values of the urinary calcium-to-creatinine (UCa/Cr) ratio.
sections.Results: The study included 118 subjects with a median age of 16 years (range: 2–32). Nine patients exhibited Uca/Cr ratios indicative of hypercalciuria with an estimated prevalence of 7.6% (95% confidence interval: 3.5–14.0). Among these, four patients (44%) had radiographic evidence of urinary calculi; all of them were asymptomatic.
sections.Conclusion: The prevalence of hypercalciuria in pwCF treated with ETI is comparable to that of the general population. Nevertheless, given the aging CF population and its association with silent urinary calculi, routine monitoring of urinary calcium excretion should be considered to prevent long-term renal complications.

Aim: To investigate the prevalence of PBS among patients in the North Caucasus and establish its association with CFTR gene mutations. Methods: This study included 160 patients from various republics of the North Caucasus: Chechen – 83 patients, Karachay-Cherkess – 28, Dagestan – 22, Ingushetia – 13, North Ossetia-Alania – 9, Kabardino-Balkaria – 5. In Chechen, correlation a...

sections.Objectives: In some previous articles, it was shown that the main feature of cystic fibrosis in patients from the Chechen Republic is the predominance of PBS. Currently, there are no studies aimed at analyzing the prevalence of PBS in other regions of the North Caucasus and its association with CFTR gene mutations has not been established. Aim: To investigate the prevalence of PBS among patients in the North Caucasus and establish its association with CFTR gene mutations.
sections.Methods: This study included 160 patients from various republics of the North Caucasus: Chechen – 83 patients, Karachay-Cherkess – 28, Dagestan – 22, Ingushetia – 13, North Ossetia-Alania – 9, Kabardino-Balkaria – 5. In Chechen, correlation analysis was used with patients who had at least one allele of variant 1677delTA(1), E92K(2) and patients with other mutations of the CFTR gene(3), in Karachay-Cherkessia: patients with variant W1282X on at least one allele and patients with other mutations of the CFTR gene. In Dagestan: at least one allele has variant 1677delTA(1), R1066C(2), and patients with other CFTR gene mutations.
sections.Results: Electrolyte disorders were detected in 94 (58.7%) patients. A high frequency of PBS was found in three republics, significantly more often in Chechen – 66.7% (p<0.01), less often in Karachay-Cherkessia (57,1%) and Dagestan (55%). The occurrence of PBS is not associated with CFTR gene mutations in both the Chechen (p1,2= 0.96; p1,3 = 0.1), Karachay-Cherkessia (p= 0.56) and the Republic of Dagestan (p1,2 = 0.52; p1,3 = 0.31). It was found that in three regions with a high frequency of PBS, excessive wrapping of infants is observed.
sections.Conclusion: Children with cystic fibrosis from various regions of the North Caucasus have a high incidence of PBS (from 50 to 66.7%), mainly in the Chechen Republic. The occurrence of PBS does not depend on mutations of the CFTR gene and is probably related to the continental climate of the district and intraethnic characteristics.

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) is approved for people with CF (pwCF) ≥2 years with at least one F508del variant. This retrospective single-center study aimed to evaluate liver involvement in pwCF treated with ETI.
sections.Methods: PwCF treated with ETI at the CF Center of the Basilicata Region were included. Demographic data and laboratory parameters (gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), total bilirubin (TB), and platelet count) were collected at baseline and after 6, 12, and 24 months. Non-invasive biomarkers of CF–related liver disease (CFLD) were calculated, including the AST-to-platelet ratio index (APRI), the GGT-to-platelet ratio (GPR), and the Fibrosis-4 index (FIB-4). Steatosis (controlled attenuation parameter [CAP] ≥288 dB/m) and fibrosis (liver stiffness measurement [LSM] ≥7 kPa) were assessed in 26 of 73 patients at the last visit using FibroScan® (Echosens).
sections.Results: 73 patients (37 M; median age 25 yrs [IQR 15–36]) were included. Median AST, GGT, and TB showed slight, non-significant (NS) increases at 12 and 24 months. Median (IQR) ALT increased from 22 (17–27) U/L at baseline to 23 (18–24) U/L at 12 months and 24 (19–30) U/L at 24 months (p<0.05). The median (IQR) APRI score increased from 0.18 (0.139–0.24) to 0.219 (0.171–0.273) at 12 months (p<0.05); however, 98.63% of patients had an APRI <0.5 both at baseline and at 12 months, indicating absence of significant fibrosis. The median (IQR) GPR score decreased from 0.168 (0.123–0.227) at baseline to 0.152 (0.12–0.211) at 12 months (p<0.05). A NS increase in median FIB-4 was observed. Median (IQR) CAP and LSM values were 164 dB/m (164–223) and 3.83 kPa (2.93–3.98), respectively. One patient showed steatosis (CAP 335 dB/m) and fibrosis (LSM 11.8 kPa), consistent with clinical and laboratory findings.
sections.Conclusion: ETI demonstrated a favorable hepatic safety profile, with no meaningful changes in non-invasive biomarkers of CFLD.

Department of Paediatrics, Dublin, Ireland, 4Royal Brompton & Harefield Hospitals, Department of Paediatric Respiratory Medicine, London, United Kingdom, 5National Heart and Lung Institute, Imperial College London, London, United Kingdom, 6Children’s Health Ireland at Crumlin, Department of Paediatric Respiratory Medicine, Dublin, Ireland

sections.Objectives: The highly effective cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) therapy elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has transformed the management of people with Cystic Fibrosis (PwCF). However, its effects on bone mineral density (BMD) remain uncertain.
sections.Methods: We conducted a longitudinal cohort study assessing changes in BMD before and after ETI initiation in children and adults with CF across five Irish hospitals. Participants were identified from RECOVER, a multicentre observational study of real-world ETI outcomes. Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) scans were analysed both pre- and post-ETI initiation. Paired t-tests assessed BMD changes, with effect sizes calculated using Cohen’s dz. Secondary outcomes included biochemical markers of bone metabolism.
sections.Results: Paired DXA data were available for 22 adults (age range 18-49 years) and 12 children (age range 6-17 years). The median interval between pre- and post-ETI DXA scans was 58.4 months (range 15.7-111.4) in adults, and 77.3 months (range 21.3-137.7) in children. In adults, lumbar spine BMD showed a small increase after ETI initiation (mean change +0.13 Z-score; p=0.24; dz=0.27), while hip BMD showed a non-significant decrease (−0.32 Z-score; p=0.09; dz=0.84). In children, no significant changes were observed at the lumbar spine (−0.05 Z-score; p=0.84; dz=0.08) or total body less hip (TBLH) (−0.15 Z-score; p=0.74; dz=0.05). Baseline lumbar z-scores were predominantly within normal range with no patients demonstrating values ≤ -2.0 at ETI initiation. No significant biochemical changes were identified.
sections.Conclusion: No statistically significant changes were observed over a median follow-up of approximately 3 years after ETI and BMD. The absence of severe baseline bone deficit may partly explain the lack of significant BMD change observed following ETI initiation in children. Longer-term studies are needed to determine whether sustained ETI exposure influences bone health in PwCF.

sections.Objectives: Chronic intestinal inflammation is a hallmark of CF and is associated with a markedly elevated burden of abdominal symptoms (AS) in people with CF (pwCF). Consequently, comparing AS in pwCF, people with inflammatory bowel disease (pwIBD) and healthy controls (HC) may provide new insights and contribute to a better understanding of GI involvement in pwCF. COMET-CF-IBD (Comprehensive Omic and SyMptom Evaluation, a Translational study) aims to improve understanding of chronic gastrointestinal inflammation and its clinical impact in pwCF.
sections.Methods: Using the CFAbd-Score, the first CF-specific GI-PROM developed and validated following FDA-guidelines, we assessed and compared AS in children and adults with CF, IBD and HC. Higher scores in CFAbd-Score represent an increased burden of symptoms. The analysis included retrospective data of a subgroup of pwCF before commencing a new therapy with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI).
sections.Results: 37 pwCF receiving ETI, 41 pwIBD and 63 HC were prospectively included. Retrospective data of 19 pwCF before ETI yielded a median total CFAbd-Score of 10.6pts which during ETI decreased to 5.8pts (p=0.07). Altogether, of the three subgroups, pwIBD were observed to have the highest total scores (median: 13.2pts), significantly higher than pwCF (7.7pts/p<0.05) and HC (5.9pts/p<0.0001). Analogously, scores related to disorders of appetite (DA) and quality of life (QoL) domains were significantly higher in pwIBD (DA:4pts/QoL:12.5pts) compared to pwCF (DA:0pts/QoL:0pts) and HC (DA:0pts/QoL:0pts). Furthermore, scores related to bowel movement were highest in pwIBD (16.7pts), compared to pwCF (10pts/p>0.05) and HC (7.5pts/p<0.01). On the other hand, total CFAbd-Scores and the disorders of appetite domain resulted significantly elevated in pwCF, compared to HC (p<0.05).
sections.Conclusion: The burden of AS is lower in pwCF compared to pwIBD. However, compared to HC, the CF-related mulltiorgan abdominal involvement causes a relevantly enhanced burden of symptoms, above all, in pwCF not receiving highly potent CFTR-modulating therapies.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Aristotle University of Thessaloniki, CF Unit, 3rd Paediatric Dept, Hippokration Hospital, Thessaloniki, Greece, 2University of Thessaly, Department of Nutrition and Dietetics, School of Physical Education, Sport Science and Dietetics, Trikala, Greece

sections.Methods: Our study included 30 patients with CF. Bone mineral density was assessed by dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA). Measurements were performed at the right and left femoral bones and the lumbar spine; results were expressed as Z-scores. Physical activity levels were assessed using the Godin Leisure-Time Activity Questionnaire. With respect to nutritional markers, we examined body mass index (BMI), 25-hydroxyvitamin D, calcium, phosphorus, and handgrip strength (HGS). HGS was assessed by using an electronic hand dynamometer. Quality of life was assessed using the revised CFQ-R.
sections.Results: No significant differences in bone mineral density Z-scores were observed between adolescents and young adults. Median Z-scores for the right femur were −0.35 (IQR: 0.83) in adolescents and −0.45 (IQR: 0.67) in young adults (p=0.553). For the left femur, median Z-scores were −0.30 (IQR: 0.95) and −0.25 (IQR: 0.83), respectively (p=0.822). Regarding the spine, children had a lower median Z-score [−1.00 (IQR: 1.00)] than young adults [−0.55 (IQR: 0.98)], but the difference was not significant (p = 0.676). No statistically significant correlations were observed between bone mineral density Z-scores and physical activity levels, nutritional markers (BMI, 25-OH vitamin D, calcium, phosphorus, handgrip strength), quality of life scores, and elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) treatment.
sections.Conclusion: Adolescents and young adults in our cohort demonstrated reduced bone mineral density, although differences between the age groups were not statistically significant. The lack of significant associations with physical activity, nutritional status, quality of life, and ETI may reflect the small sample size, underscoring the need for larger studies and ongoing monitoring of bone health.

sections.Objectives: To evaluate the potential influence of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) therapy on the progression of pancreatic imaging abnormalities in patients with Cystic Fibrosis.
sections.Methods: A total of 196 patients with Cystic Fibrosis treated with ETI and who underwent High-Resolution Computed Tomography (HRCT) were included (93 males, 103 females; mean age at first CT 15 years; mean age at start of therapy 24 years). A longitudinal evaluation of the pancreatic parenchyma included in the caudal portions of chest HRCT scans was performed using available pre- and post-therapy examinations (mean follow-up 88 months; 774 CT scans analyzed). Pancreatic parenchyma was independently segmented by two readers, and Hounsfield Unit (HU) density histograms were computed using dedicated research image analysis software (Mango, Research Imaging Institute, UTHSCSA). A multivariate analysis using a generalized linear mixed model was conducted to assess the effect of ETI therapy on changes in the distribution of acinar (18–70 HU) and adipose (–34 to –190 HU) pancreatic components before and after treatment.
sections.Results: A statistically significant time-related increase in the adipose component (β = 9.04; p = 0.0036) and a reduction in the acinar component (β = –12.85; p = 0.0031) were observed. Although pancreatic involution continued over time, a significant difference in the temporal response slope (β) was found post-treatment compared to pre-treatment for both the adipose (β = –0.40; p = 0.0022) and acinar components (β = 0.63; p = 0.0011) (p < 0.001). These findings indicate that ETI therapy induces significant changes in both intercept and slope for pancreatic adipose and acinar components.
sections.Conclusion: Despite the ongoing loss of acinar tissue and increase in adipose tissue, ETI therapy appears to significantly influence the progression of pancreatic structural changes in patients with Cystic Fibrosis.

Our aim was to evaluate changes in pancreatic status among paediatric CF patients within our regional network, from diagnosis to present. Methods: We undertook a retrospective audit of all faecal elastase (FE-1) levels measured in all CF patients in a paediatric network, from their diagnostic test, up to present. Exclusion criteria were patients with a Cystic Fibrosi...
presentations.authors_text: C.A. Berry1, C.J. Woodland1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Alder Hey Children's NHS Trust, Nutrition and Dietetics, Liverpool, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Pancreatic insufficiency (PI) is a common complication in cystic fibrosis (CF), historically considered irreversible in most patients. However, with earlier diagnosis and improved therapies, pancreatic status may change over time, particularly in patients who are able to start highly effective modulator therapy (HEMT) at a younger age. Our aim was to evaluate changes in pancreatic status among paediatric CF patients within our regional network, from diagnosis to present.
sections.Methods: We undertook a retrospective audit of all faecal elastase (FE-1) levels measured in all CF patients in a paediatric network, from their diagnostic test, up to present. Exclusion criteria were patients with a Cystic Fibrosis Screen Positive Inconclusive Diagnosis (CFSPID) designation. Patients both on and off HEMT were included in the data. We defined PI as those who had a FE-1 level <200µg/g.
sections.Results: Of 282 patients included, 260 (92%) patient’s pancreatic status remained unchanged from diagnosis, 224 (86%) of which were PI. Of the 22 patients who had a change in pancreatic status, 6 patients changed from pancreatic sufficiency (PS) to PI. 13 patients changed from PI to PS. Three patients changed from PS to PI back to PS. The mean age of the 16 patients who changed from PI to PS was 5 years, compared to a mean age of 10 years of the whole patient cohort. The median age of starting HEMT was 23 months and the median time from starting HEMT to PS was 10 months in these patients.
sections.Conclusion: Our results show that the patients who recovered pancreatic function are preschool age children and started HEMT at a young age. CF patients are able to start HEMT at a younger age and our results reinforces the importance of regular nutritional assessment and routine monitoring of FE-1 for all CF patients both PI and PS. Getting faecal samples from patients can be challenging but there is increasing clinical justification for requesting this as part of routine CF care.

This audit assessed adherence to both national and local standards and explored whether surveillance frequency could be reduced whilst still enabling earliest possible detection of liver complications. Methods: Electronic records for all children with CF at CUH (2014-2025) were reviewed. Exclusions: aged <12 months, >16 years, or CF-SPID. Data collection included USS frequency per patient, positive USS liver findings, age at detection, and if subseq...
presentations.authors_text: J. Coulson1, K. Lock1, R. Poole2, W. Kozlowska1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Addenbrooke's Hospital, Paediatric Respiratory, Cambridge, United Kingdom, 2Addenbrooke's Hospital, Paediatric Gastroenterology, Cambridge, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: National CF standards recommend liver ultrasound (USS) from age three, with further imaging as clinically indicated. At CUH, a paediatric CF centre, local guidance mandates annual liver USS from age one, creating a more intensive surveillance programme. This audit assessed adherence to both national and local standards and explored whether surveillance frequency could be reduced whilst still enabling earliest possible detection of liver complications.
sections.Methods: Electronic records for all children with CF at CUH (2014-2025) were reviewed. Exclusions: aged <12 months, >16 years, or CF-SPID. Data collection included USS frequency per patient, positive USS liver findings, age at detection, and if subsequent management was initiated. Data from 2020-2021 were excluded due to Covid‑19 disruption.
sections.Results: All 123 included patients met the national minimum standard for USS screening. Adherence to the local annual USS standard was 79.7%. A total of 34 patients (27.6%) had positive USS findings, with 23 commencing ursodeoxycholic acid. Only two positive USS findings were detected in children under age three. 25 patients missed ≥1 scan (33 total missed scans), with 66.7% of missed scans from 2022 onwards. Results were discussed with consultants across hepatology, gastroenterology and respiratory, with recommendations that annual USS is most appropriate for patients with GI surgical history, who are at higher risk of portal hypertension and biliary complications whereas lower risk patients could follow official national surveillance guidelines.
sections.Conclusion: Current local surveillance exceeds national guidance and may not be necessary for all patients. A risk‑stratified approach - annual USS for patients with GI surgical history from age one and USS as indicated from age three for lower risk groups - could reduce unnecessary imaging while maintaining safety. Updating local policy, refining USS indications, and ongoing monitoring of outcomes will support safe implementation.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1«National Medical Research Center for Children’s Health» of Ministry of Health of Russian Federation, Moscow, Russian Federation, 2I.M.Sechenov First Moscow State Medical University, Moscow, Russian Federation, Moscowrs, Russian Federation, 3Morozovskaya Children's City Clinical Hospital, Moscow, Russian Federation, Moscow, Russian Federation, 4Research Centre for Medical Genetics, Moscow, Russian Federation

sections.Objectives: carbohydrate metabolism disorders (CMD) in people with cystic fibrosis (CF) remain clinically relevant even in the era of variant-specific therapy (VST). Despite the pronounced clinical effect of VST in CF, published data on its impact on glucose metabolism are contradictory, and the prevalence and patterns of glucose abnormalities in children are still insufficiently studied. Therefore, assessment of glucose metabolism in children with CF (cwCF) receiving VST is of interest.
sections.Methods: twenty cwCF and elevated HbA1c were included in the study. Group I — 10 children receiving VST (median age (Me) = 14.27 years [11.64–14.73]; 2 children on two-component and 8 on three-component regimens); Group II — 10 children not receiving VST (Me = 12.58 years [11.24–14.83]). All cwCF underwent an oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) with measurements at three time points (0, 60, 120 minutes).
sections.Results: HbA1c was elevated in both groups (Group I: Me = 6.4% [6.17–6.59]; Group II: Me = 6.41% [6.28–6.54]). After OGTT, CMD were identified in 6/10 (60%) children in Group II (median age of affected children Me = 12.58 years [11.49–13.67]): indeterminate glycemia (INDET) n = 4 (40%), impaired glucose tolerance (IGT) n = 1, and cystic fibrosis-related diabetes (CFRD) n = 1. Group I had lower numbers of cwCF with CMD (two children with INDET and two with IGT); the median age of affected children in Group I was Me = 12.81 years [11.54–14.33]. Correlation analysis of blood glucose levels at the three OGTT time points between groups showed no statistically significant differences (p0 = 0.481; p60 = 0.165; p120 = 0.393).
sections.Conclusion: our observations suggest that VST may have a potentially favorable effect on glucose metabolism in children with CF. These findings underscore the need for regular metabolic monitoring, including OGTT, in all children with CF regardless of whether they receive targeted therapy or not. Follow-up of the cohort will continue.

At each time point, weight (kg), weight Z-score, height (cm), height Z-score, Body Mass Index (BMI, kg/m²), BMI Z-score, Fat Mass (FM, kg), Fat Free Mass (FFM, kg), Mid-Upper...
presentations.authors_text: C. Brignole1, V. Spina1, R. Guarise1, S. Volpi1, G. Tridello1, M. Signorini1, M. Cipolli1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Verona Cystic Fibrosis Center - AOUI VERONA, Verona, Italy

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) can lead to nutritional challenges in pediatric patients, including malnutrition and growth failure. The advent of CF modulators, such as elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI), has brought significant improvements in lung function and nutritional status. The aim of our study is to explore the impact of ETI treatment on growth parameters in children aged 6-12 years (yrs) with CF.
sections.Methods: Patients with CF (pwCF) were evaluated before the start (T0), after 1 year (T1), and after 2 yrs (T2) of ETI treatment. At each time point, weight (kg), weight Z-score, height (cm), height Z-score, Body Mass Index (BMI, kg/m²), BMI Z-score, Fat Mass (FM, kg), Fat Free Mass (FFM, kg), Mid-Upper-Arm Circumference (MUAC, cm), MUAC Z-score, Triceps Skin Fold (TSF, mm), and Forced Expiratory Volume in 1 second (FEV1,%) were measured. Data were compared using the Wilcoxon signed-rank test. The correlation between variables was assessed using the Pearson correlation coefficient. A p-value <0.05 was considered statistically significant.
sections.Results: A total of 28 pwCF (8 male, 20 female) with a median age of 9 years (range 6-12) were included. Significant increases were observed after 1 year of ETI in weight (3.3 kg, p<0.001), weight Z-score (0.1, p=0.01), height (6 cm, p<0.001), BMI (0.4 kg/m², p<0.001), FM (0.3 kg, p<0.01), FFM (3 kg, p<0.001), TSF (1.5 mm, p<0.01), MUAC (1 cm, p=0.001), MUAC Z-score (0.1, p=0.04), and FEV1 (8.5%, p=0.001). After 2 years, 18 out of 28 had available data, and similar results were found for most variables. The increase in BMI after 1 year was strongly correlated with the increase in FM (r = 0.8, p<0.001).
sections.Conclusion: We found encouraging improvements in growth parameters in children with CF, with benefits continuing beyond the first year of ETI treatment. These findings should be monitored prospectively to provide valuable insights into the evolving nutritional needs of children with CF starting ETI early.

A sample of 30 adults with...
presentations.authors_text: J. Snowball1,2, A. Adler3, P. Lally4, N. Talbot2, D. Koutoukidis1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Oxford, Nuffield Department of Primary Care Health Sciences, Oxford, United Kingdom, 2Oxford University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Oxford Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Oxford, United Kingdom, 3University of Oxford, Radcliffe Department of Medicine, Oxford, United Kingdom, 4University of Surrey, Guildford, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Approximately 40% of adults with cystic fibrosis (CF) now live with overweight or obesity due to advances in CF care and improved survival. Despite this emerging clinical issue, no trials of intentional weight loss strategies have been conducted in this population, and the feasibility , acceptability and safety of weight loss programmes remain unknown. The primary objective of this study is to determine the feasibility of progression to a definitive randomised controlled trial (RCT).
sections.Methods: This abstract describes the protocol for a 24-week, multicentre, unblinded, parallel-group randomised controlled feasibility trial with an embedded qualitative study. A sample of 30 adults with CF and body mass index (BMI) ≥27 kg/m² will be randomised in a 2:1 ratio to either a structured weight loss intervention or usual care. Randomisation will be stratified by age (<40 years vs ≥40 years) and presence of CF-related diabetes. The trial is delivered remotely, with study assessments and intervention support provided via video consultation. The intervention provided free of charge includes weekly behavioural support from a specialist CF dietitian, a meal replacement product, and a portion-controlled fresh ready meal. Semi-structured interviews will be conducted with participants and healthcare professionals. Feasibility will be assessed through recruitment, engagement, adherence and retention, with exploratory analyses of between-group differences in body weight, body composition, health-related quality of life, lung function, blood pressure, glycaemic control and blood lipids measured at baseline and 24 weeks. Adverse events will be monitored to assess intervention safety.
sections.Conclusion: EASE-CF will generate feasibility, acceptability and safety data to inform the design of a future definitive RCT of intentional weight loss in adults with CF. Ethical and regulatory approvals have been obtained, and recruitment is due to commence in January 2026.

Data were...
presentations.authors_text: A. Tomažič1, I. Ćatić2, D. Cigulajkić2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University Medical Centre Ljubljana, Department of Hospital Nutrition and Dietotherapy, Ljubljana, Slovenia, 2University Medical Centre Ljubljana, Department of Pulmonology and Allergy, Ljubljana, Slovenia

sections.Objectives: To develop and evaluate a cystic fibrosis (CF) specific nutritional screening questionnaire for early identification of adult patients at nutritional risk in routine outpatient care.​ ​
sections.Methods: A CF-specific nutritional screening questionnaire was developed by a clinical dietitian and implemented in routine outpatient care over an 8-month period. The questionnaire assesses recent weight loss, appetite changes, gastrointestinal symptoms, stool consistency, and self-reported functional capacity. Items were weighted and scored, classifying patients as low (0–4 points), moderate (5–8 points), or high (≥9 points) nutritional risk. Four questionnaires were excluded due to incomplete completion. Data were analysed using descriptive statistics.​ ​
sections.Results: Twenty-four adult CF patients were included (13 males, 11 females), with a mean age of 30.2±8.5 years and a mean BMI of 21.8±3.1 kg/m². Overall, 75.0% of patients were classified as low nutritional risk, 20.8% as moderate risk, and 4.2% as high risk. Female patients more frequently reported gastrointestinal and appetite-related symptoms, whereas male patients more commonly reported reduced physical endurance. The patient classified as high nutritional risk (11 points) had a normal BMI (21.5 kg/m²) but presented with reduced appetite, early satiety, nausea during meals, decreased physical strength, and bloating. Among patients with moderate nutritional risk, BMI ranged from 16 to 26 kg/m², demonstrating that clinically relevant nutritional risk would not have been identified by BMI alone.​ ​
sections.Conclusion: This CF-specific nutritional screening questionnaire is a practical and time-efficient tool for early identification of clinically relevant nutritional risk, including in patients with normal BMI. By capturing symptom burden and functional decline beyond anthropometry, it supports timely dietetic intervention in routine CF care. Further validation against established nutritional assessment tools is needed.

presentations.title: Longitudinal trends in nutritional status and associations with lung function in adults with cystic fibrosis: data from the UK CF Registry
presentations.summary: Introduction: Optimising nutritional status (NS) remains a key priority for adults with CF (awCF). Reports on NS trends in the UK are limited and the relationship between BMI and lung function in the CFTR modulator era remains unclear. Aim: To describe longitudinal trends in NS in UK awCF and associations of BMI and BMI change with clinical outcomes by modulator use. Method: Data on all awCF aged ≥ 18 years recorded in the UK CF Registry between 2007-2023 were analysed. AwCF were categorised by BMI according to ESPEN-ESPGHAN 2024 definitions: underweight, target range, overweight, and obese. Linear models were used to study the associations of BMI and BMI change with lung function, by modulator use. Results: D...
presentations.authors_text: J. Barrett1, S. Collins2, E. Granger3, J. Lowdon4, V. Lynch5, S. Maguire3, K. Powell6, C. Smith7, J. Snowball8, R. Keogh3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University Hospitals Birmingham NHS Foundation Trust, West Midlands Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Birmingham, United Kingdom, 2Royal Brompton & Harefield Hospitals, Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, London, United Kingdom, 3London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, London, United Kingdom, 4Leeds Children's hospital, Leeds, United Kingdom, 5Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, Northern Ireland Regional Respiratory Centre, Belfast City Hospital, Belfast, United Kingdom, 6York and Hull Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, York Hospital NHS Foundation Trust, York, United Kingdom, 7Royal Alexandra Children's Hospital,, Brighton, United Kingdom, 8Oxford University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Oxford, United Kingdom

sections.Methods: Data on all awCF aged ≥ 18 years recorded in the UK CF Registry between 2007-2023 were analysed. AwCF were categorised by BMI according to ESPEN-ESPGHAN 2024 definitions: underweight, target range, overweight, and obese. Linear models were used to study the associations of BMI and BMI change with lung function, by modulator use.
sections.Results: Data on 8,869 awCF from 82,604 annual reviews were available. Between 2007 and 2023, awCF within the target BMI range decreased from 71.5% to 57.0%, overweight rose from 15% to 28.8%, obesity rose from 2.7% to 9.7%, and underweight decreased from 10.7% to 4.5%. Higher BMI was associated with better lung function. Increasing BMI in underweight awCF saw the largest increase in lung function. BMI increases in overweight/obese awCF was not associated with better lung function. Modulator therapy did not influence the association between BMI and lung function (p=0.99).
sections.Conclusion: This large, registry-based study is the first to examine trends in nutritional status in awCF in the UK and associations with clinical outcomes. Our findings align with other national registry data and highlight the need to prioritise the management and understanding of excess weight and its clinical implications. These trends highlight the need for proactive nutritional management strategies to optimise health outcomes, to minimise the risk of overweight/obesity and associated co-morbidities in awCF.

Wilcoxon signed-rank test was used for paired comparisons. Results: The cohort comprised 38 children...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1University Hospital Brno, Department of Paediatric Infectious Diseases, Brno, Czech Republic, 2Masaryk University, Faculty of Medicine, Brno, Czech Republic

sections.Objectives: To evaluate real-world effects of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) on nutritional status and pancreatic exocrine function in children with cystic fibrosis (CF) following its introduction into clinical practice in the Czech Republic.
sections.Methods: A retrospective analysis was performed 12 months after ETI became available for children aged 2–6 years. Patients younger than 12 years at ETI initiation were included. Changes in body mass index (BMI, percentile) and faecal pancreatic elastase were analysed. Subgroup analyses were conducted according to prior treatment with lumacaftor/ivacaftor (LUM/IVA). Wilcoxon signed-rank test was used for paired comparisons.
sections.Results: The cohort comprised 38 children with CF (18 females, 20 males) with a mean age of 7.3 ± 2.7 years (median 6.6). ETI was initiated at a mean age of 5.7 ± 2.0 years (median 5.8). Twenty-four patients had received prior (lumacafotr/ivacaftor) LUM/IVA therapy. In this subgroup, no statistically significant change in BMI percentile was observed after ETI initiation (p = 0.19), although a positive trend was noted (median change +6.5). In contrast, children without prior LUM/IVA therapy demonstrated a significant increase in BMI percentile following ETI initiation (p = 0.0023; 95% CI 3.97–14.56). In this group, faecal pancreatic elastase levels increased significantly after ETI compared with baseline (Wilcoxon signed-rank test, n = 24, p < 0.000001), with a median increase of 252 µg/g (95% CI 170–360).
sections.Conclusion: In routine clinical practice, ETI therapy was associated with significant improvements in nutritional status and pancreatic exocrine function in children with CF, particularly in those without prior LUM/IVA treatment. These findings support early initiation of ETI in paediatric CF patients.

presentations.summary: Background: Nutritional impairment remains a major challenge in patients with cystic fibrosis and is strongly associated with poorer pulmonary outcomes and reduced survival. The introduction of highly effective CFTR modulator therapy, particularly the triple combination elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has been associated with significant improvements in nutritional status, including weight gain and body mass index (BMI). Aim: To evaluate changes in BMI and nutritional status after 1 year of ETI in patients with cystic fibrosis. Methods: This retrospective observational cohort included 54 patients with cystic fibrosis (mean age 19.5±6.8 years; range 7–40 years). Anthropometric data were collected at base...
presentations.authors_text: L. Spirevska1, E. Krsteska1, V. Cvejoska Cholakovska1,2, A. Andreevska Stepanovska1, S. Fustikj1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University Children's Clinic, Ss.Cyril and Methodius University in Skopje, North Macedonia, Department for Cystic Fibrosis, Skopje, North Macedonia, The Republic of, 2University Children's Clinic, Ss.Cyril and Methodius University in Skopje, North Macedonia, Department of Pulmonology and Allergology, Skopje, North Macedonia, The Republic of

sections.Background: Nutritional impairment remains a major challenge in patients with cystic fibrosis and is strongly associated with poorer pulmonary outcomes and reduced survival. The introduction of highly effective CFTR modulator therapy, particularly the triple combination elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has been associated with significant improvements in nutritional status, including weight gain and body mass index (BMI). Aim: To evaluate changes in BMI and nutritional status after 1 year of ETI in patients with cystic fibrosis.
sections.Methods: This retrospective observational cohort included 54 patients with cystic fibrosis (mean age 19.5±6.8 years; range 7–40 years). Anthropometric data were collected at baseline and after 3, 6, 9, and 12 months of therapy. Changes in BMI were analysed using repeated-measures analysis of variance with Bonferroni-adjusted pairwise comparisons. Changes in nutritional categories (underweight, normal weight, overweight/obesity) were assessed using McNemar’s test. Statistical significance was set at p<0.05.
sections.Results: Mean BMI increased significantly from 20.2±4.1 kg/m² at baseline to 21.6±4.2 kg/m² after 12 months (mean change +1.45 kg/m²; p<0.001). The largest increase occurred within the first 3 months (+0.82 kg/m²; p<0.001), followed by smaller but statistically significant increments thereafter. The proportion of underweight patients decreased from 40.7% to 20.8%, while the prevalence of normal weight increased from 44.4% to 58.5% (p=0.001). In the paediatric subgroup (n=23), BMI percentiles improved significantly (+11.5 percentile points; p=0.001)
sections.Conclusion: One year of ETI therapy resulted in statistically and clinically significant improvements in BMI, characterised by rapid early gains and sustained improvement over time, leading to a marked reduction in undernutrition.
