presentations.affiliations_text: 1UNICAMP, Campinas, Brazil

sections.Objectives: To describe the quality of the diet and its relationship with lung function and nutritional status in adolescents with cystic fibrosis (CF).
sections.Methods: A questionnaire was applied to 47 adolescents (12–19 years old) followed at a university reference center. Lung function impairment was classified as mild (FEV1pp≥60%), moderate (FEV1pp 41–59%) or severe (FEV1pp<40%) using spirometry. Food consumption frequency was classified as rarely, 1-2 times/week, 3-4 times/week, or ≥ 5 times/week. Linear regression analysis was used to model the relationship between lung function, BMI/age Z-score and food frequency. Principal component analysis (PCA) was performed to identify patterns of diet quality.
sections.Results: The mean BMI/age Z-score was -0.62. Approximately 60% of adolescents had FEV1≥60%, and 21% had severe impairment (FEV1<40%). Better BMI/age Z-score were observed in groups with higher consumption frequencies of vegetables and fruit (≥5/week), white meat (>3-4/week), and oil (>3-4/week). Linear regression models identified significant relationships: BMI/age Z-Score=-3.25+ 0.8182*meat_b+0.5082*vegetables_fruit (p<0.001): Increased white meat, fruit, and vegetable consumption positively influenced the BMI/age Z-Score; lung function = 0.6927 + 0.3356*vegetables-0.2406*fruit_vegetables-0.4550*fast_food (p<0.001): Lower consumption of fruit and vegetables and higher fast-food intake were associated with more severe lung function; and lung function = 0.39376 -0.32345 *ZSCORE (p<0.0001): The BMI/age Z-Score positively influenced lung function. PCA confirmed the positive relationship between fruit, vegetables and white meat consumption and BMI/age Z-score.
sections.Conclusion: Higher consumption of fruit, vegetables and meat has a positive influence on the BMI/age Z-score and lung function among adolescents with CF.

presentations.title: Nutritional status and bone strength in children and adolescents with cystic fibrosis: an observational study
presentations.summary: Introduction: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is an autosomal recessive hereditary disease. Malnutrition is a common clinical manifestation, mainly due to malabsorption, decreased energy intake, and increased nutritional needs. Additionally, bone metabolism in these patients may also be compromised, resulting in low bone density and a consequent increased risk of fracture. This study aims to characterize the nutritional status and the bone strength (BS) of children and adolescents with CF. Materials and Methods: This is an observational, analytical, cross-sectional study. Clinical history (type of mutation, pharmacotherapy, FEV1, and serum parameters) was collected in 25 patients. Nutritional status was characterized by...
presentations.authors_text: E. Dionisio1, S. Castanheira2, R. Bragança3, S. Castanhinha3, A.C. Moreira4
presentations.affiliations_text: 1ULS São José, Nutrição, Lisboa, Portugal, 2Escola Superior de Saude de Lisboa, Nutrição, Lisboa, Portugal, 3ULS São José, Pneumologia, Lisboa, Portugal, 4Escola Superior de Saude de Lisboa; H&TRC-Health Technology Research Center, Nutrição, Lisboa, Portugal

sections.Methods: This is an observational, analytical, cross-sectional study. Clinical history (type of mutation, pharmacotherapy, FEV1, and serum parameters) was collected in 25 patients. Nutritional status was characterized by weight, height, and BMI. Bone Strength was evaluated by the speed of sound in the radius and tibia using bone ultrasound. A 95% confidence level was established.
sections.Results: Median age was 8.4 ± 5.03 years, with the majority (56%) aged between 2 and 12 years. According to the BMI-age-z-score, 88% of patients had adequate nutritional status, but 2 children had weight impairment, and 1 child had height impairment, when assessed by weight-for-age and height-for-age z-scores, respectively. Although most patients had adequate BS for age and sex, there were 35.3% and 16.7% cases of osteopenia, when assessed in the radius and tibia, respectively. There were no significant correlations between BS and nutritional status, but when compared by groups, we found were significantly lower FEV1 values in the group with compromised BS, both in the radius (p=0.045) and in the tibia (p=0.048).
sections.Conclusion: Although the data reflect a population mostly with adequate nutritional status, some cases of osteopenia have already been verified. It is therefore essential to have follow-up appointments with specialized CF specialists that provide preventive care not only on nutritional status but also in promoting bone health, thus increasing the quality of life of these patients.

presentations.title: Assessment of Fat Mass Index and Fat-Free Mass Index in adult patients with cystic fibrosis after initiation of triple modulator therapy

sections.Objectives: Analyze the impact of TMT on Fat Mass Index (FMI) and Fat-Free Mass Index (FFMI) in a group of adult patients with CF, before and after the initiation of this therapy.

FMI and FFMI, with respective classifications according to reference percentile values. Data were analyzed using SPSS statistical software and were considered statistically significant at (p<0,05).
sections.Results: Statistical analysis demonstrated the presence of significant changes over time, confirming the beneficial effect of TMT on the nutritional status. However, some patients transitioned to elevated body composition states, such as increased FMI, or in some cases consistently remained at low FFMI levels. Certain factos, such as physical activity level and dietary patterns, were not included in this analysis, which may represent a limitation of this study.
sections.Conclusion: This study demonstrates the significant impact of TMT on FMI and FFMI variables; but the implementation of a highly personalized intervention is essential. This study provides valuable insights into body composition in adults with CF.

sections.Objectives: ETI has significantly altered the nutritional trajectory of cwCF, with clinical rollout outpacing the development of nutritional guidance. This study aimed to 1. systematically review the literature on ETI and nutrition 2. develop expert consensus-based guidance 3. highlight research gaps.
sections.Methods: This project was registered on PROSPERO (ID: 587618) and guided by a multidisciplinary panel of 22 clinicians and a parent representative. Research questions were developed using the PICO framework across domains including anthropometry, body composition, vitamins, minerals, cholesterol, diet, endocrine and exocrine pancreatic status and ETI administration. A literature search was conducted across Medline, Embase, CINAHL, and Cochrane (up to and including April 2025). Studies were screened, assessed using SIGN criteria, and summarised in evidence tables. Draft statements were refined through iterative discussion, with final consensus achieved (≥80% agreement) using real-time Delphi. Research priorities were determined based on the highest proportion of “Strongly Agree” responses.
sections.Results: Thirteen research statements were developed, representing the highest-priority gap in each domain; the top four are presented here.
sections.Conclusion: Significant research gaps remain, with unanimous agreement that reassessing the appropriateness of a BMI target ≥50th percentile is a key priority. sections.Objectives: Reduced muscle mass, strength and function are associated with poor exercise tolerance, and survival in people with CF. This study aimed to characterise and explore the relationship between body composition, muscle strength and function in people with CF (pwCF).
sections.Methods: Retrospective data was collected from clinically stable adults who attended annual review assessments in 2024 and 2025. Data on age, genotype, CFTR modulator use, FEV1, exocrine pancreatic status, diabetes status and P. Aeruginosa colonisation was recorded. Body composition was measured using bioelectrical impedance analysis (Seca M515). Muscle strength was assessed using Peak Torque at 60°/s (Biodex® PRO 3), and muscle function using peak workload (Wpeak - watts) during cardiopulmonary exercise test (CPET). Statistical analysis was performed using SPSS v29, with significance set at p<0.05.
sections.Results: The study included 123 pwCF (54% male); mean age was 33 years (SD 10.8). 108(88%) were pancreatic insufficient, 33(27%) had CF related diabetes and 82 (67%) were colonised with P.aeruginosa. Mean BMI was 24kg/m2 (SD3.46), and mean FEV1 was 81%. Clinical Data CategorySub-CategoryNumber Percentage %Wpeak n=63Low1931%Normal 1829%High2540%Peak torque % of predicted value n=60 Low3050%Normal 2237%High813%Skeletal Muscle Mass (SMM) n=123Low2925%Normal 9275%High0 Fat Mass Index (FMI) n=123Low22%Normal 7661%Increased 3529%High108% Independently, as age and BMI increased, muscle strength decreased (p<0.05). FEV1 positively correlated with muscle function but negatively with age (p<0.05). Muscle mass was positively associated with BMI, muscle function and strength (p<0.05).
sections.Conclusion: Impaired muscle mass, strength and function were common despite normal FEV1 and BMI in pwCF. Results reinforce the clinical need for body composition measurement rather than BMI as a determinant of muscle strength and function. Targeted interventions including resistance exercise and nutritional strategies are important to preserve muscle health and functional outcomes in pwCF.

presentations.authors_text: M. Dunnage1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1St Bartholomew's Hospital, Bart's Health NHS Trust, Bart's Adult CF Centre, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Non-medical prescribing (NMP) can improve service efficiency, innovation and is cost saving (Babashahi et al, 2025). In the UK, legislation changed in 2016 and dietitians can now train to become supplementary prescribers (SP). Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a multisystem disease involving pancreatic exocrine insufficiency, diabetes and gastrointestinal symptoms such as reflux and constipation. The CF Dietitian has the specialist knowledge to prescribe medications related to the nutritional management of patients with CF (NHS England, 2015). SP prescribe medications from a clinical management plan (CMP) agreed between a doctor and the SP. There are no specific formulary restrictions, though the SP must prescribe for conditions within their scope of practice. (BDA, 2016; RPS, 2021) This service evaluation sought to review the prescribing activity of a dietetic SP in one adult CF centre.
sections.Methods: All medications prescribed between September 2024 and December 2025 were recorded, including the dose and any adverse events. The location of the prescribing activity was also reviewed.
sections.Results: MedicationFrequencyPancreatic Enzyme Replacement Therapy27Laxatives (Macrogol, sodium docusate, Gastrografin®, Picolax®)14Insulin (Degludec, Lispro, Glargine, Aspart)4Flash glucose sensors1Proton pump inhibitors1Vitamin supplements (colecalciferol, CF specific multivitamin)3Table 1: Frequency of medications prescribed by a dietetic supplementary prescriber in one cystic fibrosis centre in the UK Fifty medications were prescribed in 40 episodes, for 18 patients. Three were inpatients. All patients had a CMP signed prior to SP. All patients consented to dietetic prescribing. There were no medication errors or adverse events noted. Pancreatic Enzyme Replacement Therapy was the most common medication prescribed due to the recent shortage in UK community pharmacies, requiring this to be dispensed from the hospital pharmacy. The signing of the CMP was a barrier to prescribing more frequently on an ad hoc basis and during inpatient admissions.
sections.Conclusion: Dietetic SP in CF brings dietetic and medical management strategies together. It is safe, efficient and can reduce the number of referrals to other prescribers which is likely to result in cost savings. Further research is needed to review the efficacy of dietetic prescribing in CF in comparison to other medical or NMPs.

Since 2022, modulator therapy has been extended to children aged over 1 or 2 years, depending on genotype. Aim: To assess the sustained effects of modulator therapy on pancreatic function in children who became PS and to determine whether earlier modulator initiation increases PS r...
presentations.authors_text: E. Sheppard1, G. Buckley1, A. Maitra2, A. Shawcross2, K. Rose2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Royal Manchester Children's Hospital, Therapies and Dietetics, Manchester, United Kingdom, 2Royal Manchester Children's Hospital, Paediatric Respiratory Medicine, Manchester, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Recent studies show that CFTR modulator use in children with Cystic Fibrosis (CF) improves gastrointestinal symptoms, reduces pancreatic enzyme use, improves vitamin status and may lead to recovery of pancreatic function (Rosenfeld Lancet Resp Med 2018). A 2022 review at our centre found 3/9 children with CF on modulators, who had a faecal elastase (FE) sample collected had become pancreatic sufficient (PS; FE >200μg/g). Since 2022, modulator therapy has been extended to children aged over 1 or 2 years, depending on genotype. Aim: To assess the sustained effects of modulator therapy on pancreatic function in children who became PS and to determine whether earlier modulator initiation increases PS rates.
sections.Methods: Children aged 1–14 years prescribed a CFTR modulator at our large paediatric centre were identified. Patient records were reviewed for FE and fat-soluble vitamin levels.
sections.Results: Of 105 children on a modulator with baseline pancreatic insufficiency (PI), 59 underwent FE testing after reported gastrointestinal changes or >6 months of modulator use. Nine patients (8.5%) became PS. Twenty-three (22%) showed a rise in FE to 15–200μg/g, while 27 remained <15μg/g. No repeat FE was available for 46 patients due to presence of gastrointestinal symptoms or reluctance to provide samples. Of those who became PS, median age at modulator initiation was 2 years (range 1–9 years). Time to PS ranged from 3 months to 5 years across different modulator therapies. Repeat testing (n=3) showed sustained FE >200μg/g for up to 3 years. In patients with rising FE who remained PI, 19/23 required reduction of fat-soluble vitamin doses due to elevated levels.
sections.Conclusion: This real-world data demonstrates that children with CF can become PS up to 5 years after starting modulator therapy, with sustained improvement in those retested. Partial recovery of pancreatic function is often associated with elevated vitamin levels. Monitoring of fat-soluble vitamins post modulator initiation and consideration of annual FE testing may be beneficial.

Aim: We aimed to look at trends in pancreatic function in children with pancreatic insufficiency in the first year of life who were commenced on Orkambi® prior to age two years. Methods: Patients born between January 2021 and June 2024 were identified from our patient cohort and information on age at initiat...
presentations.authors_text: E. Sheppard1, G. Buckley1, A. Maitra2, A. Shawcross2, K. Rose2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Royal Manchester Children's Hospital, Therapies and Dietetics, Manchester, United Kingdom, 2Royal Manchester Children's Hospital, Paediatric Respiratory Medicine, Manchester, United Kingdom

sections.Background: In the UK the licence for prescribing lumacaftor/Ivacaftor (Orkambi®) was extended to children over 1 year with a diagnosis of cystic fibrosis homozygous for F508del in early 2023 (Rayment AM J Respir Crit Care Med 2022). Our large tertiary paediatric centre and shared care network routinely offer initiation of Orkambi® to eligible patients age one year, prior to initiation of ETI at two years. Aim: We aimed to look at trends in pancreatic function in children with pancreatic insufficiency in the first year of life who were commenced on Orkambi® prior to age two years.
sections.Methods: Patients born between January 2021 and June 2024 were identified from our patient cohort and information on age at initiation of modulator therapy and any faecal elastase (FE) results were obtained from our electronic patient record.
sections.Results: We identified 20 patients who were F508del homozygous and commenced on Orkambi® prior to two years of age. Faecal elastase was checked in 13/20 patients (clinical decision – usually if any changes in stool / gastrointestinal symptoms were noted during clinical reviews). 7/20 (35%) patients have become pancreatic sufficient (PS, with FE level >200ug/g), with six of these becoming PS within a year of commencing Orkambi® and prior to moving to ETI. The 7th patient became PS after moving onto ETI. All 7 have discontinued pancreatic enzyme therapy. A further six patients had some increase in pancreatic function (with a FE 15-200ug/g). FE hadn’t been tested in 7 patients.
sections.Conclusion: Our real-world data has demonstrated that early therapy with CFTR modulators including lumacaftor/ivacaftor which is licenced from 12 months of age may lead to greater numbers of pwCF becoming pancreatic sufficient.

A fortnightly virtual ‘vitamin club’ was introduced to streamline result follow-up, comprising of a specialist paediatric respiratory dietitian and prescribing pharmacist. This multidisciplinary approach aimed to improve efficiency, reduce delays in decision making, and ensure consistent specialist-led care. Aim: To evaluate the ‘vitamin club’ by quantifying reviewed results, abn...
presentations.authors_text: M. Healy1, L. Schembri2, S. Kenny2, C. Walker2, I. Balfour-Lynn3, S. Bentley1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Royal Brompton Hospital, Pharmacy, London, United Kingdom, 2Royal Brompton Hospital, Dietetics, London, United Kingdom, 3Royal Brompton Hospital, Paediatric Respiratory, London, United Kingdom

sections.Background: Annual assessments at our paediatric cystic fibrosis (CF) centre include serum vitamins A, D and E to guide supplementation. With widespread CFTR modulator use (such as elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor [ETI]), studies report elevated fat-soluble vitamin levels, especially vitamin A [1], reinforcing the need for monitoring. A fortnightly virtual ‘vitamin club’ was introduced to streamline result follow-up, comprising of a specialist paediatric respiratory dietitian and prescribing pharmacist. This multidisciplinary approach aimed to improve efficiency, reduce delays in decision making, and ensure consistent specialist-led care. Aim: To evaluate the ‘vitamin club’ by quantifying reviewed results, abnormal findings and prescription changes.
sections.Methods: Retrospective review of children with CF managed within ‘vitamin club’ between August ’24 and July ’25. Data were collected from annual review records and electronic patient records.
sections.Results: 246 children were reviewed; complete results were initially available for 228 (93%), while 17 (7%) required repeat sampling. Interventions were needed in 68 (28%) children, 87% on CFTR modulators. Adjustments included: • Vitamin A: nine dose increases, six decreases (five on ETI), no discontinued prescriptions. Zinc added for two children. • Vitamin E: 14 reductions (all on ETI), seven increases, two discontinued. • Vitamin D: 20 high one-off doses for deficiency (<50 ng/mL), six maintenance dose increases. Adherence was addressed in five children without changing the prescription.
sections.Conclusion: ‘Vitamin club’ enabled specialist-led interventions in over a quarter of children, ensuring optimal supplementation. Continued monitoring remains essential in the modulator era. A key strength is the effective use of the multidisciplinary team for streamlined care. References: Schembri L, et al. Impact of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor on fat-soluble vitamin levels in children with cystic fibrosis. J Cyst Fibros.2023;22(5):843-846.

sections.Objectives: To evaluate changes in pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT), dietary intake and body composition in preschool children aged 2–5 years with cystic fibrosis (CF) who initiated modulator therapy with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor® (ETI), evaluated after 12 months of treatment. Materials and
sections.Methods: This was a prospective, single-centre pilot study. Variables analysed before (pre) and after (post) 12 months of ETI included body mass index (BMI) z-score, fat mass percentage, fat-free mass percentage, energy intake, van de Kamer test, fat intake, lipase units (LU) per kg per day and LU per gram of fat consumed. A 3-day dietary record including PERT was collected (nutritional analysis performed using DietSource® 4.0 software). Body composition was assessed by electrical bioimpedance analysis (Maltron®). Statistical analysis was performed using STATA 18.0.
sections.Results: A total of 17 children with CF (69% female) initiated ETI treatment. Median age was 3.4 years (interquartile range 2.9–4.8), and 100% were diagnosed through newborn screening. All patients had pancreatic insufficiency. All carried at least one F508del mutation (31% homozygous). Table 1 summarises the values of the variables of interest and the associated p values for differences between time points. sections.Conclusion: In children with cystic fibrosis at our centre, the introduction of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor was not associated with statistically significant changes in BMI z-score, body composition or overall dietary intake after 12 months of treatment. However, the significant adjustment in lipase units per gram of fat consumed indicates a more appropriate matching of pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy to dietary fat intake following ETI, which may enhance nutritional management in this age group.

presentations.title: Nutritional status of cystic fibrosis patients in a country without neonatal screening: data from the Republic of Moldova
presentations.summary: Optimal nutritional status is a key determinant of pulmonary function and survival in cystic fibrosis (CF). Aim: To assess the nutritional status of CF patients followed at the national CF center in the Republic of Moldova (RM). Methods: We performed a retrospective cross-sectional analysis of CF patients registered at the Center for Cystic Fibrosis during the follow-up year 2024. Demographic data, age at diagnosis, anthropometric parameters and pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy use were analyzed descriptively. Results: A total of 60 patients were included, of whom 52 were actively followed. The mean age at follow-up was 14.6 years (range 0.6–38.7), and 29.8% of patients were adults. The mean age at diagno...
presentations.authors_text: C. Tomacinschii1,2, C. Conica1,2, D. Efros1, N. Buga1, R. Selvestru1, N. Barbova1,2, S. Sciuca1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Nicolae Testemitanu State University of Medicine and Pharmacy, Pediatrics, Chișinău, Moldova, Republic of, 2Institute of Mother and Child, Chișinău, Moldova, Republic of

sections.Methods: We performed a retrospective cross-sectional analysis of CF patients registered at the Center for Cystic Fibrosis during the follow-up year 2024. Demographic data, age at diagnosis, anthropometric parameters and pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy use were analyzed descriptively.
sections.Results: A total of 60 patients were included, of whom 52 were actively followed. The mean age at follow-up was 14.6 years (range 0.6–38.7), and 29.8% of patients were adults. The mean age at diagnosis was 3.23 years, reflecting delayed diagnosis in the absence of neonatal screening. Mean height Z-scores were below international reference values across all age groups, with the most pronounced deficit observed in adolescents aged 12-17 years (mean −1.4). Weight impairment was more severe, particularly in adolescents (mean weight Z-score -2.0) and young adults aged 18-29 years (mean -2.2), with extreme values as low as -6.8. In children, the mean BMI Z-score was -1.2; 50-57.1% of adolescents aged 12-17 years had BMI Z-scores <−2, with similar prevalence in males and females. Among adults, mean BMI was 18.5 kg/m², with lower values observed in males compared with females; the majority of adult patients had BMI ≤20 kg/m². Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy was prescribed in 92.3% of patients, consistent with a high prevalence of pancreatic insufficiency.
sections.Conclusion: CF patients in the RM exhibit marked nutritional compromise, likely related to delayed diagnosis and lack of disease-modifying therapies. These findings emphasize the critical need for early diagnosis strategies, optimized nutritional interventions and improved access to comprehensive CF care in resource-limited settings.

presentations.title: Exploring hand grip strength to promote a healthy weight and lifestyle in adults with cystic fibrosis
presentations.summary: Objectives: Hand grip strength (HGS) is an emerging biomarker associated with overall health status, mortality risk, disability, and nutritional assessment in the general population. Lower HGS has been linked to poorer clinical outcomes in individuals with cystic fibrosis (CF), including increased exacerbations, reduced lung function, lower BMI, and poorer bone health. This project aimed to explore associations between HGS, BMI and lung function, and assess patient acceptability. Secondary aims were to evaluate whether monitoring HGS has a place at annual review assessment, as a tool to motivate adults with cystic fibrosis (CF) to improve physical activity and nutritional status. Methods: 9 adults with CF atte...
presentations.authors_text: A. Marshall1, A. Smith1, H. Sheddon2, S. Hunt3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1NHS Tayside, Adult CF Service, Dundee, United Kingdom, 2Queen Margaret University, Physiotherapy (MSc Student), Edinburgh, United Kingdom, 3Robert Gordon University, Physiotherapy (MPhys Student), Aberdeen, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Hand grip strength (HGS) is an emerging biomarker associated with overall health status, mortality risk, disability, and nutritional assessment in the general population. Lower HGS has been linked to poorer clinical outcomes in individuals with cystic fibrosis (CF), including increased exacerbations, reduced lung function, lower BMI, and poorer bone health. This project aimed to explore associations between HGS, BMI and lung function, and assess patient acceptability. Secondary aims were to evaluate whether monitoring HGS has a place at annual review assessment, as a tool to motivate adults with cystic fibrosis (CF) to improve physical activity and nutritional status.
sections.Methods: 9 adults with CF attending outpatient clinics and due for annual review, were recruited for assessment. Baseline measures included hand grip strength (dynamometer), BMI, dietary intake, physical activity and lung function (FEV₁). Participants received feedback on grip strength and tailored advice to encourage activity and improve energy balance. Qualitative feedback captured patient perspectives on the approach.
sections.Results: Across the cohort, mean grip strength corresponded to the 28th percentile relative to normative data. No clear correlations were observed between HGS and physical activity, BMI, or lung function; notably, the patient with the highest grip strength reported no regular exercise. Most participants demonstrated normal BMI ranges, limiting the ability to detect nutrition-related trends. Nonetheless, patients expressed high engagement with the HGS testing process and viewed grip strength feedback as motivating and informative.
sections.Conclusion: Grip strength monitoring appears feasible and acceptable in CF care, with potential to open conversations on physical activity and nutritional health. Incorporating this approach into dietetic practice could enhance patient motivation, support weight management, and contribute to long-term outcomes.

presentations.title: Impact of nutritional quality improvement project: the first 1000 days of life
presentations.summary: Introduction: Newborn screening for cystic fibrosis (CF) offers an opportunity for early medical and nutritional interventions, potentially leading to improved clinical outcomes. Higher body mass index (BMI) percentiles at two years of age have been strongly associated with improved pulmonary function in later childhood. Epidemiological evidence indicates that enhancing the nutritional status of young children is linked to more favorable pulmonary outcomes. The aim of this study is to investigate the effects of the quality improvement project implemented at our center on children under the age of two who are closely monitored by dietitian. Methods: This is a single center QI project initiated in 2019. CF patie...

sections.Methods: This is a single center QI project initiated in 2019. CF patients diagnosed via newborn screening or symptoms within the first year of life were included. These patients were evaluated monthly by the CF dieatitian in the center or reported their measurements to the dietitian via phone, email, or other communication methods. Based on their anthropometric data and clinical symptoms, nutritional recommendations were provided and enzyme replacement therapy dosages were adjusted accordingly.
sections.Results: Over a period of six years, 66 patients were included. Six patients were diagnosed after the six month years old. The median age (min-max) at initiation of follow-up is 92.5 ( 3.0–365.0) days. Diagnosis was made via newborn screening program in 53 (75.7%) of the patients. The median age (min-max) at diagnosis was 0.1 (0.0-1.0) years. An increase in weight, height, and BMI percentiles was observed during follow-up. Over a two-year follow-up period, the weight percentile increased from 23.3 to 56.3, the height percentile from 20 to 30.3, and the BMI percentile from 20.7 to 46.6.
sections.Conclusion: Close monitoring in patients with cystic fibrosis has been found to have a positive impact on growth. This study is also important in demonstrating the positive impact of quality improvement projects implemented at the cystic fibrosis center.

Methods: A total of 160 cwCF were reviewed in our clinical cohort. Medical notes were reviewed for those who had stopped PERT, including g...
presentations.authors_text: J. Lowdon1, T. Lee1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Leeds Children's Hospital, Cystic Fibrosis Unit, Leeds, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Pancreatic Insufficiency (PI) was previously thought to be irreversible in Cystic Fibrosis (CF), but in the era of Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Conductance Regulator modulators (CFTRm), this is now being refuted (1). Our aim was to identify and review the clinical status of children with CF (cwCF) who are PI, on a CFTRm, who have been able to stop Pancreatic Enzyme Replacement Therapy (PERT), in our clinical cohort.
sections.Methods: A total of 160 cwCF were reviewed in our clinical cohort. PI was determined with a faecal pancreatic elastase-1 (FE-1) level of <100ug/g stool for moderate PI and <200ug/g stool for severe PI at diagnosis. Medical notes were reviewed for those who had stopped PERT, including genetics, pancreatic elastase 1 level (FE-1 level), date when PERT was stopped, clinical symptoms, vitamin supplementation and growth parameters post stopping PERT.
sections.Results: Eleven PI cwCF have stopped PERT after commencing a CFTRm. Three presented with meconium ileus at diagnosis. Ten are homozygous for Phe508del. Eight were on elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (Kaftrio®) at the time of stopping PERT, one on lumacaftor/ivacaftor (Orkambi®). Age at time of stopping PERT ranged from one year six months - 11 years five months. Only four were able to provide a FE-1 level within the accepted range for a pancreatic sufficient (PS) level (>200ug/g stool). Five remain low, one has refused to provide a sample and one is awaiting the result. The main reason cited for stopping PERT, despite a low FE-1 level, is constipation. Weaning off PERT varied individually. Since stopping PERT, no worsening gastro-intestinal (GI) symptoms or growth parameters have been identified. No one has managed to stop vitamin supplementation.
sections.Conclusion: It is important to continue to monitor pancreatic function in cwCF on any CFTRm. Whilst some cwCF demonstrated an improvement in FE-1 levels consistent with PS, others were still able to stop PERT based on clinical symptoms. Consideration therefore needs to be given to GI symptoms, as well as FE-1 levels. 1. Stephenson et al. Changes in fecal elastase-1 following initiation of CFTR modulator therapy in pediatric patients with cystic fibrosis. Journal of Cystic Fibrosis 22 (2023) 996–1001.

presentations.title: The effect of targeted therapy with CFTR modulators on the relationship between nutritional status, carbohydrate and lipid metabolism in children with cystic fibrosis
presentations.summary: Relevance: Targeted therapy (TT) has become integral to cystic fibrosis (CF) management by addressing the underlying defect. Alongside its pulmonary benefits, TT may systemically affect metabolic parameters such as glycemic control and lipid profiles, which are common complications impacting prognosis and quality of life in CF. Understanding this metabolic influence is essential for holistic patient care. Materials and methods: A total of 76 children with CF (cwCF) aged 6 to 17 years and 11 months were included in the comparative study. Among them, 39 cwCF (Group 1) received only basic therapy, and 37 were on TT (Group 2). The groups were comparable in terms of age and sex. CwCF in Group 2 had been receiving T...

sections.Methods: A total of 76 children with CF (cwCF) aged 6 to 17 years and 11 months were included in the comparative study. Among them, 39 cwCF (Group 1) received only basic therapy, and 37 were on TT (Group 2). The groups were comparable in terms of age and sex. CwCF in Group 2 had been receiving TT for at least 3 months. We assessed the impact of therapy on nutritional status as body mass index for age Z-scores (BAZ), glycemic control (fasting glucose, HbA1c), and lipid profile (total cholesterol, HDL, LDL). Statistical comparisons used Student's t-test or the Mann-Whitney U-test, as appropriate. Effect size was calculated using Cohen's d. Correlations between BAZ and biochemical parameters were analyzed with Pearson's coefficient.
sections.Results: cwCF receiving TT (Group 2) showed a significant 75.7% increase in BAZ (p=0.0087, d=0.65) and a 5.7% reduction in HbA1c (p=0.0003, d=0.47) compared to those on basic therapy (Group 1). The lipid changes presented a dual effect: while beneficial HDL cholesterol increased significantly (16.1%; p=0.0101), there were also concerning rises in both total cholesterol (14.6%; p=0.0024) and LDL cholesterol (16.4%; p=0.0390), respectively. No significant difference was found in fasting glucose (p=0.377), and no strong correlations (|r|<0.26, p>0.1) were detected between BAZ and other metabolic markers.
sections.Conclusion: TT in CF has a dual metabolic effect: improving glycemic control but inducing dyslipidemia. This warrants personalized monitoring of both respiratory and metabolic parameters for timely intervention.

presentations.title: Essential fatty acid imbalance in a paediatric cystic fibrosis population: a single-centre study
presentations.summary: Introduction/Objectives: Essential fatty acids (EFA) imbalance, characterized by increased levels of arachidonic acid (AA) and decreased levels of docosahexaenoic acids (DHA), has been documented in cystic fibrosis (CF). EFA intestinal absorption is particularly compromised in CF due to pancreatic insuficiency. Despite this detrimental pro-inflammatory profile, DHA suplementation is not widely recommended. This study aimed to evaluate EFA levels in a paediatric CF population and look for associations with clinical features. Materials and Methods: Observational, prospective, single-centre study evaluating paediatric patients with CF. Data collected included serum levels of AA, EPA and DHA, inflammatory markers,...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Hospital Pediátrico, ULS Coimbra, Paediatric Gastroenterology and Nutrition Unit, Coimbra, Portugal, 2Serviço de Pediatria, USF Dão Lafões, Viseu, Portugal

sections.Objectives: Essential fatty acids (EFA) imbalance, characterized by increased levels of arachidonic acid (AA) and decreased levels of docosahexaenoic acids (DHA), has been documented in cystic fibrosis (CF). EFA intestinal absorption is particularly compromised in CF due to pancreatic insuficiency. Despite this detrimental pro-inflammatory profile, DHA suplementation is not widely recommended. This study aimed to evaluate EFA levels in a paediatric CF population and look for associations with clinical features. Materials and
sections.Methods: Observational, prospective, single-centre study evaluating paediatric patients with CF. Data collected included serum levels of AA, EPA and DHA, inflammatory markers, pulmonary function, chronic respiratory infections, nutritional status, exocrine pancreatic function and cystic fibrosis hepatobiliary involvement (CFHBI).
sections.Results: The cohort included 27 CF patients, 16 were males, with a mean age of 8.27 years (0.4–16.7). Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency was present in 88.9% of patients and CFHBI in 14.8%. CFTR modulator therapy was used by 66.7% of patients. Mean BMI z-score was −0.23 (−2.22–1.87). Median CRP was 0.05 mg/dL (IQR 0.04–0.14). Most patients presented EFA levels within reference ranges. However, 3 (11%) patients had DHA deficiency; 2 had AA deficiency and 1 had high AA levels. Mean AA/DHA ratio was 4.29 (1.69-9.12). Patients with DHA deficiency had significantly higher AA/DHA ratio (p=0.028). An association between DHA deficiency and CFHBI (p=0.049) was found. No other significant associations between low EFA and clinical or laboratory parameters were found.
sections.Conclusion: Most patients exhibited serum EFA levels within reference ranges, however 3 patients had DHA deficiency. Larger prospective studies may help to determine clinical significance of EFA imbalance and to clarify the benefit of targeted EFA supplementation strategies, particularly in patients with CFHBI.

Method: Files of 20 CF patients evaluated at Developmental Pediatrics outpatient clinic between January 2019 and July 2025 were reviewed. ASQ domain scores, de...

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a multisystemic disease where malnutrition, recurrent infections, and frequent hospitalizations may negatively impact child development. The Ages and Stages Questionnaires (ASQ) are a reliable screening tool designed to evaluate communication, gross motor, fine motor, problem-solving, and personal-social domains. This study aims to examines developmental characteristics of children with CF using the ASQ inventory and investigates relationship with developmental scores and demographic, anthropometric, and clinical data.
sections.Methods: Files of 20 CF patients evaluated at Developmental Pediatrics outpatient clinic between January 2019 and July 2025 were reviewed. ASQ domain scores, demographic, anthropometric, clinical, and laboratory data were recorded. Relationships were analyzed using Spearman correlation; simple and multiple linear regression analyses were performed to identify independent predictors.
sections.Results: In 20 patients (n=11, 55% female), median age was 4.59 years; median BMI was 15.6 kg/m². Developmental delays occurred in fine motor (n=5), problem-solving (n=3), personal-social (n=2), and communication (n=1). Maternal education (rs=0.632, p=0.003) and Vitamin D levels (rs=0.494, p=0.027) positively correlated with problem-solving scores. Daycare attendance linked to higher fine motor scores (p=0.045). Regression identified maternal education (β=0.724, p=0.006) and Vitamin D (β=0.529, p=0.009) as significant predictors of problem-solving. Table 1:Demographic and clinical characteristics of the study group Characteristic Median Min-Max n (%) Gender - Female - Male 11 (55%) 9 (45%) Age (years) 4.5 3 – 6 Body weight (kg) 17.5 12 – 22 Height (cm) 105.5 91 – 119 Body mass index (kg/m²) 15.6 12.9 – 17.9 Age at diagnosis (months) 11 1 – 30 Number of hospitalizations 10-4 Total days of hospitalization 90-26 Maternal age (years) 3425-39 Paternal age (years) 3726-47
sections.Conclusion: The development of children with CF is significantly affected by maternal education level, daycare attendance, and Vitamin D levels. Daycare education, nutritional regulation, and vitamin supplementation play an important role. Early comprehensive developmental assessment and monitoring using tools such as the ASQ are crucial for timely detection of delays and early initiation of supportive interventions.

sections.Objectives: CFTR modulators may improve or restore pancreatic function in young children with CF (cwCF)(1). There is limited evidence on pancreatic recovery and weaning pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT). Following our previous work on changes in PERT and faecal elastase-1 (FE-1) in cwCF aged 2-5 years post-elexacaftor/ivacaftor/tezacaftor (ETI) at 3 months, we sought to review changes in PERT through to 1 year.
sections.Methods: We retrospectively collected the following data: genotype, FE-1(µg/g), average daily PERT dose, anthropometry and prior modulator (PM) use in cwCF aged 2-5 years at baseline and 3 monthly intervals post-ETI. Pancreatic insufficiency (PI) was classified as (µg/g stool): <100 severe, 100-200 borderline, >200 pancreatic sufficient. High PERT dose >10,000 as per ECFS guideline(2), moderate PERT dose 3,000-9,999 and <3,000 low dose (units/lipase/kg). Data were analysed using PRISM, comparisons by Wilcoxon signed rank test.
sections.Results: 29 cwCF were included: 17 homozygous dF508, 11 heterozygous dF508 and one other. 17 were PM. All had severe PI and were taking PERT at baseline. Median (IQR) age 4.4 (3.3-5.15) years at ETI initiation. Table: PERT doses pre-post ETI Average daily PERT doses (units/lipase/kg)Baseline (n=29)3 months (n=29)6 months (n=29)1 year (n=28)Median (IQR) *p <0.057,518 (6,411-10,625)6,018* (3,544-8250)6,250* (3,628-8076)6,085* (4,349-7,380)High Moderate Low7 22 02 23 43 21 51 23 4 There was a statistically significant reduction in PERT doses at all time points compared to baseline. At 6 and 12 months improvements in FE-1 were seen in some children, however only one was able to stop PERT at 12 months. BMI z-score remained optimal.
sections.Conclusion: We showed a reduction in average daily PERT doses of 1,433 units/lipase/kg in this cohort of young children at 1 year post-ETI, without compromising growth. While this reduction was statistically significant, it remains unclear if it is clinically significant as only one patient was able to stop PERT. Attempts are being made to standardise FE-1 monitoring and PERT adjustments with ETI, however our data suggests further work is needed and an individualised approach may be required. Davies JC et al. Lancet Resp Med 2016 Wilschanski M et al. Clin Nutr 2024

sections.Objectives: CFTR modulators may improve Fat Soluble Vitamin (FSV) absorption with risk of high levels (1). We investigated change in serum FSV levels A, D and E, and CF specific supplementation in a real world population of young children with Cystic Fibrosis (cwCF) after initiation of Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI).
sections.Methods: Retrospective data review was performed in pancreatic insufficient patients aged 2-5 years at baseline, 3, 6 and 12-months post ETI initiation. Routine non-fasting serum FSV levels taken when clinically stable were analysed against local reference ranges. Data collected also included genotype, prior modulator (PM) use and CF specific FSV supplementation dosing at baseline and 12-months. Data was analysed using PRISM.
sections.Results: Twenty nine children were included 17 homozygous dF508, 11 heterozygous dF508, 1 other. Median (IQR) age 4.4 (3.3-5.2) y at ETI initiation. 17 were PM. Vitamin E peaked at 12-months. Vitamin D levels fluctuated with statistical significance between baseline to 6-months. Standard and reactive adjustments were made to CF specific FSV supplementation between baseline and 12-months.Vitamin A dosing decreased by 806.9 IU, Vitamin E and D dosing increased by 21.5 IU and 322.3 IU respectively.
sections.Conclusion: In this population of cwCF aged 2-5 years increases in serum FSV levels were seen despite adjustments to CF specific supplementation. Vitamin A levels remained high particularly at 6-months despite this. Results should be interpreted in context of a small sample size but indicate younger cwCF should be considered for lower dosing of CF specific or generic supplementation, and require routine monitoring of FSV levels especially at 3 and 6-months post initiation of ETI. Updated guidelines on starting doses in modulator eligible cwCF should be considered. Crehua-Gaudiza,E,(2025) JPGN

Results: Twenty-seven patients (66% male, mean age 12.2±5.2 years) were included. Nineteen patients (70%) received elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor + ivacaftor and the remaining patients received ivacaftor. BMI Z-score improved from −1.19±0.19...
presentations.authors_text: F.G. Ersan1, A.T. Aslan1, A.B. Cetinkaya1, T. Sismanlar Eyuboglu1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Gazi University Faculty of Medicine, Pediatric Pulmonology, Ankara, Turkey

sections.Objectives: CFTR modulators improve pancreatic function and may increase fat-soluble vitamin levels in cystic fibrosis (CF). This study aimed to evaluate changes in vitamin levels in patients receiving CFTR modulator therapy.
sections.Methods: CF patients receiving modulator therapy for ≥3 months were retrospectively analyzed. Vitamin levels, BMI Z-score, sweat chloride, pulmonary function, fecal elastase, and bone mineral density (BMD) were assessed at baseline, 3 and 12 months.
sections.Results: Twenty-seven patients (66% male, mean age 12.2±5.2 years) were included. Nineteen patients (70%) received elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor + ivacaftor and the remaining patients received ivacaftor. BMI Z-score improved from −1.19±0.19 to −0.72±0.97 and −0.52±1.10 at 3rd and 12th months (p<0.001, p=0.03). Sweat chloride decreased from 83.6±17.3 to 67.5±16.2 and 53.7±13.7 mmol/L (both p<0.001). FEV₁ Z-score increased from −1.18±1.76 to −0.06±1.32 and −0.06±1.45(p=0.02, p=0.04). Median fecal elastase increased from 41.6 to 176.6 µg/g (p=0.286). Median vitamin A levels increased significantly from 0.32 to 0.47 and 0.40 mg/L (p=0.05, p=0.014). Vitamin E showed a non-significant increase, while vitamin D and INR remained unchanged. Vitamin levels changes were not associated with modulator type. Vitamin supplementation was reduced in 15% of patients. BMD Z-scores did not change significantly.
sections.Conclusion: CFTR modulator therapy was associated with a significant increase in vitamin A levels in patients with CF. Although vitamin E levels showed a numerical increase, this was not statistically significant. Close monitoring of vitamin levels in CF patients receiving modulator therapy is essential to prevent unnecessary supplementation and to reduce the risk of hypervitaminosis.

sections.Objectives: To compare body composition in CF patients receiving standard and pathogenetic therapy.
sections.Methods: The study included 64 CF patients over 18 years of age; 40 of them received targeted therapy.
sections.Results: In the targeted therapy group (TTG), men had a BMI of 21.4 kg/m2 (15-32.8 kg/m2), with BMI below 22 kg/m2 = 68%; women had a BMI of 19 kg/m2 (16.6-24.8 kg/m2), with BMI below 22 kg/m2 = 81%. 52% of men had low FFM (fat-free mass) values; 24% of women had a FFM deficiency. %FM (the ratio of body fat to body weight) was normal in 58% of men, exceeded normal range in 32%, and was low in 10%. Among women, normal %FM values were observed in 62% of female patients, exceeded the norm in 33%, and low values were observed in 5% of patients. In the non-target group (NTTG): BMI in men = 18 kg/m2 (14.6 - 22 kg/m2), less than 22 kg/m2 in 92%; BMI in women 19.5 kg/m2 (16.4 - 31.2 kg/m2), less than 21 kg/m2 in 75%. 37% of men have low FFM values; in women, 16% have a FFM deficiency. The average %FM value in men was 11.8% (4.7 - 17.4%). 77% of men had normal %FM values, while 23% had low values. The average %FM value in women was 26.9% (17 - 46%). Among women, 56% had normal %FM values, 32% had excess values, and 12% had low values. In TTG Waist-to-Hip Ratio (WHR) ratio in men = 0.9 (0.79-1.08), WHR ratio in women = 0.82 (0.72-0.96). In NTTG WHR ratio in men was 0.88 (0.8 - 1.07). The average WHR ratio in women was 0.78 (0.66 - 1.08), indicating an increase in visceral fat compared to non-targeted patients.
sections.Conclusion: These data indicate an increasing risk of metabolic disorders during targeted therapy, necessitating adjustments of dietary recommendations.

presentations.title: NutriActe: evaluation of the self-management of fat-soluble vitamin levels by a nutritionist at a paediatric cystic fibrosis clinic in Quebec, Canada.
presentations.summary: Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) often causes pancreatic insufficiency and malabsorption of fat-soluble vitamins. Monitoring levels is vital for growth and bone health, yet results may take as much as 15 days, requiring later follow-up. This study evaluates the impact of systematic review by a nutritionist to adjust vitamin supplements at a pediatric CF clinic. Since 2019, the newly updated activities for nutritionists in Québec in 2019 includes the ability to prescribe vitamins and minerals. Methods: We reviewed pediatric CF charts for vitamins A, D, and E levels during pre- (PRE) (June 2018–May 2019) and post-implementation period (POST) (June 2024–May 2025), excluding the pandemic period. The primary object...
presentations.authors_text: C. Beaudry1, P. Daigneault2, L. Gregori3, C. Héroux3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1CHU de Québec-Université Laval, Department of Nutrition, Québec, Canada, 2CHU de Québec-Université Laval, Department of Pediatrics, Québec, Canada, 3CHU de Québec-Université Laval, Department of Pharmacy, Québec, Canada

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) often causes pancreatic insufficiency and malabsorption of fat-soluble vitamins. Monitoring levels is vital for growth and bone health, yet results may take as much as 15 days, requiring later follow-up. This study evaluates the impact of systematic review by a nutritionist to adjust vitamin supplements at a pediatric CF clinic. Since 2019, the newly updated activities for nutritionists in Québec in 2019 includes the ability to prescribe vitamins and minerals.
sections.Methods: We reviewed pediatric CF charts for vitamins A, D, and E levels during pre- (PRE) (June 2018–May 2019) and post-implementation period (POST) (June 2024–May 2025), excluding the pandemic period. The primary objective was to compare the time interval between the release of vitamin panel results and management by the care team, defined as the point when a team member reviewed the complete vitamin panel. Secondary outcomes comprised the global rate of follow-up and the rate of follow-up following abnormal findings.
sections.Results: A total of 140 patients with CF were included: 107 in PRE and 98 in POST. These patients accounted for 175 and 142 samples for each study periods respectively. The average time for result management decreased from 28.2 days in PRE to 7.0 days in POST (P<0.0001). Rate of follow-up within 4 months of sampling increased from 42% in PRE compared to 91% in POST (P<0.0001). In PRE, 138 samples contained at least one abnormal value compared to 89 in POST; of these, 46% versus 89% were managed. During PRE, management was performed in 25.7% of cases by a physician, 5.1% by a nutritionist, and 10.9% by a pharmacist, compared to 1.4%, 88.7%, and 4.2%, respectively, during POST.
sections.Conclusion: Analysis of both study periods indicates a marked improvement in the management of vitamin panel results for patients in our CF clinic. This improvement coincides with the 2019 expansion of nutritionists’ scope of practice in Québec, which introduced new allowable activities.

presentations.title: Is sweat test a reliable marker for adjusting sodium supplementation in CF patients treated with modulators?
presentations.summary: In the era of CFTR modulators, optimizing sodium intake in patients with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) remains a major clinical challenge. Excessive supplementation may cause long-term complications, while discontinuation increases the risk of dehydration. Sweat test (ST), historically used for diagnosis, varies across individuals and genotypes and could potentially guide salt intake. This study aimed to assess whether ST correlates with tools used to estimate sodium requirements in these patients. Methods: We conducted a retrospective analysis of sodium intake, urinary ionograms, and ST measurements in adult and pediatric patients followed at the Montpellier CF Reference Center. Correlation analyses, as well as sens...
presentations.authors_text: H. Attew1, A. Coudrat1, R. Theiler1, M. Argenson1, D. Trioleyre1, J. Genuardi1, J. Tenenbaum2, S. Ramirez1, E. Lamour1, F. Socchi1, R. Chiron1,3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1CHU de Montpellier, CRCM/Hopital Arnaud de Villeneuve, Montpellier, France, 2CHU de Montpellier, Pédiatrie Spécialisée / Hopital Arnaud de Villeneuve, Montpellier, France, 3Université de Montpellier, HydroSciences Montpellier, CNRS, IRD, Montpellier, France

sections.Methods: We conducted a retrospective analysis of sodium intake, urinary ionograms, and ST measurements in adult and pediatric patients followed at the Montpellier CF Reference Center. Correlation analyses, as well as sensitivity and specificity tests, were performed to explore the relationship between ST (including its change before and after ETI therapy) and parameters used to estimate sodium needs based on French HAS recommendations for healthy populations.
sections.Results: No significant correlation was observed between post-ETI sweat tests (or its pre/post-ETI delta), salt intake, percentage of sodium needs covered, or urinary Na/K ratio (P > 0.05) in our cohort. However, we also confirm that ST variation differed by mutation class, with a smaller decrease observed in heterozygous class III mutations.
sections.Conclusion: In CF patients treated with CFTR modulators, ST does not appear to be a reliable marker for evaluating or adjusting sodium needs. Recommendations should continue to rely on a comprehensive clinical and biological assessment rather than on sweat chloride alone.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1CHU de Lille, Service de Diabétologie/Nutrition, Lille, France, 2CHU de Lille, Service de Pneumologie, Lille, France, 3CHU de Lille, CRCM, Lille, France

sections.Methods: We conducted a single-center observational study in adult CF patients (≥18 years) treated with ETI for at least 12 months at Lille University Hospital, France. All patients carried at least one ETI-responsive mutation. Data of annual report on respiratory function, nutritional status, body composition (assessed by bioimpedance analysis), pancreatic function, and diabetes were collected at baseline and 12 months later.
sections.Results: A total of 49 patients (mean age ± SD, 32.5±10.5 years; 26.5% without the F508del variant) were included. After one year of ETI therapy, sweat chloride levels decreased by a median [Q1 – Q3] of -43 [-73.5 – -22.5] mEq/L (p<0.001) and amylase levels by -13 [-30.0 – -2.5] U/L (<0.001). FEV1 (force expired volume in 1 second) improved by +14.3 ± 10.7% of the predicted value, and the FEV1/FVC (forced vital capacity) ratio by +3.4±7.2% (p<0.01). The number of pulmonary exacerbations decreased (p < 0.001), and body weight increased by +4.8±4.1 kg, with a BMI increase of +1.7±1.4 kg/m² (p<0.001). Hand grip strength improved by +2 [-2 – 6] kg (p=0.008), as did fat-free mass, albumin and transthyretin levels but also fat mass, and total cholesterol levels (p<0.001). These benefits were comparable between patients with and without F508del variant.
sections.Conclusion: ETI therapy should not be limited to patients with the F508del variant. Although effective across all disease aspects, patients should be aware of the impact of ETI on body fat mass, cholesterol levels and its potential long-term consequences especially in these population where cardiovascular risk is not yet well known.

presentations.title: Triangulation of findings from patients and healthcare professionals on challenges in eating behaviours and evaluation of potential utility of mindful eating practices in supporting healthy eating.

To explore barriers and facilitators to using mindful eating (ME) practices to support healthy eating behaviours. Methods: Triangulation was conducted across four data sources: patient evaluation of ME practices after a 5-day intervention (n=10), patient interviews (n=5), HCP ME workshops/focus groups (n=37), HCP ME practices evaluation (n=13). Each dataset was analysed independently and then compared using a triangulation matrix to identify convergence, complementarity, dissonance and silence. These comparisons were used to develop integrated meta-t...
presentations.authors_text: H. Egan1, M. Miley1, R. Massey-Chase2, K.-L. Watson2, R. Keyte1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Birmingham City University, Psychology and Counselling, Birmingham, United Kingdom, 2Kings College Hosptial, Adult Cystic Fibrosis Service, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: To explore how patients and Health care professionals (HCP) understand and experience challenges in providing and receiving eating behaviours support. To explore barriers and facilitators to using mindful eating (ME) practices to support healthy eating behaviours.
sections.Methods: Triangulation was conducted across four data sources: patient evaluation of ME practices after a 5-day intervention (n=10), patient interviews (n=5), HCP ME workshops/focus groups (n=37), HCP ME practices evaluation (n=13). Each dataset was analysed independently and then compared using a triangulation matrix to identify convergence, complementarity, dissonance and silence. These comparisons were used to develop integrated meta-themes.
sections.Results: Findings from patients and HCP converged on the understanding that changing legacy eating behaviours is challenging due to perceptions of higher weight as being safer, lack of experience and knowledge around weight management, unpreparedness for weight changes and medicalisation of eating behaviours. There was some dissonance around perceived reasons for weight gain and the extent to which these were attributable to changed eating behaviours. Both HCP and patients reported that further training was needed to understand and utilise ME practices effectively. Evaluations of ME practices were mixed, patients found considering levels of hunger and satiety before eating helpful, but focusing on the taste and texture of food unhelpful as it reduced social aspects and pleasure in eating.
sections.Conclusion: Synthesising HCP and patient perceptions of eating behaviours informs more effective practice to support weight regulation, behaviour change and understanding of the sustained emotional impact of legacy eating behaviours. Constructs of ME such as non-judgement and acceptance aid healthier eating and wellbeing. HCP report needing practical training, resources and case-study examples to increase confidence and skills in delivering psychosocial support to patients.

presentations.title: Audit of magnesium levels in UK paediatric cystic fibrosis patients: assessing the clinical value of routine testing
presentations.summary: Introduction: The prevalence and clinical significance of hypomagnesemia in paediatric cystic fibrosis (CF) patients remains unclear. There is variation in CF guidelines regarding recommendation for routine Magnesium (Mg) monitoring, raising questions about its value in clinical practice. Furthermore, the impact of the introduction of Highly Effective Modulator Therapy (HEMT) on Mg monitoring requires consideration. This audit aims to evaluate Mg levels in paediatric CF patients and determine if there is clinical justification for routine testing of patient’s Mg levels. Method: We conducted a retrospective audit of all Mg measurements taken from paediatric CF patients at Alder Hey Children’s Hospital over a 10...
presentations.authors_text: C.J. Woodland1, C.A. Berry1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Alder Hey Children's NHS Trust, Nutrition and Dietetics, Liverpool, United Kingdom

sections.Methods: We conducted a retrospective audit of all Mg measurements taken from paediatric CF patients at Alder Hey Children’s Hospital over a 10-year period. The audit assessed the frequency of abnormal results, both elevated and reduced Mg levels. Data from neonates who were receiving parental nutrition (PN) was not included. Ninety eight patients had data audited with eighty nine included in study results. Nine patients were excluded: those with a Cystic Fibrosis Screen Positive Inconclusive Diagnosis (CFSPID) (n=5). ; patients receiving PN for prolonged periods (n-1) and patients that had missing Mg data (n=3).
sections.Results: Across the 10-years, 775 Mg levels were collated from the included patients. Of these, 42 (5%) were below the reference range and 4 (0.5%) were above. Notably, only 7 of the 42 low results (16%) occurred after initiation of HEMT.
sections.Conclusion: Mg abnormalities were infrequent in the paediatric CF cohort, with only 5% of measurements below and 0.5% above the reference range. No treatment for these abnormal levels was commenced. The low prevalence, suggests that routine Mg monitoring may offer limited clinical benefit especially in an increasingly healthy CF population on HEMT. Targeted testing based on individual risk factors may be a more appropriate approach.

presentations.summary: Objectives: Medical traumatic stress refers to the psychological and physiological responses to pain, injury, serious illness, medical procedures and invasive or frightening treatment experiences. It is associated with decreased medication adherence and impaired relationships with the clinical team. People living with cystic fibrosis face a lifetime of illness related experiences that have the potential to be traumatic. Eye-Movement Desensitisation and Reprocessing (EMDR) is a widely used evidenced based therapy in the treatment of Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder and other anxiety based conditions. This presentation aims to explore the utility of EMDR as a treatment for symptoms of medical trauma in adults livi...
presentations.authors_text: R. Jamieson1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1NHS Lothian, East of Scotland Adult Cystic Fibrosis Service, Edinburgh, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Medical traumatic stress refers to the psychological and physiological responses to pain, injury, serious illness, medical procedures and invasive or frightening treatment experiences. It is associated with decreased medication adherence and impaired relationships with the clinical team. People living with cystic fibrosis face a lifetime of illness related experiences that have the potential to be traumatic. Eye-Movement Desensitisation and Reprocessing (EMDR) is a widely used evidenced based therapy in the treatment of Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder and other anxiety based conditions. This presentation aims to explore the utility of EMDR as a treatment for symptoms of medical trauma in adults living with cystic fibrosis.
sections.Methods: Adults living with cystic fibrosis who presented to the psychology service with symptoms of medical traumatic stress were offered EMDR as a treatment option. EMDR was carried out either in person or online via video call, according to patient preference. Symptoms of generalised anxiety, post-traumatic stress and mood were assessed by standardised self report measures before commencing treatment, and again on completion of therapy. Qualitative feedback on the acceptability of the therapy was also gathered on completion.
sections.Results: EMDR treatment led to a reduction in anxiety and trauma related symptoms for most patients. It also led to an improvement in mood for some patients. Patients often reported increased engagement with healthcare, and they described an improved ability to advocate for themselves in the healthcare setting and in their personal lives.
sections.Conclusion: Results suggest that EMDR is a viable treatment option for symptoms of medical traumatic stress, leading to a reduction in symptoms of trauma and anxiety for most. Patients found EMDR to be an acceptable treatment option, and overall reported that the therapy was a challenging and worthwhile process.

Results: T...
presentations.authors_text: S. Graziano1, C. Di Savino1, A.L Quittner2, F. Alghisi3, A. Boni3, E. Montemitro3, S. Vicari1, R. Cutrera3, D. Menghini1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Bambino Gesù Children’s Hospital, IRCCS, Psychology Unit, Child & Adolescent Psychiatry Unit, Rome, Italy, 2Joe DiMaggio Hospital, Cystic Fibrosis, Pulmonary & Sleep Center, Hollywood, United States, 3Bambino Gesù Children’s Hospital, IRCCS, Cystic Fibrosis and Pulmonology Unit, Rome, Italy

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) therapy was extended to children with CF aged 6–11 years in October 2022. ETI is associated with clinical improvements and enhanced health-related quality of life. While potential mental health side effects have been reported in adults, data in pediatric populations remain limited.
sections.Methods: Children eligible for ETI and their parents completed longitudinal assessments during routine visits at baseline (T0), 3-mos (T1), 6-mos (T2), 12-mos (T3) after ETI. Measures: a) physical parameters (FEV₁, BMI); b) HRQoL (CFQ-R); c) mental health (PSC-17); d) parental depression (PHQ-9) and anxiety (GAD-7); and e) ad hoc neurocognitive side-effects questionnaire.
sections.Results: This study included 45 children (F/M=23/22; M age=8.1 years; M FEV₁=100, SD=15.4; M=BMI 16.9, SD=3.8). FEV₁ remained stable across all time points, while BMI showed a significant increase at 12-mos (T0<T3; p<.001). Parents reported improvements in CFQ-R: Body Image (T1<T3; p<.05), Health Perceptions (T0<T3; p<.05), and Respiratory Symptoms Functioning (T0>T1–T3; p<.001) in their children. Treatment Burden remained low, indicating substantial burden (range 59–66). On the PSC, attention problems were reported most frequently (9–19%). The percentage of patients with internalizing problems and attention difficulties increased from T0 to T1 (4 to 10 and 16 to 19, respectively). Neurocognitive side-effects were reported at all assessment points, including irritability (48–59%), agitation/hyperactivity (41–59%), concentration difficulties (50–59%), insomnia (16–25%), and headache (34–50%). No significant improvements over time were found.
sections.Conclusion: Overall, ETI was associated with significant improvements across physical domains. Attention difficulties, agitation/hyperactivity, and irritability were common in children receiving ETI. These findings confirm the need for systematic monitoring of ETI effects over time.

Methods: A REDCap survey was developed by the CFF Mental Health Advisory Committee (Research) and emailed to CF providers by the CF Foundation May 1, 2025. Results: Survey...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Joe DiMaggio Cystic Fibrosis, Pulmonary and Sleep Center, Pulmonary Department, Hollywood, United States, 2Jacobs School of Medicine and Biomedical Scien, Buffalo, United States, 3Bambino Gesu Children's Hospital, Psychology Unit, Child & Adolescent Psychiatry Unit, Rome, Italy, 4Amsterdam University Medical Center, Child & Adolescent Psychiatry & Psychosocial Care, Amsterdam, Netherlands, 5University of Colorado, Denver, United States, 6Children's Hospital of Richmond, Pediatrics, Richmond, United States, 7Community Partner, Northville, United States, 8Cystic Fibrosis Foundation, Bethesda, United States, 9Maine Health, Center for Interdisciplinary Population and Health Research, Westbrook, United States

sections.Objectives: People with cystic fibrosis (PWCF) face a lifetime of potentially traumatic, illness-related experiences including medical visits, lab tests and invasive procedures (PICC lines, surgeries). These can cause symptoms of trauma and medical traumatic stress (MTS), such as negative mood, anxiety, and hyperarousal which may lead to decreased adherence, avoidance of procedures, and worse mental health (MH). This study assessed knowledge, training, clinical practices and barriers to addressing MTS in a national US sample of CF providers.
sections.Methods: A REDCap survey was developed by the CFF Mental Health Advisory Committee (Research) and emailed to CF providers by the CF Foundation May 1, 2025.
sections.Results: Surveys were completed by 285 providers. Respondents were from pediatric (40%), adult (32%) and combined (28%) centers s (20%), and included multidisciplinary providers: mental health (36%) respiratory/physical therapists (16%) and others (8%). Over 90% agreed that nearly all PWCF experience MTS, that providers should routinely assess for symptoms and teach coping skills. Most recognized MTS signs and symptoms (69%), but fewer knew about prevention strategies (48%) or screening tools (25%). Knowledge gaps were identified in scientific basis of MTS, trauma reactions, basic interventions and trauma-informed care (TIC). Among MH providers, the main barriers were limited resources and time (%). For other providers, barriers included insufficient training (>93%), limited time (physicians 98%, nurses 76%), perceptions that TIC was outside their scope (physicians 75%, nurses 73%, RT/PTs 54%). Personal distress was a barrier for 37% of non-MH providers. Half of respondents had evidence-based training in TIC, however, nearly all (99%) wanted additional training.
sections.Conclusion: Providers widely recognized the importance of MTS in CF care but lacked effective training and resources. Robust TIC education and systematic support are essential to improve care.

presentations.title: Correlation of substance use among adolescent cystic fibrosis patients with mental health screenings using CRAFFT, PHQ-9, and GAD-7 screening tools
presentations.summary: Objectives: To assess correlations between PHQ-9 and GAD-7 mental health screening scores with CRAFFT scores indicating problematic substance use behaviours. Methods: Participants diagnosed with Cystic Fibrosis ages 15-20 (n=40) were recruited over a 4-month period. Substance use was characterised using the CRAFFT survey, with scores categorised as negative (0-1) or positive (>2). The GAD-7 and PHQ-9 are validated screening tools for generalised anxiety disorder and depression in adolescents, with scores characterised as minimal (0-4), mild (5-9), moderate (10-14) and severe (>15). Associations between CRAFFT, PHQ-9, and GAD-7 were analysed using Spearman’s rank correlation test. Statistical analyses were perf...
presentations.authors_text: S. Hill, B.S.1, C. Fowler, M.D.2, H. Searcy, Pharm.D.2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1UAB Heersink School of Medicine, Birmingham, United States, 2UAB Medicine, Pediatric Pulmonology, Birmingham, United States

sections.Objectives: To assess correlations between PHQ-9 and GAD-7 mental health screening scores with CRAFFT scores indicating problematic substance use behaviours.
sections.Methods: Participants diagnosed with Cystic Fibrosis ages 15-20 (n=40) were recruited over a 4-month period. Substance use was characterised using the CRAFFT survey, with scores categorised as negative (0-1) or positive (>2). The GAD-7 and PHQ-9 are validated screening tools for generalised anxiety disorder and depression in adolescents, with scores characterised as minimal (0-4), mild (5-9), moderate (10-14) and severe (>15). Associations between CRAFFT, PHQ-9, and GAD-7 were analysed using Spearman’s rank correlation test. Statistical analyses were performed with significance defined as p<0.05.
sections.Results: Of 40 patients surveyed, with a mean age of 17.7±1.66, 42.5% screened positive for any substance use in the past year. The most common substances used were alcohol (30%) and marijuana (28%). Of those who reported substance use in the past year, 47% reported polysubstance use. 20% of patients surveyed recorded CRAFFT scores of >2. A moderate positive monotonic relationship between increasing GAD-7 scores and increasing CRAFFT scores was found with a Spearman’s rank correlation coefficient of 0.455 and p<0.05. No other statistically significant correlations between mental health screening tools and the CRAFFT screening tools were found.
sections.Conclusion: There is notable substance use among adolescent CF patients and implementing screening tools could help those at-risk for problematic substance use. There was a moderate positive monotonic relationship between increasing GAD-7 scores and increasing CRAFFT scores. More research is needed to determine the full scope of the correlation between mental health disorders and substance use among adolescent CF patients. CF adolescents need to be screened for substance use and mental health separately to determine risk level for anxiety, depression, and substance misuse.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Marmara University, Division of Paediatric Pulmonology, Istanbul, Turkey, 2Marmara University, Selim Coremen Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Istanbul, Turkey

sections.Objectives: To evaluate procedural and general anxiety levels in children with cystic fibrosis and to explore age-related patterns of anxiety in routine clinical care.
sections.Methods: Children aged 0–18 years with cystic fibrosis followed at the Paediatric Pulmonology Cystic Fibrosis Centre, were included. Procedural anxiety was assessed using the Children’s Fear of Medical Procedures Scale (CFPAS). General anxiety was evaluated using the Spence Children’s Anxiety Scale (SCAS): preschool parent-report (0–6 years), child and parent-report (7–13 years), and self-report (≥14 years). Anxiety scores were analysed across three age groups. CFPAS scores were categorised as low (0–1) or moderate–high (2–3) anxiety.
sections.Results: A total of 147 children with cystic fibrosis were included (76 males, 71 females). Anxiety related to venepuncture, intravenous cannulation and throat swab collection decreased with increasing age, whereas anxiety related to spirometry, procedures under general anaesthesia and bronchoscopy showed an increasing age-related trend. Bronchoscopy was associated with the highest anxiety levels. SCAS assessments demonstrated clinically relevant anxiety across all age groups. In children aged 0–6 years, parent-reported anxiety was identified in approximately 29%. In the 7–13-year age group, anxiety was reported by children in 9.2% of cases, whereas parent-reported anxiety was observed in 49.2%. Among adolescents aged ≥14 years, clinically relevant anxiety was identified in approximately 25% based on self-report. Across age groups, obsessive–compulsive symptoms and fear of physical injury were the most frequently reported anxiety subdomains.
sections.Conclusion: Procedural and general anxiety are common in children with cystic fibrosis and demonstrate age-related patterns. Routine anxiety screening and age-tailored procedural preparation strategies should be integrated into multidisciplinary cystic fibrosis care to improve patient experience and clinical engagement.

presentations.title: The effect of cognitive behavioural therapy in cystic fibrosis: a systematic review
presentations.summary: Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF), in addition to its multisystem involvement and increased life expectancy, is associated with psychological problems such as depression, anxiety, and difficulties coping with chronic illness. Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) is a goal-oriented psychotherapeutic approach that aims to improve emotional and psychological functioning by modifying maladaptive thought patterns and behaviours. However, its effects in this population remain underexplored. This review aimed to synthesise current evidence on CBT-based interventions for managing psychosocial problems associated with cystic fibrosis. Methods: PubMed, PEDro, MEDLINE, Scopus, EMBASE, Web of Science, Google Scholar, and th...
presentations.authors_text: N. Akyazı1, A. Demirel2, S. Bilgin2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Tarsus University, Physiotherapy and Rehabilitation, Mersin, Turkey, 2Hacettepe University, Physiotherapy and Rehabilitation, Ankara, Turkey

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF), in addition to its multisystem involvement and increased life expectancy, is associated with psychological problems such as depression, anxiety, and difficulties coping with chronic illness. Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) is a goal-oriented psychotherapeutic approach that aims to improve emotional and psychological functioning by modifying maladaptive thought patterns and behaviours. However, its effects in this population remain underexplored. This review aimed to synthesise current evidence on CBT-based interventions for managing psychosocial problems associated with cystic fibrosis.
sections.Methods: PubMed, PEDro, MEDLINE, Scopus, EMBASE, Web of Science, Google Scholar, and the Cochrane Library were searched from inception to 2025 using MeSH terms. Study selection, data extraction, risk of bias assessment, and methodological quality assessment were conducted independently by two reviewers. The study protocol was registered in PROSPERO (CRD420251016152).
sections.Results: A total of 6,499 records were identified in the initial search. Following screening and duplicate removal, three randomised controlled trials were included. Despite the limited number of studies, strong evidence supported the effectiveness of CBT-based interventions in reducing depressive symptoms in patients with CF. Evidence regarding the effects on anxiety and coping skills was limited. Furthermore, strong evidence indicated that CBT-based interventions had no significant effect on quality of life or pulmonary function.
sections.Conclusion: CBT-based interventions appear effective in reducing depressive symptoms in children and adolescents with CF. However, further studies are required to clarify their effects on coping skills, anxiety, quality of life, and pulmonary function. Keywords: cystic fibrosis; cognitive behavioural therapy; depression; anxiety; coping; quality of life; pulmonary function.
