Methods: In this retrospective observational study, 34 pwCF aged 12–21 years (median 17.3) initiating ETI between 2020 and 2022 were included. SC and FEV₁ were measured at baseline and annually over four years (VK1-4). Psychological distress was screened annually using the PHQ-9 (score ≥7). Adherence was assessed by MPR (1.0 = full medication su...
presentations.authors_text: A. Moeser1,2, M. Pavlova1, N. Beiersdorf1, K. Erler1, A. Nader1, M. Lorenz1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Jena University Hospital, Department of Pediatrics, Cystic Fibrosis Centre for Children and Adults, Jena, Germany, 2Jena University Hospital, Institute for Infectious Diseases and Infection Control, Jena, Germany

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) sustainably improves lung function (FEV₁), quality of life and sweat chloride (SC) levels in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). Psychological comorbidities may impair treatment adherence. This study assessed ETI adherence using SC and medication possession rate (MPR) and evaluated the impact of psychological distress.
sections.Methods: In this retrospective observational study, 34 pwCF aged 12–21 years (median 17.3) initiating ETI between 2020 and 2022 were included. SC and FEV₁ were measured at baseline and annually over four years (VK1-4). Psychological distress was screened annually using the PHQ-9 (score ≥7). Adherence was assessed by MPR (1.0 = full medication supply). Patients with prior CFTR modulator therapy, insulin-dependent diabetes or organ transplantation were excluded.
sections.Results: Fourteen pwCF (44.1%) showed psychological distress (mean PHQ-9 scores 11.1 vs. 2.8). Median SC decreased significantly from 101.9 mmol/L (SD 9.1) at baseline to 46.8 mmol/L (SD 17.6) in year 1 (p<0.001) and remained stable thereafter. pwCF with psychological distress demonstrated greater SC variability during follow-up, with significantly higher SC levels in VK 4 (p<0.05). In this group, MPR declined significantly from 95.4% to 75.5% (p<0.01). FEV₁ improved significantly in year 1 compared to baseline (+14.1%, p<0.001) and remained stable in both groups.
sections.Conclusion: ETI leads to sustained improvements in lung function and sweat chloride in pwCF. A substantial proportion of patients exhibited psychological distress, which was associated with increased long-term variability in sweat chloride and declining MPR but not lung function. Structured psychological monitoring and targeted interventions may be essential to support adherence and long-term treatment success.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Joe DiMaggio Cystic Fibrosis, Pulmonary and Sleep Center, Pulmonary Department, Hollywood, United States, 2Massachusetts General Hospital, Psychiatry, Boston, United States, 3University of Kansas Medical Center, Kansas City, United States, 4National Jewish Health, Denver, United States, 5Yeshiva University and Albert Einstein College of Medicine, New York, United States, 6Jacobs School of Medicine and Biomedical Scien, Buffalo, United States, 7University of Kansas, Lawrence, United States, 8Community Advisor, Chagrin Falls, United States, 9Florida State University, Orlando, United States, 10Community Partner, Northville, United States, 11Jacobs School of Medicine and Biomedical Sciences, Buffalo, United States

sections.Objectives: A growing literature indicates adults with CF (AWCF) struggle with mental health (MH) challenges in addition to depression and generalized anxiety; however, limited prevalence data exists on these other conditions (e.g., medical traumatic stress {MTS}). Our primary aim is to conduct a national psychometric validation of two newly developed measures: General Mental Health Screener (GEMS-CF) to assess MH comorbidities and CF transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) modulators (GEMS-ModSE) to assess side-effects.
sections.Methods: Three novel recruitment and design features characterize this protocol: 1) purposive sampling in development phase to enhance representativeness of AWCF (yielding 35% minoritized, 27% LGBTQ+, 30% low income) and 2) “planned missing data design” to reduce respondent burden, and 3) testing resilience as a moderator of MH conditions on quality of life. Five CF Centers will enroll 250 AWCF to complete: a Core Battery of measures (e.g., MTS, resilience), with 125 AWCF completing Battery A (e.g., substance misuse, disordered eating), 125 Battery B (e.g., cognition, trauma), and a random sample (n=100) completing test-retest of GEMS-CF and GEMS-ModSE, test of memory and processing speed (SDMT) and the MINI diagnostic interview. Proposed Analyses:1) reliability (Cronbach’s alpha >0.70, test-retest reliability >0.80, convergent validity r’s>0.50, criterion validity Spearman Rho >0.50; 2) higher prevalence of MH comorbidities in AWCF vs community; 3) >75% rating screeners as “very acceptable” and “very helpful;”4) resilience will moderate the negative impacts of mental health symptoms on quality of life.
sections.Results: Recruitment at 5 CF Centers begins in Q1 2026.
sections.Conclusion: GEMS-CF will assess mental health comorbidities not currently addressed by annual MH screening. GEMS-ModSE will identify side-effects for AWCF taking CFTR modulators. These instruments will be disseminated to ECFS CF Centers to enhance mental health screening and treatment.

sections.Objectives: Given the effectiveness of ACT with CF (O’Hayer et al., 2024), to further our aim of making this protocol accessible to all adults with CF, we have designed and implementing and testing an ACT with CF Self-Help Toolkit.
sections.Methods: The Toolkit delivered via electronic platform is offered to 60 adults (18+) with CF, with elevated PHQ-9 and/or GAD-7 score (<4). Participants are randomly assigned to receive the Toolkit immediately or after a six-week delay. Participants complete self-report measures of anxiety, depression, psychological flexibility, cognitive fusion, and quality of life at baseline, after the Toolkit, and three months later. Electronic health record data inform spirometry (FEV1, FVC). Measures of satisfaction and time spent on each component are obtained. The Toolkit comprises 6 sequential modules and associated kernels, one per week including brief (1-3 minute) videos delivered by young adults living with CF, and infographics demonstrating key ACT with CF interventions.
sections.Results: Data collection is ongoing, complete by ECFS. We will examine changes in outcome variables over time, as well as differences between the immediate-access and six-week delay groups. Power calculations were based on a pre-to-post R² of 0.60 between covariates and post-treatment psychological functioning, an attrition rate of 20%, and regression modelling controlling for baseline psychological functioning and FEV1, with treatment group as the variable of interest. We anticipate 80% power to detect a Cohen’s d = 0.85 effect size difference using a sample of 60 participants.
sections.Conclusion: If effective, the ACT with CF Self-Help Toolkit could be a workable alternative to individual psychotherapy for adults with CF who are not ready for, or are unable to access, traditional psychotherapy, due to barriers such as work, childcare, geographical constraints, financial limitations, and a lack of providers trained in empirically supported psychotherapies for living with CF.

sections.Objectives: Elevated rates of depression and anxiety in The International Depression and Anxiety Epidemiological Study (TIDES) led to international guidelines which integrated mental health (MH) screening into CF care. However, TIDES did not include children <12 years. Increased awareness of MH in young children and side-effects of CFTR modulators have increased the urgency for addressing MH concerns in young children. This study (named MindKids-CF in Italy) aims to: 1) evaluate Italian prevalence of depression, anxiety and behavior problems in children 2-11; 2) compare performance of two widely used measures, Pediatric Symptom Checklist (PSC) and PROMIS modules and 3) characterize the neuropsychological side-effects of modulator therapy.
sections.Methods: As adapted from the US TIDES 2.0 protocol, we will enroll 500 children with CF <12 years at 11 Italian CF Centers, generating national prevalence estimates. Parents and children will complete a MH assessment twice at routine visits, separated by 6-9 months: PSC, PROMIS (sleep, cognition, emotional functioning), SDQ for convergent validity, CFQ-R (QOL), CF-PAS (procedural anxiety), modulator side-effects; and parental PHQ-8, GAD-7. Results (expected): A higher prevalence of MH problems will be found in children with CF compared to published norms; PSC and PROMIS will demonstrate adequate reliability (α >.70), sensitivity and specificity (>.80). Prevalence estimates will be generated and the best screener will be identified to update MH guidelines for children < 12 years in the EU.
sections.Conclusion: Use of these screening measures will facilitate development of prevention and intervention efforts for young children to improve daily functioning and quality of life. TIDES 2.0 will provide critical evidence to extend MH screening and care to younger children with CF in the EU and US. Funding: Supported by a grant from the Fondazione per la Ricerca sulla Fibrosi Cistica (FFC# Strategic Project 2025-2028).

sections.Objectives: Behavioural and sleep disturbances are increasingly recognised adverse effects of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI). With ETI licensed from 2 years of age in the UK, distinguishing drug-related effects from developmental or pre-existing behavioural symptoms is challenging.
sections.Methods: We report two paediatric cases where the Paediatric Pre-School/School Symptom Checker (PPSC/PSC) supported objective assessment and decision-making.
sections.Results: Case 1: A girl with cystic fibrosis (CF), homozygous for F508del, commenced ETI at 2 years of age. She had normal growth, neurodevelopment, sleep, and a non-clinical baseline PPSC score. Within weeks she developed irritability, emotional lability, hyperactivity, and sleep disturbance, with a clinically elevated PPSC score (25/36). There were no intercurrent illnesses or psychosocial stressors, and behavioural strategies were ineffective. Symptoms caused family disruption and parental distress, requiring psychological support. ETI cessation led to symptom resolution within two weeks and PPSC normalisation (3/36). Re-challenge at reduced dose caused rapid recurrence, prompting discontinuation. Case 2: A boy with CF, homozygous for F508del, commenced ETI at 6 years of age, having previously tolerated lumacaftor/ivacaftor. He had mild baseline behavioural symptoms (PSC 10/70). Following ETI initiation, behavioural and sleep symptoms worsened (PSC 20/70), unresponsive to behavioural strategies or melatonin. After 4-week ETI withdrawal, symptoms improved (PSC 5/70). ETI was recommenced at half dose with stable behaviour and acceptable sweat chloride (45 mmol/L).
sections.Conclusion: Objective symptom monitoring demonstrated reproducible ETI-associated behavioural adverse effects. These symptoms impact family wellbeing and create difficult decisions around dose reduction or cessation of a life-prolonging therapy. Routine screening, multidisciplinary review, and shared decision-making are essential when initiating ETI in children.

sections.Background: People with CF (pwCF) are living longer. A rising number are overweight, and 20–30% have CF diabetes (CFD). Prior work shows higher cardiovascular (CV) events than in non-CF population (Frost 2023). QRisk3 guides 10-year CV prevention in the general population (Hippisley-Cox 2017). We created a nurse-led pathway using a locally adapted CF-QRisk3 tool.
sections.Methods: We developed a CF-CV multidisciplinary pathway with a Preventative Cardiology Team. CF Nurse Specialists screened pwCF aged 40–84 years (Sept 2025–Jan 2026). QRisk3 tool was modified: rheumatoid arthritis served as a surrogate for CF-chronic inflammation; scores further adjusted for CFD. Eligible pwCF attending in-person CF clinics had non-fasting lipids, HbA1c, thyroid, BP, smoking status, and family history checked. Subsequent interventions advised and high-risk cases (>20% risk CV event in 10 years) were reviewed in the CF-CV MDT.
sections.Results: Overall, 48 pwCF were screened. Nineteen (39.6%) warranted interventions, some multiple, due to high CV risk (Table). Five (10.4%) declined advised management, one stopped statin therapy due to side effects.
sections.Conclusion: A high proportion of pwCF had elevated CF-CV risk warranting investigative and therapeutic intervention. We achieved full assessment uptake, and minimal refusal of interventions. This pathway is feasible for proactive CF specific CV-risk management in liaison with primary care. Screening is ongoing with the CF-CV protocol being part of clinical practice. sections.Background: The National Institute of Clinical Excellence recommended the discontinuation of heparinised saline in all central venous catheters in the UK in 2012, however, this recommendation has not yet been fully embraced in cystic fibrosis (CF) care. Heparinised saline use is associated with risks such as infection, thrombocytopenia, bleeding and interactions with other drugs. Research indicates that 0.9% sodium chloride presents a safer, equally effective, and more cost-efficient option,1 therefore we ceased use of heparinised saline in TIVAD maintenance at University Hospitals Plymouth in February 2024.
sections.Methods: We conducted a retrospective review of TIVAD maintenance and complications.

One patient (p11) reported resistance in their TIVAD following admission at their transplant centre. Linogram was normal and subsequent flushes had no resistance. There was one incident of line infection following home intravenous antibiotic administration (p9).
sections.Conclusion: We found no TIVAD occlusion after 18 months of using 0.9% sodium chloride for TIVAD maintenance, even when patients have had flushes less frequently than advised. Longer studies with larger numbers may be beneficial. During the COVID-19 pandemic flush frequency was reduced in line with shielding, patients have demonstrated a reluctance to return to prior frequency. TIVAD insertion is declining due to modulator therapy significantly reducing the frequency of intravenous antibiotic use. sections.Objectives: At a time of significant transformation in cystic fibrosis (CF) care in the United Kingdom, we explored how workload for CF professionals is evolving in paediatric and adult care settings.
sections.Methods: A bespoke questionnaire was developed with advice from CF professionals and people with CF. Questions were tested and refined in early 2025 with CF professionals not previously involved in development. It was then disseminated via professional networks and Cystic Fibrosis Trust. Responses were collected online March-April 2025 and analysed descriptively by setting, with a complementary thematic analysis of free text comments.
sections.Results: Sixty-nine respondents worked in adult care; 49 worked in paediatrics. The sample included doctors, nurses, physiotherapists, dietitians, and other staff from more than 40 different CF centres and clinics. Table 1: Proportion of respondents who perceived a change in workload since early 2020 in three of 19 areas covered in the questionnaire. Area of work:Inpatient CareVirtual / Remote careInterventionsPerceived changeAdult (n=66)Paediatric (n=47)Adult (n=66)Paediatric (n=38)Adult (n=64)Paediatric (n=47) Increased7.6%2.1%81.8%57.9%59.4%51.1% No change7.6%8.5%9.1%13.2%32.8%34.0% Decreased84.8%89.4%9.1%28.9%7.8%14.9% Qualitative comments confirmed that workload is moving from inpatient settings to more outpatient, virtual and community care. Simultaneously, the focus of care is shifting from lung health to a wider range of issues, such as CF diabetes, gastrointestinal symptoms, mental health, and social wellbeing. In paediatric care, there is a new focus on prevention and healthy living, while adult services are dealing with increasing demand for fertility, pregnancy, and menopause support, and emerging complexities of older age.
sections.Conclusion: In adult and paediatric settings, CF workload is evolving, rather than reducing. Monitoring this evolution will be vital in future to ensure clinical standards and guidelines are updated in line with new needs, innovations, and emerging best practices.

sections.Objectives: The Cork Adult CF Centre (3CF) designed a digital mixed-methods survey to identify key areas where pwCF may require additional information/support, and their preferred formats for receiving it.
sections.Methods: The survey, developed and validated collaboratively with the CF MDT, was distributed via SMS to 180 adult pwCF (pre- and post-transplant) attending 3CF. Responses were anonymised.
sections.Results: 80 pwCF responded to the survey. Table 1 demonstrates topic breakdown, response number, mean ages, positive and negative topic ratings. All 14 topics presented identified in the survey were considered important by respondents. One-to-one sessions were the preferred format for receiving support and information. In specific subgroups certain topics were identified as particularly relevant, e.g. perimenopause and menopause in 16 perimenopausal women (mean age 39). Discussion &
sections.Conclusion: These findings underscore the continued importance of the MDT in reviewing and addressing the evolving holistic needs of pwCF. One-to-one support is preferred. sections.Objectives: To explore cystic fibrosis (CF) professionals’ ideas and priorities for the future at a time of significant transformation due to new treatments and technologies.
sections.Methods: We worked with CF professional and community representatives to co-produce a questionnaire to understand the changing care landscape in CF in the United Kingdom (UK), and to capture ideas for the future. We specifically asked for up to three ideas as well as respondents’ top priority. Responses were gathered online March-April 2025. Free text comments were analysed thematically to identify common themes and sub-themes.
sections.Results: Over 135 top priorities and 350 ideas were shared by 142 CF professionals. Ideas and priorities often aligned, with the latter describing high level ambitions while ideas provided actionable suggestions to achieve said ambitions. Two key themes emerged. Priorities in Theme 1 focused on ensuring equitable access to treatments and care that adapts around individual patients’ needs and seeks to reduce burden. Ideas under this theme included widened access to modulators, enhanced psychosocial support offers, and patient empowerment. To reduce burden, professionals suggested patient-initiated contact, rationalising appointments and tests, moving care to the community, as well as leveraging technology. Theme 2 focused on staff and team development, as well as ways of working, to maintain ability to deliver high quality CF care. Ideas in this theme highlighted opportunities to upskill existing staff in new and emerging areas, and to increase collaboration with primary care and other disciplines. Professionals also felt continued multidisciplinary working was vital.
sections.Conclusion: CF professionals in the UK saw equitable access and person-centred approaches as a top priority to maintain quality of care. Staff development and exploring new ways of working within and beyond the CF team were identified as critical facilitators to enable this.

presentations.summary: Objectives: Values-Based Health Care emphasises the measurement of what matters to patients alongside clinical parameters to deliver meaningful outcomes whilst reducing unnecessary burden. Our adult centre is trialling a digital platform (PerEmpo) designed to facilitate person-centred value and goal setting. sections.Objectives: Values-Based Health Care emphasises the measurement of what matters to patients alongside clinical parameters to deliver meaningful outcomes whilst reducing unnecessary burden. Standardised patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) are often used, assuming uniform priorities, which may not reflect the diverse needs of adults with cystic fibrosis (CF) in the post-modulator era. Our adult centre is trialling a digital platform (PerEmpo) designed to facilitate person-centred value and goal setting.
sections.Methods: At annual review, patients are supported to identify values and goals which matter to them and to rate how much CF is currently getting in the way of these on a 0-5 Likert scale (lower scores represent less negative impact). They also set health management specific goals and rate their attainment of these on a 0-5 Likert scale (higher scores represent greater attainment). Information is stored on a central system (with patient access) and re-rated during their next appropriate clinical contact (approximately 3-monthly intervals).
sections.Results: To date, 18 people have completed the initial stage with the pilot ongoing. Thematic analysis of qualitative information within the goals and values showed three main themes: 1. Personal Wellbeing, including health management and maintaining independence; 2. Social and Leisure, highlighting the importance of relationships, hobbies and travel; 3. Career growth and development, reflecting aspirations for progression. However, within these themes the values and goals were specific to each individual. The median rating of the impact of CF was 2 (range 1-5) and median rating of attainment of CF specific goals was 2 (range 1-5).
sections.Conclusion: This project aims to better understand what matters most to patients to support collaborative decision making about their health and care. Systematic ways of individualising care to ensure high value is particularly important with the growing CF population.

presentations.title: The introduction of a new annual review process for the modern era of cystic fibrosis: a single centre’s experience
presentations.summary: Objectives: The annual review (AR) process for adults with CF at King’s College Hospital comprised a one-day assessment with patients reviewed by each MDT speciality. Imaging scans were requested but not timetabled. Patients found this AR lengthy, repetitive and stressful. Improved health on modulators and a drive to optimise the value delivered led to reviewing of the AR process. Methods: Changes were iterative over 2 years. These included: single point of contact review by CF practitioner, formal MDT AR meeting to discuss each patient (with individual specialty review then arranged as required), scans scheduled, and formalised assessment template sent to GP with feedback summary. Additionally, feedback is di...
presentations.authors_text: K. Channon1, M. Kapatou1, H. Parkinson1, S. Floyd1, R. Heise1, M.D Waller1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1King's College Hospital NHS Foundation Trust, Adult Cystic Fibrosis, London, United Kingdom, 2King's College London, Centre for Human & Applied Physiological Sciences, Faculty of Life Sciences & Medicine, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: The annual review (AR) process for adults with CF at King’s College Hospital comprised a one-day assessment with patients reviewed by each MDT speciality. Imaging scans were requested but not timetabled. Patients found this AR lengthy, repetitive and stressful. Improved health on modulators and a drive to optimise the value delivered led to reviewing of the AR process.
sections.Methods: Changes were iterative over 2 years. These included: single point of contact review by CF practitioner, formal MDT AR meeting to discuss each patient (with individual specialty review then arranged as required), scans scheduled, and formalised assessment template sent to GP with feedback summary. Additionally, feedback is discussed with patients during telephone consultation 6 weeks post-AR. Anonymised patient and staff feedback were collected to evaluate the new process.
sections.Results: In 4-months, 62 AR were completed. From these, 27/62 patients completed a questionnaire. 25/27 rated their AR as extremely useful/useful. 21/27 patients rated the new process as better, citing reasons as “quicker”, “less repetitive”, “more convenient”, “less stressful” and “more joined up”. 5/27 patients rated it as the same. Patients reported the most important aspects at AR were discussing overall health and reviewing their test results. 11/18 members of the MDT completed a feedback questionnaire. They reported the new AR process was notably less repetitive for patients, had clear ownership of plans and feedback, shorter time for patient but less rushed, and MDT AR meeting was useful/very useful.
sections.Conclusion: Overall, patients and staff feel that the new system has less repetition and increased efficiency. Some staff were initially resistant to change but with open conversations and MDT AR meetings this had led to more efficient use of clinical time. We next plan to integrate linking patients’ values and goals from a digital platform (PerEmpo) into their annual review as part of ongoing AR development.

Methods: The CF team developed a specialist emergency on-call service. The model incorporates all clinic...
presentations.authors_text: K. France1, K. Hasney2, J. Ackerley1, M. Robinson2, R. Thomas1, D. Shiferaw2, T. Daniels1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1York Hull Cystic Fibrosis Centre, York, United Kingdom, 2York Hull Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Hull, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: In the UK, CF Centres need to provide 24-hour access to specialist advice as part of NHSE service requirements (NHSE, 2018), CF Trust (CF Trust, 2024) and NICE guidelines (NICE, 2017). Prior to 2020, the CF Centre lacked a dedicated out-of-hours service, relying upon a generic respiratory ward for urgent CF queries. We developed a responsive and safe way of delivering an on-call service, staffed by the specialist CF MDT. A nationwide survey of UK CF centres, highlighting practices and challenges in delivering 24-hour specialist advice, demonstrate that this is a unique and bespoke on-call model.
sections.Methods: The CF team developed a specialist emergency on-call service. The model incorporates all clinical members of the CF MDT. The model provided a direct telephone advice line, enabling clinicians to deliver out-of-hours support. As the service evolved, monthly supervision and review were introduced to refine pathways and optimise procedures. On-call clinicians documented patient interactions. Support included updated standard operating procedures, clinical and patient resources, information cards, and website content.
sections.Results: In 2025, there were 136 calls. 64% of these calls were at the weekends and 36% during the week. Out of these calls, 41% were emergencies, 30% were planned with the remaining 29% were patient or clinician initiated and non-urgent. No adverse events or clinical incidents were reported, highlighting maintenance of safe and effective practice.
sections.Conclusion: This adaptive, multidisciplinary on-call model enables safe and timely access to expert specialist CF care which is scalable and adaptive for emergency service delivery. NHS England (2018) https://www.england.nhs.uk/wp-content/uploads/2018/08/Cystic-fibrosis-adult.pdf Cystic Fibrosis Trust (2024) Standards for the clinical care of children and adults with cystic fibrosis in the UK National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (2017) https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng78.

sections.Background: The CFNA is dedicated to empowering cystic fibrosis nurses through education, resources, and peer support, helping them deliver exceptional care to patients and families. A survey of CFNA members conducted in 2023 highlighted a lack of use of the CFNA website with members stating it was ‘outdated’ and ‘not user friendly’.1 66% of members reported they would like content on social media platforms.
sections.Methods: In response the CFNA committee worked with Amperative® to develop an updated website (www.cfna.org.uk). The new website featured a public area to showcase CFNA’s work and a private members-only forum for discussions, educational resources and regional/national networking. The CFNA also welcomed a ‘social media and content representative’ to the committee. CFNA members were resurveyed in January 2026.
sections.Results: Since July 2024, the forum has grown to 184 members generating 153 posts and generated 354 comments, with acitivty rising monthly. In the last quarter, 16 users contributed 24 posts and 58 comments across diverse topics. Social media accounts were created, Instagram has 91 members, Facebook 74 and X 53. A LinkedIn account has recently been added. The 2026 survey received 47 responses. 96% were aware of the new website, 77% had used the website, comments included ‘User friendly and very interactive’ ‘Much improved’ Usefulness of the forum was rated 4.42 out of 5. CFNA Member forum use20232026Never20%9%Yearly48%15%4-6 times yearly25%47%1-2 times monthly5%23%Weekly2%6% 20232026Aware of Fiona Lomas award58%94%Aware of education grants76%89% Members are happy with the level of presence on social media and enjoy updates via these channels.
sections.Conclusion: Increased visibility and ease of use has positively impacted members. There is an increased awareness of educational grants and awards. Forum use has increased and continues to grow, fostering communication and an increased sense of community among CFNA members. 1CFNA (2024) A new era for CF Nurse Specialists: Rebuilding the Cystic Fibrosis Nursing Association (CFNA) post COVID-19 pandemic. Journal of Cystic Fibrosis, Volume 23, S196 - S197.

Methods: A retrospective review was conducted of all CF suspected and CF carrier NBS notifications managed by the CF nursing team between August and December 2025. Results: Eight infants were identified through NBS: five with suspected CF and three as CF carriers. Follow-up telephone contact occurred within...
presentations.authors_text: L. Wooldridge1, A.-M. Martin1, S. Evans1, L. Churchill1, S. Stone1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Birmingham Women's and Children's NHS Foundation Trust, Cystic Fibrosis, Birmingham, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: To assess the impact of transferring responsibility for Cystic Fibrosis (CF) suspected and CF carrier Newborn Screening (NBS) notifications to the CF nursing team, focusing on workload, timeliness of follow-up, and the ethical challenges.
sections.Methods: A retrospective review was conducted of all CF suspected and CF carrier NBS notifications managed by the CF nursing team between August and December 2025. Data included number and type of notifications, time to family contact, diagnostic outcomes, and contextual factors affecting communication and care delivery.
sections.Results: Eight infants were identified through NBS: five with suspected CF and three as CF carriers. Follow-up telephone contact occurred within 0–5 days, with a mean response time of two days. One family had not been contacted due to a consultant-led decision in the context of planned withdrawal of neonatal care. Most infants notified as suspected CF were subsequently confirmed to have CF; one was designated as CF Screen Positive, Inconclusive Diagnosis (CFSPID). Families of infants identified as CF carriers received appropriate counselling regarding future implications. One additional CF case was identified outside the NBS programme. Despite a substantial increase in workload and the ethical discomfort associated with describing results as “CF suspected” in line with national pathways, as well as the psychological burden of conveying complex and life-changing information by telephone, follow-up remained timely and compliant with NBS standards.
sections.Conclusion: The transfer of NBS notification responsibilities to the CF nursing team has increased workload and highlighted significant ethical and emotional challenges. Compliance with national standards has been maintained, these findings underscore the need for greater recognition of the psychological impact, ethical complexity, and service capacity implications inherent in delivering CF NBS notifications.

presentations.title: Digital transformation of paediatric cystic fibrosis care: a cost-benefit evaluation
presentations.summary: Objectives: Specialized care for children and adolescents with cystic fibrosis (CF) involves complex and resource-intensive care pathways. To optimize workflows, support multidisciplinary teams, and enable structured evaluation, the CF centre in Gothenburg, Sweden implemented the digital care platform Hope. This study assessed the economic impact and cost–benefit of digital tools in CF care. Methods: A before–after evaluation was performed using routinely collected operational data from the CF care team, including healthcare professionals and patients/caregivers, collected in 2023–2024. Benefits were identified using semi-structured national benefit calculation templates linked to defined tasks, volumes, and s...
presentations.authors_text: F. Lundqvist1, M. Svedberg1, V. Mellqvist1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Queen Silvia’s Children’s Hospital, Gothenburg, Sweden

sections.Objectives: Specialized care for children and adolescents with cystic fibrosis (CF) involves complex and resource-intensive care pathways. To optimize workflows, support multidisciplinary teams, and enable structured evaluation, the CF centre in Gothenburg, Sweden implemented the digital care platform Hope. This study assessed the economic impact and cost–benefit of digital tools in CF care.
sections.Methods: A before–after evaluation was performed using routinely collected operational data from the CF care team, including healthcare professionals and patients/caregivers, collected in 2023–2024. Benefits were identified using semi-structured national benefit calculation templates linked to defined tasks, volumes, and stakeholders. For key activities, quantitative time-and-motion studies were conducted before and after implementation, recording minimum, most likely, and maximum time spent. Results are presented as verified benefits observed in clinical practice and as an extended scenario including planned workflow improvements to be implemented from July 2026, with full effect expected in 2027. All costs were converted using an average exchange rate of 1 EUR = 10.75 SEK.
sections.Results: Verified clinical implementation yielded a likely net economic benefit of approximately €746,000 over 2025–2030 (uncertainty interval €740,000–€772,000), with a positive net benefit already in 2025. This corresponds to a return on investment of approximately 2:1 over six years. Including planned workflow improvements from July 2026 increased the estimated net benefit to approximately €1.21 million. The main contributors were reduced staffing needs enabled by digital workflows and substitution of in-person monthly visits with digital video visits, followed by automated national CF registry transfers and more efficient planning and booking.
sections.Conclusion: Implementation of a digital care platform in paediatric CF care was associated with early economic benefits and a positive return on investment.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Trudy Havermans, Leuven, Belgium, 2Katholieke Universiteit Leuven, Leuven, Belgium, 3University Hospitals Leuven, Leuven, Belgium

sections.Objectives: This study reports on the preliminary validation of the Flemish Lung Transplant Health Related QOL survey (FLT-QOL). The aim is to construct a valid Quality of Life (QOL) scale to use during annual outpatient LT clinic to guide and improve multidisciplinary care for lung transplanted people with CF (LTpwCF).
sections.Methods: The Lung Transplant Health-Related Quality of Life (LT-QOL) Survey (Singer et al, 2015) includes 60 items (10 scales measuring: symptoms, health perceptions, functioning, and well-being) and was translated into Flemish using standard translation guidelines. Patients completed the FLT-QOL survey during outpatient clinics as well as the GAD-7, PHQ-9, WHO5 and a Visual Analogue scale (VAS-QOL). Demographic and medical variables were obtained from medical records.
sections.Results: The LT clinic cares for 119 LTpwCF of whom 85 (71%) were stable at the time of the study. 69/85 (81%) participated: mean age 43 (range 23-65); 38 females. Time since LT was 11,9 years (SD=3.5; range 3-26). Mean lung function FEV1%pred. was 79% (SD=19%; range 39-129%); mean was BMI 21 (SD=3.5; range 16-32); and 54 (78%) were diagnosed with CFRD. Cronbach alpha’s for the FLT-QOL scales ranged from 0.76 to 0.96 indicating good internal consistency. High correlations (possible redundancy) were found for FLT-health distress and FLT-worry about future health (r=.82). Correlations between FLT-QOL scales were moderate to good (r=.23 to r=.82). Hypothesized associations between medical variables and FLT scales were weak. Hypotheses regarding concurrent validity were confirmed with significant correlations found between FLT-QOL scales and scores on the GAD7, PHQ9, WHO5 and VAS (r= 0.28-0.84; p<0.05-0.01) (table 1).
sections.Conclusion: This study shows preliminary construct and concurrent validity of the FLT-QOL. Association with medical parameters was weak. Inclusion of the FLT-QOL survey in an annual screening program can provide important additional information of wellbeing in LTpwCF. Methods: We employed an independent researcher to assess the WF programme by reviewing existing service data and internal resources. The evaluator spoke directly with people involved with the programme. This included 12 in-depth interviews with staff, referrers and beneficiaries. Survey responses and feedback were analysed to build a clear picture of the effectiveness and successes of the programme....
presentations.authors_text: G. Silverman1, J. Gregory1, B. Kilgariff1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Cystic Fibrosis Trust, Information, Services and Programmes, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Work Forwards (WF) is an innovative programme, run by the Cystic Fibrosis Trust, supporting people affected by CF into secure and fulfilling employment. A three-year pilot was funded by the National Lottery Community Fund. In 2025, we evaluated the programme to understand its impact, reach and success.
sections.Methods: We employed an independent researcher to assess the WF programme by reviewing existing service data and internal resources. The evaluator spoke directly with people involved with the programme. This included 12 in-depth interviews with staff, referrers and beneficiaries. Survey responses and feedback were analysed to build a clear picture of the effectiveness and successes of the programme.
sections.Results: ​ 2022-2025TargetAchievedOne-to-one advice135265Workshops3030Digital Case studies100110Target Reach70050,035 - 453 direct and 49,582 indirect via resources/communications Video resources on employment rights and workplace support for people affected by CF were co-produced with the community. Beneficiaries valued the support on discussing CF, rights, discrimination, and reasonable adjustments. They all reported increased confidence to have these conversations at work. Feedback showed: 89% feel more confident about steps to find or stay in work that is right for them and move their employment situation forward 93% feel more hopeful about career opportunities. 95% feel satisfied with the support 97% would likely recommend WF to others
sections.Conclusion: WF has delivered a highly successful, pioneering pilot. Across the three-year funded period the programme exceeded targets for one-to-one advice and digital case studies. It produced valued resources including podcasts, employer videos and achieved measurable positive outcomes. Clients reported increased confidence, hope, practical progress in work and improved ability to balance CF and employment. This work has evidenced that there is an ongoing need for specialist, CF-focused, employment support.

In early 2026, we combined all available survey data since 2021, to evaluate the support provided over the past 5 years. Results: People affe...
presentations.authors_text: B. Kilgariff1, M. Delooze1, W. Coates1, L. Horne1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Cystic Fibrosis Trust, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Cystic Fibrosis Trust has offered an enhanced package of winter support since 2021, aiming to protect CF health by targeting financial support at low income households. Between Oct 21 and Mar 25, 1927 grants were provided, to 1222 households. Grant recipients were also offered support and signposting from our welfare team. In 21-22, grants were £500. In subsequent years, grants were £225, usually split over 3 months.
sections.Methods: Each year, we have conducted detailed surveys to understand the impact of the grants and the challenges faced by low income households. In early 2026, we combined all available survey data since 2021, to evaluate the support provided over the past 5 years.
sections.Results: People affected by CF reported positive impacts on their physical and mental health: % strongly agree/agree Average (2021-2025)I have felt less stressed and anxious93%I have felt happier87%I have been less worried about basic costs83%I feel that the grant is helping to keep me / the person with CF well this winter99% Over 89% agreed that without the grant, they would have struggled with essential costs of heating/eating. “It's prevented me from going back into hospital during a period that I was going downhill sat in the cold house.” All CF professionals surveyed felt that the support improved their patients’ physical and mental health. Some commented that this was “a life-line”. 60% reported that they felt the support ‘helped reduce the risk of my patient(s) being admitted to hospital this winter’. Survey data showed the reality of life with CF on a low income: In the past 12 months…% strongly agree/agree Average (2021-2025)Have you found yourself having to choose between buying the food you need or heating your home?59%Have you struggled to buy the basics needed to stay well with CF? (e.g. buying enough food, keeping your home warm)78%Have you gone without basic toiletries or hygiene products because you couldn't afford to buy what you needed?40%Have you missed a hospital or CF clinic appointment because you didn't have enough money to go?24%
sections.Conclusion: These surveys show the precarious nature of life with CF on a low income, with people forced to choose between essentials, go without hygiene products, and miss hospital appointments due to cost. Evidence shows the value of a relatively small financial intervention, supported by information and further support. Professionals and households affected by CF feel this support has improved physical and mental health. This is important for policymakers, charities, and CF teams to consider.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Astana Medical University, Department of Internal Diseases N3, Astana, Kazakhstan, 2Multidisciplinary City Hospital No1, Pulmonology Department, Astana, Kazakhstan

sections.Objectives: To assess anxiety levels and quality of life in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF).
sections.Methods: The study included 31 (15 male) pwCF, aged 23,3 (17 to 45) yrs. Psychoemotional state was assessed using the GAD-7 and quality of life (QoL) - the SF-36 questionnaires,
sections.Results: The lowest QoL scores were observed for role functioning due to emotional state (43,7) and general health (41,1). The highest average scores were observed for the domains of physical functioning (69,1), pain intensity (61,1) and social functioning (56). The life activity domain and physical role functioning domain had similar mean scores of 49,8 and 50 points, respectively. When analyzing the overall data, the physical health component total score was 55,3 (female 55.9; male 51,9) and the psychological health component 50,7 points, respectively (female 52,6; male 53,5). The overall GAD-7 score averaged 7,83 for the patient cohort, female - 9,1 and male 6,5. The highest scores were observed for "excessive worry about various things" – 1,35; "inability to relax" – 1,22. Fewer responses were found for "inability to cope with anxiety" (0,9), "extreme anxiety: "I can't find a place for myself" (0,9) and "fear of something scary" (0,9). Older age was associated with more frequent anxiety symptoms, including irritability (p=0,031), difficulty relaxing (p=0,026), increased susceptibility to anxiety (p=0,009). The GAD-7 total score did not show a statistically significant correlation with age (ρ=0,268; p=0,145).
sections.Conclusion: Adult pwCF demonstrate a heterogeneous, generally reduced QoL, the presence of anxiety symptoms of varying severity. Older age is associated with increased anxiety manifestations. These findings suggest the need of a comprehensive approach to managing pwCF, including psychosocial support along with basic therapeutic options.

presentations.title: Self-Identity for adolescents and adults with cystic fibrosis: a qualitative synthesis
presentations.summary: Objectives: People with cystic fibrosis, a typically childhood-onset condition, develop their identity alongside their illness. While identity coherence supports wellbeing and adjustment, cystic fibrosis, demands may disrupt this process. Research on identity in cystic fibrosis, is fragmented, with findings often embedded in broader topics. This review aimed to synthesised qualitative studies to provide a coherent understanding of identity in adolescents and adults with cystic fibrosis. Methods: A systematic search of seven databases identified 34 eligible studies, which were analysed using thematic synthesis. Results: Four themes were generated: (1) Making sense of identity: Normality versus difference; (2) M...
presentations.authors_text: S. MacFarlane1, C. Murray1, R. Faulkner2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Lancaster University, Division of Health Research, Lancaster, United Kingdom, 2Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust, Manchester, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: People with cystic fibrosis, a typically childhood-onset condition, develop their identity alongside their illness. While identity coherence supports wellbeing and adjustment, cystic fibrosis, demands may disrupt this process. Research on identity in cystic fibrosis, is fragmented, with findings often embedded in broader topics. This review aimed to synthesised qualitative studies to provide a coherent understanding of identity in adolescents and adults with cystic fibrosis.
sections.Methods: A systematic search of seven databases identified 34 eligible studies, which were analysed using thematic synthesis.
sections.Results: Four themes were generated: (1) Making sense of identity: Normality versus difference; (2) Made to feel different: Social perceptions emphasising CF; (3) Maintaining identity and reducing difference; and (4) Shifting identities: Health status changes disrupting identity.
sections.Conclusion: Identity in CF is dynamic, shaped by negotiations of normalcy, social perceptions, and health changes, with strategies employed by people with cystic fibrosis to reduce difference.

sections.Objectives: Parents of children with cystic fibrosis (CF) frequently remain in hospital during repeated admissions, yet their psychological experiences are often overlooked. This study explored how parents experience identity, scrutiny, and coping within the hospital environment, and how everyday communication practices shape emotional safety and resilience.
sections.Methods: A qualitative study using Interpretative Phenomenological Analysis (IPA) was conducted. Five parents of children with CF who had experienced repeated or prolonged hospital admissions participated in semi-structured interviews. Interviews explored emotional impact, parental identity, interactions with healthcare professionals, coping strategies, and unmet support needs. Data were analysed idiographically and across cases. Internal Family Systems (IFS) and Polyvagal Theory informed interpretation.
sections.Results: Three superordinate themes were identified: (1) “Mum culture” and loss of identity, where parents described being addressed by generic labels rather than by name, contributing to feelings of invisibility and reduced confidence; (2) Scrutiny and the institutional gaze, characterised by vigilance, fear of judgement, and safeguarding-related anxiety, which limited expression of vulnerability; (3) Protective coping through emotional control, where parents relied on suppressive strategies to appear competent. Outward resilience was maintained at significant emotional cost.
sections.Conclusion: Hospital culture strongly shapes parental identity, emotional safety, and coping during CF admissions. Routine communication practices and perceived scrutiny can promote emotionally suppressive coping rather than supported resilience. Simple relational changes such as addressing parents by name, acknowledging expertise, and brief emotional check-ins may reduce burden and improve parent–professional collaboration.

presentations.title: Exploring processes and experiences of comfort among adults living with cystic fibrosis: a qualitative study
presentations.summary: Background: Advances in cystic fibrosis (CF) care, particularly CFTR modulator therapies, have increased life expectancy and transformed CF into a long-term adult condition. However, not all people with CF are eligible for these therapies, resulting in different therapeutic contexts. While outcomes have improved, little is known about how comfort is experienced within these changing realities. Understanding comfort as a dynamic and multidimensional process is essential to inform nursing care beyond symptom control. Aim: To explore experiences and processes of comfort among adults living with cystic fibrosis under different therapeutic conditions and identify implications for nursing practice. Methods: This ong...
presentations.authors_text: A. Nabais1,2, H. Laranjeira3, A. Góis3, M.C. Roquette Viana4, P. Pontífice-Sousa4
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Universidade Católica Portuguesa - Faculdade de Ciências da Saúde e Enfermagem, PhD Student, Lisboa, Portugal, 2Escola Superior de Enfermagem da Universidade de Coimbra, Enfermagem de Reabilitação, Coimbra, Portugal, 3Unidade Local de Saúde de Coimbra, Serviço de Pneumologia, Coimbra, Portugal, 4Universidade Católica Portuguesa - Faculdade de Ciências da Saúde e Enfermagem, Enfermagem, Lisboa, Portugal

sections.Background: Advances in cystic fibrosis (CF) care, particularly CFTR modulator therapies, have increased life expectancy and transformed CF into a long-term adult condition. However, not all people with CF are eligible for these therapies, resulting in different therapeutic contexts. While outcomes have improved, little is known about how comfort is experienced within these changing realities. Understanding comfort as a dynamic and multidimensional process is essential to inform nursing care beyond symptom control. Aim: To explore experiences and processes of comfort among adults living with cystic fibrosis under different therapeutic conditions and identify implications for nursing practice.
sections.Methods: This ongoing qualitative study adopts an interpretive, constructivist approach. Ten semi-structured interviews were conducted with adults living with CF, including participants receiving CFTR modulators and others not eligible. Interviews explored experiences of adulthood with CF, meanings of comfort and strategies used to manage comfort in daily life and healthcare. Data analysis is iterative and inductive. Preliminary findings: Comfort was described as a dynamic process shaped by physical, emotional and relational dimensions. It was linked to adaptation to adult life and maintaining daily activities and life projects. Among participants receiving modulators, comfort was associated with symptom relief, bodily normalisation and emotional changes, including relief, hope, uncertainty and identity renegotiation. Those not eligible relied more on relational support, routines and continuity of care. Nurses were central in sustaining comfort across contexts.
sections.Conclusion: Comfort emerges as an evolving, context-dependent process. Nursing care that recognises these differences while supporting adaptation may better address long-term needs in adult CF.

This study aimed to explore the current lived experience of people with CF (pwCF) and their families in Australia, to inform responsive and future-focused care and support systems. Methods: An online anonymous survey was distributed nationally via social media and emails lists, focussing on demographics, diagnosis experiences, physical and mental health, treatment history and access, and social and economic impacts of CF. Quantitative data were analysed descriptively to summarise outcomes across domains. Fr...
presentations.authors_text: G. Handley1, S. Quattromani1, K. Johnson1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1CF Together, Carnegie, Australia

sections.Objectives: The CF landscape has changed significantly in recent years with widespread access to CFTR modulators (CFTRm), leading to more people living longer and shifting social, health and economic needs. This study aimed to explore the current lived experience of people with CF (pwCF) and their families in Australia, to inform responsive and future-focused care and support systems.
sections.Methods: An online anonymous survey was distributed nationally via social media and emails lists, focussing on demographics, diagnosis experiences, physical and mental health, treatment history and access, and social and economic impacts of CF. Quantitative data were analysed descriptively to summarise outcomes across domains. Free-text responses were analysed using thematic coding.
sections.Results: 231 responses were received, including 50% pwCF, 47% parents of pwCF and 3% partners of pwCF, from all Australian states and territories. Nearly half of respondents reported a diagnosis or experience of anxiety (48%) and over one-third reported depression (36%). The burden of managing CF was rated as moderate to very high by 84% of respondents. While majority (75%) reported currently taking a CFTRm, 68% experienced at least one CFTRm side effect, most commonly brain fog (24%), and 18% reported modifying CFTRm use due to side effects. Gaps in primary care integration were identified, with 22% lacking regular general practitioner (GP) access and 41% reporting no coordination between their GP and CF team. Financial strain was common: 63% reported a major to moderate impact of CF on household finances, and 35% reported not purchasing a medicine due to cost.
sections.Conclusion: While CFTRm have transformed clinical outcomes for many, pwCF and their families continue to face significant mental health challenges, treatment burden, care coordination gaps and financial strain. These findings highlight the need for evolving care and support models to address the broader and changing needs of the CF community.

Results: To date, HBBI has provided grant funding to 87 CF owned busin...
presentations.authors_text: J. Gregory1, G. Silverman2, B. Kilgariff2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Cystic Fibrosis Trust, Information, Services and Programmes, London, United Kingdom, 2Cystic Fibrosis Trust, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Self-employment, and the flexibility to schedule workloads around CF, is an enticing option for adults with CF who want to work. However, it comes with its own set of challenges. HBBI was set up in memory of Helen Barrett, a business woman with CF, following her death in 2013. The award has been supporting adults with CF in the UK to build confidence and pursue their business ideas.
sections.Methods: HBBI aims to provide a platform for adults with CF to pursue their business ambitions through grant funding, whilst also providing business planning support and a range of online sessions dedicated to business development and sustainability.
sections.Results: To date, HBBI has provided grant funding to 87 CF owned businesses, including winners who are post-transplant and/or who are currently unable to benefit from modulators. Winners told us the award built their confidence, improved their mental health, and enabled them to take positive steps towards achieving their career goals “The award gave me a huge sense of confidence in my own ability which further down the line has helped me immensely in my career.” HBBI is now developing its offer to ensure that supported businesses are able to launch sustainably through our business plan, one-to-one business planning support, online networking; and webinar sessions on a range of business topics.
sections.Conclusion: The demand after over a decade of delivery of HBBI, highlight the ongoing need for access to support for business owners with CF. Funding has helped to establish businesses across a wide range of sectors. Winners have opened physical premises, and a winner secured further funding through BBC’s Dragon’s Den. Others have been able to use their business to build confidence and improve their employability. However, there is a need for further support, and the Trust is looking to expand HBBI to build an effective support framework to help ensure that CF owned businesses can find long-term success.

presentations.summary: Objectives: Incorporating stakeholders’ views into care delivery is rightly gaining traction across healthcare. Qualitative research is a robust way to gain systematic and valuable insights into the experiences and perspectives of those with lived and professional experience of cystic fibrosis (CF). It can provide important contextual information either independently or alongside quantitative methods. In the post-modulator era, with significant changes to the physical, emotional and social wellbeing of many people with CF (pwCF), as well as changes to care delivery, understanding the experiences and perspectives of those living with, and working in, CF is arguably more important than ever. The Cystic Fibrosis...
presentations.authors_text: D. Beever1, R. Dobra2,3, J. Snowball4,5, Cystic Fibrosis Qualitative Research Network
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Nottingham, School of Medicine, Nottingham, United Kingdom, 2Imperial College London, National Heart and Lung Institute, London, United Kingdom, 3Royal Brompton Hospital, Department of Respiratory Paediatrics, London, United Kingdom, 4University of Oxford, Nuffield Department of Primary Care Health Sciences, Oxford, United Kingdom, 5Oxford University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Oxford Adult CF Centre, Oxford, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Incorporating stakeholders’ views into care delivery is rightly gaining traction across healthcare. Qualitative research is a robust way to gain systematic and valuable insights into the experiences and perspectives of those with lived and professional experience of cystic fibrosis (CF). It can provide important contextual information either independently or alongside quantitative methods. In the post-modulator era, with significant changes to the physical, emotional and social wellbeing of many people with CF (pwCF), as well as changes to care delivery, understanding the experiences and perspectives of those living with, and working in, CF is arguably more important than ever. The Cystic Fibrosis Qualitative Research Network (CFQRN) was established to provide a space for peer support, shared learning & collaboration amongst those interested in qualitative research in CF.
sections.Methods: Social media and personal networks were used to promote the CFQRN. As an initial collective formed, a website (https://cfqrn.carrd.co/) was established as a landing page for interested individuals.
sections.Results: The network currently has 46 members (with 30 new members in the last 6 months) across 36 organisations and includes healthcare professionals, researchers and pwCF. The research interests of group members are diverse, including ageing, exercise and diet. Since December 2024, we have held quarterly virtual meetings, including research presentations, methodological discussion, training opportunities and promotion of group members’ research.
sections.Conclusion: The growth of the CFQRN indicates significant interest in the use of qualitative methods in CF. The network aims to support individuals to develop their understanding of qualitative research, enhance their skills and increase the quality when using such methods. We plan to expand the network to collaborate on grant applications and obtain funding to undertake qualitative research at this important juncture in CF care.

presentations.title: Developing an early career writers’ forum for healthcare professionals and scientists working in cystic fibrosis research
presentations.summary: Objectives: Many training pathways, especially in healthcare, place little emphasis on teaching academic writing skills. These become increasingly important as healthcare professionals’ (HCPs) and scientists’ careers progress. In line with The ECFS’s commitment to develop the next generation of CF researchers, we set up an Early Career Writers’ Forum (ECWF), a programme for early career (EC) researchers to expand their writing and presenting skills with the support of each other and the guidance of established researchers. Methods: We shared a survey within the integrated CF care and research teams at The Royal Brompton Hospital and Imperial College London to gauge the value of an ECWF and collate session sugg...
presentations.authors_text: R. Dobra1,2, C. Short1,2, J.C Davies1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Imperial College London, National Heart and Lung Institute, London, United Kingdom, 2Royal Brompton Hospital, Department of Respiratory Paediatrics, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Many training pathways, especially in healthcare, place little emphasis on teaching academic writing skills. These become increasingly important as healthcare professionals’ (HCPs) and scientists’ careers progress. In line with The ECFS’s commitment to develop the next generation of CF researchers, we set up an Early Career Writers’ Forum (ECWF), a programme for early career (EC) researchers to expand their writing and presenting skills with the support of each other and the guidance of established researchers.
sections.Methods: We shared a survey within the integrated CF care and research teams at The Royal Brompton Hospital and Imperial College London to gauge the value of an ECWF and collate session suggestions. 29/30 respondents said they would find an ECWF 'really useful' or ‘would have found it really useful’ earlier in their career. Specific sessions were requested.
sections.Results: Since Jan 2025, we have organised eight hybrid sessions by internal and external speakers using a variety of formats. Sessions are recorded. Topics include guidance on Imperial’s PhD assessments and “How to Write a Lay Summary”. Core sessions will be repeated two-yearly for new staff. The forum is iterative; for example, learners requested a ‘trouble shooting’ workshop which was popular and will be repeated within the programme. Further sessions will be added if requested. Sessions have been attended by >55 EC writers (~25 AHPs, 15 medics, 15 scientists) who consider themselves EC, 28 recordings have been shared. Although aimed at our local team, the ECWF has been advertised by word of mouth; staff from seven external UK-based institutions have joined sessions.
sections.Conclusion: The ECWF is an evolving, responsive forum that has been well attended by our CF team and beyond. Learners have shaped the forum to meet their needs. Those outside our team have sought to join sessions. Given the apparent need and interest, we would encourage others to establish a similar programme.

Patients set the agenda and crucially, staff from across the multidisciplinary team attend not to lead, but to listen and learn. Group guidelines were set to...
presentations.authors_text: J. Swarbrooke1, L. Haigh1, D. Ross1, N. Shaw1, I.J Clifton1, A. Sawant1, G. Spoletini1, D.G Peckham1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1St James University Hospital, Adult Cystic Fibrosis, Leeds, United Kingdom, 2Leeds Institute of Medical Research at St James's, University of Leeds, Leeds, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Our primary objective was to rebuild a Cystic Fibrosis (CF) community in a safe and sustainable way, giving people with CF (PwCF) the chance to lead conversations and shape their own care. We wanted to create a group where patients felt heard, valued, and connected and improve the experience of care and patient outcomes for PwCF.
sections.Methods: Patients were approached to be part of the CF patient focus group through face-to-face interaction and multiple social media and patient network platforms, to establish a patient representative group for PwCF. Patients set the agenda and crucially, staff from across the multidisciplinary team attend not to lead, but to listen and learn. Group guidelines were set to encourage and positive safe space, and to protect patient confidentiality.
sections.Results: The patient focus group has led to tangible, co-produced improvements in the inpatient environment, communication, and service delivery for PwCF. Patient feedback has directly informed changes to the ward environment, ensuring it is safer, more comfortable, and better aligned with patient needs. The group has also improved communication by keeping patients informed of changes within the multidisciplinary team, and the services. The group influences the future of PwCF by discussing what matters most to them, ensuring that our research portfolio reflects their priorities. This approach helps improve patient recruitment and retention.
sections.Conclusion: The group continues to generate ideas, communicate openly and build on the momentum we’ve created. Together, we have developed a flexible platform that can adapt to the evolving health and social needs of PwCFs, while keeping them safe and minimising the impact on their daily lives.

Benefits to both teams The Lviv team praised the...
presentations.authors_text: R. Dobra1,2, M. Sheremet3, B. Belkarty4, J. King2,1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Imperial College London, National Heart and Lung Institute, London, United Kingdom, 2Royal Brompton Hospital, Department of Respiratory Paediatrics, London, United Kingdom, 3Clinical Center of Children’s Healthcare, Lviv, Ukraine, 4Royal Brompton Hospital, Department of Adult Cystic Fibrosis, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: The ECFS Twinning Programme partners experienced & emerging sites to optimise CF care at the emerging site. Royal Brompton Hospital, London, & adult/paediatric CF centres in Lviv, Ukraine are twinned. Due to the war in Ukraine, the London team could not visit Lviv, so Lviv teams visited London. Here we evaluate the visit, sharing our findings for those interested in the programme.
sections.Methods: Surveys shared via Qualtrics, interviews conducted/transcribed in Teams. Interview transcripts & survey free-text coded in NVivo. Content analysis used to interpret themes.
sections.Results: Dec25-Jan26; 29 surveys(7 Lviv,22 London) & 4 interviews(1 Lviv,3 London); 4 themes. Benefits to both teams The Lviv team praised the visit, feeling it would improve patient care. The London team highlighted multiple benefits including prestige of participating. Comparing cultures Lviv noted London’s clinicians were more guideline driven; both teams noted a greater emphasis on the multidisciplinary team in London. However, teams discovered many challenges e.g. poor adherence are cross-cultural. Logistical challenges Covering service delivery/personal commitments in their absence required significant planning for the Lviv team. Setting up the visit was time consuming for the London team. Securing funding e.g. for interpreters & accommodation was difficult, compounded by London’s high costs. Strengths & weaknesses Both teams felt the programme was well balanced & enjoyed the case exchange & social programme. The Lviv team commented that being in London meant they could engage in the visit away from clinical/personal demands. Both teams emphasised the need for more interpreters.
sections.Conclusion: The twinning visit was useful, enjoyable & rewarding for both teams. Adequate funding, especially for interpreter services, is essential to ensure a successful visit. Although deviating from the expected visit structure, both teams highlighted benefits to the emerging team visiting the established centre.

sections.Objectives: Advanced lung disease (ALD) is a leading cause of mortality in CF. While Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) has shown significant clinical benefits, little is known about treatment adherence neuropsychological side-effects in the ALD population following ETI. This study evaluated self-reported adherence, health-related quality of life (HRQoL), mental health, and ETI-related side-effects in patients with ALD.
sections.Methods: A prospective, cross-sectional study was conducted at the CF Center of Florence, Italy. Participants (N=31, age>12y) with ALD (ppFEV1<40% or transplant referral at baseline) were enrolled from an ETI compassionate use program. After 36m of therapy, patients completed the Treatment Adherence Questionnaire-CF (TAQ-CF), CF Questionnaire-Revised (CFQ-R), PHQ-9 (depression), GAD-7 (anxiety), and an ad-hoc neuropsychological side-effect survey. Clinical data (ppFEV1, BMI, sweat chloride SC) were tracked from baseline to 36m.
sections.Results: Clinical parameters improved significantly from baseline to 36m: SC decreased, while ppFEV1 and BMI increased (p<0.05). HRQoL scores significantly improved across most CFQ-R domains. The mean overall adherence rate was 75.6%; 45.2% reported high and 51.6% moderate adherence. No significant association was found between adherence levels and clinical or mental health outcomes. However, neuropsychological side-effects were prevalent: 29% reported concentration difficulties and 19% reported insomnia. Significant correlations were found between GAD-7 scores and the number of reported side-effects (p<0.05).
sections.Conclusion: Patients with ALD maintain high-to-moderate treatment adherence 36m after initiating ETI, likely due to high disease burden. While ETI provides pulmonary and HRQoL improvements, a subset of patients experiences persistent neuropsychological side-effects correlated with anxiety. These findings highlight the need for a holistic approach to ALD management that monitors both physical and mental well-being.

Methods: We recruited 14 AWCF experiencing chronic pain via CF Foundation’s Community Voice and conducted semi-structured qualitative interviews about living with CF and pain, including questions about pain management and experiences of shared decision-making. We developed a codebook and conducted thematic analysis. Results: Participants were 23-64 years old; 10 identified as female, 3 male, 1 gender non-binary...
presentations.authors_text: D. Friedman1, T.M. Mullen1, M. Behrman2, J. Robichaud2, R. Wei1, N. Chaudhary1, J. He2, A. Chadwick2, J. Greenberg1, L. Yonker1, A.M. Georgiopoulos1, A.S. Bruce2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Massachusetts General Hospital, Boston, United States, 2University of Kansas Medical Center, Kansas City, United States

sections.Objectives: Sixty to 90% of adults with cystic fibrosis (AWCF) experience pain, often chronic in nature. Effective communication between patients and CF care teams is crucial for optimal pain management. Our goal was to learn from AWCF about their pain management experiences through in-depth interviews.
sections.Methods: We recruited 14 AWCF experiencing chronic pain via CF Foundation’s Community Voice and conducted semi-structured qualitative interviews about living with CF and pain, including questions about pain management and experiences of shared decision-making. We developed a codebook and conducted thematic analysis.
sections.Results: Participants were 23-64 years old; 10 identified as female, 3 male, 1 gender non-binary; 2 as Hispanic/Latinx; 12 White; 7 were taking elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI). Most (92.9%) reported trying multiple pain treatments (medication and non-medication). Participants reported largely positive pain management experiences with CF teams, and more negative experiences with non-CF providers, especially when inpatient. Themes highlighted key elements of pain management communication (e.g., validation of pain, empathetic care, effective care coordination, responsiveness and consistency). Table 1 presents exemplar quotes of these elements and components of shared decision-making.
sections.Conclusion: Interviews with AWCF highlight experiences with effective patient-provider communication in CF pain management, as well as experienced barriers to optimal care. Increased understanding and validation of CF pain as well as coordinated and co-developed pain management plans are needed. Table 1. Key themes of pain management communication Key themes of pain management communicationPositive experiencesNegative experiencesValidation of pain experiences“I am so grateful for the team I have now, because they have never made me feel like it's all in my head...[my CF doctor] will say ‘absence of evidence is not evidence of absence, if you tell me that’s how you feel I believe it.’”“As far as like pleurisy, I've had doctors say, ‘Well, that's not a real pain’ or...’You're calling it pain, but it's not pain. It's...inflammation’ or something like that.”Empathetic care“Some will still sit down and talk to you at eye level. One...she'll come in and talk to you...at the end, she's like,‘OK, now it's time for you to ask me questions.’ She specifically stops early enough to be sure that she's covering anything else that you might be concerned about. So that really helps with at least feeling heard.”“I had a GI physician who I actually had to go to counseling for medical trauma because...she would tell me things like ‘no one wants you for a patient, because you're so complicated’.Effective Care Coordination“The pulmonology team…will have my records to say, ‘Okay, this is how we have to treat and take care of him, because he has cystic fibrosis’ and the...GI team, and Endo, and everyone else …will be able to take that over...because they have all of my …chart notes.”“I had one [J tube] surgically that was excruciatingly painful...The surgeon had never done one on a CF patient and it was a different type of surgery for him, so I guess he and the GI doctor didn't really communicate about what to put in, so I had this big inflexible tube sticking out of me for seven months. It was awful.”Care team is responsive and accessible“With Epic and MyChart, I can just shoot a message to my doctor and the whole CF team and get a response or a phone call within probably 30 minutes. And you're like, “okay, this is what's happening. Do I need to make an appointment? Should I go to the ER?”“It's difficult at times getting into contact with them, because the doctors are not always in the clinics every day. Once I'm finally in contact, it's a whole other difficulty, because …over the phone, they can't understand what your pain is like and where it's coming from and … how it's affecting you, which sometimes it can also be difficult for them to take it serious enough to understand that we need to do something about it. Sometimes…they just tell me to wait …for the pain to slowly improve on its own.”Consistency/Feeling well known by the provider“My pain management doctor and I have been able to discuss things, and...we know each other very well, we see each other every other month, and we understand each other.”“But I have had…the hospital pain team see me. They're not, obviously, the same pain physician that I see outpatient every few months. So they just look at my file and walk in, and say, “hey, how's your pain? Is the pump covering everything while you're here?”, that kind of stuff.”Shared decision-makingShared understanding of cause of pain “My doctors now know that I am in a science-based field... and so they've done a good job of explaining it in terms that I will understand. So that I can better understand my body.”“I think mostly frustrating. Because like I said, they don't really have an answer and, sometimes they brush it off like it's, something you just have to learn to accept sometimes. So it gets very frustrating and sometimes it gets to a point where…I wonder whether or not to bring it up in the first place.”Experiences with providers eliciting patient treatment preferences“It's been better since...I switched from pediatric to adult, and I think that really made a difference ...I started going to those doctor’s appointments by myself and I kind of led that conversation as opposed to my mom or dad leading that conversation when I was a pediatric patient. And I think the adult doctor does a better job of talking to me on my skill level than they used to just explain everything to my parents while I was sitting there.”“You want to know what's wrong...it cannot be in your head. I did tell one of my CF doctors, you know, this is what I'm dealing with and he was like, “Oh yeah, this is something you have to get used to.” and I'm like “I shouldn't have to get used to it though”...so that part is kind of frustrating.” Patient values and preferences included in a treatment plan “I've listened to them, they've listened to me, and we've come to a decision together and not just them telling me what I'm going to do.” “My doctor...says: ‘What do you want to do?’ And then after I…say what my thoughts are, he either agrees or says, ‘Well, why don't we do this?’, and ‘Because of this’, and there's always a reason for why he wants to do what he does, but he almost always gives me multiple options that I can choose from of… how we're going to go forward with treatment.”"[A doctor] said, “We're going to come down on and… and take her off the...pain pump, PCA, … today”...He hadn't even come in my door to ask me how I was doing or anything. So, he comes in, I said “Wouldn’t it be nice if you came and talked to me first before making decisions on my behalf?””

presentations.title: The socioeconomic determinants of lung function in children with cystic fibrosis
presentations.summary: Objectives: Children with cystic fibrosis (CF) from socioeconomically deprived backgrounds have poorer health outcomes, including reduced lung function. This study assessed the relationship between socioeconomic status and lung function among children with CF at a large paediatric CF centre.
