presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Manchester, School of Medical Sciences, Manchester, United Kingdom, 2Royal Manchester Children's Hospital, Department of Paediatric Research, Manchester, United Kingdom, 3Manchester University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Depart of Biostatistics, Manchester, United Kingdom, 4Royal Manchester Children's Hospital, Department of Paediatric Respiratory Medicine, Manchester, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Children with cystic fibrosis (CF) from socioeconomically deprived backgrounds have poorer health outcomes, including reduced lung function. This study assessed the relationship between socioeconomic status and lung function among children with CF at a large paediatric CF centre.
sections.Methods: Royal Manchester Children’s Hospital (RMCH), a leading tertiary CF centre in northwestern England, cares for 330 paediatric CF patients, including 200 receiving direct treatment and 130 through shared care. A retrospective study was conducted using anonymised data, including the Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD decile) as a socioeconomic indicator, percentage predicted FEV₁ (ppFEV₁), BMI z-score, sweat chloride levels, CF genotype, the number of antibiotic courses in the past year, and reports of adherence issues. Relationships between FEV₁ and these variables were assessed via Pearson correlation coefficients. Scatter plots with trend lines were created to illustrate the link between IMD decile and FEV₁. P values were calculated to determine statistical significance.
sections.Results: Data were complete for 129 children with CF aged 5 to 17 years. The IMD decile distribution was predominantly low, suggesting most participants lived in deprived areas. A weak positive correlation was observed between IMD decile and ppFEV1 (R² = 0.06), suggesting that children from less deprived areas had slightly better lung function. Nonetheless, the IMD decile explained only a small part of the variation. Additionally, ppFEV1 showed a weak correlation with younger age and male sex.
sections.Conclusion: Findings indicate that socioeconomic disparities in lung function persist, but to a lesser extent than at the national level, where deprivation accounts for more variability.

sections.Objectives: Advances in diagnosis and treatment have prolonged the life expectancy of patients with cystic fibrosis (CF). The increasing number of diagnosed cases underscores the importance of primary healthcare providers, including home healthcare personnel, in patient management. To evaluate the knowledge level of home healthcare staff regarding CF and to assess knowledge gain following a structured training session.
sections.Methods: A total of 35 healthcare professionals participated in the study. A 22-item questionnaire covering CF characteristics, treatment options, and clinical management was administered before and after a three-hour visual training program. Pre- and post-training results were compared, and subgroup analysis was performed according to profession.
sections.Results: Participants’ mean age was 32.7±8.5 years, and 54% were female. 34% of the participants were physicians, 40% nurses, 17% emergency medical technicians. Only 32.4% had received formal CF training during their education. Correct response rates significantly improved in general knowledge (66.6% → 91%), nutrition (33.3% → 100%), medication knowledge (0 → 75%), airway clearance (25% → 50%), and exacerbation management sections (50% → 75%) (p<0.05). No significant change was observed in the nebulizer hygiene section. Pre-training correct response rates were 63.6% for physicians, 45.5% for nurses, and 40.9% for technicians, increasing post-training to 93.1%, 72.7%, and 68.2%, respectively. Physicians, as expected, demonstrated significantly higher knowledge levels than nurses and technicians (p<0.05).
sections.Conclusion: Targeted training programs for primary healthcare personnel significantly improve knowledge regarding CF and contribute to more effective patient care. Expanding such educational initiatives may enhance the quality and efficiency of home healthcare services.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1St Vincent's University Hospital, National Referral Centre for Adult Cystic Fibrosis, Dublin, Ireland, 2Cystic Fibrosis Registry of Ireland, Dublin, Ireland, 3University College Dublin, School of Public Health, Physiotherapy and Sport Science, Dublin, Ireland

sections.Objectives: While research has established that people with CF (PWCF) show elevated rates of anxiety and depression, gaps remain in understanding factors influencing mental wellbeing in the current era. This study aims to (1) explore associations between mental health and demographic and clinical factors in PWCF in Ireland and (2) develop a predictive model showing factors associated with anxiety and depression in PWCF in Ireland.
sections.Methods: Retrospective medical chart reviews were conducted from January 2023 to the present date, with data collection ongoing until April 2026. Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) and Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD-7) scores were extracted, alongside relevant demographic (age, gender, relationship status, education level, employment status) and clinical data (current FEV1, baseline FEV1, genotype, diabetes status, insulin requirement, liver disease status, whether prescribed highly effective modulator therapy, number and length of hospital admissions in the prior 12 months). Descriptive statistics will show prevalence and symptom severity, with regression analyses examining associations between mental health and demographic and clinical factors.
sections.Results: Of data analysed to date, most of the sample was female; in a relationship; educated at college level; and working full time, respectively. Preliminary results show anxiety and depression scores above clinical thresholds in 13% and 11% of study participants, respectively. We hypothesise that those with lower lung function, diabetes, requiring insulin, not taking highly effective modulator therapy, and with more hospital stays to be the sub-group(s) at greater risk of elevated depression and anxiety scores based on regression analyses.
sections.Conclusion: Anxiety and depression remain prevalent among PWCF. By identifying risk factors for mental health symptoms, we can refine mental health screening and access to psychosocial support to further develop comprehensive models of CF care.

presentations.authors_text: N. Murray1, J. Witt2, E. Dixon1, M. Puckey1, S. Bentley1, K.J Henney1, T. Nickels1, M. Ovens1, I. Balfour-Lynn1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Royal Brompton Hospital, Dept of Paediatric Cystic Fibrosis, London, United Kingdom, 2Cystic Fibrosis Trust, Research and Healthcare Data, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: From the time of cystic fibrosis (CF) diagnosis, good quality information and education about CF are vital to support families. We sought to explore how we could improve our approach to information and education provision at the Royal Brompton Hospital paediatric CF centre.
sections.Methods: We used a semi-structured interview approach to explore parents’ experiences of information and educational materials about CF, and their ideas for improvements. Families were invited to complete an interview with a member of the CF team in clinic, by phone, or at a home visit. Responses to interview questions were transcribed and coded thematically to identify opportunities for improvement.
sections.Results: Seven families were interviewed. Verbal information given by the CF team was rated highly, as it was felt to be more useful and relevant to the individual family. Opinions on written resources, such as leaflets, varied and these were rated less highly. Some parents recalled feeling overwhelmed during the period after diagnosis, noting it is important to have the right amount of information provided at the right time. Parents felt the best times for information provision were routine touchpoints (newborn screening visit, home visits, clinics) and important milestones (e.g. diagnosis, starting nursery/school). Key recommendations: Provide clear and up to date contact details from the outset Stagger information provision and focus on what is most relevant to the family at the time Share structured, practical information and advice, such as action plans and tips from peers Offer information in different formats so parents can use what works best for them
sections.Conclusion: Interviews with families have provided valuable insights into how information offers around CF could be strengthened. The recommendations generated identify key considerations to help CF teams enhance their education provision. We now plan to update our current approach and resources in light of these findings.

Outcomes were assessed using clinical stability and drug-specific objective monitoring, including microbiology surveillance, spirometry, swea...
presentations.authors_text: V. Pankhania1, S. Popple1, C. Shentall1, E. Wilson1, C. Mulcahy1, I. Ahmed1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University Hospitals of Leicester, Leicester, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor(ETI) has transformed outcomes in cystic fibrosis (CF), but its impact on long-term medication burden in children and young people(CYP) is incompletely described. We evaluated the extent and safety of deprescribing following ETI initiation in a paediatric CF cohort.
sections.Methods: We conducted a retrospective, single-centre observational study of CYP with CF aged 2–18 years who commenced ETI between 2020 and 2024 and received therapy for ≥1 year. The number and type of long-term medications stopped after ETI initiation were recorded. Outcomes were assessed using clinical stability and drug-specific objective monitoring, including microbiology surveillance, spirometry, sweat chloride(SwCl), serum vitamin levels, faecal elastase (FE), and antibiotic use.
sections.Results: 58 CYP initiated ETI; 39 (67%) stopped ≥1 long-term medication.In total, 78 medications were deprescribed, with 4 (5%) restarted. Of those stopping treatment, 14 (36%) stopped 1 medication, 10 (26%) stopped 2, and 15 (38%) stopped 3.Nebulised anti-pseudomonal therapies accounted for 14 (18%) withdrawals, with no re-isolation during follow-up.Dornase alfa was stopped in 11 cases(14%),with 1 restart. Sodium chloride supplements accounted for 26 (33%) withdrawals; 3 were restarted. Prophylactic antibiotics were stopped in 4 patients, antibiotic use was reduced in 3 and unchanged in 1 the next year. Fat-soluble vitamins comprised 16 (20%) withdrawals, with 4 patients requiring vitamin D supplementation. Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy was withdrawn in 7 patients (8%), all remaining off treatment with sustained FE. Overall, 38 (65%) CYP remained off ≥1 medication.
sections.Conclusion: ETI enabled rationalisation of long-term CF therapies, with only 5% requiring reintroduction, supporting reduced treatment burden. Available clinical and biochemical data supported safe deprescribing. Standardised monitoring and structured follow-up are recommended to maintain clinical stability.

This study aimed to reassess patient preferences for clinical and non-clinical treatment outcomes in a UK CF population following introduction of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI). presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of East Anglia, Norwich Medical School, Norwich, United Kingdom, 2NIHR ARC East of England, Cambridge, United Kingdom, 3University of Manchester, Division of Infection, Immunity & Respiratory Medicine, Manchester, United Kingdom, 4Patient Representative, London, United Kingdom, 5Imperial College London, London, United Kingdom, 6University of Liverpool, Women's and Children's Health, Liverpool, United Kingdom, 7Evidera, London, United Kingdom, 8University College London, Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: In 2020, we ran a discrete choice experiment (DCE) that showed people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) valued reductions in treatment burden, including inhaled therapies and physiotherapy, alongside clinical outcomes. Since then, introduction of highly effective CFTR modulators has changed clinical trajectories and daily treatment experience. This study aimed to reassess patient preferences for clinical and non-clinical treatment outcomes in a UK CF population following introduction of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI).
sections.Methods: An open-invitation online survey incorporating a patient co-designed DCE was administered to adults with CF across the UK. Participants completed 12 choice tasks comparing hypothetical CF treatment profiles defined by effects on lung function, abdominal symptoms, life expectancy, mental wellbeing, inhaled medication use, and physiotherapy requirements. Preferences analysed using a multinomial logit model.
sections.Results: The DCE analysis included 117 pwCF (median age 40 years [range 19–79]; 47% men; mean self-reported ppFEV1 69.5% [standard deviation 22.9]). Maintaining lung function and life expectancy remained most valued outcomes. Respondents showed a marked aversion to deteriorations in lung function, worsening abdominal symptoms, and declines in mental wellbeing. Compared with the 2020 survey, a reduction in treatment burden from inhaled medications or physiotherapy was now less prominent in mean preferences, although individual priorities varied.
sections.Conclusion: Following ETI introduction, pwCF continue to prioritise preserving lung function and avoiding deterioration in health and wellbeing. Treatment burden remains important for some, but appears less central to average preference than previously observed. These findings suggest that maintaining current health states may be more salient to pwCF than treatment simplification alone, with implications for treatment evaluation and shared decision-making.

Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD) scores were obtained, then allocated into two groups. Group 1 had IMD scores between 8 and 10 (least deprived), Group 2 had IMD scores between 1 and 3 (most deprived). 10 patients were randomly sampled from each. Each pe...
presentations.authors_text: B. Miller1, V. Holland1, T. Sharp1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Nottingham University Hospital, Wolfson Cystic Fibrosis Unit, Nottingham, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: A literature review by McGarry et al. in 2019 concluded that low socio-economic status was associated with increased mortality and worse lung function amongst people with Cystic Fibrosis (CF). The objective of this study is to consider how socio-economic status may impact the efficacy of modulators amongst adults with CF.
sections.Methods: Delta F508 homozygous patients on a full dose of Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) were selected from our center. Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD) scores were obtained, then allocated into two groups. Group 1 had IMD scores between 8 and 10 (least deprived), Group 2 had IMD scores between 1 and 3 (most deprived). 10 patients were randomly sampled from each. Each person’s FEV₁% from their 2019 and 2024 annual assessment was collected. The Medicines Possession Ratio (MPR) for two years (2024/2025) was also collected. We analysed the relationship between membership of each cohort and change in FEV₁% after commencing ETI and MPR rate.
sections.Results: Each cohort was separated into two groups. For group 1 (age=18-25), cohort 1 had a higher FEV₁% in both 2019 and 2024. The rate of improvement in FEV₁% predicted between the two cohorts on ETI is very similar. For group 2 (age≥26), both cohorts began with a mean FEV₁% of approximately 50%. However, those in the cohort 1 had a greater rate of improvement on ETI. The MPR suggests that people over 25 in cohort 1 have the best adherence. However, there may be other factors impacting the rate of FEV₁% improvement. Mean FEV₁%20192024Cohort 1 (Age=18-25)83.496.5Cohort 1 (Age≥26)49.370.4Cohort 2 (Age=18-25)69.282Cohort 2 (Age≥26)47.162.5Conclusion: This study suggests that people living in the most deprived areas experience less improvement in FEV₁% after starting ETI, when compared with those living in the least deprived. Research should be conducted with a larger sample size to establish the statistical significance of this relationship and the reasons why.

Results: Sixty-eight participants (60%) were...
presentations.authors_text: U. Skogeland1,2, I. de Monestrol1,2, K. Lindblom2, T. Godskesen3,4, T. Pincikova1,2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Karolinska Institutet, Department of Paediatrics, CLINTEC, Huddinge, Sweden, 2Karolinska University Hospital, Stockholm CF Centre, Huddinge, Sweden, 3Nord University, Faculty of Nursing and Health Sciences, Bodø, Norway, 4Centre for Research Ethics & Bioethics, Department of Public Health and Caring Sciences, Uppsala, Sweden

sections.Objectives: To examine real-world adherence, tolerability, and clinical responses to ETI in adult pwCF.
sections.Methods: In total, 115 participants aged ≥18 years treated with ETI at Stockholm CF Centre were included from 1 December 2023 to 30 November 2025. Adherence was defined as taking the prescribed ETI dose within two hours of the scheduled time; participants with an adherence score ≥ 0.80 were considered adherent. A study-specific questionnaire was completed during clinical visits (1–6 per participant). Associations between adherence and changes in forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV₁) and weight were analysed using Spearman’s and Pearson’s correlation tests.
sections.Results: Sixty-eight participants (60%) were non-adherent (<0.80). Median adherence was 0.50 (IQR 0.00–1.00); mean 0.54 (SD = 0.43). Seventeen participants improved their adherence during the study; however, despite fewer missed doses, adherence remained below the predefined threshold (≥0.80). Lung function improved following ETI initiation, with median FEV₁ changes varying by age groups. Adherence was not associated with FEV₁ change (Spearman’s ρ = –0.014, p = 0.885). However, FEV₁ change showed a significant positive correlation with weight gain (Spearman’s ρ = 0.189, p = 0.044). Median relative weight gain was 3.8%. Thirty-four participants (29.5%) were prescribed reduced ETI doses due to adverse effects; their adherence was comparable to that of participants taking the full dose.
sections.Conclusion: Self-reported adherence to ETI was suboptimal. Despite limited adherence, participants showed improvements in lung function and weight. Changes in FEV₁ correlated with weight gain but not with adherence. ETI was generally well tolerated, although over one-quarter of participants required a dose reduction. Further studies are needed to evaluate adherence to ETI and its effects over a longer time period.

presentations.authors_text: S. Bansal1, J. Hanna2, S. Armstrong2, J. Faughey2, V. Mills2, E. Wright2, S. Caskey2, D. Downey2,3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Queen’s University Belfast, School of Medicine, Belfast, United Kingdom, 2Northern Ireland Regional Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, BHSCT, Belfast, United Kingdom, 3Centre for Experimental Medicine, Queen's University Belfast, Belfast, United Kingdom

sections.Background: CFTR modulators (CFTRm) have transformed CF care. Real-world patient-reported outcomes (PROs) are essential to understand improvements and unmet needs. This study evaluated PROs in adults receiving CFTRm and explored patient perception of effectiveness and drivers of dissatisfaction.
sections.Methods: Over a 3-month period, adults attending CF clinics completed a bespoke, non-validated questionnaire capturing PROs. Additional items assessed perceived effectiveness, side effects, adherence, and overall satisfaction. Demographic data and clinical history were recorded. Responses were analysed descriptively.
sections.Results: Of the cohort of 88 patients, 94% were currently on CFTRm and 67% on ETI; 54% were male, and 80% were under 45 years. Values represent change in PROs compared with pre-CFTRm status. Improvements were most pronounced for respiratory symptoms (90%), exercise tolerance (77%), energy (70%), and quality of life (91%). Nutrition and gastrointestinal outcomes showed more modest gains (41–70%). Mental health and sleep demonstrated substantial heterogeneity, with worsening reported by 27%. Overall, 90% believed CFTRm effectively managed symptoms. Side effects were acceptable to 69% whereas 6% reported unacceptable side effects, most related to sleep disturbance or anxiety. Adherence was high and 40% reported no missed doses. Forgetting and travel were the main reasons for missed doses. Dissatisfaction (14%) was primarily linked to mental health and sleep disturbances.
sections.Conclusion: In this real-world adult cohort, CFTRm were associated with patient-reported improvements in respiratory outcomes, energy, and quality of life, alongside high adherence and overall satisfaction. Mental health and sleep disturbances were key drivers of dissatisfaction and non-adherence. Use of a non-validated questionnaire represents a limitation. Future studies incorporating validated PRO instruments are needed to fully understand patient experiences and long-term outcomes.

presentations.title: Impact of modulator therapy on unscheduled patient contact in a paediatric cystic fibrosis (CF) shared care service
presentations.summary: CF patients rely on the multidisciplinary team (MDT) for clinical support, advice, and care coordination though both scheduled and unscheduled contacts.. We previously reviewed and presented data on nature of unscheduled contacts in our service at ECFS 2019 (ref P084). This was prior to CF modulator era. Objective: To analyse and compare unscheduled patient/carer contacts at our paediatric CF service following the commencement of modulator therapy and to identify themes and outcomes for patient management. Methods: A retrospective evaluation of the patient electronic system was conducted from 01/10/2024 to 01/10/2025. 16 patients aged 0–15 years were included. Patient satisfaction surveys were also performed....
presentations.authors_text: N. Rao1, K. Barker1, D. Poulton1, L. Warburton1, J. Watkinson1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust, Royal Manchester Childrens Hospital (Wythenshawe), Paediatrics, Manchester, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: To analyse and compare unscheduled patient/carer contacts at our paediatric CF service following the commencement of modulator therapy and to identify themes and outcomes for patient management.
sections.Methods: A retrospective evaluation of the patient electronic system was conducted from 01/10/2024 to 01/10/2025. 16 patients aged 0–15 years were included. Patient satisfaction surveys were also performed. Results were compared with the 2019 audit.
sections.Results: A total of 1761 contacts were recorded, of which 1609 (91%) were unscheduled, an increase from 61% in 2019. Scheduled contacts decreased to 152 (8.6%) from 39%. Unscheduled contact by method shifted significantly with text/WhatsApp 89.8% (42% in 2019) being the preferred method followed by telephone 6.7% (39%), email 1.7% (10%), PAU 1.1% (6%), ward 0.37% (2%), A&E 0.19% (0%). Reasons for unscheduled contact were for treatment (38%), advice/support (13.9%), home visits (6.2%), results (6%), and other queries (6.7%). Reductions were noted in contact for symptom management (12%), equipment (1.8%), and IV therapy (0.3%). Patient satisfaction remained high, with a mean score of 5/5 for MDT communication, knowledge, and service provision.
sections.Conclusion: Unscheduled patient contact remains significant, with a strong preference for rapid digital communication. Our findings highlight there is a need for ongoing role of the MDT in supporting patients in the modulator era and emphasises the importance of flexible communication methods and proactive care planning to. The introduction of modulator therapy correlates with reduced IV antibiotic use and fewer hospital-based reviews, allowing greater community-based management.

presentations.title: Service evaluation on initiation of CFTR modulators in an adult cystic fibrosis population
presentations.summary: Introduction: Introduction of CFTR modulators has changed the care of people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). As treatment options continue to expand, understanding pwCF perspectives on the initiation of these therapies through real-world patient-reported outcomes is key. Therefore, we aimed to evaluate the initiation of CFTR modulators in an adult CF population attending a regional CF centre. Method: A structured questionnaire was developed by CF multidisciplinary (MDT) team and over a three-month period was completed by eligible pwCF during clinic appointments. Anonymised patient experiences on starting CFTR modulators were transcribed into Microsoft Excel and coded for descriptive analysis. Results: Eighty-eigh...
presentations.authors_text: J. Hanna1, S. Armstrong1, S. Bansal2, J. Faughey1, V. Mills1, E. Wright1, S. Caskey1, D. Downey1,3
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Northern Ireland Regional Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, BHSCT, Belfast, United Kingdom, 2Queen’s University Belfast, School of Medicine, Belfast, United Kingdom, 3Centre for Experimental Medicine, Queen's University Belfast, Belfast, United Kingdom

sections.Methods: A structured questionnaire was developed by CF multidisciplinary (MDT) team and over a three-month period was completed by eligible pwCF during clinic appointments. Anonymised patient experiences on starting CFTR modulators were transcribed into Microsoft Excel and coded for descriptive analysis.
sections.Results: Eighty-eight pwCF completed the survey and were predominantly male (55%), with 67% receiving ETI. Most common ages groups were 18-24, 25-34 and 35-44 years, representing 23%, 32% and 26% of respondents. Respondents agreed that before commencement, they received clear information and felt involved in the decision-making process, with over 95% selecting agree or strongly agree. Over 90% agreed that their questions and concerns had been addressed, and over 97% reported that they felt supported when commencing by selecting agree or strongly agree. Free-text responses to two open ended questions corroborated quantitative findings, with respondents most often describing initiation as well explained with clear information. Suggestions for improvement included local LFTs and information on patient experiences to date, particularly side effects.
sections.Conclusion: Initiation of CFTR modulators was associated with high levels of patient-reported understanding, involvement in decision-making, and perceived support, with findings consistent across quantitative and free-text responses. Use of a non-validated questionnaire represents a limitation. Future studies with validated questionnaires, qualitative interviews and more diverse populations are needed to further understand patient experiences.

presentations.title: Democratizing cystic fibrosis care: effects of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor on quality of life, depression, and anxiety in patients with cystic fibrosis in a low-income population

Methods: This was a prospective, longitudinal, observational study. Participants were recruited and assessed at baseline (T0) and after 3 (T1), 6 (T2), and 9 (T3) months of ETI use. sections.Objectives: To evaluate the impact of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) therapy on quality of life, depression, and anxiety in individuals with cystic fibrosis (CF).
sections.Methods: This was a prospective, longitudinal, observational study. Participants were recruited and assessed at baseline (T0) and after 3 (T1), 6 (T2), and 9 (T3) months of ETI use. Outcomes included quality of life (CF Questionnaire–Revised, CFQ-R), depression (Patient Health Questionnaire-9, PHQ-9), and anxiety (Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7, GAD-7). Paired t-tests or Wilcoxon signed-rank tests were used to compare baseline and follow-up outcomes, depending on the data distribution. A p<0.05 was considered statistically significant.
sections.Results: Twenty-three participants were included, with a mean age of 14.3 (SD: 5.9) years, median age at diagnosis 7.0 months. Most were male (56.5%), non-White (73.9%), and 56.5% were F508del heterozygous. Quality-of-life assessment demonstrated significant improvement after initiation of ETI in multiple domains, particularly physical functioning, vitality, health perceptions, social functioning, weight, and respiratory symptoms, all of which showed consistent increases at T1, T2, and T3 compared with baseline (p<0.05). In contrast, the treatment burden, body image, role functioning, and digestion domains did not show significant differences across the evaluated periods. A reduction in depression scores was also observed, with lower values at T1 and T2 compared with baseline. Conversely, anxiety scores showed only a downward trend, without statistical significance.

sections.Objectives: Improvements in life expectancy and quality of life among people with CF (PwCF) have led many to consider their reproductive needs and goals. Additionally, CF modulators have improved female fertility and pregnancy rates in the United States (US) have doubled in the modern era. We explored the experiences and decision-making of PwCF related to long-acting reversible contraception (LARC).
sections.Methods: We recruited PwCF aged 18-35 years from across the US who had ever used an intrauterine device (IUD) or implant to participate in semi-structured interviews related to LARC initiation, use, and removal. We conducted thematic analysis on the transcripts.
sections.Results: We interviewed 21 PwCF. Key themes included: 1) PwCF have misconceptions about the impact and safety of hormonal contraception on CF and their interactions with CF modulators; 2) Reasons for obtaining LARC included desires to simplify their daily health routine, desire for high efficacy methods, and improving menstrual symptoms; 3) The 2022 decision by the US Supreme Court to overturn protections for abortion is very concerning to PwCF and, in many cases, influenced participants’ LARC decisions; 4) Many participants who had used an IUD desire improved pain management during the insertion and removal process; 5) Most participants report no coercion from healthcare providers to choose LARC with the majority making the decision to receive a LARC independent of their CF team.
sections.Conclusion: Use and selection of contraception is increasingly relevant for PwCF. Future work should be done to address misinformation and uncertainty related to the impact and safety of contraception in CF.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Glasgow, School of Infection and Immunity, Glasgow, United Kingdom, 2West of Scotland Adult CF service, Respiratory Medicine, Glasgow, United Kingdom

sections.Background: In the UK, there are rising numbers of people with gender dysphoria and 0.5% of the population identify as transgender. Transgender people with CF (TpwCF) are becoming more common in paediatric and adult services with little research or guidance available into specific care for them. Many people with gender dysphoria seek help from online or private gender services, rather than their GP or CF medical teams- creating a vacuum of knowledge surrounding key parts of their care. Many health services operate binary sex based coding for all patients, and as such, physiological outcomes and automatic sex-based cancer screening services may become challenging for TpwCF. Aims: To understand the prevalence of gender dysphoria within our local CF service and highlight several key discussion points for CF teams looking after TpwCF
sections.Results: The West of Scotland Adult CF service has a prevalence of 1.3% of TpwCF (n=5 of 372). 4 are transgender men and 1 is a transgender woman. 20% have socially transitioned (living in preferred gender without medical intervention), 80% have medically transitioned gender (are taking exogenous hormones and/or surgery). 2 additional pwCF have de-transitioned back to their gender assigned at birth. Individual discussion regarding TpwCF’s preferred pronouns and agreed documentation of transgender status as a medical diagnosis is necessary for clarity for the MDT. Several physiological outcomes also need to be considered- such as utilising biological sex for haemoglobin levels, pulmonary function testing and cancer screening services to ensure that appropriate care is not missed or recorded inadequately. Open discussion with patients regarding planning for additional private healthcare needs such as exogenous hormone prescriptions and gender related surgeries should be approached openly and documented.
sections.Conclusion: Further research into this field with TpwCF is crucial to provide guidance for those with gender dysphoria and their CF teams.

sections.Objectives: Romantic partners are a key source of support for adults with cystic fibrosis, therefore building and sustaining good quality romantic relationships is important. However, the physical and psychological demands of this life-limiting condition can complicate navigation of romantic relationships.
sections.Methods: This qualitative study aimed to explore the experience of romantic relationships for adults with cystic fibrosis. Semi-structured interviews were conducted and interpretative phenomenological analysis (IPA) was utilised to analyse the data from seven participants with cystic fibrosis.
sections.Results: Four themes emerged: To share or not to share: fear and visibility influencing cystic fibrosis communication; For better or for worse: cystic fibrosis informing partner choice; “There are three people in the relationship: Me, cystic fibrosis, and my partner”: integrating cystic fibrosis in romantic relationships; and Coping alignment: relationship success versus health.
sections.Conclusion: The findings offer novel insights into how cystic fibrosis influences romantic relationships, with implications for clinical practice and directions for future research.

sections.Objectives: Improved life expectancy and the arrival of CFTR modulators have made motherhood a realistic goal for many women with cystic fibrosis (CF). However, the transition to obstetrical care remains complex. This study explores the gap between the perceptions of women with CF regarding their maternity care and the perspectives of the healthcare professionals(HCPs) supporting them.
sections.Methods: A dual-methodological approach was employed : 1) A descriptive qualitative literature review of PubMed-indexed articles(IMRaD format) from the last five years. 2) Semi-structured interviews conducted with women living with CF and HCPs involved in their care to compare lived experiences with clinical approaches.
sections.Results: The study identifies significant barriers to optimal care. Women with CF report a lack of clear information regading fertility, contraception and the specific impact of pregnancy on respiratory function. They often perceive their pregnancy as being managed through the lens of "high risk" rather than a physiological process. Conversely, HCPs express a sense of helplessness for want of standardized guidelines, insufficient interprofessional coordination and limited data on the long-term safety of CFTR modulators during pregnancy. Psychological support was found to be consistently undervalued in both the literature and fields interviews.
sections.Conclusion: Obstetrical care for women with CF is often perceived as inadequate, largely due to uncertainty and limited knowledge rather than individualized understanding. Enhancing nursing education, strengthening interdisciplinary collaboration, developing clear care pathways and recognising women with CF as active partners in care are essential to improving support for motherhood in CF.

Methods: We conducted a cross-sectional observational study of AYAs with CF aged ≥12 years at a single CF centre. Transition readiness was evaluated using the Transition Readiness Assessment Questionnaire (TRAQ 6.0). Depressive and anxiety symptoms were measured with PHQ-9 and GAD-7. Demographic variables included age and gender, while c...

sections.Objectives: Advances in cystic fibrosis (CF) care have improved survival rates, leading to an increase in adolescents and young adults (AYAs) needing to transition from paediatric to adult healthcare. Transition readiness is crucial for maintaining continuity of care and optimising long-term health outcomes, yet its psychological and clinical factors in CF remain understudied.
sections.Methods: We conducted a cross-sectional observational study of AYAs with CF aged ≥12 years at a single CF centre. Transition readiness was evaluated using the Transition Readiness Assessment Questionnaire (TRAQ 6.0). Depressive and anxiety symptoms were measured with PHQ-9 and GAD-7. Demographic variables included age and gender, while clinical variables included BMI, ppFEV₁, use of CFTR modulator therapy, pulmonary exacerbations in the previous year, and CF-related diabetes. Determinants of transition readiness were analysed through multivariable linear regression.
sections.Results: Thirty-three patients were included (36.4% female; mean age 16.1±2.8 years); 84.8% received CFTR modulators, and 21.2% had CF-related diabetes. Age was an independent predictor of overall transition readiness (β=0.728, p<0.001), while higher depressive symptom burden was associated with lower readiness (β=−0.800, p=0.006). The model explained substantial variance (R² = 0.60, p = 0.006). Age and depressive symptom burden influenced all four TRAQ domain subscales as well. CF-related diabetes showed a positive association with the domains Keeping appointment (β=0.474, p=0.031) and Tracking health issues (β=0.492, p=0.034) on TRAQ. Other clinical variables were not significantly associated with TRAQ scores.
sections.Conclusion: The results of our study indicate that age and depressive symptoms are key determinants of transition readiness in AYAs with CF, while CF-related diabetes may enhance engagement in self-management. These findings support integrating mental health screening and targeted transition support into routine CF care.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Cystic Fibrosis Trust, London, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: We developed Strong Beginnings so that families have a community of peer support from the start of their journey with CF. We aim to create connections between parents of children aged under 24 months, thereby improving wellbeing and reducing anxiety for parents in the early days of coping with the condition.
sections.Methods: We run one online event per month, for parents of children with CF aged up to 24 months. We run informal peer-support sessions, led by parent volunteers. These sessions are unstructured and are a relaxed place to talk, share experiences and connect. We also pilot various events, including baby massage, a presentation and conversation around anxiety and infection risk facilitated by a CF Psychologist, and a Q+A with our youth group (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tBF33g3XwCs).
sections.Results: Parents say how beneficial our events are for their wellbeing and mental health. They tell us that meeting other parents is immensely valuable. Parents often identify attending our events as a turning point on their journey with CF: “Our experience with CF was incredibly isolating… it was a very lonely place to be. Then we joined the call and everything changed. It was the first time we had found true comfort in sharing with people who genuinely understood”. Parents say that our events contribute to them feeling able to live their lives and support their child to experience the world, when previously, anxiety had limited them. “We went to the farm which we hadn’t been brave enough to do before and this was directly linked to the confidence that came from the session. We’re really grateful (as I’m sure my son was whilst riding a tractor around and seeing the animals!!)”
sections.Conclusion: Facilitating peer-support for parents of new babies with CF is vitally important. The impact of sharing experiences with others in the community is huge. These connections improve wellbeing and help parents to think positively about their child’s life and future.

sections.Objectives: Few qualitative studies explore paediatric Cystic Fibrosis Diabetes (CFD). This service evaluation explored experiences of CFD diagnosis and Cystic Fibrosis (CF) multidisciplinary team (MDT) support.
sections.Methods: Two patients and parents were interviewed by a Trainee Clinical Psychologist who was not part of the normal patient care team. Interviews were recorded by Microsoft Teams, transcribed and anonymised. The interviewer, CF Dietitian and Psychologist used Thematic Analysis (Braun & Clarke, 2006).
sections.Results: Themes identified: Making sense of CFD Diagnosis can feel unexpected despite prior awareness of the potential. Diagnosis as a process Adjustment was influenced by whether diagnosis felt gradual/logical or sudden. Clear, relational communication supported coping. Impact and burden CFD increased treatment complexity and burden; parents experienced more emotional and future-focused burden while patients experienced more practical, bodily and social challenges. Normalisation with time Routines established and became less emotionally charged in time. Patients described challenges with injections and device visibility, while parents considered skill acquisition/adherence. Routines reduced anxiety; adjustment required support beyond instruction. Quality communication MDT communication was valued by all, including relational continuity, clear explanation, honesty about uncertainty, accessibility and feeling heard. Patients prefer negotiation, not instruction. Visibility, identity and social context CFD affects body image, peer relationships and identity. Visible treatments heighten feelings of difference/social discomfort.
sections.Conclusion: Despite a small sample, findings give insight for best practice delivering CFD diagnosis from the understanding that CFD awareness did not equate to psychological readiness. MDT support was valued for providing individualised support, with repeated developmentally appropriate discussion and reassurance identified as helpful.

Due to the changes in the CF landscape, it is important to develop an up-to-date understanding of CF experiences. Moreover, previous research has mainly focused on individuals or dyads, but working together with whole families is key to improving clinical care (Hisert et al., 2023). Thus, an understanding of experiences at a family-level was sought. Methods: Four families with a child with CF were recruited and asked to provide creative items to depict their experiences of CF. Family-level interviews were conducte...
presentations.authors_text: R.E.M. Hatton1, T. Lee2, G. Latchford1, S. Pini1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Leeds, Leeds, United Kingdom, 2Leeds Teaching Hospitals NHS Trust, Leeds, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: Whilst treatment for Cystic Fibrosis (CF) has been recently transformed with developments in modulator drugs (De Boeck, 2020), it remains a serious genetic condition (Bowen & Hull, 2015). Due to the changes in the CF landscape, it is important to develop an up-to-date understanding of CF experiences. Moreover, previous research has mainly focused on individuals or dyads, but working together with whole families is key to improving clinical care (Hisert et al., 2023). Thus, an understanding of experiences at a family-level was sought.
sections.Methods: Four families with a child with CF were recruited and asked to provide creative items to depict their experiences of CF. Family-level interviews were conducted and the creative items provided by the family were used as the stimuli for discussions. Interviews were analysed with Interpersonal Phenomenological Analysis (IPA).
sections.Results: Results showed that the diagnosis and subsequent adjustments required can feel overwhelming. Families learn to navigate this through negotiations both within and outside of the family. This can look different for each unique family. As the child develops or health needs change, renegotiations are needed. Children and families with CF stand out from peers and this can be difficult. Fear exists around the child becoming unwell and things becoming overwhelming again.
sections.Conclusion: This study shows a journey that families engage in when CF is diagnosed. CF clinical teams should consider how they communicate the initial diagnosis and pacing the information they provide. Consideration is also needed when exacerbations or complications occur. Peer support from families with more experience of CF may help newly diagnosed families. Changes in treatment can require changes to family roles and families may benefit from support in thinking through these changes.

The F508del mutation directly destabilizes the nucleotide-binding domain 1 (NBD1) of CFTR. Stabilization of NBD1 may be critical to achieving complete correction of the F508del-CFTR defect. None of the approved modulators directly stabilize NBD1. presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 07 - Old and new – exploring the portfolio for cystic fibrosis treatment

sections.Objectives: At least two-thirds of people with CF (pwCF) on standard-of-care (SOC) modulators do not achieve normal CFTR function, defined by sweat chloride levels <30 mmol/L. Further, some do not tolerate these modulators. Therefore, a significant unmet need exists for novel modulators. The F508del mutation directly destabilizes the nucleotide-binding domain 1 (NBD1) of CFTR. Stabilization of NBD1 may be critical to achieving complete correction of the F508del-CFTR defect. None of the approved modulators directly stabilize NBD1.
sections.Methods: We assessed the safety, tolerability, and pharmacokinetics of two novel oral NBD1 stabilizers, SION-719 and SION-451, in two randomized, placebo-controlled single-agent phase 1 single ascending dose and multiple ascending dose clinical trials in healthy adult participants.
sections.Results: SION-719 and SION-451 were generally safe and well tolerated at all dose levels administered. No serious adverse events were reported. Most treatment-emergent adverse events (TEAEs) were mild to moderate. No TEAEs led to participant discontinuation of study drug, and none met dose-limiting criteria. There were no trends of significance in clinical safety data. Based on in vitro CF human bronchial epithelial cell assay data, the concentration targets for clinically meaningful benefit compared with SOC for SION-719 and SION-451, as either an add-on to SOC or in dual combination with one of Sionna’s complementary modulators, were achieved with single and multiple doses.
sections.Conclusion: Novel NBD1 stabilizers SION-719 and SION-451 were generally safe and well tolerated in healthy participants, and both compounds achieved concentrations that are predicted to deliver clinically meaningful benefit in pwCF and the F508del genotype. SION-719 is advancing in a Phase 2 proof of concept study and SION-451 is in a Phase 1 dual combination study.

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 08 - Life course outcomes in modern cystic fibrosis: transplantation, ageing and pregnancy across registries

sections.Objectives: To examine whether older cystic fibrosis (CF) patients experience inferior outcomes following lung transplant (LT).
sections.Methods: Adult (≥18 years) CF patients undergoing LT from 01/2005-09/2024 were identified in the United Network for Organ Sharing database and stratified by age at transplant: 18-29, 30-39 (reference), 40-49, and ≥50 years. Postoperative outcomes were acute rejection, hospital length of stay, and post-transplant dialysis, ventilator support, extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO), reintubation, airway dehiscence, tracheostomy, and stroke. Survival was assessed using Kaplan-Meier curves and multivariable Cox proportional hazards models. Sensitivity analyses examined whether effects differed pre- vs. post-2020 with introduction of CF modulator therapy.
sections.Results: Of 3,195 CF patients undergoing LT (median age 30 years), 1,528 (47.8%) were 18–29, 959 (30.0%) 30–39, 471 (14.7%) 40–49, and 237 (7.4%) ≥50. Compared to recipients aged 30–39, older patients were more likely male and to have a smoking history (p<0.001), but less likely to receive pre-transplant ECMO (p=0.014) and had shorter waitlist times (p=0.006). Older recipients also received organs from progressively older donors (p<0.001). Post-transplant, reintubation rates (p<0.001) and hospital length of stay (p=0.007) increased with age. After adjustment, older age was not independently associated with postoperative complications. Despite similar morbidity, 1-year survival was significantly lower for recipients ≥50 years versus the reference group (aHR=3.95, 95% CI=1.61–9.73, p=0.003). Compared to patients 30–39, those 40–49 (aHR=1.68, 95% CI=1.32–2.14, p<0.001) and ≥50 (aHR=3.25, 95% CI=2.13–4.94, p<0.001) had progressively higher overall mortality. These associations remained consistent pre- vs. post-2020.
sections.Conclusion: Advanced age predicts worse post-LT survival among CF patients, with recipients ≥50 years showing nearly fourfold higher 1-year mortality despite similar complications.

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 09 - Rethinking airway clearance and physical activity in the CFTR modulator era: adherence, burden and innovation

sections.Objectives: To describe the development of a proposed risk assessment tool by the Canadian Cystic Fibrosis Physiotherapy Advisory Group (CCFPAG) to guide clinicians in decision-making around decreasing or stopping daily airway clearance therapies (ACT).
sections.Methods: In recent years, despite a lack of clinical trial data to support the de-escalation of ACT, people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) are decreasing or stopping ACT independently. The CCFPAG is a trans-Canadian group of physiotherapists with varying years of clinical experience. For some, without the historical context of CF lung disease, it can be challenging to continue recommending traditional ACT and for others, it is equally challenging to recommend decreasing daily ACT without strong scientific evidence. In 2025, the CCFPAG met in a series of working groups to develop a preliminary tool to support clinicians in evaluating which pwCF may be able to decrease/stop daily ACT.
sections.Results: A list of clinical factors and their associated values was produced and then stratified in lower, moderate or higher risk zones. Thresholds of risk were set for each factor that aligned with Canadian consensus guidelines. Table 1 outlines the three risk categories and current considerations that need to be evaluated when determining whether ACT routines can be changed for pwCF. For all risk zones, ACT should be increased during pulmonary exacerbations, with a drop in percent predicted FEV1 (ppFEV1) from baseline, changes in baseline health status and/or with clinical discretion. Table 1 Clinical Evaluation FactorsLower Risk ZoneModerate Risk ZoneHigher Risk ZoneCFTR modulator status: time on modulator>1 year on modulator with stable clinical status <1 year on modulator or intermediate clinical status Not on modulator (due to age, genetics or intolerance) CFTR modulator: clinical response and adherenceAdequate clinical response and adherence to modulator therapy Suboptimal clinical response to modulator therapy, decreased adherence to modulator or altered dosing Stopped modulator therapy due to intolerance (e.g. liver or mental health side effects) or personal choice ppFEV1 range>90% (children), >70% (adults)70-90% (children), 50-70% (adults) <70% (children), <50% (adults) Lung imaging (CT or x-ray)No bronchiectasis or mucus pluggingMild bronchiectasis and/or mucus plugging Moderate to significant bronchiectasis and/or mucus pluggingPulmonary exacerbation rate (as determined by CF physician)No pulmonary exacerbations in previous yearMinimal number of pulmonary exacerbations in previous year Moderate-high number of pulmonary exacerbations in previous yearSputum productionMinimal or no daily sputum productionTransient daily sputum production Daily sputum production CoughMinimal or no daily coughIntermittent/stable daily cough Increase in daily cough Risk zone statementLower Risk: It may be safe to pause regular daily airway clearance therapies if all risk factors are in the lower risk zone. Moderate Risk: It may be safe to decrease airway clearance therapies if all risk factors are in the lower/moderate risk zone. Higher Risk: Regular daily airway clearance therapies should be completed if 1 or more risk factor is in the higher risk zone.
sections.Conclusion: This ACT risk assessment tool will guide clinicians in decision-making on decreasing or stopping daily ACT. Next steps in the work involve revising and evaluating the tool at clinics across the country in the context of clinical care. A coordinated quality improvement approach will be implemented to test the proposed tool for safety and applicability.

This study describes contemporary diabetes management practices within a large adult CF centre. Methods: Clinical and prescribing data was extracted in 2025 from the electronic patient records (EMIS Web). Individuals with transient gestational diabetes were excluded. Results: 439 adults were included (245 m; media...
presentations.authors_text: L. Lewis1, D. Shimmin1, H. Saumtally2,1, S. Pearson1, I. Clifton2,1, G. Spoletini1, A. Sawant1, D.G Peckham2,1

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 10 - Precision monitoring and management of cystic fibrosis related diabetes

sections.Objectives: While insulin remains the mainstay of CF diabetes (CFD) management, increasing use of non-insulin glucose lowering therapies is emerging in clinical practice. This evolution may be partly attributable to the adoption of CFTR modulators (CFTRm), which improved nutritional status, reduce pulmonary infections and decreases inflammation, factors that contribute to altered insulin sensitivity. This study describes contemporary diabetes management practices within a large adult CF centre.
sections.Methods: Clinical and prescribing data was extracted in 2025 from the electronic patient records (EMIS Web). Individuals with transient gestational diabetes were excluded.
sections.Results: 439 adults were included (245 m; median age 35 yrs, range 17–79). 160 subjects (36.5%) (90 m; median age 40 yrs, range 18–75) had CFD. Non-insulin therapies included, metformin, DPP-4 inhibitor, GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist and SGLT2 inhibitors. 111/160 (69.4%) were on insulin (57 m; median age 40 years, range 18–75).Sixteen patients received adjunctive glucose-lowering therapy alongside insulin, including metformin (n=6), tirzepatide (n=6), sitagliptin (n=11), tirzepatide/metformin (n=1), and dapagliflozin/tirzepatide (n=1). Forty-nine patients with CFRD (30.6%) were not on insulin (33 m; median age 40 years, range 21–65). Management included diet (n=12), sitagliptin (n=24), linagliptin (n=4), tirzepatide/metformin (n=4), tirzepatide (n=1), dapagliflozin/metformin (n=1), and empagliflozin/metformin/tirzepatide (n=1). Analysis of longer-term clinical outcomes is ongoing.
sections.Conclusion: Diabetes management in CF is evolving in response to changing disease phenotypes and an expanding range of glucose lowering therapies. Targeting insulin resistance, particularly in the context of CFTRm associated weight gain, may be of increasing clinical relevance. As therapeutic options broaden, a more individualised approach to CFD is likely to play a central role in optimising metabolic and clinical outcomes.

sections.Background: DHL is the combination of health literacy and digital competency skills needed to allow patients/care teams to engage with care through digital health tools and online environments. Within CF Telehealth, there is no clear framework for what skills and proficiencies are needed for both care teams and patients to engage properly with telehealth. The EU DigComp 2.2 (EU-DC) Framework outlines the digital competencies needed for EU citizens.
sections.Objectives: Using the EU-DC, this research sought to identify what proficiency levels are needed for each competency for both CF teams and PwCF.
sections.Methods: The EU-DC competency and proficiency tables were converted into an online survey. This survey was then distributed to the ECFS Telehealth Working Group to determine the required proficiency level for PwCF and CF teams using telehealth (54 members with expertise in CF telehealth).
sections.Results: 27 experts completed the survey [Table1 summarises the average proficiency level score for each competency]. All 5 EU-DC competencies were seen as necessary for CF telehealth. Generally, CF care teams should have advanced proficiency levels (so that they could also support PwCF & carers), while PwCF/Carers were expected to have a foundational knowledge in most competency areas.
sections.Conclusion: The EU-DC framework provides a useful foundation for what DHL competencies and proficiencies are needed to support CF telehealth. Overall, all competencies were seen as necessary, with greater emphasis on CF care teams having a higher proficiency level in these areas. Funding: Supported by a Vertex CF-RIA award. presentations.title: Finding the right route to the lung: precision gene editing therapy for cystic fibrosis
presentations.summary: Effective treatments for all individuals with cystic fibrosis (CF) continue to present significant challenges. CRISPR-based technologies, especially base editing, offer a promising avenue towards finding a cure. However, to minimize off-target effects, the components required for base editing must be expressed transiently in the target cells. Delivering these components as ribonucleoprotein (RNPs) complexes via engineered virus-like particles (eVLPs) presents a potentially safer alternative, given their limited lifespan within cells. By utilizing envelope glycoprotein pseudotyping, eVLPs can be customized to specifically target lung epithelial cells, addressing a crucial gap in CF treatment. Objectives: Our go...
presentations.authors_text: L. Santos1, M. Branco1, D. Schenkwein2, A.S. Coroadinha1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1iBET - Instituto de Biologia Experimental e Tecnológica , Cell Line Development & Molecular Virology Lab - Animal Cell Technology Unit, Oeiras, Portugal, 2A.I. Virtanen Institute for Molecular Sciences, Kuopio, Finland
presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 07 - Old and new – exploring the portfolio for cystic fibrosis treatment

sections.Objectives: Our goal is to evaluate the effectiveness of eVLPs pseudotyped with three different viral envelope glycoproteins in delivering base editing components to target lung cells.
sections.Methods: As proof of concept, we developed a lung epithelial cell line that stably expresses a mutant version of the Green Fluorescent Protein (GFP) that lacks fluorescence. This will allow us to assess how effectively the eVLPs can deliver the editing components to cells.
sections.Results: Our findings show that eVLPs can effectively deliver base-editing components into cells, allowing precise correction of the GFP mutation and restoring its fluorescence. Of the three tested envelopes, one exhibits a high affinity for lung cells, resulting in four times more editing efficiency.
sections.Conclusion: These results highlight the potential of base editing as a precise gene-correcting approach for CF, especially for nonsense mutations that are unresponsive to current therapies. This strategy could provide a viable pathway to restore CFTR function and mitigate disease progression.

sections.Objectives: To characterize trends in cystic fibrosis (CF) lung transplantation (LT) following approval of elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor (Trikafta).
sections.Methods: We identified CF patients ≥18 years undergoing LT from 01/2016-09/2024 in the United Network for Organ Sharing database. Patients were stratified into pre- and post-Trikafta eras (2016-2019 vs. 2020-2024). We compared transplant volumes, transplant centers, recipient characteristics, and post-transplant survival between eras. Temporal trends in transplant volume were evaluated using the Mann-Kendall test. Survival was assessed using Kaplan-Meier analysis and multivariable Cox regression.
sections.Results: In total, 1,135 CF patients underwent LT during the study period (pre-Trikafta: 922; post-Trikafta: 213). Annual CF LT volume decreased by 81%, from 231 transplants/year pre-Trikafta to 45 transplants/year post-Trikafta, representing a significant downward trend (p=0.001). The number of centers performing CF LT decreased from 63 to 52, with median transplants per center declining from 10 (IQR: 7-19) to 3 (IQR: 2-6.5) (p<0.001). High-volume centers (>10 transplants) declined from 31 (49%) to 3 (6%). Post-Trikafta recipients had similar age at LT compared with pre-Trikafta (median 32 vs. 31 years, p=0.15), as well as comparable rates of pre-LT extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) and mechanical ventilation. Post-Trikafta recipients spent less time on the waitlist (median 40 vs. 53 days, p=0.34). Racial distribution also shifted significantly, with proportionally more Black (6.1% vs 3.7%) and Hispanic (13.6% vs 5.6%) recipients post-Trikafta (p<0.001). One-year post-LT survival was similar between eras (91.5% vs 91.7%, p=0.84).
sections.Conclusion: The introduction of Trikafta was associated with an 81% reduction in CF LT volume and marked consolidation of center participation, with near elimination of high-volume CF LT programs. This shift has profound implications for transplant program planning, resource allocation, and organ distribution policy.

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 09 - Rethinking airway clearance and physical activity in the CFTR modulator era: adherence, burden and innovation

sections.Background: In the context of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) treatment, the anecdotally reported frequency of airway clearance (AC) and chronic inhaled mucoactive therapy (CIMT) has declined with many people with CF (pwCF) stopping altogether, some on their own accord. This study aims to quantify AC and CIMT de-escalation after ETI initiation.
sections.Methods: This quality improvement project included all pwCF at our pediatric CF centre who started ETI between December 2019 and September 2025. AC and CIMT practice were collected from the electronic medical record.
sections.Results: Between December 2019 and September 2025, 92 pwCF started ETI (51% female, mean age 8.3 years, 47% F508del homozygous, mean ppFEV1 92.0%). Prior to ETI, 76/92 (83%) individuals performed daily AC. Of these, 24/76 (32%) stopped and 18/76 (24%) reduced AC after starting ETI. 17/24 (71%) stopped AC within two years of starting ETI; 10 (42%) within the first year. 11/18 (61%) reduced AC within two years of starting ETI; 3 (17%) within the first year. The median (interquartile range) time to stopping AC was 14.2 (7.6, 24.9) months and for reducing AC 21.6 (16.8,42.4) months. Prior to starting ETI, 45/92 (49%) pwCF were taking hypertonic saline (HTS) and 26/92 (28%) were taking dornase alfa (DA). After starting ETI, 24/45 (53%) stopped HTS and 22/26 (85%) stopped DA. 17/24 (71%) stopped HTS within two years of starting ETI; 5 (21%) within the first year. 16/22 (73%) patients stopped DA within two years of starting ETI; 9 (41%) within the first year. The median time to stopping HTS was 17.2 (13.4, 26.7) months and for DA 13.6 (9.14, 24.76) months. Analysis of AC and CIMT de-escalation kinetics using logistic mixed-effects models is ongoing.
sections.Conclusion: The reduction of AC and CIMT use after ETI initiation is substantial, but variable in extent and timing. Understanding the impact of this treatment de-escalation on long term lung health is critical to appropriate treatment of pwCF in the modern era.

However, there is evidence for earlier onset of glucose tolerance abnormalities in those with CF and need for minimally invasive tool to track their trajectory and quantify the impact of CFTR modulator Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI). We tested changes in continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) metrics before and after ETI initiation across different pediatric age groups. sections.Methods: We included participants to the French MODUL-CF registry aged 3-18 years with at least 24 hours of CGM data preceding the initiation of ETI and a 2nd CGM 12±3 months following drug initiation. The cohort was stratified in 3 groups based on the age at ETI initiation: <6 (n=), 6-12 (n=) and >12 years (n=). Percentage time in hypo- (<70mg/dL) and hyper-(>140mg/dL)glycemia and in target range (TIR, 70-140mg/dL) were compared before and after ETI.
sections.Results: We included 98 patients with CF who were started on ETI. Those aged >12 y displayed a marked increase of TIR (p=0.029) with a reduction of percentage time in hypoglycemia at 12 months ETI(<70mg/dL) (p <0.001). The 6-12 years group displayed a lower time spent in hyperglycemia (p < 0.001), while CGM metrics did not differ before and after starting ETI in participants younger than 6 years.
sections.Conclusion: CGM metrics describe an improvement of glucose control in those older than 6 years receiving ETI , with relatively stable profile in the youngest ones.

sections.Background: Acute pulmonary exacerbations (APEs) are often managed by phone without spirometry. This QI project studies a protocolized evaluation and treatment of pwCF with acute respiratory symptoms thru telehealth and remote objective monitoring.
sections.Methods: This multi-site QI initiative expands urgent telehealth evaluation. Inclusion criteria were pwCF age 6 years and up. Subjects enrolled received spirometers, scales, and pulse oximeters. A protocol for APE treatment was based on FEV1 and CRISS score at the time of exacerbation. Key process measures included number of acute telehealth visits scheduled within 48 hours, number of visits with objective data collected and adherence to treatment algorithm.
sections.Results: 153 subjects have been enrolled. Mean age is 29.8±14.7 and 65% are female. 89% are Caucasian, 4.8% African American, 4.1% Hispanic. Baseline FEV1 is 72.6±27.0 % predicted. 84% of subjects reported current use of a CFTR modulator. There have been 144 acute calls, which have resulted in 107 telehealth visits for urgent evaluation. At the telehealth visit, mean FEV1 was 63.6±19.6. In 62% of cases an oral antibiotic was prescribed, in 10.5% IV antibiotics were prescribed. Hospitalization was recommended for 16%. 80% of participants had follow up visits scheduled. At follow up FEV1 was 69.7±25.7. 6.9% of patients had further escalation of antibiotic therapy and 12.6% were hospitalized. Data collection is ongoing.
sections.Conclusion: pwCF and CF providers have been willing to utilize telehealth with expanded remote monitoring for APEs. By using process maps and PDSA cycles we have been successful in scheduling telehealth visits within 2 business days of a sick call and have utilized home spirometry in 75% of telehealth calls and had follow up visits in 80% of cases. The initiative is ongoing with a remaining goal being to assess patient satisfaction with the use of telehealth for APEs.

sections.Objectives: Health benefits of yoga are widely recognised for the general population and some medical conditions, but evidence of clinical efficacy is limited for CF. We conducted ‘Yoga Outcomes Get Assessed in CF (YOGA-CF)’ – a UK multicentre RCT, to investigate effectiveness of a bespoke CF-specific online 12-week yoga intervention for adults with CF.
sections.Methods: Participants were randomised to usual care or yoga. Spirometry, one-minute sit-to-stand, the Cystic Fibrosis Questionnaire-Revised (CFQ-R) and other questionnaires were collected via video-call pre- and post-intervention (0 and 12 weeks), and after 12-weeks of follow-up (week 24). The primary outcome measure was difference in respiratory-related quality of life as measured by CFQ-R at 12-weeks. Sample size was calculated to detect a minimally clinically important difference of 4 on the CFQ-R respiratory domain, with power of 80%, 5% significance level, assuming a standard deviation (SD) of 12 (total n=314 including 10% allowance for drop-out). An economic evaluation will estimate cost-effectiveness.
sections.Results: 27 sites opened from April 2024; 1,265 patients screened with 340 (27%) recruited (108% of target). Main reasons for ineligibility (n=568; 45%) included: unstable CF (n=116) and other trial involvement (n=93). 357 (28%) declined participation [main reason: lack of time (n=142)]. Recruited demographics: 66% female; mean age 36y (SD=11); mean percent predicted FEV1 78% (SD=21) (table). To date, 31 serious adverse events, all unrelated, or unlikely related, to intervention. 30 withdrawals, main reason: lack of time (n=13). Final follow-up January 2026. Primary outcome data to be presented.
sections.Conclusion: YOGA-CF completed to time and target, highlighting the CF community’s appetite and desire to identify non-pharmacological strategies to improve outcomes. Main results will be available for ECFC 2026, contributing significantly to the evidence base for yoga as an exercise option for people with CF. presentations.title: Waitlist and lung transplant outcomes by sex for adults with cystic fibrosis: the United States experience
presentations.summary: Objective: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a key indication for lung transplantation (LT). Despite medical advances, many adults develop end-stage lung disease and require LT; women with CF experience earlier infections and worse life outcomes. The impact of sex on access to LT and post-LT outcomes is unclear. We aimed to quantify differences in waitlist and post-LT outcomes by sex. Methods: We utilized the United Network for Organ Sharing (UNOS) database, including adults listed for single-organ LT from January 2005 to November 2023. We analyzed baseline and post-LT outcomes with Chi-square and Kruskal-Wallis tests. Waitlist and transplant outcomes were modeled with Fine-Gray and Cox proportional hazards respectively...

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a key indication for lung transplantation (LT). Despite medical advances, many adults develop end-stage lung disease and require LT; women with CF experience earlier infections and worse life outcomes. The impact of sex on access to LT and post-LT outcomes is unclear. We aimed to quantify differences in waitlist and post-LT outcomes by sex.
sections.Methods: We utilized the United Network for Organ Sharing (UNOS) database, including adults listed for single-organ LT from January 2005 to November 2023. We analyzed baseline and post-LT outcomes with Chi-square and Kruskal-Wallis tests. Waitlist and transplant outcomes were modeled with Fine-Gray and Cox proportional hazards respectively. We adjusted for age, BMI, lung allocation score (LAS), waitlist time and post-LT length of stay (LOS). Medians were reported as median [IQR] and hazard ratios (HR) as 95% confidence interval.
sections.Results: Women were younger (28[23-37] vs 30[25-39]; p<0.001), had lower BMI (19.2[17.9-20.9] vs 19.8[18.2-22.0]; p<0.001) and more diabetes (52.9% vs 45.9%; p<0.001) with similar LAS to men. Women waited longer (79[20-216] vs 57[17-166] days; p<0.001), had lower 1-year transplant rates (68.9% vs 77.0%; p<0.001) and higher 1-year waitlist death/deterioration (12.1% vs 8.6%; p<0.001). Fine-Gray subdistribution HR for female sex for waitlist deterioration was 1.329[1.07-1.65], p=0.009. Among recipients (male 1490, female 1417), ventilation was prolonged in women (>5d vent 19.4% vs 13.1%; p<0.001) and LOS was longer (18[13-28] vs 17[13-25]; p=0.01). Post-LT survival was similar by sex (1-year 90.3% vs 91.4%; 5-year 61.7% vs 62.6%). Adjusted HR for female sex in post-LT mortality was 0.928[0.831-1.036], p=0.18.
sections.Conclusion: Among US adults with CF evaluated for LT, female sex is associated with longer wait time, worse waitlist outcomes and greater postoperative resource use; long-term post-LT survival is comparable. Expedited LT workup and listing for women must be considered.

sections.Objectives: To trial Intrapulmonary Ventilation (IPV); a new airway clearance technique (ACT) device at the All Wales Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre (AWACF) and gain insight into patient subjective experience using Sentec’s IPV1® device. The IPV1® delivers fast air bursts during inspiration and expiration to loosen secretions, reduce atelectasis, and splint the airways for gas exchange. Purpose: i) To identify patient perceived benefits/difficulties of IPV1® system during respiratory exacerbations at the AWACF ii) To compare patient experience of the IPV1® to their prior experience using the discontinued MetaNeb device.
sections.Methods: The IPV1® was trialled on people admitted from 15/10/25-18/11/25. Average stay was 12 days. 6 patients used the IPV1®, 4 completed the questionnaire (2 male and 2 female) on discharge to note comparisons to the MetaNeb, duration of treatments, changes in symptoms and overall opinion.
sections.Results: All patients reported improved mucus clearance and preferred IPV1® over MetaNeb. 75% found the IPV1® easier to use and more comfortable, with improved breathing. 75% of patients found exercise tolerance improved since using IPV1® and noted they would feel confident using the device independently. In fact, one patient was able to at the end of their stay. Participants noted their overall impression. Positive thoughts included that the device was ‘easy to set up’, ‘easy to use’ and ‘not complicated’. They felt the adjustable amplitude allowed vibrations to ‘shake all parts of the body’ and aid ‘deeper breaths’. One said it is ‘an awesome device’. Some challenges highlighted that, ‘it took a while to get used to', caused ‘cheeks and jaw to hurt’ and the ‘display screen is too small’.
sections.Conclusion: The IPV1® was reported as a preferable ACT by all when compared to the MetaNeb. Overall, it seems benefits outweighed the challenges. As the MetaNeb is being discontinued, the questionnaire shows the IPV1® to be a positive alternative therapy for inpatient treatment.

sections.Objectives: This study aimed to identify early predictors of cystic fibrosis-related diabetes (CFRD) in children by comparing standard oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) categories against physiological markers of beta-cell function.
sections.Methods: Data from 161 pediatric patients with cystic fibrosis (CF) were analyzed. Researchers reviewed historical metabolic data, including OGTT glucose categories, insulin sensitivity (OGIS), and beta-cell glucose sensitivity (B-GS)—calculated via modeling glucose, insulin, and C-peptide levels over 180 minutes. Simple plasma C-peptide levels at 30 minutes (CP30) were also evaluated. The primary outcome was a CFRD diagnosis. Logistic regression assessed predictors, including the impact of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) modulator therapy.
sections.Results: Over a median seven-year follow-up, 27% of patients developed CFRD. While 60% of the cohort initiated ETI therapy, it was not a statistically significant protective factor in the models. Traditional OGTT categories and insulin sensitivity (OGIS) showed poor predictive power. Conversely, beta-cell markers were highly discriminatory: B-GS: Higher sensitivity was a significant protective factor (OR 0.96, 95% CI 0.93-0.99; p=0.004). CP30: Higher levels were a strong independent protective factor (OR 0.51, 95% CI 0.27-0.81; p<0.01), identifying patients with preserved function unlikely to progress.
sections.Conclusion: Standard glucose tolerance categories in early life are inadequate predictors of CFRD risk. Markers of beta-cell function, specifically B-GS and the easily measured CP30, are superior predictors of progression. CP30 offers a practical clinical tool to stratify high-risk patients for closer monitoring.

sections.Methods: This was a randomized, two-arm trial in pwCF, children, and adults, conducted over 52 weeks in a paediatric CF Unit. The study population consisted of two groups: a Usual Care (UC) group, in which pwCF received routine follow-up at cystic fibrosis outpatient clinics, and a Home Spirometry (HS) group, in which telemonitoring with weekly home spirometry was implemented in addition to usual care. Lung function was assessed using electronic devices connected to a centralized platform accessible via the CF Unit.
sections.Results: Overall, 104 pwCF were included in the analysis, comprising 52 patients in the HS group and 52 in the UC group, with median age 16 years (SD: 7). The adherence of patients in the telemonitoring program in the period of 52 weeks was 21.2% with 11 of 52 patients performing a spirometry once per week in more than 80% of their chances of spirometry follow-up, while twenty five patients performed a spirometry in more than 50% of their chances (48.1% adherence). A statistically significant difference was observed between the two groups in the time to initiation of antibiotic therapy following the first exacerbation (p= 0.026). The HS group was associated with a higher hazard of earlier antibiotic initiation than the UC group (hazard ratio [HR]: 1.67; 95% CI: 1.06-2.64). Moreover, time to antibiotic initiation after the second exacerbation differed significantly between groups (p = 0.026), with a higher hazard of earlier treatment in the HS group (HR: 1.92; 95% CI: 1.08-3.39).
sections.Conclusion: PwCF participated in a telemonitoring program for 52 weeks and demonstrated adequate adherence. The implementation of the program improves the management of pulmonary exacerbations by facilitating earlier initiation of antibiotic therapy.
