sections.Objectives: The emergence of Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Receptor modulators (CFTRm) and digital health technologies create opportunities to reorganise cystic fibrosis (CF) care. MuCopilot is a software as a medical device designed for remote patient monitoring, integrating smartphone-based tests assessing lung function (Mobile Sustained Phonation Test), exercise capacity (Mobile Endurance Walking Test), nocturnal cough (Mobile Sleep Cough Monitoring Test) and a CF-specific symptom and quality-of-life questionnaire (CF-scan). The validation study MuControl (NCT06147778) evaluates the clinical performance of MuCopilot.
sections.Methods: French people with CF (pwCF), aged ≥18 in a stable condition, were recruited from eight French CF centers (Bordeaux, Lyon, Marseille, Nice, Reims, Roscoff, Rouen, Toulouse). Over 3 months, participants completed 9 times the MuCopilot modules: at Day 0 (inclusion), D3, D5, D7, M1, M2 and one day before the exit visit (M3) with supervised evaluations at D0 and M3. The association between at-home digital tests and in-clinic standard tests will be assessed using Pearson correlation.
sections.Results: Seventy pwCF were enrolled (52.9% female, mean age 33.5 ± 11.3 years, 92.9% using CFTRm). 1869 digital evaluations were collected: 540 phonation tests, 538 walking tests, 363 cough tests and 428 questionnaires. Two participants discontinued due to serious adverse events unrelated to MuCopilot. The completion rates of the modules varied from 69.1% for CF-scan questionnaire to 55.9% for the sleep cough monitoring test. Correlations between digital and standard clinical tests will be available for congress.
sections.Conclusion: This analysis demonstrates the feasibility of implementing MuCopilot across multiple French CF centers. MuControl allowed the collection of a high volume of digital evaluations with strong engagement from pwCF. These early results support the potential use and clinical integration of a clinically validated MuCopilot for remote monitoring of CF.

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 08 - Life course outcomes in modern cystic fibrosis: transplantation, ageing and pregnancy across registries

sections.Objectives: Lung transplantation (LTx) is an established therapy for patients with advanced cystic fibrosis (CF), yet robust long-term, single-centre outcome data remain limited. We analysed three decades of LTx in Zurich, focusing on survival by transplant indication and the relative long-term advantage in CF.
sections.Methods: This retrospective single-centre cohort included all lung transplantations performed at the University Hospital Zurich between 1992 and 2024 (n=688). Survival status was systematically verified using hospital follow-up and official registries. Overall survival was estimated using Kaplan–Meier methods and compared by log-rank tests. Cox proportional hazards models assessed the association between indication and mortality, with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) as reference.
sections.Results: Median overall survival was 8.04 years, with survival of 86%, 60%, 40%, 29% and 16% at 1, 5, 10, 15 and 20 years, respectively, and survival extending beyond 30 years. Survival did not differ across transplant decades (p=0.50). Indication was a strong determinant of outcome. CF recipients showed the most favourable long-term survival (mean 9.50±6.88 years; median 9.25 years). Compared with COPD, CF was associated with substantially lower mortality (HR 0.53, 95% CI 0.41–0.68; p<0.001), while idiopathic interstitial pneumonia, idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension and other indications did not differ significantly. Increasing recipient age was associated with higher mortality (per-year HR 1.02, 95% CI 1.02–1.03; p<0.001), as was older donor age (>60 vs <30 years: HR 1.98; p<0.001). Survival was comparable between donation after circulatory death and donation after brain death (HR 0.88; p=0.56). Bilateral transplantation was associated with improved survival compared with single LTx (p=0.005).
sections.Conclusion: Over 30 years, long-term survival after LTx in Zurich remained stable, with CF showing a sustained survival advantage over other indications. These findings reinforce CF as an indication with particularly favourable long-term post-transplant outcomes and highlight age and procedure type as key determinants of survival.

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 09 - Rethinking airway clearance and physical activity in the CFTR modulator era: adherence, burden and innovation

sections.Objectives: Exercise and airway clearance techniques (ACTs) are both routinely recommended for people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). In the era of Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) therapy, some people using physical activity as a substitute for traditional ACTs. To investigate how adults CF on ETI self-changed home ACTs routines with physical activity.
sections.Methods: An observational analysis was conducted on 230 patients undergoing ETI therapy aged ≥18 years referred to Verona Cystic Fibrosis Center. A self-administered questionnaire was provided to pwCF to assess the participants’ and practice regarding the use of exercise as a substitute for traditional airway clearance techniques.
sections.Results: A total of 230 adults with cystic fibrosis (133 females; mean age 35.7 ± 11.4 years) were included in the analysis. The mean frequency of physical activity was 2.6 ± 1.5 times per week. Among adults performing airway clearance techniques (ACTs) (n = 207), 82 patients (39.6%) reported replacing at least some ACTs sessions with physical activity. In the subgroup of patients reporting ACTs replacement with physical activity (n = 95), the reported frequency was 1–2 times per week (51.6%), 3–4 times per week (38.9%), and 5–7 times per week (9.5%). Individual aerobic exercise was the most commonly reported form of physical activity (91.6%).
sections.Conclusion: The increase of patients’ substitution of ACTs with physical activity has an important impact on the management of pwCF in the era of ETI. Physiotherapists should actively explore patient’s beliefs as a part of the process for prescribing and monitoring new or modified exercise and airway clearance regimens. Further studies are required to evaluate the clinical effectiveness of physical activity as a substitute for traditional airway clearance techniques.

sections.Objectives: Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) can detect glycaemic abnormalities before the onset of cystic fibrosis-related diabetes (CFRD), yet whether these early changes have clinical importance remains uncertain. This study investigated the associations between CGM-derived metrics and key clinical parameters in young people with cystic fibrosis (CF).
sections.Methods: This retrospective analysis included young people with CF aged 10–21 years who had at least one CGM assessment within a three-year period from 2021 to 2024. Patients on insulin therapy or with confounding health conditions were excluded from the study. CGM metrics included estimated HbA1c, average glucose levels, time below range (TBR; glucose<3.9 mmol/L), time in range (TIR; glucose 3.9–10.0 mmol/L), and time above range (TAR; glucose>10 mmol/L). Clinical variables included ppFEV₁, BMI z-score, frequency of pulmonary exacerbations, bacterial colonisation status, and CFTR modulator therapy. Associations were analysed using linear mixed models.
sections.Results: Twenty-six patients (58% male; 77% homozygous for the ΔF508 mutation; all pancreatic insufficient) contributed a total of 48 CGM measurements. None of the participants received systemic corticosteroids during the assessment periods. TAR emerged as the main CGM parameter linked to clinical status: higher BMI z-score predicted increased TAR (β=1.20, 95% CI 0.09–2.31; p=0.035), while higher ppFEV₁ was associated with decreased TAR (β =-0.12, 95% CI -0.20 to -0.05; p=0.002). No significant associations were observed between clinical parameters and other CGM metrics.
sections.Conclusion: In our study, higher TAR was associated with higher BMI and lower lung function, suggesting that improved nutritional status in the era of CFTR modulators might be accompanied by underlying metabolic vulnerabilities. These findings support including CGM in routine surveillance, particularly among patients with higher BMI or declining lung function, to enable earlier intervention before overt CFRD develops.

sections.Objectives: Cough has been associated with disease severity and identified as a sign of inadequate disease control and early symptom of pulmonary exacerbation in different chronic lung diseases. In the presented study, continuous nocturnal cough monitoring is evaluated in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) to describe the natural history of nocturnal cough in CF patients receiving standard of care CF treatment.
sections.Methods: International, multi-centre prospective observational study on nocturnal continuous, objective cough monitoring by a therapy monitoring app (PARI Connect App) over a 24 weeks period in pwCF >12years aiming to enroll 250 pwCF. Subjective cough severity is reported daily by a visual analogue scale also integrated in the therapy management app. Clinical and sociodemographic parameters, CF specific and cough related quality of life are assessed initially and at 3 and 6 months.
sections.Results: 89/250 pwCF have been enrolled so far, 58% female, median age 28y (IQR 19.0-37.5y), median FEV1 79%pred (IQR 60-95%pred), 78/89 (87.6%) receiving CFTR modulator therapy (74/78 (94.9%) elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor). 55/87 (63.2%) had no pulmonary exacerbation in the previous 12months before inclusion in the study. So far, over a median observation duration of 129 days/patient 4,939 cough monitorings with a median duration of 7.7 hours (IQR 6.9-8.6 hours) and a median number of 1 cough (IQR 0-5 coughs) per monitoring have been recorded. The individual statistical analysis of cough frequency per patient is still pending but will be available by the time of the ECFS.
sections.Conclusion: This study will provide evidence on the suitability of non-invasive, nocturnal cough monitoring as a measure of disease control. Additionally, our study will provide a critical assessment of the utility of digital cough monitoring as a remote endpoint in future therapy studies. The study is funded by an independent innovation grant from Vertex pharmaceuticals.

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 07 - Old and new – exploring the portfolio for cystic fibrosis treatment

sections.Objectives: Pulmonary symptoms in cystic fibrosis (CF) dramatically impair patient health and quality of life. The FDA-approved mucolytic recombinant human DNase1 (dornase alfa) shows reduced efficacy in many patients due to actin-rich mucus. Next-generation DNase-based therapeutics must combine resistance to actin inhibition, robust catalytic activity, and scalable manufacturing. Simple cells such as E. coli offer ideal production platforms but have so far failed to express soluble, active human DNase1L2, a DNase1 paralog naturally resistant to actin inhibition. To overcome this bottleneck, we exploited ancestral sequence reconstruction (ASR), an evolutionary protein engineering technique that resurrects ancient enzymes with potentially superior properties, aiming to identify actin-resistant, catalytically active DNase variants optimized for efficient microbial production.
sections.Methods: ASR was used to infer ancient DNase sequences predating the divergence of DNase1 and DNase1L2. Reconstructed variants were expressed in E. coli and evaluated for solubility, enzymatic activity, and resistance to actin inhibition. DNase activity was characterized with agarose gel assays and fluorescence-based assays using fluorophore-labeled DNA. AlphaFold structural predictions were used to analyze active-site conservation and potential determinants of actin resistance.
sections.Results: An ancestral DNase variant inferred from deep evolutionary nodes of the DNase1/DNase1L2 clade showed improved solubility compared to DNase1L2 while retaining catalytic activity and strong resistance to actin inhibition under conditions that markedly inhibit recombinant human DNase1.
sections.Conclusion: This study identifies a robust, actin-resistant ancestral DNase with potential to overcome key limitations of current CF mucolytic therapy. Leveraging ASR enables access to DNase functionalities not readily achievable with extant human enzymes, supporting the development of more effective and accessible CF treatments.

sections.Objectives: Once thought of as a pediatric disease, the proportion of adults living with cystic fibrosis (CF) in Canada soared from <10% in the 1970’s to over 65% today. As care improves, people with CF are living longer and healthier lives, but little is known about these older adults. The objectives of this study were to: 1) quantify the population of older adults over time, and 2) describe the cohort of older adults.
sections.Methods: This study included all individuals age ≥40 years reported in the Canadian CF Registry between 2015-2024. Objective 2 was restricted to 2024. Continuous variables were summarized using median and interquartile range (IQR), and categorical variables using frequency and proportion.
sections.Results: The number of people age ≥40 increased by nearly 70% from 2015 to 2024, with the largest increase among those age ≥60. The proportion of individuals age ≥40 among the whole CF population increased from 15.1% in 2015 to 23.4% in 2024. Table 1 describes characteristics of older adults in 2024. The proportion of individuals who were homozygous F508del decreased with age, and among those age ≥60 only 68.0% had an F508del (compared to >85% of the younger age groups). 27.8% of those age ≥60 had two non-F508del variants, and most were considered mild variants (class III-V). The majority of those aged <60 years were diagnosed with CF before age 5, while most of those age ≥60 were diagnosed in adulthood, and 32.2% were diagnosed after age 50. There were no differences in clinical outcomes between the age groups, however the oldest age group had the lowest prevalence of common CF-related comorbidities (CF-related diabetes and pancreatic insufficiency) but highest prevalence of osteoporosis. The oldest age group also had the lowest proportion of history of CFTR modulator use.
sections.Conclusion: In 2024, nearly a quarter of all Canadians with CF were age ≥40, with the cohort age ≥60 being the fastest growing. This oldest age group appeared to be the healthiest, with lower prevalence of typical CF comorbidities. Prevalence of traditional age-related comorbidities (e.g., cardiovascular disease) were unknown due to data limitations, so it is critical that CF registries evolve to capture this information, for a more fulsome understanding of the health of older adults with CF. sections.Background: Elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor (ETI) therapy improves respiratory function and prognosis in CF patients (pwCF). Given the benefits, physical activity (PA) must be incorporating into pwCF's lifestyle, for their health and quality of life (QoL).
sections.Objectives: We evaluate PA and sedentary behavior (SB) of adults pwCF and assess correlation between PA/SB, with clinical and socio-demographic parameters, QoL and motivation towards health-oriented PA.
sections.Methods: Participants were enrolled in the ‘Lyon Adult CF Center‘, in a cross-sectional observational single-center study (clinicaltrials.gov identifier NCT06507956). All subjects answered self-administered questionnaires: Global Physical Activity Questionnaire-ONAPS-PAQ for PA and SB; CF Questionnaire-Revised (CFQ-R) for QoL; EPICES score for social deprivation and motivation scale towards health-oriented physical activity (EMAPS). Clinical data were also collected.
sections.Results: Two hundred and ninety participants were included: 162 males; 18 lung transplanted patients; 231 taking ETI; median (m) age 32 years; m FEV1 79 of predicted; m BMI 22.5kg/m2; m EPICES score 17. Activity profiles were characterized by the combination of PA level and SB: Profile PA /SB Low sedentary Moderate sedentary High sedentary (167) Inactive (34) 2923Active 92664Very Active 136480
sections.Conclusion: We identified pwCF whose lifestyle habits reflect a greater health risk. By successfully identifying this patient profile, physiotherapists and adapted physical activity instructors could offer them specific and tailored MVPA interventions for the benefit of their global health. A subgroup of high sedentary or inactive pwCF taking ETI will be studied more specifically using accelerometers follow-up and a qualitative semi-structured interview identifying motivational barriers and levers for action. Keywords: CFTR; Cystic Fibrosis; Adult; Chronic disease; Physical Activity; Sedentary behavior; Lifestyle; Aging; Quality of Life.

Here, we apply unsupervised machine learning to Cystic Fibrosis Foundation Patient Registry (CFFPR) data to identify clusters of clinically-similar encounters, then examine whether transitions between these clusters are associated with changes in outcomes. Methods: We examined encounter-level data (therapeutics, microbiology, and laboratory data) from 1.9M clinical encounters in the CFFPR from 2011 to 2023, from 41K pwCF without lung transplant. We utilized a mean imputation and forward-fill strategy for missing...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Northwestern University Feinberg School of Medicine, Pulmonary and Critical Care Medicine, Chicago, United States

sections.Objectives: There is significant heterogeneity in outcomes of people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). Understanding when and why clinical trajectories diverge will be critical in identifying at-risk pwCF. Here, we apply unsupervised machine learning to Cystic Fibrosis Foundation Patient Registry (CFFPR) data to identify clusters of clinically-similar encounters, then examine whether transitions between these clusters are associated with changes in outcomes.
sections.Methods: We examined encounter-level data (therapeutics, microbiology, and laboratory data) from 1.9M clinical encounters in the CFFPR from 2011 to 2023, from 41K pwCF without lung transplant. We utilized a mean imputation and forward-fill strategy for missing data, used k-means clustering to identify encounters with similar characteristics, then identified encounters that directly precede cluster transitions (“transition-associated encounters”). Differences in characteristics were examined using t-tests for continuous variables and chi-squared tests for categorical variables.
sections.Results: Optimizing for differences in lung transplant-free survival, we identified 8 distinct clusters of encounters. 505K encounters were transition-associated out of 1.8M non-terminal encounters; 38K of 41K pwCF experienced at least one cluster transition. Transition-associated encounters were associated with a higher rate of 1-year lung transplant-free survival than those that were not (98.5% vs. 97.4%, p<0.001). Transition-associated encounters were associated with lower average age and higher average percent-predicted FEV1.
sections.Conclusion: Unsupervised clustering identifies clinical states associated with lung transplant-free survival. Our findings suggest that transitions between clinical states are common and are associated with improved morbidity and mortality. These transitions are more frequent in younger patients with higher FEV1, suggesting that clinical "flux" may be a hallmark of earlier disease stages.

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 07 - Old and new – exploring the portfolio for cystic fibrosis treatment

sections.Objectives: Forskolin-induced swelling (FIS) in patient-derived intestinal organoids (PDIOs) is a promising biomarker to predict individual drug response in pwCF. Previous communication with EMA underlined biomarker validation needs, including evaluation of rare CFTR variants and identification of low or non-responders. We aimed to characterise preclinical FIS responses of rare CFTR variants to a novel triple CFTR modulator, diponecaftor. Based on FIS responses, patients were stratified as presumable responders or non-responders for inclusion in a phase IIb double-blind diponecaftor clinical trial, supporting validation by EMA.
sections.Methods: The predictive value of FIS was evaluated using diponecaftor, across three cohorts. Cohort A (n=26) included randomly selected pwCF with rare variants. Cohort B (n=15) included pwCF with rare variants preselected for high FIS response. Both cohorts participated in an 8-week double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover trial with outcomes including ppFEV₁ and SCC. FIS assays were done for A and B. Cohort C (n=11) consisted of F508del homozygotes from a 4-week diponecaftor trial and served as reference. FIS was measured in matched CF-PDIOs enabling validation of FIS as biomarker of therapeutic response.
sections.Results: Mean FIS responses were higher in cohort B than in cohort A and comparable to cohort C. Improvements in ppFEV₁ and SCC were greater in cohorts B and C than in cohort A. FIS correlated more strongly with clinical outcomes than clinical outcomes with each other. Placebo responses showed substantial intra-patient variability.
sections.Conclusion: FIS in PDIOs may predict individual clinical responses to CFTR modulators more reliably than clinical outcomes alone. Placebo variability highlights limitations of relying only on clinical measures. Combining FIS with clinical data may facilitate identification of non-responders and enable personalized therapy development for pwCF with rare CFTR variants in future trials. H2020-SC1-2017-755021

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a genetic, multiorgan disorder associated with meconium ileus, reduced birth weight and shorter gestational age. However, evidence on gestational characteristics in pregnancies affected by foetal CF remains limited. We aimed to investigate potential intrauterine effects of foetal CF on pregnancy and at birth outcomes.
sections.Methods: Data from the Medical Birth Registry of Norway (1967–2021) were linked to the Norwegian CF Registry. Foetal CF was defined as offspring with a confirmed diagnosis according to the CF registry criteria. A total of 308 CF births were compared with 3.1 million non-CF births. Data were prospectively recorded during pregnancy and at birth. Multiple pregnancies and cases with combined maternal and foetal CF were excluded. We compared live births with and without foetal CF using independent samples T-test or Mann-Whitney U test for continuous variables, and chi-square test for categorical variables.
sections.Results: CF newborns had mean birth weight 157 g lower than non-CF newborns (95% CI 96–219). The z-score for birth weight specific for sex and gestational length was modestly but significantly reduced (-0.2; 95% CI -0.3– -0.1). The birth weight to placenta weight ratio was significantly lower (median 5.1; IQR 4.5–5.8) in CF than in non-CF newborns (median 5.4; IQR 4.8–6.0; p<0.01), while placenta weight did not differ. CF pregnancies were associated with a significantly increased risk of polyhydramnios, with an odds ratio of 7.1 (95% CI 4.3–11.8) compared to non-CF pregnancies. Pre-eclampsia and gestational hypertension were not associated with CF in the offspring.
sections.Conclusion: Lower birth weight in CF newborns is consistent with previous reports; however, the increased prevalence of polyhydramnios and altered birth weight to placenta weight ratio suggest distinct gestational adaptations associated with foetal CF. These findings may have clinical implications as intrauterine precision therapies for CF evolve.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Barts Health NHS Trust, London, United Kingdom
presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 09 - Rethinking airway clearance and physical activity in the CFTR modulator era: adherence, burden and innovation

sections.Objectives: People with cystic fibrosis (CF) are often less active than their healthy peers. Previous research has demonstrated that increased exercise levels correlate with an improvement in FEV1 while aerobic capacity is an important survival predictor within CF. This study aimed to explore current exercise levels within a paediatric CF centre and identify barriers to increasing these levels.
sections.Methods: A 31 question anonymous electronic survey was sent to children with CF aged 5 - 17 years old (n=63) and their parents in December 2025. The survey consisted of a mixture of multiple choice and open ended questions looking at; demographics, current exercise levels, perceptions of the benefits of exercise and barriers to exercise.
sections.Results: A 30% response rate (n=19) was achieved (58% female, n=11; 42% male, n=8) of which 73.6% (n=14) were taking modulators. 100% (n=19) of respondents engaged in moderate-vigorous weekly exercise with 36.8% (n=7) completing over 300mins per week. Respondents identified the following themes with regards to the benefit of exercise – positive impact on lung health, keeping physically fit and healthy, keeping strong and enjoying it. Barriers to exercise identified by paediatric respondents included time, weather, cost, motivation and being unwell. Parents were in agreement, citing lack of space as an additional barrier. Both children and parents highlighted ideas for and access to local gyms/clubs as the most useful strategies to encourage increased exercise levels.
sections.Conclusion: Although all respondents engage in weekly exercise the majority are not meeting the World Health Organisation guidelines. In the era of highly effective modulator therapy, improved treatments and life expectancy for people with CF, focus on preventable secondary diseases is important. It is imperative that we empower this population to build a positive, healthy and consistent relationship with exercise throughout their life.

Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) metrics were collected prior to HCL initiation and after six months of therapy. In individuals previously using open-loop insulin pump...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Manchester NHS Foundation Trust, Manchester Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Manchester, United Kingdom
presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 10 - Precision monitoring and management of cystic fibrosis related diabetes

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis–related diabetes (CFRD) is the most common complication of cystic fibrosis (CF) and is associated with poorer long-term outcomes and reduced quality of life. Its management places a substantial treatment burden on affected individuals. Hybrid closed-loop (HCL) insulin pump therapy is well established in type 1 diabetes, but evidence for its effectiveness in CFRD remains limited.
sections.Methods: We conducted a single-centre retrospective study of adults with CFRD who had transitioned to HCL therapy for more than six months. Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) metrics were collected prior to HCL initiation and after six months of therapy. In individuals previously using open-loop insulin pump therapy, additional data on insulin dosing requirements were analysed.
sections.Results: Data were available for 11 individuals (8 previously using open-loop insulin pump therapy and 3 using insulin injections). Significant improvements were observed across multiple glycaemic parameters, including increases in median time in range 4–10 mmol/L from 43% [34-50] to 76% [ 59-79] (p=0.003) and mean time in range 4–8 mmol/L from 24.4%±8.6 to 47.2%±13.2 (p<0.001), alongside significant reductions in mean time above range (p<0.001) and average glucose 11.2 [10.5-12.5] to 8.2 [8-10.4] mmol/L (p=0.003). At baseline, no individual achieved a time in range >70%, compared with 64% at six months. Mean HbA1c decreased significantly (60.1±6.1 vs 50.2±9.4 mmol/mol, p=0.004). There were no significant changes in glycaemic variability or time below range. Among individuals previously using insulin pumps, the proportion of total daily insulin delivered as basal insulin increased from 38% to 51%.
sections.Conclusion: In routine clinical practice, initiation of HCL therapy was associated with significant improvements in glycaemic control in adults with CFRD, without increased hypoglycaemia, and with potential to reduce treatment burden, supporting the emerging role of advanced diabetes technologies in CFRD management.

sections.Objectives: In response to evolving global educational needs and increasing digital access, the ECFS Education Committee launched a structured webinar programme. Building on its earlier success and informed by international feedback from a previously published survey, the committee prioritised this initiative. The aim was to enhance international knowledge exchange, promote inclusivity, and support continuous professional development across diverse healthcare systems.
sections.Methods: Between 2022 and 2025, the committee coordinated, supported and delivered speciality group open access webinars covering core clinical topics, key publications, and interdisciplinary themes. Annual registration, attendance and geographic data were collected and analysed.
sections.Results: Webinar frequency increased from six in 2022 to 20 in 2025 with attendance growing from 565 to 1664. Across the four year period, the programme has supported 48 live webinars attended by 4,412 individuals. These webinars have been viewed in 37 countries across four continents: 26 in Europe, 5 in Asia, 3 in North America, and 3 in South America. Education content was accessed in 10 low- and middle- income countries. Growing engagement from ECFS Special Interest and Working Groups has been pivotal in broadening the diversity of topics and strengthening overall interest. Table 1. sections.Conclusion: The expansion of the Society’s online webinar programme has significantly improved global engagement, educational reach, and diversity of topics. Continued investment in digital learning supports equitable access to international medical education and fosters cross-border collaboration. Future directions include the potential for real-time translation and further development of novel webinar packages.

sections.Objectives: The most effective treatment for people with CF (pwCF) carrying F508del mutation has been, until now, the triple combination of Elexacaftor-Tezacaftor-Ivacaftor (ETI). Although initially developed for F508del, CFTR modulators can correct the underlying defect(s) in other CFTR mutants, such as missense variants located in the ICL4 domain. However, several CFTR variants showed minimal or no functional improvement following ETI exposure. We evaluated the responsiveness of selected CFTR variants unresponsive to ETI to the novel combination of CFTR modulators Vanzacaftor-Tezacaftor-Deutivacaftor (VTD).
sections.Methods: CFTR variants previously classified as non-responsive to ETI were analyzed using functional assays in Ussing chambers on primary nasal epithelia cells collected from pwCF. CFTR activity was assessed after treatment with ETI and VTD, and results were compared to untreated controls to determine changes in channel function.
sections.Results: For this analysis, we recruited patients carrying ETI non-responsive variants, particularly missense variants not reported to belong to class I. As an example, we analyzed two patients heterozygous for the rare missense variant H147P and a minimal function, non-rescuable class I variant. Short-circuit current analysis of CFTR-mediated chloride transport in DMSO-treated epithelia showed severely reduced CFTR activity (2.7 ± 0.44 µA). Total CFTR current increased to 4.4 ± 0.36 µA after ETI treatment and reached 9.4 ± 0.91 µA following VTD treatment. These findings indicate a differential response of certain CFTR variants to VTD compared with ETI.
sections.Conclusion: VTD demonstrates potential efficacy in some CFTR variants unresponsive to ETI, supporting its role as a promising therapeutic option for expanding personalized treatment strategies in Cystic Fibrosis. Further studies are warranted to confirm these findings and assess their clinical relevance.

While selected pregnancy outcomes are included in the 2023 European Cystic Fibrosis Society Patient Registry (ECFSPR) report, country-level and demographic data are unavailable. We aimed to assess the availability, scope and harmonisation of pregnancy-related data reporting in national CF registry reports from countries contributing to the ECFSPR. sections.Objectives: Pregnant females with cystic fibrosis (FwCF) are at increased risk of obstetric and perinatal complications. As pregnancy among FwCF becomes increasingly common, robust surveillance of pregnancy-related outcomes is essential to inform safe clinical practice and promote maternal health equity. While selected pregnancy outcomes are included in the 2023 European Cystic Fibrosis Society Patient Registry (ECFSPR) report, country-level and demographic data are unavailable. We aimed to assess the availability, scope and harmonisation of pregnancy-related data reporting in national CF registry reports from countries contributing to the ECFSPR.
sections.Methods: Publicly available national CF registry reports from ECFSPR-contributing countries were reviewed to assess inclusion of pregnancy data. Pregnancy-related outcomes and maternal characteristics were extracted to permit cross-country comparison and appraisal of data harmonisation.
sections.Results: National CF registry reports were available for 14 of the 43 ECFSPR-contributing countries, of which 8 included pregnancy data. Marked variation was observed in the scope of reported outcomes, ranging from basic documentation of births to detailed pregnancy outcomes, maternal characteristics and antenatal CFTR modulator (CFTRm) exposure. Heterogeneous terminology was used to define common outcomes, and the inclusion criteria for minimum age and lung transplant status of pregnant FwCF differed between countries.
sections.Conclusion: The reporting of pregnancy-related outcomes in national registry data is limited across ECFSPR-contributing countries, and substantial variation in scope, terminology and population characteristics hinders cross-country comparison. In the CFTRm era, efforts are needed to standardise national reporting of salient pregnancy outcomes to support effective surveillance, particularly for offspring exposed to CFTRms in utero, and to promote equity in CF maternal health. Granular, country-level data analysis is ongoing.

sections.Objectives: Patient-reported outcomes regarding daily functioning and participation are under-explored relative to clinical outcomes of Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Conductance Regulator modulator (CFTRm) therapy, particularly in resource-limited settings. This study describes the lived experiences of people living with CF (PLwCF) following CFTRm initiation in South Africa.
sections.Methods: Convergent mixed-methods study incorporating an online survey (n=37, 48% response rate) and semi-structured interviews (n=6) with South African PLwCF aged 6-60 years receiving CFTRm. Survey data compared pre- and post-CFTRm experiences using chi-square tests. Qualitative data from interviews and open-ended survey responses reached data saturation and underwent inductive thematic analysis.
sections.Results: Participants reported dramatic improvements in the frequency, nature and severity of distressing respiratory symptoms (p<0.0001) after implementing CFTRm. Duration of airway clearance and inhalation treatment duration reduced from majority 1-3+ hours to <1 hour (p<0.0001); regular exercise and social/recreational participation increased from 62% to 92% (p=0.04); and 21.6% to 48.6% (p = 0.006) respectively, with reduced school/work absenteeism (p<0.0001). The proportion with perceived 'excellent health' increased from 5% to 64% (p<0.0001) after CFTRm. Thematic analysis revealed ten major themes including respiratory health transformation, dramatic treatment burden reduction, liberation and normalization of daily life, enhanced physical capacity, profound psychosocial impact, improved growth, reduced hospitalizations, positive family impact, increased energy, and altruistic hope for universal CFTRm access.
sections.Conclusion: CFTRm profoundly transforms the lived experiences of South African PLwCF, with meaningful improvements in daily functioning, participation, and wellbeing. Findings provide compelling evidence supporting advocacy for equitable CFTRm access in resource-limited settings.

sections.Background: Abnormal glucose tolerance (AGT) can develop early in children with cystic fibrosis (CF). The Insulinogenic Index (IGI), derived from the Oral Glucose Tolerance Test (OGTT), is a surrogate marker of pancreatic β-cell function and has been proposed as a risk stratification tool for CF-Related Diabetes (CFRD) in adults. However, its utility has not yet been evaluated in children.
sections.Objectives: To investigate the association between AGT and IGT over the course of Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) therapy in children with CF aged 6-11 years.
sections.Methods: Twenty-two children (median age 7 years, range 6–11) underwent OGTT before starting ETI and after 12 months. AGT was defined as Impaired Fasting Glucose (IFG), Indeterminate Glycemia (INDET), Impaired Glucose Tolerance (IGT), or CFRD. IGI was calculated as Δinsulin (µUI/mL) over Δglucose (mmol/L) during the first 30 minutes of the OGTT. Mixed models were used to estimate marginal means for IGI by AGT status and ETI therapy, adjusting for age.
sections.Results: Before ETI, seven children had AGT (4 INDET, 2 INDET+IGT, 1 IGT), increasing to 10 after 12 months of ETI (4 INDET, 1 CFRD, 1 INDET+IFG, 1 INDET+IFG+IGT, 1 INDET+IGT, 1 IFG+IGT, 1 IGT). AGT was associated with lower IGI (p=0.04), while IGI did not change significantly over the course of ETI therapy (p=0.60).
sections.Conclusion: AGT is common in children with CF before routine CFRD screening age. ETI therapy does not appear to modify this condition. Larger studies are needed to determine whether IGI can be a useful early risk stratification tool. Table 1. presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 11 - Telehealth and digital tools in care and education

sections.Objectives: Optimising Health (OH) is a quality improvement, multidisciplinary team comprising social workers, dietitians, a physiotherapist, and parent partners at a paediatric CF centre (n=325) serving a multistate area in the western USA. OH aims to identify perceived barriers to CF care, resource gaps, and underserved age groups. Four digital health programmes were developed by the OH team to foster community, improve health literacy, and promote the well-being of children with cystic fibrosis (CwCF) and their families.
sections.Methods: Surveys of CwCF (ages 8–15) and parents identified priority topics for virtual education. The pilot programme, Great Outcomes Achieved Together (GOAT), enrolled 30 CwCF (ages 8–12) in two online cohorts, focusing on healthy lifestyles, health literacy, and peer connection. Youth Engaged in Treatment Independently (YETI) targeted 13 teens (ages 13–15) with independence, self-care, and connection topics. Two additional programmes—Salty Seminars (webinars for parents of CwCF) and CF Strong Challenges (family-based health activities)—aimed to engage families across all age groups. Pre- and post-programme digital surveys assessed impact.
sections.Results: Mean improvements in healthy behaviours were 11.8% (GOAT Cohort 1), 15.5% (GOAT Cohort 2), and 12.2% (YETI). Confidence in managing CF care rose 28% among YETI participants. Feedback emphasised empowerment and peer support: “I liked knowing I’m not the only CF kid in the world.” Parents reported improved confidence and adherence to CF care. Since 2024, 88 participants have joined OH programmes; 100% of survey respondents supported continuation.
sections.Conclusion: These innovative, multidisciplinary digital programmes demonstrated measurable gains in health behaviours, confidence, and engagement among CwCF and families. Future priorities include scaling to broader age ranges, enhancing carer involvement, integrating gamified learning, and developing longitudinal tracking to assess sustained impact.

sections.Background: SPL84 is an inhaled antisense oligonucleotide (ASO), currently being evaluated in an ongoing global Phase 2 study in pwCF with the 3849+10kb C->T (3849) mutation. Interim results indicate that SPL84 is safe, well tolerated, and shows positive preliminary efficacy. SPL84 directly and specifically corrects the underlying splicing defect, restoring wild-type CFTR mRNA and translation of a fully functional CFTR channel. In studies with 3849 patient-derived HBE and HNE cells, SPL84 treatment restored CFTR function to normal levels. In 3849/modulator-responsive mutation heterozygous patients, combined treatment with SPL84 and CFTR modulators may provide an additional benefit beyond either therapy alone.
sections.Methods: The effect of SPL84, ETI (VX-661, VX-445, VX-770) and the combinational treatment of SPL84+ETI were assessed in HBE cells derived from heterozygous patients carrying the 3849 and F508del mutations. The effect was determined by analysis of CFTR-mediated Cl- current measured in Ussing chamber or TECC Ieq assays.
sections.Results: SPL84 treatment from the basal or apical side of fully differentiated 3849/F508del HBE cells leads to substantial restoration of functional CFTR expression from the 3849 allele. Combinational treatment with SPL84+ETI led to significantly higher levels of CFTR function compared to each drug alone, indicating the potential for additive benefit of both drugs in heterozygote patients.
sections.Conclusion: Studies in HBE cells support the potential benefit of combinational treatment with SPL84 and modulator drugs over each drug alone in heterozygote people with CF carrying the 3849 mutation and a modulator responsive mutation. The combinational additive benefit will be evaluated in the 2nd part of the phase 2 study.

sections.Objectives: The prevalence of pregnancies in women with cystic fibrosis (CF) has increased since introduction to elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) but there is still a lack of knowledge about the impact of pregnancy. The aim was to describe the pregnancies to women with CF at the Stockholm CF centre.
sections.Methods: Data from all female patients at the Stockholm CF centre was collected from the Swedish CF Registry during the period 2010 to 2025. Outcome variables included: pregnancy, lung function as forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV1) and ETI therapy.
sections.Results: Of a total of 104 women with CF, 37 (36%) have had at least one live birth with a total of 63 live births (range 1-4 births). Of the 37 women, 17 were homozygous for F508del and 32 were pancreas insufficient. Baseline data prior to first pregnancy: age 31 (±4.7) years, body mass index 22.5 (±3), FEV1 2.4 L (±0.68). Prior to ETI, 49 births were reported (1.2 births/year) and 14 births were reported since ETI (3.5 births/year). Eight continued ETI therapy throughout pregnancy and the remaining six paused during the first trimester but then resumed. The number of births resulting from in vitro fertilization (IVF) decreased from 51% before ETI to 7% after ETI. Lung function data was analysed for 23 women, as women with a CF diagnosis after pregnancy and shared care patients were excluded due to no data. From a baseline FEV1 of 2.27 L (range 1.05-3.72), there was a decrease of 3% during pregnancy but no clear difference related to ETI. There was an overall decrease in FEV1 of 5% one year postpartum. Despite a small sample, it was interesting that those on ETI improved 2% whilst those without decreased 8%.
sections.Conclusion: The data showed a marked increase in the number of spontaneous conceptions since introduction to ETI. No data is available about unsuccessful pregnancies. ETI may have a positive impact on postpartum lung function. We will continue careful follow up and support of peri- and postpartum women.

sections.Background: CFTR modulators have improved QOL and lung function among pwCF and seem to affect the face of CF.
sections.Objectives: The study aimed to evaluate physical activity, exercise capacity, adherence to physiotherapy, and QoL among children with CF 2 years after initiation of CFTR modulators.
sections.Methods: Forty-five children and adolescents with CF were evaluated before and 2 years after the initiation of CFTR modulators. They completed three self-administered, validated questionnaires: the Godin Leisure-Time Exercise Questionnaire, the Manchester Q, and the DISABKIDS for QoL. The Godin Leisure-Time Exercise Questionnaire was used to evaluate physical activity; QOL was assessed using the DISABKIDS Questionnaire; the Manchester Questionnaire was used to assess adherence to CF treatment. Moreover, pwCF performed spirometry and the six-minute walking test (6MWT).
sections.Results: Mean age of the CF population was 14.5 (±7.6) years, mean BMI 19.58 (±4.1) kg/m2, and mean FEV1% 91.15 ± 20.46%. Before the initiation of ETI, the majority of children (48%) reported performing physiotherapy once or twice a day, 36% reported performing it daily, and 16% reported performing it occasionally. According to the GODIN Questionnaire, 68% of the CF group were active, 27% were moderately active, and 5% were sedentary before initiation of CFTR modulators. Two years later, adherence to physiotherapy and inhaled medication decreased significantly (p<0.05), while QOL, physical activity, FEV1pp, and exercise capacity assessed with the 6MWT improved significantly (p<0.05).
sections.Conclusion: CFTR modulators showed significant benefits among pwCF in lung function, physical activity levels, exercise capacity, and QOL, despite decreased adherence to physiotherapy and inhaled medication. More research is needed on changes in the everyday lives of pwCF.

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a genetic disorder characterised by progressive lung function decline and chronic airway inflammation. Cystic fibrosis-related diabetes (CFRD) is the most common extrapulmonary complication of CF and is associated with increased morbidity and mortality. Although a strong association between CFRD and impaired lung function has been established, the underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study aimed to compare airway microbiome composition between individuals with CF with and without CFRD.
sections.Methods: Sputum samples from pwCF with and without CFRD were analysed for inflammatory cytokines using MesoScale assays and total bacterial load via quantitative PCR targeting the 16S rRNA gene. Microbiome profiling was performed using high-resolution 16S rRNA sequencing, with diversity assessed through richness and evenness metrics. Bray–Curtis dissimilarity was applied to evaluate differences in microbial community composition between groups.
sections.Results: Forty-four pwCF were included, of whom 59% had CFRD. The CFRD group exhibited significantly lower lung function and elevated sputum IL-1β levels compared to pwCF without CFRD. Microbiome analysis revealed reduced bacterial diversity in the CFRD group, although overall community composition did not differ significantly between groups. Genera such as Abiotrophia, Anaeroglobus, and Escherichia-Shigella were enriched in CFRD, whereas Neisseria, Prevotella, and Streptococcus were more abundant in pwCF without CFRD.
sections.Conclusion: CFRD is associated with impaired lung function and increased airway inflammation. This study provides the first evidence of reduced bacterial diversity in the sputum microbiome of individuals with CFRD. While CFRD status alone did not markedly alter overall microbial composition, findings indicate a trend towards a more dysbiotic microbial profile in pwCF with CFRD. Table 1. sections.Background: Home spirometry has become an integral component of CF care following the widespread adoption of telehealth and hybrid care models. While its feasibility and clinical utility have been well described, less is known about longer-term real-world perceptions among pwCF and CF healthcare professionals (HCPs).
sections.Methods: A cross-sectional, paper-based survey was conducted at an adult CF centre using the NuvoAir home spirometry system. Eligible participants included pwCF with ≥1 year of home spirometry experience and HCPs actively involved in CF care. Surveys comprised Likert-scale and open-ended questions exploring engagement with spirometry around clinic visits, knowledge and understanding of spirometry, perceived clinical value, and psychological impact. Open-ended responses were analysed using inductive thematic analysis (Table 1).
sections.Results: Surveys were completed by 101 pwCF and 30 HCPs. Most pwCF reported frequent completion of spirometry before and after both face-to-face (F2F) and virtual clinics. In contrast, HCPs generally perceived lower levels of engagement. There were no difference in reported spirometry engagement between F2F and virtual clinics, suggesting similar attitudes to home spirometry between the two clinics. Over half of pwCF reported high confidence in interpreting their spirometry results. Both pwCF and HCPs viewed home spirometry as clinically valuable for routine monitoring and during exacerbations. 30% of pwCF reported to be “never anxious” when completing spirometry. 74% pwCF were “very comfortable” completing spirometry in front of HCPs, 10.1% were "uncomfortable". HCPs, especially physiotherapists, expressed that there is an existing role for in-clinic supervised spirometry for selected patients.
sections.Conclusion: Home spirometry is well accepted by both pwCF and HCPs. Flexible, individualised implementation is required to maximise clinical benefit while minimising psychological burden. Main themes from open-ended responses:Example quotesPositive attitudes towards home spirometry among HCPs “Remote appointments would not be possible without it.” “Extremely valuable resource for some patients.” “Overall, I think it is a positive addition… can be helpful when unwell and patients perform at home.” Positive attitudes towards home spirometry among pwCF “Happy and content. Allows me to check my lungs’ condition.” “Very positive. I wouldn’t want to not have it now.” “I prefer it at home for easy access when needed.” “It’s good to be able to keep track of your blows, rather than every few months during visits.” “Very useful in situations such as prolonged bouts of illness.” Clinical utility of supervised in-clinic spirometry compared with unsupervised home spirometry (responses from HCPs) “Still important to complete supervised spirometry from time to time, as you can assess more about technique than can be shown by the flow volume loop.” “Useful to observe the patient doing their blow during clinic as part of assessment.” “Whilst it is useful to have some spirometry results before a clinic visit, it is often useful to observe the patient doing their blow during clinic as part of the assessment.” “As a physio, I like doing spirometry with patients as it is a way of assessing their chest. Can often hear sputum – and can open up conversations – even if they say they are non-productive.” Anxiety related to home spirometry (responses from pwCF) “Don’t mind, just anxiety around results.” “Have anxiety regarding results, so avoid” “Sometimes does not connect, stressful, sometimes does not work” Home spirometry monitoring frequency and patient anxiety (responses from HCPs) “[Should do spirometry] before routine appointments and when unwell, [although] too frequent spirometry can lead them to become fixated on numbers alone” “Ideally 3 to 4 [spirometry sessions] a year minimum. Sometimes doing it more frequently increases anxiety, and there is no opportunity to discuss or alleviate this unless at clinic.” Table 1: Sample quotes from respondents

Primary ALI cultures from pwCF (N = 21) and healthy controls (N = 20) were assessed using electrophysiology with a modified Multi Transepithelial...

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 07 - Old and new – exploring the portfolio for cystic fibrosis treatment

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is caused by pathogenic CFTR variants, leading to defective epithelial chloride transport, increased mucus viscosity, and impaired airway function. Although CFTR modulators including Trikafta® have improved outcomes for many people with CF (pwCF), those carrying rare or class I CFTR variants still lack effective therapy.
sections.Methods: We evaluated CFTR modulators and chitosan-mediated wild-type CFTR (wtCFTR) mRNA therapies in patient-derived nasal air–liquid interface (ALI) cultures using integrated functional and morphological approaches. Primary ALI cultures from pwCF (N = 21) and healthy controls (N = 20) were assessed using electrophysiology with a modified Multi Transepithelial Current Clamp (MTECC) Ussing chamber, immunofluorescence imaging, and analyses of mucus viscosity and epithelial barrier integrity.
sections.Results: CFTR modulators restored chloride channel function in 10 of 13 pwCF cultures, as measured using the MTECC Ussing chamber and validated by apical CFTR localization via immunofluorescence. However, cultures from pwCF with rare or class I CFTR variants showed no functional improvement. These non-responsive cultures were treated with wtCFTR-mRNA delivered via chitosan-based nanoparticles, resulting in restoration of CFTR function across the treated cohort, with transepithelial conductance ΔGt increasing from -0.01135 ± 0.04027 mS/cm2 before treatment to 0.2566 ± 0.04621 mS/cm2 (N = 11, n = 34) after treatment. CF cultures exhibited elevated MUC5AC and claudin-1 expression, increased mucus viscosity, and compromised epithelial barrier integrity, all markedly improved following successful CFTR rescue by both modulators and mRNA therapy.
sections.Conclusion: This study highlights the therapeutic potential of chitosan-mediated CFTR mRNA delivery as a personalised therapeutic strategy for CF patients, particularly those with rare CFTR variants that are non-responsive to current modulator therapies.

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is characterized by marked heterogeneity in disease severity and progression. While genetic determinants play a central role, growing evidence suggests that environmental exposures may significantly modulate clinical outcomes. The objective of this study was to identify distinct clinical trajectories of CF in children and to assess the contribution of environmental factors—particularly air pollution and passive exposure to tobacco smoke—to disease severity and progression using data from the French Cystic Fibrosis Registry.
sections.Methods: We analyzed data from the French CF Registry, which covers ~90% of CF patients in France. For this study, we selected children aged 6–12 years (n=909). Unsupervised clustering, including latent class analysis of longitudinal lung function, identified patient subgroups. Clinical, environmental, and sociodemographic data—including passive smoke exposure and air pollution (NO2, PM2.5, PM10), were integrated to characterize subgroup profiles.
sections.Results: Four distinct CF subgroups emerged. Patients with accelerated lung function decline and lower baseline lung function were disproportionately exposed to air pollution (p<0.001 worse lung function group vs others) and second-hand smoke (p<0.001). These exposures were associated with increased rates of infections, bacterial colonization, digestive comorbidities, and hospitalizations.
sections.Conclusion: Environmental exposures, particularly air pollution and second-hand smoke, are linked to more severe CF trajectories. Integrating exposome data into clinical phenotyping can improve risk stratification and inform targeted interventions to optimize patient outcomes. Acknowledgements: We thank Vaincre la Mucoviscidose and the CF centres of the French CF Registry for data access, as well as all participating patients. This work is part of the REMEDIA project, funded by the EU Horizon 2020 Program (Grant No. 874753).

presentations.session_title: ePoster Session 09 - Rethinking airway clearance and physical activity in the CFTR modulator era: adherence, burden and innovation

sections.Objectives: Airway clearance treatments (ACT) are essential for maintaining lung health in many people with cystic fibrosis. Project Fizzyo suggested that correctly performed ACTs are associated with improved lung function, but many children and young people (CYP) with CF often use ineffective techniques (Filipow, 2023). ImpACT4CF integrates a real-time breath sensor with visual feedback to optimise ACT technique, guiding breath quality, monitoring adherence, and logging treatment data. This preliminary study evaluates the effect of real-time feedback on ACT quality in healthy adults.
sections.Methods: Adults (>18 years) without respiratory disease and naïve to ACT, were recruited. In a randomised crossover design, each participant acted as their own control across two devices (Acapella, PEP mask), two sessions (7-14 days apart) and three feedback modes Record: no feedback, Graph: breath waveforms, and Coach: real-time coaching with target cues. Breath pressure and length were recorded using the sensor. Breaths were classified as conformant if mean expiratory pressure was between 5-25cmH₂O and length 2.3-4.5s.
sections.Results: 16 healthy adults completed 2,844 breaths. Conformity increased stepwise with feedback intensity. Record: 66% (Acapella) vs 62% (PEP); Graph: 83% vs 73%; Coach: 96.5% vs 100%. This within-device increase was statistically significant (p<0.001) with a large effect size. ACT technique errors related to breath length being too short (51%) or long (28%) were almost entirely eliminated in Coach mode. Breath‑to‑breath variability was greatest in Record, reduced in Graph, and lowest in Coach, for both devices. There was a learning effect with higher conformity in Record mode on second visit (p<0.004), but no device-specific order effect.
sections.Conclusion: Real-time visual feedback produced much improved breath conformity, and may support more effective ACTs. The ImpACT4CF pilot trial is now evaluating the impact of real‑time feedback on ACT quality in CYP with CF.

sections.Objectives: Digital interventions are often postulated to be most relevant for adolescents, but little is known on differential effects across life stages in cystic fibrosis (CF). This analysis investigated age-specific effects of a telemonitoring-supported intervention on inhalation adherence in people with CF (pwCF).
sections.Methods: We conducted a post-hoc subgroup analysis of the conneCT CF study, a randomized controlled trial comparing standard of care (control group, CG) with a digital intervention (intervention group, IG) (Thee S. BMC Pulm Med. 2021). The intervention combined among others objective adherence monitoring via the eFlow® rapid Nebuliser System, a self-management app (PARI Connect App), and coaching. In this post-hoc analysis, median adherence was compared between CG (n=109) and IG (n=95) across 5 age groups (12–16, 17–21, 22–30, 31–40, >40 years).
sections.Results: In the overall cohort, previously published analysis showed no difference between IG and CG for the primary endpoint time to first pulmonary exacerbation (PEx). However, higher adherence was significantly associated with lower risk of PEx. Also, adherence was significantly higher in the IG compared to the CG (52% vs. 38%) (Thee S. ECFS 2025). Age-stratified analysis showed nearly the same high adherence in the IG and CG in pwCF aged 12–16 and >40 years (53-60%). In the 17–21 and 22–30 year age groups, median adherence dropped overall but the IG demonstrated 20% higher adherence compared to the CG (IG 30%, 29%; CG 10%, 12%, respectively). The main effect was observed in pwCF 31–40 years, with a median adherence of 75% (IG) compared to 39% (CG).
sections.Conclusion: This post-hoc analysis indicates the impact of digital-supported self-management on inhalation adherence in CF may be age-dependent. While parental supervision in adolescents and established routines in older adults seem to result in high adherence levels, pwCF aged 17–40 years appear to derive the greatest benefit of using digital support tools.

sections.Objectives: Cystic Fibrosis-Related Diabetes (CFRD) is the most common comorbidity in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) and insulin is the standard treatment. However, the association between insulin-treated CFRD and lung function is unclear: observational studies suggest clinical benefits but are limited by small samples and confounding. We assessed the long-term effects of insulin therapy in pwCF and CFRD using data from the European Cystic Fibrosis Society Patient Registry (ECFSPR).
sections.Methods: Using a Target Trial Emulation (TTE) approach, we compared pwCF aged ≥12 newly diagnosed with CFRD between 2018 and 2023 who initiated and sustained insulin therapy versus those who did not initiate insulin, over 5 years of follow-up. The endpoint was longitudinal changes in percent predicted FEV₁ (ppFEV₁). Treatment effects were estimated using Marginal Structural Models (MSMs) with inverse probability weighting to handle measured confounders and censoring. Effect modification by CFTR modulator use was assessed using an extended MSM with a treatment-modulator interaction.
sections.Results: The study included 1,371 pwCF (median age 22 years, 49.2% male); 61.8% initiated insulin within the first year after CFRD diagnosis. We found no evidence that insulin use affects long-term ppFEV₁ trajectories compared with no insulin (difference -3.62; 95% CI: -9.33 to 2.10). Among pwCF not receiving CFTR modulators, sustained insulin use over 5 years was associated with lower ppFEV₁ compared with no insulin use (-7.64; 95% CI: -13.78 to -1.50). In contrast, among modulator users, this long-term difference was attenuated (-0.36; 95% CI: -8.48 to 7.75).
sections.Conclusion: Using ECFPSR data, we found no evidence that insulin therapy was associated with long-term improvement in lung function overall. However, the association between insulin use and ppFEV₁ varied by CFTR modulator use: differences were smaller among modulator than among non-users, suggesting heterogeneous treatment effects.

sections.Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) pregnancy rates have risen following initiation of highly effective modulator therapy. Current physiotherapy standards of care (SOC) have not been updated to reflect this. We aim to describe current UK practice in maternal CF physiotherapy and identify priorities for standardised care and future research.
sections.Methods: Lead physiotherapists at all 23 UK CF centres were invited to complete a bespoke online questionnaire.
sections.Results: Twelve (52%) centres responded. Mean centre size was 298 (SD 128), with a mean annual pregnancy of 6.5 (SD 3.7). Three centres (25%) had a CF and Obstetrics pathway. Time to initial MDT review following confirmed pregnancy varied: 2 weeks (n=4), 4 weeks (n=3), 8 weeks (n=2), or dependent on pre-maternal health (n=3). Review frequency ranged from 2-12 weeks, most commonly every 4 weeks (41%) increasing with gestation: 41% in the first, 50% in the second, and 58% in the third trimester. All physiotherapists review at MDT clinics and ad hoc, reviewing lung function (n=12), inhaled therapies (n=12), respiratory status (n=11), airway clearance techniques (ACT) (n=11), exercise (n=11). ACT was commonly altered in second (42%) and third trimesters (67%), with addition of inspiratory pressure (n=4) or PEP (n=4); 50% added or increased inhaled mucolytics. Exercise prescription varied. 70% of centres review within 24 hours post-partum, the remainder limited by off-site obstetric care. Post-partum management were mixed.
sections.Conclusion: Despite common management themes in CF pregnancy, variation exists reflecting a lack of evidence-based guidance. Clinically, this may result in inconsistent care and inequitable patient experience. The update of the CF physiotherapy SOC alongside development of integrated CF–obstetric MDT pathways, may improve consistency and quality of care. Future research should focus on defining optimal physiotherapy interventions, timing of reviews, exercise and ACT strategies to inform evidence-based guidelines.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Bath, Department of Psychology, Bath, United Kingdom, 2University Hospitals Bristol & Weston NHS Foundation Trust, Psychological Health Services, Bristol, United Kingdom

sections.Objectives: UK National guidance indicates that all patients in paediatric and adult cystic fibrosis (CF) services should be offered psychology annual reviews (PAR). The Bristol Lifespan CF Psychology service trialled implementing an automated online PAR questionnaire. A service evaluation of this initiative aimed to audit uptake of PAR and gather patient feedback on the experience.
sections.Methods: An audit of the online PAR was conducted over a 3-month period. Within this time frame, adult patients (n = 86) received a service specific questionnaire and paediatric patients (n = 20) an adapted version of a paediatric wellbeing measure. Psychologist attendance at paediatric annual review appointments supported data collection. The number of patients who received an invite to PAR during this period was compared to the previous year. Patient feedback questionnaires were sent to patients who received the online offer of PAR.
sections.Results: More patients were offered PAR during the audit period (paediatrics = 100%, adults = 91%) than the previous year (paediatric = 29%, adults = 40%). 70% of paediatric patients and 22% of adult patients completed the PAR questionnaires. There was an increase of patient contact with CF psychology following the introduction of online PAR. Whilst response rates were low; overall PAR feedback generally indicated positive support for this process.
sections.Conclusion: Overall, the online PAR system appeared successful in supporting the services to adhere to national guidance, patients were satisfied with the process and there was an increase of patient contact with the psychology service. This did however increase workload for psychologists in terms of active monitoring and increased uptake of support.

presentations.summary: Introduction: Cystic fibrosis (CF) lung disease is hallmarked by chronic infection and inflammation. We previously showed that airway macrophages, crucial for defence against infections and resolution of inflammation, are dysfunctional in CF. However, the relative influence of cell-intrinsic (CFTR) and extrinsic (lung environment) factors in driving these processes is unclear. Aim: To assess the role of intrinsic and extrinsic factors in dysfunction of airway macrophages in CF. Methods: Monocyte–derived macrophages (MDMs) from people with CF and healthy donors (HD) were incubated with either control medium only or exposed to bronchoalveolar-lavage fluid (BALF) from highly inflamed CF lungs (BALFInfl-HI; n=4) o...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Erasmus University Medical Centre - Sophia Children's Hospital, Department of Paediatrics, Laboratory of Paediatrics, Infection and Immunity group, Rotterdam, Netherlands, 2Erasmus University Medical Centre - Sophia Children's Hospital, Department of Paediatrics, division of Respiratory Medicine and Allergology, Rotterdam, Netherlands, 3Erasmus University Medical Centre, Department of Radiology and Nuclear Medicine, Erasmus MC Cancer Institute, Rotterdam, Netherlands
presentations.session_title: Workshop 10 - Immune and inflammatory processes in the cystic fibrosis airway: novel insights

sections.Methods: Monocyte–derived macrophages (MDMs) from people with CF and healthy donors (HD) were incubated with either control medium only or exposed to bronchoalveolar-lavage fluid (BALF) from highly inflamed CF lungs (BALFInfl-HI; n=4) or low-inflamed CF lungs (BALFInfl-LO; n=3) to mimic CF lung environment. Cytokine production, surface marker expression, and phagocytic capacity were analysed.
sections.Results: Exposure to BALFInfl-HI induced a stronger inflammatory phenotype in CF MDMs than in HD MDMs, characterized by 2-fold higher levels of secreted TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β. In contrast, BALFInfl-LO elicited less strong activation of CF and HD MDMs. CF MDMs exposed to BALFInfl-HI expressed consistently lower levels of scavenger receptors CD163, CD206 and CD91 than HD MDMs. Consequently, phagocytosis of Pseudomonas aeruginosa was reduced in CF MDMs compared to HD MDMs. In both CF and HD MDMs, BALFInfl-HI increased the expression of PD-1 and PD-L1, which was linked to impaired efferocytosis. MDMs exposed to BALFInfl-LO showed similar levels of phagocytosis and efferocytosis as control medium exposed MDMs.
sections.Conclusion: These data show that both intrinsic and extrinsic factors impact on macrophage phenotype and their bacterial uptake capacity. However, only extrinsic factors affect their tissue-cleaning and pro-resolving functions. Our data may explain why inflammation is not completely resolved after initiation of CFTR modulator therapy.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 11 - The changing face of cystic fibrosis wellbeing, risk and reproductive realities

sections.Background: Data of lung transplanted (LT) people with Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF) are often embedded in larger studies on LT. This study aims to specifically review the burden of treatment and illness (Burden) on stable LTpwCF.
sections.Methods: Demographic and medical variables and indicators of Burden were obtained from medical records over the past year. Quality of life (QOL) was assessed using the Flemish LT Quality Of Life survey (FLT-QOL).
sections.Results: The Leuven (Belgium) LT clinic cares for 119 LTpwCF of whom 85 (71%) were stable at the time of the study. 69/85 consented to the study: mean age 43 (range 23-65), 38 females. Time since LT was 11,9 years (SD=3.5; range 3-26). Mean lung function FEV1%pred. was 79% (SD=19; range 39%-129%); mean BMI was 21 (SD=3.5; range 16-32) and 54 (78%) were diagnosed with CFRD. Common complications related to LT were CLAD (25%), cancer (15%) and PTLD (7%). Patients were prescribed with 9-28 different drugs, mostly for LT and CF. Other common prescriptions were pain medication (34%) and psychotropic medicines (23%). Patients attended the multidisciplinary LT clinic at least 3 times a year. Over the past year, patients consulted a mean total of 24 specialist (range 3-21). Patients attended a mean sum of 32 outpatient clinics (range 7-81); 33% had been hospitalized and 24% had undergone surgery. Table 1 shows associations between Burden parameters and scales of the FLT-QOL. Regression analyses showed that sum of outpatient visit and number of treatments were strong predictors of variance in FLT-QOL scales (p<.001-<0.05). BMI predicted variance on the FLT general QOL (p<.001).
sections.Conclusion: The data indicate to high burden in terms of treatments, prescriptions and hospital visits. Patients consulted many specialists over and above LT and CF care. Sum of outpatient clinics was a strong predictor of variance in several areas of QOL. LTpwCF pay a high price to stay well and are in need of well-coordinated inter- and multidisciplinary care. presentations.summary: Objectives: CF-HomeSpIT [ISRCTN86523335] is a prospective internally-controlled interventional trial in children comparing home-sampling using spontaneous early morning cough-clear (emCC) and home sputum-induction (hSI) with matched same-day clinic sampling using cough swab (CS), spontaneous clinic cough-clear (cCC) and clinic sputum-induction (cSI). https://www.uhwchildren.com/respiratory/cf-homespit/ Methods: Children age>6 yrs with CF at the Children’s Hospital for Wales already using Hypertonic saline (HS) were eligible for recruitment. emCC was obtained on waking, using 3 rounds of standardised expectoration: COUGH, HUFF, COUGH, CLEAR THROAT (hawk), and SPIT. hSI was obtained using HS with normal home phy...

sections.Objectives: CF-HomeSpIT [ISRCTN86523335] is a prospective internally-controlled interventional trial in children comparing home-sampling using spontaneous early morning cough-clear (emCC) and home sputum-induction (hSI) with matched same-day clinic sampling using cough swab (CS), spontaneous clinic cough-clear (cCC) and clinic sputum-induction (cSI). https://www.uhwchildren.com/respiratory/cf-homespit/
sections.Methods: Children age>6 yrs with CF at the Children’s Hospital for Wales already using Hypertonic saline (HS) were eligible for recruitment. emCC was obtained on waking, using 3 rounds of standardised expectoration: COUGH, HUFF, COUGH, CLEAR THROAT (hawk), and SPIT. hSI was obtained using HS with normal home physiotherapy routine, using the same standardised expectoration. Same-day clinic samples (cCC and cSI) were taken after routine CS by a physiotherapist using standardised procedures and expectoration.
sections.Results: 64 patients performed 96 sampling events. Median age was 11 yrs. Median FEV1% was 94%. 93% were on modulator therapy. 20% had a wet cough. 92/96 (93%) home sampling attempts were successful (median duration 13 minutes (range 15-40)). 40 CF pathogens were identified in 34 (35%) individuals from all sampling combined. emCC, hSI, CS, cCC and cSI isolated 22, 22, 10, 23 & 23 pathogens respectively. 28/40 (sensitivity 0.7) were isolated on home samples combined. 31/40 (sensitivity 0.77) were isolated from clinic samples combined.
sections.Conclusion: Home sputum-induction using a standardised approach to expectoration is possible, well tolerated, and has comparable pathogen yield to supervised clinic sputum-induction. Pre-saline spontaneous cough-clear sampling gives additional gains in pathogen detection, may contain higher pathogen titre and should be incorporated into all sputum-induction protocols. Clinic spontaneous cough-clear samples in isolation identify twice as many pathogens as CS and present a novel, quick addition to pathogen surveillance in clinic.

presentations.summary: W1282X, the 5th most common Cystic Fibrosis causing variant, generates a premature terminating codon (PTC), causing lack of full-length functional CFTR protein production and nonsense-mediated mRNA decay. presentations.summary: CFTR-mediated ion transport displays significant variability across the population. This heterogeneity is observed not only in people with Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF) where individuals with identical genotypes may showvariable CFTR activity and responses to modulators, but also in non-CF subjects, who exhibit a wide range of baseline CFTR activity. This suggests that factors extrinsic to the CFTR gene itself modulate its functional output. We hypothesized that intrinsic differences in the broader epithelial ion transport network, such as the balance between sodium absorption and auxiliary chloride secretion, may constitute a common mechanism driving this functional variability in both healthy and disease states. sections.Methods: To characterize this "background" ion transport environment, we performed differential gene expression analysis on airway epithelial cells. We specifically screened for dysregulation in non-CFTR ion channels, focusing on the soluble carrier (SLC) and epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) families, to identify factors limiting airway hydration independently of CFTR expression.
sections.Results: Transcriptomic profiling indicated that CFTR mRNA levels were stable and not the primary driver of functional divergence. However, the supporting ion transport network was significantly altered. We observed upregulation of the amiloride-sensitive ENaC subunits SCNN1B and SCNN1G, favoring sodium hyperabsorption. Simultaneously, the alternative chloride transporter SLC26A9 was strongly downregulated, as was SLC26A4.
sections.Conclusion: These data suggest that an unfavorable transporters ratio may act as a "functional brake," decoupling CFTR expression from physiological airway hydration. This mechanism may provide a molecular rationale for the unexplained variability in CFTR activity. It implies that in subjects with this specific transcriptomic signature, CFTR correction alone may be insufficient, highlighting the existence of alternative critical targets to promote chloride secretion.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 10 - Immune and inflammatory processes in the cystic fibrosis airway: novel insights

sections.Objectives: In cystic fibrosis (CF), defective CFTR-mediated chloride secretion leads to airway surface dehydration, impaired mucociliary clearance (MCC), bacterial infection, inflammation, and lung damage. Although Trikafta improve CFTR function in patients carrying the F508del mutation, airway inflammation often persists. IL-17A and IL-17F play a key role in CF lung inflammation. This study aimed to compare their impact on ion transport and airway surface properties in bronchial epithelia.
sections.Methods: Human bronchial epithelia were treated with IL-17A, IL-17F, or their combination. Transepithelial ion transport was assessed using short-circuit current recordings. The IL-17RA-neutralizing antibody brodalumab was used to dissect receptor-specific signaling. Airway surface viscosity was measured by fluorescence recovery after photobleaching (FRAP).
sections.Results: Both IL-17A and IL-17F enhanced CFTR function; however, only IL-17A significantly increased ENaC activity. Brodalumab inhibited the IL-17A-induced upregulation of both CFTR and ENaC but did not block CFTR stimulation mediated by IL-17F. FRAP analysis revealed that IL-17A promoted a hyperviscous airway surface, whereas IL-17F favored a more fluid airway environment. In F508del epithelia treated with Trikafta, CFTR function was markedly amplified by IL-17A, IL-17F, or their combination. Notably, IL-17F-mediated enhancement of F508del-CFTR rescue persisted even in the presence of brodalumab.
sections.Conclusion: IL-17A and IL-17F exert distinct effects on airway epithelial ion transport and surface properties. While IL-17A promotes airway surface hyperviscosity, IL-17F supports a more fluid surface and enhances CFTR rescue independently of IL-17RA signaling. These findings suggest that selective inhibition of IL-17A signaling, while preserving IL-17F activity, may represent a promising therapeutic strategy in CF, although possible side effects associated with IL-17RA inhibition should be carefully considered.
