sections.Objectives: Pain can be a significant burden in people with CF (pwCF). The objective of this study was to evaluate pain in adults and its characteristics across multiple countries, and to develop pain profiles for pwCF.
sections.Methods: Adults from the United States of America (USA), Canada and the United Kingdom (UK) were invited to complete an online questionnaire from April 2024 to June 2025, consisting of four validated tools: Brief Pain Inventory (BPI), Pain Catastrophising Scale (PCS), PAGI-SYM and PAC-SYM and open-ended questions surrounding their experiences with pain and pain management. The data were then collated to identify the characteristics of individuals who experience different levels of pain severity and to explore the clinical characteristics or risk factors associated with pain.
sections.Results: There were 75 international respondents, including 43 from the USA, 17 from Canada, 15 from the UK, and 117 Australian respondents. No significant differences were found in pain prevalence (p = 0.174) between the countries. Notably, previous findings surrounding modulator use and the frequency and severity of pain were validated. The pain severity cohorts (i.e., “mild”, “moderate” and “severe”) showed significant differences in age, specifically in the mild and severe cohorts, BMI, pain interference, catastrophising, PAGI-SYM and PAC-SYM scores. Furthermore, the perceived efficacy of pain management and the locations of bodily pain were significant across these cohorts, enabling the development of pain profiles.
sections.Conclusion: This international study highlights that the under-management and under-recognition of pain in pwCF is a global issue. It is the first study to identify pain profiles based on severity, grouping pain locations, and nutritional status. Further investigations are needed to improve the evaluation of pain patterns, identify causality factors, and increase awareness within CF clinics, in order to personalise pain management in CF and improve health outcomes.

sections.Objectives: To determine whether postage impacts microbiological growth of paired paediatric cough swabs sent from clinic and via post.
sections.Methods: Two simultaneous cough swabs were taken by physiotherapists from children attending in person CF clinics between Dec 2024–Aug 2025. At random, one was sent via usual protocols to the microbiology lab whilst the other was packaged into biological specimen postal kits. Microbiological growths between paired samples were compared. Time and date of postal sample arrival were used to calculate time delay to the closest 0.5hr. Actions taken on positive postal samples were documented.
sections.Results: Ninety-three paired samples were received; 19/93 postal samples were positive, the majority being Gram negative bacteria and most commonly Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) (4/19). Only 2/93 non-postal samples were positive (1 Pa, 1 Staphylococcus aureus (Sa); both also detected on their paired postal samples). In the prior 18months, 22% of the patients had isolated Sa and 12% isolated Pa. Median time delay for positive postal samples was 93.5hrs (IQR 70.5-130.3hrs) compared with 72hrs (IQR 65.3-96.3hrs) for negative postal samples (p>0.05). A positive postal sample prompted a change in management in 7/19 cases (6 commenced antibiotics, 1 had an induced sputum).
sections.Conclusion: Postal samples were more likely to be positive than non-postal samples, with Pa being a prominent pathogen isolated from postal samples. Clinical decisions around treatment of positive postal results varied, influenced by patient symptoms and uncertainty about the validity of the results. This project is ongoing with the intention to include adults and other chronic suppurative lung diseases to further understand the validity of discrepant postal samples and add to the required body of evidence in this field.

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Telethon Institute of Genetics and Medicine (Tigem), Pozzuoli, Italy

sections.Objectives: The CFTR chloride channel is essential for airway surface hydration and mucociliary clearance, and its activity is regulated by cAMP-dependent phosphorylation. We previously observed that pharmacological activation of the TRPV4 calcium channel stimulates CFTR function, but the mechanism underlying this interaction was unclear. In this study we show that TRPV4 and CFTR are expressed in distinct epithelial cell types, indicating that TRPV4-induced CFTR activation occurs through a paracrine mechanism.
sections.Methods: Expression patterns of TRPV4 and CFTR were analyzed in airway epithelial cells to determine their cellular localization. A luciferase-based assay was used to measure ATP release following TRPV4 activation at the apical surface. Pharmacological inhibitors and receptor antagonists were employed to dissect the contribution of purinergic and adenosine signaling pathways to CFTR activation.
sections.Results: TRPV4 and CFTR were found to be expressed in distinct epithelial cell types, indicating that CFTR activation occurs via a paracrine mechanism. Activation of TRPV4 induced significant ATP release at the apical surface. Extracellular ATP stimulated CFTR activity through activation of both P2Y2 purinergic receptors and A2B adenosine receptors. Combined inhibition of these receptors nearly abolished TRPV4-dependent CFTR activation.
sections.Conclusion: These findings identify a TRPV4–ATP/adenosine–CFTR signaling axis that links mechanical and chemical stimuli to the regulation of airway surface hydration. This paracrine pathway provides new insight into the control of mucociliary clearance and may have important implications for airway diseases.

sections.Objectives: Highly Effective Triple Modulator Therapy (HEMT), such as Kaftrio, that targets the Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Regulator (CFTR), channel has revolutionised treatment in People with CF (PwCF). However, some individuals receiving modulator therapy continue to exhibit residual airway inflammation and bronchiectasis. Extracellular Vesicles (EVs) are nanoparticles involved in intracellular communication and regulating inflammation. We aim to characterise the effect of Kaftrio on EVs and their associated protein targets in clinical samples from PwCF, to identify potential EV proteins regulating inflammation.
sections.Methods: Sputum (n=40) and Nasal Lavage (NL) (n=60) samples from PwCF were obtained (at base point (0M), six months (6M) and 12 months (12M) post-Kaftrio initiation). EVs were isolated by precipitation/ultracentrifugation and characterised using Nanoparticle Tracking Analysis (NTA), immunoblotting, Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) and Mass Spectrometry (MS)-based proteomics.
sections.Results: NTA revealed significant reductions in EV numbers at 12M post-Kaftrio initiation, in the 50-1000nm size range (p>0.05). Particles were confirmed to be EVs using immunoblotting and TEM. MS-based proteomics revealed 189 significantly changing proteins post-Kaftrio, with 92 proteins changing from 0M to 6M and 89 changing from 0M to 12M. These proteins represented pathways and processes dysregulated in CF relating to CFTR misfolding (e.g. proteasome), CF immune biology (neutrophil activation) and extracellular matrix remodelling. Discussion: We observed a decrease in the number of EVs in NL and sputum from PwCF undergoing modulator therapy. Post-treatment protein profiles demonstrated that Kaftrio has a restorative effect on dysfunctional CF signalling modulated by EVs. However several regulators of inflammation, including neutrophil regulators from the S100 family, were not corrected by Kaftrio, indicating their potential as therapeutic targets. Shared authors JH, EAP

sections.Objectives: As people with cystic fibrosis (CF) live longer, it has become apparent they have an increased risk of developing cancer, at a younger age, especially bowel cancer. In the UK, CF care teams have begun to offer cancer screening and surveillance information, promotion and care. We aimed to conduct, to our knowledge, the first qualitative studies to examine patients’ and clinicians’ experiences, challenges and needs in this growing area of CF care.
sections.Methods: Semi-structured interviews with, to date, 29 people with CF and 34 multidisciplinary clinicians (e.g., consultants, nurses, dietitians, endoscopists) (N=63), recruited from six UK adult CF centres. Data are analysed concurrently using the framework method of thematic analysis. A patient representative group is involved in all research stages including data analysis and dissemination.
sections.Results: Key emerging findings, from ongoing data collection, show generally low awareness of CF-increased cancer risk among patients, and desire for more and earlier information from CF teams, and increasing provision of CF-tailored colonoscopy screening by CF teams, with high patient receptivity to this. Data suggest lower focus by CF teams on the national cancer screenings (e.g. cervical, breast), with potential for patients to therefore view these as not crucially important if not promoted within CF care. Interviews elucidate patients’ questions, concerns, and misunderstandings around cancer risk and care, and highlight patients’ evolving use of internet-based information and support (e.g. peer social media, AI chatbots).
sections.Conclusion: Data are providing rich insight into potential ways to enhance experiences and effectiveness of cancer preventive and early detection care for people with CF. Findings will help inform and develop patient information and support, practice recommendations and service provision. Funded by: UK National Institute for Health and Care Research, Bowel Research UK & UK CF Trust.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 12 - Finding airway pathogens after ETI: where, when and how should we sample?

sections.Background: Regular screening for bronchopulmonary colonisation is standard of care for people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). Induced sputum (IS) is considered the gold standard for microbiological surveillance. Since the initiation of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI), sputum production has declined, potentially affecting surveillance strategies. This study investigated changes in bronchopulmonary colonisation before and after ETI initiation using IS samples.
sections.Methods: Microbiological data from IS samples were retrospectively analysed over 3 years. Analyses assessed dynamics of chronic colonisation and the emergence of intermittent pathogen detection (≥2 but <4 positive detections/year). Findings were correlated with structural lung disease assessed by CT using the Bhalla score.
sections.Results: A total of 1,535 IS samples (576 pre; 959 post-ETI) from 101 pwCF (61.4% adults; 51% female; mean age 22 years [6–48]) were analysed. Chronic colonisation in adults declined significantly 2 years after ETI initiation (68% vs. 42%, p=<.001), with marked reductions in Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus (p=.008 and <.001). Although paediatric numbers were limited for statistical analysis, chronic colonisation halved. Pathogens were detected intermittently in 27% of adults and 36% of children, most commonly S. aureus. Structural lung abnormalities persisted in 89% of patients. No association was found between intermittent colonisation and bronchiectasis, whereas chronic colonisation was associated with bronchiectasis in the paediatric cohort (p=.004).
sections.Conclusion: IS sampling reveals a significant reduction in chronic bronchopulmonary colonisation in adults, despite persistent structural lung disease. Intermittent pathogens remain prevalent across age groups; long-term studies are required to clarify the clinical relevance of these microbiological findings. Regular IS-based surveillance should remain an integral component of CF care.

presentations.summary: Objectives: Complex alleles (CA) of F508del-CFTR, i.e. the presence of more than one variant in cis with F508del variant, are reported to modify both clinical manifestations and the response to modulators. presentations.session_title: Workshop 13 - From allele to human: the complexity of CFTR

sections.Objectives: Complex alleles (CA) of F508del-CFTR, i.e. the presence of more than one variant in cis with F508del variant, are reported to modify both clinical manifestations and the response to modulators. In a large cohort of Italian people with Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF), we recruited unrelated CF patients, heterozygous for the F508del and a minimal function variant, who were referred as having limited or negligible beneficial effects following treatment with Elexacaftor, Tezacaftor, Ivacaftor (ETI). CFTR NGS analysis revealed that the majority of these subjects were carriers of F508del CA, not responsive to ETI. We asked whether these patients benefit from the treatment with the newly approved combination Vanzacaftor, Tezacatfor (VT).
sections.Methods: We performed functional and biochemical studies ex vivo on primary cultures of nasal epithelial cells (HNEC) derived from patients with CF and in vitro on heterologous expression systems.
sections.Results: Functional studies on differentiated nasal epithelia confirmed a lack or negligible rescue of CFTR activity by ET treatment for all patients. By contrast, pharmacological modulation with VT increased by two-fold the CFTR-dependent current only on one of the CA under study. In vitro functional and biochemical studies on heterologous expression systems regarding the rescue of CA-derived protein by both ET and VT, showed, in some cases, differences in the pattern of rescue as compared to data collected on primary HNECs, possibly due to the different cell background.
sections.Conclusion: Our study provides information regarding the sensitivity of ultra-rare F508del-CFTR CA to treatment with the novel Vanzacaftor combination. Data collected suggest that pwCF carrying F508del-CFTR CA and not responding to ET, could benefit from the treatment with VT, extending precision medicine–based therapeutic approaches. Supported by The Italian Ministry of Health (PNRR-MR1-2023-12378412), FFC Ricerca (FFC#10/2021, Kaftrio nella vita reale substudy 1).

presentations.session_title: Workshop 14 - New actors in epithelial pathophysiology

sections.Background: Nonsense mutations in the CFTR gene hamper protein synthesis, generating severely truncated CFTR forms unresponsive to present therapies. Recently we reported that in vitro cultured human bronchial epithelial cells (HBECs) showed an enhanced response to a triple compound combination including VX-809 as CFTR corrector, ELX-02 as a RT agent and the eRF3a degrader CC-90009 when treated for 72 hours with IL-4 or TNFα plus IL-17. We are currently investigating at the molecular level the mechanism by which cytokine stimulation together with drug treatment enhance CFTR function.
sections.Methods: In vitro differentiated HBECs from a patient with the G542X mutation were cultured ± IL-4 or TNFα/IL-17A for 72 h. In the last 24 h, cells received ELX-02 + VX-809 and CC-90009 (combo). Bulk RNA-seq assessed gene expression changes under treatments.
sections.Results: We focused on IL-4 given its strong effect on full-length CFTR expression. Gene-set enrichment analysis (GSEA) of RNA-seq data showed significant downregulation of the reactome terms “nonsense mediated decay (NMD) pathway”, “rRNA processing”, “response of EIF2AK4-GCN2 to amino acid deficiency” in epithelia treated with IL-4 alone compared to control condition. The same datasets appear downregulated also in epithelia treated with the combo alone and in epithelia where IL-4 was added to the combo. IL-4 appears to contribute to the downregulation of the NMD pathway decreasing UPF3A and PABPC1 transcripts.
sections.Conclusion: Transcriptomic analysis of G542X-CFTR bronchial epithelial cells suggests inhibition of NMD by IL-4 treatment. A deeper investigation is needed to reveal novel molecular targets belonging to translation fidelity and/or other post-translational modifications pathways that can be modulated with other pharmacological strategies. Funding: Cystic Fibrosis Foundation (GALIET22I0), Italian Cystic Fibrosis Foundation (FFC#9/2022, GMRF#1/2024), and Italian Ministry of Health (GR-2018-12367126).

presentations.session_title: Workshop 10 - Immune and inflammatory processes in the cystic fibrosis airway: novel insights

sections.Objectives: Progressive lung damage in cystic fibrosis (CF) is tightly linked to chronic infection and inflammation, yet the immune mechanisms connecting early Pseudomonas aeruginosa colonization to sustained immunopathology remain incompletely defined. We aimed to characterize pathogenic Th17 responses in CF lungs and their microbial drivers.
sections.Methods: We combined immune profiling of CF lung and blood samples with transcriptomics, TCR repertoire analysis, epithelial injury assays, and dendritic cell–T cell polarization models using early and chronically adapted clinical P. aeruginosa isolates.
sections.Results: We identify two IFNγ-producing pathogenic Th17 subsets—Th1/17⁺ and pTh17 cells—selectively enriched in CF lungs colonized by P. aeruginosa. These populations derive from protective conventional Th17 precursors through in situ reprogramming and clonal expansion, acquiring the capacity to impair epithelial barrier integrity and amplify pro-inflammatory signaling. P. aeruginosa persists within dendritic cells and promotes an IL-1β/IL-23–dominated polarizing milieu that favors IFNγ⁺ Th17 differentiation over classical Th1 responses. Strikingly, early clinical isolates, prior to chronic adaptation, already display a strong ability to rewire Th17 fate, inducing a transcriptional reprogramming that mirrors pathogenic Th1/17⁺ and pTh17 cells resident in CF lungs
sections.Conclusion: Our data identify a clinically relevant immune–microbial circuit linking P. aeruginosa infection to pathogenic Th17 plasticity and epithelial damage in CF, highlighting novel targets for precision immunomodulatory interventions.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 11 - The changing face of cystic fibrosis wellbeing, risk and reproductive realities

sections.Objectives: Nearly 98% of males with cystic fibrosis (MwCF) are born with congenital bilateral absence of the vas deferens. With recent advancements in treatment and quality of life, more adults with CF are pursuing family building, yet infertility remains unchanged among MwCF, requiring them to utilize assisted reproductive technology (ART) or other family-building methods..
sections.Methods: We recruited MwCF and their partners from across the United States who have considered or pursued biologic parenthood via ART to participate in semi-structured dyadic interviews. We explored experiences with fertility referral and evaluation, sperm extraction, in vitro fertilization (IVF), and other family-building methods. Thematic analysis is ongoing.
sections.Results: To date, we have interviewed 21 MwCF (age range 28-48 years) and their partners (age range 25-45 years). Preliminary themes include: 1) Most couples cited cost as their primary concern about pursuing ART, even among those who had insurance coverage or received charitable subsidies; 2) Couples desired more information about the impact of cystic fibrosis (CF) on sperm quality, ART success rates in CF, and expected timelines of the ART process; 3) Many MwCF feel guilt or helplessness about the physical toll of IVF on their partners; 4) For MwCF, the greater medicalization of the female partner in ART was a role reversal; 5) Couples highlighted the importance of teamwork and communication while undergoing ART.
sections.Conclusion: Preliminary results show that pursuing ART involves financial, logistical, physical, and emotional challenges. Given the near universality of infertility among MwCF, future work should focus on reducing barriers and providing multi-factorial support, and investing in research to optimize sperm quality for MwCF, all of which could have implications for patient populations beyond CF.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 12 - Finding airway pathogens after ETI: where, when and how should we sample?

sections.Background: ​​​​Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) is a major pathogen associated with progressive lung function decline in cystic fibrosis (CF), making early detection crucial. Sputum culture remains the reference method but has limited sensitivity in cases of low bacterial load or viable but non-culturable bacteria. Pa-specific quantitative PCR (qPCR), due to its higher sensitivity, is particularly relevant for patients producing little or no sputum, a situation increasingly frequent with CFTR modulators.
sections.Objectives: To describe respiratory sampling practices and the use of Pa-specific qPCR across French CRCMs, and to assess practice, result interpretation, and clinicians’ interest.
sections.Methods: A national questionnaire developed by the Roscoff CRCM was distributed via the MUCO-CFTR network to physicians working in adult, paediatric, or mixed CRCM. It focused on respiratory sample collection, sputum induction, and Pa-specific qPCR use.
sections.Results: Forty-one of 47 CRCMs (87%) responded. In sputum-producing patients, sputum culture remained the standard diagnostic method. In non–sputum-producing patients, practices varied: 62.5% performed induced sputum, 16.5% used pharyngeal swabs, and 12.5% used both. Induced sputum was performed systematically in 37.5% of centres and annually or based on clinical criteria in 31.3%. Only 20.8% of centres had access to Pa-specific qPCR, although 86.1% expressed interest. qPCR was mainly used in Pa-never/free or intermittently infected patients and rarely in chronic infection. A positive qPCR with negative culture usually led to closer monitoring or eradication therapy, most commonly inhaled tobramycin. In asymptomatic patients, 1 positive target prompted follow-up, whereas 2 or 3 led to treatment.
sections.Conclusion: This survey highlights marked heterogeneity in respiratory sampling practices and limited access to Pa-specific qPCR across French CRCMs, despite strong professional interest. Harmonisation of Pa surveillance strategies is needed in the era of CFTR modulators.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 13 - From allele to human: the complexity of CFTR

sections.Objectives: The relationship between CFTR function and phenotype remains incompletely understood. Prior work has yielded heterogeneous findings, suggesting linear and non-linear associations. We sought to characterize the CFTR genotype-function-phenotype relationship using data from the Clinical and Functional TRanslation of CFTR (CFTR2) database and human nasal epithelial (HNE) theratyping assays.
sections.Methods: Mean sweat [Cl-] from 85,737 individuals in the CFTR2 database harboring 478 CFTR genotypes were linked to variant functional measures obtained in heterologous cell lines. Total CFTR genotype function was calculated as the average percent wild-type chloride conductance of both alleles. Linear regression models weighted by sample size were applied to evaluate the function- sweat [Cl-] relationship. A similar framework was applied to a cohort with available HNE data, linking sweat [Cl⁻] to CFTR function for 85 individuals with 42 genotypes.
sections.Results: The association between CFTR function and sweat [Cl-] varied across the functional spectrum. While a global linear model demonstrated an overall significant association in CFTR2 data, this approach obscured substantial heterogeneity across the functional range. Piecewise linear regression models fit within specified functional ranges revealed marked attenuation of slope magnitude with increasing function, with the steepest associations observed at low function and progressively reduced slope magnitude at higher function. Despite markedly smaller sample size, directionally similar attenuation was observed in HNE data.
sections.Conclusion: Large-scale registry data suggest that the function-sweat [Cl-] relationship is not sufficiently described by a single linear effect but may be more accurately represented by a logarithmic relationship in which incremental changes in CFTR activity have differential phenotypic impact across the functional spectrum. Further modeling incorporating complementary data may provide additional insights.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 14 - New actors in epithelial pathophysiology

sections.Background: Pulmonary disease is the main cause of morbidity and mortality in cystic fibrosis (CF), and recovery of lung function relies on efficient repair of airway damage, particularly in advanced disease. Beyond its role in mucociliary clearance, CFTR directly regulates epithelial repair. Therefore, both the CFTR defect and chronic infection impair this process in CF airways, highlighting the need for pro-repair therapeutic strategies. Objectives and
sections.Methods: Our aim was to dissect how CFTR contributes to epithelial repair and how CFTR modulators (CFTRm), alone or combined with complementary CFTR-independent approaches, enhance repair. Primary airway epithelial cultures from people with CF (pwCF) carrying diverse CFTR mutations were used to assess early and late repair processes, including directed cell migration, progenitor cell proliferation and regeneration of a differentiated ciliated epithelium.
sections.Results: Our data confirm CF airway epithelia displayed defective repair. CFTR rescue using CFTRm (Lumacaftor/Ivacaftor or Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor) enhanced coordinated cell migration with activation of integrins at the wound edge, accelerated wound closure and promoted restoration of a tight, ciliated epithelium. Strategies reducing P. aeruginosa and S. aureus virulence preserved epithelial repair under infectious conditions. In parallel, activation of K+ channels induced significant pro-repair effects, including in cultures from pwCF carrying CFTRm non-responsive mutations.
sections.Conclusion: These findings identify CFTR as a central regulator of airway epithelial repair and support combined targeting of CFTR, K+ channels and/or bacterial virulence to restore epithelial integrity, with potential benefits in patients with advanced pulmonary disease or limited responsiveness to current CFTR modulators. Funding: CIHR, AIRS Network

presentations.session_title: Workshop 10 - Immune and inflammatory processes in the cystic fibrosis airway: novel insights

sections.Objectives: Despite the transformative impact of Highly Effective CFTR Modulator Therapy (HEMT) on people with Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF), persistent pulmonary inflammation remains a critical driver of progressive lung damage. Current anti-inflammatory options are limited by significant side effects, highlighting an urgent need for safe, HEMT-compatible therapies that specifically target the excessive influx of polymorphonuclear neutrophils (PMNs) into the airways. We characterized GY971, a novel anti-inflammatory candidate designed to reduce PMN recruitment by downregulating chemokine expression in respiratory epithelial cells, without the cyclooxygenase (COX) inhibition associated with traditional NSAIDs.
sections.Methods: GY971 was evaluated across several in vitro, ex vivo, and in vivo models, including primary CF bronchial/nasal cells (ALI culture), zebrafish, and murine lung infection models.
sections.Results: Our findings demonstrate that GY971: -Inhibits the release of PMN-recruiting chemokines at nanomolar concentrations in primary CF epithelial cells. -Decreases in vivo PMN infiltration in infected models without compromising bacterial clearance. -Maintains full compatibility with HEMT, showing no interference with F508del-CFTR functional rescue. -Exhibits a favorable safety profile, with no detectable toxicity in zebrafish or murine organs, no genotoxicity, and no significant off-target activity on the SafetyScreen44™ panel.
sections.Conclusion: The activity of GY971 is mediated by interference with the interaction between activated NF-kB and its consensus DNA sequences within the promoters of key pro-inflammatory cytokines. This mechanism parallels the beneficial effects of high-dose ibuprofen on PMN reduction but avoids the gastrointestinal toxicity linked to COX-1 inhibition. Recognized as an Orphan Drug by the EMA in 2024, GY971 represents a promising, highly specific approach to managing CF airway inflammation.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 11 - The changing face of cystic fibrosis wellbeing, risk and reproductive realities

sections.Objectives: CFTR modulators such as elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) have improved life expectancy and health outcomes for people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF), leading to an increasing number choosing the path of parenthood. This study evaluates maternal, paternal, and neonatal outcomes in pwCF using ETI during conception and/or pregnancy in a large adult CF center with over 300 pwCF in the Netherlands.
sections.Methods: A retrospective medical record study included at least 35 pwCF (25 women, 10 men). Data from before conception until one year postpartum were collected. The main focus was maternal health; these outcomes included FEV₁, pulmonary exacerbations, antibiotic use, BMI, and pregnancy-related complications. Secondly, we wanted to evaluate obstetric and neonatal outcomes and breastfeeding practices. Thirdly, the health and outcomes of fathers using ETI during TESE/PESA procedures were evaluated.
sections.Results: Maternal FEV₁ and body weight of mothers remained stable before, during, and after pregnancy. Pulmonary exacerbations increased during pregnancy and were significantly more frequent postpartum (P=0.03). Gestational diabetes occurred in 30% of pregnancies. Rates of labor induction and caesarean section were relatively high. Except for two cases of ileus in neonates, all pregnancies resulted in healthy infants; 85% were full-term without obstetric complications. Despite recommendations against breastfeeding during ETI therapy, 20% of mothers breastfed. All six men using ETI during TESE/PESA became fathers of healthy infants. Additional data are currently collected and will be available at the conference meeting.
sections.Conclusion: In this cohort ETI use during the perinatal period was associated with stable maternal lung function and weight and favorable neonatal outcomes. Although pulmonary exacerbations increased in mothers, particularly postpartum, nearly all pregnancies resulted in healthy infants, supporting the safety of ETI during pregnancy and parenthood in pwCF.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 12 - Finding airway pathogens after ETI: where, when and how should we sample?

sections.Objectives: Sputum is the gold standard for routine respiratory microbiology in cystic fibrosis (CF); however, in pwCF on ETI it has become more difficult to obtain in clinical practice. This study compared bacterial burden and pathogen detection using a series of different sampling methods to determine how closely they approximate sputum, in a cohort of pwCF who were Pseudomonas aeruginosa (PA) negative on standard hospital culture post-ETI.
sections.Methods: Total bacterial density was calculated by summing colony-forming units across all cultured species per subject (n=32) and sample type, and reported using method-specific units (CFU/g for sputum [SPU], CFU/mL for bronchoalveolar lavage [BAL], bilateral panlobar wash [BLW] and saliva [SAL] and cough swab [SWA] as CFU/mL QSRS diluent). Results are shown as median and IQR range. Core pathogens were defined according to the literature.
sections.Results: Total bacterial burden differed substantially by sampling method. SAL samples showed highest bacterial counts (median 3.7 × 10⁸ CFU/mL; IQR 9.6 × 10⁷-9.5 × 10⁸; n=23). SPU samples also showed high bacterial burden, with a median of 2.9 × 10⁷ CFU/g (IQR 1.3 × 10⁷-5.9 × 10⁷; n=26). BAL and BLW samples yielded substantially lower bacterial counts, with median values of 1.0 × 10⁴ CFU/mL (IQR 3.7 × 10³-3.4 × 10⁴; n=27) and 6.9 × 10³ CFU/mL (IQR 2.0 × 10³-2.4 × 10⁴; n = 27) respectively. SWA samples demonstrated median values of 2.6 x 105 CFU/mL diluent (IQR 2.3 × 10³-1.9 × 106; n=23). For detection of core pathogens, relative to SPU, BAL and BLW detected 33% of core pathogens, compared with 22% for SAL and SWA samples. PA was detected in SPU only from one subject and SPU, BAL and BLW samples from two subjects, but was not detected in either SAL or SWA specimens.
sections.Conclusion: BAL and BLW most closely approximated SPU for lower-airway microbial detection of core pathogens, while SAL provided broader but less specific microbial recovery. No alternative sampling method fully recapitulated SPU.

presentations.title: Development of an unbiased and sensitive AI-based morphological pipeline for challenging cystic fibrosis diagnoses
presentations.summary: Objectives: Diagnosing CF can be challenging in individuals carrying rare or poorly characterised CFTR variants, where conventional tests (genetics and sweat chloride test) are inconclusive. presentations.session_title: Workshop 13 - From allele to human: the complexity of CFTR

sections.Objectives: Diagnosing CF can be challenging in individuals carrying rare or poorly characterised CFTR variants, where conventional tests (genetics and sweat chloride test) are inconclusive. Forskolin-induced swelling (FIS) of patient-derived intestinal organoids (PDIOs) is used to determine CFTR function but does not capture steady-state organoid morphology. Previous morphology-based approaches, such as rectal organoid morphology analysis (ROMA), rely on predefined thresholds or manual bias, limiting diagnostic utility . To address this, our consortia (ADVANCE-CFTR), have developed an unbiased AI-based morphological analysis pipeline to sensitively analyse steady state morphology for diagnostic use.
sections.Methods: Calcein green fluorescence images of PDIOs were analysed using this pipeline to extract quantitative morphological features, including lumen/organoid intensity ratios (IR) and size. Morphological profiles were assessed at steady state and following FIS assays. Proof-of-concept analysis was performed using one wild-type (WT) and one CF organoid line (F508del/F508del), with additional assessment following CFTR modulator exposure (ETI).
sections.Results: Compared with ROMA, AI-derived analysis classified a significantly higher fraction of organoids as WT-like across matched images (paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test, n=10 images, p <0.01). Using this approach, WT and CF organoids were clearly distinguished, with WT organoids characterised by an IR <1 profile. Following CFTR treatment with ETI under FIS, the proportion of CF organoids classified as WT-like increased from 2% to 69%, consistent with functional rescue observed in FIS assays.
sections.Conclusion: AI-based analysis of organoid morphology can distinguish WT from CF organoids in an unbiased way and detect modulator-associated recovery, providing a complementary diagnostic readout to FIS. This proof-of-concept study supports further validation of AI-driven morphology for challenging CF diagnostics.

presentations.title: Proteases, GAGs and fibronectin a harmful trio in predisposing the cystic fibrosis airway epithelium infections
presentations.summary: Structural changes in the CF lungs result from a protease/anti-protease imbalance favouring recurrent respiratory infections by opportunistic bacteria such as Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa). We have recently shown that the CF airway epithelium exhibits a defect in apicobasal polarization, characterized by luminal deposition of fibronectin that promotes the adhesion of Pa. Interestingly, it is not fibronectin in its integral form that is responsible for Pa adhesion, but the fragments generated by proteolysis. To target efficiently Pa adhesion, it is crucial to uncover the proteases involved in fibronectin remodelling. presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Glasgow, School of Infection and Immunity, Glasgow, United Kingdom, 2Newcastle University, Translational and Clinical Research Institute, Faculty of Medical Sciences, Newcastle upon Tyne, United Kingdom, 3University of Glasgow, School of Cancer Sciences, Glasgow, United Kingdom
presentations.session_title: Workshop 10 - Immune and inflammatory processes in the cystic fibrosis airway: novel insights

sections.Background: Calprotectin (S100A8/A9) is a major biomarker of CF lung disease, being associated with increased inflammation during exacerbation, predicting future exacerbation in people with CF and improvements with CFTR modulators. Although calprotectin is thought to be expressed primarily by neutrophils, advanced spatial and single cell RNA sequencing techniques allow us to refine the expression pattern of calprotectin across multiple cell types in primary lung tissue. This study will define the cellular populations in CF and non-CF expressing calprotectin in the lung and how this relates to inflammation. Aims: To determine lung cell expression of S100A8/A9 and visualise the spatial expression of S100A8/A9 in the inflamed lung.
sections.Methods: Using publicly available data (Schupp et al and Carrero et al) and our own source data (lung explants) we mapped calprotectin expression across major cell types in the lung. For spatial assessment we employed Naonstring CosMx SMI platform with 6000plex gene panel. A bespoke analysis pipeline was developed for cell margin identification, transcriptional profiling and data visualisation.
sections.Results: Re-analysis of publicly available data demonstrates the expression of S100A8 and S100A9 in granulocytes, monocytes and epithelial cells. S100A9 expression is generally higher than S100A8 expression in epithelial populations. Spatial analysis demonstrates multiple significant differentially expressed genes between healthy and CF samples across multiple cell types including immune cells, epithelial cells and stromal cells. S100A9 is expressed by ciliated, secretory and basal cells as well as transcriptionally active neutrophils. Expression of S100A9 in control epithelial cells was lower with fewer inflammatory cells present.
sections.Conclusion: This data supports the hypothesis that calprotectin may have a multi-faceted role across several cell types and demonstrates the power of spatial technologies in visualising inflammation in the lung.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 11 - The changing face of cystic fibrosis wellbeing, risk and reproductive realities

sections.Objectives: Women with CF have suboptimal use of contraception and a significant rate of unplanned pregnancy. We developed a nurse-led initiative to provide advice and initial prescription of contraception to women attending the CF clinic.
sections.Results: Of 50 pregnancies in wwCF attending our Centre, 17 (34%) were not planned, 15 (30%) did not have partner CF gene testing pre-conception and 5 pregnancies were terminated. Of 67 wwCF who were in a heterosexual relationship, 9 (13%) stated that they did not want to become pregnant but were not using contraception and 11(16%) were relying on condom contraception. As part of a service improvement project a CF nurse-prescriber, supported by a gynaecologist, undertook further training in contraception to provide advice to wwCF on their contraceptive options and to offer initial bridging contraception prescription of a progestogen only pill directly from the CF clinic. Discussions during these consultations indicate that some wwCF demonstrate reluctance to engage with family-planning services. Some have misunderstandings about fertility in CF and concerns around contraception and aspects of CF such as absorption of medication, bone health and potential interactions with other CF medications. Areas of ongoing development include staff training, written patient information, and links with women’s health and specialist family planning services.
sections.Conclusion: Currently pregnancy planning and pregnancy prevention are suboptimal, and some wwCF do not access family planning services. A consultation with a CF nurse prescriber, focused on contraception advice and prescription aims to reduce the rate of unplanned pregnancy.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 12 - Finding airway pathogens after ETI: where, when and how should we sample?

sections.Objectives: Chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa pulmonary infection has long been considered irreversible in People With Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF). After the initiation of the highly effective modulator treatment elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI), pathogen detection rates have declined with some patients even becoming culture negative. This study tests the hypothesis that P. aeruginosa persists in the airways of pwCF previously classified as chronically infected, even when routine cultures have remained negative for at least one year following initiation ETI.
sections.Methods: Prospective, single-centre cohort study (May 2024–June 2025). Including pwCF aged >18 years with prior chronic P. aeruginosa infection, who had become culture-negative for ≥12 months after ETI initiation. Participants underwent a Complete Broncho-Sinus Sampling (CBSS) protocol, including systematic bronchoalveolar lavage of all lung lobes and sinonasal endoscopy, with culture and 16S nanopore sequencing. Daily suppressive antibiotic therapy was paused ≥14 days prior to sampling.
sections.Results: Ten participants (median age 31 years; median duration of prior chronic infection 24 years) were enrolled. No P. aeruginosa was detected by culture in any CBSS sample. 16S sequencing identified a single occurrence of P. aeruginosa (relative abundance 1.9%) from one participant, who subsequently experienced microbiological relapse during follow-up. Across 2,539 cumulative, antibiotic-free, follow-up days and 71 sputum cultures, only this single relapse occurred.
sections.Conclusion: These findings demonstrate that long-standing chronic P. aeruginosa infection can be cleared following ETI. This study adds evidence that chronic infections might not be irreversible and highlights the need to re-evaluate antibiotic treatment guidelines in the era of CFTR modulators.

presentations.title: The CFTR & chloride conundrum: comparing sweat chloride and clinical phenotype between the D1152H and R117H-7T variants
presentations.summary: Introduction: D1152H and R117H-7T are variants of variable clinical consequence (VVCC) that differ in clinical presentation. Sweat chloride concentration (SCC) is a key diagnostic and prognostic measure for CF. SCC can be variable with VVCCs and more likely to fall in the normal or borderline diagnostic range. We hypothesised that people with D1152H have a lower SCC, yet paradoxically higher rates of bronchiectasis compared to R117H-7T. Aim: A between-group comparison of SCC and clinical phenotype for the 2 VVCCs. Methods: A retrospective review of adults and children compound heterozygous for D1152H or R117H-7T and a CF-causing variant attending CF, CFTR-related disorder or CF screen-positive inconclusive dia...

presentations.affiliations_text: 1Royal Brompton Hospital, Guy's and St Thomas' NHS Foundation Trust, Cystic Fibrosis, London, United Kingdom, 2Poole Hospital, University Hospitals Dorset NHS Foundation Trust, Poole, United Kingdom, 3National Heart and Lung Institute, Imperial College London, London, United Kingdom

sections.Methods: A retrospective review of adults and children compound heterozygous for D1152H or R117H-7T and a CF-causing variant attending CF, CFTR-related disorder or CF screen-positive inconclusive diagnosis clinics before January 2024. Data included sex, age at diagnosis, referral route, SCC, FEV1, bronchiectasis and other CFTR-related complications. For some analyses, adults (typically presenting clinically) and children (primarily detected via newborn screening) were considered separately. Data are presented as median [IQR]. Applicable non-parametric tests were used for analysis.
sections.Results: Sixty-nine people were included; 21 with D1152H and 48 with R117H-7T. Of these, 38 were adults (12 D1152H and 26 R117H-7T) and 31 were children (9 D1152H and 22 R117H-7T). There was no association between variant and adult vs children. Median SCC was lower in those with D1152H (21.2 mmol/L [17.5-27.0]) than R117H-7T (33.0 mmol/L [28.1-53.4]; p<0.001). There was no statistically significant different between SCC in adults and children with D1152H (26.8 vs 19.0 mmol/L, p>0.05). Adults with R117H-7T had significantly higher SCC than children (49.2 mmol/L [32.5–56.8] vs 30.5 mmol/L [23.9–33.0]; p<0.001). CT-confirmed bronchiectasis was present in 6/11 adults with D1152H vs 2/26 with R117H-7T (54.5% vs 7.7%; p=0.0016).
sections.Conclusion: Adults with a D1152H variant have markedly higher rates of bronchiectasis than the R117H-7T group, despite normal median SCC. A normal SCC in D1152H should not be assumed to indicate mild disease and close attention should be paid to those with this variant.

Using transcriptomic and post-transcriptional analyses of CF human airway epithelial cells, we identified dynamic stabilization of the gap junction protein Connexin 43 (Cx43) during epithelial maturation. Jana i Ireny Rudników, Rabka-Zdrój, Poland, 10CF Centre University Hospital Motol, Prague, Czech Republic, 11SpliSense Ltd., Jerusalem, Israel, 12CF Center, Department of Pediatrics, Hadassah Medical Center and Faculty of Medicine, Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Jerusalem, Israel, 13CF Center West Brandenburg, Potsdam, Germany

sections.Background: SPL84 is an inhaled antisense oligonucleotide (ASO) for the treatment of people with pwCF carrying the 3849 +10kb C->T (3849) mutation. SPL84 was shown to fully restore CFTR function in patient-derived human nasal epithelial cells (HNEs) and human bronchial epithelial cells (HBEs). A Phase 1 study was successfully completed in healthy volunteers. SPL84-002 is a global Phase 2a multiple ascending dose study in 3849 pwCF to evaluate the safety and preliminary efficacy of SPL84.
sections.Methods: Participants had diagnosis of CF and two CF causing mutations, with the 3849 mutation on at least one allele, were above age 18, with FEV1 40-90 % predicted, and were not taking CFTR modulators. Two dose cohorts were completed; 25 mg and 50 mg; n=8 in each group, randomized at a ratio of 3:1 active:placebo (6 active, 2 placebo), for a total of 12 participants receiving 9 weekly active doses of SPL84.
sections.Results: There were no drug-related Serious Adverse Event (SAEs) in the study, and there were no significant drug-related AEs. A meaningful clinical improvement in lung function was observed in ~70% of 25 mg participants and 50% of 50 mg participants, as reflected by change from baseline in ppFEV1 ≥ 5. In both cohorts, an average upward improvement trend in ppFEV1 over time was observed, with an average change from baseline in ppFEV1 of 5 for both cohorts. A positive response in change from baseline in ppFEF25-75 was also observed, which was in general in correlation to the response in ppFEV1. Additionally, ~70% of 25 mg participants and 50% of 50 mg participants had a positive response in the CF Questionnaire-Revised Respiratory Symptom Score (CFQ-R RSS) quality of life measurement.
sections.Conclusion: This is the first evidence of potential clinical benefit of inhaled ASO therapy in a pulmonary disease, supporting advancement of the program to the expanded 3849 patient population. An additional cohort is planned to examine the combined effect of SPL84 and CFTR modulators.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 16 - Further optimising measures of cystic fibrosis pulmonary disease

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has been shown to be safe and effective in people with cystic fibrosis (CF) aged >6 years with at least one F508del-CFTR allele but there are very few studies in pre-school children (PSC-CF) regarding CF lung structural damage. The primary objective of this study is to evaluate the impact of ETI on lung structure in children with CF aged 2–5 years.
sections.Methods: Clinical data, pulmonary function tests and sputum induction were performed at baseline (M0) and at 1 year (M12) in 211 children enrolled within the French real-life study, MODUl-CF (NCT04301856). Neutrophil elastase, calprotectin, IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8 and TNF-α concentrations were measured at M0 and M12 in sputum secretions.
sections.Results: 63 children experienced transient sleep and or behavior disorders, which were resolved for most of them at M12. Decreases in sweat chloride concentration (-55.2mmol/L; 95% confidence interval [CI],-59.2 to -51.1; n=120 at M12), weight z-score (+ 0.15; 95% CI,+0.07 to +0.22; n = 208) and ppFEV1 (+ 17.8; 95% CI,+6.9 to +28.7; n = 71) were observed at M12. There was a significant reduction in mucus plugs (p=0.003) and airway wall thickening (p=0.006). at M12 (paired analysis of 55 chest CT). This was correlated with improvements in sputum pro-inflammatory markers. The median ratio of Bronchial outer diameter relative to that of its adjacent Artery (Bout/A) was within normal range at baseline (1.14 (0.94 ; 1.28, n=71) and did not change significantly at M12. Baseline Bout/A was predictive of the decrease of the proportion of severely dilated Bronchial/artery pairs (Bout/A ≥ 1.5) (OR=0.02(95% CI 0.001, 0.23); p=0.006).
sections.Conclusion: Inflammatory airway remodeling improves significantly at 12 months ETI in children 2 to 5 years old. Bronchial dilatation at baseline is predictive of lung structure change at 1 year. Early intervention with ETI may reverse the progression of structural lung disease in young children with CF.

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) therapy has greatly changed the course of CF for most people living with this disease. While the impact of ETI on bacterial lung pathogens and the wider microbiota is now being increasingly studied, fungal pathogens and the respiratory mycobiota are poorly understood. Here we investigated changes to the respiratory mycobiome before and through 3 years of ETI therapy in adults with CF (awCF).
sections.Methods: Targeted ITS1 sequencing of the respiratory mycobiome was performed on pre- and on-ETI respiratory samples from 276 awCF attending clinics in the UK (Cardiff, Manchester, Southampton), USA (Vermont, Pittsburgh) and Canada (Calgary). Pre-samples were taken within 6-months prior to starting therapy. On-ETI samples were stratified by duration (6 months, 1 year, 2 years, and 3 years).
sections.Results: Only 169 awCF had a detectable fungal signature, with 32.0% of pre-ETI and 66.3% of on-ETI samples fungal positive. Mycobiota diversity increased with duration on therapy, and composition became significantly more similar over time from 1 year on-ETI (Mann-Whitney p<0.0001, all timepoints from 1 year). Aspergillus fumigatus prevalence reduced on treatment (from 11.6% pre-ETI to 3.3% at 3 years on-ETI). Similarly, Candida parapsilosis prevalence reduced (42.1% to 23.3%), while Exophiala dermatitidis was undetectable at 3 years. Candida albicans and Candida dublinensis change was negligible. Conversely, both Malassezia restricta and Rhodotorula mucilaginosa increased pre-ETI to 3 years on-ETI (43.4% to 66.6%, and 16.9% to 36.6%, respectively).
sections.Conclusion: Here we investigated the impact of ETI on the mycobiome for the first time. Our results indicate the mycobiome is highly personalised to the individual, and long-term ETI therapy is associated with significant compositional change and changes in pathogen prevalence. We recommend more exploration into the poorly understood and understudied mycobiome and its clinical significance.

presentations.title: The challenging case of a child with cystic fibrosis whose mother has come to believe she is a sovereign citizen. How did we get here and what do we do now?
presentations.summary: Background: AG is a 15-year-old child with pancreatic insufficient cystic fibrosis (CF) whose parents are ‘May’ and ‘Ed’. Growing up, AG appeared to be happy and well-cared for in an insular and home-schooled environment. While May believed strongly in naturopathy and AG was unvaccinated, most CF therapies were still accepted. From diagnosis, May voiced strong opinions on perceived lapses in governmental responsibilities. Her first child had been removed from the home by child protection services (CPS). Despite these challenges, the CF team developed a relationship with the family that could be described as built on fragile trust. May and Ed’s marriage ended during the covid-19 pandemic; Ed became estranged fr...
presentations.authors_text: A. Gravelle1, M. Chilvers1, J. Louie1, T. Theriault1,1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1British Columbia Children's Hospital, Cystic Fibrosis Clinic, Vancouver, Canada
presentations.session_title: Workshop 18 - Complex psychosocial issues/nursing case studies

sections.Background: AG is a 15-year-old child with pancreatic insufficient cystic fibrosis (CF) whose parents are ‘May’ and ‘Ed’. Growing up, AG appeared to be happy and well-cared for in an insular and home-schooled environment. While May believed strongly in naturopathy and AG was unvaccinated, most CF therapies were still accepted. From diagnosis, May voiced strong opinions on perceived lapses in governmental responsibilities. Her first child had been removed from the home by child protection services (CPS). Despite these challenges, the CF team developed a relationship with the family that could be described as built on fragile trust. May and Ed’s marriage ended during the covid-19 pandemic; Ed became estranged from the family and May experienced significant emotional distress. Over the next three years May and AG moved multiple times and there was great effort to support the family with housing, food security, and legal aid. After several years of no contact Ed called the clinic asking about AG’s health, triggering May and AG to flee. A series of events ensued and CPS became involved. Thereafter, May sent increasingly strange and threatening emails and derogatory phone messages to CF staff indicating she felt betrayed by those on the team she had trusted. After May stopped bringing AG for CF health visits, a court ordered AG to be examined. May tried to stop this which culminated in AG's removal from May’s care and placement into a group home. Staff reported that AG appeared very traumatized by the forced separation from May. While AG initially continued daily CF care, over time AG stopped all CF therapies (including pancreatic enzymes and elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor). AG refused health assessments and referred to themself as a “stolen child” who would only listen to their mother. When AG’s health was thought to have deteriorated (reported daily coughing and thin appearance), CPS and the CF team met for a complex case consult but before any strategies could be implemented, AG fled the foster home and was reported missing for one week. AG was located at May’s home where she informed police that the no-contact order did not apply as she was a “sovereign citizen” [one who believes they are not subject to government laws]. AG is now back in foster care and refusing all CF therapies; this case is ongoing. Discussion: The main issues arising from this case study include: Legal: under the Infants Act, AG could be considered a “mature minor” who can legally refuse healthcare but requires a medical assessment in order to determine competency. Ethical: all four principles of medical ethics (beneficence; non-maleficence; autonomy; and justice) apply to this complex case. Mental Health: an assessment is required after which AG could be admitted as an involuntary patient but this could further erode trust and needs to be carefully considered. Roles of Nursing and Social Work: these have been crucial throughout AG’s life while building a therapeutic relationship with May and AG, and after AG was placed into foster care. Distress of CF healthcare team who are witnessing in effect AG’s ‘slow suicide’. Questions: Did the CF team stray from core components of a therapeutic relationship by not acting sooner on May’s growing ideology? How could the CF team have intervened differently? What is the most difficult part of this case? If AG becomes an ‘involuntary patient’ how can the CF team begin to build trust again? How can the CF team begin to heal?

presentations.session_title: Workshop 16 - Further optimising measures of cystic fibrosis pulmonary disease

sections.Objectives: Cystic Fibrosis (CF) is an inherited multisystem disorder primarily affecting the respiratory system. Pulmonary involvement remains the leading cause of morbidity and mortality in CF patients. High-Resolution Computed Tomography (HRCT) is considered the gold standard for evaluating structural lung abnormalities. However, manual interpretation of HRCT images is time-consuming, observer-dependent, and may result in variable outcomes. Artificial Intelligence (AI)-based automated analysis methods hold the potential to overcome these limitations.
sections.Methods: In this retrospective study, chest HRCT images from 48 patients with CF aged 10 to 18 years, were analyzed. Five distinct pulmonary findings bronchiectasis, bronchial dilatation, bronchial wall thickening, mucus plugging, and attenuation increase in the HRCT images were annotated by two experienced radiologists. A deep learning-based diagnostic model was developed, and its performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, F1 score (a balanced measure of sensitivity and precision), and Jaccard index (overlap between predicted and actual findings) metrics.
sections.Results: The developed deep learning-based model achieved an average accuracy of 97%, sensitivity of 97%, specificity of 97%, and F1 score of 97% across the five evaluated pathological findings in chest HRCT images of CF patients.
sections.Conclusion: This study demonstrates that deep learning-based models can accurately detect multiple pulmonary abnormalities in chest HRCT images of patients with CF. AI-assisted systems have the potential to support clinicians in clinical decision-making, facilitate early identification of complications, and contribute to individualized patient monitoring. Nevertheless, ensuring the safe and responsible use of AI requires careful attention to data security, algorithmic transparency, and rigorous validation processes.

We evaluated such in our large cohort of adult pwCF commencing ETI. Methods: Annual review data from 2019 (pre ETI) for 388 pwCF at our centre were compared with the 2025 cohort of 384 receiving ETI and to all respiratory microbiology samples obtained over a 10‑year period (n=11697). Analyses included pwCF chronically infected with Pa pre-ETI, with eradication defined as no positive sputum samples (>=3) post-ETI. Clinical...

sections.Methods: Annual review data from 2019 (pre ETI) for 388 pwCF at our centre were compared with the 2025 cohort of 384 receiving ETI and to all respiratory microbiology samples obtained over a 10‑year period (n=11697). Analyses included pwCF chronically infected with Pa pre-ETI, with eradication defined as no positive sputum samples (>=3) post-ETI. Clinical characteristics were compared between those who had/had not eradicated Pa using multivariable logistic regression to determine independent associations.
sections.Results: There were 103 pwCF with chronic infection with sufficient sampling pre/post-ETI. Eradication occurred in 30/103 (29.1%). Those who eradicated were younger (age 30 vs 35, P = 0.0147), had better lung function (ppFEV1 79 vs. 53.0, p<0.0001). 22 had Liverpool epidemic strain Pa of which 27% had eradicated. Multivariable logistic regression identified Liverpool Epidemic Strain [OR (95%CI) 0.23 (0.05-0.91) p=0.03], ppFEV1 [OR (5%CI) 1.05 (1.01-1.11) p=0.02], baseline Northern Score [OR (95%) 0.73 (0.92-1.1) p=0.049] and CFLD [OR (95%) 5.7 (1.2- 26.6) p=0.02] as independent predictors of sustained infection despite ETI. Age commencing ETI, BMI, pancreatic insufficiency, CFRD and use of nebulised antibiotics were not related to Pa eradication.
sections.Conclusion: A combination of host and microbial factors appear to be relevant for predicting post-ETI eradication of Pa where, in general, markers of milder disease severity increased chances of eradication. The association between CFLD and eradication warrants further investigation and the long-term clinical outcomes of post-ETI eradication require evaluation.

presentations.summary: Background: Advances in cystic fibrosis (CF) care have improved survival and quality of life, and more women with CF (wwCF) now pursue pregnancy. Although outcomes have improved overall, wwCF with advanced lung disease and nutritional compromise remain at high risk for maternal morbidity and adverse fetal outcomes. Assisted reproductive technologies (ART), including in vitro fertilization (IVF), are increasingly needed due to higher rates of infertility in wwCF, creating dilemmas where reproductive autonomy intersects with substantial medical risk. We report a 31-year-old wwCF (two class I CFTR mutations, non-responsive to CFTR modulators) with severe lung disease and comorbidities who requested IVF, highlight...
presentations.authors_text: M. Cohen-Cymberknoh1, A. Hershko Klement2
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Hadassah Hebrew University Medical Center, Pediatric Pulmonology and Cystic Fibrosis Center, Jerusalem, Israel, 2Hadassah Hebrew University Medical Center, IVF Unit, Mount Scopus, Jerusalem, Israel
presentations.session_title: Workshop 18 - Complex psychosocial issues/nursing case studies

sections.Background: Advances in cystic fibrosis (CF) care have improved survival and quality of life, and more women with CF (wwCF) now pursue pregnancy. Although outcomes have improved overall, wwCF with advanced lung disease and nutritional compromise remain at high risk for maternal morbidity and adverse fetal outcomes. Assisted reproductive technologies (ART), including in vitro fertilization (IVF), are increasingly needed due to higher rates of infertility in wwCF, creating dilemmas where reproductive autonomy intersects with substantial medical risk. We report a 31-year-old wwCF (two class I CFTR mutations, non-responsive to CFTR modulators) with severe lung disease and comorbidities who requested IVF, highlighting the clinical and ethical tension between patient-centered goals and uncertainty in advanced CF. Discussion: A 31-year-old wwCF (pancreatic insufficiency, CF-related diabetes, BMI ~20 kg/m²) and long-standing severe lung disease (FEV₁ ~25% predicted) sought pregnancy after >1 year of unsuccessful attempts to conceive. Despite multiple pulmonary exacerbations (PEx) requiring intravenous antibiotics over the previous 4 years, she was clinically stable. Her reproductive evaluation was normal, but her partner had severe oligo-terato-asthenospermia (CFTR-mutations negative), making IVF with intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) the only viable option. A multidisciplinary team (CF, fertility, maternal–fetal medicine, ethics) counselled extensively on risks of ovarian stimulation and pregnancy, including respiratory decline, anesthesia/procedural risk, increased PEx, and fetal complications. The team expressed high concern and uncertainty rather than an absolute recommendation against pregnancy. The patient, an orthodox Muslim, declined surrogacy for cultural reasons, and remained committed to biological motherhood. After counselling, she showed sustained behavioral change with improved adherence, modest weight gain, and clinical stabilization with slight lung function improvement over the next year, prompting reassessment of physiologic reserve while acknowledging persistent high risk. This case illustrates a recurring dilemma in contemporary CF care: how to balance respect for autonomy with non-maleficence when pregnancy is desired in the context of severe disease. It also highlights the importance of structured shared decision-making, transparent communication of uncertainty, and institutional ethical support. As increasing numbers of wwCF, some with significant comorbidity, pursue pregnancy and ART, multidisciplinary pathways that integrate CF, fertility, maternal-fetal medicine and ethics are essential, alongside clearer guidance for counselling and risk stratification in advanced CF.
sections.Conclusion: As reproductive aspirations rise among wwCF, high-risk scenarios requiring IVF/ICSI are increasingly encountered. This case underscores the complex interface between patient autonomy, clinician responsibility, and medical uncertainty in severe CF, and suggests that strong patient motivation may meaningfully improve adherence and short-term clinical status, factors that can influence risk assessment. Early fertility counselling, coordinated multidisciplinary care, and clearer frameworks for shared decision-making in advanced disease are urgently needed to support equitable, consistent and ethically grounded care for wwCF pursuing pregnancy.

presentations.summary: Objectives: F508del, the most prevalent CF-causing mutation, leads to a loss of F508 within CFTR’s first nucleotide-binding domain (NBD1). This destabilizes NBD1, impairing CFTR folding and weakening NBD1’s interface with CFTR transmembrane domains. In preclinical studies, combining suppressor mutations that stabilize NBD1 with others that improve CFTR assembly has been shown to correct F508del to wild-type (WT) levels. Without NBD1 stabilization, correction of F508del was significantly reduced. Approved modulators have significantly improved outcomes for people with CF (pwCF), but do not restore normal CFTR function in two-thirds of patients. No approved modulator directly stabilizes NBD1. With the goal of fu...
presentations.authors_text: S. Bercury1, A. Madanjian1, S. Altmann1, J. Foley1, A. Hunnicutt1, J. Liao1, Z. Gao1, G. Topalov1, M. Munson1, G. Hurlbut1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Sionna Therapeutics, Waltham, United States

sections.Objectives: F508del, the most prevalent CF-causing mutation, leads to a loss of F508 within CFTR’s first nucleotide-binding domain (NBD1). This destabilizes NBD1, impairing CFTR folding and weakening NBD1’s interface with CFTR transmembrane domains. In preclinical studies, combining suppressor mutations that stabilize NBD1 with others that improve CFTR assembly has been shown to correct F508del to wild-type (WT) levels. Without NBD1 stabilization, correction of F508del was significantly reduced. Approved modulators have significantly improved outcomes for people with CF (pwCF), but do not restore normal CFTR function in two-thirds of patients. No approved modulator directly stabilizes NBD1. With the goal of further improving CFTR function to deliver meaningful clinical benefit to pwCF, Sionna has advanced two potent first-in-class small molecule NBD1 stabilizers, SION-451 and SION-719, into clinical trials. Here we assess their impact on F508del-CFTR protein half-life in the presence and absence of CFTR domain assembly correctors.
sections.Methods: Using metabolic pulse-chase labeling, we assessed the impact of SION-451 and SION-719 on the half-life of mature F508del-CFTR in the presence and absence of TMD1-directed corrector SION-2222, ICL4-directed corrector SION-109, or ETI.
sections.Results: We demonstrate that NBD1 stabilizers can increase the half-life of the F508del-CFTR to levels seen with WT CFTR. This effect was apparent even when NBD1 stabilizers were used as single agents, an effect not observed with ETI.
sections.Conclusion: We have previously shown that SION-451 or SION-719, in combination with complementary modulators, enable full correction of F508del-CFTR maturation, trafficking and function in preclinical CF models. Here we show robust increases in CFTR half-life in vitro in response to treatment with NBD1 stabilizers that was not seen in response to ETI treatment alone, suggesting NBD1 instability is a central driver of F508del-CFTR’s greatly increased degradation rate.

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has transformed cystic fibrosis (CF) care, with significant improvements observed across multiple clinical outcomes. However, reports of its potential effect on structural lung disease, including bronchiectasis (Bx) and bronchial wall thickening (BWT), remain sparse. We assess ETI’s impact on Bx and BWT among people with CF (pwCF) in Denmark, hypothesising that ETI reduces Bx and BWT severity as evaluated by high-resolution computed tomography (HRCT).
sections.Methods: One baseline and one follow-up HRCT scan were included for each pwCF. All scans were performed during clinician-assessed clinical stability and evaluated for structural lung abnormalities using automatic bronchus-artery ratio (BA) scoring. Outcomes included %Bx1.1 and %Bx1.5, defined as the percentage of bronchi with an outer diameter widening of ≥1.1 and ≥1.5 times that of the adjacent artery, respectively, and %BWT, defined as the percentage of bronchi with wall thickening. Preliminary
sections.Results: By now, 312 HRCT scans from 156 pwCF (54 children, 102 adults; 53% females) have been evaluated, nearly half of our cohort. Median (IQR) time between HRCT and ETI initiation was -0.17 (-0.5; -0.07) years for baseline and 1.02 (0.97; 1.16) years for follow-up. Mean (95%CI) %Bx1.1 decreased from 61.6% (58.3; 64.9) pre-ETI to 56.2%(49.5; 62.9)% post-ETI, a reduction of -5.43 (-8.81; -2.05), p=0.0018. Mean %Bx1.5 changed from 24.2% (21.6; 26.8) to 23.2% (18.3; 28.0) post-ETI, with non-significant difference of -0.98 (-3.21; 1.24), p=0.3842. In contrast, mean %BWT decreased markedly from 74.6% (71.5; 77.7) pre-ETI to 57.2% (51.1; 63.3) post-ETI, yielding a substantial reduction of -17.5 (-20.4; -14.5), p<0.0001.
sections.Conclusion: ETI reduces bronchial widening and wall thickening after the first year of ETI treatment, though larger bronchiectasis persists. Further analyses and longer follow-up will increase our understanding of ETI’s effect on structural lung disease in pwCF.

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) provides clinical benefits for people with Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF), although treatment response varies, possibly due to lung microbiological features. We investigated Pa adaptation to ETI by integrating genomic, transcriptomic, phenotypic, and clinical data to identify adaptive traits and explain outcome variability.
sections.Methods: Fifty-four clonal Pa isolates were collected from 11 pwCF before and up to 28 months after ETI initiation. Phenotypic traits associated with acute and chronic infection were analyzed. Whole-genome and RNA sequencing of 35 isolates tracked within-host evolution. Six isolates were exposed in vitro to ETI to assess possible direct impacts. Clinical outcomes were evaluated and associations with bacterial features were explored using Factorial Analysis of Mixed Data.
sections.Results: Pa adaptation to ETI was strain-dependent, with limited overlap across clonal lineages. However, conserved pathways related to metabolism, virulence, and antibiotic resistance were consistently affected at genomic and transcriptomic levels. Phenotypic changes included biofilm modulation, variable pyocyanin production, and altered antibiotic susceptibility. In vitro ETI exposure induced transcriptional and genetic changes partially overlapping with in vivo findings, supporting a direct ETI effect. Clinically, pwCF showed significant improvement, with a median 15.5% increase in FEV₁ and reduced exacerbations, hospitalizations, and intravenous antibiotic use. Greater benefit was associated with isolates lacking mucoidy and LasR-associated phenotypes, hypermutability, and reduced pyocyanin production.
sections.Conclusion: While ETI markedly improves clinical outcomes, Pa persists through flexible, strain-specific adaptive mechanisms. Monitoring pathogen evolution during ETI therapy may support precision management of chronic infection in CF. Study supported by the Italian CF Foundation (FFC#16/2021) and the ECFS/CF Europe Research Fellowship.

This study is the first to administer ETD001 to people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF), and was designed to be run in two parts: Parts A and B. sections.Methods: Part A enabled a first evaluation of the safety, tolerability and PK of repeat doses of ETD001 over 7 days in pwCF. ETD001 (4.5 mg) was delivered as an inhaled aerosol generated by a Pari eFlow nebuliser, over 2-3 minutes, twice daily (BiD). Part A enrolled 10 pwCF (3 placebo, 7 treated) with a mean (±SD) age of 37.1 (11.9) years and mean BMI (±SD) of 21.5 (2.4) kg/m². ETD001 was well tolerated, with no safety signals to prevent progression into Part B. A single serious adverse event (pulmonary exacerbation) was reported during the post-treatment phase that was considered unlikely related to treatment. There was no incidence of hyperkalaemia. Part B used a double-blind, placebo-controlled, cross-over design to examine the effect of repeat BID dosing of ETD001 (4.5 mg) over 28 days on absolute change in ppFEV1 in addition to safety and PK. Participants were randomised to receive either placebo or ETD001 for 28 days in a 2 group, 2 sequence cross-over design, with a 28-day washout period. The primary objective was to understand whether ETD001 delivers a clinically meaningful, and statistically significant change in ppFEV1 from baseline to day 28 compared to placebo. Part B enrolled a total of 47 pwCF with a mean (±SD) age of 35.0 (10.4) years, mean BMI (±SD) of 21.4 (3.0) kg/m² and mean (±SD) ppFEV1 of 61.2 (14.8)%.

sections.Conclusion: Data are being analysed as of the time of abstract submission and will be presented in full at the meeting. Russell et al., (2025) Journal of Cystic Fibrosis. S1569-1993(25)02529-9

presentations.title: ​Respiratory abnormalities in subjects referred for congenital absence of the vas deferens and carrying two CFTR mutations
presentations.summary: Objectives: The majority of patients with congenital absence of the vas deferens (CAVD) have two mutations in the CFTR gene and are diagnosed with a CFTR-RD. CAVD has been associated with mild respiratory disease assessed by spirometry and physical evaluation. The objective of this study is to characterize respiratory abnormalities on CT scans in subjects with CAVD carrying two CFTR mutations. Method: We conducted a single-center retrospective study of 122 subjects referred to CAVD assessment for which two CFTR mutations were identified from 2002 to 2025 (next-generation sequencing). They underwent a systematic evaluation of CFTR-related organ dysfunction (medical history, sweat test, chest CT scans, spirometr...
presentations.authors_text: E. Krantschenko1, A. Pagin1, T. Perez1, C. Leroy1, F. Marcelli1, O. Le Rouzic1, C. Audousset1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1CHU de Lille, Lille, France
presentations.session_title: Workshop 16 - Further optimising measures of cystic fibrosis pulmonary disease

sections.Objectives: The majority of patients with congenital absence of the vas deferens (CAVD) have two mutations in the CFTR gene and are diagnosed with a CFTR-RD. CAVD has been associated with mild respiratory disease assessed by spirometry and physical evaluation. The objective of this study is to characterize respiratory abnormalities on CT scans in subjects with CAVD carrying two CFTR mutations.
sections.Methods: We conducted a single-center retrospective study of 122 subjects referred to CAVD assessment for which two CFTR mutations were identified from 2002 to 2025 (next-generation sequencing). They underwent a systematic evaluation of CFTR-related organ dysfunction (medical history, sweat test, chest CT scans, spirometry). CT scan abnormalities were classified as presence of bronchiectasis, mucus plugging or airway wall thickening based on radiology reports. Airflow obstruction was defined by FEV1/FVC<0.70.
sections.Results: Among the 122 subjects, 119 was classified as CFTR-RD. The median age was 32 years [29;35], mean FEV1 was 4.23 L (±0.58) and 101% predicted (±12.3). The median value for sweat test was 65.5 mEq/L [47.2;91]. The most common mutations were F508del (53%, n=64) associated in trans with a TG12T5 mutation (34%, n=38), R117H (21%, n=19), and D443Y, G576A, R668C (15%, n=11). The prevalent CT scan abnormalities were airways wall thickening, bronchiectasis, and bronchiolectasis in 13% (n=15), 4% (n=5) and 4% (n=5) of cases, respectively. Twenty percent (n=23) had CT scan abnormalities, including 3 subjects with airflow obstruction and 5 with respiratory symptoms.
sections.Conclusion: Among subjects with CAVD and carrying two CFTR mutations, 20% had abnormal CT scan among them, 5% had an airflow obstruction. The initial assessment of airway involvement in patients with CAVD carrying two CFTR gene mutations should include a chest CT scan and pulmonary function tests that are more sensitive than spirometry.
