presentations.session_title: Workshop 17 - Airway microbiology in the CFTR modulator era: pathogen dynamics, adaptation and the changing microbiome

sections.Background: Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) therapy has shown clinical benefit for numerous patients with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). While its impact on the airway microbiome is increasingly investigated, the influence of the microbiome on the therapy effectiveness and its potential prognostic value for clinical outcome, remain poorly understood.
sections.Methods: Sputum samples from 54 pwCF were collected at different time points. Samples underwent metaproteomic analysis to characterize the dynamic of the microbiome profile. Metaproteomic data were integrated with clinical parameters, including lung function (ppFEV1) microbiology and antibiotic use.
sections.Results: ETI therapy was associated with significant increase in microbiome alpha diversity and anaerobes abundance. Longitudinal analysis identified three distinct pulmotypes based on microbiome trajectories, one associated with lower lung function (ppFEV1 <70%) and two associated with higher initial ppFEV1 values. In addition, patients showing ppFEV1 improvements after ETI initiation displayed distinct functional dynamics compared to those who did not. Machine-learning models leveraging these longitudinal profiles accurately predicted ppFEV1 values at the individual level.
sections.Conclusion: Under ETI therapy, the airway microbiome undergoes heterogeneous, patient-dependent changes that can be captured by distinct pulmotypes. Importantly, microbial functional activity emerged as a strong predictor of clinical outcomes, supporting the potential of microbiome-based stratification and prognostic tools in CF.

presentations.title: Addressing disengagement and health decline: what are we missing?!
presentations.summary: Background: In our large adult cystic fibrosis (CF) centre, we adhere to national guidelines on the management of CF. However, "standard CF care" does not suit all patients. We have a cohort of patients that deteriorate rapidly despite receiving standard CF MDT care. Typical patient characteristics include poor adherence, unstable lung function, low or falling BMI, frequent exacerbations and reliance on frequent rescue intravenous (IV) antibiotics, frequent non attendance at appointments, psychological and/or social difficulties and difficulty communicating honestly with the MDT. We have created a bespoke outpatient service to engage such patients and support adherence called BOOST (Better Outpatient Outcome S...
presentations.authors_text: H. Green1, M. CLegg1, H. Oxley1, F. Dowdall1, W. Foo1, F. Lewis1, A. Orr1, H. Winterbottom1, A. Jones1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust, Manchester Adult Cystic Fibrosis Centre, Manchester, United Kingdom
presentations.session_title: Workshop 18 - Complex psychosocial issues/nursing case studies

sections.Background: In our large adult cystic fibrosis (CF) centre, we adhere to national guidelines on the management of CF. However, "standard CF care" does not suit all patients. We have a cohort of patients that deteriorate rapidly despite receiving standard CF MDT care. Typical patient characteristics include poor adherence, unstable lung function, low or falling BMI, frequent exacerbations and reliance on frequent rescue intravenous (IV) antibiotics, frequent non attendance at appointments, psychological and/or social difficulties and difficulty communicating honestly with the MDT. We have created a bespoke outpatient service to engage such patients and support adherence called BOOST (Better Outpatient Outcome Support Team). BOOST consists of a consultant, specialist nurses, physiotherapist, occupational therapist, dietitian and the psychosocial team. Any MDT member can refer a patient to BOOST. Each enrolled patient receives intensive home support using a combination of weekly texts or calls, regular face to face reviews in a day case setting or home visits. Patients have open access to contact all MDT members in working hours. Biweekly MDT meetings are held to discuss progress, problems, patient feedback and priorities. Mel, who is ineligible for CFTR modulators, was one of the first patients enrolled on this programme in 2020 and had poor adherence, high IV antibiotic requirements, accelerated FEV1 decline and frequent non attendance in clinic. With intensive support Mel had periods of stability but these were short lived and although her engagement with the CF team hugely improved she was unable to sustain adherence. With growing experience of managing similar patients within this service, and the extremely high proportion of disengaged and non adherent patients we have found to be neurodiverse, we screened Mel for ADHD despite her displaying no significant physical hyperactivity. In the 12 months prior to her ADHD assessment Mel had 6 courses of rescue IV antibiotics, achieving a maximum end of IV antibiotic treatment FEV1 of 50% predicted. Mel was diagnosed with ADHD at her assessment and commenced on Methylphenidate and was then able to put into practice all the things she had worked on with the BOOST MDT. Her lung function increased to 61% predicted and in the 12 months post treatment initiation for ADHD successfully maintained an FEV1 >50% predicted with no courses of intravenous antibiotics. Discussion: We have had very successful outcomes with providing intensive MDT support to a selected group of disengaged and rapidly deteriorating patients with CF. In our experience “completely disengaged” patients often have a very in depth knowledge of their disease, objective measurements and prognosis. With individualized intensive support, habits of a lifetime and prognosis can be changed. We have found that patients communicate very well by text message (often better than verbally). A combination of positivity, praise, frequent communication (between MDT members and to the patient), joint problem solving and visual feedback are key factors in improving these patients’ outcomes. Recognising and treating ADHD in non adherent, disengaged patients can have a significant positive impact on their physical and mental health.
sections.Conclusion: Using an alternative approach with rapidly deteriorating, non-adherent, disengaged patients and screening for and treating ADHD can result in significant improvements in patient outcomes and satisfaction.

presentations.title: SPL5AC, MUC5AC lowering ASO for cystic fibrosis and other muco-obstructive diseases
presentations.summary: Background: Airway mucus is a key defense barrier and is primarily composed of the mucins MUC5AC and MUC5B. In cystic fibrosis (CF), impaired CFTR function causes airway dehydration and increased mucin concentration. In addition, goblet cells and glandular hyperplasia in CF and other muco-obstructive diseases (asthma, COPD, NCFB) drives mucin overproduction. An elevated MUC5AC-to-MUC5B ratio in patients leads to thick, obstructive mucus and chronic infection and inflammation, with higher MUC5AC levels and mucus plugging correlating with increased disease severity. Splisense is developing an antisense oligonucleotide (ASO) to specifically reduce MUC5AC, aiming to improve mucus clearance and relieve airway obstr...
presentations.authors_text: E. Ozeri Galai1, Y.S. Oren1, N. Liu2, C.D Stampfer1, R.M. Elgrabli1, T. Blinder1, S.A. Jubeh1, T. Mordechai1, C.M. Evans2, B. Kerem1,3, G. Hart1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Splisense, Haddasah Ein Kerem, Jerusalem, Israel, 2University of Colorado, Division of Pulmonary Science and Critical Care Medicine, Colorado, United States, 3Hebrew University, Genetics, Jerusalem, Israel

sections.Background: Airway mucus is a key defense barrier and is primarily composed of the mucins MUC5AC and MUC5B. In cystic fibrosis (CF), impaired CFTR function causes airway dehydration and increased mucin concentration. In addition, goblet cells and glandular hyperplasia in CF and other muco-obstructive diseases (asthma, COPD, NCFB) drives mucin overproduction. An elevated MUC5AC-to-MUC5B ratio in patients leads to thick, obstructive mucus and chronic infection and inflammation, with higher MUC5AC levels and mucus plugging correlating with increased disease severity. Splisense is developing an antisense oligonucleotide (ASO) to specifically reduce MUC5AC, aiming to improve mucus clearance and relieve airway obstruction and inflammation.
sections.Methods: The efficacy and durability of SPL5AC was tested in IL-13 induced HBE cells and in muco-obstructive disease mouse models (IL-13, ovalbumin, Aspergillus and House dust mite) by analyzing MUC5AC RNA and protein (Western Blot or immunohistochemical staining). The effect on airway hyperresponsiveness (AHR) was analyzed using a FlexiVent.
sections.Results: SPL5AC, the clinical lead candidate, demonstrated robust efficacy and durability in multiple in vitro and in vivo disease models. In IL-13–induced HBE cells, SPL5AC penetrated the thick apical mucus layer and reduced MUC5AC RNA and protein. In vivo, SPL5AC lowered Muc5ac expression, reduced mucus plugging and inflammation, and inhibited methacholine-induced airway hyperresponsiveness. Pharmacodynamic analyses showed high potency and stability, with durable effects lasting at least 28 days, supporting dose selection and regimen for Phase 1/2 studies.
sections.Conclusion: Restoring proper mucus viscoelasticity and clearance in the lungs of patients with muco-obstructive diseases like CF, remains a major goal. Our results demonstrate that SPL5AC ASO has a potential therapeutic benefit for CF and other muco-obstructive patients with the objective to advance to first in Human in 2026.

To account for differences in patient size, the CSA of each airway was normalised by the CSA of the trachea and the dynamic change in CSA was f...
presentations.authors_text: Y. Brown1, N. Stewart1, D.J. Jakymelen1, L. Smith1, J. Wild1, MAGNIFY Strategic Research Centre
presentations.affiliations_text: 1University of Sheffield, POLARIS, University MRI, Division of Clinical Medicine, Sheffield, United Kingdom
presentations.session_title: Workshop 16 - Further optimising measures of cystic fibrosis pulmonary disease

sections.Objectives: To quantify static and dynamic airway morphology using 1H-MRI in healthy volunteers (HV) and people with Cystic Fibrosis (CF).
sections.Methods: 3D Ultrashort Echo-Time (UTE) MRI of the lungs was acquired during tidal breathing and reconstructed at both end-inspiration (EIVT) and end-expiration (FRC). Synopsys’ Simpleware™ and a custom python script were used to segment and quantify the size and shape of visible airways. The ellipse axis ratio (EAR) quantified airway shape (EAR=1 represents a circle and 0 a line). The lumen cross-sectional area (CSA) quantified size. To account for differences in patient size, the CSA of each airway was normalised by the CSA of the trachea and the dynamic change in CSA was found using EIVT - FRC/EIVT(trachea).
sections.Results: The median (IQR) of HV/CF subjects are: M:F=3:2/4:6, age=34 (34-39)/24 (23-38) years, BMI=24.2 (22.9-24.7)/23.5 (20.4-25.9) kg/m2, FEV1pp=74 (55-89) %. At both EIVT and FRC, 100% of airways were segmentable to the 3rd generation and 37.5% (HV) and 62.5% (CF) to the 4th generation. Greater variance in lumen size and shape was observed across the CF cohort when compared to the HV cohort. Median (IQR) EAR at FRC and normalised change in CSA in the trachea respectively were 0.83 (0.12), 0.04 (0.07) in CF and 0.88 (0.04), 0.04 (0.01) in HV. At generation 4, normalised CF airways were larger and had a greater dynamic change in CSA when compared to HV. Those with lower FEV1pp had the largest CSA and the largest dynamic change. At generation 4, EAR at FRC was lower in CF compared to HV: 0.06 (0.01), CF: 0.07 (0.04), suggesting these airways are more elliptical compared to HV.
sections.Conclusion: Using 1H UTE-MRI, airway morphology can be segmented to the 4th generation and often beyond enabling dynamic airway analysis. Both static and dynamic airway morphology is highly variable in CF compared to healthy controls. In CF, the trachea and 4th airway generation on average tend to be less circular and have greater distensibility.

Using computerised tomography (CT) reports pre-ETI initiation, subjects were classified as diffuse or focal bronchiectasis (Bx) and the proportion of total cough swab/sputum samples provided in years -2 pre- and +2 post-ETI start date that were Pa positive (+) was calculated as median[IQR}% per person per year,...
presentations.authors_text: A. Mundell1, D. Fenn2, J. Ish-Horowicz2, M. Mossop2, N. Ramadan1, M. Rosenthal1, T. Semple3, C. Short2, J. Davies1,2, J. King1,2

sections.Objectives: Investigate the association of baseline structural lung disease with changes to Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) isolation frequency following elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) in people with chronic Pa infection.
sections.Methods: Retrospective cohort analysis of 136 people with CF at Royal Brompton Hospital with Leeds defined chronic Pa infection in ≥1 of 2 years pre- individualised ETI start date. Using computerised tomography (CT) reports pre-ETI initiation, subjects were classified as diffuse or focal bronchiectasis (Bx) and the proportion of total cough swab/sputum samples provided in years -2 pre- and +2 post-ETI start date that were Pa positive (+) was calculated as median[IQR}% per person per year, pppy). Data normality was checked (Shapiro-Wilk) and groups based on Bx distribution were compared using Mann Whitney (for proportions pppy) and Chi square tests (for apparent clearance).
sections.Results: Of 136 people (67:69 M:F, mean age 41.3 years, range 16-83 years), 131 had pre-ETI CT imaging within 6.5 years; reports described 85 diffuse Bx, 41 focal Bx, 2 none and 1 not specified. Fewer samples pppy were collected post-ETI (year -2, 8.0[4.5], +2, 3.1[2.2]), with no difference in provision by Bx distribution. Pre-ETI (year -2) 100[23.8]% samples pppy were Pa+; no difference by Bx distribution. Post-ETI (year +2) people with focal Bx had fewer Pa+ samples pppy (33.3[100]%) than those with diffuse Bx (100[73.9]%; p<0.05). 22 people had no Pa+ samples in year +2. This apparent clearance was more likely with focal (n=14, 34.1%) than diffuse (n=8, 9.4%) Bx (OR 5.0 [95% CI 1.9-13.3], p<0.001).
sections.Conclusion: Focal Bx appears associated with greater reduction in Pa isolation frequency and greater apparent clearance following ETI treatment in people with chronic Pa infection. Formal CT scoring (PRAGMA-CF) is now underway to examine effect of Bx distribution as well as severity, wall thickening, and mucus plugging on Pa isolation frequency (results available for conference).

presentations.title: Challenging adult cystic fibrosis cases with chronic kidney disease
presentations.summary: Background: Although hepatic side effects of modulator drugs used in cystic fibrosis (CF) treatment are known, renal side effects have not been clearly described. Dosage adjustment of modulator drugs based on glomerular filtration rate(GFR) has not been defined. Here we share the clinical outcomes of our patients with low GFRs who are on modulators. Discussion: Patient 1: A 35-year-old male, diagnosed CF at 6 months. His mutations were F508del and 2789+5G>A that are eligible for Trikafta that he is on since May 2024. In 2007 during follow-ups, proteinuria was detected and a kidney biopsy revealed focal segmental glomerulosclerosis. Until 2023, he was not a on dialysis routinely, but occasionally presented with...
presentations.authors_text: B. Akyol1, Y. Yalçın1, A. Alkan1, O. Karcıoğlu1
presentations.affiliations_text: 1Hacettepe University, Chest Diseases, Ankara, Turkey
presentations.session_title: Workshop 18 - Complex psychosocial issues/nursing case studies

sections.Background: Although hepatic side effects of modulator drugs used in cystic fibrosis (CF) treatment are known, renal side effects have not been clearly described. Dosage adjustment of modulator drugs based on glomerular filtration rate(GFR) has not been defined. Here we share the clinical outcomes of our patients with low GFRs who are on modulators. Discussion: Patient 1: A 35-year-old male, diagnosed CF at 6 months. His mutations were F508del and 2789+5G>A that are eligible for Trikafta that he is on since May 2024. In 2007 during follow-ups, proteinuria was detected and a kidney biopsy revealed focal segmental glomerulosclerosis. Until 2023, he was not a on dialysis routinely, but occasionally presented with flank pain and later told he needed a kidney transplant. In April 2025, he started undergoing hemodialysis 3/7 routinely. His renal function tests remained at baseline levels. Neither a decrease in FEV1, nor any exacerbations were observed during the hemodialysis periods. Patient 2: A 31-year-old male, born in Saudi Arabia, with a homozygous F508del mutation, was diagnosed CF after birth. In 2022, his creatinine levels was increased, prompting a kidney biopsy, then revealed a diagnosis of IgA nephropathy. He was started Trikafta in August 2024, but treatment was interrupted due to a court order requiring the drug to be obtained at that time. During follow-ups, an indication for kidney transplantation emerged, and he was started on peritoneal dialysis until the transplant. With the inclusion of Trikafta in Türkiye’s reimbursement coverage, Trikafta resumed almost simultaneously with peritoneal dialysis. Since there is no defined protocol on the dosage adjustment of Trikafta in patients with low GFRs, Trikafta was administered once in two days for a month with close monitoring. Then the dosage was raised to full regimen after not seeing any drop in GFRs. With Trikafta, FEV1 levels increased, exacerbations ceased, and cough and sputum production resolved. Our patient is still on treatment. Patient 3: A 34-year-old female with G85E and 1811+1.6kbA>G mutations was diagnosed at 11 months old. In 2018, when she was 27 years old, she was put on the waiting list for lung transplantation. She has received Trikafta in August 2021. She was able to use Trikafta for 3 weeks prior to the lung transplant. After the transplant, she was started on Tacrolimus and her GFRs began to drop. It was thought that the patient had persistent renal impairment resulting from immunosuppressive therapy, so the treatment was switched to Everolimus. Due to the patient's recurrent sinusitis, she was decided to reinstate Trikafta. Again, since there is no defined protocol on the dosage adjustment of Trikafta in patients with low GFRs and also with lung transplant, we started to administer Trikafta twice in every 3 days with close monitoring. There was no decrease in the GFRs so far, and we will be sharing the follow-up results.
sections.Conclusion: While the renal dosage of modulator drugs remains unspecified, we have endeavored to monitor adverse effects in our patients through the implementation of adaptive regimens. The lack of renal adverse effects observed with Trikafta in three patients with chronic kidney disease indicates that such side effects are infrequent. Nevertheless, additional data is required to ascertain if modulator medications can be administered safely to patients with renal impairment.

presentations.summary: Cystic fibrosis (CF) airway pathology is marked by the presence of hypertrophic goblet cells as faithfully modeled in air-liquid interface (ALI) primary cultures from CF donors, exhibiting functional deficits in mucociliary clearance (reduced air surface liquid and particle transport velocity). presentations.session_title: Workshop 16 - Further optimising measures of cystic fibrosis pulmonary disease

sections.Objectives: Clinical and functional response to elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) is heterogeneous in children with cystic fibrosis (CF). We analyzed if the number of ETI-responsive CFTR variant could predict sweat chloride concentration (SCC) and lung clearance index (LCI) evolution in children treated with ETI, using real-world data.
sections.Methods: SCC and LCI evolution were collected in children with CF in our local pediatric center before and during their first year of treatment by ETI. Data included children without F508del treated by ETI off-label thanks to the French compassionate program. CFTR variant responsiveness to ETI was determined using the program’s classification.
sections.Results: Among 201 children included, 131 carried two ETI-responsive CFTR variants and 70 carried only one. 26% started ETI before 6 years of age and 34% between 6 and 12 years. 49% received CFTR modulators prior to ETI. Median SCC under ETI was 25 mmol/L (IQR 16-38) in children with two ETI-responsive CFTR variants and 36 mmol/L (27-51) in those with only one (p<0.0001). Multivariable analyses showed that number of ETI-responsive CFTR variant and age at ETI initiation were predictive factors of SCC in children treated by ETI (p=0.01 and p=0.0006 respectively). Sex and prior CFTR modulators use were not. LCI before and after ETI initiation was available in 151 children, 101 with two ETI-responsive CFTR variants and 50 with only one. Multivariable analyses showed that only LCI value prior to ETI was predictive of LCI evolution in children treated by ETI (p<0.0001). SCC after ETI initiation and number of ETI-responsive CFTR variant were not.
sections.Conclusion: Children with CF with two ETI-responsive CFTR variant normalized SCC more often than those with only one. However, lung function evolution was similar. These findings suggest maximal improvement in lung function is reached by current CFTR modulators and earlier access in life should be prioritized over more potent CFTR function restoration.

presentations.session_title: Workshop 17 - Airway microbiology in the CFTR modulator era: pathogen dynamics, adaptation and the changing microbiome

sections.Objectives: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has significantly improved quality of life for people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). As shown in culture- and sequencing-based studies of pwCF 12 years and older, ETI decreases the abundance of typical CF pathogens in the airways. For three years, ETI has also been available for children with CF aged 6-11 years. To investigate the impact of early ETI treatment on the airway microbiome our study employs shotgun metagenomics for high resolution identification of bacteria, fungi and DNA viruses.
sections.Methods: In this prospective post-approval study, cough swabs from n=15 cwCF aged 6-11 years at baseline, 3 months and 24 months after initiation of ETI therapy were collected. DNA was extracted under ultra-clean wet-lab conditions, followed by metagenome sequencing. Fragment libraries were sequenced on an Illumina NovaSeq. Processing of the sequencing reads was performed by our in-house developed sequencing pipeline Wochenende prior to statistical analyses.
sections.Results: In this prospective post-approval study, treatment with ETI improved CFTR function and clinical parameters in cwCF aged 6-11 years, similar to published studies. With respect to airway microbiology, microbial α-diversity remained unchanged throughout ETI therapy. However, the high resolution of metagenomics permitted us to identify age as a central factor influencing the abundance of several commensal taxa. During the course of our study, we did not detect colonization with Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Staphylococcus aureus occurred only at low abundance in six of 15 baseline samples and in two of 13 samples at month 3 but was absent from the two-year follow-up time point.
sections.Conclusion: Metagenomic profiles appeared to be more strongly influenced by age than by ETI treatment status, suggesting that, in this age group, inter-individual variability and developmental factors may play a more dominant role in shaping the respiratory microbiome than therapeutic intervention(s).

presentations.session_title: Workshop 18 - Complex psychosocial issues/nursing case studies

sections.Background: Psychologic side effects in people with CF (pwCF) receiving elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) are rare, albeit they have been reported. Recently, the new triple therapy with vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaftor (VTD) was approved for pwCF with at least one non-class-1-mutation. It is highly relevant to see whether similar VTD-related neuropsychological effects may emerge. Discussion: We report on a 12-year-old male pwCF with primary exocrine pancreatic sufficiency carrying both class-2 and class-5 CFTR mutations. Thriving regularly without pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy, since 2024 he reported periods of increasingly severe acute abdominal pain associated with moderate increases in serum lipase and a decline of fecal pancreatic elastase. The recurrent pain combined with pain-induced panic attacks, required repeated inpatient treatment and pain control with opioids. Nevertheless, MRI and ultrasound imaging only revealed non-severe pathologies, including minimal liver abnormalities. Upper GI endoscopy revealed a small ulcus, and colonoscopy showed an abnormal sigma-rotation, which did not explain the severity of his symptoms. In November 2024, he started treatment with ETI, which substantially improved his abdominal symptoms. However, severe psychological distress resulted, including suicidal thoughts, necessitating a pause of ETI therapy. Under continuous psychological therapy, ETI then was reintroduced but administered only once a week in order to control abdominal symptoms while avoiding psychological decompensation. In October 2025, the patient transitioned to therapy with VTD, and a relief of abdominal symptoms, normalization of serum-lipase and fecal elastase have been achieved. Notably, he has since remained free of psychological symptoms, even while receiving full-dose VTD.
sections.Conclusion: In the reported case, the switch from ETI to VTD resulted in the relief of pancreatic pain, without the psychological side effects related to previous ETI therapy.
